Title: Master Ball
The Master Ball, known as マスターボール (Master Ball) in Japanese, debuted as a Poké Ball variant during Generation I. It guarantees the capture of any wild Pokémon. Its creation is attributed to Silph Co. Employing it from the Bag during a wild encounter unfailingly secures the wild Pokémon, with the sole exception being the spectral Marowak encountered within Pokémon Tower. Contrary to the frequent misunderstanding that the Master Ball possesses a slight failure probability in specific generations, its mechanism involves completely bypassing the standard catch formula, thereby eliminating any chance-based computations.

Within Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, deploying a Master Ball circumvents the manual aiming feature for Poké Balls; instead, a cinematic sequence depicts the Master Ball capturing the selected Pokémon. Should it be deployed against Legendary Pokémon in these titles, they will try to deflect the ball during this cutscene, yet their capture remains assured. Its use is restricted in circumstances where Poké Balls are generally unusable, like wild battles involving multiple present opponents or encounters with a trial Pokémon. When thrown in a Trainer battle, the opposing Trainer will swat the item away, resulting in the item's waste (in Generation III and prior) or merely the forfeiture of the player's turn (from Generation IV onward).

Commencing with Generation VI, Pokémon offspring inherit their containment Poké Ball from their progenitors. Nevertheless, any progeny otherwise destined to inherit a Master Ball, Cherish Ball, or Strange Ball will instead be housed within a regular Poké Ball. The move Fling will prove unsuccessful if executed while holding a Master Ball. The antagonists leading Team Rainbow Rocket store their Pokémon inside Master Balls. Both AI Sada and AI Turo utilize Master Balls for their Pokémon. In The Indigo Disk, Kieran briefly holds Terapagos within a Master Ball, which Terapagos shatters upon adopting its Stellar Form.

One of these balls can be observed at the Poké Ball Boutique located in Lumiose City. Pokémon Stadium 2 features the Master Ball in its mini-game, Furret's Frolic. It stands as the largest ball within that mini-game, making a later appearance than others and contributing 5 points. In Pokémon XD, Master Balls are employed during the Battle Bingo game. Players begin with two and have the opportunity to acquire an additional one from a designated ? space. A Master Ball-themed ornamental rug was obtainable from the Hero's Catalogue in Pokémon Dream World. The Master Ball card is obtainable in Pokémon Trading Card Game 2: The Invasion of Team GR!.

For Pokémon GO players, the Master Ball initially became accessible on May 22, 2023. It guarantees the capture of any Pokémon encountered. Its throw cannot fail; instead, a cinematic showing the Pokémon's capture activates, bypassing the standard throwing mechanics. Within a Raid Battle, Max Battle, or Team GO Rocket battle's bonus challenge, the Master Ball stands alone as the sole Poké Ball selectable from the player's Bag. Furthermore, it is impossible to discard it from the Bag. Employing a Master Ball does not grant bonus XP for executing Curveball Throws or successfully hitting the target circle. Rather, a specific "Master Ball Throw" bonus of 1,000 XP is awarded.

Both Pokémon Pinball and Pokémon Pinball: Ruby & Sapphire feature the Master Ball multiplying bonus points by a factor of ×4. Its effect of guaranteeing capture without fail is conferred upon each Poké Ball. In Pokémon Frienda, once a specified number of moves are expended and at least one opposing Pokémon has fainted, players may insert ¥100 for an opportunity to capture all remaining opposing Pokémon. A rotating wheel then dictates which Poké Balls are deployed against the Pokémon, with a Master Ball representing one of the extremely rare possibilities. Within Pokémon Pokopia, a Master Ball housing a Mewtwo materializes atop the renovated abandoned skyscraper in Sparkling Skylands. Upon Ditto's approach, Mewtwo awakens and liberates itself from its containment.

The Master Ball is referenced on the Poké Ball trophy in Super Smash Bros. Brawl, where it is designated as the most potent variant of Poké Ball. In Super Smash Bros. for Nintendo 3DS/Wii U, the Master Ball manifests as a throwable item. While largely akin to the regular Poké Ball item, the Master Ball specifically summons rare and Legendary Pokémon, Zoroark, or a Goldeen. A trophy depicting the Master Ball also exists in both versions of this game. For North America, descriptive text states these potent, valuable balls can apprehend any wild Pokémon. In Smash Bros., elusive Pokémon frequently emerge from them. A Master Ball is readily identifiable by its distinctive purple hue and prominent 'M' on its upper surface. A Pokémon released from a Master Ball possesses the potential to alter the course of a battle. European descriptions refer to it as a rare Poké Ball type that always succeeds in catching a Pokémon. Tossing one in this game will enlist the aid of the Mythical or Legendary Pokémon contained within. Their purple coloring and large 'M' make Master Balls simple to recognize. Deploy one to rapidly shift the momentum of any conflict! Super Smash Bros. Ultimate sees the Master Ball operating identically to its predecessors, calling forth a Legendary or Mythical Pokémon, Zoroark, or Goldeen.

Misty possessed a beach ball adorned to resemble a Master Ball in the episodes Beauty and the Beach and A Hot Water Battle. This particular Master Ball-themed beach ball made another appearance in the Pikachu short, Pikachu's Ice Adventure. A depiction of a Master Ball was displayed on a billboard affixed to a structure in Neon Town during The Song of Jigglypuff. Within Whiscash and Ash, Sullivan tried employing a Master Ball as a final effort to capture Nero, an exceptionally large wild Whiscash that had consistently evaded him. Yet, Nero consumed the Master Ball before vanishing back into the water, thus remaining uncaught. By the episode's conclusion, Nero was observed toying with the Master Ball using its tongue. The Japanese ending theme from Pokémon the Series: Diamond and Pearl, titled Which One ~ Is It?, featured a Master Ball. Nidothing's video in Found You, Fuecoco! included a mention of the Master Ball.

Although it never physically appeared in Pokémon Origins, the Master Ball was showcased during the Silph Co. president's secretary's demonstration in File 3: Giovanni. She stated that the Master Ball was still under development, existing merely as a prototype at that time. She further explained this was Team Rocket's motivation for seizing their office building: they aimed to expedite the Master Ball's development and then pilfer it upon completion, thereby ensuring exclusive ownership. Following Team Rocket's expulsion from the premises by Red, it was noted that the Master Ball's development was temporarily suspended.

During Mewtwo Strikes Back!, Dr. Fuji tried to capture Mew with a Master Ball, but was unsuccessful as Mew evaded the thrown item. And Mewtwo... Three! disclosed that Blaine owned a Master Ball. Shortly thereafter, Red deployed it to capture Mewtwo on Blaine's behalf. Malamar Traps unveiled that Blaine had recaptured Mewtwo using the Master Ball after their reunion at the Pokémon Village. In (Violent) Earthquake! Cyrus's Consuming Ambition, Cyrus presented Hareta with a Master Ball, acknowledging that Hareta had stirred his emotions, instructing him to carry it to Mount Coronet. During Bonds Connect Across Space-Time..., the Master Ball slipped from Hareta's pocket at the Spear Pillar, prompting Professor Rowan to inform him of its inherent ability to capture any Pokémon unfailingly. Aiming to halt the clash between Dialga and Palkia, Hareta endeavored to use the Master Ball on Palkia, but Palkia, possessing absolute command over space, successfully vanished from the Master Ball's trajectory before swatting it away. In The Legendary Pokémon Appears!!, Red utilized a Master Ball to capture Articuno. Satoshi received a Master Ball from the Silph Co. president in PZ07, a gesture of gratitude for liberating his company from Team Rocket's grasp.

According to Pokémon Power (issue 2, page 3), Professor Oak was credited with the Master Ball as his most recent invention. He publicly disclosed its existence during a press conference. Professor Oak granted Silph Co. a license for the Master Ball design, and the company's initial field trials were reported as encouraging. As a Trainer card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Master Ball first appeared in English within the Original Series' Gym Challenge, and in Japanese during the Original Era's Expansion Sheet 2. Subsequent reprints of this Trainer card include the Japanese-exclusive Intro Pack Neo (during the Neo Era), the Japanese-exclusive Pokémon VS expansion (in the VS Era), the English e-Card Series (mirroring the Japanese e-Card Era), and the English EX Series (corresponding to the Japanese ADV Era and PCG Era). In its capacity as a Trainer card, this card

Title: Ultra Ball

This entry focuses on the contemporary version of the Ultra Ball. For the Ultra Ball featured in Legends: Arceus, consult Ultra Ball (Hisui). To learn about the Poké Ball known as "Ultra Ball" in Japanese, refer to Beast Ball.

Known in Japanese as ハイパーボール (Hyper Ball) and sometimes simply called the Hyper Ball in English, the Ultra Ball is a specific kind of Poké Ball that first appeared in Generation I. It represents an enhanced iteration of the Great Ball, primarily utilized for capturing wild Pokémon. Within Pokémon Crystal, during the Goldenrod Department Store's rooftop sale event, a single Ultra Ball could be purchased for $1000.

When deployed from a player's Bag during a wild Pokémon encounter, this item initiates an attempt to capture the wild Pokémon, applying a catch rate multiplier of 2×. However, should it be employed against an Ultra Beast, its catch rate modifier alters dramatically to 410/4096× (approximately 0.1×). The Ultra Ball shares the same usage restrictions as other Poké Balls; it is unusable in scenarios like wild battles involving multiple opposing Pokémon simultaneously or when facing a trial Pokémon. In a Trainer battle (unless functioning as a Snag Ball for a Shadow Pokémon), the opposing Trainer will block its effect, resulting in the item's loss in Generation III and prior games, or simply the forfeiture of the player's turn from Generation IV onwards. Furthermore, deploying it against the ghostly Marowak will lead to the Pokémon evading it, causing the ball to be lost.

A notable roster of Trainers house their Pokémon within Ultra Balls: Lusamine, Faba, Guzma, Plumeria, Ryuki, Molayne, Sina, Dexio, Grimsley, Anabel, Wally, Colress, and Cynthia. Additionally, several Trainer classes, specifically those sharing character models with any of the aforementioned individuals (such as the Eevee User or Principal), entrust their Pokémon to Ultra Balls. Rose, Leon (except when in the Battle Tower), Mustard, Honey, Peony, and Peonia are also known to use Ultra Balls for their Pokémon. Certain Gym Leaders* (with the exceptions of Nessa, Allister, and Piers) also utilize Ultra Balls for their teams. Trainers identified as "strong," indicated by a black background on their overworld text bubble, likewise store their Pokémon in Ultra Balls. Vinnie and Ivor are also users of Ultra Balls.

A visual representation of an Ultra Ball is present at the Poké Ball Boutique located in Lumiose City. In Pokémon Stadium 2's mini-game "Furret's Frolic," Ultra Balls appear, each contributing three points.

In Pokémon GO, Ultra Balls are employed during wild Pokémon encounters to facilitate capture attempts, providing a 2× catch rate modifier. Players receive Ultra Balls as rewards for achieving any level from 20 upwards. Additional acquisition methods include finishing specific Special Research tasks and spinning the Photo Discs of PokéStops or Gyms (for players at Level 20 or higher). On occasion, Ultra Balls are featured within limited-time box offerings in the game's Shop. Across Pokémon Pinball and Pokémon Pinball: Ruby & Sapphire, the Ultra Ball serves to triple bonus points, applying a ×3 multiplier. In Pokémon Frienda, after a set number of moves have been used and at least one opposing Pokémon has fainted, players can insert ¥100 for a chance to catch all of the opposing Pokémon. A spinning wheel then determines which Poké Balls are utilized for the Pokémon, and the Ultra Ball is among the choices presented.

The Ultra Ball made an appearance in "Which One ~ Is It?", a Japanese ending theme for Pokémon the Series: Diamond and Pearl. Furthermore, numerous Ultra Balls were observed within the Poké Ball Factory in the episode "A Frenzied Factory Fiasco!". "A Glaring Rivalry!" showed Gladion entrusting his Lycanroc to an Ultra Ball. "Let Sleeping Pokémon Lie!" featured two Ultra Balls in Team Rocket's boss fantasy. In "Acting True to Form!", James's Poké Ball collection included an Ultra Ball. Leon utilized an Ultra Ball to try and catch Eternatus in "Sword and Shield... The Legends Awaken!", though the attempt failed. Quillon captured a Registeel with an Ultra Ball in "Chasing to the Finish!", and was implied to have used the same method for Regice and Regirock. Amethio displayed an Ultra Ball in "Roar of the Black Rayquaza" to signify his goal of capturing the Black Rayquaza. Chalce's attempt to net the Black Rayquaza with an Ultra Ball in "The Plan to Capture Rayquaza" was thwarted by Roy's Wattrel. "File 3: Giovanni" depicted Giovanni containing his most formidable Pokémon, Rhyhorn and Rhydon, within Ultra Balls. In "File 4: Charizard," Red successfully captured the Legendary Pokémon Articuno, Zapdos, Moltres, and Mewtwo using Ultra Balls. A Trainer in "The Scoop" employed an Ultra Ball to capture a Deoxys in space. Leon was observed with an Ultra Ball towards the conclusion of "Letter." "Training" showcased three Ultra Balls housing Bea's Pokémon. Mustard's Mienshao was housed in an Ultra Ball in "The Gathering of Stars." "The Champion" revealed Leon's Aegislash and Charizard to be kept in Ultra Balls. Within "The Wish," Zinnia's Salamence was seen in an Ultra Ball, and in that same installment, May utilized an Ultra Ball to capture Rayquaza. Elaine's capture of Mewtwo with an Ultra Ball was unveiled in "The Discovery," where two other Ultra Balls subsequently appeared—one belonging to Green and another held by an Ace Trainer. In "Haunting My Dreams," Ash attempted to capture a giant Haunter known as Black Fog using an Ultra Ball, but the Ghost Pokémon used Self-Destruct just before the ball could hit it, choosing to kill itself rather than be caught by a human.

Within the Pokémon Adventures manga, members of the Elite Four are depicted as keeping their Pokémon in Ultra Balls. Team Rocket was shown to have captured the legendary birds using Ultra Balls in "Holy Moltres." "Crunch!! Snowy Battle" revealed Gordie and Melony to house their Pokémon in Ultra Balls. Peony's Copperajah was seen in an Ultra Ball in "Boom!! Reunion in a New Land." Finally, "Rrmbll!! Serious Tower Battle" disclosed Mustard's Urshifu to be kept in an Ultra Ball.

In the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Ultra Ball card, first appearing in Dark Explorers with artwork by 5ban Graphics, allows a player to discard two cards from their hand to search their deck for any Pokémon. Prior to its release as a dedicated card, an Ultra Ball was visible in the artwork of Rocket's Sneak Attack from the Team Rocket expansion.

A stylized "H" is integrated into the Ultra Ball's design, a reference to its Japanese designation, Hyper Ball. Curiously, its English name corresponds to the Japanese name for the Beast Ball. The term "Ultra" within the Beast Ball's Japanese name denotes "Ultra Beasts." It is probable that the Beast Ball's English localization was altered to avoid redundancy with the Ultra Ball, opting instead for "Beast" to specifically indicate Ultra Beasts.

The Ultra Ball seemingly underwent a brief design alteration during the early stages of Generation III. Its in-game appearance in Ruby and Sapphire, Pokémon Pinball: Ruby & Sapphire, and the Japanese versions of FireRed and LeafGreen depicted a solitary, thick yellow stripe centrally positioned on the upper half of the Ball. Conversely, all other titles within that generation reverted to the typical large, uppercase "H" shape for the yellow pattern.

Title: Great Ball

This entry focuses on the contemporary version of the Great Ball. For information regarding the Great Ball originating from Legends: Arceus, please consult Great Ball (Hisui). Known in Japanese as スーパーボール (Super Ball), the Great Ball is a variety of Poké Ball first appearing in Generation I. It functions as an enhanced iteration of the standard Poké Ball, employed for capturing wild Pokémon. During the Goldenrod Department Store rooftop sale in Pokémon Crystal, a Great Ball can be acquired for $500.

During a wild Pokémon encounter, deploying a Great Ball from the Bag will initiate a capture attempt, applying a 1.5× catch rate multiplier. However, when targeting an Ultra Beast, this modifier is invariably fixed at 410/4096× (approximately 0.1×). Its deployment is restricted in scenarios where any Poké Ball is unusable, including wild battles featuring multiple active opponents or against a trial Pokémon. Should it be employed in a Trainer battle (unless utilized as a Snag Ball against a Shadow Pokémon), the opposing Trainer will repel it, resulting in the loss of the ball itself (in Generation III and prior) or merely the player's turn (from Generation IV onwards). Attempting to use it on the spectral Marowak will cause the Pokémon to evade it, thereby wasting the item. Certain Trainer categories house their Pokémon within Great Balls, such as Aether Foundation Employees (excluding those wearing masks).

Within Pokémon Stadium 2, Great Balls are featured in the mini-game Furret's Frolic, where each is valued at two points. In Pokémon Shuffle, upon defeating a currently uncaptured Pokémon that is not a Trainer's Mega Evolved Pokémon, players automatically receive an opportunity to catch it with a standard Poké Ball. Should the Pokémon elude this initial Poké Ball, the player is offered the option to buy a Great Ball. These Great Balls are priced at 2500 Coins in the 3DS iteration and 3500 Coins in Pokémon Shuffle Mobile, and are instantly deployed against the Pokémon. Should a Great Ball prove unsuccessful, another can be acquired, provided the player has not already expended four Great Balls on the current capture attempt. If the fourth Great Ball fails, or if the player opts not to acquire a Great Ball at any point, the Pokémon escapes, bringing the stage to an end. Furthermore, the Pokémon holds a chance to flee each time it breaks free from a Great Ball. Employing a Great Ball effectively doubles the Pokémon's existing catchability, thereby enhancing the likelihood of a successful capture. This effect does not reapply with subsequent Great Ball purchases. Infrequently, Super Catch Power may trigger following the catchability doubling but prior to the Great Ball purchase prompt. Super Catch Power elevates the player's chances of capturing the Pokémon to at least 50%. This advantage can exclusively be utilized by acquiring a Great Ball, as the opportunity to throw a standard Poké Ball has already passed. Catchability culminates at 100%, presented as "MAX." Consequently, if the catchability is already at 51% or greater, the Great Ball will not completely double the catchability.

In Pokémon GO, Great Balls may be deployed during encounters with wild Pokémon to try and secure a capture, applying a 1.5× catch rate modification. Players receive Great Balls as rewards upon reaching any level between 12 and 19. They can also be obtained by spinning the Photo Disc at PokéStops or Gyms (for players at Level 12 or above).

In both Pokémon Pinball and Pokémon Pinball: Ruby & Sapphire, the Great Ball applies a ×2 multiplier to bonus points. Within Pokémon Frienda, once a predetermined number of moves have been executed and at least one opponent Pokémon has fainted, players have the option to insert ¥100 for an opportunity to capture all remaining opposing Pokémon. A rotating wheel then dictates which Poké Balls are deployed against the Pokémon, with a Great Ball being one such possibility.

A Great Ball made an appearance in Which One ~ Is It?, a Japanese closing theme from Pokémon the Series: Diamond and Pearl. Several Great Balls were observed at the Poké Ball Factory in A Frenzied Factory Fiasco! A duo of Great Balls featured in Team Rocket's boss fantasy during Let Sleeping Pokémon Lie! In Acting True to Form!, James's Poké Ball assortment included a Great Ball. Bluto was depicted storing his Seismitoad in a Great Ball in Mad About Blue!

Red successfully captured an Arbok using a Great Ball in File 4: Charizard. In the Pokémon Adventures manga, Gym Leaders residing outside the Galar region utilize Great Balls, which are supplied by the Pokémon Association, to house their Pokémon. A Team Rocket Grunt was depicted with Great Balls fastened to his belt in A Glimpse of the Glow. Platinum's Froslass, Cherrim, and Pachirisu are all contained within Great Balls, a consequence of their prior ownership by Gym Leaders. Froslass debuted in To and Fro with Froslass, while Cherrim and Pachirisu first appeared in Double Trouble with Dialga and Palkia III. Lance's Dragonite was entrusted to a Great Ball by Bruno in One Tough Togepi after becoming separated from Lance and running wild. Gold subsequently retained possession of it until its eventual return to Lance in All About Arceus II.

Main article: Great Ball (EX FireRed & LeafGreen 92)

In the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Great Ball debuted as a Trainer card during the English EX Series (corresponding to the Japanese PCG Era). Its initial release occurred in the Japanese Flight of Legends expansion and the English EX FireRed & LeafGreen expansion, featuring artwork by Nakaoka. Originally, its effect permitted players to scour their deck for any Basic Pokémon (excluding Pokémon-ex), place it onto their Bench, and then shuffle their deck. During the English Black & White Series (the Japanese BW Era), the card's classification changed to an Item card, and errata updated its effect. Currently, it enables the player to examine the top seven cards of their deck, select one Pokémon card to add to their hand, and then return and shuffle the remaining cards into their deck. The Great Ball has seen numerous reprints subsequently.

Team Magma's Great Ball and Team Aqua's Great Ball represent distinct Team Magma and Team Aqua themed iterations of the Great Ball. Both are Item cards, released in the English Double Crisis expansion and the Japanese Magma Gang VS Aqua Gang: Double Crisis expansion. Team Magma's Great Ball allows players to locate a Basic Team Magma Pokémon and a Basic Energy from their deck and add them to their hand. Similarly, Team Aqua's Great Ball enables players to find a Basic Team Aqua Pokémon and a Basic Energy from their deck and place them into their hand. Following the use of either card, the deck must be shuffled.

Exclusively in Generation I, the Great Ball boasts a superior catch rate compared to the Ultra Ball under specific circumstances.

Title: Safari Ball

Introduced in Generation I, the Safari Ball (Japanese: サファリボール Safari Ball) is a specialized Poké Ball designed for capturing wild Pokémon within a Safari Game. When initially deployed in a wild encounter, it applied a 2× catch rate modifier. Historically, players could only possess these Poké Balls for the duration of a Safari Game. The catch rate modifier for the Safari Ball was subsequently adjusted to 1.5×. In Pokémon Sword and Shield, however, its catch rate modifier was 1×, though this was reverted to 1.5× in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. Presently, the Safari Ball's catch rate modifier is again 1×. With the release of The Isle of Armor Expansion for Pokémon Sword and Shield, the Safari Ball became obtainable beyond the confines of a Safari Game and gained utility in standard wild encounters. Its deployment is restricted in contexts where standard Poké Balls are also unusable, such as wild encounters featuring two or more adversaries simultaneously. Should a player attempt to use it in a Trainer battle, the opposing Trainer will deflect the ball, thus costing the player their turn.

Participants in a Safari Game are supplied with 30 Safari Balls, meant exclusively for the current session. These items are not placed in the player's Bag, and any unused ones are retrieved at the game's culmination. Exclusively in Pokémon Yellow, players lacking the standard $500 entry fee for the Safari Zone could instead tender all their available funds to receive a quantity of Safari Balls proportionate to the sum paid. If entirely without money, entry was free, granting a solitary Safari Ball.

Despite not being an obtainable item in Generation VI, a Safari Ball is visibly displayed in Lumiose City's Poké Ball Boutique. Moreover, the sign for Kiloude City's Friend Safari building showcases a Safari Ball. However, the Friend Safari does not function as a typical Safari Zone, and Safari Balls cannot be legitimately acquired or utilized within it.

In Pokémon GO, the mobile adaptation, this item is designated as the GO Safari Ball: a special, event-exclusive Poké Ball boasting a superior catch rate over other types. A "GO" emblem on its top surface marks its only visual divergence from standard Safari Balls. The GO Safari Ball is distributed within Pokémon GO during specified GO Wild Area events. When a Wild Area event is active, players can obtain GO Safari Balls by spinning Photo Discs at PokéStops and Gyms. Furthermore, players possessing an event ticket can receive GO Safari Balls via hourly Timed Research. For the 2025 GO Wild Area event specifically, Supply Balloons delivering GO Safari Balls will materialize hourly for event ticket holders. Any unused GO Safari Balls will expire and disappear from the player's Bag at the close of each event day. Currently, Pokémon caught using GO Safari Balls cannot be transferred to Pokémon HOME via the GO Transporter; this restriction, however, is set to be lifted in the future, at which point transferred Pokémon will somehow display the GO Safari Ball differently. Conversely, Pokémon captured with GO Safari Balls can be moved to Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee! via GO Park. As with other Pokémon transferred there, the GO Safari Ball is superseded by the Poké Ball used to catch the Pokémon once inside GO Park. GO Safari Balls are unusable during Scatterbug or Shadow Pokémon encounters (except for Shadow Raids, provided the player holds an event ticket). Additionally, they cannot be deployed in Legendary or Mythical encounters stemming from research tasks.

Episode EP035 of the animated series saw Kaiser provide Ash and his friends with 30 Safari Balls for their visit to the Kanto Safari Zone. Ash attempted to catch various rare Pokémon, but he ultimately used all of his Safari Balls solely on Tauros. The Safari Balls containing Ash's Tauros reappeared in Showdown at the Po-Ké Corral; however, for an unexplained reason, whenever Ash has subsequently used one of his Tauros in a battle, it has been sent out from a standard Poké Ball. A Safari Ball featured in "Which One ~ Is It?", a Japanese ending theme from Pokémon the Series: Diamond and Pearl, concurrently marking the animated series' debut of the Ball's in-game design. In "A Frenzied Factory Fiasco!", while Safari Balls themselves were absent from the Poké Ball Factory, Meowth was briefly painted with the Safari Ball's camouflage pattern. This revealed their manufacturing at the factory and also marked the first time the games' design of Safari Balls appeared in the animated series outside of openings and endings.

In File 4: Charizard, Red used a Safari Ball to catch a Chansey. Separately, in PT14, multiple Trainers tried but failed to catch the troublesome Chansey with Safari Balls. Safari Balls first appeared in "Long Live the Nidoking!" and "A Hollow Victreebel," where Red received them from the robotic Safari Zone guide, Pidgebot, eventually catching a large herd of Safari Zone Pokémon with them. During "A Skuffle with Skorupi," Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum entered the Great Marsh and were given Safari Balls for their Safari Game, aiming to catch a Grass-type Pokémon for Platinum to use against Crasher Wake. Ultimately, they failed to catch any new Pokémon. In "Weavile Wobbles But It Won't Fall Down," Silver received Safari Balls upon entering the Johto Safari Zone. He tried to use one on an Arbok, but Eusine stopped him for violating the Safari Zone's no-battling rule by having his Weavile attack the Arbok. Satoshi received Safari Balls when he entered the Safari Zone in Fuchsia City.

The Safari Ball is distinctive as the only Poké Ball type introduced in Generation I that is not present in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. In the Generation I games, the Safari Ball functioned identically to the Ultra Ball. Due to a programming oversight, this made Pokémon with high catch rates, including many found in the Safari Zone, significantly harder to catch at full health.

Title: Net Ball

Debuting in Generation III, the Net Ball (Japanese: ネットボール Net Ball) is a specialized variant of Poké Ball. It serves to capture wild Pokémon, proving particularly effective against those of the Water or Bug types. Its development originated in Mossdeep City.

When activated from the Bag during a wild Pokémon encounter, it attempts to secure the target. An elevated catch rate modifier applies if the Pokémon is either Water- or Bug-type; however, no cumulative bonus is granted if the Pokémon possesses both types. The Net Ball evaluates the Pokémon's *current* typing for its effect, even if that typing has been altered (e.g., through a move like Soak).

Specifically, for Water- or Bug-type Pokémon, the Net Ball provides a 3× catch rate modifier; against other types, it uses a 1× modifier. In another context, it bestows a 3.5× catch rate modifier against Water- or Bug-type Pokémon, while maintaining a 1× modifier otherwise. However, when an Ultra Beast is the target, the catch rate modifier is consistently set to 410/4096× (approximately 0.1×).

The Net Ball has been featured across various Pokémon media. It made an appearance in "Which One ~ Is It?", a Japanese ending theme for Pokémon the Series: Diamond and Pearl. In "A Frenzied Factory Fiasco!", numerous Net Balls were visible at the Poké Ball Factory, and Pikachu was notably briefly adorned with a Net Ball pattern.

Within the manga, Mr. Briney utilized Net Balls to secure a trio of Wailmer he had reeled in during "Stick This in Your Craw, Crawdaunt! I". A Net Ball was also seen in "A Sketchy Smattering of Smeargle" as Emerald contemplated which Poké Ball to use for Jirachi. More of Emerald's Net Balls were showcased in "Sneaky Like Shedinja II", "The Final Battle III", and "The Final Battle V".

In the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Net Ball first appeared in the English Lost Thunder set and the Japanese Fairy Rise set. This Trainer card enables the player to search their deck for a Basic Pokémon or an Energy card and then add it to their hand.

In-game dialogue from Pokémon Ruby, Sapphire, and Emerald features a Sailor in the Mossdeep City Poké Mart stating that Dive Balls and Net Balls are exclusively manufactured in Mossdeep. Conversely, in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, the same Sailor mentions that these particular Poké Balls were once Mossdeep's signature products.

Title: Dive Ball

The Dive Ball (Japanese: ダイブボール Dive Ball) is a specific kind of Poké Ball that first appeared in Generation III. Its primary function is to capture wild Pokémon, exhibiting a higher success rate during encounters in aquatic environments, which initially referred specifically to underwater encounters. Its creation took place in Mossdeep City. When deployed from the player's Bag during a wild Pokémon confrontation, it initiates an attempt to secure the wild Pokémon. A boosted catch rate modifier is associated with it under particular circumstances. This item is unusable in scenarios where Poké Balls are generally restricted, for instance, during wild battles involving multiple opposing Pokémon simultaneously, or when facing a trial Pokémon. Should it be deployed in a Trainer battle (with the exception of its use as a Snag Ball against a Shadow Pokémon), the rival Trainer will deflect it, thereby consuming the item without effect. When aimed at the ghost Marowak, the Pokémon will evade the ball, resulting in its waste.

An underwater application of the Dive Ball yields a 3.5× catch rate modifier; otherwise, its modifier remains 1×. Given the absence of underwater regions in Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen, its modifier will consistently be 1× in these titles. In Pokémon Colosseum and Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, Phenac Stadium counts as an underwater locale, thus the Dive Ball applies a 3.5× catch rate modifier when used there. Activating the Dive Ball while surfing, fishing, or submerged underwater grants a 3.5× catch rate modifier; otherwise, a 1× modifier is applied. When employed against an Ultra Beast, the catch rate modifier is universally adjusted to 410/4096× (~0.1×). Should the target Pokémon be present on or within water, the Dive Ball benefits from a 3.5× catch rate modifier; otherwise, it operates with a 1× modifier.

This amplified catch rate exclusively applies to Pokémon that natively swim in water, or to those typically terrestrial Pokémon that have entered a body of water. Species that perpetually fly or hover are not eligible for this bonus, with the exception of cases like Tropius, where a Pokémon that usually traverses land exhibits a "flying over water" animation rather than "swimming in water." Furthermore, the aquatic area adjacent to the Firescourge Shrine is erroneously not categorized as "water" due to a software bug, meaning no Pokémon, even those otherwise suitable, will gain an enhanced catch rate from Dive Balls in that location.

Pokémon belonging to Trainers of the Swimmer and Surfer classes are typically housed in Dive Balls. A Dive Ball was featured in "Which One ~ Is It?", a Japanese ending theme sequence for Pokémon the Series: Diamond and Pearl. In the episode "A Frenzied Factory Fiasco!", several Dive Balls were observable within the Poké Ball Factory. "We Know Where You're Going, Eevee!" depicted Lana capturing her Eevee, named Sandy, with a Dive Ball. During "Splash, Dash, and Smash for the Crown!", a Dive Ball was visible within James's assortment of Poké Balls. "Mad About Blue!" showcased Gorman storing his Nidorina and Phanpy in Dive Balls. Ludlow consistently uses Dive Balls for his Pokémon, a practice first observed in "The Future I Choose" with his Quagsire, and again in "Transform! Hero of the Seas, Palafin" with his Sharpedo. The Dive Ball made its debut appearance in "A Sketchy Smattering of Smeargle," as Emerald pondered which of his Poké Balls would be suitable for Jirachi's capture. Additional Dive Balls belonging to Emerald were subsequently featured in "Sneaky Like Shedinja II," "The Final Battle III," and "The Final Battle V." "Toasty!! Battle Against Toxapex" revealed Nessa's Drednaw to be contained within a Dive Ball.

Within the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Dive Ball debuted as an Item card during the English XY Series, corresponding to the Japanese XY Era. Its initial English release occurred in the Primal Clash expansion, featuring illustrations by Toyste Beach. In Japan, the Tidal Storm expansion marked its first appearance. This card enables the player to locate a Water-type Pokémon from their deck and add it to their hand. A Sailor character located in the Mossdeep City Poké Mart in Pokémon Ruby, Sapphire, and Emerald states that Dive Balls and Net Balls are exclusively manufactured in Mossdeep. However, in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, the same Sailor character conveys that these items were formerly Mossdeep's specialized products. Both Pokémon Colosseum and Pokémon XD treat Phenac Stadium as an underwater environment, thus conferring an elevated catch rate to the Dive Ball when utilized within its bounds. As Dive Balls are not typically obtainable in these titles, and Pokémon trading becomes accessible only after the main story, players are unable to acquire Dive Balls before reaching the post-game. The sole snaggable Shadow Pokémon that manifest in Phenac Stadium during the post-game reside in the Card e Room, a feature exclusive to the Japanese version of Colosseum and requiring the scan of a specific e-Reader card for access; consequently, these represent the only Pokémon across both games for which the Dive Ball's augmented catch rate can be effectively applied.

Title: Nest Ball

The Nest Ball (Japanese: ネストボール Nest Ball), a variety of Poké Ball, made its debut in Generation III. This item is utilized for capturing wild Pokémon, demonstrating greater efficacy against those of lower levels. When deployed from the Bag during an encounter with a wild Pokémon, it attempts to secure its capture.

When directed at a low-level Pokémon, the Nest Ball's catch rate modifier receives a boost, calculated specifically by generation:
*   In Generations III through IV, the modifier is 40−Level10×, with a minimum value of 1×.
*   During Generation V, the formula is ⌊(41−Level)×409610⌋4096×, also with a minimum of 1×.
*   From Generation VI onward, the calculation is ⌊(41−Level)×409610⌋4096× if the Pokémon's level falls between 1 and 29; otherwise, it is 1×.
*   Within Pokémon Legends: Z-A, the modifier is 41−Level10× if the Pokémon's level is between 1 and 29, and 1× in other instances.
However, should the Nest Ball be deployed against an Ultra Beast, its catch rate modifier is invariably fixed at 410/4096× (equivalent to 0.1×).

The Nest Ball cannot be deployed in scenarios where any Poké Ball is unusable, such as during wild battles involving multiple opposing Pokémon or against a trial Pokémon. In Trainer battles (excluding its use as a Snag Ball on a Shadow Pokémon), the opposing Trainer will block its deployment; this action resulted in the item being wasted prior to Generation IV, but since Generation IV, it only consumes the player's turn. Furthermore, if used against the spectral Marowak, the Pokémon will evade the ball, rendering it useless.

Both Team Skull Grunts and Team Yell Grunts utilize Nest Balls to house their Pokémon.
In the anime, a Nest Ball was depicted in "Which One ~ Is It?", a Japanese ending theme from Pokémon the Series: Diamond and Pearl. "A Frenzied Factory Fiasco!" showcased numerous Nest Balls at the Poké Ball Factory. In "Splash, Dash, and Smash for the Crown!", a Nest Ball was visible as part of James's collection of Poké Balls. "Bidoof's Big Stand" featured a Trainer using a Nest Ball to catch the episode's namesake Bidoof.
Within the manga, a Nest Ball first appeared in "A Sketchy Smattering of Smeargle" when Emerald pondered which Poké Ball to use for Jirachi. Additional Nest Balls owned by Emerald were later seen in "Sneaky Like Shedinja II," "The Final Battle III," and "The Final Battle V."

The Nest Ball made its introduction as an Item card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game during the English Sun & Moon Series (which corresponds to the Japanese Sun & Moon Era). Its initial release was in the Japanese Collection Moon expansion and the English Sun & Moon expansion, both featuring artwork by Toyste Beach. A Secret print of the card, with artwork by the same artist, was also included in these sets. It saw a reprint in the Japanese GG End subset, this time with artwork by Ryo Ueda. Subsequently, during the English Scarlet & Violet Series (the Japanese Scarlet & Violet Era), it was reprinted once more in both the Japanese Scarlet ex expansion and the English Scarlet & Violet expansion. This reprint included both a Regular card and a hyper rare variant, each showcasing artwork by the original illustrator, Toyste Beach. In gameplay, the card enables the player to search their deck for any Basic Pokémon and place it directly onto their Bench, after which the player must shuffle their deck.

Title: Repeat Ball

Originating in Generation III, the Repeat Ball (known in Japan as リピートボール Repeat Ball) is a specific variant of Poké Ball. Its primary function is to capture wild Pokémon, with a heightened chance of success if the target species has previously been recorded in the player's Pokédex. The Devon Corporation is credited with its development. Employed during a wild encounter directly from the Bag, it initiates an attempt to ensnare the encountered Pokémon. Should the target Pokémon already be listed as owned within the player's Pokédex, this item applies an elevated catch rate modifier.

This particular Poké Ball cannot be deployed in scenarios where any Poké Ball is restricted, for instance, during wild battles involving multiple opposing Pokémon or when facing a trial Pokémon. Attempting to use it in a Trainer battle will result in the opponent deflecting it, rendering the item useless (an exception being its use as a Snag Ball against a Shadow Pokémon). Furthermore, if directed at the ghost Marowak, the Pokémon will evade it, thus squandering the Repeat Ball. When aimed at a Pokémon already documented as owned in the player's Pokédex, it provides a 3× catch rate modifier; otherwise, the modifier remains 1×. In another specific game mechanic, if the Pokémon is already marked as owned in the player's Pokédex, the Repeat Ball applies a 3.5× catch rate modifier; otherwise, it maintains a 1× modifier. However, when targeting an Ultra Beast, the catch rate modifier is invariably fixed at 410/4096× (approximately 0.1×).

Noteworthy users include various Scientists and masked Aether Foundation Employees, who house their Pokémon within these particular Balls. Specifically, in Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, Iwao is observed to utilize Repeat Balls for his Pokémon. The film Jirachi: Wish Maker depicted Brendan's Shiftry as being held within a Repeat Ball. This item also made an appearance in "Which One ~ Is It?", a Japanese ending theme featured in Pokémon the Series: Diamond and Pearl. Furthermore, "A Frenzied Factory Fiasco!" showcased numerous Repeat Balls at the Poké Ball Factory. Its initial appearance in the manga occurred in "A Sketchy Smattering of Smeargle," where Emerald pondered which of his Poké Balls would be best suited for capturing Jirachi. Additional Repeat Balls belonging to Emerald were subsequently featured in "Sneaky Like Shedinja II," "The Final Battle III," and "The Final Battle V." In the Pokémon Trading Card Game, specifically from the Primal Clash expansion, the Repeat Ball enables a player to scour their deck for a Pokémon bearing an identical name to one of their Pokémon already active in play, then retrieve that card into their hand.

Title: Timer Ball

This device, known as the Timer Ball (Japanese: タイマーボール Timer Ball), made its debut as a type of Poké Ball in Generation III. Its primary function is to capture wild Pokémon, with its efficacy increasing proportionally to the duration of the encounter. Its creation is attributed to the Devon Corporation. Activating it from the Bag during a wild encounter initiates an attempt to capture the target Pokémon. A unique catch rate modifier applies, which scales up with the total number of turns elapsed in the battle (the turn counter begins at 0), determined by the following formula:

Notably, within *Pokémon Legends: Z-A*, the turn count is reinterpreted as the cumulative sum of all moves executed by both the wild Pokémon and the player's current combatant from the battle's outset. In this title, bonus increments occur every 10 moves: For fewer than 11 moves, the modifier remains 1×. Between 11 and 20 moves, it becomes 2×. From 21 to 30 moves, the multiplier is 3×. Once 30 or more moves have accumulated, a 4× multiplier is applied.

However, when an Ultra Beast is the target, its catch rate modifier is invariably fixed at 410/4096× (approximately 0.1×), irrespective of turns.

This item is unusable in scenarios where standard Poké Balls are restricted, including wild encounters involving multiple opposing Pokémon or against a trial Pokémon. Attempting to deploy it in a Trainer battle (unless functioning as a Snag Ball for a Shadow Pokémon) results in the opposing Trainer deflecting it, which consumed the item itself in Generations prior to IV, but merely wastes the player's turn from Generation IV onwards. Furthermore, if aimed at the ghost Marowak, the spectral entity will evade the capture attempt, rendering the ball ineffective.

The Timer Ball made a visual appearance in "Which One ~ Is It?", a Japanese ending theme for *Pokémon the Series: Diamond and Pearl*. Its initial depiction in the *Pokémon Adventures* manga was in "A Sketchy Smattering of Smeargle" and "Skirting Around Surskit I", where Emerald made an attempt to capture Jirachi using one. Nevertheless, Guile Hideout, also intent on capturing Jirachi, intercepted the Ball with his sword, severing it prior to impact. Additional Timer Balls belonging to Emerald were subsequently featured in "Sneaky Like Shedinja II", "The Final Battle III", and "The Final Battle V".

Within the *Pokémon Trading Card Game*, the Timer Ball was unveiled as an Item card during the English Sun & Moon Series (corresponding to the Japanese Sun & Moon Era). Its inaugural release occurred in the Japanese *Collection Sun* expansion and the English *Sun & Moon* expansion, featuring illustrations by Toyste Beach. Upon activation, this card permits the player to flip two coins; for each "heads" result, the player may seek out an Evolution Pokémon from their deck, show it to the opponent, and then place it into their hand.

Excluding the Master Ball, Timer Balls are capable of delivering some of the most favorable catch rates among all Poké Ball varieties. Across Generations III and IV, the Timer Ball surpasses the Ultra Ball in catch rate once 11 turns have elapsed, and outperforms the Dusk Ball after 26 turns. In Generations V and VI, this advantage manifests sooner: outperforming Ultra Balls after 4 turns, and Dusk Balls after 9 turns. And from Generation VII onwards, they prove superior to Ultra Balls following 4 turns, and to Dusk Balls after 7 turns. Uniquely, among all Poké Balls introduced in the initial three generations, the Timer Ball stands as the sole one that has not featured in the animated series proper, its appearances being confined to opening and ending sequences.

Title: Luxury Ball

Known in Japanese as ゴージャスボール (Gorgeous Ball), the Luxury Ball is a specific kind of Poké Ball that made its debut in Generation III. Its primary function is to capture untamed Pokémon encountered in the wild. Any Pokémon housed within a Luxury Ball will develop friendship at an accelerated pace. This benefit extends even to Pokémon that are bred and consequently inherit this particular Poké Ball from a parent.

Within the game Pokémon Black, players have the option to acquire Luxury Balls from Black City, with a price tag of $50000. Furthermore, in Pokémon Black 2 and White 2, during the specific Funfest Mission "Enjoy Shopping!", these Balls become available for purchase at a significantly reduced cost of $400.

Deploying it from the Bag during an encounter with a wild Pokémon initiates an attempt to ensnare the creature, applying a standard catch rate multiplier of 1×. However, should it be directed at an Ultra Beast, the catch rate multiplier invariably shifts to 410/4096× (approximately 0.1×).

This item is rendered unusable in circumstances where Poké Balls generally cannot be deployed, for instance, during wild battles involving multiple opposing Pokémon simultaneously, or when facing a designated trial Pokémon. Should a player attempt to use it in a Trainer battle (unless acting as a Snag Ball against a Shadow Pokémon), the opposing Trainer will successfully repel it, resulting in the player's turn being forfeited (and the item itself being lost in generations preceding Generation IV). When aimed at the spectral Marowak, the creature will evade the Ball, leading to its waste.

Any increment to a Pokémon's friendship value, when that Pokémon is held in a Luxury Ball, receives an additional boost of 1 point. Certain Trainer classes, specifically Gentlemen, Madames, and those described as sharing their models, are known to house their Pokémon within Luxury Balls. Both Sordward and Shielbert are notable for containing their Pokémon in Luxury Balls. Furthermore, the Toxel received by the player as a gift on Route 5 is specifically found inside a Luxury Ball. The Trainer classes Ortega and O'Nare also keep their Pokémon in Luxury Balls.

In the film "Jirachi: Wish Maker," Brendan's Aggron was depicted as being housed in a Luxury Ball. One of these Balls made an appearance in "Which One ~ Is It?", an ending theme for the Japanese run of Pokémon the Series: Diamond and Pearl. During the episode "A Frenzied Factory Fiasco!", numerous Luxury Balls were observed within the Poké Ball Factory. As Pikachu was being pursued by Meowth through the facility, Pikachu struck Meowth with a Luxury Ball, momentarily pulling him inside and allowing Pikachu to create some space. Upon Meowth's escape from the Ball, he remarked on its surprisingly comfortable interior. "First Catch in Alola, Ketchum-Style!" showcased a Luxury Ball among James's personal Poké Ball collection. After Jessie's efforts to capture a Mimikyu using standard Poké Balls proved unsuccessful, she seized the Luxury Ball James had been meticulously polishing and utilized it, successfully catching Mimikyu, much to James's visible disappointment. In "A Young Royal Flame Ignites!", it was disclosed that Viren kept his Pangoro within a Luxury Ball. The Luxury Ball also received a mention in one of Nidothing's videos, featured in "Found You, Fuecoco!".

Its initial appearance in the Pokémon manga was in "A Sketchy Smattering of Smeargle," where Emerald pondered which of his Poké Balls would be best suited for catching Jirachi. Later, in "Skirting Around Surskit II," Emerald deployed a Luxury Ball against Jirachi; however, despite Jirachi being drawn inside, Guile Hideout employed his Surskit's Psybeam to prevent the Poké Ball from sealing and subsequently obliterated it. Additional Luxury Balls belonging to Emerald were subsequently observed in "Sneaky Like Shedinja II," "The Final Battle III," and "The Final Battle V." During "Innocent Scientist," Blake employed Luxury Balls in his attempts to capture the Genesect under Colress's control, ultimately achieving success.

The Luxury Ball, initially featured in the Stormfront expansion of the Pokémon Trading Card Game, is recognized as one of the rarer Poké Ball types within the video games, although its efficacy in catching Pokémon (its catch rate) mirrors that of a regular Poké Ball. Similarly, its TCG counterpart allows a non-LV.X Pokémon to be retrieved from the deck, provided that no other Luxury Ball card is currently in the discard pile.

Uniquely among Poké Balls, the Luxury Ball confers a permanent and ongoing effect upon the Pokémon it successfully captures. Before the advent of Generation III, no Poké Ball imposed a lasting influence on the Pokémon caught within it, nor was the specific type of Ball utilized visible in a Pokémon's summary screen. The very existence of the Luxury Ball and its unique benefit became feasible solely because, from Generation III onwards, the index number identifying the Poké Ball used for capture was permanently encoded within a Pokémon's internal data structure.

Title: Premier Ball

Known in Japanese as プレミアボール (Premier Ball), this particular variety of Poké Ball made its debut in Generation III. Its primary function is to capture wild Pokémon. Its creation is credited to the Devon Corporation. When deployed from the player's Bag during a wild encounter, it makes an attempt to secure the wild Pokémon, employing a catch rate multiplier of 1×. However, against an Ultra Beast, this modifier is fixed at 410/4096× (approximately 0.1×). This item is unusable in contexts where Poké Balls are generally restricted, including wild battles featuring multiple active opponents or when facing a trial Pokémon. During a Trainer battle, an opponent will typically bat it away, causing the player to forfeit their turn (and, before Generation IV, the ball itself), unless it's deployed as a Snag Ball against a Shadow Pokémon. When thrown at the spectral Marowak, the Pokémon will evade it, resulting in the loss of the ball. The move Fling will not succeed if the Pokémon attempting it is holding a Premier Ball.

Cyrano utilizes Premier Balls to house his Pokémon. Furthermore, Clavell is depicted with six Premier Balls, though his battle Pokémon are actually contained within standard Poké Balls. In specific bonus challenges, such as Raid Battles, Team GO Rocket battles, and Max Battles, the Premier Ball functions as a unique, exclusive item. These Premier Balls are never deposited into the player's Bag; any that remain unused are discarded once a challenge concludes. Its capture rate is calculated as 1.05N, with N representing the quantity of Premier Balls already expended within the bonus challenge. Beginning with a 1× catch rate modifier (when N = 0), akin to a standard Poké Ball, this multiplier escalates geometrically following each subsequent throw. Whether this escalating catch rate multiplier has a fixed upper limit remains an open question. In any encounter where Premier Balls are deployed, Shiny Pokémon are guaranteed to be captured with a 100% success rate. Moreover, all Shadow Pokémon abandoned by Giovanni are assured captures. When engaging in Raid Battles, the Premier Ball serves to capture the vanquished Raid Boss. The quantity of Premier Balls granted is contingent upon two factors: the highest Friendship level achieved with another participant, and the duration required to complete the raid (excluding Mega and Primal Raids). During Team GO Rocket battles, Premier Balls facilitate the rescue of Shadow Pokémon. The number of Premier Balls awarded is based on:

An instance of a Premier Ball was depicted in "Which One ~ Is It?", a Japanese ending theme featured in Pokémon the Series: Diamond and Pearl. In the episode "A Frenzied Factory Fiasco!", one such ball was observable within the Poké Ball Factory. Gladion was shown to keep his Silvally (at the time a Type: Null) in a Premier Ball during "Rising from the Ruins!". As part of James's collection of Poké Balls, a Premier Ball made an appearance in "Acting True to Form!". This particular Premier Ball belonging to James was seen again in "Splash, Dash, and Smash for the Crown!". During Team Rocket's radio program, "Radio Lulled the Mischievous Stars!", a Premier Ball was presented as a prize and ultimately claimed by Ren. Similar to his portrayal in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Clavell is observed wearing Premier Balls in Pokémon Horizons: The Series, initially noted in "Welcome to Naranja Academy!". The Premier Ball made its initial manga appearance in "A Sketchy Smattering of Smeargle", specifically when Emerald pondered which of his Poké Balls would be best suited for capturing Jirachi. In the Great Encounters expansion of the TCG, the Premier Ball was introduced, enabling a player to locate a Pokémon LV.X from either their deck or discard pile and add it to their hand. Notably, it stands as the sole Poké Ball type not originating from Generation I that players can utilize in Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. Upon undergoing Dynamax, a Poké Ball takes on the appearance of an enormous Premier Ball.

Title: Dusk Ball

For the specific Poké Ball employed by the Iron-Masked Marauder, which shares its Japanese nomenclature with this item, consult the Dark Ball entry. This item, known in Japanese as ダークボール (Dark Ball), is a distinct variety of Poké Ball that first appeared in Generation IV. Its primary function is to capture wild Pokémon, with an elevated chance of success when deployed either during nighttime hours or within cavernous environments. During a wild Pokémon encounter, deploying it from the Bag initiates an attempt to secure the Pokémon. Its catch rate modifier receives a boost when utilized during the evening, at night, or inside a cave. However, its capture efficacy is not enhanced within indoor structures, irrespective of the time of day. This item is subject to the general restrictions on Poké Ball usage; for instance, it is ineffective in wild battles involving multiple concurrent opponents or when facing a trial Pokémon. Should a player attempt to employ it during a Trainer battle, the adversary will intercept it, resulting in a lost turn for the player. When deployed within a cave (excluding Mirage Caves) or under the cover of night, the Dusk Ball confers a 3.5× catch rate multiplier; otherwise, its modifier remains at 1×. Exclusively during Generation V, in specific indoor locations such as the Strange House and Volcarona's chamber within Relic Castle, Dusk Balls retain only a 1× modifier, even during nocturnal periods. Later, if utilized during nighttime or inside a cavern, the Dusk Ball provides a 3× catch rate multiplier; in all other scenarios, it maintains a 1× modifier. However, when targeting an Ultra Beast, the catch rate modifier is invariably fixed at 410/4096× (approximately 0.1×). During the phenomena of the Darkest Day or when at Newmoon Island, this Poké Ball grants a 3× modifier, even if it is daytime. Within Area Zero, the 3× modifier is exclusively active within its various caves and the lower cavern section, irrespective of the prevailing time in other parts of Paldea. Both Punk Guys and Punk Girls are known to house their Pokémon within Dusk Balls. Notable Trainers Marnie, Allister, and Piers also store their Pokémon in this type of Poké Ball. The subsequent Trainer classes are observed to utilize Dusk Balls for their Pokémon:

This item was depicted in "Which One ~ Is It?", a Japanese ending sequence from the Pokémon the Series: Diamond and Pearl anime. Multiple instances of Dusk Balls were visible at the Poké Ball Factory during the episode "A Frenzied Factory Fiasco!". A Dusk Ball was featured as part of James's Poké Ball assortment in "Acting True to Form!", making a subsequent reappearance in "Splash, Dash, and Smash for the Crown!". During the Poké Problem bonus segment of SM127, Gladion was illustrated capturing his father's missing Zoroark with a Dusk Ball. This specific Dusk Ball then debuted in English dub in "Final Rivals!". Saturn utilized a Dusk Ball to capture Heatran in "The Arceus Chronicles (Part 1)". The captured Heatran later rampaged and inadvertently destroyed its own Dusk Ball by melting it in "The Arceus Chronicles (Part 2)". Marnie's Grimmsnarl was confirmed to reside within a Dusk Ball in the episode "Battling Turned Up to Eleven!". Ult was observed carrying a Dusk Ball in "Crash! Team Dragon Rampage!", which was subsequently disclosed in "The Dusk Ball That Wouldn't Open" to hold his Dragonite, a gift from his father. Monta successfully captured Kyurem with a Dusk Ball in "Pokédex Complete!". Allister's practice of keeping his Pokémon in Dusk Balls was also depicted in "Glittering!! The Tapestry in the Vault".

For an in-depth exploration, refer to the primary article: Dusk Ball (Mysterious Treasures 110). Its debut in the Pokémon Trading Card Game occurred during the English Diamond & Pearl Series, coinciding with the Japanese DP Era. It initially appeared as a Trainer card in the "Secret of the Lakes" expansion in Japan and the "Mysterious Treasures" expansion in English-speaking regions, featuring illustrations by Ryo Ueda. The card was subsequently reprinted as an Item card within the Japanese "Paradise Dragona" subset, showcasing fresh artwork from Studio Bora Inc. This card grants the player the ability to examine the bottom seven cards of their deck, select a Pokémon from among them to add to their hand, and then shuffle their deck. Despite their distinct characteristics, the Dusk Ball and the Dark Ball share the same appellation in Japanese.

Title: Heal Ball

Known in Japan as ヒールボール (Heal Ball), this specific variant of Poké Ball first appeared in the fourth generation of games. Its primary function is to capture wild Pokémon. Any Pokémon successfully captured using a Heal Ball experiences a complete restoration of its condition. During a confrontation with a wild Pokémon, deploying it from the Bag applies a standard 1× multiplier to the catch rate. Upon a successful capture, the Pokémon's Hit Points (HP), Power Points (PP), and any status conditions are entirely replenished. Between Generation IV and VII, this inherent healing effect was primarily beneficial if the newly caught Pokémon joined the active party, as creatures transferred to the Pokémon Storage System were already fully restored. However, in titles where the Storage System does not automatically heal Pokémon, the Heal Ball's effect applies universally. When attempting to capture an Ultra Beast, the catch rate modifier deviates significantly, being fixed at 410/4096× (approximately 0.1×).

In the narrative of Episode RR within *Ultra Sun* and *Ultra Moon*, Lillie's Clefairy resides within a Heal Ball. It is also observed that Pokémon Center Ladies entrust their Pokémon to Heal Balls. Specific Trainer classes are also known to use Heal Balls for their Pokémon: A Heal Ball made an appearance in "Which One ~ Is It?", a Japanese ending theme featured in *Pokémon the Series: Diamond and Pearl*. From the episode "A Showcase Debut!" onward, Serena can be seen with a distinctive pink backpack adorned with the Heal Ball's design. Within "A Frenzied Factory Fiasco!", the production of Heal Balls was depicted at the Poké Ball Factory. During "Rescuing the Unwilling!", it was revealed that Gladion's Umbreon resides within a Heal Ball. In "Acting True to Form!", a Heal Ball formed part of James's extensive Poké Ball collection. This same item was observed once more in "Splash, Dash, and Smash for the Crown!".

Title: Quick Ball

The Quick Ball (Japanese: クイックボール Quick Ball) represents a category of Poké Ball that made its debut in Generation IV. Its purpose is to capture wild Pokémon, demonstrating enhanced efficacy when employed at the very beginning of a confrontation. Activating this item from the Bag during a wild encounter initiates an attempt to secure the wild Pokémon. When deployed on the inaugural turn of an encounter, it applies an elevated catch rate modifier.

Should it be deployed during the first turn of an encounter, the Quick Ball benefits from a 4× catch rate modifier; otherwise, it provides a standard 1× modifier. Additionally, when utilized on the initial turn of a battle, it offers a 5× catch rate multiplier, reverting to a 1× multiplier for subsequent turns. The Quick Ball cannot be used in circumstances where Poké Balls are generally restricted, such as in wild battles where two or more opponents are present, or against a trial Pokémon. If a player attempts to use it in a Trainer battle, the opposing Trainer will deflect the Quick Ball, resulting in a wasted turn for the player. However, when targeting an Ultra Beast, the catch rate modifier is fixed at 410/4096× (approximately 0.1×).

In Pokémon Legends: Z-A, the bonus condition for the Quick Ball's enhanced effect is altered: it applies only if neither the wild Pokémon nor the player's active Pokémon has made any moves since the start of the battle. Furthermore, this bonus is reduced to 3× in that title.

Several characters are known to use Quick Balls for their Pokémon. Mela and Crispin house their Pokémon in Quick Balls, as do Canari, Mani, and Griselle.

Within the game Pokémon Frienda, Quick Balls can be launched at up to five distinct patches of grass during the "Get it now!" segment. If the player initially chose the "Get it in battle!" option and subsequently captured any Pokémon after a battle, the quantity of grass patches available decreases; for instance, capturing one Pokémon after a battle would result in four patches being presented. Each throw carries a cost of ¥100, and the specific patch of grass targeted by the Quick Ball is determined randomly.

The Quick Ball has also appeared in various media. It was featured in "Which One ~ Is It?", a Japanese ending theme for Pokémon the Series: Diamond and Pearl. In the episode "Acting True to Form!", a Quick Ball was part of James's personal Poké Ball collection, reappearing later in "Splash, Dash, and Smash for the Crown!". Bluto was depicted keeping his Croagunk in a Quick Ball during "Mad About Blue!". In "Abyssal Ruins," Blake handed Whitley a Quick Ball, instructing her to capture a Karrablast with it; this Karrablast was then immediately traded for Blake's newly caught Shelmet, prompting both Pokémon to evolve. A Quick Ball was observed among the Poké Balls Y employed while attempting to catch an Absol in "Scizor Defends." Arven utilized a Quick Ball to capture a Skwovet, saving it from the Titan Dondozo.

Main article: Quick Ball (Mysterious Treasures 114)
In the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Quick Ball was introduced as an Item card within the Mysterious Treasures expansion (known as Secret of the Lakes in Japan). This card allows the player to reveal cards from their deck sequentially until a Pokémon card is found. Its function bears a resemblance to the Fast Ball. The Sword & Shield expansion (Shield expansion in Japan) issued an erratum that modified the Quick Ball's effect. It now functions as a more specialized variant of the Ultra Ball, necessitating the discard of a card from hand to allow the player to search their deck for a Basic Pokémon and add it to their hand. Consequently, all previous printings of the Quick Ball card are to be interpreted according to the Sword and Shield version's text.

Beginning with Generation V, Pokémon possessing a catch rate of 153 or higher are guaranteed to be captured by a Quick Ball if it is used on the first turn of battle, provided it is not an Ultra Beast or subject to other specific encounter conditions (such as the "difficulty" penalty applied from a higher-leveled Pokémon before obtaining the eighth Badge in Generation VIII). In Generation IV, Pokémon with a catch rate of 192 or greater are assuredly caught, though those with catch rates ranging from 151 to 191 instead boast a 99.994% chance of success.

Due to the nature of overworld encounters in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, it is possible to repeatedly apply the Quick Ball's enhanced catch rate modifier to the same Pokémon by strategically fleeing and then re-initiating the encounter. In contrast to the Quick Ball, the Timer Ball operates with an inverse effect.

Title: Cherish Ball

Hailing from Generation IV, the Cherish Ball (プレシャスボール in Japanese, meaning "Precious Ball") stands as a distinct category of Poké Ball. It frequently serves as the containment method for event-exclusive Pokémon. Starting with Generation IV, these special Pokémon releases typically arrive in Cherish Balls, with the sole exception being when they are given out as Eggs. Acquiring a Pokémon within a Cherish Ball is exclusively possible through this distribution method. The Cherish Ball itself is not an item that can be legitimately acquired by players, thus precluding its regular usage. Should it somehow be obtained and deployed from the Bag during a wild encounter, its function would be to attempt capture of the wild Pokémon, applying a catch rate modifier of 1×. In Generation VI and subsequent games, Pokémon produced through breeding typically inherit the specific Poké Ball of their parent. Nonetheless, any offspring that would otherwise inherit a Cherish Ball, Master Ball, or Strange Ball will invariably find themselves housed in a conventional Poké Ball instead.

Notably, Ace Trainer Deanna possesses a Tropius encased within a Cherish Ball. This serves as an allusion to Shokotan, whose Japanese name is shared by Deanna. Indeed, Shokotan's Tropius holds the distinction of being the inaugural event Pokémon ever distributed inside a Cherish Ball. Within the episode "Victini - A New Friend!", Monta was awarded a Victini, housed in a Cherish Ball, as an event prize.

Turning to the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Cherish Ball, which debuted in the Japanese Espeon & Deoxys-GX TAG TEAM GX Starter Set, grants its user the ability to scour their deck for a Pokémon-GX card, unveil it, and subsequently transfer it to their hand. Following this, the player must then shuffle their deck. A Secret Full Art variant of the card subsequently emerged in the Japanese Miracle Twin expansion; both versions of the print were included in the English Unified Minds expansion, all featuring artwork by 5ban Graphics. An alternative art version, brought to life by Ryo Ueda, made its debut in the Japanese Alter Genesis expansion and was also released as one of the Yellow A Alternate cards in English.

Within Pokémon Platinum, should a player illicitly introduce Cherish Balls and employ one to capture the in-game Regigigas, the game mistakenly registers this Regigigas as an event acquisition, thereby enabling access to the Rock Peak, Iceberg, and Iron Ruins. Paradoxically, despite the impossibility of a hatched Pokémon genuinely originating from a Cherish Ball, six specific Eggs were distributed in Japan and South Korea during 2017 that, upon hatching, yielded Pokémon effectively contained within Cherish Balls. Among all Poké Balls introduced into the core series games before Pokémon Legends: Arceus, the Cherish Ball and Park Ball uniquely remain unobtainable within Pokémon Sword and Shield. Pecharunt stands alone as the sole Mythical Pokémon that has never been offered inside a Cherish Ball. During the "Mochi Mayhem" event, which marked its initial catchable appearance, players are granted the freedom to select any Poké Ball of their preference for its capture, given that Pecharunt's encounter is pre-programmed to ensure a guaranteed capture regardless of the chosen Ball.

Title: Potion

The Potion, known in Japanese as キズぐすり (Wound Medicine), is a medicinal item first introduced in Generation I. Its core purpose is to restore a Pokémon's HP. It has four enhanced equivalents: Super Potion, Hyper Potion, Max Potion, and Full Restore. When deployed from the Bag on a Pokémon, it typically heals 20 HP. In Pokémon Yellow, its application to the player's Pikachu also boosts friendship. Notably, in Pokémon Legends: Arceus, this item restores a Pokémon's HP by 60. As a Wonder Launcher item, the Potion costs 2 points for its use.

Potions make an appearance in Chapter 4 of Detective Pikachu, where an aspiring Pokémon Ranger named Milo Green utilizes them. Milo is first observed using a Potion to mend an injured Ducklett, and later to revive a Feebas. He states that he obtains his Potions from Dr. Waals, who creates them using ingredients collected at Lake Cappucci. In the game Pokémon Conquest, a Potion can be used to heal a Pokémon for 20 HP. This item is available for purchase at any Shop for 300-210 gold and can be sold for 150-195 gold. Outside of battle, Potions can be used to restore a Pokémon's HP by 20. However, they are unusable during combat. Potions become obtainable starting at level 5; players receive them as rewards for leveling up to any level from 5 to 9.

The Potion card is featured in the Pokémon Trading Card Game and Pokémon Trading Card Game 2: The Invasion of Team GR!, while the Potion Energy card is exclusive to the latter game. Furthermore, the Potion card is available in Pokémon Trading Card Game Pocket. In Pokémon Masters EX, Potions can only be employed by specific sync pairs that possess them as an item move. Additionally, a Potion variant named Mini Potion All can be used by certain sync pairs. Both of these item moves share a 2 MP cost and are tagged with the "Heals" effect. A Potion provides more HP restoration than Mini Potion All, but Mini Potion All heals all allies, whereas a Potion affects only a single ally. A full list of all sync pairs who can learn Potion or Mini Potion All can be found here. In Pokémon UNITE, the Potion serves as a battle item, unlocked at Trainer Level 4. It restores a Pokémon's HP by 160 + 20% of its max HP and has a 30s cooldown.

Regular Potions have been occasionally used in Pokémon the Series. Typically, Brock, Cilan, and Clemont carry them in their backpacks, using them to treat wounded Pokémon. Potions appear to sting slightly upon application, suggesting they both heal and sterilize wounds. Tracey has also been seen with regular Potions intended for oral consumption. Beginning with Pokémon the Series: Sun & Moon, the regular Potion became the most frequently used Potion variant in the animated series. In "A Conspiracy to Conquer!", Clemont administered a Potion to Meowth off-screen, with the bottle only briefly visible as he returned it to his backpack. "The Sun, the Scare, the Secret Lair!" showcased multiple Potions within Professor Kukui's medical kit. Professor Kukui used a Potion to heal the Rockruff he was caring for in "Rocking Clawmark Hill!". During "Lulled to La-La Land!", Lillie listed Potions among the items she brought to a camp with her classmates, though the dub mistakenly referred to them as "lotions." A flashback in "Don't Ignore the Small Stufful!" showed Wicke using a Potion on an injured Whimsicott. In "Healing the Healer!", Goh applied a Potion to treat a wound on Suicune's leg. JN136 featured Quillon using a Potion on an injured Beartic, a scene omitted from the episode's English dubbed version. Ash treated Latias's injured wing with a Potion in "The Road Most Traveled!". Brock used a Potion on an injured Hatenna in "Must Be Our Heroes and the Witch!". In "The Same Moon, Now and Forever!", Brock mended an injured Spearow using a Potion. Ash, in "Ash and Latios", employed one of Brock's Potions to tend to a wounded Latios. After a battle loss in "The Pendant That Starts It All (Part One)", an Indigo Academy student used a Potion to heal his Machoke. A flashback in "Farewell, Sprigatito?" depicted Marnya applying a Potion to assist in treating a wounded Sprigatito. Following a confrontation with Lucius's Kleavor in "Kleavor the Solitary Warrior", Liko and Dot administered Potions to heal Floragato and Quaxwell's injuries. In "Better, Farther, Stronger!", Liko and Ult each used a Potion to restore their Charcadet and Sableye, respectively, after battling the Explorers. The Pokémon Masters Animated Trailer depicted Brock healing Rosa's Snivy with a Potion after it sustained injury from a Beauty's Lotad's Energy Ball. "Blossom's Dream" featured Blossom purchasing several Potions from a Poké Mart, one of which she later used on a wild Nidoran♂. During Emerald's Battle Pyramid challenge in "Chipping Away at Regirock", Crystal's Phanpy retrieved a Potion for him. Pearl applied a Potion to heal his Chimchar, Chimler, in "A Conk on Cranidos's Cranium". After a battle against N in "Listening to Pokémon", Black employed several Potions to restore his Pokémon's health. Sun utilized a Potion to heal his Litten, Dollar, following his confrontation with the Masked Royal at the Iki Town festival in "The Announcement and the Prize". In PMDP02, Red attempted to throw a Potion at Clefairy for health recovery, but Weavile thwarted him by using Embargo.

The Potion debuted as a Trainer card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game's English Original Series (Japanese Original Era) within the Base Set expansion, featuring artwork by Keiji Kinebuchi. Subsequently, it reappeared as an Item card in the Black & White expansion, showcasing new artwork by Ayaka Yoshida, initially released in the Japanese Beginning Set. This card has since seen multiple reprints throughout the TCG's history. The Trainer card versions from the Base Set, Expedition Base Set, and EX Unseen Forces permitted players to remove two damage counters from one of their Pokémon. However, from the Black & White print and subsequent releases, the card enables players to heal 30 damage from a chosen Pokémon. Potions have also appeared in the artwork of various other cards. Numerous officially licensed figures and merchandise have also incorporated the Potion.

Regarding Potions that restore a fixed HP amount (Potion, Super Potion, Hyper Potion) in Generation VII, the Potion offers the best value at regular prices, healing 1 HP per $10. In contrast, the Hyper Potion is the least economical at 1 HP per $12.5, with the Super Potion falling in between at 1 HP per $11.67. Conversely, before Generation VII, among the Potions providing a set HP recovery (Potion, Super Potion, and Hyper Potion), the Potion was the least cost-efficient at its standard price, yielding only 1 HP per $15. The Hyper Potion proved most economical at 1 HP per $6, and the Super Potion was moderately priced at 1 HP per $14.

Title: Antidote

Known in Japanese as どくけし (Antidote), this status condition healing medicine made its debut in Generation I. Its primary function is to alleviate a Pokémon's poisoned status. When utilized from the Bag, it effectively neutralizes the effects of poisoning on a Pokémon. Appearing as a Wonder Launcher item, the Antidote requires 4 points for activation. Within the game Pokémon Conquest, this item also serves to cure a Pokémon from poisoning. Trainers can acquire an Antidote from any Shop for a price ranging from 100 to 70 gold, and may resell it for 50 to 65 gold.

Its initial mention occurred in the episode A Venipede Stampede!, where Cilan administered some to cure Ash after he had been poisoned by a wild Venipede's Poison Point. Another reference appeared in An Undersea Place to Call Home!, with Lindsey discreetly employing one to treat Ash's Pikachu, who was badly poisoned by a wild Skrelp's Toxic. The Antidote first made a visual appearance in The Sun, the Scare, the Secret Lair!. Professor Kukui provided Ash with one from his medical kit, enabling him to cure his Rowlet from the poison inflicted by James's Mareanie's Sludge Bomb. Lana, in The One That Didn't Get Away!, utilized Antidote-laced food wrapped around her fishing lure to heal a Kyogre suffering from poisoning. Throughout The Pokémon Center Lady, Mollie consistently employed Antidotes to cure wild Pokémon at Woody Park, which had been poisoned by a Laquium-affected Vileplume. Red, during The Secret of Kangaskhan, applied an Antidote to heal a baby Kangaskhan from her poisoning. In Toasty!! Battle Against Toxapex, Henry administered an Antidote to restore his Thwackey after it was poisoned by Nessa's Toxapex's Baneful Bunker. PASV22 saw Scarlet attempt to use an Antidote on her poisoned Tarountula. However, disoriented by Atticus's poison techniques, she mistakenly applied a Paralyze Heal, leading to no change in her Pokémon's condition. Shu, in GDZ68, offered his Pikachu an Antidote to counteract the poisoning caused by a Trainer's Kingdra's Toxic.

The Antidote prominently features in the artwork for the "Contents of Trainer's bag" merchandise line, which was exclusively available at the Pokémon Center. Early depictions of the Antidote portrayed it as a vaccine contained within a syringe-type device. This visual was subsequently altered in Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen, where it became a "spray-type medicine."

Title: Burn Heal
