Beginning in Generation I, the Burn Heal (Japanese: やけどなおし Burn Heal) functions as a medicinal item. Its primary purpose is to alleviate the burn status condition affecting a Pokémon. Directly applied from a Trainer's Bag, it specifically remedies a Pokémon's burn. This item is also accessible within the Wonder Launcher system, where utilizing it there requires an expenditure of 4 points. Moreover, in the realm of Pokémon Conquest, the Burn Heal performs the same restorative action against burns for a Pokémon. Available for acquisition at any standard Shop for a price ranging from 300 to 210 gold, it can subsequently be divested for 150 to 195 gold. Its presence was noted in the episode "The Pillar of Fire," where Friede presented one to Dot, enabling the recovery of her Quaxwell during their confrontation with Lucius's Gouging Fire, after the "Practicing Pokémon" sustained a burn from Gouging Fire's Burning Bulwark attack. Within the Pokémon fan community, a persistent misunderstanding suggests that the Moon Ball would offer an enhanced catch rate specifically for wild Pokémon that evolve via the Burn Heal item in the Generation II titles. For a comprehensive understanding of the Moon Ball's actual mechanics in that particular generation, consult the entry on the Moon Ball oversight.

Title: Ice Heal

First appearing in Generation I, the Ice Heal (Japanese: こおりなおし Ice Heal) is a medicinal item. Its primary purpose is to remedy the frozen status condition affecting a Pokémon. When activated from the Bag and directed at a Pokémon, it successfully removes the freezing ailment. As part of the Wonder Launcher inventory, the Ice Heal is accessible for use, requiring an expenditure of 4 points. Within the Pokémon Conquest game, the Ice Heal also functions to eliminate the frozen state from a Pokémon. This item is procurable from any Shop for a cost ranging from 300 to 210 gold, and conversely, it can be vended for 150 to 195 gold. In the events of The Final Dimensional Duel VI, Charon recounted administering an Ice Heal to his Heatran subsequent to it being struck by Diamond's Regigigas's Icy Wind. Likewise, in To the Pokémon League!!, Red applied an Ice Heal to Clefairy after it had been frozen by Lorelei's Dewgong.

Title: Awakening

For information regarding the Awakening Emera found in Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon, please refer to Emera. The Awakening (Japanese: ねむけざまし Drowsiness Cure) is a medicinal item first introduced in Generation I, primarily designed to rouse a Pokémon from sleep. When applied to a Pokémon from the Bag, it remedies the sleep status, or, specifically in Pokémon Legends: Z-A, the drowsy status. It also appears as a Wonder Launcher item, requiring 4 points for activation. Within Pokémon Conquest, this item similarly functions to rouse a Pokémon from slumber. Its market value ranges from 100-70 gold for purchase at any Shop, and it can be resold for 50-65 gold. In the Japanese cut of A Scare in the Air, a woman secured an Awakening as a lottery reward; however, in the English dub, this prize was altered to "beautiful refrigerator magnets." Y utilized an Awakening in Charizard Transforms to revive her Greninja, Croaky, who had been incapacitated by Xerosic's Malamar's Hypnosis. Similarly, Jun applied an Awakening to awaken Hareta's Piplup in The Mystery Boy, Jun!!, after Mars's Purugly's Hypnosis had caused it to fall asleep. The Call function in Pokémon Colosseum and XD: Gale of Darkness performs an identical role, rousing sleeping Pokémon effectively mimicking the Awakening's effect.

Title: Paralyze Heal

Introduced as a medicinal item in Generation I, the Paralyze Heal (Japanese: まひなおし Paralysis Heal) was known as Parlyz Heal from Generation I through Generation V. Its primary function is to remove the paralysis status condition from a Pokémon. Notably, the English instruction manual for Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen also refers to this item as Paralyz Heal. Employing it from the Bag on a Pokémon invariably alleviates paralysis. This item is also featured within the Wonder Launcher system, where its use requires 4 points. Within the game Pokémon Conquest, the Parlyz Heal similarly serves to restore a Pokémon afflicted by paralysis. A Parlyz Heal is available for acquisition from any Shop, priced between 200-140 gold, and yields 100-130 gold when sold. The item received a mention in the episode "Pokémon Scent-sation!", where Erika proposed using some on Ash's Bulbasaur following an attack by her Tangela's Stun Spore; however, Ash chose not to accept the offer. Another instance appears in "Weezing Winks Out," detailing Koga's intervention to heal Blue, who had been paralyzed by Agatha's Gengar's Lick, by delivering a Paralyze Heal concealed within one of his signature shuriken Poké Balls. In PASV22, Scarlet intended to apply an Antidote to her poisoned Tarountula; nevertheless, disoriented by Atticus's poison techniques, she mistakenly deployed a Paralyze Heal, which consequently had no effect on her Pokémon's poisoned status. Furthermore, in Pokémon Adventures, Blue's paralysis is remedied by a Paralyze Heal, marking a unique occasion as the first time a general status condition healing item, distinct from regional specialties, was utilized to treat a human.

Title: Full Restore

Debuting in Generation I, the Full Restore (known in Japanese as かいふくのくすり, or Recovery Medicine) functions as a medicinal item. It completely replenishes a Pokémon's HP and eliminates all non-volatile status conditions and confusion. This item stands as an improved counterpart to the Potion, Super Potion, Hyper Potion, Max Potion, and Full Heal. In Pokémon Crystal, it was priced at $600 during the Goldenrod Department Store's rooftop sale. When applied to a Pokémon from the Bag, it delivers a full HP restoration and cures the Pokémon of all non-volatile status conditions, including poison, paralysis, sleep, burn, and freeze. A notable feature in Pokémon Yellow is its ability to boost friendship when given to the player's Pikachu. When employed during a battle, the Full Restore additionally now provides a cure for confusion. However, an oversight in the Generation II games means the item fails to cure a Pokémon of Nightmare (even after waking up from sleep) if used by an opponent in battle. The Full Restore is also featured as a Wonder Launcher item, requiring 13 points for its activation. Within Pokémon Conquest, it serves to completely restore a Pokémon's HP and remove ailments such as poison, paralysis, sleep, burn, freeze, and confusion. A Full Restore can be acquired from the traveling merchant for 3000 gold, and conversely sold for a price ranging from 1500 to 1950 gold. In the episode A Mission of Ultra Urgency!, Ash and his classmates were equipped with a medical kit for their Ultra Guardian missions, which contained several healing supplies, among them three Full Restores. On their initial mission, Lillie utilized one of these Full Restores to attend to a Snorlax that had been drained by a Buzzwole. Later, in Full Moon and Many Arms!, Lillie deployed another Full Restore from the same medical kit to assist in Lunala's recovery. Following Ult's defeat by Roy in Mega Evolution - Ult, Master Mayonnaise presented Ult with a Full Restore to aid his Sableye's healing. This item is depicted in the artwork for the Contents of Trainer's bag merchandise line, an exclusive offering from the Pokémon Center.

Title: Max Potion

Introduced in Generation I, the Max Potion (Japanese: まんたんのくすり, meaning "Tank-Filling Medicine") is a medicinal item primarily used to restore a Pokémon's HP. It stands as an enhanced version of the Potion, Super Potion, and Hyper Potion, though it is superseded by the even more potent Full Restore. Administered from the Bag, it completely rejuvenates a Pokémon's health to its maximum capacity. In Pokémon Yellow, a unique interaction occurs where its use on the player's Pikachu also results in increased friendship.

A notable limitation existed in the Generation I and II core series games: the Max Potion was capped at healing only up to 999 HP, rather than a full restoration. This was despite the highest legitimate HP a Pokémon could possess being 703 for a level 100 Chansey in Generation I, or 713 for a level 100 Blissey in Generation II. Similarly, early releases of Pokémon Platinum contained an erroneous programming detail, causing the Max Potion to restore only up to 1000 HP, a figure exceeding the highest natural HP of 714 for a level 100 Blissey without employing cheats. Subsequent game revisions rectified this, allowing for healing to the absolute maximum HP.

The Max Potion also features as a Wonder Launcher item, requiring 10 points for its use. Within Pokémon Conquest, it serves to completely replenish a Pokémon's HP. Max Potions can be purchased from any Level 3 Shop for 2500-1750 gold and sold for 1250-1575 gold. Outside of combat, these items are effective for entirely restoring a Pokémon's HP, but their use is prohibited during battle. In the Shop, Max Potions are available in bundles of ten, priced at 200. From level 25 onwards, Max Potions become acquirable through gameplay, with players receiving them upon leveling up to any level from 25 and beyond. Additionally, the player can repeatably obtain Max Potions by.

Their inaugural appearance in the anime occurred in "A Mission of Ultra Urgency!", where Ash and his classmates received a medical kit containing three, among other healing items, designated for their Ultra Guardian missions.

As an Item card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game, Max Potion was introduced during the English Black & White Series (Japanese BW Era) in the Emerging Powers expansion, featuring artwork by 5ban Graphics. This card grants the player the ability to heal all damage from one of their Pokémon, but subsequently requires the player to discard all Energy attached to that Pokémon. Furthermore, a Max Potion can be observed within the illustration of the Yellow A Alternate art print for the Trainers' Mail card, originating from the Roaring Skies expansion.

Title: Hyper Potion

Introduced in Generation I, the Hyper Potion (Japanese: すごいキズぐすり, or "Amazing Wound Medicine") is a medical item designed to replenish a Pokémon's HP. It serves as an enhanced version of both the Potion and Super Potion, though it is itself surpassed by the Max Potion and Full Restore.

Regarding its acquisition, Pokémon Crystal offered it for $1000 during a special rooftop sale at the Goldenrod Department Store. In Join Avenue's Market, players could purchase Hyper Potions in sets of a dozen for $10800, which effectively brought the individual unit cost down to $900.

Initially, using a Hyper Potion from the Bag would restore a Pokémon's health by 200 HP. In Pokémon Yellow, a unique interaction saw its use on the player's Pikachu also boost friendship. A specific event in Pokémon Black 2 and White 2 involved a lady at Shopping Mall Nine who, once daily, would request the player purchase a Hyper Potion, providing the necessary funds. If the player revisited her on the same day with the item in their Bag, she would grant an Energy Root as a reward; conversing with her on a subsequent day instead yielded an EnergyPowder. In later iterations, including Pokémon Sword and Shield, as well as Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, applying a Hyper Potion from the Bag restores 120 HP. Pokémon Legends: Arceus sets its healing effect at 150 HP.

This item also features in the Wonder Launcher system, requiring 8 points for its deployment.

Within Pokémon Conquest, the Hyper Potion restores 200 HP to a Pokémon. It can be acquired from any Level 2 or 3 Shop, with prices ranging from 1200-840 gold for purchase and 600-780 gold for sale. Usable exclusively outside of battle, it heals for 200 HP, but cannot be utilized during combat. Players can repeatedly obtain Hyper Potions via various methods starting at level 15, and are also rewarded with them upon leveling up to any point between levels 15 and 24.

Several instances of the Hyper Potion have appeared in different media. Green was seen with one in her Bag during The Discovery. In Double Trouble with Dialga and Palkia I, Byron administered a Hyper Potion to Gardenia's Cherubi amid the Sinnoh Gym Leaders' confrontation with Team Galactic Commanders at the Spear Pillar. Charon later referenced using a Hyper Potion on his Heatran in The Final Dimensional Duel VI, following an injury sustained while battling Diamond's Regigigas.

The Hyper Potion also exists as a Trainer card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game, making its debut in the Skyridge expansion of the English e-Card Series (known as the Japanese e-Card Era). Katsura Tabata provided the artwork for this card, which allows players to discard either one or two basic Energy cards attached to one of their Pokémon. Discarding a single Energy card enables the removal of up to three damage counters from that Pokémon, while discarding two Energy cards permits the removal of up to five damage counters.

On November 28, 2024, a collection of Pokémon travel merchandise exclusive to the Pokémon Center included a set of three travel bottles, one of which was designed to resemble the Hyper Potion.

Regarding cost-effectiveness for items that heal a fixed amount of HP (Potion, Super Potion, and Hyper Potion), its standing has shifted. Prior to Generation VII, the Hyper Potion was the most economical choice at normal pricing, providing 1 HP for every $6 spent, whereas the Potion was the least efficient at 1 HP per $15, and the Super Potion fell in the middle at 1 HP per $14. However, in Generation VII, this dynamic reversed: the Hyper Potion became the least cost-effective, offering 1 HP per $12.5, with the Potion emerging as the most efficient at 1 HP per $10, and the Super Potion positioned between them at 1 HP per $11.67.

Notably, the Hyper Potion remains the sole variant of Potion yet to be featured in the animated series.

Title: Super Potion

The Super Potion (Japanese: いいキズぐすり, Good Wound Medicine), a type of medicinal item, made its debut in Generation I of the Pokémon series. Its primary purpose is to replenish a Pokémon's Hit Points. Positioned as an upgrade to the basic Potion, it itself has three more advanced variants: the Hyper Potion, Max Potion, and Full Restore. During the Goldenrod Department Store rooftop sale in Pokémon Crystal, a Super Potion could be purchased for $500.

When administered from the Bag, the amount of HP recovered by a Pokémon varies by game. Early iterations of the game saw it heal 50 HP. Notably, in Pokémon Yellow, applying a Super Potion to the player's Pikachu also enhanced its friendship. Later, in Pokémon Sword and Shield, as well as Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, its healing potency was set to 60 HP. Pokémon Legends: Arceus grants a more substantial 100 HP recovery from this item.

In terms of crafting, one Super Potion is a necessary component for creating a Hyper Potion. The Super Potion also functions as an item in the Wonder Launcher, requiring 4 points for its use.

Within the world of Pokémon Conquest, Super Potions restore 50 HP. They are available for purchase at any Shop, with prices ranging from 700 to 490 gold, and can be sold for 350 to 455 gold. In this title, Super Potions are exclusively for out-of-battle use, providing 50 HP of healing, and cannot be utilized during combat. Players can acquire Super Potions once they reach level 10, and are granted these items upon leveling up to any stage between 10 and 14.

Super Potion cards have also featured in the Pokémon Trading Card Game. It was present in the original Pokémon Trading Card Game and its sequel, Pokémon Trading Card Game 2: The Invasion of Team GR!. Initially released as a Trainer card in the Base Set expansion of the English Original Series (Japanese Original Era), with artwork by Keiji Kinebuchi, this card allowed players to remove up to four damage counters from one of their Pokémon by discarding an attached Energy card. Later, it was re-released as an Item card in the XY expansion, featuring new artwork by 5ban Graphics, first appearing in the Japanese Collection Y. This updated version enabled players to heal 60 damage from one of their Pokémon, but required discarding an Energy attached to that Pokémon.

In Pokémon Masters EX, the Super Potion is a specialized item move, usable only by specific sync pairs.

Throughout Pokémon the Series, Super Potions have made frequent appearances. Carried commonly by characters like Brock, Cilan, and Clemont in their backpacks, they serve to mend injured Pokémon. The sensation of using a Potion is sometimes depicted as slightly stinging, hinting at both healing and antiseptic properties. Tracey was once observed using a form of Potion that was ingested orally. Among all Potion types, the Super Potion was the most prevalent in the animated series, regularly applied to heal Pokémon until Pokémon the Series: Sun & Moon, when standard Potions took its place.

Several specific instances highlight its use in the anime:
*   In "Here Comes the Squirtle Squad!", after Pikachu suffered a severe injury from a Goldeen while being pursued by the Squirtle Squad, Ash was permitted to fetch a Super Potion from town to heal his captured Pokémon. This marked the first mention of a Potion in the animated series.
*   Brock reportedly used a Super Potion off-screen to treat a hurt Horsea in "Tentacool & Tentacruel".
*   In "The Kangaskhan Kid", Brock applied a Super Potion to a wounded baby Kangaskhan.
*   Brock was seen using a Super Potion on an injured Sandslash during "A Chansey Operation".
*   Ash utilized one of Brock's Super Potions to aid a wild Houndour with an injury in "Hour of the Houndour".
*   In "You Never Can Taillow", Brock employed a Super Potion twice: first, to heal Ash's Pikachu after a battle with a wild Taillow, and later in the same episode, to treat the newly caught Taillow itself.
*   May used a Super Potion to tend to a wild Swablu she befriended in "True Blue Swablu".
*   Brock healed Dawn's Piplup with a Super Potion in "When Pokémon Worlds Collide!".
*   In "Leave It To Brocko!", Brock treated a minor injury on a wild Nuzleaf with a Super Potion.
*   Brock used a Super Potion to address a slight injury on a wild Bidoof in "Faced with Steelix Determination!".
*   Cynthia administered a Super Potion to Paul's Torterra in "Top-Down Training!" after its defeat by her Garchomp.
*   Brock applied a Super Potion to Meowth in "Unlocking the Red Chain of Events!", treating a wound sustained during an encounter with Team Galactic.
*   Kellyn used a Super Potion on Aura Sphere Riolu in "Pokémon Ranger and the Kidnapped Riolu! (Part 1)" to mend its arm injury.
*   Cilan cured his Pansage with a Super Potion in "A Home for Dwebble!" after it was struck on the head by a rock; Iris later resolved the resulting fever with herbal medicine.
*   Iris used a Super Potion to treat a wound on a wild Venipede in "A Venipede Stampede!".
*   Alder applied a Super Potion to a wild Gigalith's wounds in "Ash Versus the Champion!".
*   Cynthia used a Super Potion to help heal Meloetta in "All for the Love of Meloetta!" after it was ambushed and injured by Team Rocket.
*   Professor Oak utilized a Super Potion on a Rotom in "To Catch a Rotom!", prompting the grateful Rotom to voluntarily join his team.
*   In "Team Rocket's Shocking Recruit!", Cilan treated Iris's Axew's injury with a Super Potion.
*   Brock healed a wild Gyarados with a Super Potion in BWS01, following its incapacitation by Cilan's Pansage's Grass Whistle.
*   Nurse Joy used a Super Potion to treat a wild Lapras's wound in "Bonnie for the Defense!".
*   Dr. White administered a Super Potion to Beatrice's Bidoof's gums in "A Fork in the Road! A Parting of the Ways!".
*   A flashback in "A Trip Down Memory Train!" showed Clemont using a Super Potion on a wild Bunnelby, which had been injured in a fight with a wild Diggersby.
*   Nurse Joy was depicted in a flashback in "Cloudy Fate, Bright Future!" healing Froakie's wounds with a Super Potion.
*   Alain used a Super Potion on his Charizard during his challenge to defeat ten consecutive Mega Evolution Trainers in "Mega Evolution Special IV".
*   Kimia applied a Super Potion to an Amaura in "Volcanion and the Mechanical Marvel" after it was caught in a Pokémon hunter's trap.
*   In "The Power of Us", an alternate continuity film, Toren provided Ash with a Super Potion to assist in healing injured Pokémon.

Regarding merchandise, a collection of Pokémon travel goods, exclusive to the Pokémon Center, included a set of three travel bottles, one of which was designed to resemble a Super Potion. This item was launched on November 28, 2024.

Title: Full Heal

The **Full Heal** (Japanese: **なんでもなおし Heal-All**) is a medicinal item first appearing in **Generation I**. Its purpose is to alleviate a Pokémon from any **non-volatile status conditions** and **confusion**. This item functions as an enhanced version of the **Antidote**, **Paralyze Heal**, **Burn Heal**, **Ice Heal**, and **Awakening**, consolidating their individual effects. Furthermore, the **Full Restore** serves as an even more potent equivalent.

Initially, the **Full Heal** was visually represented as a vaccine contained within a syringe-like apparatus; however, its design evolved into a "spray-type medicine" in **Pokémon FireRed** and **LeafGreen**. During the **Goldenrod Department Store rooftop sale** in **Pokémon Crystal**, a **Full Heal** could be acquired for **$500**. At the **Join Avenue's Market**, **Full Heal** can be purchased in batches of twelve for **$5400**, which calculates to **$450** per individual item.

When utilized from the **Bag** on a Pokémon, it clears all **non-volatile status conditions** such as **poison**, **paralysis**, **sleep**, **burn**, and **freeze**. An anomaly in the **Generation II** games meant that the item would not relieve a Pokémon of **Nightmare** (even post-awakening from **sleep**) or **confusion** if deployed by an opponent during battle. If employed from the **satchel** upon a Pokémon, it eradicates all **non-volatile status conditions**, specifically **poison**, **paralysis**, **drowsy**, **burn**, and **frostbite**.

The **Full Heal** is also featured as a **Wonder Launcher** item, requiring **6 points** for its use. Within **Pokémon Conquest**, this item can be administered to a Pokémon to cure **poison**, **paralysis**, **sleep**, **burn**, **freeze**, and **confusion**. In **Terrera**, a **Full Heal** can be obtained from the **Shop** for **600-420 gold** and sold for **300-390 gold**.

The **Full Heal** card is accessible in the **Pokémon Trading Card Game** and **Pokémon Trading Card Game 2: The Invasion of Team GR!**, while the **Full Heal Energy** card is exclusively present in the latter title. In **Pokémon Masters EX**, **Full Heals** are exclusive to specific **sync pairs** who possess them as an **item move**. As a **battle item** in **Pokémon UNITE**, unlocked at **Trainer Level 14**, the **Full Heal** eliminates all **status conditions** from one of the player's Pokémon and grants immunity to **hindrances** for **1.5s**, operating on a **50s cooldown**.

A **Full Heal** made an appearance in **Finale!! The Battle Against the Other Dimension!**, where **Moon** supplied one to **Gladion** for treating his injuries.

The **Full Heal** was incorporated as a **Trainer card** in the initial release of the **TCG**, known as the **Base Set** (or **Expansion Pack** in Japan), featuring artwork by **Keiji Kinebuchi**. Following several subsequent printings, **Kinebuchi** provided new artwork for the Japanese **Pokémon-e Starter Deck**, which comprised a segment of the English **Expedition Base Set**. The **Full Heal** saw no further reprints until the **SoulSilver Collection**, at which point it was reclassified as an **Item card**, also showcasing fresh artwork by **Takashi Yamaguchi**. Although this particular print was included in the English **HeartGold & SoulSilver expansion**, the **Trainer card** classifications were not adopted until the **Black & White expansion**, which additionally contained a **Full Heal** reprint. Originating from Japan's **White Collection**, this reprint once again presented new artwork, this time by **5ban Graphics**. **5ban Graphics** was also responsible for the art of the subsequent reprint, found in the **Furious Fists expansion** (the Japanese **Rising Fist expansion**). The **Japanese Expansion Pack 20th Anniversary** and English **Evolutions expansion**—both sets commemorating the **Pokémon** franchise's **20th anniversary**—reissued the card with its original **Kinebuchi** artwork. Concurrently, new artwork by **Yoshinobu Saito** graced some **Japanese S-P Promotional cards** and the **Rebel Clash expansion**. This card acts to remove all **Special Conditions** from one of the player's **Active Pokémon**.

In the inaugural **Pokémon Trading Figure Game** expansion, **Next Quest**, the **Full Heal** was featured as a **Trainer card**. Its effect is to eliminate all **Special Conditions** from one of the player's Pokémon. The artwork employed for this version is identical to that of the **Full Heal Trainer card** from the **TCG's Expedition Base Set**.

Title: Revive

A medicinal item originating from Generation I, the Revive (known in Japanese as げんきのかけら, or Vitality Fragment) brings a fainted Pokémon back to consciousness, replenishing half of its total Hit Points. A more potent variant of this item is known as the Max Revive. Regarding its cost, a Revive can be acquired for $1200 within Pokémon Crystal during the Goldenrod Department Store's rooftop sale event. At the Market found in Join Avenue, players can purchase Revives in bundles of twelve for $13500 per dozen, which calculates to an individual price of $1125 per Revive. Activating it from the Bag on a Pokémon that has fainted will restore the Pokémon to consciousness and recover 50% of its total HP. Interestingly, it remains usable even on Shedinja, despite that Pokémon consistently possessing a maximum HP of 1 (thus technically achieving full HP restoration). The Revive is also featured as an item within the Wonder Launcher, requiring 11 points for its activation. Its application is restricted to outside of combat situations; it will bring a fainted Pokémon back and restore half its maximum HP, but cannot be utilized during an active battle. Pokémon may faint in Gym battles, Raid Battles, or when removed from a Gym; however, those that faint in Trainer Battles are not considered fainted once the match concludes. This item becomes available to players beginning at level 5. Players are granted Revives upon leveling up to any stage between level 5 and 29. Furthermore, they can be acquired by finishing specific Field Research or Special Research tasks.

In the realm of the Pokémon Trading Card Game and Pokémon Trading Card Game 2: The Invasion of Team GR!, the Revive card can be found. Its presence has also been noted in other media: a Revive was fleetingly visible in Green's Bag during The Discovery. During the events of You Need to Chill Out, Regice, Emerald deployed a Revive to restore his Sceptile mid-battle against Brandon. This strategic move enabled Sceptile to execute an unexpected Iron Tail from behind on Brandon's Regice, securing victory for Emerald. Following this encounter, Emerald disclosed that he had collected an impressive 99 Revives throughout his Battle Pyramid challenge. Separately, in The Battle Rages On, Saturn was observed using a Revive off-panel to revive his Rhyperior after it had been knocked out by Hareta's Piplup.

Within the Pokémon Trading Card Game, Revive initially debuted as a Trainer card in the Base Set expansion, part of the English Original Series (or Japanese Original Era), featuring illustrations by Keiji Kinebuchi. It saw a subsequent reprint within the Japanese Nivi City Gym Theme Deck. Later, its classification shifted to an Item card for the Black & White expansion, showcasing fresh artwork by 5ban Graphics, first appearing in the Japanese White Collection expansion. This specific print was then reissued in the Japanese Battle Theme Deck: Victini and the Master Deck Build Box EX, which launched on September 14, 2012. The XY Series included another reprint, with new artwork by Toyste Beach, in the Roaring Skies expansion (initially from the Japanese Emerald Break expansion). A retro version of the card was made available in the Evolutions expansion, originating from the Japanese Expansion Pack 20th Anniversary subset, which featured Keiji Kinebuchi's original artwork alongside a card template updated to evoke the look of the Original Series. The Base Set and Nivi City Gym versions of this Trainer card allowed a player to move a single Basic Pokémon card from their discard pile directly onto their Bench. Subsequently, the player was required to place damage counters on that Pokémon equivalent to half of its HP, rounded down. However, the Black & White and XY series printings revised the card's effect by eliminating the requirement to add damage counters. Furthermore, an image of a Revive can be observed within the illustration of the Yellow A Alternate art print of the Trainers' Mail card, originating from the Roaring Skies expansion. This item has been a subject of various officially licensed merchandise. Visually, the Revive bears a resemblance to a Star Piece from the game Paper Mario.

Title: Max Revive

Originating in the first generation of games, the Max Revive (known in Japanese as げんきのかたまり, meaning Vitality Clump) is a specific type of medicinal item. Its primary function is to bring a fainted Pokémon back to consciousness and fully replenish its Hit Points. Applying it from the Bag to a Pokémon that has collapsed will successfully revive that Pokémon and completely restore its health. Within the Wonder Launcher system, this item is available for activation at a cost of 14 points. Outside of combat scenarios, a Max Revive serves to return a fainted Pokémon to active status, granting them full HP; however, their use is prohibited mid-battle. Pokémon can faint during Gym battles, Raid Battles, or when removed from a Gym; conversely, Pokémon that faint in Trainer Battles automatically recover from their fainted state once the battle concludes. The Shop offers Max Revives for purchase in packs of six, priced at 180. Beyond simply buying them, players can acquire Max Revives through gameplay mechanics once they reach level 30 or higher. Specifically, players receive these items upon leveling up to any subsequent level from 30. Furthermore, completion of specific Field Research and Special Research tasks provides a repeatable method for players to earn Max Revives. A Max Revive card exists within Pokémon Trading Card Game 2: The Invasion of Team GR!.

In the episode "Up in the Air," Skyla utilized a Max Revive to aid an injured wild Pidove during a gathering of Gym Leaders at the Celestial Tower. N, in "A Wretched Reunion," employed multiple Max Revives to heal his Pokémon following his conflict with Black, White, and Cedric Juniper, as he was making preparations to head back to Team Plasma's headquarters. During "Finale!! The Battle Against the Other Dimension!," Moon administered a Max Revive to Solgaleo, enabling it to join Lunala and Zygarde in a combined assault aimed at vanquishing Necrozma. Henry, in "Crackle!! Retrieve the Sword & Shield," restored Galarian Moltres using a Max Revive.

Within the Pokémon Trading Card Game, Max Revive debuted as a Trainer card during the English Original Series (corresponding to the Japanese Original Era) in the Gym Challenge expansion, featuring illustrations by Hikaru Koike. Subsequently, it was reissued as an Item card, showcasing entirely new artwork by 5ban Graphics and a distinct effect, initially appearing in the Japanese Collection X before its inclusion in the English XY expansion. The Gym Challenge iteration of this Trainer card permits the player to move one Basic Pokémon from their discard pile directly to their Bench after discarding two Energy cards from their hand. The XY print, however, changed this Trainer card's function to simply allow a player to place any Pokémon from their discard pile onto their Bench. Furthermore, Max Revive was integrated as a Trainer card in the inaugural Pokémon Trading Figure Game expansion, titled Next Quest. This version enables the player to transfer one of their Pokémon from the Pokémon Center directly to their Bench. Notably, it shares the identical artwork featured on the Max Revive Trainer card from the TCG's Gym Challenge expansion.

The Max Revive has also appeared across various officially licensed merchandise. Its visual design bears a resemblance to konpeitō, a traditional sugar candy with Portuguese and Japanese origins. From a geometric perspective, the Max Revive strongly mirrors a tetrakis hexahedron – a polyhedron formed by replacing each square face of a cube with a square-based pyramid. The version found in the Sinnoh Underground, however, exhibits a distinct geometric form, seemingly possessing square antiprismatic symmetry. In Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, should Celebi faint during the confrontation with Giovanni, the player character (Ethan or Lyra) will observe this and apply a Max Revive to Celebi, understanding that it must recover to facilitate their return to their original timeline.

Title: Fresh Water

Known in Japanese as おいしいみず (Delicious Water), Fresh Water is a medicine item that first appeared in Generation I. This beverage is designed to restore a Pokémon's Hit Points. Its origin is attributed to Mt. Silver, a detail found in the Japanese item descriptions across Pokémon Gold, Silver, Crystal, and Pokémon Stadium 2. This sourcing is similarly noted in the Korean item descriptions for Pokémon Gold and Silver.

Acquisition is possible at various locations for specific prices: $1000 in Black City; $300 at Skyarrow Bridge; and $400 from vending machines at Hoenn's Route 112 and Mt. Chimney Cable Car stations, as well as Alola's Routes 8 and 13. Alternatively, a more affordable price of $150 is available from vending machines located at the Team Magma Hideout, the Team Aqua Hideout, and the Game Freak Office in Heahea City. During Generation V games, a gourmet enthusiast on Route 5 will purchase it for $200.

Administering Fresh Water to a Pokémon from the Bag results in a 50 HP restoration, consuming the item upon use. Additionally, when applied from the Bag to a Pokémon, it can restore 30 HP, also being consumed after use.

Within Kanto-based titles, presenting Fresh Water to a parched girl at the Celadon Department Store yields TM13 (Ice Beam), TM16 (Light Screen), or TM06 (Light Screen) in return. Access to Saffron City in Generation I games is granted upon the player providing a Fresh Water, Soda Pop, or Lemonade to one of the city's guards. In Pokémon Black 2 and White 2, players may offer Fresh Water to a thirsty individual stationed at each of Unova's bridges, commencing with Skyarrow Bridge, for a total of five instances. As a reward for the bottle given at Skyarrow Bridge, he provides twelve bottles of Moomoo Milk. For players of Pokémon Sun and Moon, giving a Fresh Water to the Trial Guide on Route 13, located at the Haina Desert entrance, will result in receiving three Adrenaline Orbs. In Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, if a player provides a Fresh Water to a Hiker within Haina Desert, they will subsequently acquire ten additional Fresh Waters from him at the Route 13 motel.

In the episode 'Never Spritz a Knotty Sudowoodo', Todd Snap consumed a bottle of Fresh Water. Unable to finish it, he poured the remainder onto what he mistook for a tree, which was actually a wild Sudowoodo. This provoked the Sudowoodo to attack, necessitating Emerald's intervention to placate her.

A Trainer card named Fresh Water Set debuted in the Pokémon Trading Card Game during the English XY Series, corresponding to the Japanese XY Era. Its initial release occurred in the Tidal Storm expansion, and it first appeared in English with the Primal Clash expansion, featuring artwork by Toyste Beach. The card saw a reprint in the Brilliant Stars expansion (Japanese: Star Birth expansion), this time with an illustration by AYUMI ODASHIMA. The Fresh Water Set card provides healing, removing 20 damage from all of the player's Pokémon.

This item has been depicted in numerous officially licensed figures and merchandise. A physical rendition of Fresh Water will be available for purchase at PokéPark Kanto starting February 5, 2026. Considering all special purchase prices, Fresh Water stands as the most cost-efficient HP recovery item in the core series when acquired for $100 at the Kalos Power Plant in Pokémon X and Y, offering 1 HP of recovery for every $2 spent.

Title: Soda Pop

This entry details the consumable item. For the song sharing this name, please consult Pokémon the First Movie (soundtrack). サイコソーダ (Saiko Soda) in Japanese, Soda Pop is a medicinal item first appearing in Generation I. This beverage replenishes a Pokémon's health points (HP). Vending machines on Hoenn's Route 112 and Mt. Chimney Cable Car stations, as well as Alola's Routes 8 and 13, offer it for purchase at a premium of $600. Conversely, the vending machines found within the Team Magma Hideout and Team Aqua Hideout sell it for a reduced cost of $250. Players in the Generation V titles have the option to sell it to the gourmet enthusiast located on Route 5 for $300. When utilized from the Bag on a Pokémon, it provides 60 HP recovery. It is expended upon use. When used from the Bag on a Pokémon, it restores 50 HP to that Pokémon. Its use results in consumption. In games set in Kanto, a parched girl at the Celadon Department Store will accept it in trade for TM48 (Rock Slide), TM20 (Safeguard), or TM09 (Reflect). During Generation I gameplay, offering a Fresh Water, Soda Pop, or Lemonade to a Saffron City guard grants the player entry into the metropolis. The episode "The Fourth Round Rumble" depicted Team Rocket slipping on a Soda Pop can during their escape from Officer Jenny. Within "Triple Leaders, Triple Threats!", Cress proposed bringing Ash a Soda Pop, an offer Ash declined, preferring a Gym battle over the refreshment. Stephan was observed consuming Soda Pop in the episode "Mission: Defeat Your Rival!". A vendor presented Axew with a Soda Pop in "Lost at the League!". "This Magik Moment!" featured a Soda Pop as an element of a television program featuring Mallow, Lillie, and Lana. This item has also appeared in various officially licensed merchandise and figures. A real-world iteration of Soda Pop will be purchasable at PokéPark Kanto starting February 5, 2026. Under typical purchase conditions, Soda Pop stands as the most economical HP restoration item, yielding 1 HP for every $6 expended. Nonetheless, a Max Potion (along with Full Restore and Revives in specific scenarios) may prove more financially efficient given particular conditions; the optimal use case for a Max Potion involves a Blissey possessing maximum EVs and IVs in HP with only 1 HP remaining, leading to approximately 1 HP recovered per $3.5 spent. Early artwork from Generation I renders the item's name as "Psycho Soda." Additionally, "Psycho Soda" is its depiction in Chapter 8 of Chuang Yi's Pokémon Pocket Monsters translation. The inspiration for Soda Pop might be ramune, a Japanese carbonated beverage. Yuki Koide was shown drinking a Soda Pop in the fifth installment of A Pocketful of Adventures.

Title: Lemonade

Lemonade (Japanese: ミックスオレ Mix au Lait) is a medicinal drink first introduced in Generation I, purposed for restoring a Pokémon's HP. Vending Machines in Hoenn, specifically at the Route 112 and Mt. Chimney Cable Car stations, as well as on Alola Route 13, offer this item for a price of $700. A more affordable option exists at $300 from Vending Machines located within the Team Magma Hideout and Team Aqua Hideout. In the Generation V games, it can be exchanged for $350 when sold to the gourmet maniac found on Route 5.

When utilized from the Bag, Lemonade rejuvenates a Pokémon by 80 HP, after which it is consumed. Similarly, when applied from the Bag to a Pokémon, it restores 70 HP and is subsequently consumed. Within the Kanto-based games, the beverage can be presented to a parched girl at the Celadon Department Store in trade for TM49 (Tri Attack), TM33 (Reflect), or TM07 (Protect). In the original Generation I titles, players could furnish a Saffron City guard with Fresh Water, Soda Pop, or Lemonade to gain entry to the city. Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen allow players to leave a Lemonade at the Memorial Pillar to acquire TM42 (Facade). Furthermore, Lemonade features as one of the beverages available at Pokémon Center Cafés in Pokémon Sun, Moon, Ultra Sun, and Ultra Moon.

In the anime episode "Electric Shock Showdown," Misty was observed purchasing a can of Lemonade from a vending machine inside the Vermilion City Pokémon Center. During "Brave the Wave," Jessie and James served Lemonade cans to two customers; these were referred to as "Super Sodas" in the dub. "Abandon Ship!" saw a disguised Team Rocket provide Ash and his companions with individual cans of Lemonade while traveling by boat to Slateport City. Later in the same episode, on the Forsaken Ship, Tommy supplied the group with additional Lemonades. A Lemonade made a fleeting appearance among the items in Red's Bag within "A Hollow Victreebel." "Pokémon Primers: Colors" depicts a glass of Lemonade in the section dedicated to yellow objects and Pokémon, accompanied by the description: "Yamper is excited to try some Lemonade!"

Lemonade has also been featured across various officially licensed figures and merchandise. A real-world rendition of Lemonade/Mix au Lait, branded as "Fruits Au Lait," is scheduled for release at PokéPark Kanto on February 5, 2026. This product is crafted from dairy, oranges, apples, peaches, bananas, and soybeans.

Title: Moomoo Milk

Originating in Generation II, Moomoo Milk (Japanese: モーモーミルク) is a medicinal beverage designed to replenish a Pokémon's Hit Points. Specifically within the Generation V titles, this item fetches a price of $500 when offloaded to the gourmet maniac found on Route 5. Administering it to a Pokémon directly from the Bag grants a recovery of 100 HP, after which the item is fully consumed. In the Pokémon installments Sun, Moon, Ultra Sun, and Ultra Moon, Pokémon Center Cafés feature Moomoo Milk among their selection of beverages. Even though Moomoo Milk isn't procurable as an item in Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon, Miltank is known to supply it to Lively Town's inhabitants. Subsequent to the game's epilogue, Miltank enlists Krookodile and Krokorok, identified as the town's gangsters, to assist with her delivery tasks.

Beyond healing, Moomoo Milk serves as a cooking ingredient, boasting a base Strength of 98. Furthermore, players' helper Pokémon are capable of gathering this item. The episode "Tanks for the Memories!" depicts Ash and his companions visiting the Mountain Hut Maid Café, an establishment renowned for its Moomoo Milk, which they sampled personally. During "An Egg Scramble!", Khoury offered bottles of Moomoo Milk for sale at the Johto Festival. Additionally, Khoury's father treated Ash and his friends to ice cream cones crafted from Moomoo Milk, while James was observed consuming a pilfered bottle of the drink.

Ash and Cameron were shown enjoying Moomoo Milk in "Mission: Defeat Your Rival!", and Stephan partook of a bottle in "Lost at the League!". "Young Kiawe Had a Farm!" featured Kiawe and Ash distributing Moomoo Milk from Kiawe's family farm to various Alolan residents, such as Mallow and Harry. Lillie provided Moomoo Milk to Snowy during a visit to Kiawe's family farm in "Big Sky, Small Fry!". Officer Jenny's Gumshoos was observed drinking it in "Mallow and the Forest Teacher!". "Mounting an Electrifying Charge!" depicted Ash and Sophocles enjoying Moomoo Milk, and later in the same episode, Ash offered some to Sophocles's Charjabug during a Charjabug race pit stop to boost its energy. Following Sophocles's team's victory, Kiawe seized the opportunity to promote his Moomoo Milk on television. During "A Dream Encounter!", Kiawe presented a bottle of Moomoo Milk to Nebby, but the Legendary Pokémon, mirroring its reaction to most other provisions, refused it, much to Kiawe's dismay. "The Dex Can't Help It!" showed Kiawe delivering an entire silo of Moomoo Milk to Mallow. For "A Pinch of This, a Pinch of That!", Chloe utilized Moomoo Milk to prepare whipped cream. While she was retrieving sugar, Bray Zenn appropriated the whipped cream, incorporating it as a "secret ingredient" in his curry. Drake offered hot Moomoo Milk to Ash and Goh in "Looking Out for Number Two!". James's Morpeko was observed consuming Moomoo Milk across the episodes "Bewitch, Battle, and Bewilder!" and "Valor: A Strategic Part of Battling!". "The Rainbow and the Pokémon Master!" featured Ash and Pikachu sharing some Moomoo Milk. In "Found You, Fuecoco!", Roy's grandfather presented a bottle of Moomoo Milk alongside a meal for Liko, Roy, and Friede. Murdock's use of Moomoo Milk to bake donuts for Dot was unveiled in "The Door That Never Opens". During their visit to Olivine City in "Ludlow's Homecoming!", Liko, Roy, Dot, and Ludlow purchased Moomoo Milk for both themselves and their Pokémon.

In the manga chapter "Miltank Melee", Eusine received multiple bottles of Moomoo Milk from the Moomoo Farm. However, Crystal's subsequent surprise appearance caused him to inadvertently drop and shatter every bottle he carried. Cynthia's grandmother is fond of drinking Moomoo Milk bottles at Café Cabin, an affinity first revealed in "Suffering Psyduck". Cassius was observed consuming a bottle of Moomoo Milk in "Overthrowing a Tyrunt".

Within the Pokémon Trading Card Game, an initial Trainer card named "Moo-Moo Milk" debuted in the English Neo Series (Japanese Neo Era) via the Neo Genesis expansion. This card saw reprints in the Pokémon VS expansion and the Expedition Base Set. Subsequently, the card was reissued under the revised name "Moomoo Milk" as a Trainer card (known as an Item card in Japanese) during the English HeartGold & SoulSilver Series (Japanese LEGEND Era), specifically in the HeartGold & SoulSilver expansion. Japanese editions of "Moomoo Milk" have since been reprinted within the Steelix Constructed Standard Deck and the HS Trainer Kit. The artwork for this card underwent modification between its Japanese and English releases within the Neo Genesis set. The initial illustration (also by Tomokazu Komiya), portraying a Sentret suckling from a cow's udder, was altered due to concerns over its potential inappropriateness for the TCG's younger demographic. This same art alteration was then applied to later Japanese reprints, including those in the Pokémon VS and Pokémon-e Starter Deck. When played as "Moo-Moo Milk", the card instructs the player to flip two coins, subsequently removing two damage counters from a Pokémon for each successful heads. The updated "Moomoo Milk" card, conversely, allows for two coin flips, but removes three damage counters from a Pokémon for every heads achieved.

Moomoo Milk has also appeared in a variety of officially licensed merchandise. Notably, a figure from The Pokémon Company International's Delibird Holiday Express line includes a tank car proudly displaying Moomoo Milk branding.

Title: Energy Powder

The medicinal item known as Energy Powder (Japanese: ちからのこな Energy Powder) made its debut in Generation II. Prior to Generation VI, its name was presented as EnergyPowder. This herbal remedy is primarily designed to replenish a Pokémon's Hit Points (HP).

When utilized directly from the Bag on a Pokémon, it provides a restorative effect of 50 HP; however, this comes at the cost of decreasing the Pokémon's friendship. The item is expended after each application. Similarly, when employed from the Bag on a Pokémon, it restores 60 HP, while still incurring a reduction in friendship. Like the previous instance, it is consumed upon use.

A notable textual peculiarity appeared in Pokémon Diamond and Pearl: when pluralized or preceded by an indefinite article, the item's name was rendered with a lowercase 'p' (e.g., "Energypowders" and "an Energypowder"). This display anomaly was subsequently corrected in Pokémon Platinum and Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, where both specific cases properly exhibited a capital 'P', aligning with standard capitalization.

Generations II – III – IV – V – VI – VII – VIII – IX

Title: Energy Root

Debuting in Generation II, the Energy Root (Japanese: ちからのねっこ Energy Root) is a medicinal item. It functions as an herbal remedy designed to restore a Pokémon's Hit Points. When administered to a Pokémon from the Bag, it replenishes 200 HP, yet simultaneously reduces its friendship; the item is consumed after use. When deployed from the Bag on a Pokémon, it provides 120 HP in healing, but this action also lowers the Pokémon's friendship, and the item is likewise consumed following its application.

In the episode A Rush of Ninja Wisdom!, Sanpei offered a pill derived from an Energy Root to Ash after his Froakie had been defeated in battle. The restorative proved instantly effective, despite Froakie's strong dislike for its bitter taste.

The Energy Root was also introduced as a Pokémon Tool Trainer card within the Pokémon Trading Card Game. It was released in the EX Unseen Forces expansion during the English EX Series (corresponding to the Japanese PCG Era). Ryo Ueda provided the illustration for this card, which has never been reprinted. The Pokémon to which this card is attached gains an additional 20 HP, but is unable to utilize any Poké-Powers or Poké-Bodies.

Title: Heal Powder

Making its debut in Generation II, Heal Powder (Japanese: ばんのうごな Cure-all Powder) is classified as a medicinal item. This herbal medicine offers a cure for all non-volatile status conditions and confusion afflicting a Pokémon. When utilized from the Bag on a Pokémon, it effectively eliminates all non-volatile status conditions (including poison, paralysis, sleep, burn, and freeze) along with confusion, though this action results in a decrease in the Pokémon's friendship. The item is expended upon use.

In the anime episode The Brockster Is In!, a vial of Heal Powder was observed among the various items gathered from cruise ship passengers, intended to assist in recuperating the poisoned Pokémon under Normajean's care.

Heal Powder also emerged as a Trainer card within the Pokémon Trading Card Game. Its initial release occurred during the English Neo Series (known in Japan as the Neo Era) as part of the Neo Destiny expansion. This card has never seen a reprint. It showcases an illustration collaboratively created by Ryuta Kusumi and CR CG gangs. The card’s text instructs the player to flip a coin; if the result is heads, one of their Pokémon is no longer Asleep, Confused, Paralyzed, or Poisoned, and 2 damage counters are removed from that Pokémon.

Title: Revival Herb

Known in Japanese as ふっかつそう Revival Herb, the Revival Herb is a medicinal item that made its debut in Generation II. This herbal remedy is specifically designed to bring a fainted Pokémon back to consciousness. Administered from the Bag to an unconscious Pokémon, it not only revives the creature but also fully restores its Hit Points. However, this potent effect comes at the cost of decreasing the Pokémon's friendship. Each use consumes the herb. A notable exception exists in Pokémon Ruby and Sapphire, where its effect is limited to restoring only half of a Pokémon's maximum HP, rather than a complete recovery.

These herbs made their debut in the animated films, specifically White—Victini and Zekrom and Black—Victini and Reshiram. In the latter, Luis, accompanied by his Lillipup, was depicted discovering some; conversely, in a flashback within White—Victini and Zekrom, Luis was shown providing Damon with a small vial of Revival Herb potion. They became central to the plot of Stopping the Rage of Legends! Part 1 and Part 2. Clay stipulated that he would only accept Ash's Gym battle challenge if Ash could procure a supply of Revival Herbs for him. Ash and his companions initially sought them at the Driftveil Market, locating only one preordered specimen, which necessitated a journey to Milos Island. Upon arrival, however, they were dismayed to learn that a drought had caused all the island's Revival Herbs to perish. There, Ash's group encountered Lewis, who recounted an ancient tale involving Tornadus, Thundurus, and Landorus. A past conflict between Tornadus and Thundurus nearly devastated the island until Landorus intervened, subsequently receiving healing from a shrine maiden and her Gothorita using a Revival Herb after sustaining battle injuries. In appreciation, Landorus transformed the island into a lush haven, abundant with Revival Herbs. After Ash and his friends foiled Team Rocket's attempt to capture the forces of nature, mirroring the ancient legend, the three Legendary Pokémon re-established the island's former fertility, enabling Ash to gather ample Revival Herbs for Clay. These vital items were delivered to the Driftveil City Gym Leader at the start of Battling the King of the Mines!, prompting Clay to honor his agreement and commence a Gym battle with Ash for securing the herbs.

A small flask containing Revival Herb potion was entrusted by Anna to Oscar's Stoutland for delivery to Mewtwo in Mewtwo — Prologue to Awakening. Nevertheless, upon locating Mewtwo, the Genetic Pokémon rejected the remedy, declaring its aversion to human assistance. During the events of Searching for a Wish!, Lotus diligently attempted to cultivate Revival Herbs, despite the arid conditions in Capacia Island's mountainous region. Her efforts bore fruit when Jirachi replenished the local water sources, leading to a flourishing growth of the herb crops. Numerous Revival Herbs featured in Currying Favor and Flavor!, where Ash and Mallow gathered them as components for "Akala Curry." Subsequently, they employed a pair of these herbs to restore Totem Lurantis and its ally Castform following Ash's island trial. Intrigued, Litten sampled the herbs, and, akin to Castform, found their bitter flavor disagreeable. In GRRR!! The Irrepairable Gears, Piers utilized a Revival Herb, gifted by Nessa, to revive his Malamar after Eternatus had rendered it unconscious.

In the Pokémon Trading Card Game, a counterpart known as the Life Herb exists, sharing its Japanese name with the Revival Herb. This card premiered as a Trainer card in the English EX Series (corresponding to the Japanese ADV Era) within the EX Hidden Legends set, featuring artwork by Ryo Ueda. While the Life Herb was subsequently re-categorized in Japan as an Item card during the LEGEND Era, its English classification remained unchanged, as the English Trainer card convention for this terminology was adopted only after this card's most recent printing. It has seen reprints in the English EX FireRed & LeafGreen and Platinum expansions, and across several Japanese releases including the Venusaur/Charizard/Blastoise Random Constructed Starter Decks, Metagross Constructed Starter Deck, Gift Box Emerald, and Entry Pack DPt. Its effect permits the player to flip a coin; should the outcome be heads, one of their Pokémon is instantly relieved of all Special Conditions and has 6 damage counters removed. Before the Platinum expansion, this card was restricted from being used on Pokémon-ex.

Within the video games, the visual design of the Revival Herb bears a resemblance to the sundew, itself a plant recognized for its medicinal properties.

Title: Ether

For the character whose Japanese name is Ether, please consult Cherie. The Ether, identified in Japanese as ピーピーエイド (PP Aid), is a medicinal item first introduced in the inaugural Pokémon generation. It replenishes 10 Power Points (PP) for one of a Pokémon's moves. Three more potent versions exist: Max Ether, Elixir, and Max Elixir. When applied to a Pokémon from the Bag, it precisely restores 10 PP to one of its moves. This item also features as a choice within the Wonder Launcher system. Activating it there requires 12 points. In the story "Chipping Away at Regirock," a memorable event occurred: during Emerald's Battle Pyramid challenge, a wild Misdreavus used Grudge, reducing the PP of his borrowed Shedinja's Shadow Ball to zero. To remedy this, Emerald utilized an Ether, which his borrowed Phanpy had previously retrieved, on Shedinja to restore the move. In the Pokémon Trading Card Game, Ether debuted as an Item card within the English Black & White Series (known as the Japanese BW Era), specifically released in the Plasma Storm expansion. This card has never been reprinted. As a Trainer card, its effect permits the player to view the top card of their deck, subsequently allowing them to attach it to one of their Pokémon if it happens to be a basic Energy card.

Title: Max Ether
The Max Ether (Japanese: ピーピーリカバー PP Recover) is a medicinal item first introduced in Generation I. This item's main purpose is to completely restore the PP of one selected move for a Pokémon. It represents an enhanced form of the Ether, while the Max Elixir serves as its superior evolution. Administered directly from the Bag to a Pokémon, it will wholly recharge the Power Points of one of its moves. Its presence extends to the world of Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Rescue Team DX. Within this particular game, a Pokémon consuming it will experience a complete PP restoration for one of its moves. Should it be launched, the target Pokémon gains the exact same benefit as if it were consumed, though it cannot be intercepted. Additionally, an unutilized sprite for a Wonder Launcher item exhibits a coloration similar to the Max Ether. Notably, in Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Rescue Team DX, every wild Abra, once vanquished, is assured to yield a Max Ether.

Title: Elixir

This restorative item, known as the Elixir (ピーピーエイダー PP Aider in Japanese), made its debut as a medicine in Generation I, where it was alternately spelled "Elixer" through Generation II. Its primary function is to replenish 10 PP across every move a Pokémon possesses when utilized from the Bag. Positioned as an enhanced version of the Ether, the Elixir itself is surpassed by a more potent variant, the Max Elixir. The Elixir also features in Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon. Within that game, when a Pokémon consumes it, it completely restores 10 PP for all of its moves and additionally replenishes 5 Belly. Should it be thrown, the targeted Pokémon will experience the identical restorative effect as if it had ingested the item (it is uncatchable); however, a sticky Elixir will inflict 2 damage instead. Furthermore, an unused sprite exists for a Wonder Launcher item, sharing a similar color scheme with the Elixir.

Title: Max Elixir

First introduced in Generation I, the Max Elixir (ピーピーマックス PP Max in Japanese) is a type of medicinal item. Curiously, it was spelled "Max Elixer" in the Generation I and II games. When utilized from the Bag on a Pokémon, this item completely replenishes the Power Points for all of its moves. It functions as an enhanced version of both the Max Ether and the Elixir.

The Max Elixir appears across several Pokémon Mystery Dungeon titles, including Red Rescue Team and Blue Rescue Team, Explorers of Time, Explorers of Darkness and Explorers of Sky, Gates to Infinity, and Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon. In these games, consuming the Max Elixir fully restores a Pokémon's move PP and also grants it 5 Belly. A specific benefit in Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon is its ability to clear the sealed status condition from the Pokémon. Should the item be thrown, it imparts the same restorative effects as if consumed by the target Pokémon (though it cannot be intercepted). If a thrown Max Elixir is sticky, it will additionally inflict 2 damage.

At Spinda's Juice Bar, the Max Elixir can be incorporated into a blended beverage, potentially yielding various outcomes: a medium IQ boost, a new recruitment, an Egg as a gift, or access to an unexplored dungeon. Notably, in Explorers of Sky, it has a Lookalike Item counterpart called the Mix Elixir.

The Max Elixir also made its debut as an Item card within the Pokémon Trading Card Game. It was released during the English XY Series (which corresponds to the Japanese XY Era) as part of the BREAKpoint expansion. A Japanese reprint of this card later appeared in the Premium Trainer Box during the Sun & Moon Era. As a Trainer card, it grants the player the ability to search the top 6 cards of their deck and attach one basic Energy card found there to a Basic Pokémon on their Bench. Furthermore, a Max Elixir is depicted in the artwork for the Yellow A Alternate art print of the Trainers' Mail card, originating from the Roaring Skies expansion.

It's worth noting that the item's Japanese designation, "PP Max", refers to an entirely distinct item in the English localization. In contrast, the English item known as PP Max is called "Point Max" in Japanese. An unused sprite exists for a Wonder Launcher item that bears a color scheme similar to that of the Max Elixir.

Title: Lava Cookie

The Lava Cookie, known in Japanese as フエンせんべい (Huen Rice Cracker), is a medicinal product first introduced in Generation III. This regional specialty from Lavaridge Town offers a cure for all non-volatile status conditions and confusion afflicting a Pokémon. Within the Generation V games, players have the option to sell the Lava Cookie to the gourmet maniac situated on Route 5 for $4000. When utilized from the Bag on a Pokémon, its effect is to remove all non-volatile status conditions—namely poison, paralysis, sleep, burn, and freeze—as well as confusion. In Pokémon: Magikarp Jump, Lava Cookies appear as edible items for Magikarp, becoming available at Trainer Rank 42 and costing 7,564 to unlock. An instance of a Lava Cookie occurred in "The Brockster Is In!", where an elderly passenger offered it to Brock to assist in treating a poisoned Pokémon under Normajean's care. Jessie and Morpeko were observed consuming Lava Cookies in "Battling as Hard as Stone!". In "The Final Battle I", Gold was depicted enjoying some Lava Cookies at the Hoenn Battle Frontier. As this marked his first visit to the Hoenn region, he chose to try its renowned delicacy, finding them utterly delicious. The Lava Cookie has also been represented in various officially licensed merchandise and collectible figures. Visually, Lava Cookies are thought to be inspired by senbei, a traditional Japanese rice cracker, specifically one wrapped with nori (seaweed).

Title: Berry Juice

For the Berry Juice item obtainable at the Juice Shoppe, please refer to the Juice Shoppe article. This item, known in Japanese as きのみジュース (Berry Juice), is a medicinal beverage first appearing in Generation II. It serves as a restorative drink, replenishing a Pokémon's Hit Points. Furthermore, it possesses utility as a held item. Specifically in the Generation V titles, players have the option to sell it to the gourmet enthusiast located on Route 5 for a price of $1500. When held, if the Pokémon's HP drops beneath 50% of its maximum, the Berry Juice will be automatically consumed, restoring 20 HP to the Pokémon. Applied directly from the Bag to a Pokémon, this item will immediately recover 20 HP. Its usage results in its consumption. The episode "A Better Pill to Swallow" depicted Old Man Shuckle, accompanied by his own Shuckle, preparing diverse types of Berry Juice, which possessed varied effects such as alleviating indigestion or drawing Pokémon closer. In "Defending the Homeland!", Clemont referenced his incorporation of Berry Juice into a novel recipe he had recently concocted for his companions. "Flying Pikachu, Rising Higher and Higher!" disclosed that the Shuckle accompanying the Rising Volt Tacklers was responsible for producing Berry Juice for the team's consumption. The illustration on the Shining Fates Morpeko card showcases Berry Juice. Despite being an unobtainable item within the Generation III titles, Berry Juice was nevertheless programmed as a held item for wild Shuckle encountered in Altering Cave in Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen. Furthermore, it was also designated to be held by both Shuckle and Aipom found in Pokémon Emerald's Altering Cave. Nonetheless, these specific Pokémon would only manifest in said location following the utilization of Mystery Gift at a Wonder Spot that distributed the Altering Cave event, an occurrence which ultimately never transpired. Although wild Shuckle do appear in the Safari Zone in Pokémon Emerald once the National Pokédex is acquired, any caught there are found to exclusively hold Oran Berries. Generation II – III – IV – V – VI – VII – VIII – IX

Title: Sacred Ash

The Sacred Ash (Japanese: せいなるはい Sacred Ash) is a form of medicine initially introduced in Generation II, frequently connected with Ho-Oh. This item is exclusively usable outside of battle and is consumed upon activation. When retrieved from the Bag, its powerful effect triggers if at least one Pokémon in the player's party has fainted: it completely restores the HP and PP of every Pokémon in the party. More specifically, it revives all fainted Pokémon and fully replenishes their HP.

In the Pokémon Trading Card Game, Sacred Ash debuted as an Item card during the English XY Series, which corresponds to the Japanese XY Era. It first appeared in the Flashfire expansion, featuring an illustration by PLANETA. This specific Trainer card permits the player to take five Pokémon from their discard pile and shuffle them back into their deck.

Title: Protein

Protein, known as タウリン (Taurine) in Japanese, is a vitamin type that made its debut in Generation I. Its primary function is to enhance a Pokémon's Attack stat. In Pokémon Crystal, Protein was available for $7800 during the Goldenrod Department Store's special rooftop sale. Within the Hoenn region, Protein retailed for $4900 during the Energy Guru's sale event. At Join Avenue's Market, it could be purchased in dozens for $99960, which calculates to $8330 per individual bottle.

When used from the Bag on a Pokémon, Protein bestows 2560 Attack Stat Experience upon the target Pokémon per application, continuing until the Pokémon reaches 25600 Attack Stat Experience. From Generation II onwards, its use also results in a minor increase to the Pokémon's friendship. Protein is consumed upon use and cannot be employed during battles.

Under the Effort Value (EV) system, applying Protein from the Bag grants 10 Attack EVs to the chosen Pokémon per use, with its utility initially restricted until the Pokémon had accumulated 100 Attack EVs. This action also provides a slight boost to the Pokémon's friendship. Similar to earlier mechanics, Protein is expended when used and is not usable in combat. Subsequently, Protein's use was expanded, allowing it to be given to Pokémon already possessing 100 or more Attack EVs, thereby enabling the Attack EVs to be raised up to the single-stat maximum of 252 or the overall total cap of 510 through this method.

Protein also appears in the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, including Red Rescue Team and Blue Rescue Team, Explorers of Time, Explorers of Darkness and Explorers of Sky, Gates to Infinity, and Super Mystery Dungeon. In these games, it acts as a consumable that, when ingested, increases a Pokémon's Attack by 3 and replenishes 5 Belly. If thrown, it delivers the same effect as if consumed by the Pokémon it strikes (it cannot be caught); a sticky Protein will additionally inflict 2 damage. Protein can be blended into a beverage at Spinda's Juice Bar, although this process offers no unique special effects.

In the episode "The Clubsplosion Begins!", a full set of vitamins (excluding PP Up and PP Max) was announced as the prize for winning the Clubsplosion, a situation akin to the set of wings being the prize for the Club Battle. Stephan eventually claimed these items. "Weavile Wobbles But It Won't Fall Down" depicted various kinds of vitamins being sold at the Safari Zone Gate. A collection of keyholders, designed after Protein and the other Vitamins, was released on December 12, 2024, exclusively at the Pokémon Center.

Title: Iron

Should your search pertain to the Beldum given to the player in a trade, bearing the moniker "Iron," refer to the entry Steven Stone → Iron. This item, known as Iron (ブロムヘキシン Bromhexine in Japan), debuted in Generation I as a vitamin category supplement. Its primary function is to enhance a Pokémon's Defense stat. During the Energy Guru's sale event in Hoenn, the cost of an Iron item is $4900. Purchases of Iron at Join Avenue's Market are made in packs of twelve, costing $99960 for each dozen, which works out to $8330 for every bottle. When applied from the Bag to a Pokémon, each use bestows 2560 Defense Stat Experience upon the target, up to a maximum of 25600 Defense Stat Experience. In Generation II, a minor boost to the Pokémon's friendship also occurs with its use. This item is used up upon activation and cannot be utilized during battles.

When utilized from the Bag on a Pokémon, it imparts 10 Defense Effort Values (EVs) to the chosen Pokémon per application, stopping once the Pokémon has accumulated 100 Defense EVs. Furthermore, it slightly enhances the Pokémon's friendship. Like before, the Iron is depleted after use and remains unusable within combat scenarios. More recently, Iron's usage was expanded, now permitting its application even on Pokémon already possessing 100 or more Defense EVs, thereby enabling the Defense EVs to be elevated up to the individual stat limit of 252, or contributing towards the overall EV ceiling of 510.

Iron can be found in the following Pokémon Mystery Dungeon installments: Red Rescue Team and Blue Rescue Team, Explorers of Time, Explorers of Darkness, and Explorers of Sky, Gates to Infinity, and Super Mystery Dungeon. In these games, it is a consumable that, when a Pokémon eats it, boosts its Defense by 3 and replenishes 5 Belly. Throwing the item yields the same effect as consumption for the Pokémon it lands on (it cannot be intercepted); if it has the Sticky status, it will also inflict 2 damage. While Iron is an ingredient that can be blended into drinks at Spinda's Juice Bar, it does not produce any special outcomes.

The episode "The Clubsplosion Begins!" featured the announcement that a comprehensive set of vitamins (omitting PP Up and PP Max) would be awarded to the winner of the Clubsplosion, in a manner parallel to the set of wings being the Club Battle's prize. Stephan eventually emerged as the recipient of these items. Within the episode "Weavile Wobbles But It Won't Fall Down," multiple vitamin varieties were observed for sale at the Safari Zone Gate. On December 12, 2024, a series of keyholders, modeled after Iron and the other Vitamins, was launched. The Pokémon Center held exclusive rights to sell this item.

Title: Carbos

The Carbos, known as インドメタシン (Indometacin) in Japanese, is a vitamin item first introduced in Generation I. Its primary purpose is to enhance a Pokémon's Speed stat. In the Hoenn region, Carbos could be purchased for $4900 during the Energy Guru's special sale. At Join Avenue's Market, it was available in dozens for $99960, which breaks down to $8330 per bottle.

When utilized from the Bag on a Pokémon, it imparts 2560 Speed Stat Experience to the target Pokémon per application, until the Pokémon accumulates 25600 Speed Stat Experience. In Generation II, this item additionally increases the Pokémon's friendship a little. Carbos is consumed upon usage and is not permitted for use during battles.

From Generation III onwards, using Carbos from the Bag adds 10 Speed EVs to the designated Pokémon with each application, up to a limit of 100 Speed EVs for that statistic. It also provides a small boost to the Pokémon's friendship. Similar to previous generations, Carbos is consumed upon use and cannot be employed during combat. Recent game mechanics now allow Carbos to be used on Pokémon that already possess 100 or more Speed EVs, enabling the Speed EVs to be elevated towards the single-stat cap of 252 or the overall EV total of 510 through this method.

Carbos also makes an appearance in Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon. There, it functions as a consumable item that, when imbibed, elevates a Pokémon's Speed by 3 points and restores 5 Belly. If thrown, it will confer the identical effect as consumption to the Pokémon it strikes (though it cannot be caught); should it be sticky, it will additionally inflict 2 damage.

In the anime episode "The Clubsplosion Begins!", a complete set of vitamins (excluding PP Up and PP Max) was announced as the prize for the Clubsplosion tournament, drawing a parallel to the set of wings offered in the Club Battle. Stephan was the eventual winner of these items. Subsequently, in "Weavile Wobbles But It Won't Fall Down," various types of vitamins, including Carbos, were observed being sold at the Safari Zone Gate.

A collection of keyholders, designed in the likeness of Carbos and the other Vitamins, is scheduled for release on December 12, 2024. These will be sold exclusively through the Pokémon Center.

Title: Calcium

Known in Japan as リゾチウム (Lysozyme), Calcium is a vitamin type that made its debut in Generation I. It serves to enhance a Pokémon's Special Attack. In the Hoenn region, Calcium is available for $4900 during the special Energy Guru promotion. At Join Avenue's Market, it is sold in dozens for $99960, meaning each bottle costs $8330. When a Pokémon is given Calcium from the Bag, it gains 2560 Special Stat Experience per use, up to a total of 25600 Special Stat Experience. From Generation II onwards, it also offers a slight boost to the Pokémon's friendship. The item is consumed upon administration and cannot be deployed in combat.

When utilized from the Bag, Calcium bestows 10 Special Attack Effort Values (EVs) upon the chosen Pokémon per application, until the Pokémon reaches 100 Special Attack EVs. Additionally, it provides a minor increase to the Pokémon's friendship. Calcium is used up when applied and cannot be engaged during battle. It is now permissible to use Calcium even on a Pokémon that already possesses 100 or more Special Attack EVs, thereby allowing its Special Attack EVs to be increased to the single-stat cap of 252 or the overall EV total of 510 through this method. This particular change raises the usage limit to a maximum of 26 Calciums on a single Pokémon.

Calcium features in several Pokémon Mystery Dungeon titles: Red Rescue Team and Blue Rescue Team, Explorers of Time, Explorers of Darkness, and Explorers of Sky, Gates to Infinity, and Super Mystery Dungeon. In these games, it functions as a consumable item that, upon ingestion, raises a Pokémon's Special Attack by 3 and restores 5 Belly. If the item is thrown, it imparts the same effect as consumption to the Pokémon it strikes (though it cannot be caught); a sticky Calcium will also inflict 2 damage. Calcium can be blended into a drink at Spinda's Juice Bar, though this process yields no distinct special effects.

The anime saw Calcium's debut in "The Joy of Pokémon," an episode where Nurse Joy presented a giant Magikarp with an entire bottle of these tablets. In "The Clubsplosion Begins!," a complete assortment of vitamins (excluding PP Up and PP Max) was declared the grand prize for the Clubsplosion winner, drawing a parallel to the set of wings offered as the prize for the Club Battle. Stephan eventually emerged as the recipient of these items. "Weavile Wobbles But It Won't Fall Down" showcased various kinds of vitamins being sold at the Safari Zone Gate. A collection of keyholders, designed to resemble Calcium and the other Vitamins, has been issued, with a release date of December 12, 2024. This merchandise was exclusively available through the Pokémon Center.

Title: Rare Candy

First appearing in Generation I, the item known as Rare Candy (ふしぎなアメ, or Mystery Candy, in Japanese) belongs to the "Candy" category. Across the English instruction manuals for Generation I, II, and III titles, Rare Candy was categorized with vitamins and various battle items within the overarching "Pokémon Power-Ups" section. During the Generation V era of games, players could exchange a Rare Candy for a sum of $10000 by selling it to the gourmet enthusiast located on Route 5. Utilizing this item from the Bag on a chosen Pokémon causes its level to increment by 1, with an upper limit of level 100. Upon achieving a new level through this method, the Pokémon's experience points will reset to the lowest amount necessary for its current level. Its application is restricted to times when not engaged in combat. A Pokémon gaining a level via a Rare Candy will acquire any moves learned at that specific level and will undergo evolution if it meets the criteria for level-up evolution. An exception exists in Pokémon Stadium, where Pokémon advanced in level by a Rare Candy do not learn new moves or evolve (though this functionality was restored in Pokémon Stadium 2). Should a Rare Candy be employed on a fainted Pokémon, that Pokémon will be revived. If a Rare Candy both revives and levels up a Pokémon, its health will be restored to either 2 HP, or it will match the increase in its maximum HP (with the notable exception of Shedinja, which consistently revived with its full 1 HP before Generation VI). Furthermore, this item contributes a minor increase to the Pokémon's friendship value. Applying a Rare Candy to a fainted Shedinja no longer has the effect of reviving it from its incapacitated state. When a Rare Candy is consumed by a level 100 Pokémon that possesses the capability to evolve upon leveling (encompassing methods such as reaching a specific level, having high friendship, or learning a particular move, among others) and currently fulfills its evolutionary prerequisites, the Pokémon will evolve without any level increase; however, fainted Pokémon will not experience revival through this particular evolution method. A player has the option to halt this evolution process by pressing the B Button, though doing so results in the forfeiture of the Rare Candy. Should a player try to apply a Rare Candy to a level 100 Pokémon holding an Everstone, the evolution will be prevented, and the Rare Candy will not be used up. Tandemaus stands as an exception, as it cannot evolve through the application of a Rare Candy, requiring instead to level up specifically during battle. Within the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon franchise, Joy Seeds serve as the functional counterpart to Rare Candies. Its debut in Pokémon GO occurred on June 22, 2017. Players in Pokémon GO can apply Rare Candies to any Pokémon they own, converting a selected quantity of these items into standard Candy specific to that Pokémon's evolutionary lineage. December 2020 saw the introduction of Candy XL, enabling Pokémon to exceed Lv. 40 in power. Concurrently, Rare Candy XL was released, fulfilling an identical role to regular Rare Candy but for the purpose of Candy XL. Any repeatable acquisition methods mentioned are typically random and lack guarantees, unless explicitly stated otherwise. The item made an appearance in the episode titled "Caterpie's Big Dilemma." Diverging from its game mechanics, where it elevates a Pokémon's level, the episode depicted a Rare Candy causing Pokémon to grow to gigantic proportions (occasionally rendering them uncontrollable).
