This deviation was attributed to it being a specially altered, experimental variant, which Dr. Gordon labeled "Mystery Candy Complete." Brock commented that the authentic item would simply "enhance a Pokémon's abilities." Additionally, an oversight in the dubbing process led to the Rare Candy being called "Mystery Candy," a direct translation of its Japanese nomenclature. A number of Rare Candies were briefly visible within N's room during a flashback sequence in "The Plan." In the narrative "Innocent Scientist," Blake disclosed that he had provided his Dewott with a Rare Candy and a Resist Wing prior to engaging Genesect in combat, thereby marginally bolstering its defenses sufficiently to withstand an Electric-type Techno Blast. The Rare Candy debuted as a Trainer card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game within the English EX Series (corresponding to the Japanese ADV Era) as part of the EX Sandstorm expansion. Subsequently, its classification changed to an Item card in Japanese during the LEGEND Era, with an equivalent reclassification occurring in the English Black & White Series (since this category was not present beforehand). Numerous reprints have occurred, including in the English HeartGold & SoulSilver Series (Japanese LEGEND Era), English Black & White Series (Japanese BW Era), English XY Series (Japanese XY Era), English Sun & Moon Series (Japanese Sun & Moon Era), English Sword & Shield Series (Japanese Sword & Shield Era), and the English Scarlet & Violet Series (Japanese Scarlet & Violet Era). Before the Black & White release, the card permitted players to evolve a Basic Pokémon directly into either a Stage 1 or Stage 2 Pokémon from their hand. Following the debut of Black & White, an erratum was published for the card, aligning its effect with that of Pokémon Breeder from the Base Set, thereby enabling a player to evolve a Basic Pokémon straight into a Stage 2 Pokémon from their hand. This revised effect first appeared in print in the Dark Explorers expansion. A Rare Candy advertisement, featuring the directive "Look for it at your local supermarket!", was present on page 3 of Pokémon Power #1 magazine. Nonetheless, at the time of the magazine's publication, this particular item was not available for purchase in any Generation I game. During Generations I and II, Pokémon that reached level 101 or beyond (achievable exclusively through glitch exploitation) could be further leveled using Rare Candies up to level 255; applying a Rare Candy to a level 255 Pokémon would cause its level to loop back to 0. (Rare Candies are unusable on level 0 Pokémon.) Junichi Masuda, in an interview, suggested that while humans could potentially ingest Rare Candies, they would likely find them unpalatable, comparing the experience to consuming dog food in reality. Generation VIII also saw the introduction of Exp. Candy, an item that grants Pokémon predetermined quantities of experience. Generally, Rare Candies tend to be less efficient than Exp. Candy, as their effect is limited to providing just enough experience for the next level. However, they can become the most efficient candy type if a Pokémon needs over 30,000 experience points at its current level to advance (a scenario exclusively found among Pokémon belonging to the Medium Slow, Slow, or Fluctuating experience groups).

Title: Zinc

A vitamin item first appearing in Generation III, Zinc (Japanese: キトサン Chitosan) serves to enhance a Pokémon's Special Defense. In the Hoenn region, a single Zinc could be acquired for $4900 during an Energy Guru promotional event. Join Avenue's Market offered Zinc in packs of twelve for $99960, which breaks down to $8330 per bottle. When utilized from the Bag on a Pokémon, each application bestows 10 Special Defense EVs upon the target, continuing until it reaches 100 Special Defense EVs in that stat. Additionally, it provides a slight increase to the Pokémon's friendship. Zinc is consumed upon use and cannot be employed during battles. Recent updates allow Zinc to be used even on Pokémon already possessing 100 or more Special Defense EVs, enabling the Special Defense EVs to be raised to the individual stat limit of 252 or contribute towards the total EV cap of 510.

Zinc is featured in several Pokémon Mystery Dungeon titles: Red Rescue Team and Blue Rescue Team, Explorers of Time, Explorers of Darkness, and Explorers of Sky, Gates to Infinity, and Super Mystery Dungeon. In these games, prior to Gates to Infinity, this consumable would boost a Pokémon's Special Defense by 3 and restore 5 Belly points when ingested. A Special Defense stat already at 255 means it cannot be increased further. Throwing Zinc yields the identical effect as consumption for the Pokémon it strikes (and it cannot be caught); if the item is sticky, it also inflicts 2 damage.

Zinc can be blended into beverages at Spinda's Juice Bar, though it does not impart any unique effects when prepared this way. In the anime episode "The Clubsplosion Begins!", it was announced that the victor of the Clubsplosion tournament would receive a full collection of vitamins (excluding PP Up and PP Max), analogous to the set of wings awarded in the Club Battle. Stephan ultimately won these items. "Weavile Wobbles But It Won't Fall Down" depicted various kinds of vitamins being sold at the Safari Zone Gate. A range of keyholders, designed after Zinc and the other Vitamins, was released on December 12, 2024, as a Pokémon Center exclusive.

Title: Old Gateau

This medicinal item, known as the Old Gateau (Japanese: もりのヨウカン Forest Yōkan), made its debut in Generation IV. It originates as a regional delicacy from the Old Chateau and effectively remedies a Pokémon from all non-volatile status conditions and confusion. The Pokémon the Series: Diamond and Pearl episode "Get Your Rotom Running!" additionally refers to it as the Mirage Gateau.

Within Generation V titles, players have the option to sell an Old Gateau to the gourmet maniac located on Route 5 for $4000. When deployed from the Bag onto a Pokémon, it offers a complete cure for all non-volatile status conditions (poison, paralysis, sleep, burn, freeze) and confusion. In Pokémon: Magikarp Jump, Old Gateaux are present as a food item suitable for Magikarp consumption. These are made available upon reaching Trainer Rank 73, with an unlock price of 429,910.

Its inaugural appearance occurred in "Get Your Rotom Running!", an episode featuring Ash and his companions visiting the Old Chateau to sample the renowned confection, a suggestion made by Professor Oak. In "Beating the Bustle and Hustle!", James noted the inclusion of Old Gateau and Lum Berries on the menu of the Sinnoh Pokémon Hustle Refreshment Room. A single piece of Old Gateau was observed in "Getting a Jump on the Competition!". Furthermore, James's Morpeko was depicted consuming Old Gateaux in both "The Fiery Road to Mastership!" and "Valor: A Strategic Part of Battling!".

During their visit to the Old Chateau, as illustrated in "Robust Rotom", Diamond presented Old Gateau to Platinum for their tea time. Professor Rowan was portrayed eating some Old Gateau in "The Final Dimensional Duel XI". A miniature Old Gateau, along with its packaging, formed part of the Sinnoh goods set within the Pokémon World Market merchandise line, produced by Re-Ment and offered exclusively at the Pokémon Center. Additionally, the Munchlax figure from The Pokémon Company International's Delicious Adventure line is depicted clutching an Old Gateau. Separately, actual edible Old Gateaux (Yōkan) were distributed in 2017 and 2021, available solely through the Pokémon Center.

The designation "Old Gateau," in both its English and Japanese renditions, embodies a wordplay rooted in its discovery location. Regarding the English version, the French word "gâteau" (meaning "cake") creates a pun with "château." In the Japanese context, the Chateau bears the name "Forest Manor" (Japanese: もりのようかん), whereas the Gateau is termed "Forest Yōkan" (Japanese: もりのヨウカン); these two distinct expressions function as homophones, both pronounced "mori no yōkan." Translator Nob Ogasawara formulated the English names "Old Chateau" and "Old Gateau" with the specific intent of retaining the original Japanese text's pun. Nob stated that his capacity to contribute more significantly to the localization process from Generation III onwards was a factor in successfully preserving the Old Chateau/Old Gateau pun. The French and Italian localizations also feature a linguistic play connecting "gâteau" and "château," though it remains undetermined whether these iterations were influenced by the English translation or developed independently.

Within the game environment, the singular term "Old Gateau" is typically pluralized as "Old Gateaux." Nonetheless, the text for the Souvenir Set within Join Avenue explicitly states: "A set of two Old Gateaus. The cake heals all the status problems of one Pokémon."

Title: Dire Hit

The Dire Hit, a battle item introduced in Generation I, is known in Japanese as クリティカット (Criticut). Prior to Generation V and in Hisui, its Japanese name was クリティカッター (Criticutter). This item temporarily enhances a Pokémon's critical hit ratio during combat. At Join Avenue's Market, consumers can purchase Dire Hits in dozens, with each dozen costing $5850, which breaks down to $487.5 per individual item.

This item effectively mimics the influence of the move Focus Energy, a move whose mechanics have evolved across various generations. Importantly, Dire Hit does not stack with Focus Energy or with additional Dire Hits. A known glitch occurs when the item is consumed from the Bag during a battle: the Pokémon's critical hit chance is paradoxically reduced by 75% for the duration of that specific battle.

When used from the Bag during battle, the Dire Hit typically elevates a Pokémon's critical hit ratio by one stage while the Pokémon remains in battle, and it also marginally increases its friendship. This specific effect boosts the default critical hit probability from 17/256 to 1/8. Alternatively, a Dire Hit can boost the Pokémon's critical hit ratio by two stages during battle, again leading to a slight increase in friendship. This two-stage enhancement alters the standard critical hit odds as follows: from 1/16 to 1/4 in Generations III-V, from 1/16 to 1/2 in Generation VI, or from 1/24 to 1/2 in Generation VII and subsequent games.

The Dire Hit manifests as three distinct Wonder Launcher items, each capable of increasing the critical hit ratio by 1, 2, or 3 stages, respectively. These cost 3, 5, or 7 points to deploy. In the context of Pokémon Masters EX, Dire Hits are exclusively usable by specific sync pairs that possess them as an item move. Furthermore, several variant Dire Hit item moves are accessible to particular sync pairs. All such item moves universally require 2 MP to use. A comprehensive directory of all sync pairs capable of acquiring Dire Hit, Dire Hit +, Dire Hit All, or Dire Hit All + can be accessed at a specified location.

In Generation V, a boy situated in Nacrene City notes that the item is referred to as Dire Hit within the Unova region. This remark alludes to the alteration of the item's Japanese name from クリティカッター (Criticutter) to クリティカット (Criticut), a change implemented to accommodate the numeric level distinctions for Wonder Launcher items. This particular concern regarding character limits did not affect the translated English games, as "Dire Hit" is considerably shorter than the 12-character maximum for English item names.

Title: X Attack

The X Attack (Japanese: プラスパワー Plus Power), a battle item, was first introduced in Generation I. It provides a temporary boost to a Pokémon's Attack stat. In Join Avenue's Market, X Attacks are sold in packs of twelve for $4500, equating to a unit price of $375 per item. When activated from the Bag during battle, it elevates the Attack of the Pokémon currently engaged in combat by one stage. Starting with Generation II, it also provides a slight increase to the Pokémon's friendship. Separately, when deployed from the Bag mid-battle, it boosts the Attack of the active Pokémon by two stages. It also confers a small increase in friendship. As a Wonder Launcher item, the X Attack is available in four distinct forms, offering Attack stat enhancements of 1, 2, 3, or 6 levels. Activating these variations costs 3, 5, 7, or 11 points, respectively.

In Super Smash Bros. for Nintendo 3DS and Wii U, the X Attack, alongside X Defense and X Speed, functions as equipment usable by any of the five playable Pokémon. This item increases the bearer's Attack stat while simultaneously reducing their Defense. While some versions may possess a special effect, those without such effects are categorized into three rarity tiers: normal, Threefold, and Sixfold. After rescuing a distressed Mii in an area, there's a chance they might offer X Attacks (among other items like X Defenses, X Speeds, or Potions). The specific item provided is determined randomly upon their rescue. The X Attack provides its Attack stat increase for a limited duration.

Within the Pokémon Trading Card Game and Pokémon Trading Card Game 2: The Invasion of Team GR!, the PlusPower card can be found. In Pokémon Masters EX, X Attacks are usable exclusively by specific sync pairs who have them as an item move. Furthermore, a specialized version known as X Attack All is also available to particular sync pairs. Each of these item moves requires 2 MP. An X Attack elevates only the user's Attack, whereas an X Attack All boosts the Attack of all allied Pokémon. A comprehensive roster of sync pairs capable of learning either X Attack or X Attack All is available here.

Within Pokémon UNITE, the X Attack is a battle item that becomes accessible once a Trainer reaches Level 7. For a duration of 7 seconds, it augments the player's Pokémon's Attack and Special Attack by 20%, and its basic Attack Speed by 25%. This item has a 40-second cooldown period.

In "Movie Time! Zorua in The Legend of the Pokémon Knight!", a Wonder Launcher X Attack was seen as an element of the film "Wonder Fighters the Movie Part 24: The Mystery of the Wonder Launcher". In an advertisement segment observed by Ash, Iris, and Cilan, two Trainers engaged in combat with a Golurk and an Escavalier, employing Wonder Launchers to empower their Pokémon. Notably, the Wonder Fighter character applied an X Attack to her Golurk. An X Attack was noted among the counterfeit goods that Green attempted to sell to Red in "Wartortle Wars."

The English version of X Attack carries the direct translation of its Japanese designation, PlusPower. It debuted as a Trainer card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game's English Original Series (also known as the Japanese Original Era), within the Base Set expansion, featuring art by Keiji Kinebuchi. This card was reissued in the Base Set 2 expansion. It saw subsequent reissues in the Japanese Tamamushi City Gym and Yamabuki City Gym theme decks, and also the Chikorita Side Deck from the Japanese Intro Pack Neo. It was later reissued with revised text and fresh artwork by Ryo Ueda as part of the Diamond & Pearl expansion, initially launched in the Japanese Space-Time Creation expansion. A crucial distinction between the original and this version lay in the timing of its effect: the initial print's boost was applied after factoring in Weakness and Resistance, whereas the newer print's effect took place prior to these calculations.

This specific print was reissued in the Secret Wonders expansion, first appearing in the Japanese Dialga LV.X and Palkia LV.X Constructed Standard Decks, both half decks of the Japanese Magmortar vs Electivire Deck Kit, and the Infernape Half Deck of the Japanese Entry Pack '08. Another reprinting occurred in the Platinum expansion, initially released as part of the Giratina Half Deck in the Japanese Entry Pack DPt. PlusPower's classification in Japan shifted to an Item card during the LEGEND Era, a change not reflected in English prints until the Black & White expansion's debut. It saw a reprint in the Unleashed expansion, showcasing new artwork by Hideaki Hakozaki, and first appeared in the Raichu Starter Deck of the Japanese Battle Starter Decks. This edition altered the phrase "Active Pokémon" in its second paragraph to "Defending Pokémon," intending to clarify the card's target. However, it later underwent errata to revert to the phraseology originally found in the Diamond & Pearl print. Subsequently, it was reprinted in the Japanese Reviving Legends expansion, again with artwork by Ryo Ueda. This particular print was subsequently incorporated into the Black & White expansion, having first been issued in the Japanese White Collection expansion with new artwork by 5ban Graphics. In Japan, it was later reissued as a BW-P Promotional card, distinguished by a commemorative stamp, and presented to individuals who took part in the Summer Carnival Cup during the Summer Carnival from July 23 to August 28. Further reprints included the Japanese Master Deck Build Box EX and Everyone's Exciting Battle. PlusPower causes the Pokémon it is affixed to to inflict an additional 10 damage points upon the Defending Pokémon during the current turn.

The X Attack was featured as a Trainer card in the inaugural Pokémon Trading Figure Game expansion, Next Quest. It boosts the damage dealt by a single Pokémon by 30 during the current turn. Its artwork is identical to that found on the PlusPower Trainer card from the TCG's Base Set.

Title: X Defense

The X Defense, a battle item first introduced in Generation I, was previously known as X Defend until Generation VI. In Japanese, it is called ディフェンダー (Defender). Its primary function is to temporarily enhance a Pokémon's Defense stat.

Within Join Avenue's Market, X Defend items can be acquired in groups of twelve for $4950 per dozen, which calculates to a unit price of $412.5 for each X Defend. When deployed from the Bag mid-battle, it boosts the Defense of the active Pokémon by 1 level. From Generation II onwards, this item also confers a slight increase in the Pokémon's friendship. Later, its effect when utilized from the Bag during a confrontation was noted as increasing a Pokémon's Defense by 2 levels, additionally offering a modest boost to its friendship.

X Defend also features as one of four distinct Wonder Launcher items, offering increases to the Defense stat by 1, 2, 3, or 6 levels. These respective boosts consume 3, 5, 7, or 11 points.

In Super Smash Bros. for Nintendo 3DS/Wii U, X Defense (alongside X Attack and X Speed) functions as an equipment piece usable by any of the five playable Pokémon. This item elevates the holder's Defense attribute while simultaneously decreasing their Speed. Although some versions may possess a special effect, those without such effects come in three distinct rarities: normal, Threefold, and Sixfold. When a distressed Mii is successfully rescued in an area, they might occasionally present X Defenses (among other items like X Attacks, X Speeds, or Potions), with the specific item received being randomly determined upon their rescue. Generally, the X Defense provides a Defense stat increase for a sustained period.

The Defender card is a component of the Pokémon Trading Card Game and its sequel, Pokémon Trading Card Game 2: The Invasion of Team GR!. In Pokémon Masters EX, X Defenses are exclusively available to certain sync pairs who possess them as an item move. A variant, X Defense All, is also accessible to specific sync pairs. Both of these item moves possess an MP cost of 2. An X Defense exclusively raises the user's Defense, whereas an X Defense All enhances the Defense of all allied Pokémon. A comprehensive roster of sync pairs capable of learning X Defense or X Defense All can be referenced here. Furthermore, an advanced version named X Defense All + exists, which similarly has 2 MP and elevates the Defense of all allies by 3 stat ranks. This particular item move is uniquely employed by Lillie & Prinplup during Victory Road.

The English localization of X Defense directly translates its Japanese name, Defender. The Defender card made its initial appearance in Trainer Deck A, predating the official launch of the Base Set. This particular printing is identifiable by its red border and "Trainer Deck A" emblazoned on the reverse side. The card was subsequently included in the Base Set, featuring artwork by Keiji Kinebuchi, and was first released within the Japanese Expansion Pack. It saw a reprint in the Base Set 2 expansion. Additional reprints occurred in the Japanese Hanada, Kuchiba, Tamamushi, and Yamabuki City Gym theme decks, as well as the Totodile Half Deck from the Japanese Intro Pack Neo. A later reprint of the card was featured in the Undaunted expansion, showcasing new artwork by Ryo Ueda, and initially released in the Japanese Reviving Legends expansion. Concurrently with the Black and White series, this card underwent an erratum, altering the text from "by an opponent's attack" to simply "by attacks." The Defender card serves to mitigate damage inflicted upon the Pokémon it is attached to by attacks, reducing it by 20 during the opponent's subsequent turn.

Title: X Speed

An X Speed (スピーダー in Japanese, transliterated as Speeder) is a combat consumable first appearing in the inaugural Generation of Pokémon games. Its function is to provide a temporary enhancement to a Pokémon's Speed attribute. Within the Market district of Join Avenue, X Speed can be acquired in bundles of twelve for $3150 per dozen, effectively costing $262.5 for a single unit. Activating an X Speed from the Bag while engaged in combat results in the active Pokémon's Speed being increased by one stage. Starting from Generation II and subsequent titles, using it also slightly improves the Pokémon's friendship. When deployed from the Bag during a confrontation, it elevates the Speed of the current battling Pokémon by two stages. Furthermore, it contributes a small amount to the Pokémon's friendship. Within the Wonder Launcher mechanic, X Speed manifests as four distinct items, capable of augmenting the Speed stat by either 1, 2, 3, or 6 stages. Each variant demands 3, 5, 7, or 11 points for activation, in corresponding order.

In Super Smash Bros. for Nintendo 3DS/Wii U, X Speed is present as a piece of equipment, alongside X Attack and X Defense, usable by any of the five designated playable Pokémon characters. This equipment boosts the wielder's Speed attribute at the cost of reducing their Attack. Though it may sometimes possess a unique secondary effect, the versions devoid of such effects are classified into three levels of rarity: normal, Threefold, and Sixfold. Upon successfully aiding a troubled Mii character within an area, they will periodically employ X Speeds, among other items like X Attacks, X Defenses, or Potions. The specific item distributed is determined by chance at the moment of their rescue. The X Speed provides a sustained enhancement to the Speed statistic.

Within Pokémon Masters EX, X Speeds are exclusively available for use by specific sync pairs who possess them as an 'item move' in their skillset. Furthermore, various alternative forms of X Speed are accessible to particular sync pairs. All iterations of X Speed, including its standard form, consume 2 MP, with the sole exception of EX Speed, which requires only 1 MP. A comprehensive compilation detailing all sync pairs capable of learning X Speed, X Speed +, EX Speed, X Speed All, or X Speed All + is available via this reference. Shifting to Pokémon UNITE, the X Speed functions as a battle item, becoming available once a Trainer reaches Level 8. It augments the user's Pokémon's locomotion speed by 45% and grants immunity to movement speed debuffs for a duration of 7 seconds. A 50-second cooldown period applies after its activation. Within Pokémon Trading Card Game Pocket, X Speed is represented as an Item card, permitting the reduction of an Active Pokémon's Retreat Cost for the span of one turn.

During Movie Time! Zorua in "The Legend of the Pokémon Knight"!, a Wonder Launcher X Speed made an appearance within a film titled Wonder Fighters the Movie Part 24: The Mystery of the Wonder Launcher. In the promotional segment viewed by Ash, Iris, and Cilan, two Trainers engaged in combat with a Golurk and an Escavalier. Both utilized Wonder Launchers to enhance their Pokémon, specifically showing the Wonder Fighter's adversary applying an X Speed to his Escavalier.

Title: X Accuracy

Debuting in Generation I, the X Accuracy (known in Japanese as ヨクアタール, meaning "Hits Well") is a specific variety of battle item. Its primary function is to temporarily enhance a Pokémon's accuracy. Merchants at Join Avenue's Market offer X Accuracy for purchase in bundles of twelve, with each dozen priced at $8550, which calculates to $712.5 per individual X Accuracy.

When deployed from the Bag during a skirmish, it enables the Pokémon's offensive maneuvers to bypass standard accuracy checks. This also ensures that one-hit knockout moves connect successfully, though they will still be ineffective if the adversary possesses superior Speed (in Generation I) or a higher level (in Generation II) than the user.

In a distinct application from the Bag during battle, it augments the active Pokémon's accuracy by 1 stage. This action also confers a minor boost to the Pokémon's friendship. However, under this effect, one-hit knockout moves are no longer impacted by X Accuracy.

In yet another instance of its use from the Bag during combat, it elevates the active Pokémon's accuracy by 2 stages. Similarly, this also contributes a small amount to the Pokémon's friendship. Again, one-hit knockout attacks remain unaffected by X Accuracy in this scenario.

The X Accuracy is represented as four distinct items within the Wonder Launcher system, providing Accuracy increases of 1, 2, 3, or 6 stages. Activating these effects costs 3, 5, 7, or 11 points, respectively.

As a Trainer card, X Accuracy was introduced in the Next Quest expansion. When played, if the designated Pokémon's spin results in a Miss during that battle turn, it is allowed to spin again.

In the game Pokémon Duel, X Accuracy is implemented as a Plate. Utilizing it causes a Pokémon that spins a Miss during that battle turn to advance to the subsequent wheel segment.

Within Pokémon Masters EX, only certain sync pairs who feature X Accuracies as an item move are able to employ them. Additionally, a variant called X Accuracy All is also available to specific sync pairs. Both of these item moves consume 2 MP. An X Accuracy exclusively improves the user's accuracy, whereas an X Accuracy All boosts the accuracy of all allied combatants. A comprehensive list of all sync pairs capable of acquiring either X Accuracy or X Accuracy All is accessible here.

Finally, in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, issuing the "Call" command to an awake, non-Shadow Pokémon fulfills the identical in-battle role as an X Accuracy.

Title: Fluffy Tail

Debuting in Generation III, the item known as the Fluffy Tail (Japanese: エネコのシッポ Eneco's Tail) serves primarily to facilitate immediate disengagement from encounters with wild Pokémon. When deployed from the Bag mid-battle against a wild Pokémon, it ensures the player's instant escape, overriding any conditions that would typically hinder such an attempt. However, in the specific context of Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, players are unable to employ it to flee from wild Pokémon found within Poké Spots. Its functionality differs significantly within Pokémon UNITE, where the Fluffy Tail applies a negative status effect, or debuff, to a wild Pokémon for a duration of 3s. This debuff both incapacitates the affected Pokémon, preventing attacks, and causes direct damage dealt to them to activate a supplementary hit. This extra hit deals damage calculated as 100% of Attack + 60% of Sp. Attack + 10 * (Level - 1) + 100, and is subject to a 0.5s cooldown period before it can occur again. Beyond its in-game applications, the Fluffy Tail also appears visually in the artwork for the illustration rare version of the Litten card from the Temporal Forces expansion.

Title: Blue Flute

Also recognized by its Japanese name, あおいビードロ (Blue Vidro), the Blue Flute is a medicinal item that debuted in the third generation of games. It is categorized as one of the various Flute items. Within the Generation V titles, this particular flute can be divested to the wealthy enthusiast in Undella Town, fetching a price of $7000. Its function is described as waking a sleeping Pokémon when utilized, whether on the field or in battle. More specifically, its application in combat will indeed rouse a slumbering Pokémon. However, it is crucial to note that the Blue Flute is not usable in any capacity outside of battle. As a result, it holds no practical utility beyond its sale value. In the episode "Serving Up the Flute Cup!", a Blue Flute was featured among the collection of flutes designated for the victor of the Battle Frontier Flute Cup, an honor ultimately achieved by Ash. Alongside the Red and Yellow Flutes, the Blue Flute was reassigned to the medicine pocket in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, signifying its placement in three distinct bag compartments across three generations of games.

Title: Yellow Flute

Debuting as a medicinal item in Generation III, the Yellow Flute (Japanese: きいろビードロ Yellow Vidro) belongs to the category of flutes. During combat, this item can cure a Pokémon's confusion. Outside of its direct battle application, its most notable function is its saleability: in the Generation V titles, players may offload it to the Undella Town billionaire maniac for a price of $7500. The anime episode Serving Up the Flute Cup! featured a Yellow Flute as part of the prize collection awarded to the victor of the Battle Frontier Flute Cup, a competition ultimately claimed by Ash. Its storage location within the player's Bag has shifted significantly; in Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, the Yellow Flute, alongside the Blue and Red Flutes, was relocated to the medicine pocket. This change signifies its placement in three distinct bag pockets across three generations. Notably, in Generation III, it stands alone among the flutes as the only one acquireable beyond the Hoenn region without the need for trading.

Title: Red Flute

This item, known in Japanese as あかいビードロ (Red Vidro), is a medicinal tool that made its debut in the third generation of games. It belongs to the category of "flutes." Within the Generation V titles, players have the option to exchange the Red Flute for $7500 by selling it to the eccentric billionaire residing in Undella Town. Should a Pokémon become infatuated during combat, deploying this item will immediately cure that status condition. Apart from being sold, the Red Flute has no other practical function. In combat scenarios, it is again capable of curing a Pokémon afflicted by infatuation. During the episode "Serving Up the Flute Cup!", a Red Flute was featured as part of the collection of flutes offered as a prize for the victor of the Battle Frontier Flute Cup, an event ultimately claimed by Ash. Mimey, specifically, was observed utilizing the Red Flute musically as the episode concluded. In *Omega Ruby* and *Alpha Sapphire*, this item – alongside the Blue Flute and Yellow Flute – experienced a relocation to the medicine pocket within the player's bag, signifying that it has occupied distinct bag compartments across three successive generations.

Title: Black Flute

Debuting in Generation III, the Black Flute (くろいビードロ Black Vidro in Japanese) is a particular class of item, counted among the various flutes. Within the Generation V titles, this item can be traded to the wealthy enthusiast in Undella Town for a sum of $8000. When activated in the field, it reduces the likelihood of encountering wild Pokémon by 50%; this specific effect continues until a transition to a different map occurs. However, aside from its selling price, the Black Flute possesses no other practical utility. Conversely, when engaged in the field, it causes wild Pokémon to emerge at levels augmented by 1 to 4. This particular change in wild Pokémon levels dissipates as soon as the player exits the current area. In the anime episode Serving Up the Flute Cup!, a Black Flute was part of the assortment of flutes promised as the reward for the Battle Frontier Flute Cup, which Ash ultimately secured.

Title: White Flute

Originating in Generation III, the White Flute (known in Japanese as しろいビードロ, or White Vidro) is a member of the flute item category. Within the Generation V games, this item can be sold to the eccentric billionaire residing in Undella Town, fetching a price of $8000. When utilized in the field, it significantly boosts the encounter rate for wild Pokémon by 50%. This amplified frequency remains active until the player transitions to a different map area. Apart from its temporary in-field effects, its primary practical utility ultimately lies in its resale value. Additionally, deploying it in the field causes wild Pokémon to appear 1 to 4 levels lower than usual. This level-reduction effect dissipates once the player departs from the current location. In the anime installment "Serving Up the Flute Cup!", a White Flute was included among the promised prizes for the champion of the Battle Frontier Flute Cup, a competition ultimately won by Ash. Notably, in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, an uncommon phenomenon allows for the discovery of wild Level 1 Pokémon by employing the White Flute in areas where Pokémon typically spawn at Level 5 or lower. The specific species that can be encountered at Level 1 through this method are: Tentacool, Geodude, Goldeen, Magikarp, Poochyena, Zigzagoon, Wurmple, Lotad, Seedot, Taillow, Wingull, Ralts, Surskit, Shroomish, Slakoth, Nincada, Whismur, Skitty, Shellos, Chatot, Lilliput, Pidove, Tympole, Sewaddle, Zorua, and Gothita.

Title: Shoal Salt

An item first appearing in Generation III, the Shoal Salt (Japanese: あさせのしお Shoal Salt) is known for its role in crafting. Within the Hoenn region, it serves as a component for creating Shell Bells. More specifically, trainers can present four units of Shoal Salt along with four Shoal Shells to an old man residing within Shoal Cave in Hoenn to receive a Shell Bell. Beyond its crafting utility, Shoal Salt also holds monetary value in the Generation V games; it can be liquidated for $7000 when sold to the gourmet maniac found on Route 5. A notable misunderstanding arose in Generation IV, fueled by an error in the *Pokémon Diamond and Pearl: Official Nintendo Player's Guide*, suggesting that Shoal Salt itself possessed effects akin to the Leftovers item. This, however, was incorrect; the item that offers a comparable healing effect to Leftovers is the Shell Bell, which, ironically, is synthesized from Shoal Salt and Shoal Shells.

Title: Shoal Shell

An esteemed commodity first appearing in Generation III, the Shoal Shell (Japanese: あさせのかいがら, or Shoal Seashell) holds significant value. In the Hoenn region, this item collaborates with Shoal Salt to forge Shell Bells. Moving to the Generation V games, trainers can divest themselves of a Shoal Shell for $7000 by selling it to the ore enthusiast located within the Icirrus City Pokémon Center. More precisely, within Hoenn's Shoal Cave, an aged individual will craft a Shell Bell for the player upon receiving a collection of four Shoal Salt and four Shoal Shells.

Title: Red Shard

This collectible, known in Japanese as あかいかけら (Red Shard), first appeared as an item type in Generation III. It belongs to the category of Shard items. Within the Generation V titles, players can exchange a Red Shard for $200 with the ore maniac found inside the Icirrus City Pokémon Center. On Hoenn's Route 124, the Treasure Hunter offers a Fire Stone in return for a single Red Shard. For those playing Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, Brilliant Diamond, or Shining Pearl, ten Red Shards can be traded with the shard collector situated on Route 212 to obtain TM11, which teaches Sunny Day. In Pokémon Platinum, various Move Tutors accept quantities of 2, 4, 6, or 8 Red Shards to impart specific moves to Pokémon. HeartGold and SoulSilver players have two options: trade a Red Shard with the Juggler in Violet City for a Cheri, Leppa, and Pecha Berry, or swap it with the Juggler in Fuchsia City to receive a Persim, Razz, and Pomeg Berry. Furthermore, in Pokémon Black 2 and White 2, the Move Tutor in Driftveil City will teach Pokémon moves in exchange for 2, 4, 6, 8, or 10 Red Shards. Post-game in Alola, an elderly individual who materializes within the Festival Plaza castle will provide a Bottle Cap in exchange for 30 Red Shards. To craft a Star Piece in Pokémon Legends: Arceus, three Red Shards are among the necessary components. Red Shards briefly appeared in Team Rocket's imaginative sequence in the episode The Grass Menagerie!. During the Stoutland Search treasure hunt on Akala Island, organized by Olivia for Ash and his classmates in Treasure Hunt, Akala Style!, Ash successfully located one of every type of Shard.

Title: Blue Shard

The Blue Shard, known in Japanese as あおいかけら (Blue Shard), made its debut as an item in Generation III. It belongs to the category of Shards. Within the Generation V titles, players have the option to vend the Blue Shard to the ore maniac situated within the Icirrus City Pokémon Center, fetching a price of $200. Across the Hoenn region, a single Blue Shard may be traded with the Treasure Hunter found on Route 124 in exchange for a Water Stone. For trainers playing Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, Brilliant Diamond, and Shining Pearl, providing ten Blue Shards to the shard collector located on Route 212 will yield TM18 (Rain Dance). Pokémon Platinum offers the opportunity to teach Pokémon various moves by trading 2, 4, 6, or 8 Blue Shards with the resident Move Tutors. When journeying through Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, a lone Blue Shard can be exchanged with the Juggler residing in Violet City to acquire a Chesto, Oran, and Wiki Berry. Similarly, trading a single Blue Shard with the Juggler present in Fuchsia City grants a Bluk, Kelpsy, and Cornn Berry. In Pokémon Black 2 and White 2, the Lentimas Town Move Tutor accepts quantities of 2, 4, 6, 8, or 10 Blue Shards in return for teaching Pokémon new moves. Within the Alola region, after completing the main story, a total of 30 Blue Shards can be presented to the old man who appears within the Festival Plaza castle to receive a Bottle Cap. For crafting endeavors in Pokémon Legends: Arceus, three Blue Shards are among the necessary components for assembling a Star Piece. During the Stoutland Search treasure hunt, titled "Treasure Hunt, Akala Style!" which Olivia arranged for Ash and his classmates, Ash successfully located one of each type of shard.

Title: Yellow Shard

Known in Japanese as きいろいかけら, the Yellow Shard is an item initially brought forth in Generation III, classified as one of the Shards. In the Generation V titles, players may dispose of a Yellow Shard by selling it to the ore maniac situated inside the Icirrus City Pokémon Center, fetching $200. Hoenn trainers can exchange a single Yellow Shard with the Treasure Hunter on Route 124 for a Thunder Stone. For those journeying through Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, Brilliant Diamond, or Shining Pearl, surrendering ten Yellow Shards to the shard collector on Route 212 will secure TM37 (Sandstorm). Pokémon Platinum allows Move Tutors to teach Pokémon moves in return for 2, 4, 6, or 8 Yellow Shards. In Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, trading a single Yellow Shard to the Juggler in Violet City yields an Iapapa, Aspear, and Sitrus Berry; a similar exchange with the Juggler in Fuchsia City provides a Pinap, Grepa, and Nomel Berry. Trainers in Pokémon Black 2 and White 2 can utilize the Humilau City Move Tutor to impart new abilities to their Pokémon by offering 2, 4, 6, 8, or 10 Yellow Shards. Post-game in Alola, an elderly individual who materializes within the Festival Plaza castle will trade a Bottle Cap for 30 Yellow Shards. Ash Ketchum acquired one of every shard type during the "Treasure Hunt, Akala Style!" event, part of a Stoutland Search treasure hunt orchestrated by Olivia for him and his fellow students. Notably, the Yellow Shard stands alone as the sole shard type inaccessible within Pokémon Legends: Arceus.

Title: Green Shard

The Green Shard (Japanese: みどりのかけら, translated as Green Shard) first appeared as an item category in Generation III. It is a member of the broader group known as Shards. In games from Generation V, players can sell a Green Shard for $200 to the ore fanatic situated within the Icirrus City Pokémon Center. Throughout the Hoenn region, a single Green Shard can be exchanged with the Treasure Hunter on Route 124 for a Leaf Stone. In Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, Brilliant Diamond, and Shining Pearl, ten Green Shards are required to trade with the shard collector on Route 212 to receive TM07 (Hail). For those playing Pokémon Platinum, Move Tutors will accept 2, 4, 6, or 8 Green Shards in return for teaching various moves to Pokémon. In Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, trading one Green Shard with the Juggler in Violet City will provide an Aguav, Lum, and Rawst Berry. Another Green Shard can be given to the Juggler in Fuchsia City to obtain a Wepear, Hondew, and Durin Berry. Within Pokémon Black 2 and White 2, the Nacrene City Move Tutor offers to teach Pokémon moves in exchange for 6, 8, 10, or 12 Green Shards. In Alola, after completing the main story, an elderly gentleman appearing in the Festival Plaza castle will exchange 30 Green Shards for a Bottle Cap. Crafting a Star Piece in Pokémon Legends: Arceus requires three Green Shards as one of its necessary components. Green Shards were featured in "The Relicanth Really Can," discovered by Adam after they detached from a Relicanth's fins. He initially believed them to be emeralds until his wife, Evelyn, accurately identified them as Green Shards. She mentioned that Green Shards were historically utilized for making jewelry in ancient times, though their current market value is significantly less. Following another treasure hunt, Adam unearthed a chest completely full of Green Shards. Ash collected one of each shard type during the "Treasure Hunt, Akala Style!" episode, while participating in a Stoutland Search treasure hunt organized by Olivia for him and his classmates.

Title: Super Repel

The Super Repel (Japanese: シルバースプレー Silver Spray) is an item type initially introduced in Generation I. It has two analogous counterparts, the Repel and Max Repel. This item provides a protective effect for 200 steps, identical in duration to its related versions. When activated from the Bag while traversing the overworld, it prevents certain wild Pokémon from appearing: In Generation I, and again from Generation VI onwards, this applies to wild Pokémon whose level is lower than the first Pokémon in the player's party. From Generation II through V, its effect targets wild Pokémon with a level inferior to the first *non-fainted* Pokémon in the party.

In Generation I exclusively, it's possible to deploy this item even if a previously used Repel, Super Repel, or Max Repel is still active; however, doing so will override the ongoing effect, wasting any remaining steps from the former item. From Generation II onwards, it becomes impossible to use this item while another of its type is currently in effect. Starting with Pokémon Black 2 and White 2, should the item's effect wear off and the player possesses more of them, the game automatically offers the option to use another one immediately.

For Generation I only, the active duration of this item is not saved; upon resuming a saved game, any prior uses are disregarded. However, from Generation II onwards, the quantity of remaining steps is stored within the game's data.

The mechanism for counting steps varies: No steps are tallied when the player is moving automatically, either as part of an in-game event or as a result of stepping on a specific floor tile. In Generation I, merely changing the player character's direction counts as an additional step, thus reducing the item's duration. For instance, moving 5 steps south and then 5 steps west totals 11 steps. Similarly, altering direction without physical movement (e.g., attempting to walk into a wall while turning) also registers as a step. Conversely, from Generation II onwards, a change in direction no longer contributes to the step count.

No step is counted when the player uses Surf from the party menu (for transitions between land and water or vice versa). However, physically walking directly from water to land *does* count as a step. Steps are also not recorded when the player passes through any game warp point, including a door, a cave entrance, stairs, a hole in the ground, or a teleport tile. Similarly, traversing from one area to another, marked by shifts in the color palette and background music (e.g., the precise moment a player leaves Viridian City and enters Route 2), does not decrement the step count. Turning around while situated on a stairs tile or a teleport tile also prevents a step from being counted. When indoors, moving onto or away from the two-tile horizontal carpet immediately adjacent to an exit (such as from a house, building, or elevator) does not count as steps. The two exit tiles in the Pokémon Tower function identically, despite the absence of a visible carpet. Likewise, walking within or away from the healing zone on the fifth floor of the Pokémon Tower does not count as steps. The game features stairs that occupy a floor tile (e.g., within a Pokémon Center) and stairs that occupy a wall tile (e.g., in the player's house).

This item provides no protection against encounters initiated by fishing, Sweet Scent, or against in-game event Pokémon visible in the overworld. Specific exceptions include: in Pokémon Emerald, it fails to prevent wild Pokémon from appearing in the Battle Pike and Battle Pyramid; in Pokémon White, it's ineffective against wild Pokémon in the White Forest; in Pokémon X and Y, it does not stop Pokémon from emerging from dirt mounds on Route 13. In Pokémon Sword and Shield, while it does prevent hidden encounters, it has no effect on symbol encounters or wanderers.

Cost analysis reveals that from Generation VII onwards, the Super Repel costs $3.5 per step. In comparison, the Max Repel costs $3.6 per step, and the standard Repel costs $4 per step. Before Generation VII, the Super Repel was priced at $2.5 per step. During this period, the Max Repel cost $2.8 per step, and the standard Repel was $3.5 per step.

Title: Max Repel

Hailing from Generation I, the Max Repel (known in Japanese as ゴールドスプレー, or Gold Spray) stands as a notable item. It effectively represents an enhanced version of the lesser Repel and Super Repel items. Players can acquire a Max Repel in Pokémon Black's Black City marketplace for a cost of $5500. Alternatively, within Join Avenue's Market, these items are available for bulk purchase in sets of twelve, priced at $6300 per unit, which translates to an individual cost of $525 for each Max Repel.

Offering an identical function to both the Repel and Super Repel, this item maintains its effect for 250 steps. Its activation from the Bag while traversing the overworld blocks encounters with specific wild Pokémon. Specifically, in Generation I and starting from Generation VI, this protection applies to any wild Pokémon possessing a level inferior to that of the lead Pokémon in the player's party. During Generations II through V, the same restriction applies, but against wild Pokémon whose level is lower than the first non-fainted Pokémon in the player's party. Unique to Generation I, players were able to deploy another Max Repel (or any other Repel variant) even if a prior one was still active; doing so would nullify the ongoing effect and forfeit any unspent steps. However, from Generation II forward, the game prevents the use of this item while an existing Repel effect is already in progress. Beginning with Pokémon Black 2 and White 2, once the effect of a Max Repel concludes, the game conveniently prompts the player, inquiring if they wish to deploy another, provided they possess additional units of the item.

Only in Generation I, this item's active duration was not preserved in saved game data; consequently, resuming a game meant any prior usage would be disregarded. From Generation II onward, however, the count of remaining steps is properly stored within the game's save file. Steps are not consumed while the player character moves automatically, either during an in-game event or by traversing specific floor tiles. In Generation I, any change in the player character's facing direction counts as an additional step, thereby reducing the item's duration. For example, a movement sequence of five steps southward followed by five steps westward would register as eleven steps. Similarly, merely altering direction without physical movement (e.g., attempting to walk into a wall) also expends a step. Using Surf via the party menu, whether transitioning from land to water or water to land, does not register as a step. However, directly walking from a body of water onto land does count. Passage through any game warp point—such as a door, cave entrance, stairs, ground hole, or teleport tile—does not increment the step counter. Similarly, moving between distinct areas, signaled by changes in the color palette or background music (like leaving Viridian City for Route 2), consumes no steps. Turning around while positioned on a stairs tile or a teleport tile also avoids counting a step. While indoors, moving onto or away from the two-tile horizontal carpet adjacent to a house, building, or elevator exit does not count towards the step total. This rule also applies to the invisible exit tiles of the Pokémon Tower and the healing zone on its fifth floor. From Generation II onward, simply changing direction no longer counts as a step. Furthermore, traversing stairs that occupy a floor tile (like those in a Pokémon Center) or wall tile (such as in the player's house) are not counted as steps.

The Max Repel holds no sway over encounters triggered by fishing, the move Sweet Scent, or scripted in-game event Pokémon visible on the overworld. In Pokémon Emerald, its effect is nullified within the confines of the Battle Pike and Battle Pyramid, allowing wild Pokémon to appear. Likewise, in Pokémon White, it fails to deter wild Pokémon from emerging in the White Forest. For Pokémon X and Y players, this item offers no protection against Pokémon found within dirt mounds on Route 13. In Pokémon Sword and Shield, while it effectively prevents hidden encounters, it does not stop symbol encounters or wanderers.

In the episode *Climbing the Tower of Success!*, a Max Repel was among the designated search items for the Wishing Bell Festival's scavenger hunt. Lillie, in *Lulled to La-La Land!*, recalled deploying Max Repels to maintain a safe distance from wild Pokémon during a forest camping trip with Ash and her classmates. This preventative measure unintentionally safeguarded her from a wild Morelull, which otherwise ambushed and sapped the energy from everyone else. The item also appears prominently in the visual design for the 'Contents of Trainer's bag' merchandise series, sold exclusively at the Pokémon Center.

Title: Escape Rope

First appearing in Generation I, the Escape Rope (あなぬけのヒモ Hole-Escape Rope in Japanese) functions as a specific category of in-game item. Its primary purpose is to facilitate an exit from enclosed locations such as caves or dungeons. Generally, this item is consumed upon use; however, in Pokémon Sword and Shield, it is categorized as a Key Item. Upon activation in the overworld within a cave or dungeon, the trainer is transported to the most recently visited Pokémon Center for healing, or their residence if no healing has occurred at a Center. A programming oversight in Pokémon Red and Blue permits its use to depart from either the Sea Cottage or the Vermilion City Pokémon Fan Club; this anomaly was rectified in Pokémon Yellow. Alternatively, when utilized in the overworld inside such locations, the user can be returned to the specific entrance point from which they initially entered. Its functionality is absent in Pokémon Colosseum and XD: Gale of Darkness.

Should a player manage to deploy an Escape Rope to depart the Inside of Truck at the beginning of Pokémon Ruby, Sapphire, and Emerald, prior to the truck's departure (despite the item not being legitimately obtainable then), they will find themselves relocated to the front of Petalburg City's southwestern house, while the truck itself stays put in Littleroot Town. Furthermore, exiting any building in Petalburg City while the truck remains present triggers a corrupted rendition of the animation depicting the player's exit from the truck. Turnback Cave is an area where the Escape Rope's use is prohibited for departure. Locations such as Spear Pillar, the Distortion World, and the Hall of Origin are not categorized as caves for the purpose of this item's use. In Pokémon Sword and Shield, the Escape Rope transforms into a Key Item offering unlimited usage. Conversely, in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, its consumable nature is reinstated. During weekends in Anville Town, a specific individual offers a Revive in trade for two Escape Ropes.

Within the Ruins of Alph in both Pokémon Crystal and HeartGold and SoulSilver, an Escape Rope is indispensable for unveiling the concealed chamber located in the northeastern section. For Pokémon Crystal, the item is expended, and the player departs the area as usual. However, in Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, the item remains intact, and the player's position does not change. The episode Hurray for Heracross depicts Bugsy encountering a "ESCAPE" puzzle within the Ruins of Alph, leading him to utilize an Escape Rope. This action caused the wall to give way, revealing Suicune.

In the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Plasma Storm expansion marked the debut of the Escape Rope as a Pokémon Tool Trainer card, appearing during the English Black & White Series (corresponding to the Japanese BW Era). Subsequent reprints occurred across the XY, Sun & Moon, and Sword & Shield Series. The Burning Shadows expansion (known as To Have Seen the Battle Rainbow in Japan) also featured a Secret Rare gold etched rendition of the card. Its effect mandates that both players swap their Active Pokémon with a Benched Pokémon, with the opposing player performing their switch prior to the user. Beyond its in-game and TCG appearances, the Escape Rope has been prominently showcased in various officially licensed merchandise.

Within Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen, trainers are capable of employing an Escape Rope to exit the Saffron Gym; this specific Gym is unique in these titles for allowing such an escape. Finally, in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, while designated as a Key Item, the Escape Rope remains entirely unutilized in gameplay.

Title: Repel

Debuting in Generation I, the Repel (known as むしよけスプレー, or Repellent Spray, in Japanese) is an inventory item. While the Super Repel and Max Repel offer enhanced power, the standard Repel provides an identical deterrent effect, lasting for a period of 100 steps. When deployed from the player's Bag in the game's overworld, its primary function is to prevent the appearance of specific wild Pokémon. The criteria for this prevention vary by generation: For Generation I and all subsequent games from Generation VI onward, it affects wild Pokémon whose level is lower than that of the leading Pokémon in the player's party. However, between Generation II and Generation V, the effect targeted wild Pokémon with a level inferior to the first *conscious* member of the player's party.

Uniquely in Generation I, a player could activate a Repel even if another of its variants (Repel, Super Repel, or Max Repel) was already active; this action would overwrite the existing effect, effectively nullifying any remaining duration from the prior item. From Generation II onward, this functionality was altered, making it impossible to employ a Repel while another's effect is already ongoing. A convenience feature introduced in Pokémon Black 2 and White 2 allows the game to automatically inquire if the player wishes to deploy another Repel as soon as an active one expires, provided more of the item are available in the inventory.

The item's effect was not preserved upon saving in Generation I; consequently, resuming a game meant any active Repel duration was disregarded. However, from Generation II forward, the count of remaining steps became a part of the saved game data. Steps are not depleted if the player character moves automatically due to an in-game event or by interacting with specific floor tiles.

It's important to note that in Generation I, merely altering the player's facing direction consumes a step, even without actual movement. For instance, five steps southward followed by five steps westward would total 11 steps. Similarly, turning into a wall still costs a step. However, turning around while positioned on a stairs tile or teleport tile does not consume a step. When indoors, traversing onto or away from the two-tile horizontal carpet situated near an exit (be it a house, building, or elevator) is exempt from the step count. This also applies to the invisible exit tiles of the Pokémon Tower and the healing zone on its fifth floor. From Generation II onward, this rule regarding direction changes was removed, meaning turning around no longer consumes a step. Furthermore, navigating through stairs that occupy a floor tile (like those in a Pokémon Center) or wall tile (such as in the player's home) also does not decrement the Repel's duration. The Repel's step counter also remains unaffected by transitions through various warp points in the game world, encompassing doors, cave entrances, stairwells, ground holes, or teleport tiles. Similarly, moving between distinct areas that trigger a change in the game's color palette or background music (like departing Viridian City to enter Route 2) does not consume a step. An exception is made when using Surf via the party menu to transition between land and water, or vice-versa, which consumes no steps. Conversely, simply walking directly from a water tile onto a land tile *does* count as a step.

The Repel offers no protection against Pokémon encountered via fishing, the move Sweet Scent, or designated in-game event Pokémon visible in the overworld. Specific exceptions exist across titles: In Pokémon Emerald, it fails to deter wild Pokémon within the Battle Pike and Battle Pyramid. Similarly, in Pokémon White, the item is ineffective in the White Forest. Pokémon X and Y players will find it does not prevent encounters from dirt mounds on Route 13. In Pokémon Sword and Shield, while preventing hidden encounters, it offers no defense against symbol encounters or wanderers.

The Repel has also appeared in other media. In "Cutting the Ties that Bind!", Scott utilized a Repel to safeguard himself, Brock, May, and Max from a wild Beedrill swarm. Another instance saw a Bug Catcher deploy a Repel prior to venturing into a Bug Pokémon-filled forest in "Wake Up—You're Snorlax!". It also made a fleeting appearance within Red's Bag in the episode "A Hollow Victreebel." Within the Pokémon Trading Card Game, Repel debuted as an Item card in the English Sun & Moon Series, appearing in the Sun & Moon expansion (corresponding to the Japanese Sun & Moon Era and also printed in the Japanese Sun & Moon Starter Set). This particular Trainer card enables the player to compel their opponent to swap their Active Pokémon with one of their Benched Pokémon. Beyond the games and anime, the Repel has been showcased in various officially licensed merchandise.

Title: Sun Stone

First appearing in Generation II, the Sun Stone (たいようのいし in Japanese) is a specific type of Evolution stone. Within the Generation V titles, players have the option to divest themselves of a Sun Stone by selling it to an item enthusiast located within Icirrus City's Pokémon Center, fetching a price of $3000. This item facilitates the evolution of various Pokémon, and its use leads to its consumption. Specifically, it enables Petilil to transform into Lilligant (when not in Hisui) or Hisuian Lilligant (within the Hisui region).

It makes an appearance across several Pokémon Mystery Dungeon titles: Red Rescue Team and Blue Rescue Team, Explorers of Time, Darkness, and Sky, as well as Gates to Infinity. In these games, it can prompt the evolution of certain Pokémon when offered at the Luminous Cave or Luminous Spring, or when utilized within a dungeon, always resulting in its expenditure. Should a Sun Stone be thrown, it inflicts 1/2 damage (or 2 damage if sticky). Notably, in Red Rescue Team, Blue Rescue Team, Explorers of Time, Explorers of Darkness, and Explorers of Sky, deploying this item inside a dungeon expends it without any discernible effect.

Pokémon Conquest sees Petilil's evolution into Lilligant and Cottonee's evolution into Whimsicott, provided their link is enhanced while their associated Warrior holds a Sun Stone. In this game, a Sun Stone can be acquired from the traveling merchant for 5000 gold, and subsequently resold for a range of 2500-3250 gold.

In Pokémon GO, the Sun Stone is indispensable for the evolution of specific Pokémon: Gloom necessitates 100 Oddish Candy alongside a Sun Stone to become Bellossom; Sunkern transforms into Sunflora with 50 Sunkern Candy and a Sun Stone; Cottonee progresses to Whimsicott requiring 50 Cottonee Candy and a Sun Stone; Petilil advances to Lilligant with 50 Petilil Candy and a Sun Stone; and Helioptile evolves into Heliolisk using 50 Helioptile Candy and a Sun Stone. This item became available in Pokémon GO on February 16, 2017. Players can begin to rarely acquire Sun Stones starting from level 10. Although generally uncommon, commencing March 21, 2017, players are assured an Evolution item upon earning the seventh-day streak bonus for spinning a Photo Disc. Furthermore, a Sun Stone is awarded upon concluding part 2 of the Special Research titled "A Ripple in Time".

To evolve a Gloom into Bellossom in Pokémon Pinball: Ruby & Sapphire, the player must collect three Sun Stone symbols.

Ash Ketchum was awarded a Sun Stone for his victory in the Bug-Catching Contest, depicted in "The Bug Stops Here". Subsequently, in "Moving Pictures", he employed it to evolve a wild Sunkern. An episode titled "Whichever Way the Wind Blows" showcased a Sun Stone and a Leaf Stone during a segment clarifying Gloom's branching evolutionary paths. "Mystery on a Deserted Island!" showcased the Sun Stone alongside every other Evolution stone present as of Generation V. In "The Cave of Trials!", a complete collection of Evolution stones was observable within a stone shop in Geosenge Town. Furthermore, a Trainer acquired a Sun Stone from this very shop to evolve their Helioptile into a Heliolisk. Clemont subsequently disclosed that he, too, had previously utilized a Sun Stone for the evolution of his own Helioptile into Heliolisk.

During "The Last Battle VI", Gold's Sunkern, named Sunbo, underwent evolution into a Sunflora. This transformation was attributed to intense sunlight generated by Sunbo's Sunny Day move, which reflected off nearby rocks in Ilex Forest, thereby mimicking the effects of a Sun Stone. Within "Ring Ring Goes Beldum", Steven Stone discovered a Sun Stone as he and Ruby made their escape from a pack of wild Mawile within Granite Cave.

A gallery of cards either referencing or depicting the Sun Stone is presented below. This item has also been included alongside other Evolution stones in a merchandise collection bearing the same designation. Furthermore, a Sun Stone is visible on the artwork for the "Old Amber" card sleeve package. Intriguingly, the Sun Stone's nomenclature aligns with that of a real-world gemstone.

Title: Moon Stone

The Moon Stone (Japanese: つきのいし Moon Stone) is an Evolution stone that made its debut in Generation I. In the Generation V installments of the core series games, players have the option to sell a Moon Stone to an item maniac located within the Icirrus City Pokémon Center for a sum of $3000. Its primary purpose involves facilitating the evolution of several distinct Pokémon species, a process which always expends the stone. From Generation IV onward, Pokémon that evolve through the use of this stone, along with their evolutionary relatives in Generation IV, benefit from an enhanced catch rate when players attempt to capture them using a Moon Ball.

This particular stone is also present in various Pokémon Mystery Dungeon titles, including Red Rescue Team and Blue Rescue Team, Explorers of Time, Darkness, and Sky, and Gates to Infinity. Within these games, it can be utilized to evolve certain Pokémon either by presenting it at the Luminous Cave or Luminous Spring, or by employing it within a dungeon setting, an action that consumes the item. If a Moon Stone is thrown, it inflicts 1/2 damage, or 2 damage if it is sticky. It's important to note that in Red Rescue Team, Blue Rescue Team, Explorers of Time, Explorers of Darkness, and Explorers of Sky, using this item inside a dungeon will consume it without any resulting effect.

In Pokémon Conquest, both Jigglypuff and Munna undergo evolution into Wigglytuff and Musharna, respectively, when their Warrior's link is improved while that Warrior is equipped with a Moon Stone. A traveling merchant offers the Moon Stone for purchase at a price of 5000 gold, and it can be resold for 2500-3250 gold. The Moon Stone also features in Pokémon Pinball and Pokémon Pinball: Ruby & Sapphire, where obtaining three Moon Stone symbols allows the player to evolve any eligible Pokémon. For Pokémon Sleep, a Moon Stone is a necessary component for the evolution of specific Pokémon, and it is consumed upon use. Clefairy requires 80 Clefairy Candy in addition to a Moon Stone to transform into Clefable. Similarly, Jigglypuff needs 80 Jigglypuff Candy and a Moon Stone to evolve into Wigglytuff, and Munna requires 80 Munna Candy plus a Moon Stone for its evolution into Musharna.

In the anime episode "Clefairy and the Moon Stone," a colossal Moon Stone was discovered deep within Mt. Moon, becoming an object of veneration for a local Clefairy group. This relic was subsequently pilfered by Team Rocket and ultimately obliterated; the resulting fragments rained down upon the Clefairy, prompting some of them to evolve into Clefable. During this episode, the characters put forth the theory that the Moon Stone was responsible for the presence of various Pokémon—particularly Clefairy—in the Pokémon world. A flashback within "Delcatty Got Your Tongue" divulged that Dr. Abby opted to pursue a career as a Pokémon Doctor shortly after claiming the title of Top Coordinator. Dr. Moss mentored her in Pokémon treatment, and as a token of appreciation for her efforts, bestowed upon her a Moon Stone, which Abby then employed to evolve her Skitty, nicknamed Johnny, into Delcatty. Another gigantic Moon Stone was observed at Mt. Moon in "A Real Cleffa-Hanger." In "Mystery on a Deserted Island!", the Moon Stone was showcased alongside all other Evolution stones available up to Generation V. A complete collection of Evolution stones was spotted on display in a stone shop located in Geosenge Town in "The Cave of Trials!". The Moon Stone made an appearance in a fantasy sequence in "A Dream Encounter!", where it was revealed that Lusamine had used one to evolve her Clefairy into Clefable. "Infinite Possibilities!" depicted multiple Moon Stones being utilized by a group of Clefairy during an evolution ritual. In "Clefairy Tale," Ash, Professor Oak, and Bill bore witness to a Clefairy evolution ceremony involving a substantial Moon Stone.

The chapter "To Evolve or Not to Evolve, That Is the Question!" centered on Evolution stones, with the Moon Stone prominently featured. Professor Oak first referenced a Moon Stone in "Gyarados Splashes In!" as a rock bearing a crescent moon-shaped indentation capable of immensely enhancing a Pokémon's power. In the subsequent chapter, Team Rocket embarked on a search for one in Mt. Moon, though Red ultimately unearthed it. In "The Winged Legends," Red employed his Moon Stone to evolve Green's Clefairy into a Clefable, preparing it to combat Thu-Fi-Zer during the Silph Co. confrontation with Team Rocket. Ruby's Skitty, Kiki, underwent evolution into a Delcatty in "Guile from Mawile" after coming into contact with one of Steven's Moon Stones. Another Moon Stone belonging to Steven was sighted in "Ring Ring Goes Beldum." In "Give It Your Best, Blastoise," during the skirmish against the Deoxys Duplicates, Green utilized two Moon Stones to evolve her Jigglypuff, named Jiggly, and her Nidorina, named Nido, into Wigglytuff and Nidoqueen, respectively. "Homecoming" disclosed that Black's Munna, Musha, had evolved into a Musharna using a Moon Stone he received from Caitlin. Misty was depicted wearing a Moon Stone on a necklace in "Receive The Moon Stone!!". Red challenged her to a battle for it, and while his Clefairy successfully defeated her Blastoise, the Moon Stone was absconded with by an unknown party in the interim.

The Moon Stone was issued as a Trainer card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game during the Japanese Original Era, specifically within Expansion Sheet 1. This card saw an exclusive release in Japanese and has never been reprinted. Its effect permits the player to draw 1 Colorless Evolution card from their deck and add it to their hand. A gallery featuring cards that depict, mention, or hold the Moon Stone is also available. The Moon Stone has been showcased alongside other Evolution stones within a merchandise collection bearing the same name.

A glitch discovered in the Generation I games allows Pokémon that typically require an Evolution stone to evolve without one, provided the Pokémon levels up during a battle where a specific Pokémon was sent out; for the Moon Stone, this designated Pokémon is Exeggutor. Until Generation IV, the Moon Stone distinguished itself from other evolution stones by being the sole one that could not be acquired an unlimited number of times per game. It shares its nomenclature with an actual gemstone. In the Chuang Yi translation of Pokémon Pocket Monsters, the item is referred to as "Moon Rock." While its in-game description characterizes the Moon Stone as black, most visual renderings (even within the games themselves) depict it as gray. Poké Lid #033 in Sanuki showcases a Clefairy clutching a Moon Stone. Furthermore, a Moon Stone serves as one of the symbols on the slot machines at the Veilstone Game Corner.

Title: Fire Stone

Known in Japanese as ほのおのいし, or "Flame Stone," this Evolution stone first appeared in Generation I. Players of the Generation V titles could exchange a Fire Stone for $3000 by selling it to an item enthusiast within Icirrus City's Pokémon Center. This item facilitates the evolution of various Pokémon, being consumed upon use. Specifically, it enables Hisuian Growlithe to transform into Hisuian Arcanine. However, it is ineffective for evolving the Partner Eevee or an Eevee possessing the Gigantamax Factor into Flareon.

Within the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, the Fire Stone is present in titles such as Red Rescue Team and Blue Rescue Team, Explorers of Time, Darkness, and Sky, and Gates to Infinity. Here, it can trigger the evolution of certain Pokémon when offered at a Luminous Cave or Luminous Spring, or when utilized within a dungeon, invariably consuming the item. Should it be thrown, it inflicts 1/2 damage, increasing to 2 damage if it is "sticky." A notable distinction for Red Rescue Team, Blue Rescue Team, Explorers of Time, Explorers of Darkness, and Explorers of Sky is that employing this item inside a dungeon still results in its consumption, but without any evolutionary benefit.

In Pokémon Conquest, both Eevee and Pansear achieve evolution into Flareon and Simisear, respectively, provided their link is strengthened while their Warrior holds a Fire Stone. This item is obtainable from the traveling merchant for 5000 gold, and can be resold for a range of 2500 to 3250 gold. For Pokémon Pinball and Pokémon Pinball: Ruby & Sapphire, players must collect three Fire Stone symbols to evolve eligible Pokémon. In Pokémon Sleep, this stone is a mandatory, single-use item for certain evolutions. Specifically, Vulpix needs 80 Vulpix Candy and a Fire Stone to become Ninetales; Growlithe requires 80 Growlithe Candy and a Fire Stone to evolve into Arcanine; and Eevee also needs 80 Eevee Candy and a Fire Stone to transform into Flareon.

The Fire Stone's anime debut occurred in "The Battling Eevee Brothers," where it was part of a collection owned by the titular siblings. During this episode, two of the four brothers presented a Fire Stone and a Thunder Stone to Brock and Ash, respectively, intending for them to evolve Vulpix and Pikachu, but both trainers declined. A collection of these stones became central to the plot of "The Stolen Stones!", as Ramona and Keegan were transporting them to a Fire-type Pokémon competition where they were designated as prizes. Team Rocket intercepted the delivery, stealing the stones, which then required Ash and his companions to assist in their recovery.

"A Hole Lotta Trouble" depicted Steven's Aron digging up a Fire Stone following Aggron's Hyper Beam sending Team Rocket away from Granite Cave. In "Last Call — First Round!", Ursula employed both a Fire Stone and a Water Stone during the Sinnoh Grand Festival's Performance Stage to evolve her pair of Eevee into a Flareon and Vaporeon, respectively. The episode "Mystery on a Deserted Island!" showcased the Fire Stone alongside every other Evolution stone introduced up to Generation V. Later, a complete collection of Evolution stones was displayed in a stone merchant's establishment in Geosenge Town, as seen in "The Cave of Trials!".

The episode "To Train, or Not to Train!" featured a Fire Stone at the Eevee Evolution Lab, where efforts to evolve an Eevee using the stone proved unsuccessful. In "Out of Their Elements!", Harmony and Billy each tried to use a Fire Stone and a Thunder Stone, respectively, on their Eevee partners. A mishap, however, caused them to drop the stones on the incorrect Eevee, resulting in evolutions opposite to their goals. Their subsequent attempt to rectify this with additional stones failed, as the already-evolved Pokémon were unaffected. By the episode's conclusion, their remaining stones were given to Chloe.

The Fire Stone was among the Evolution stones highlighted in the chapter "To Evolve or Not to Evolve, That Is the Question!". In "The Kindest Tentacruel," Yellow learned from the Pokémon Fan Club Chairman about a fabled underwater dome beneath Vermilion Harbor. This dome supposedly contained unique Evolution stones that, unlike conventional ones, could be used repeatedly without being consumed, a legend substantiated by Red's Poliwhirl's Evolution attributed to one such stone. A wild Tentacruel later guided Yellow to this dome, where she found a Leaf Stone, but the Fire, Thunder, and Water Stones were absent. It was subsequently revealed that Giovanni had provided these missing stones to Red, allowing him to freely evolve and devolve his Eevee.

During their confrontation with the Shadow Triad in "Triple Threat," Cilan, Chili, and Cress each utilized an Evolution stone—a Leaf Stone, Fire Stone, and Water Stone, respectively—to evolve their Pansage, Pansear, and Panpour into Simisage, Simisear, and Simipour. "Final Battle: Versus The Salon Maiden" showed Enta obtaining a Fire Stone from Rald, which he then used to evolve his Vulpix into Ninetales. Beyond the games and anime, the Fire Stone is referenced or depicted in numerous cards within the Pokémon Trading Card Game and has also been prominently featured in various official licensed figures and merchandise.

A known glitch in the Generation I games allowed Pokémon that typically require an Evolution stone to evolve without one. This occurred if the Pokémon leveled up during a battle where the player had deployed a specific Pokémon—for evolutions requiring a Fire Stone, this specific Pokémon was Missingno. (index number 32). Furthermore, in Pokémon: Let's Go, Eevee!, if a player offers a Fire Stone to their Partner Eevee, it will refuse the item and thus not evolve. This refusal also applies if a Thunder Stone or Water Stone is presented to the Partner Eevee.

Title: Thunder Stone

Known as かみなりのいし (Lightning Stone) in Japanese, this Evolution stone, which debuted in Generation I, was stylized as "Thunderstone" before Generation VI. Across the Generation V titles, players could sell a Thunderstone to an item enthusiast within the Icirrus City Pokémon Center, fetching a price of $3000. It serves as a catalyst for the evolution of various Pokémon, a process that inherently consumes the stone. Its primary function is to trigger Pikachu's evolution into Raichu (or Alolan Raichu when in Alola). However, it is ineffective on specific Pikachu variants, namely Cosplay Pikachu, Pikachu in a cap, the Partner Pikachu, and any Pikachu possessing the Gigantamax Factor. From Generation VIII onwards, Magneton can evolve into Magnezone with this stone. Notably, it also fails to evolve the Partner Eevee or an Eevee with the Gigantamax Factor into Jolteon. Similarly, Nosepass can be transformed into Probopass using this item, beginning in Generation VIII. An exception exists for Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, where the Thunder Stone cannot facilitate this evolution for Nosepass. Lastly, Charjabug evolves into Vikavolt through the Thunder Stone's power, also from Generation VIII onward.

In the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, the Thunderstone makes an appearance in titles such as Red Rescue Team and Blue Rescue Team, Explorers of Time, Darkness, and Sky, and Gates to Infinity. Within these spin-off games, it can trigger the evolution of certain Pokémon when offered at the Luminous Cave or Luminous Spring, or when utilized inside a dungeon. This application results in the stone's consumption. Should it be thrown, it inflicts 1/2 damage, increasing to 2 damage if sticky. However, in Red Rescue Team, Blue Rescue Team, Explorers of Time, Explorers of Darkness, and Explorers of Sky, using the item directly within a dungeon will consume it without any evolutionary effect. For Pokémon Conquest, both Pikachu and Eevee undergo evolution into Raichu and Jolteon, respectively, by strengthening their link while their associated Warrior holds a Thunderstone. This item is obtainable from the traveling merchant for 5000 gold and can be resold for a value ranging from 2500 to 3250 gold. In Pokémon Pinball and Pokémon Pinball: Ruby & Sapphire, players must collect three Thunder Stone symbols to enable the evolution of any relevant Pokémon.

Within Pokémon Sleep, specific Pokémon necessitate a Thunder Stone for evolution, which is used up upon application. Pikachu's transformation into Raichu demands 80 Pikachu Candy alongside a Thunder Stone. Similarly, Magneton needs 80 Magnemite Candy and a Thunder Stone to become Magnezone. Eevee's evolution into Jolteon requires 80 Eevee Candy plus a Thunder Stone. Finally, Charjabug evolves into Vikavolt with 80 Grubbin Candy and this stone.
