Its first appearance in the anime occurred in Electric Shock Showdown, where Nurse Joy presented Ash with a Thunder Stone, intending for him to evolve his Pikachu to overcome Lt. Surge's Raichu. Despite the offer, Pikachu declined. The Battling Eevee Brothers showcased a Thunder Stone among a collection belonging to the Eevee siblings. During this episode, two of the brothers proposed a Fire Stone and a Thunder Stone to Brock and Ash for their Vulpix and Pikachu, respectively, but both trainers chose not to accept. A visual representation of the Thunder Stone was featured in A Bite to Remember, illustrating Brock's explanation of the evolutionary process. Pika and Goliath! confirmed that Ash had retained his Thunder Stone, held in reserve should Pikachu ever desire to evolve. When a situation reminiscent of the Vermilion City encounter arose once more, Pikachu again declined to use the item. Observing his unwavering resolve against evolving, Team Rocket pilfered the stone with intentions of selling it.

During the Wishing Bell Festival contest in Climbing the Tower of Success!, Ash's challenge included locating a Thunder Stone in a scavenger hunt. He encountered a young man holding one who attempted to throw it to him from afar. The stone almost hit Pikachu, but the Pokémon swiftly evaded it, allowing Ash to secure the catch. Mystery on a Deserted Island! presented the Thunder Stone alongside every other Evolution stone introduced up to Generation V. In To Catch a Pokémon Smuggler!, a Thunder Stone was shown as part of Clemont's explanation of evolution, using Pikachu's transformation into Raichu as an illustrative example of stone-based evolution. The Cave of Trials! depicted a complete collection of Evolution stones displayed within a stone shop in Geosenge Town. Gotta Catch a What?! featured numerous Thunder Stones, as wild Pikachu in a valley were unearthing them to evolve. Goh's recently captured Pikachu discovered one, presenting it to her Trainer. Subsequently, Goh employed the Stone to evolve his Pikachu into Raichu, aiding in the defeat of Team Rocket. In To Train, or Not to Train!, a Thunder Stone was observed when staff at the Eevee Evolution Lab attempted, unsuccessfully, to evolve an Eevee with it. Out of Their Elements! saw Harmony and Billy try to use a Fire Stone and a Thunder Stone, respectively, on their Eevee companions. In an unfortunate mishap, they mistakenly dropped the stones on the incorrect Eevee, resulting in evolutions into forms contrary to their intentions. Their subsequent effort to rectify the error with their backup stones proved futile, as the items had no impact on the already-evolved Pokémon. The episode concluded with them entrusting their remaining stones to Chloe.

The chapter To Evolve or Not to Evolve, That Is the Question! prominently featured Evolution stones, with the Thunder Stone among them. The Kindest Tentacruel saw the Pokémon Fan Club Chairman recount to Yellow a legend regarding a submerged dome beneath Vermilion Harbor. This dome supposedly contained a unique collection of Evolution stones that, unlike conventional ones, did not vanish post-evolution, permitting repeated use. Red's Poliwhirl's evolution was attributed to one of these very stones, confirming the legend's veracity. Subsequently, a wild Tentacruel guided Yellow to the dome, where she discovered a Leaf Stone, but the Fire, Thunder, and Water Stones were absent. It was later disclosed that Giovanni had provided these missing stones to Red, enabling him to freely evolve and devolve his Eevee. At Indigo Plateau, Satoshi's Pikachu employed a Thunder Stone to evolve into a Raichu just prior to Satoshi departing to confront the Elite Four.

Images of cards that reference or display a Thunder Stone in the Pokémon Trading Card Game are presented below. Furthermore, the Thunder Stone has been represented in various officially licensed figures and merchandise.

A Generation I game glitch allowed Pokémon typically needing an Evolution stone to evolve without one. This occurred if the Pokémon leveled up during a battle where the player had deployed a particular Pokémon. For the Thunderstone, the specific Pokémon required was Growlithe. Should a player in Pokémon Yellow try to use a Thunder Stone on their Pikachu, the Pokémon will reject it. Interacting with Pikachu subsequently causes it to shake its head. However, traded Pikachu are exempt from this refusal. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, offering a Thunder Stone to one's Partner Pokémon results in their refusal to accept it, thus preventing evolution. For the Partner Eevee specifically, this same rejection occurs if a Fire Stone or Water Stone is presented.

Title: Water Stone

This item, known as みずのいし in Japan, is an Evolution stone that first appeared in the inaugural generation of Pokémon games. Players in Generation V titles have the option to sell a Water Stone to a specific item enthusiast located within the Icirrus City Pokémon Center for a sum of $3000. Its primary function is to facilitate the evolution of certain Pokémon, a process which expends the stone. Notably, it cannot be employed to transform a Partner Eevee or an Eevee possessing the Gigantamax Factor into Vaporeon.

For players to access a concealed chamber in the Ruins of Alph's southwestern section within Pokémon Crystal and HeartGold and SoulSilver, possession of a Water Stone is a prerequisite. In Pokémon Crystal, merely carrying it in the Bag suffices. However, in Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, players must actively use the stone while positioned in front of the wall.

This stone is also present in titles such as Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Red Rescue Team and Blue Rescue Team, Explorers of Time, Darkness, and Sky, and Gates to Infinity. Here, it enables the evolution of certain Pokémon when offered at the Luminous Cave or Luminous Spring, or when employed within a dungeon, always resulting in its consumption. Should it be thrown, it inflicts 1/2 damage (or 2 if it's sticky). It's worth noting that in Red Rescue Team, Blue Rescue Team, Explorers of Time, Explorers of Darkness, and Explorers of Sky, utilizing this item inside a dungeon expends it without yielding any evolutionary outcome.

Within Pokémon Conquest, Eevee transforms into Vaporeon and Panpour into Simipour if their respective Warrior holds a Water Stone while their link is enhanced. This item is available for purchase from the traveling merchant for 5000 gold and can be resold for a value ranging from 2500 to 3250 gold. In Pokémon Pinball and Pokémon Pinball: Ruby & Sapphire, players must collect three Water Stone symbols to trigger the evolution of specified Pokémon.

Pokémon Sleep mandates the use of a Water Stone for the evolution of particular Pokémon, with the stone being used up during the process. For Eevee to become Vaporeon, specifically, 80 Eevee Candy alongside a Water Stone are necessary.

The Water Stone made its first appearance in the episode "The Battling Eevee Brothers," where it was part of a collection owned by the titular siblings. During a celebration they hosted, a Trainer was depicted using a Water Stone to evolve their Poliwhirl into a Poliwrath. It later featured in a fantasy sequence in "Go Go Ludicolo!" and then, in "Once in a Mawile," a Water Stone owned by Samantha was utilized by Brock's Lombre to become Ludicolo. This marked the inaugural instance of a primary character's Pokémon evolving through an Evolution stone shown on screen. Professor Oak provided a Water Stone to a wild Lombre in "A Faux Oak Finish!" to facilitate its evolution into Ludicolo. During the Performance Stage of the Sinnoh Grand Festival in "Last Call — First Round!," Ursula employed both a Water Stone and a Fire Stone to evolve her two Eevee into a Vaporeon and Flareon, respectively. "Mystery on a Deserted Island!" showcased the Water Stone alongside every other Evolution stone present up to Generation V. A complete collection of Evolution stones was visibly arranged in a stone vendor's shop in Geosenge Town in "The Cave of Trials!". In "On Land, In the Sea, and to the Future!", Kiley presented Chloe with a Water Stone. Chloe inquired if her Eevee wished to use it, but the Pokémon opted against immediate evolution. A separate Water Stone was also seen in a flashback within the same episode, where Kiley used it to evolve his own Eevee into Vaporeon.

The chapter "To Evolve or Not to Evolve, That Is the Question!" centered on Evolution stones. Misty expressed a desire to acquire a Water Stone for her Poliwhirl in Stone Town, a settlement on Dream Island noted as the origin for all Pokémon world Evolution stones. Nevertheless, she found it unaffordable, commenting that a cheap one could lead to a flawed evolution. Subsequently, Misty received a Water Stone from Mikey, who was facing coercion to join the "Knights of the E Stone," an organization whose membership was contingent on possessing a Pokémon evolved by such an item. Red's Poliwhirl transformed into a Poliwrath with the aid of a Water Stone in "Buzz Off, Electabuzz!" after they were submerged in Vermilion Harbor, though the specific reason for this evolution remained undisclosed until the Yellow arc. In "The Kindest Tentacruel," the Pokémon Fan Club Chairman recounted to Yellow a tale concerning a subaquatic dome situated beneath Vermilion Harbor. This dome supposedly contained a collection of Evolution stones that, unlike standard ones, did not vanish upon evolving a Pokémon, thereby permitting recurrent usage. It was confirmed that one of these legendary stones was responsible for Red's Poliwhirl's evolution. A wild Tentacruel later guided Yellow to this dome, where she discovered a Leaf Stone, but the Fire, Thunder, and Water Stones were absent. Subsequent revelations showed that Giovanni had given these missing stones to Red, enabling him to repeatedly evolve and devolve his Eevee. Shelly employed a Water Stone to evolve her Lombre into a Ludicolo during her confrontation with Sapphire's Pokémon in "Mowing Down Ludicolo." In "Triple Threat," Cilan, Chili, and Cress each utilized a Leaf Stone, Fire Stone, and Water Stone, respectively, to evolve their Pansage, Pansear, and Panpour into Simisage, Simisear, and Simipour during their skirmish with the Shadow Triad. In PASV25, Violet presented Arven with a Water Stone, an item he had acquired from Kofu, which Arven then used to evolve his Shellder into Cloyster to assist in his battle against the pair of Quaking Earth Titans: Great Tusk and Iron Treads.

A gallery detailing Pokémon Trading Card Game cards that reference or display a Water Stone is presented below. Furthermore, the Water Stone has been incorporated into various officially licensed merchandise and figures.

A programming anomaly within the Generation I titles allowed for Pokémon normally requiring an Evolution stone to evolve without one. This occurred if the Pokémon gained a level during a battle where a particular Pokémon was also deployed. For evolutions typically initiated by a Water Stone, this specific Pokémon was Onix. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Eevee!, should a player attempt to offer their partner Eevee a Water Stone, the Pokémon will decline it, thus preventing evolution. Identical outcomes are observed if a Fire Stone or Thunder Stone is offered.

Title: Leaf Stone

An evolutionary item, the Leaf Stone (known in Japanese as リーフのいし, or Leaf Stone), made its debut in Generation I. Within the Pokémon Masters Arena minigame, Pokémon Trivia Challenge, its name is rendered as "leafstone". An item maniac located within the Icirrus City Pokémon Center in Pokémon Black 2 and White 2 offers to purchase a Leaf Stone for $3000.

It serves as a catalyst for the evolution of various Pokémon, being consumed in the process. Specifically, Hisuian Voltorb transforms into Hisuian Electrode upon its application. Exeggcute evolves into Exeggutor when used outside of the Alola region, or into Alolan Exeggutor if utilized within Alola. From Generation VIII onwards, Eevee can be evolved into Leafeon using this item. However, Eevee possessing the Gigantamax Factor cannot be evolved into Leafeon via a Leaf Stone.

The Leaf Stone is featured in the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, appearing in titles such as Red Rescue Team and Blue Rescue Team, Explorers of Time, Darkness, and Sky, along with Gates to Infinity. In these games, it facilitates the evolution of certain Pokémon, requiring its consumption, either by offering it at a Luminous Cave or Luminous Spring, or by employing it within a dungeon. When thrown, it inflicts 1/2 damage normally, or 2 damage if it possesses the 'sticky' status. Notably, in Red Rescue Team, Blue Rescue Team, Explorers of Time, Explorers of Darkness, and Explorers of Sky, using the Leaf Stone in a dungeon consumes it without yielding any effect.

Pokémon Conquest sees Pansage evolve into Simisage when its Warrior is equipped with a Leaf Stone and its link is improved. A traveling merchant offers the Leaf Stone for purchase at 5000 gold, while its selling price ranges from 2500 to 3250 gold.

In Pokémon Pinball and Pokémon Pinball: Ruby & Sapphire, players must collect three Leaf Stone symbols to evolve eligible Pokémon. Pokémon Sleep mandates the use of a Leaf Stone for the evolution of specific Pokémon, with the item being consumed during the process. Weepinbell's evolution into Victreebel necessitates 80 Bellsprout Candy and a Leaf Stone. Similarly, Eevee needs 80 Eevee Candy and a Leaf Stone to evolve into Leafeon.

In the episode Make Room for Gloom, a counterfeit Leaf Stone crafted by Team Rocket made an appearance; its false nature was a pivotal plot point. A genuine Leaf Stone was first seen at the conclusion of Pikachu's Rescue Adventure. This stone enabled the Exeggcute, which had been tailing Misty's Togepi, to evolve into an Exeggutor.

The Grass Route depicted a collection of Leaf Stones as the award for triumphing in the Grass Tournament. Despite Team Rocket's attempt to pilfer the stones, Ephraim ultimately claimed them. His intention was to use these stones to evolve his parents' Pokémon. Whichever Way the Wind Blows featured both a Leaf Stone and a Sun Stone during an explanation of Gloom's branching evolutionary path. In Mystery on a Deserted Island!, the Leaf Stone was shown alongside every other Evolution stone introduced up to Generation V. The Cave of Trials! showcased a complete assortment of Evolution stones on display at a stone shop in Geosenge Town.

That New Old Gang of Mine! showed Professor Cerise's Yamper presenting Goh with a Leaf Stone it had discovered. Goh promptly utilized the stone to evolve his Exeggcute into Exeggutor. The chapter titled To Evolve or Not to Evolve, That Is the Question! centered on Evolution stones, with the Leaf Stone being among them.

The Pokémon Fan Club Chairman informed Yellow in The Kindest Tentacruel about a legendary underwater dome beneath Vermilion Harbor, said to contain Evolution stones that, unlike ordinary ones, remained after use, permitting repeated evolutions. Red's Poliwhirl's evolution, caused by one of these very stones, confirmed the legend's veracity. Subsequently, a wild Tentacruel guided Yellow to the dome, where she located a Leaf Stone, though the Fire, Thunder, and Water Stones were absent. It was later disclosed that Giovanni had provided these missing stones to Red, allowing him to evolve and devolve his Eevee at will.

Ring Ring Goes Beldum featured a Leaf Stone among Steven's collection of various stones. During their confrontation with the Shadow Triad in Triple Threat, Cilan, Chili, and Cress employed a Leaf Stone, Fire Stone, and Water Stone, respectively, to evolve their Pansage into Simisage, Pansear into Simisear, and Panpour into Simipour.

The Leaf Stone is depicted or referenced on various cards within the Pokémon Trading Card Game. Beyond the games, the Leaf Stone has appeared as a replica, an embroidery patch, and on masking tape, as part of the Evolution Stones collection. Notably, in Pokémon Black and White, the Leaf Stone stood as the sole Evolution stone that could not be sold to the ore maniac, a peculiarity rectified in Black 2 and White 2.

Title: Tiny Mushroom

Originating in Generation II, the Tiny Mushroom — rendered as ちいさなキノコ (Small Mushroom) in Japanese and styled as TinyMushroom before Generation VI — is categorized as a valuable item. In Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen, a pair of these can be traded to a Move Reminder for their services. A more substantial and valuable variant of this item exists, known as the Big Mushroom. For $500, a gourmet enthusiast situated on Route 5 in the Generation V games will acquire a Tiny Mushroom. Conversely, in Pokémon Sword and Shield, the valuable item buyer in Stow-on-Side will purchase one for $2,000. Specifically in Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen, presenting two Tiny Mushrooms to the Move Reminder on Two Island enables a Pokémon to relearn a single move. In Pokémon Quest, the Tiny Mushroom functions as a cooking ingredient used to attract wild Pokémon. This ingredient can be acquired either by successfully completing expeditions or by random chance when recycling Power Stones. During the "Stoutland Search" treasure hunt, arranged by Olivia for Ash and his classmates in the episode *Treasure Hunt, Akala Style!*, Mallow discovered several Tiny Mushrooms. In *Trade, Borrow, and Steal!*, Kricketina Kylie incorporated powdered Tiny Mushrooms into her "Kricketina Treats" concoction, which she created to try and lure a Pinsir for capture. Additionally, Tiny Mushrooms can be observed in the artwork of the Dedenne card from the Darkness Ablaze expansion.

Title: Big Mushroom
This prized collectible, known in Japanese as おおきなキノコ (Big Mushroom), made its debut in Generation II. It serves as an enhanced and more lucrative counterpart to the Tiny Mushroom. Within the Generation V titles, players can exchange a Big Mushroom for $5000 by selling it to the gourmet enthusiast located on Route 5. In the world of Pokémon Sword and Shield, the specific buyer of valuable items in Stow-on-Side will purchase this item for $10,000. For trainers playing Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen, providing a Big Mushroom to the Move Reminder situated on Two Island enables a single Pokémon to recall one forgotten move. During the episode "Defending the Homeland!", Clemont disclosed his incorporation of Big Mushrooms into a novel culinary creation prepared for the group. Their initial appearance occurred in "Treasure Hunt, Akala Style!". Mallow discovered these items while participating in a Stoutland Search treasure hunt, an event arranged by Olivia for Ash and his schoolmates. Later, in "A Recipe for Success!", Mallow acquired dried Big Mushrooms from Oranguru, intending to utilize them at Aina's Kitchen. An instance of a Big Mushroom was observed in "A Week to Go and Old Wounds," wherein Shoko provided it to one of her Patrat for holding during her demonstration of Black's Marvelous Bridge Patrat Show. Finally, a Big Mushroom features prominently within the card artwork for Dedenne from the Darkness Ablaze expansion.

Title: Pearl

Consult Pokémon Diamond and Pearl Versions for information concerning the primary game from the fourth generation. The eighth generation's main game entries are detailed under Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. The animated television series, which draws its inspiration from the Generation IV games, is titled Pokémon the Series: Diamond and Pearl. Within the Pokémon Adventures manga series, the specific chapter is designated as the Diamond & Pearl arc. Kosaku Anakubo's manga, serving as a successor to Pokémon Pocket Monsters and Pokémon Ruby-Sapphire, is known as Pocket Monsters DP. For the manga authored by Shigekatsu Ihara, refer to Pokémon Diamond and Pearl Adventure!. Sakai Takayuki's manga publication is identified as Pocket Monsters Diamond & Pearl. The rival character encountered in the Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum video games is Barry (game). His parallel figure in the Pokémon Adventures manga bears the name Pearl (Adventures). The corresponding character within the Pocket Monsters DP manga is Kazuki (Pocket Monsters DP). In the Pokémon Diamond and Pearl Adventure! manga, the identical role is taken by Jun (DPA). For his depiction in Pokémon the Series, his anime counterpart is Barry (anime). Valuable commodities found across the various Pokémon games encompass Pearl (item), Big Pearl, and Pearl String. An area located in Pokémon Rumble World is designated Pearl Marsh. A particular category of figure in the Pokémon Trading Figure Game is referred to as a Pearl figure.

Title: Big Pearl

Emerging in Generation II, the Big Pearl (Japanese: おおきなしんじゅ Big Pearl) stands as a precious collectible item. It serves as a larger and more lucrative variant of the standard Pearl. In the Generation V installments, trainers can sell the Big Pearl to the ore enthusiast found inside the Icirrus City Pokémon Center for $7500. Later, in Pokémon Sword and Shield, a dedicated valuable item buyer in Stow-on-Side will purchase the Big Pearl for $12,000. This item holds no functional purpose beyond being sold for profit. Within Pokémon Masters EX, one Big Pearl can be exchanged for 3,000 coins at the in-game shop. A Big Pearl made an appearance in the episode Crossing Crobat, where Janine located and retrieved it during a test aimed at assessing the Goldenrod Museum's security. The Big Pearl was also featured in an artwork for the Pokémon Diving merchandise collection, which was exclusively available at the Pokémon Center.

Title: Stardust

For those seeking the currency within Pokémon GO, please refer to Stardust (GO). This precious item, known in Japanese as ほしのすな (Star Sand), was initially introduced in Generation II. Across the Generation V games, Stardust can be sold for $2000 to the ore maniac found inside the Icirrus City Pokémon Center. Conversely, in Pokémon Sword and Shield, the valuable item buyer in Stow-on-Side will purchase it for $4,000. Stardust generally serves no function other than being sold, with the notable exception of Pokémon Legends: Arceus, where it acts as a crafting material for creating Star Pieces.

Title: Star Piece

The Star Piece (Japanese: ほしのかけら Star Piece) is a valuable commodity first introduced in the second generation of Pokémon games. In the Generation V titles, it can be sold to the resident ore maniac within the Icirrus City Pokémon Center for $9800. For trainers in Pokémon Sword and Shield, the valuable item buyer in Stow-on-Side will pay $14,000 for a Star Piece. In Pokémon Platinum, this item serves as a trade-in with Mr. Fuego at Fuego Ironworks for one Shard of each color. A man located in Anville Town in Unova offers to exchange a Star Piece for a PP Up, though this deal is only available on Saturdays and Sundays. To create a Comet Shard using the Cram-o-matic, three Star Pieces and an additional item must be combined.

When utilized, a Star Piece will boost the amount of Stardust a player receives by 50% for a period of 30 minutes. These items can be acquired by purchasing specific boxes available in the in-game shop during special events. Additionally, players are awarded a Star Piece upon completing part 3 of the Special Research "A Mythical Discovery", part 5 of the Special Research "A Ripple in Time", and part 8 of the Special Research "Let's GO, Meltan".

In the anime episode "Treasure Hunt, Akala Style!", Sophocles uncovered a Star Piece during a Stoutland Search treasure hunt arranged by Olivia for Ash and his classmates. The English dub incorrectly identified this item as a Comet Shard. Another episode, "Hazel VS Almond!", saw Almond win a Star Piece in a contest hosted by Grandpa, which he then gave to Hazel.

The Star Piece made its debut as a Pokémon Tool card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game within the English e-Card Series' Skyridge expansion (known as the Japanese e-Card Era). Its effect allows a player, at any time between turns, to place the appropriate Evolution card from their deck onto the Pokémon this card is attached to, if that Pokémon is Benched and has two or more damage counters. This action counts as an evolution. Meloetta is programmed to always possess a Star Piece when encountered in the wild in Pokémon Black and White, despite the fact that Meloetta cannot actually be found in the wild within those games.

Title: Nugget

First appearing in Generation I, the Nugget (known in Japanese as きんのたま, or "Gold Orb") is a prized in-game collectible. A more substantial and costly variant of this item is the Big Nugget. During Generation V gameplay, the Nugget can fetch a price of $10000 when sold to the ore enthusiast located within Icirrus City's Pokémon Center. Its sole function is commercial, lacking any utility beyond being sold. By combining three Nuggets with one other item and inserting them into the Cram-o-matic, a Big Nugget can be generated.

The Pasio Gold Rush solo event marked the debut of Nuggets as its designated exchange currency. Participants in this event acquired Nuggets through battles, which they could then redeem for various items at the shop. Consistent with other event-specific items, unspent Nuggets remaining in players' inventories at the conclusion of the event duration were automatically converted into coins. Uniquely, Nuggets converted into coins at a significantly elevated rate—100 times greater than other event items, yielding 1,000 coins per Nugget. Later, Nuggets reappeared as the trading medium for the special Rolling in Gold event, exclusively obtainable through the ranked damage challenge, Aim for the Top. In this scenario, participants were awarded Nuggets upon achieving specific total score benchmarks across the trio of Super Sparring battles.

In the animated short "Why Not Give Me a Z-Ring Sometime?", a Nugget was featured, being polished by a man until it was pilfered by an item-snatching Gengar known as "the Greedy Rapooh." "The Discovery" episode depicted a man observing Green's facile victory over all the Trainers stationed on Nugget Bridge, all while clutching a Nugget.

The Nugget made its debut as an Item card within the Pokémon Trading Card Game, released during the English Sword & Shield Series (corresponding to the Japanese Sword & Shield Era). Its initial release occurred in the Japanese Rebellion Crash and English Rebel Clash expansions, featuring artwork crafted by sadaji. This card grants the player the ability to draw three additional cards, provided it was drawn at the commencement of their turn.

Collectible, real-world Nugget replicas, functional as paperweights, were offered for sale in restricted quantities at the temporary Pokémon Let's Go! Pikachu & Eevee Cafe in November 2019. Additionally, ten such replicas were distributed as prizes to individuals who retweeted announcements pertaining to the event from the @poke_times Twitter account.

A character who bestows a Nugget upon the player on Route 2 in Pokémon Gold, Silver, Crystal, HeartGold, and SoulSilver is suggested to be identical to the individual excavating the Three Isle Path in Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen; this is because he presents two Nuggets to the player and articulates his ambition to acquire property in mainland Kanto. This consistent character subsequently emerges in Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon at the Ula'ula Ferry Terminal, where he once again grants the player a Nugget and recounts his past as a gold miner within the Sevii Islands. Other individuals distributing Nuggets can also be encountered on Sinnoh's Route 229, within Unova's Chargestone Cave, and on the third floor of Mauville City in Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire; however, any potential links these characters might have to the previously mentioned man remain unconfirmed. The recurring joke is further referenced in the Pasio Gold Rush solo event in Pokémon Masters EX, during which various characters recount their experiences meeting men who gave them Nuggets and ponder whether some of these encounters involved the same individual.

The item's Japanese designation—きんのたま (kin no tama), which literally translates to "golden ball"—also carries a colloquial meaning referring to testicles. This dual meaning has led to a number of suggestive jokes concerning the item across various games, with the most prominent example being the persistent gag involving a man bestowing Nuggets (on occasion, precisely two) upon the player.

Title: Heart Scale

An item known as the Heart Scale (Japanese: ハートのウロコ Heart Scale) made its debut in Generation III. In Pokémon Ruby, Sapphire, and Emerald, throughout Generations IV to VII, and within Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, this item can be presented to a Move Reminder to instruct a single Pokémon in one of its moves. Specifically, in Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, once 10 Heart Scales have been utilized in total, the service becomes permanently free, and the Move Reminder will continue teaching Pokémon moves without any further cost.

Heart Scales were first mentioned in a fantasy sequence in "Partner Promises!", when Mallow revealed that the "Alola Sunrise" jewelry shop crafted its pieces from Heart Scales and Corsola branches. The item's first physical depiction occurred in the Poké Problem extra scene of SM120, where Lana showed one to her classmates, humorously claiming it was a Kyogre scale. Another Heart Scale appeared in "A Recipe for Success!", acquired by Mallow from a group of Fearow visiting Oranguru's abode. In "Getting to the Heart of it All!", a Heart Scale was among the heart-shaped objects stolen by a Banette.

The Heart Scale has also been included in several officially licensed merchandise lines. In the Alola region, these items can be found at the Vast Poni Canyon trial site, suggesting they might be the shed scales from the Jangmo-o evolutionary line. This theory is further bolstered by the distinctive heart-shaped scale located on Jangmo-o's head. Notably, in Pokémon X and Y, when the Bag is organized by type, the Heart Scale is erroneously classified as an Evolution stone instead of its proper designation as an exchangeable item.

Title: Honey

For the character found in The Isle of Armor, refer to Honey (character). To find information about the island located within the Decolore Islands, see Honey Island. The item known as Honey (Japanese: あまいミツ Sweet Honey) made its first appearance in Generation IV. Its primary function is to draw wild Pokémon toward the player. Within Generation V titles, players could vend Honey to the gourmet enthusiast situated on Route 5 for a sum of $500. Employing Honey in areas such as tall grass, caves, or during surfing activities initiates an encounter with a wild Pokémon, much like the field effect of Sweet Scent. For Generation VI, its use results in a Horde Encounter if such encounters are available in the specific location; however, specifically in Pokémon X and Y, it is incapable of prompting a wild encounter if the prevailing overworld weather conditions are not clear. Across the Sinnoh region, Honey can be applied to designated Honey Trees to lure in wild Pokémon. Exclusively in Pokémon Diamond and Pearl, deploying Honey (or Sweet Scent) within a shop or Poké Mart causes the descriptive text for items available for purchase to disappear, a state that persists until the player accesses the "SELL" menu or exits the premises. This phenomenon is recognized as the Poké Mart text glitch. In Pokémon Sword and Shield, alongside Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Honey holds no functional utility beyond its ability to be sold. Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl reintroduce Honey's utility for initiating wild Pokémon battles in tall grass or caves, and for application on Honey Trees to attract wild Pokémon.

While not accessible as an item in Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon, Honey is referenced as a foodstuff made by the Beedrill and Combee inhabiting Nectar Meadow, under the supervision of their monarch and overseer, Vespiquen. Given its nourishing qualities, Budew ventures to Nectar Meadow to procure Honey for her unwell mother, notwithstanding cautions that the resident Pokémon tend to be agitated during their Honey-producing period. Mistaken for a Honey pilferer, Budew is timely saved by the player and partner from the enraged Beedrill and Combee. Following their defeat, Vespiquen steps in, clarifies the situation, and bestows some Honey upon Budew.

In Pokémon Quest, Honey functions as an ingredient for cooking recipes designed to attract wild Pokémon. It represents a potential ingredient acquired through the completion of expeditions. Furthermore, it may be obtained at random by recycling Power Stones. Its application as a cooking ingredient is marked by a base Strength value of 101. It contributes to various recipes and can be collected by the player's helper Pokémon.

During the episode "Some Enchanted Sweetening!", Honey spread on a tree drew in multiple wild Bug-type and Grass-type Pokémon, alongside Ash's Aipom and Cheryl's Burmy. This application of Honey seemed to be the work of Cheryl, whose aim was to capture a male Burmy to assist her in locating Enchanted Honey. Cheryl subsequently informed the group that Enchanted Honey was Honey accumulated by Combee within Amber Castle, found deep within Eterna Forest, and reputedly hundreds of times sweeter than conventional Honey. Aided by Ash and his companions, and with her Mothim guiding their path, she discovered the nest of a Vespiquen and its numerous worker Combee. Following assistance in defending the hive against Team Rocket, the Vespiquen offered some of its Enchanted Honey to Cheryl, Ash, his friends, and a few of their Pokémon. Everyone relished the Enchanted Honey, with the exception of Brock's Croagunk, who appeared repulsed by its sugary taste.

In "A Trainer and Child Reunion!", Team Rocket employed Honey in an endeavor to capture Aaron's Vespiquen. This instead drew in a Munchlax. For "Strategy Begins at Home!", a youthful boy named Nathaniel positioned Honey on a tree, which subsequently lured a Heracross. A flashback sequence in "Historical Mystery Tour!" disclosed that Barry had utilized Honey to entice his Heracross. He replicated this exact situation during a hallucinatory quest orchestrated by a Clown. Within "Honey for Combee", as Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum explored Floaroma Meadow, Diamond and his Pokémon detected the scent of Honey and rushed to consume it from a tree, only to face an assault from a swarm of furious Combee, for whom the Honey was originally intended. Subsequently, the trio proceeded to the Valley Windworks, encountering Mars, who filled the control room with sand in an attempt to eliminate them. Pearl and Platinum found themselves outside the structure and successfully pinpointed Diamond's whereabouts inside, rescuing him due to a gathering of Combee drawn by the aroma of Honey on Diamond and his Pokémon. The episode "Find Munchlax!!" depicted a Collector employing Honey in an effort to coax out a Munchlax. Nevertheless, the Honey was instead entirely consumed by Hareta.

Sweet Honey made its debut as an Item card within the Pokémon Trading Card Game, specifically during the English Sword & Shield Series (corresponding to the Japanese Sword & Shield Era). Its initial release occurred in the Japanese Battle Region subset and the English Astral Radiance expansion, featuring illustrations by ORBITALLINK Inc. This card permits the player to select one of their Pokémon, flip a coin repeatedly until tails appears, and then restore 40 damage to that Pokémon for every heads landed. Furthermore, Honey is an item carried by Combee from the Stormfront set. When this Combee is moved from a player's hand to their Bench, it enables the player to locate a Basic Pokémon in their discard pile and place it onto their Bench. Though present within Pokémon GO's game data, Honey remains unimplemented. It is speculated that Honey may have been initially intended for Generation II, as unutilized text in those games references an item named Sweet Honey (Japanese: あまいミツ Sweet Honey).

Standard honey has appeared across different Pokémon canons, even prior to its formal introduction in Generation IV: The episode "A Sappy Ending" showcased particular trees known to yield a honey-like sap. Similar to the game item Honey, this sap demonstrated the ability to draw in wild Bug Pokémon, specifically Heracross, Butterfree, and Pinsir. In "Wake Up—You're Snorlax!", Red employed Beedrill honey, which had fallen onto his Bulbasaur, to rouse a slumbering Snorlax. Consequently, the awakened Snorlax pursued Red until he ultimately succeeded in catching it.

Title: Growth Mulch

An item first appearing in Generation IV, the Growth Mulch (Japanese: すくすくこやし Rapid Fertilizer) is a specific kind of Mulch. Its primary function is to accelerate the development of Berry plants. In the games Pokémon Black 2 and White 2, this item has a distinct value, being sellable to the mulch maniac in Mistralton City for $1000. It reduces a Berry plant's growth duration to 75% of its typical time, but this comes at the cost of the soil drying out 50% quicker. Ultimately, the Growth Mulch offers no practical utility apart from its sale.

Title: Damp Mulch

First appearing in Generation IV, the Damp Mulch (Japanese: じめじめこやし Wet Fertilizer) is an item within the Pokémon world. This particular variety of Mulch is designed to slow down the rate at which soil around Berry plants loses its moisture. In Pokémon Black 2 and White 2, the Damp Mulch offers a selling opportunity: it can be exchanged for $1000 with the mulch maniac found in Mistralton City. Its primary effect is making soil dry out 50% more slowly, though a consequence is that a Berry plant's growth time becomes 150% of the usual duration. Aside from being sold, the Damp Mulch serves no other practical function.

Title: Stable Mulch

Introduced in Generation IV, the Stable Mulch (Japanese: ながながこやし, or Drawn-Out Fertilizer) is an item designed to extend the duration Berries remain on their plants. Specifically, it allows Berries to persist for 150% of their usual time before dropping. In Pokémon Black 2 and White 2, this Mulch can be sold to the mulch maniac in Mistralton City for $1000. Its sole practical use is its sale.

Title: Gooey Mulch
The Gooey Mulch, known in Japan as ねばねばこやし (Sticky Fertilizer), first appeared as an item during Generation IV. This particular Mulch is designed to promote more frequent regrowth among withered Berry plants. Its effect specifically enables Berry plants to re-sprout from their non-viable state 150% more often than typical. Within Pokémon Black 2 and White 2, players are able to sell Gooey Mulch to Mistralton City's mulch maniac for a return of $1000. Aside from its function as a commodity for sale, the Gooey Mulch offers no other practical utility.

Title: Root Fossil

Making its debut in Generation III, the Root Fossil (known in Japanese as ねっこのカセキ, or Root Fossil) serves as a fossil item capable of being revived into a Lileep, and it stands as the direct counterpart to the Claw Fossil. Revival into Lileep is possible when the Root Fossil is presented at designated restoration facilities. Within the Generation III titles, this fossil held the classification of a Key Item. Notably, in Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen, both the Root Fossil and its Claw Fossil counterpart exist within the game's internal data, yet they are not legitimately acquirable by players and possess no functional effect. Furthermore, the Cinnabar Lab is incapable of restoring this particular fossil.

Beginning with Generation IV, the Root Fossil ceased to be a Key Item, transitioning instead to a standard item. This change allowed for multiple copies to be acquired, for the item to be held by Pokémon, and for it to be transferred to other trainers through trade.

Although the Root Fossil has not made a physical appearance in the animated series to date, an illustration of it was showcased in the episode "Where's Armaldo?". In this instance, Professor Proctor demonstrated to Ash and his companions the process by which he had resurrected numerous Lileep and Anorith from both Root and Claw Fossils.

Within the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Root Fossil made its debut as a Trainer card in the EX Sandstorm expansion, which first launched as the Japanese Miracle of the Desert expansion. Despite its Trainer card classification, it is played on the field as if it were a Colorless-type Basic Pokémon. Nevertheless, the Root Fossil remains immune to any Special Conditions, is unable to retreat, and its owner retains the option to discard it from play at any point during their turn. An additional benefit is its capacity to have one damage counter removed from itself between turns. It also serves as the prerequisite for evolving into specific Lileep cards that originate from a Root Fossil. During the EX Series of the TCG, if a Root Fossil was Knocked Out, the opposing player did not draw a Prize card, though the fossil card itself was still discarded. This specific rule underwent modification in the Diamond & Pearl Series, coinciding with its re-release in the Legends Awakened expansion, bringing its functionality in line with other Fossil cards introduced in the immediately preceding expansion.

Later, in the Black & White Series, the Plasma Blast expansion introduced an Item card called Root Fossil Lileep. This card enables players to examine the bottom seven cards of their deck, reveal any Lileep found among them, and then place that Lileep directly onto their Bench. The remaining six cards are subsequently shuffled back into the deck. Additionally, Root Fossils appear as an element on Unidentified Fossil cards. These particular cards are treated as Basic Pokémon when played and are capable of evolving into Lileep.

Main article: Antique Root Fossil (Stellar Crown 130) The Scarlet & Violet Series brought forth the Antique Root Fossil card as part of the Stellar Crown expansion, initially released as the Japanese Stellar Miracle expansion. This iteration mirrors the earlier Root Fossil card but differentiates itself with a distinct Ability: while this card occupies the Active Spot, the attacks of the opponent's Active Pokémon require an increased energy cost.

Beyond the games and card series, the Root Fossil has been depicted in various officially licensed merchandise and collectible figures. Furthermore, the emblem designating the EX Sandstorm expansion bears a strong resemblance to both the Claw and Root Fossils.

Title: Claw Fossil

Hailing from Generation III, the Claw Fossil (ツメのカセキ in Japanese) serves as a fossil item capable of yielding an Anorith upon restoration. It stands as the direct opposite to the Root Fossil. Restoration into Anorith is possible when this fossil is presented at designated facilities. Across the Generation III titles, the Claw Fossil held the classification of a Key Item. Within Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen, although present in the game's internal data alongside the Root Fossil, it was neither legitimately acquirable nor functional. Its regeneration was specifically impossible at the Cinnabar Lab in these versions. Commencing with Generation IV, the Claw Fossil transitioned from a Key Item to a regular inventory item. This change enabled players to acquire multiple copies, have Pokémon hold them, and exchange them with others through trading.

In the episode "Where's Armaldo?", a depiction of a Claw Fossil was displayed as Professor Proctor elaborated to Ash and his companions on his process of revitalizing numerous Lileep and Anorith from both Root and Claw Fossils. The episode "Restore and Renew!" featured a Claw Fossil in the possession of the Pewter Museum of Science Curator.

The Pokémon Trading Card Game debuted the Claw Fossil as a Trainer card within the EX Sandstorm expansion, which itself was initially launched as Japan's Miracle of the Desert expansion. Despite its Trainer card classification, it functions as a Basic Pokémon of the Colorless type. Notably, the Claw Fossil remains unaffected by any Special Conditions, is unable to retreat, and can be discarded by its owner at any point during their turn. Furthermore, should it occupy the Active Spot and sustain damage from an opposing attack, its effect permits the player to place a single damage counter on the Attacking Pokémon. This card facilitates evolution into Anorith specifically for those Anorith that evolve from a Claw Fossil. In the EX Series, knocking out a Claw Fossil did not grant a Prize card to the opponent, although the card was still discarded. This rule was revised during the Diamond & Pearl Series, commencing with its re-release in the Legends Awakened expansion, aligning its mechanics with other Fossil cards from the preceding expansion. The XY Series introduced the Item card "Claw Fossil Anorith" within the Steam Siege expansion. This card enables players to inspect the bottom seven cards of their deck, unveil any Anorith discovered among them, and then place that Anorith onto their Bench. The remaining six cards are subsequently reshuffled into the deck. Claw Fossils additionally appear depicted on Unidentified Fossil cards. These specific cards are treated as Basic Pokémon when played and can also evolve into Anorith.

The Claw Fossil has also been prominently featured across various lines of officially licensed merchandise. Furthermore, the emblem for the EX Sandstorm expansion bears a likeness to both the Claw and Root Fossils.

Title: Helix Fossil

Originating in Generation I, the Helix Fossil (known in Japanese as かいのカセキ, or "Shellfish Fossil") is an ancient relic capable of being revitalized into an Omanyte. It serves as the thematic counterpart to the Dome Fossil. Bringing this item to designated regeneration sites allows for its transformation into Omanyte.

In the Generation I and III video games, the Helix Fossil functioned as a Key Item. Interestingly, within Pokémon Emerald's game data, the Helix Fossil (alongside the Dome Fossil and Old Amber) exists but is neither legitimately obtainable nor does it possess any in-game effect. Players are also unable to regenerate this specific fossil at the Devon Corporation. However, a change occurred from Generation IV onward, where the Helix Fossil ceased being a Key Item and instead became a normal item. This reclassification permits the acquisition of multiple Helix Fossils, enables them to be held by Pokémon, and facilitates their exchange with other players via trading.

The Helix Fossil was first introduced as an Item card in Pokémon Trading Card Game Pocket within the Genetic Apex expansion. Consistent with its TCG counterparts, this card is played as if it were a Colorless-type Basic Pokémon. A Helix Fossil made a brief visual appearance in File 2: Cubone, depicted being held tightly by a Super Nerd at Mt. Moon.

Within the main Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Helix Fossil debuted as a Trainer card in the Majestic Dawn expansion, which first launched in Japan as the Moonlit Pursuit expansion. As a Fossil Trainer card, once played, it is placed onto the Bench as a Colorless-type Basic Pokémon. Should a player attach a Water Energy card from their hand to the Helix Fossil, its unique Poké-Body activates, allowing them to search their deck for an Omanyte that specifically evolves from a Helix Fossil and then evolve the Helix Fossil into that Omanyte. Afterwards, the player must shuffle their deck.

During the XY Series, the Fates Collide expansion featured an Item card titled Helix Fossil Omanyte (card 102). This card provides players with the ability to examine the bottom seven cards of their deck, reveal any Omanyte they discover there, and place it directly onto their Bench. The remaining six cards are then shuffled back into the deck. The Scarlet & Violet Series brought forth the Antique Helix Fossil in the 151 expansion. This particular card functions similarly to the original Helix Fossil, also being a Fossil Trainer card. It boasts an Ability that, as long as Antique Helix Fossil occupies the Active Spot, prevents the opponent from playing any Stadium cards from their hand. Furthermore, Helix Fossils are depicted on the Mysterious Fossil and Unidentified Fossil cards, both Fossil Trainer cards capable of evolving into certain Omanyte variations. A Helix Fossil is also featured on Buried Fossil, which is a Pokémon that evolves in the same manner as Mysterious Fossil.

Beyond its in-game and card appearances, the Helix Fossil has been immortalized in various officially licensed figures and merchandise. A significant cultural phenomenon surrounding the item emerged during the initial Pokémon Red and Blue run of Twitch Plays Pokémon. The project's famously chaotic control scheme inadvertently caused the player character to constantly interact with the Helix Fossil. This repeated interaction sparked humor, leading to jokes that the protagonist was perpetually consulting it for guidance. This rapidly escalated into widespread memes depicting the player's worship of the Helix Fossil, leading fans to affectionately refer to Omanyte as "Lord Helix." In a clear nod to this internet meme, Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire contain a specific dialogue. When the game's language is set to French, prior to battle, Brawly remarks, "J'ai découvert le secret de la vraie puissance en fixant un Nautile pendant des jours et des jours..." (translating to: "I discovered the secret of true power by staring at a Helix Fossil for days and days..."). Finally, a Helix Fossil is prominently displayed on Poké Lid #141 in Mikasa, Japan, positioned alongside two Omanyte.

Title: Dome Fossil

Known in Japan as こうらのカセキ (Carapace Fossil), the Dome Fossil debuted in Generation I as a reconstructible fossil item, yielding the Pokémon Kabuto. It stands as the direct counterpart to the Helix Fossil. Bringing it to specific in-game facilities allows for its revitalization into Kabuto.

Within the initial and third generations of the main series games, the Dome Fossil was categorized as a Key Item. Notably, in Pokémon Emerald, though its data (alongside the Helix Fossil and Old Amber) exists within the game, it is neither legitimately acquirable nor functional. Players cannot revive this particular fossil at the Devon Corporation. Beginning with Generation IV, its status changed from a Key Item to a regular item, enabling players to possess multiple copies, have Pokémon hold them, and facilitate their exchange through trading.

The Genetic Apex expansion of Pokémon Trading Card Game Pocket marked the introduction of Dome Fossil as an Item card. Consistent with its other TCG versions, it functions during gameplay as if it were a Colorless-type Basic Pokémon.

The animated series featured Dome Fossils prominently in "Shell Shock!", where they formed the core structure of Fukuhara Island No. 4. There, under the influence of red moonlight, they regenerated into Kabuto, leading to the island's subsequent collapse and submersion. Another brief appearance occurred in "Ancient Family Matters!", serving as part of a visual metaphor for Roark's enthusiasm for Fossils during a discussion with Byron. In "A Night in the Nacrene City Museum!", a Dome Fossil initially appeared to be pursuing Hawes, as he asserted, before its secure placement within a display case. Hawes subsequently clarified its nature to Ash and his companions. It was later unveiled that a Yamask residing in the Nacrene City Museum was employing its Psychic move to manipulate the Dome Fossil, causing it to float and chase Hawes. This occurred after Hawes had placed the Yamask's own mask into a display case, mistakenly believing it to be a replica Yamask mask that he had received as a gift from the company responsible for the Cofagrigus replica he had acquired for the museum.

In the manga, specifically "File 2: Cubone", a Dome Fossil made a short appearance, observed in the possession of a Super Nerd at Mt. Moon.

Within the Pokémon Trading Card Game, Dome Fossil debuted as a Trainer card in the Majestic Dawn expansion, initially appearing in the Japanese Dawn Dash expansion. Classified as a Fossil Trainer card, upon its activation, it is moved to the Bench and functions as a Colorless-type Basic Pokémon. Should a player attach a Fighting Energy from their hand to Dome Fossil, its special Poké-Body permits them to search their deck for a Kabuto that specifically evolves from a Dome Fossil, then immediately evolve the Dome Fossil into that Kabuto. Following this action, the player must shuffle their deck.

The XY Series introduced the Item card "Dome Fossil Kabuto" (Fates Collide 96) in the Fates Collide expansion. This card enables players to examine the bottom seven cards of their deck, reveal any Kabuto discovered among them, and place it directly onto their Bench. The remaining six cards are then reintegrated into the deck by shuffling. More recently, the Scarlet & Violet Series' 151 expansion presented "Antique Dome Fossil", another Fossil Trainer card that mirrors the original Dome Fossil's functionality. This iteration also possesses an Ability that reduces incoming attack damage by 30. Furthermore, Dome Fossils appear on the "Mysterious Fossil" card, a Fossil Trainer card capable of evolving into Kabuto. While "Unidentified Fossil" does not depict a Dome Fossil, it similarly facilitates evolution into Kabuto. In contrast, "Buried Fossil" is a dedicated Pokémon card, visually featuring what appears to be a fossilized Kabuto, and its evolution mechanism mirrors that of Mysterious Fossil.

The Dome Fossil has also been incorporated into various officially licensed merchandise and figures. Notably, Poké Lid #274 in Toyohashi, Japan, showcases a Dome Fossil accompanying a Kabuto.

Title: Old Amber

Introduced in Generation I, the Old Amber (known in Japanese as ひみつのコハク, or Secret Amber) functions as a fossil capable of being revived into the Pokémon Aerodactyl. Within the core series titles, the Pewter Museum of Science additionally showcases the complete fossilized skeleton of an Aerodactyl. Transporting the Old Amber to specific designated sites enables its transformation into an Aerodactyl. Across the Generation I games, as well as in Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen, Old Amber held the status of a Key Item. However, in Pokémon Emerald, despite its item data being migrated from FireRed and LeafGreen, the option to regenerate it into an Aerodactyl was deactivated. Starting from Generation IV, Old Amber ceased to be classified as a Key Item.

The Old Amber made its debut as an Item card within the Pokémon Trading Card Game Pocket, appearing in the Genetic Apex expansion. Consistent with its other TCG versions, it is utilized in play as though it were a Colorless-type Basic Pokémon.

In the episode "Putting the Air Back in Aerodactyl!", Gary successfully brought an Old Amber back to life, yielding an Aerodactyl. Later, in "Restore and Renew!", Goh unearthed an Old Amber at a digging location within the Pewter Museum of Science, subsequently overseeing its regeneration into an Aerodactyl, which he then successfully captured. The Old Amber also featured in "Fossil + Pokémon = Aerodactyl!?", culminating in its revival into an Aerodactyl within a laboratory setting. Within the manga, during "Make Way for Magmar!", Red was given an Old Amber by Giovanni in Pewter City. Subsequently, in "Holy Moltres", adhering to Blaine's directives, Red resurrected the Old Amber into an Aerodactyl at the Pokémon Lab situated on Cinnabar Island, thereby integrating it as a key member of his team.

The Pokémon Trading Card Game saw the introduction of the Old Amber as a Trainer card within the Majestic Dawn expansion, which initially launched as the Japanese Dawn Dash expansion. Classified as a Fossil Trainer card, it is immediately placed onto the Bench as a Colorless-type Basic Pokémon upon being played. This card features a Poké-Body ability that completely negates any damage it would sustain from attacks while it occupies the Bench. It possesses the ability to evolve into Aerodactyl cards specifically designed to evolve from Old Amber. (For further details, refer to the main article: Old Amber Aerodactyl (Dark Explorers 97)).

Within the Black & White Series, the Dark Explorers expansion presented an Item card named Old Amber Aerodactyl. This card provides players with the option to inspect the bottom seven cards of their deck, disclose any Aerodactyl discovered among them, and then move that Aerodactyl to their Bench. The remaining six cards are subsequently re-integrated into the deck by shuffling. The Scarlet & Violet Series introduced Antique Old Amber as part of the 151 expansion. This particular card bears a resemblance to the initial Old Amber, sharing its classification as a Fossil Trainer card. It is equipped with an Ability that renders it immune to all effects from the Abilities of an opponent's Pokémon.

Because card effects rely on specific name identification, numerous effects fail to identify Old Amber cards as actual Fossil cards. This holds true even when the card itself is explicitly designated as a Fossil Trainer card. Both Mysterious Fossil and Unidentified Fossil also exist as Fossil Trainer cards; while neither depicts Old Amber, they are capable of evolving into specific Aerodactyl variants. Buried Fossil follows a similar naming convention but is categorized as a standard Pokémon, and despite lacking Old Amber, it can evolve in the same manner as Mysterious Fossil.

The Old Amber has been depicted in various officially licensed merchandise and figures. Its visual design appears to pay homage to the theory, widely disseminated by George Poinar Jr., concerning the extraction of DNA from insects preserved within fossilized amber. This theoretical connection was initially more apparent, given the inclusion of a mosquito in the item's Generation I artwork.

Title: Armor Fossil

Hailing from Generation IV, the Armor Fossil (known in Japanese as たてのカセキ, or Shield Fossil) is a prehistoric remnant that can be revitalized into the Pokémon Shieldon. It stands as the direct counterpart to the Skull Fossil. When presented at particular designated locations, this artifact can successfully be regenerated into a living Shieldon.

Within the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Armor Fossil made its debut as a Trainer card in the Mysterious Treasures expansion (Japanese release: Secret of the Lakes). Identified by its main article reference Armor Fossil (Mysterious Treasures 116), this Fossil Trainer card, once played, is placed on the Bench and functions as a Colorless-type Basic Pokémon. It possesses a special Poké-Body that activates if it sustains damage from an opponent's Pokémon's attack: the owner of Armor Fossil may continuously flip coins until a tails appears, and for every heads, the incoming damage from the Attacking Pokémon is diminished by ten. Furthermore, it serves as the base for evolving into Shieldon.

The XY Series later saw the introduction of the Item card Armor Fossil Shieldon, featured in the Steam Siege expansion (main article: Armor Fossil Shieldon (Steam Siege 98)). This card grants players the ability to inspect the bottom seven cards of their deck. Should a Shieldon be among them, the player may reveal it and place it directly onto their Bench, after which the remaining six cards are thoroughly shuffled back into the deck.

Beyond these specific cards, Armor Fossils are also depicted on Unidentified Fossil cards, which are another category of Fossil Trainer cards capable of evolving into particular Shieldon variants. An Armor Fossil is additionally visible within the illustration of the Fossil Excavator card from the Mysterious Treasures expansion. Finally, the Armor Fossil has been a recurring subject across various lines of officially licensed merchandise.

Title: Skull Fossil

Known in Japanese as ずがいのカセキ (Skull Fossil), this particular fossil item made its debut in Generation IV. It serves as the genetic precursor to Cranidos, capable of being revived into the Pokémon, and stands as the direct opposite to the Armor Fossil. When taken to designated revival facilities within the game, the Skull Fossil can be successfully transformed into a Cranidos.

Its presence in the anime adaptation commenced with its excavation from the Oreburgh Mine, depicted in the episode "Shapes of Things to Come!". Gym Leader Roark displayed considerable enthusiasm for it, even speaking to it with the conviction that it would eventually regain life. Subsequently, he arranged for its transfer to the Oreburgh Mining Museum. Later, Kiawe discovered another Skull Fossil during the "Stoutland Search" treasure hunt, organized for Ash and his classmates by Olivia, in the episode "Treasure Hunt, Akala Style!". Moreover, a holographic display featuring a Skull Fossil was observed during Sophocles's initial treasure-seeking endeavor.

Within the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Skull Fossil was introduced as card 117 from the *Mysterious Treasures* expansion, originally appearing in Japan's *Secret of the Lakes* set. Classified as a Fossil Trainer card, once activated, it occupies the Bench as a Colorless-type Basic Pokémon. It possesses a unique Poké-Body: should it be Knocked Out by damage from an opposing Pokémon's attack, its owner may continuously flip coins until a tails appears, placing one damage counter on the Attacking Pokémon for each heads. This card facilitates the evolution into Cranidos specifically originating from a Skull Fossil. Furthermore, the Unidentified Fossil cards, which are also Fossil Trainer cards, prominently display Skull Fossils and allow for their evolution into Cranidos.

Beyond the games and anime, the Skull Fossil has been prominently featured across various lines of officially sanctioned merchandise.

Title: Rare Bone

Introduced during Generation IV, the Rare Bone (known as きちょうなホネ, or "Valuable Bone," in Japanese) is categorized as a valuable item. Its primary function across all games is solely monetary; it possesses no utility beyond being sold.

For players of the Generation V titles, this item can be exchanged for $10,000 when presented to the Rare Bone maniac found on Route 18. Later, in the Galar region's Pokémon Sword and Shield, a different vendor—the valuable item buyer in Stow-on-Side—will purchase it for $11,111.

In the original Sinnoh adventures, specifically Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum, discovering a Rare Bone while excavating in the Underground adds to the player's Fossil count. However, in the remakes, Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, the Rare Bone discovered within the Grand Underground displays a distinct whitened version of the artwork originally sourced from the Pokémon Global Link. Notably, it stands as the sole in-game item whose sprite diverges from its Global Link equivalent. Before the release of Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, the Rare Bone's Bag sprite was indistinguishable from that of the Thick Club.

Title: Shiny Stone

This evolutionary item, known in Japan as ひかりのいし (Light Stone), first appeared in the fourth generation of Pokémon games. Within the Generation V titles, players could sell a Shiny Stone to an item enthusiast found in Icirrus City's Pokémon Center for a sum of $3000. Its primary function is to trigger the evolution of certain Pokémon, a process that expends the stone. The item is also featured in the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, specifically in Explorers of Time, Darkness, and Sky, as well as Gates to Infinity. In these spin-off titles, it facilitates the evolution of various Pokémon, either by being offered at the Luminous Spring or by being utilized within a dungeon, always resulting in its consumption. When thrown, the Shiny Stone inflicts damage equivalent to 1/2 of a foe's HP, or 2 HP if it possesses the sticky status. Notably, in Explorers of Time, Darkness, and Sky, employing this item during a dungeon run still uses it up but yields no practical effect.

Pokémon Conquest presents a unique evolution method: Minccino transforms into Cinccino when its link stat is enhanced while its accompanying Warrior holds a Shiny Stone. In Conquest, this stone can be acquired from the traveling merchant for 5000 gold and subsequently sold for 2500 to 3250 gold. Within Pokémon Sleep, the Shiny Stone is a prerequisite for evolving specific Pokémon and is consumed during the process. For instance, Togetic's evolution into Togekiss demands both 80 Togepi Candy and a Shiny Stone.

The episode "Mystery on a Deserted Island!" showcased the Shiny Stone alongside every other Evolution stone accessible up to Generation V. "The Cave of Trials!" depicted a complete collection of Evolution stones displayed within a stone shop located in Geosenge Town. In "Leaping Past Lopunny," Steven's collection of rare stones, discovered during his time in Sinnoh, included a Shiny Stone. Lance gifted Gold's Togepi a Shiny Stone in "All About Arceus II," which then facilitated its transformation into Togekiss in "All About Arceus VIII" following an immediate evolution into Togetic. The artwork for the Togekiss card from the Undaunted expansion features the Shiny Stone. It is also included alongside other Evolution stones in a merchandise collection bearing the same collective name.

A potential localization error might have resulted in the swapping of names between the Shiny Stone and the Dawn Stone. In the original Japanese, the Shiny Stone is known as the "Light Stone" and the Dusk Stone as the "Darkness Stone," implying their design as complementary day and night items. This particular naming convention would be more logical if the Shiny Stone had instead been designated the Dawn Stone, given dawn's direct opposition to dusk. Conversely, the Japanese title for the Dawn Stone, "Awakening Stone," better aligns with its function in prompting unique, alternative evolutions rather than adhering to a "light-dark" thematic pair. The English name "Dawn Stone" might have been selected due to the natural association between dawn and awakening.

Title: Dusk Stone

The Dusk Stone, known in Japanese as やみのいし (Darkness Stone), was introduced as an Evolution stone type in Generation IV. In the Generation V main series games, an item maniac inside the Icirrus City Pokémon Center will purchase the Dusk Stone for $3000. This item facilitates the evolution of several Pokémon, a process which invariably consumes the stone. The Dusk Stone also makes appearances in the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series: Explorers of Time, Darkness, and Sky, as well as Gates to Infinity. In these titles, it can prompt the evolution of certain Pokémon by being offered at the Luminous Spring or used within a dungeon, in both cases being consumed. When thrown, it inflicts 1/2 damage (or 2 damage if sticky). Notably, in Explorers of Time, Darkness, and Sky, using the item in a dungeon will consume it with no additional effect. Pokémon Conquest features a unique application for the Dusk Stone; Misdreavus and Lampent will evolve into Mismagius and Chandelure, respectively, when their link improves while their Warrior is equipped with one. In Conquest, the traveling merchant sells a Dusk Stone for 5000 gold, and it can be sold back for 2500-3250 gold. In Pokémon Sleep, the Dusk Stone is a prerequisite for evolving particular Pokémon and is consumed upon use; for example, Murkrow needs 80 Murkrow Candy and a Dusk Stone to evolve into Honchkrow. The anime debut of the Dusk Stone was in 'Try for the Family Stone!', an episode where Rhyanna and Mitchell sought one to evolve their Pokémon. They eventually located it and opted for a battle to decide who would claim its use. 'Mystery on a Deserted Island!' later showcased the Dusk Stone alongside all other Evolution stones available as of Generation V. A comprehensive collection of Evolution stones was observed on display at a stone shop in Geosenge Town in 'The Cave of Trials!'. In 'Dealing With A Koffing Fit', Silver's Murkrow evolved into Honchkrow after gaining experience while holding a Dusk Stone, which Green had previously given to Silver. A subsequent gallery compiles cards that are named after, hold, mention, or feature the Dusk Stone. This item has also been included, alongside the other Evolution stones, in a merchandise collection bearing the same name. Despite its availability in Pokémon Platinum, the Pokémon that evolve through its use are unobtainable in that game without trading.

Title: Dawn Stone

Known in Japan as めざめいし (Awakening Stone), the Dawn Stone first appeared as an Evolution stone in Generation IV. In the Generation V games, this item can be sold to an item maniac within the Icirrus City Pokémon Center for $3000. Its primary function is to facilitate the evolution of certain Pokémon, a process which invariably consumes the item. The Dawn Stone is also featured in the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon titles: Explorers of Time, Explorers of Darkness, and Explorers of Sky. Within these games, presenting a Dawn Stone at Luminous Spring enables a male Kirlia to become Gallade and a female Snorunt to evolve into Froslass, with the stone being used up. When thrown, it inflicts 1 damage (or 2 if sticky). Using this item directly within a dungeon consumes it without providing any other effect.

In Pokémon Conquest, an alternative method for evolution exists: a male Warrior's Kirlia or a female Warrior's Snorunt will evolve into Gallade or Froslass, respectively, upon improving their link while their Warrior is equipped with a Dawn Stone. Within Conquest, a Dawn Stone can be purchased from the traveling merchant for 5000 gold and subsequently sold for 2500 to 3250 gold. Its utility extends to Pokémon Sleep, where the Dawn Stone is an essential, single-use item required for certain evolutions. Specifically, the evolution of a male Kirlia into Gallade requires both 80 Ralts Candy and a Dawn Stone.

The Dawn Stone's appearances extend beyond the games. The anime episode "Mystery on a Deserted Island!" showcased the Dawn Stone among all other Evolution stones available up to Generation V. A full complement of Evolution stones was later observed on display in a stone shop located in Geosenge Town, as depicted in "The Cave of Trials!". In "Leaping Past Lopunny", Steven's assortment of rare stones, collected during his stay in Sinnoh, featured a Dawn Stone. In the manga chapter "Metagross Investigates", Sapphire's Kirlia, Kirly, underwent evolution into Gallade upon being hit by a Dawn Stone during a training session with Rara, who had already evolved into a Gardevoir. Steven later discovered another Dawn Stone on the ground. It has also been featured in the "Evolution stones" merchandise collection, sharing its name with the other items.

Boasting merely two applications, the Dawn Stone distinguishes itself as having the fewest uses among all Evolution stones. It stands as the singular Evolution stone whose effect on evolution is contingent upon the target Pokémon's specific gender, a prerequisite that applies to both species it can evolve. The two Pokémon requiring the Dawn Stone for evolution first appeared in Generation III, forming part of a branched evolutionary line, while their subsequent evolved forms represent cross-generational evolutions introduced in Generation IV. Upon evolving, each acquires a secondary type absent in its prior stage: Froslass obtains the Ghost type, and Gallade gains the Fighting type. Notably, both evolved Pokémon associated with the Dawn Stone are capable of Mega Evolution, establishing it as the only Evolution stone for which all of its final forms can Mega Evolve. Furthermore, the Dawn Stone uniquely stands as the only Evolution stone that has not acquired any new applications past its debut generation. Although Generation V introduced at least one new application for every other Evolution stone available at the time, the Dawn Stone remained without further expanded functionality.

Title: Oval Stone

First appearing in Generation IV, the Oval Stone (Japanese: まんまるいし Oval Stone) functions as a held item. Its primary purpose is as an Evolution item, facilitating Happiny's transformation into Chansey. Within the Generation V titles, players have the option to divest themselves of an Oval Stone for a sum of $1500 to the item maniac located within Icirrus City's Pokémon Center. Happiny will evolve into Chansey when it levels up during the day while equipped with this item; the Oval Stone is consumed in the process. Uniquely in Pokémon Legends: Arceus, Happiny's evolution into Chansey occurs by simply exposing it to an Oval Stone during daytime.

Its presence is also noted across the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon titles: Explorers of Time, Explorers of Darkness, and Explorers of Sky. At Luminous Spring, offering both a Sun Ribbon and an Oval Stone enables the player to evolve Happiny into Chansey, with both items being expended. When hurled, it inflicts a single point of damage (or two if it possesses the sticky property). Employing this item within a dungeon will result in its consumption without any discernible effect.

The episode "One Big Happiny Family!" depicted Brock carving an Oval Stone for his recently hatched Happiny, which subsequently aided her evolution into a Chansey in "The Brockster Is In!". It also received a brief mention during "Passing by Probopass and Maneuvering around Magnezone." Its likeness can be observed within the illustration of the HeartGold & SoulSilver Chansey trading card.

Despite possessing a sprite for the Underground in Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum, the Oval Stone is, in fact, absent from that location within those particular games. Prior to Generation VIII, the game interface treated the Oval Stone similarly to an Evolution stone. When chosen from the Bag, it presents a "Use" option, and upon activation, it transitions to the Pokémon party roster, much like an Evolution stone, yet every Pokémon is consistently marked as "Unable." During Generation V, its classification within the Bag was erroneous, appearing as a general item instead of its proper designation as a held item. If one sorts items by type, the Oval Stone finds itself among Evolution stones, bypassing its true category of Evolution-triggering held items. Notably, among all Evolution items bearing "Stone" in their English nomenclature, the Oval Stone stands alone in not being an actual Evolution stone.

Title: Odd Keystone

The Odd Keystone, known as かなめいし (Keystone) in Japanese, is an item category first introduced in Generation IV. Odd Keystones function as receptacles, designed to contain and seal away Spiritomb. All Spiritomb utilize this item as their foundational anchor. In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, this item is categorized as a Key Item.

Players in the Generation V titles have the option to sell the Odd Keystone for $3000 to the ore enthusiast located within the Icirrus City Pokémon Center. Across Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, Platinum, Brilliant Diamond, and Shining Pearl, the Odd Keystone finds its purpose when inserted into the Hallowed Tower, a landmark situated on Sinnoh's Route 209. Beyond the Sinnoh region, this particular item serves no functional purpose.

After an Odd Keystone is placed, engaging in conversation with 32 or more other players in the Sinnoh Underground (or 32 or more NPCs in the Grand Underground) will trigger an encounter with a wild Spiritomb upon the player's subsequent examination of the Hallowed Tower. Specifically in Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum, the requirement for interacting with 32 distinct players is theoretically stated, yet practically, a single player interaction can be registered multiple times. This occurs if the player character exits the Underground or utilizes the "close door and decorate" feature of their Secret Base between each conversation, thereby enabling an encounter with Spiritomb by repeatedly speaking to the same individual 32 times. Once embedded in the Hallowed Tower, inspecting the Odd Keystone allows players to monitor their progression, with its displayed message subtly altering as more interactions occur within the Underground.

Turning to Pokémon Legends: Arceus, once Vessa bestows the Odd Keystone upon the player, the subsequent objective is to locate 107 spectral wisps scattered across the Hisui region. The Odd Keystone, visible in the satchel, displays the current count of collected wisps. Upon successfully gathering all 107 wisps and conversing with Vessa once more, the player receives instructions to proceed to the Shrouded Ruins, where Vessa awaits along with the 108th and final wisp. Once the 108th wisp is found, the Odd Keystone disappears from the player's possession. Subsequently, interacting with the enormous, mysterious statue resembling an Odd Keystone, located in the Shrouded Ruins, will trigger a Spiritomb encounter. The Odd Keystone itself is distinctly observable as a part of Spiritomb's physical form. Furthermore, in Pokémon Legends: Arceus, should an Alpha Spiritomb materialize, its eyes, along with the entire surface of the Odd Keystone, will distinctly glow red.

In Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, an Odd Keystone manifests in the overworld on Sea Mauville. This instance cannot be acquired by the player, appearing merely moments prior to a Spiritomb battle. Ship records indicate its origin as a donation from Oreburgh Mine.

Within Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Explorers of Time, Darkness, and Sky, upon reaching the Sealed Ruin Pit, the player and their partner observe what appears to be an Odd Keystone, initially unaware of its true nature as a Pokémon. Abruptly, it transforms into Spiritomb, initiating an attack on the pair. Furthermore, in Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Explorers of Time, Darkness, and Sky, Spiritomb's visual representation when asleep shows it receding back into its Odd Keystone form.

During the episode "The Keystone Pops!", a training match between Ash's Buizel and Dawn's Aipom saw Buizel's Sonic Boom inadvertently demolish the Hallowed Tower. This action inadvertently freed a Spiritomb from the Odd Keystone that had been sealed within. Ash and Pikachu subsequently engaged Spiritomb in a battle, ultimately prevailing and re-confining it to the Odd Keystone, which was then reinstated within the reconstructed Hallowed Tower.

The episode "The Spectral Express!" depicts Ash and Goh discovering an Odd Keystone while aboard a ghost train. Ash's touch caused one of Spiritomb's constituent spirits to possess him, prompting him to engage in mischievous behavior. Enraged by the loss of its spirit, Spiritomb emerged from the keystone and initiated a rampage. After the spirit was expelled from Ash's body and Ash's Gengar successfully compelled its return to Spiritomb, the Forbidden Pokémon ceased its fury and retreated back into its keystone. Later, a cluster of Gastly and Haunter absconded with the keystone when the spectral train paused at a cemetery.

In "Bewitch, Battle, and Bewilder!", an Odd Keystone was shown integrated with Cynthia's Spiritomb. The intrinsic link between Cynthia's Spiritomb and an Odd Keystone was vividly demonstrated in "Shunning Spiritomb", specifically when the Forbidden Pokémon, after being frozen by Cyrus's Weavile, withdrew entirely into the stone. The episode "Leaping Past Lopunny" featured an Odd Keystone among a collection of uncommon stones that Steven had unearthed during his visit to the Sinnoh region.

Title: Adamant Orb

Originating in Generation IV, the Adamant Orb (Japanese: こんごうだま Diamond Orb) is a distinct type of held item.
