When Dialga grips this item, it significantly amplifies the potency of its Dragon- and Steel-type moves. This artifact stands among the revered orbs historically linked to the Pokémon of myth. Specifically, if Dialga holds it, its Dragon- and Steel-type move power experiences a 20% increase. In the Pokémon Platinum game, possessing this orb is a prerequisite for encountering Dialga at the Spear Pillar.

The Adamant Orb first appeared in the anime episode "A Secret Sphere of Influence!", where Team Galactic charged Team Rocket with its larceny from the Eterna Historical Museum. Team Rocket successfully executed the heist, falsely implicating Nando, yet ultimately failed in their attempt to escape with the orb. Subsequently, in "Losing Its Lustrous!", Team Galactic launched an assault on a police aircraft ferrying the Adamant Orb from Eterna City to Celestic Town, seizing it in the process. Within "Double Team Turnover!", after Team Galactic had acquired both legendary orbs, the Lustrous and Adamant Orbs were depicted connected to sophisticated machinery at the Team Galactic HQ. During these particular episodes, Cyrus voiced his conviction that the Adamant Orb is intrinsically tied to Dialga, and the Lustrous Orb to Palkia. By the culmination of "Unlocking the Red Chain of Events!", Team Galactic was observed transporting the orbs, along with the Red Chains, toward the Spear Pillar. In "The Battle Finale of Legend!", Cyrus strategically positioned the orbs and the lake guardians around the ruins at the Spear Pillar. This ceremonial act enabled him to summon forth Dialga and Palkia, who immediately succumbed to his dominion through the Red Chains. After Cyrus's aspiration to forge a new world was thwarted and Team Galactic was vanquished, Gary secured these orbs, deciding to entrust their return to Professor Carolina. A brief mention of the Adamant Orb was made by Platinum in "Double Trouble with Dialga and Palkia VI".

In the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Adamant Orb is depicted as a held item featured on the Majestic Dawn Dialga card. The functionality of this held item mirrors that of a Poké-Body, in that its specified effect remains active continuously whenever the Pokémon is in play.

Title: Lustrous Orb

Referred to in Japanese as しらたま (Pearl Orb), the Lustrous Orb is an equippable item first appearing in Generation IV. When held by Palkia, it enhances the potency of its Dragon- and Water-type moves. This artifact is recognized as one of the mystical orbs associated with legendary Pokémon. Specifically, if Palkia is holding it, the power of its Dragon- and Water-type attacks increases by 20%. In the game Pokémon Platinum, possession of the Lustrous Orb is requisite for encountering Palkia at the Spear Pillar.

In the anime episode "Top-Down Training!", Sinnoh Now reported that archaeologists had unearthed the Lustrous Orb—an artifact linked to the Legendary Pokémon Palkia—at the Celestic Ruins, located near the Celestic Town Historical Research Center. During "Losing Its Lustrous!", Professor Carolina was engaged in studying the Lustrous Orb and was in the process of having the Adamant Orb transported there for joint research. Upon receiving news of Team Galactic's theft of the Adamant Orb, Officer Jenny and her police force placed the Lustrous Orb under stringent security. Professor Carolina later revealed the Orb's sequestered location to Ash, his companions, Cynthia, and Cyrus for further analysis. However, after Mars triggered explosives outside the building, the entrance to the Orb room failed to properly seal. As the group attempted to move the Lustrous Orb to a different spot, Team Galactic burst in, initiating a confrontation with the group and Cynthia.

"Double Team Turnover!" documented Team Rocket's successful acquisition of the Lustrous Orb while masquerading as police officers. Ash, his friends, and Team Galactic subsequently pursued Team Rocket to the Celestic Ruins, aiming to reclaim the Orb. Cornered, Team Rocket threatened to have James's Carnivine devour the item. Mars's Purugly then unexpectedly leaped onto Carnivine's head and snatched the Lustrous Orb, enabling Team Galactic to escape with it. Back at Team Galactic HQ, both the Lustrous and Adamant Orbs were displayed connected to some form of machinery.

In "If the Scarf Fits, Wear It!", an illustration of the Lustrous Orb appeared in a Sinnoh Star article that Brock was perusing. The two Orbs made a brief appearance at the close of "Unlocking the Red Chain of Events!", where Team Galactic was observed transporting them, along with the Red Chains, to the Spear Pillar. The Orbs resurfaced in "The Battle Finale of Legend!", where Cyrus arranged them and the lake guardians around the ruins at the Spear Pillar. This ritual allowed him to summon Dialga and Palkia, both of whom were immediately brought under his dominion by the Red Chains. After Cyrus's scheme to forge a new world was thwarted and Team Galactic suffered defeat, Gary secured the Orbs and decided to return them to Professor Carolina and the Celestic Research Center. Platinum made a fleeting mention of the Lustrous Orb in "Double Trouble with Dialga and Palkia VI".

The Lustrous Orb is featured in the Pokémon Trading Card Game as a held item on the Majestic Dawn Palkia card. This particular held item operates in a manner similar to a Poké-Body, meaning the effect it bestows remains active whenever the Pokémon is in play.

Title: Grass Mail

Known in Japanese as グラスメール (Grass Mail), this specific variety of Mail item is solely found within the Generation IV Pokémon titles. Its primary function is to store a message, which can consist of up to three distinct sentence components, populated with selections from the easy chat system's predetermined lexicon of words and phrases. For example, a Trainer might choose the phrase "I love ______________!" and subsequently fill the blank with "BULBASAUR". A key design aspect ensures its readability across all linguistic versions of the games. The message content is composed by the player at the moment the item is assigned to a Pokémon as its held item. Communication between games of the same generation is facilitated by trading a Pokémon that is holding this item, thereby transferring the embedded message. The stored message persists as long as the Grass Mail is either actively held by a Pokémon or resides in the player's Mailbox. However, when deposited into the Bag or the Item Storage System, this item cannot contain a message; an option is provided to erase any existing message upon its placement in these locations. Following the deletion of its message, the item becomes available for reuse with fresh content. Critically, a Pokémon clutching this item cannot be deposited into the PC system or entrusted to the Pokémon Day Care. Furthermore, its embedded message is not decipherable within the titles Pokémon Battle Revolution or My Pokémon Ranch. In alignment with other held items, Pokémon carrying Grass Mail cannot be transported into Generation V games using either the Poké Transfer or Relocator mechanisms. This item is susceptible to removal via the move Knock Off. Nevertheless, it resists theft from Covet or Thief, causes the holder's Fling to invariably fail, and prevents Switcheroo or Trick from succeeding, whether initiated by or targeting the item's possessor. For details on Webster's mail from Generation II, consult the entry for Flower Mail → Webster's mail. Within Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, a specific side quest involves the player transporting a Grass Mail originating from Webster on Route 35 to an NPC located on Route 31, receiving TM44 (Rest) as compensation. Although initially carried by a Spearow designated as Kenya, the delivery can be completed using any Pokémon. Should the mail be handed over by a Pokémon other than the specified Spearow, that Pokémon will be promptly returned to the player subsequent to the delivery. Successfully reporting the completion of this delivery also rewards the player with an HP Up.

Title: Flame Mail

Known in Japanese as フレイムメール Flame Mail, the Flame Mail functions as a unique Mail item found solely within Generation IV titles. Players can compose a message within it, utilizing up to three designated sentence slots. These slots are populated by selecting from a predefined list of words and phrases provided by the easy chat system. An example allows the user to pick the phrase "I love ______________!" and then insert a specific term like "BULBASAUR". Its design ensures readability across all language versions of the games. The message composition occurs when the player assigns this item to a Pokémon to hold. Messages can be transmitted to other games within the same generation via the trade of the Pokémon currently holding it. This message persists for as long as the item is held by a Pokémon or kept in the Mailbox. When deposited in the Bag or the Item Storage System, this item cannot carry a message. However, players are offered the choice to remove any existing message upon storing it in these locations. After a message is deleted from the item, it becomes available for reuse with a fresh message. Pokémon carrying this item cannot be deposited into the PC or the Pokémon Day Care. Messages contained within are not viewable in Pokémon Battle Revolution or My Pokémon Ranch. Consistent with other held items, a Pokémon equipped with this item cannot be moved to Generation V games using either the Poké Transfer or Relocator. Knock Off is capable of dislodging this item. Conversely, it resists theft attempts by Covet or Thief; using Fling by the holder results in failure, and both Switcheroo and Trick will fail when employed by or against a Pokémon holding it.

Title: Bubble Mail

Referred to in Japanese as ブルーメール (Blue Mail), the Bubble Mail is a unique Mail item found exclusively in the Generation IV Pokémon titles. It serves to convey a message, offering up to three distinct sentence slots that can be completed using a predetermined selection of words and phrases from the easy chat system. For example, a player could choose the pre-set phrase "I love ______________!" and then insert the term "BULBASAUR". This design choice was made to ensure the messages could be universally read across all language versions of the games. The player composes the message at the time they assign the item for a Pokémon to hold. To transmit this message to other games within the same generation, the Pokémon holding the item must be traded. The message itself persists and remains retrievable as long as the item is either held by a Pokémon or kept within the Mailbox. However, when stored in the Bag or the Item Storage System, the Bubble Mail cannot contain a message, though there is an option to remove any existing message when placing it in these storage locations. Once an existing message has been cleared from the item, the player is then able to dedicate it to a new message. A Pokémon that is currently holding this item cannot be deposited into the PC or placed in the Pokémon Day Care. Additionally, the message cannot be viewed in Pokémon Battle Revolution or My Pokémon Ranch. In line with other held items, a Pokémon carrying Bubble Mail cannot be transferred to Generation V games using either the Poké Transfer or Relocator. While the move Knock Off is capable of dislodging this item, it cannot be stolen by either Covet or Thief. Furthermore, if the Pokémon holding it attempts to use Fling, the move will fail, and both Switcheroo and Trick will also fail if used by or on the holder.

Title: Bloom Mail

This item, known in Japanese as ブルームメール (Bloom Mail), functions as a unique piece of Mail, found solely within the games of Generation IV. Players can compose a message for it, utilizing a maximum of three distinct sentence slots. These slots are populated by selecting words and phrases from the easy chat system's predetermined lexicon. As an illustration, one might choose the sentence "I love ______________!" and then slot in the term "BULBASAUR". Its design ensures readability across game versions in any language. Message creation occurs at the moment the player assigns this item to a Pokémon for holding. Transmission of the message to other games within the same generation is facilitated by trading the Pokémon that possesses this item. The content of the message persists for as long as the Bloom Mail is either affixed to a Pokémon or lodged within the Mailbox. Conversely, no message can be associated with this item when it resides in the Bag or the Item Storage System; however, users are provided the choice to erase any pre-existing message upon its placement into these storage locations. Subsequent to a message's deletion from the item, the player gains the capacity to repurpose it with fresh content. A Pokémon carrying the Bloom Mail is barred from being deposited into the PC or entrusted to the care of the Pokémon Day Care. The embedded message becomes unreadable when observed in Pokémon Battle Revolution or My Pokémon Ranch. Consistent with other held items, a Pokémon possessing this Mail cannot undergo transfer into Generation V titles via the Poké Transfer or Relocator systems. Knock Off is capable of dislodging this item. Conversely, moves such as Covet or Thief are ineffective at pilfering it. Furthermore, if the holder attempts to use Fling, the move will fail. Similarly, Switcheroo or Trick will also fail when attempted by or against a Pokémon holding the Bloom Mail.

Title: Tunnel Mail

This particular Mail item, known in Japanese as トンネルメール (Tunnel Mail), exists solely within the Generation IV Pokémon titles. It allows for the creation of a message utilizing up to three designated sentence areas, populated by selections from the easy chat system's predefined word and phrase inventory. A player, for example, might choose the phrase "I love ______________!" and then input "BULBASAUR" into the blank. The design ensures readability across all language versions of the games. A message is inscribed onto this item by the player at the moment it is given to a Pokémon to hold. Transmitting the Pokémon that carries this item through trade enables the message's delivery to other Generation IV games. Its content persists as long as the Tunnel Mail is either equipped by a Pokémon or kept within the Mailbox. While it cannot store a message when placed in the Bag or the Item Storage System, an existing message can be optionally erased during its transfer to these locations. Upon deletion of its message, the item becomes available for reuse with fresh text. Pokémon equipped with Tunnel Mail are prohibited from being deposited into the PC or the Pokémon Day Care. The inscribed message cannot be viewed in Pokémon Battle Revolution or My Pokémon Ranch. Consistent with other held items, any Pokémon carrying this particular item cannot be moved to Generation V games via the Poké Transfer or Relocator systems. Knock Off is capable of dislodging this item. Nevertheless, Covet and Thief are ineffective at stealing it; when a Pokémon attempts to use Fling while holding it, the move will fail; and both Switcheroo and Trick will fail if attempted by or on the Pokémon holding it.

Title: Steel Mail

In Generation IV games, players encounter the Steel Mail (Japanese: スチールメール Steel Mail), a unique form of Mail. This item allows the inscription of a message comprising up to three distinct sentence slots, which are populated using words and phrases drawn from the integrated easy chat system's predefined options. For example, players can select the sentence "I love ______________!" and then insert the word "BULBASAUR". Its design ensures readability across all language versions of the games. The player composes the message at the moment the item is assigned to a Pokémon as a held item. To transmit the message to other games within the same generation, players must trade the Pokémon currently holding the Steel Mail. The message remains accessible for viewing provided the item is either actively held by a Pokémon or kept within the Mailbox. However, if the Steel Mail resides in the Bag or the Item Storage System, it cannot contain a message; upon placing it in these locations, an option becomes available to delete any currently existing message. Following the deletion of its message, the item becomes available for the player to inscribe a fresh one. Pokémon equipped with this item are prohibited from being deposited into the PC or left at the Pokémon Day Care. The inscribed message is not viewable within Pokémon Battle Revolution or My Pokémon Ranch. Consistent with other held items, a Pokémon bearing the Steel Mail cannot undergo transfer into Generation V titles via either the Poké Transfer or the Relocator. The item is susceptible to removal by the move Knock Off. Nonetheless, it resists theft attempts from Covet or Thief. Furthermore, if a Pokémon attempts to use Fling while holding it, the move will fail. Similarly, Switcheroo or Trick will prove ineffective if executed by or against a Pokémon holding the Steel Mail.

Title: Heart Mail

This particular Mail variant, known as Heart Mail (ラブラブメール Love-Love Mail in Japanese), is solely available within the Generation IV titles. Its design allows for a message comprising a maximum of three customizable sentence slots, which players can populate with specific words and phrases drawn from the easy chat system's predetermined lexicon. An example illustrates this: a player might choose the phrase "I love ______________!" and then input "BULBASAUR" into the blank. The system was engineered to ensure readability across all language versions of the games. A message is composed by the player at the moment this item is assigned to a Pokémon as a held item. Transferring the Pokémon that holds this item facilitates sending its message to other games within the same generation. This message persists as long as the item is either held by a Pokémon or kept within the Mailbox. While this item cannot contain a message when housed in the Bag or the Item Storage System, players are given the option to clear any current message upon placing it in these locations. After its message is removed, the player can then repurpose the item with fresh content. A Pokémon equipped with this item is barred from deposit into the PC or the Pokémon Day Care. Neither Pokémon Battle Revolution nor My Pokémon Ranch supports the display of this message. Consistent with other held items, a Pokémon carrying Heart Mail cannot be moved into Generation V titles via the Poké Transfer or Relocator mechanisms. It is susceptible to removal by the move Knock Off. Nevertheless, it resists theft by Covet or Thief; furthermore, it causes the holder's use of Fling to fail, and similarly results in the failure of Switcheroo or Trick when targeting or being used by the holder. Heart Mail constitutes one of the six distinct Mail types included in a special letter set, exclusive to Pokémon Centers and launched in November 2021.

Title: Snow Mail

Exclusively available within the Generation IV titles, the Snow Mail (known as ブリザードメール Blizzard Mail in Japanese) represents a distinct category of Mail items. This item facilitates a customizable message, offering a maximum of three distinct sentence slots, which players populate with words and phrases drawn from the easy chat system's predetermined vocabulary. An example might involve a player choosing the pre-formatted phrase "I love ______________!" and subsequently inserting "BULBASAUR" into the blank. Its design ensured readability across different linguistic versions of the games. A message is inscribed by the player at the moment this item is assigned to a Pokémon as its held item. Transmission of this message to other games within the same generation is achievable by trading the Pokémon currently holding it. The content of the message persists for as long as the item is either held by a Pokémon or deposited in the Mailbox. While incapable of retaining a message when placed in the Bag or the Item Storage System, players do have the choice to erase any previously inscribed message upon storing the item in these locations. Following the deletion of a message, the item becomes available for reuse by the player, ready to accept a new inscription. A Pokémon equipped with this item cannot be deposited into the PC or entrusted to the Pokémon Day Care. The message contents are not accessible for viewing within Pokémon Battle Revolution or My Pokémon Ranch. Consistent with limitations on other held items, a Pokémon gripping this item cannot be migrated to Generation V games via either the Poké Transfer or the Relocator. Its removal can be effected by the move Knock Off. Nevertheless, it resists theft attempts from Covet or Thief; its presence causes Fling to fail when the holder attempts it; and it furthermore obstructs the success of Switcheroo or Trick, whether initiated by or directed at the holder.

Title: Space Mail

The Space Mail (Japanese: スペースメール Space Mail) is a type of Mail found exclusively in Generation IV games. It allows for a message of up to three sentence slots, filled with words and phrases from the easy chat system's predefined list. For instance, players can select "I love ______________!" and insert "BULBASAUR". This design ensures readability across all game languages. The player writes the message when giving this item to a Pokémon to hold.

The message can be sent to other games of the same generation by trading the Pokémon holding it. It remains available as long as the item is held by a Pokémon or stored in the Mailbox. However, the item cannot contain a message when in the Bag or Item Storage System, though players can opt to delete any existing message when storing it there. Once a message is deleted, the item becomes reusable for a new one. A Pokémon holding Space Mail cannot be placed in the PC or the Pokémon Day Care.

Messages are not readable in Pokémon Battle Revolution or My Pokémon Ranch. Like other held items, a Pokémon carrying Space Mail cannot be transferred to Generation V games via the Poké Transfer or Relocator. While Knock Off can remove this item, it cannot be stolen by Covet or Thief. Additionally, Fling fails if used by the holder, and Switcheroo or Trick are unsuccessful when used by or on a Pokémon holding it.

Title: Air Mail

Known in Japanese as エアメール (Air Mail), the Air Mail is a specific category of Mail item found exclusively in Generation IV games. Players can compose a message for it, utilizing up to three sentence slots that are populated with words and phrases drawn from the easy chat system's predetermined list. As an illustration, one might choose the sentence "I love ______________!" and subsequently fill the blank with "BULBASAUR". The system was engineered to guarantee readability regardless of the game's localized language. Message composition occurs when the player assigns this item to a Pokémon for holding. Transmission of the message to other Generation IV games is facilitated by trading the Pokémon that carries the Air Mail. Its embedded message endures as long as the item is either actively held by a Pokémon or placed in the Mailbox. Messages cannot be retained on this item when it is kept in the Bag or the Item Storage System; nevertheless, players are given the choice to remove any existing message when placing it there. Upon deletion of its message, the item becomes eligible for reuse with a new inscription. Pokémon equipped with this item are barred from being placed into the PC or the Pokémon Day Care. The message imprinted on the Air Mail is unreadable when viewed in Pokémon Battle Revolution or My Pokémon Ranch. Consistent with limitations for other held items, a Pokémon holding Air Mail cannot be migrated to Generation V games using either the Poké Transfer or Relocator systems. Knock Off is capable of dislodging this item. Nonetheless, it resists being stolen by moves like Covet or Thief. Additionally, if a Pokémon holding it attempts to use Fling, the move will fail. Similarly, Switcheroo or Trick will also fail, irrespective of whether they are used by or against a Pokémon holding the Air Mail.

Title: Mosaic Mail

Exclusively found within the Generation IV game titles, the item known as Mosaic Mail (Japanese: モザイクメール Mosaic Mail) functions as a specific category of Mail. This particular Mail permits the inscription of a message, comprising a maximum of three distinct sentence fields. Players populate these fields with words and phrases drawn from the easy chat system's established lexicon. An illustrative example would be a player choosing the template "I love ______________!" and then integrating the term "BULBASAUR" into the blank space. Its design ensures readability across all language versions of the games. The message composition occurs at the moment this item is assigned to a Pokémon to hold. Transmission of the message to other game cartridges from the same generation is facilitated by trading the Pokémon that carries this item. The inscribed content persists for as long as the item remains equipped by a Pokémon or is housed within the Mailbox. However, the item cannot retain a message while residing in the Bag or the Item Storage System. Nonetheless, players are provided with the option to erase any current message when relocating the item to these specific inventory locations. Upon deletion of the message, the player gains the capability to repurpose the item with fresh text. A Pokémon equipped with this item is prohibited from being deposited into the PC or left at the Pokémon Day Care. Furthermore, the message content is unreadable when viewed in Pokémon Battle Revolution or My Pokémon Ranch. Consistent with other held items, any Pokémon carrying the Mosaic Mail cannot be migrated to Generation V games utilizing either the Poké Transfer or Relocator mechanisms. The item is susceptible to removal via the move Knock Off. Nevertheless, it resists theft attempts from the moves Covet or Thief. When the holder attempts to use Fling, the move will fail. Similarly, Switcheroo or Trick will also fail, whether used by or targeting the Pokémon holding this item.

Title: Brick Mail

Known as ブリックメール (Brick Mail) in Japanese, the Brick Mail is a form of Mail item found solely within Generation IV titles. This item accommodates a message structured with up to three distinct sentence slots, which users populate using predefined words and phrases drawn from the easy chat system's lexicon. As an example, a player might choose the template "I love ______________!" and then insert "BULBASAUR" into the blank. Its design inherently allows for readability across all language versions of the games. Players compose the message at the moment the item is assigned to a Pokémon to hold. The contained message can be transmitted to other games within the same generation via the trade of the Pokémon carrying it. The message persists for as long as the Brick Mail is either held by a Pokémon or kept within the Mailbox. While the Brick Mail cannot hold a message when placed in the Bag or the Item Storage System, players are offered the choice to clear any pre-existing message when depositing it into these locations. Upon the deletion of a message from the item, it becomes available for the player to inscribe a fresh one. A Pokémon holding the Brick Mail cannot be deposited into the PC or entrusted to the Pokémon Day Care. The message within is unreadable when viewed in Pokémon Battle Revolution or My Pokémon Ranch. Consistent with other held items, a Pokémon equipped with the Brick Mail cannot be moved into Generation V titles using either the Poké Transfer or the Relocator. Knock Off is capable of removing this item. Nonetheless, it resists theft attempts from moves like Covet or Thief; furthermore, it causes Fling to fail if attempted by the Pokémon holding it, and prevents Switcheroo or Trick from succeeding, whether used by or targeting the holder.

Title: Cheri Berry

The Cheri Berry (known in Japanese as クラボのみ, Kurabo Fruit) is a specific kind of Berry. Debuting in Generation III, it holds the distinction of being the initial Berry by numerical order in both the Hoenn and Sinnoh regions. This item serves as the direct spiritual successor to the PRZCureBerry from Generation II, providing an exact match in its effect when applied to or consumed by a Pokémon. Within the Generation V titles, players have the option to sell the Cheri Berry to the gourmet maniac found on Route 5, fetching a price of $20.

Should a Pokémon holding a Cheri Berry become paralyzed, it will automatically eat the Berry, thereby recovering from its paralysis. If the Cheri Berry is consumed forcefully through moves such as Fling, Pluck, Bug Bite, or Item Urge, the Pokémon that consumes it will also be relieved of paralysis. Applying a Cheri Berry from the Bag to a Pokémon will heal that Pokémon's paralysis. This action expends the Cheri Berry.

In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, the Cheri Berry offers dual utility: it can either restore a paralyzed Pokémon or be tossed near wild Pokémon to capture their focus. In Pokémon Platinum, players are able to trade Cheri Berries for accessories at the Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop. Similarly, the Pokémon Dream World allowed players to swap Berries for Décor, with specific Décor requiring Cheri Berries. Furthermore, in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, the Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop facilitates the exchange of Cheri Berries for Stickers.

The Cheri Berry features in the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, where it functions as a food item capable of curing paralysis and replenishing Belly by 5/2. In Pokémon Sleep, the Cheri Berry is among the Berries collectible by helper Pokémon to be given to Snorlax. These Berries boost Snorlax's Strength, with this effect being doubled if the Cheri Berry is Snorlax's preferred Berry. The fundamental Strength gain from a Cheri Berry is dictated by the level of the helper Pokémon responsible for collecting it. Exclusively, Fighting-type helper Pokémon on the player's team can gather Cheri Berries. Consistent with all Berries in this game, any Cheri Berries collected do not enter the player's inventory but are instead instantly consumed by Snorlax.

The Cheri Berry first appeared in the episode Berry, Berry Interesting, where May gathered several from a garden for blending with other Berries to create Pokéblocks for her Pokémon. In Cooking Up a Sweet Story!, Cheri Berries were observed within Abigail's Berry basket. They also featured in Hungry for the Good Life!. An episode titled Uncrushing Defeat! showed a Cheri Berry being used to restore Ash's Monferno. During A Fork in the Road! A Parting of the Ways!, Dr. White offered Jessie a Cheri Berry, which resolved her condition after a wild Gloom's Stun Spore attack. Cheri Berries were present at a Pokémon Center in Adventures in Running Errands!. An illustration of a Cheri Berry was shown by Rotom in Tasting the Bitter with the Sweet!. In As Long as I'm with My Friends, Roy and Fuecoco collected Cheri Berries to infuse their desired curry with spiciness, achieving their goal even after a group of Skwovet led by a Greedent stole some of their gathered items.

In Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Team Go-Getters Out of the Gate!, Cheri Berries were among the items possessed by Team Go-Getters. Regrettably, these Cheri Berries, alongside the majority of their other Berries, were consumed by Ekans of Team Meanies. Within Tripped Up by Torkoal, Ruby retrieved several Berries, a Cheri Berry included, for the purpose of Berry Blending. A Cheri Berry was observed among Sapphire's collected Berries during her journeys in I Dare Ya, Altaria... Knock Chic off My Shoulder!. In Sneaky Like Shedinja II, Cheri Berries were among those sliced by Emerald's Sceptile using Leaf Blade for its Trainer's consumption. Problematic Probopass and Mad Magnezone II depicted a Cheri Berry among numerous Berries excavated from Lax's fur by Cyrus's Probopass's Mini-Noses, during an attempt to recover Cyrus's camera hidden within Lax's fur. Lucky Lucario II showed a Cheri Berry among the Berries Diamond utilized when preparing meals for himself, his Pokémon, and Riley during their training on Iron Island. In Fussing and Fighting, a Cheri Berry formed part of Professor Juniper's dinner. Finally, Homecoming and the Brilliant Professional Golfer featured several Cheri Berries within a collection amassed by various Pokémon in Lush Jungle, aiding Moon in her quest for a unique Mirage Berry.

A gallery of cards depicting, mentioning, or featuring a Cheri Berry is provided below. This Berry has also made appearances in numerous officially licensed figures and merchandise. Measuring 0.8 inches (2 cm), the Cheri Berry holds the distinction of being the most diminutive Berry. Its design and appellation likely draw inspiration from cherries and cherry peppers.

Title: Chesto Berry

The Chesto Berry (known in Japanese as カゴのみ Kago Fruit) is a variety of Berry first introduced in Generation III. It serves as the spiritual successor to the Mint Berry from Generation II, possessing an identical effect when utilized on or by a Pokémon. In the Generation V installments, players can sell a Chesto Berry to the gourmet maniac on Route 5 for $20. If a Pokémon is holding this Berry and falls asleep, it will automatically consume the Chesto Berry and awaken. When consumed forcefully through moves such as Fling, Pluck, Bug Bite, or Item Urge, the consumer is also cured of sleep. In Pokémon Legends: Z-A, its effect shifts to curing the holder of drowsiness instead. When activated from the Bag and used on a Pokémon, it removes that Pokémon's sleep status (or drowsiness in Pokémon Legends: Z-A), thereby consuming the Berry. In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, it can be employed to rouse a drowsy Pokémon or thrown near wild Pokémon to capture their attention.

In Pokémon Emerald, the Favor Lady at the Lilycove City Pokémon Center may request 'something wham-ish,' for which a Chesto Berry can be traded for an Iron. Pokémon Platinum allows players to trade Chesto Berries for various accessories at the Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop. In the Pokémon Dream World, players could exchange Berries for Décor, with specific Décor items requiring Chesto Berries. In Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, the Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop also accepts Chesto Berries in exchange for Stickers.

The Chesto Berry appears in the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series as a food item. It both wakes up a Pokémon and replenishes its Belly by 5/2. If the Pokémon is not already asleep, consuming it grants the Sleepless status for 9-10 turns or for the remainder of the floor.

In Pokémon Sleep, the Chesto Berry is one of the types gatherable by helper Pokémon and fed to Snorlax. These Berries boost Snorlax's Strength, with the effect doubling if it's Snorlax's favorite Berry. The base Strength increase provided by the Berry is determined by the level of the helper Pokémon that collected it. Only Poison-type helper Pokémon in the player's team are able to gather Chesto Berries. As with all Berries in this game, collected Chesto Berries are not added to the player's inventory but are immediately consumed by Snorlax.

The Chesto Berry is also featured in Pokémon Pokopia. It grows naturally on Chesto Trees. Ditto can consume these Berries to restore PP, and they can also be given to other Pokémon to replenish their energy.

In the anime, Chesto Berries debuted in "An Egg Scramble!", where Brock used a mortar and pestle to grind them into a powder, which he then sprinkled on Khoury's Totodile's food to make it more appealing to the Big Jaw Pokémon. "The Island of Illusions!" showed a Chesto Berry being part of a medicine to cure Iris's Axew of its sleep. In "A Fork in the Road! A Parting of the Ways!", Chesto Berries were cultivated in Dr. White's garden. During "So You're Having a Bad Day!", Bonnie and Meowth, while fleeing a drowsy Pangoro, fell off a small cliff onto a group of Foongus. The Foongus retaliated with Spore, putting Bonnie to sleep. Since the two were tethered by a sturdy vine, Meowth had Dedenne retrieve a Chesto Berry to awaken Bonnie. In "Over the Mountain of Snow!", Bunnelby discovered a Chesto Berry as Clemont and Bonnie searched for Mountain Marigolds, needed for a sick Snover. Chesto Berries were present at a Pokémon Center in "Adventures in Running Errands!". "A Diamond in the Rough!" depicted Florges attempting to use Chesto Berries to lure out a Carbink causing trouble in the wetlands. However, Carbink ambushed Florges from behind before she could set them down. Not having noticed Carbink, Florges mistakenly blamed and angrily attacked a nearby Quagsire. A basket filled with Chesto Berries was seen at the Gadget Festival in "A Gaggle of Gadget Greatness!". Chesto Berries also made an appearance in "First Catch in Alola, Ketchum-Style!".

In the Pokémon Adventures manga, Yū's Rotom held a Chesto Berry during his Battle Tower walkthrough in "Walkthrough! Battle Tower!!", enabling it to immediately wake up when a Noctowl used Hypnosis. Ruby produced multiple Berries, including a Chesto Berry, for Berry Blending in "Tripped Up by Torkoal". A Chesto Berry was among the Berries Sapphire had gathered during her travels, shown in "I Dare Ya, Altaria... Knock Chic off My Shoulder!". Noland's rental Glalie, used in his battle with Emerald in "Gotcha Where I Wantcha, Glalie", held a Chesto Berry intended for a combination with its Rest move; however, Noland couldn't execute this combo before Glalie was defeated by Emerald's Sceptile. In "Lucky Lucario II", a Chesto Berry was observed among the Berries Diamond used to prepare food for himself, his Pokémon, and Riley during their training session at Iron Island.

Several officially licensed figures and goods have featured the Chesto Berry. Furthermore, a list exists of cards named after or depicting a Chesto Berry. In the games, the Chesto Berry is frequently paired with the move Rest, allowing the move to function like a Full Restore. This combination is colloquially termed "Resto Chesto." The design and nomenclature of the Chesto Berry might be inspired by the Chinese water chestnut.

Title: Pecha Berry
The Pecha Berry (Japanese: モモンのみ Momon Fruit) is a variety of Berry first introduced in the third Pokémon generation. Its primary function is to eliminate the poison status condition from a Pokémon. This item serves as the conceptual successor to the Generation II PSNCureBerry.

Within the Generation V games, a Pecha Berry can be monetized by selling it to the gourmet enthusiast located on Route 5 for a price of $20. Should a Pokémon holding this Berry become poisoned, it will automatically consume the Pecha Berry, thereby curing itself of the ailment. When forcibly consumed through moves like Fling, Pluck, Bug Bite, or Item Urge, the Berry also cures the consuming Pokémon of poison. Direct usage from the Bag on a Pokémon similarly cures that Pokémon of poison, expending the Berry in the process.

In the world of Pokémon Legends: Arceus, the Pecha Berry can either be administered to a poisoned Pokémon for healing or tossed near wild Pokémon to grab their attention. Pokémon Emerald offered a unique trade: players could swap a Pecha Berry for a Protein if the Favor Lady at the Lilycove City Pokémon Center specifically requested 'something slippery'. For accessory collectors, Pokémon Platinum's Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop provided the opportunity to trade Pecha Berries for various accessories. Additionally, in the now-defunct Pokémon Dream World, players once exchanged Berries for Décor, with certain Décor items specifically necessitating Pecha Berries. More recently, Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl allow players to trade Pecha Berries for Stickers at the Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop.

The Pecha Berry also features prominently in the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, where it functions as a food item. In this context, it not only cures both the Poisoned and Badly Poisoned status conditions but also replenishes Belly by 5/2. In Pokémon: Magikarp Jump, Pecha Berries can appear as nourishment for Magikarp to consume. Furthermore, the Pecha Berry is among the gatherable Berries in Pokémon Sleep, collected by helper Pokémon and fed to Snorlax. These Berries boost Snorlax's Strength, with the effect being doubled if Pecha is Snorlax's preferred Berry type. The baseline Strength increase from the Berry is determined by the level of the helper Pokémon that gathered it, and specifically, Fairy-type helper Pokémon on the player's team are capable of gathering Pecha Berries. As with all Berries in this game, gathered Pecha Berries are not stored in the player's inventory but are immediately consumed by Snorlax. In Pokémon Pokopia, Pecha Berries naturally proliferate on Pecha Trees. Here, Ditto can eat Pecha Berries to restore PP, and they can also be presented as gifts to other Pokémon to rejuvenate their energy.

Anime appearances of the Pecha Berry commenced in *The Lotad Lowdown*, where they were cultivated at the Pretty Petal flower shop. May offered a few Pecha Berries to herself and Forrest Franklin to eat in *All in a Day's Wurmple*. Ash and Pikachu discovered a Pecha Berry tree in *A Corphish Out of Water*, though an angry Shroomish, accidentally stepped on by Ash, drove them away before they could partake. *Just One of the Geysers* showed Pecha Berries stored within Brawly's fridge. At the Lilycove Berry Market, Pecha Berries were available for purchase in *Pokéblock, Stock, and Berry*. May harvested several Pecha Berries from a garden to blend into Pokéblocks for her Pokémon in *Berry, Berry Interesting*. Misty and Max consumed Pecha Berries picked by Misty in *A Real Cleffa-Hanger*. A wild Arcanine gathered Pecha Berries from a valley abundant with Pecha trees to feed its Growlithe offspring in *On Cloud Arcanine*. Team Rocket coerced a group of wild Aipom to collect a variety of Berries, including Pecha Berries, in *Slaking Kong!*. James and Meowth were seen picking Pecha Berries in *Crossing Paths*. Pecha Berries were also featured in *Hungry for the Good Life!*. *Arceus and the Jewel of Life* depicted Pecha Berries in a Berry basket. In *The Brockster Is In!*, Brock first cured Ash's Pikachu with a Pecha Berry, then later used a Pecha Berry-based medicine to treat several poisoned Pokémon. Cilan incorporated a Pecha Berry into his cooking in *Ash and Trip's Third Battle!*. Multiple Pecha Berries were present in Eindoak Town in *White—Victini and Zekrom* and *Black—Victini and Reshiram*. *The Beartic Mountain Feud!* also included Pecha Berries. Roxie provided Ash's Pignite and Pikachu with one Pecha Berry each in *Rocking the Virbank Gym! Part 2*, aiding their recovery from poison inflicted by her Pokémon. In *A Stealthy Challenge!*, Clemont used a Pecha Berry to cure Sanpei's Greninja of poison. Ash's gift to his Pokémon, detailed in *Under the Pledging Tree!*, comprised Pecha Berries among other items. Dr. White's garden in *A Fork in the Road! A Parting of the Ways!* was shown to have growing Pecha Berries. Meowth consumed Pecha Berries from a pile in *Good Friends, Great Training!*. A Performer in the Dendemille Showcase Rookie Class incorporated Pecha Berries into her Poké Puffs in *Performing with Fiery Charm!*. A large tree bearing Pecha Berries was visible in *Pikachu and the Pokémon Music Squad*. Pecha Berries were observed at a Pokémon Center in *Adventures in Running Errands!*. They were part of the Anistar City Showcase in *A Performance Pop Quiz!*. In *Mega Evolution Special IV*, Alain utilized a Pecha Berry to heal his Charizard from poison inflicted by a Trainer's Mega Venusaur's Venoshock. Dedenne offered a Pecha Berry to Squishy in *From A to Z!*, but Squishy declined, preferring sunlight as its energy source. Pecha Berries were witnessed during the Couriway Town Showcase and as a component of Ash and his friends' meal in *A Dancing Debut!*. The Poké TV segment of *Making Friends and Influencing Villains!* showed Pecha Berries. At the Nebel Plateau, Pecha Berries were present in *Volcanion and the Mechanical Marvel*. Ash and his friends prepared a feast using Pecha Berries as ingredients at Professor Sycamore's laboratory in *Battling With a Clean Slate!*. Mallow's meal for Ash and his classmates' Pokémon in *The Guardian's Challenge!* included a Pecha Berry. Pecha Berries made appearances in *First Catch in Alola, Ketchum-Style!* and *A Seasoned Search!*. In the Poké Problem extra scene of SM035, Mallow employed Pecha Berries as an ingredient to create a milder version of "Akala Curry" for Snowy, who found the spicier version unappetizing. Professor Burnet's Munchlax offered a Pecha Berry to Mareanie in *Fighting Back the Tears!*, which she rejected. *A Mission of Ultra Urgency!* depicted Pecha Berries within the Ultra Guardians' medical kit, and Team Rocket was also seen harvesting some in a forest. Pecha Berries were part of the Berry pile in Bewear's den in *Acting True to Form!*. The banned episode SM064 featured a group of Passimian playfully tossing Pecha Berries at one another. A Pecha Berry was part of a meal Ash prepared for his Pokémon in *Love at First Twirl!*. In *The Dealer of Destruction!*, Kiawe provided Ash with a Pecha Berry to heal his Pikachu from poison caused by Guzma's Golisopod's Poison Jab. Ash gave his Pikachu a Pecha Berry to cure it of poison inflicted by a Team Rocket Grunt's Toxicroak's Sludge Bomb in *A Little Rocket R & R!*. Pecha Tart was among the dishes served during the Pokémon Grand Eating Contest in *Crowning the Chow Crusher!*. Goh used Pecha Berries to heal a Suicune from poison inflicted by Pokémon hunters in *Healing the Healer!*. In *The Same Moon, Now and Forever!*, Pikachu brought Meowth a Pecha Berry to alleviate his poison, recalling Ash's similar kindness. Multiple Pecha Berries appeared in *The Future I Choose*. Liko and Dot picked Pecha Berries to enhance the flavor of Liko's curry in *As Long as I'm with My Friends*. Ludlow's Quagsire shared three Pecha Berries it was eating with Dot's Tinkatink in *A Challenge from the Explorers*.

The Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Team Go-Getters Out of the Gate! special featured Team Go-Getters in possession of Pecha Berries, though Ekans of Team Meanies consumed these and most of their other Berries. In the manga, Jō utilized a Pecha Berry to cure his Totodile of poison from Ariana's Gloom's Poison Powder in *Jō VS Team Rocket*. Ruby harvested Pecha Berries from a Berry tree in *Nixing Nuzleaf*. *Tripped Up by Torkoal* showed Ruby preparing multiple Berries, including a Pecha Berry, for Berry Blending. Emerald employed several Pecha Berries off-panel during his Battle Pike challenge to heal his Pokémon. Regrettably, he depleted his supply just before reaching Lucy in *Moving Past Milotic*, rendering him unable to cure his badly poisoned Pokémon without resorting to switching them out to activate Natural Cure. A Pecha Berry was observed among the Berries Emerald's Sceptile sliced with Leaf Blade for its Trainer's consumption in *Sneaky Like Shedinja II*. In *Problematic Probopass and Mad Magnezone II*, a Pecha Berry was also present.

Title: Rawst Berry

Debuting in Generation III, the Rawst Berry (Japanese: チーゴのみ Chīgo Fruit) is a specific kind of Berry. It serves as the direct spiritual successor to the Generation II Ice Berry, providing the exact same effect when applied to a Pokémon or consumed by one. Trainers in the Generation V titles have the option to sell a Rawst Berry to the gourmet enthusiast located on Route 5 for a price of $20. As a held item, should a Pokémon become afflicted with a burn, the Berry is automatically eaten, alleviating the status condition. Similarly, if forcefully consumed through moves like Fling, Pluck, Bug Bite, or Item Urge, the Berry remedies the consumer's burn. Directly administering it from the Bag to a Pokémon will also remove its burn, depleting the item in the process.

Its utility expands in Pokémon Legends: Arceus, where it both heals burned Pokémon and can be thrown close to wild Pokémon to capture their notice. In Pokémon Platinum, players had the opportunity to trade Rawst Berries for accessories at the Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop. During the era of the Pokémon Dream World, Berries were tradable for Décor, with certain Décor specifically calling for Rawst Berries. Modern iterations, Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, allow players to swap Rawst Berries for Stickers at the same Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop.

Within the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon franchise, the Rawst Berry functions as an edible item capable of healing burns and replenishing a Pokémon's Belly by 5/2. For Pokémon: Magikarp Jump, Rawst Berries may manifest as food for Magikarp consumption. Pokémon Sleep includes the Rawst Berry among those collectible by helper Pokémon to be given to Snorlax. These Berries augment Snorlax's Strength, with the potency increasing twofold if it aligns with Snorlax's preferred Berry type. The foundational Strength boost delivered by the Berry is tied to the gathering helper Pokémon's level. Only Ice-type helper Pokémon on the player's roster can procure Rawst Berries. Consistent with other Berries in Pokémon Sleep, collected Rawst Berries bypass the player's inventory, being promptly consumed by Snorlax. In Pokémon UNITE, Rawst Berries are visible within the mound of Berries that Greedent collides with during its Unite Move, Berry Belly Flop. In Pokémon Pokopia, Rawst Berries are naturally found growing on Rawst Trees. Ditto can consume them for PP restoration, or they can be offered to other Pokémon to recover their energy.

Anime viewers first witnessed Rawst Berries in "Grooming Furfrou!", where Jessica displayed them at Sherman's Furfrou grooming salon. A Rawst Berry was depicted in a newspaper Jessie employed to conceal herself from Ash and his companions in "To Find a Fairy Flower!". Ash distributed Rawst Berries to his Pokémon in "Under the Pledging Tree!". In "A Fork in the Road! A Parting of the Ways!", Rawst Berries cultivated in Dr. White's garden were shown. A Pokémon Center featured Rawst Berries in "Adventures in Running Errands!". During "A Performance Pop Quiz!", Rawst Berries were incorporated into the Anistar City Pokémon Showcase Rookie Class. Ash and his friends prepared a feast using Rawst Berries as ingredients at Professor Sycamore's laboratory in "Battling With a Clean Slate!". Professor Burnet utilized Rawst Berries for a Pokémon food meal prepared for Mareanie in "Fighting Back the Tears!". The banned episode SM064 featured a Rawst Berry within a wild Passimian group's nest. Ash prepared a meal for his Pokémon that included a Rawst Berry in "Love at First Twirl!". "The Young Flame Strikes Back!" showed a Rawst Berry as part of Pikachu, Poipole, and Togedemaru's meal. "The Future I Choose" included several Rawst Berries.

Within the Pokémon Adventures manga, "Tripped Up by Torkoal" depicted Ruby extracting various Berries, notably a Rawst Berry, for Berry Blending. Sapphire's travel collections, as seen in "I Dare Ya, Altaria... Knock Chic off My Shoulder!", included a Rawst Berry. "Problematic Probopass and Mad Magnezone II" featured a Rawst Berry among numerous others unearthed from Lax's fur by Cyrus's Probopass's Mini-Noses during an attempt to recover Cyrus's concealed camera. In "The Final Dimensional Duel VII," Platinum brought up Rawst Berries while discussing her study of Jupiter's Tangrowth's signature move, Natural Gift, with Palmer. Two Rawst Berries contributed to Professor Juniper's dinner in "Fussing and Fighting." During her quest for a unique Mirage Berry in "Homecoming and the Brilliant Professional Golfer," Moon discovered a Rawst Berry in Lush Jungle, with additional Rawst Berries appearing in a collection gathered by local Pokémon assisting her search.

The Rawst Berry is also featured in the Pokémon Trading Card Game, with specific cards either named after it or depicting it as a held item. According to Pokémon News #44 from Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Red Rescue Team and Blue Rescue Team, immature Rawst Berries are green, while their mature, sky-blue counterparts are paradoxically more bitter. The visual design and nomenclature of the Rawst Berry likely draw inspiration from strawberries.

Title: Aspear Berry

The Aspear Berry (Japanese: ナナシのみ Nanashi Fruit) is a variety of Berry first introduced in Generation III. It functions as the spiritual successor to the Generation II Burnt Berry, sharing an identical effect when interacting with a Pokémon. In the Generation V installments of the game series, players can sell an Aspear Berry to the gourmet maniac found on Route 5 for $20. If a Pokémon holding this item becomes frozen, it will automatically consume its Aspear Berry to thaw itself. When forcibly ingested via the move Fling, it similarly cures the consuming Pokémon of its freeze condition. Applying it from the Bag to a Pokémon will also cure that Pokémon of its frozen status, resulting in the Berry's consumption. Within Pokémon Legends: Arceus, it can be utilized to heal a Pokémon afflicted with frostbite or tossed near wild Pokémon to attract their attention. In Pokémon Emerald, a player can exchange an Aspear Berry for a Calcium if the Favor Lady at the Lilycove City Pokémon Center specifically requests 'something roundish'. Pokémon Platinum offers players the opportunity to trade Aspear Berries for accessories at the Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop. In the Pokémon Dream World, players were able to swap Berries for Décor, with certain Décor pieces requiring Aspear Berries. In Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, players can trade Aspear Berries for Stickers at the Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop. Aspear Berries can appear as food items for Magikarp to consume in Pokémon: Magikarp Jump. This Berry is also present in Pokémon Pokopia and grows naturally on Aspear Trees. Ditto can eat Aspear Berries to restore its PP, and they can also be given to other Pokémon to replenish their energy.

Aspear Berries first appeared in the episode "Cooking Up A Sweet Story!". Abigail's Pikachu, nicknamed Sugar, had been perfecting a method to use Thunderbolt on Aspear Berries, rendering them tender enough to be used as ingredients for a cake. After Sugar evolved into Raichu, it was further revealed that the Pokémon had discovered how to pulverize the Berries using its newly acquired Focus Punch. In "Under the Pledging Tree!", Ash presented Aspear Berries as gifts to his Pokémon. Dr. White's garden contained growing Aspear Berries in "A Fork in the Road! A Parting of the Ways!". "Adventures in Running Errands!" featured Aspear Berries at a Pokémon Center. They were also incorporated into the Anistar City Pokémon Showcase during "A Performance Pop Quiz!". Aspear Berries made an appearance in "First Catch in Alola, Ketchum-Style!" and "A Team-on-Team Tussle!". Sliced Aspear Berries were part of Snowy's meal in "Night of a Thousand Poses!". The banned episode SM064 showed an Aspear Berry inside the nest of a wild Passimian group. In "Tripped Up by Torkoal", Ruby produced multiple Berries, including an Aspear Berry, for Berry Blending. An Aspear Berry was among the collection of Berries Sapphire had gathered during her travels, as seen in "I Dare Ya, Altaria... Knock Chic off My Shoulder!". In "Shorting Out Electivire", Volkner's Raichu utilized its held Aspear Berry to unfreeze itself after being partially frozen by Platinum's Empoleon's Blizzard. During "Shunning Spiritomb", Cynthia attempted to give an Aspear Berry to her Spiritomb, which had been frozen by Cyrus's Weavile, but Weavile prevented the Berry's use with Embargo. In "Flying Ship", a partially eaten Aspear Berry was spotted behind Blake and Whitley after Colress froze them with Kyurem. It was later disclosed that Blake had entrusted said Berry to Kelden, anticipating Kyurem's freezing attack. "Homecoming and the Brilliant Professional Golfer" depicted Aspear Berries within a pile of Berries collected by various Pokémon at Lush Jungle, assisting Moon in her search for a special Mirage Berry. In "Crunch!! Snowy Battle", Casey threw an Aspear Berry at Gordie's Stonjourner, having mistakenly interpreted Gordie's battle with Marvin as an aggressive attack.

The Natural Gift type of this Berry is Ice, which coincidentally makes it function identically to an Ice Heal when used on a Pokémon. The Aspear Berry's visual design and its name might be inspired by the Asian pear.

Title: Leppa Berry

The Leppa Berry (known in Japanese as ヒメリのみ, Himeri Fruit) is a specific kind of Berry that debuted in the third Pokémon generation. It serves as a spiritual successor to the Generation II MysteryBerry, sharing an almost identical impact when utilized by or on a Pokémon. During the Generation V titles, players could sell this Berry to the gourmet enthusiast located on Route 5 for a price of $20. When held by a Pokémon, a Leppa Berry replenishes 10 PP for any move whose Power Points have reached zero. Should the holding Pokémon possess the Ripen Ability, the Berry's effect doubles, restoring 20 PP. Upon a Pokémon acquiring a Leppa Berry, or when Magic Room's effect concludes while the Pokémon already has at least one move at 0 PP, the Berry is instantly consumed. If multiple moves are at 0 PP, the one positioned first in the move list is the one that receives the PP restoration. In scenarios where a Pokémon is compelled to consume a Leppa Berry through actions like Fling, Bug Bite, Pluck, Stuff Cheeks, or Teatime, PP will be restored to the first move currently at 0 PP, if any such move exists. Otherwise, it restores PP to the first move that has any Power Points depleted. Using a Leppa Berry from the player's Bag on a Pokémon will restore 10 PP to a chosen move. It is expended following its application. Within Pokémon Legends: Arceus, it provides 10 PP to one of a Pokémon's moves and can additionally be cast near wild Pokémon to attract their notice. Players in Pokémon Platinum could trade Leppa Berries for various accessories at the Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop. The Pokémon Dream World allowed players to swap Berries for Décor, with certain Décor specifically requiring Leppa Berries. In Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, Leppa Berries are tradable for Stickers at the Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop. Leppa Berries make an appearance in Pokémon: Magikarp Jump as an edible item for Magikarp. Pokémon Sleep includes the Leppa Berry among the Berries collectible by helper Pokémon and fed to Snorlax. These Berries enhance Snorlax's Strength, with the effect being doubled if it is Snorlax's preferred Berry. The fundamental quantity of Strength gained from the Berry correlates with the level of the helper Pokémon responsible for collecting it. Fire-type helper Pokémon within the player's team are capable of gathering Leppa Berries. Consistent with all Berries in this game, gathered Leppa Berries do not enter the player's inventory but are instead promptly consumed by Snorlax. In Pokémon UNITE, Leppa Berries are part of the Berry pile that Greedent collides with during its Unite Move, Berry Belly Flop. The Leppa Berry is featured in Pokémon Pokopia, where it naturally grows on Leppa Trees. Ditto can consume Leppa Berries for PP restoration, and they can also be presented to other Pokémon to replenish their energy. Leppa Berries first appeared in the episode 'Pokéblock, Stock, and Berry,' available for purchase at the Lilycove Berry Market. In 'A Fork in the Road! A Parting of the Ways!,' Dr. White's garden contained growing Leppa Berries. During 'Tasting the Bitter with the Sweet!,' Rotom presented an image of a Leppa Berry. In 'The Same Moon, Now and Forever!,' Ash, his companions, Pikachu, and Meowth nearly consumed Leppa Berries before an interruption occurred. Within 'Tripped Up by Torkoal,' Ruby produced several Berries, a Leppa Berry among them, for the purpose of Berry Blending. In 'I Dare Ya, Altaria... Knock Chic off My Shoulder!,' a Leppa Berry was observed within Sapphire's collection of Berries gathered during her journeys. During 'Chipping Away at Regirock,' Crystal's Phanpy retrieved a Leppa Berry for Emerald amidst his Battle Pyramid challenge. In 'Lucky Lucario II,' a Leppa Berry was noted among the Berries Diamond employed while preparing meals for himself, his Pokémon, and Riley during their training on Iron Island. Within 'Homecoming and the Brilliant Professional Golfer,' Leppa Berries were part of the Berry pile amassed by a group of Pokémon in Lush Jungle, aiding Moon in her quest for a unique Mirage Berry. The Leppa Berry stands as one of only two Berries possessing a distinct growth duration: It was the sole Berry in Generation III to mature in 16 hours, a category it shares with the Oran Berry and Persim Berry starting from Generation IV. The only other Berry with a unique growth cycle is the Lum Berry. When utilized from the Bag, its impact mirrors that of a standard Ether. Beyond the Wonder Launcher, the Leppa Berry and Lunar Dance represent the sole means of PP restoration in link battles. If Greedent sustains damage in the games, a Leppa Berry is dislodged from its tail. The apple that Applin inhabits visually resembles Leppa Berries. Both the visual design and the nomenclature of the Leppa Berry are derived from apples. Notably, its name is an anagram of the word 'apple'.

Title: Oran Berry
Originating in Generation III, the Oran Berry (known as オレンのみ Oran Fruit in Japanese) is a specific type of Berry. It serves as the spiritual successor to the Generation II standard Berry, sharing an identical effect when a Pokémon uses or consumes it. In the Generation V installments, an Oran Berry could be sold to the gourmet maniac situated on Route 5 for $20. Should a Pokémon's HP fall below 50% at the close of a turn, it will automatically consume its held Oran Berry, restoring 10 HP. However, a glitch prevents the Oran Berry from activating if the HP drop below 50% is caused by self-inflicted confusion damage. If the holder possesses the Ripen Ability, the Berry will instead restore 20 HP. When manually used from the Bag on a Pokémon, it replenishes 10 HP to that Pokémon and is then consumed.

In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, this Berry restores 20 HP to a Pokémon, and can additionally be thrown near wild Pokémon to capture their attention. Within Pokémon Emerald, the Favor Lady in Lilycove City's Pokémon Center exchanges an Oran Berry for a Calcium if her request is for 'something roundish'. Pokémon Platinum's Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop allowed players to trade Oran Berries for various accessories, a function echoed in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl for Stickers. During the Pokémon Dream World's operation, Berries, including Oran Berries, could be exchanged for Décor.

The Oran Berry is a recurring item in the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, functioning as a food item. It restores 100 HP and 5/2 of the Belly gauge. In Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon and Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Rescue Team DX, if a user's maximum HP is less than 100 or their HP is full, consuming an Oran Berry temporarily increases their max HP by 10 for that specific dungeon. Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Explorers of Sky features a similar-looking item called the Oren Berry which, despite its identical appearance on the dungeon floor, damages the user by 10 HP. Gates To Infinity specifies that it heals 100 HP upon consumption.

In Pokémon: Magikarp Jump, Oran Berries may appear as food that Magikarp can consume. Pokémon Sleep includes the Oran Berry as one of the Berries gatherable by helper Pokémon and fed to Snorlax. These Berries elevate Snorlax's Strength, with the effect doubling if Oran is Snorlax's favorite. The fundamental amount of Strength gained from the Berry is determined by the level of the helper Pokémon responsible for gathering it. Water-type helper Pokémon in the player's team are capable of gathering Oran Berries. Like all Berries in this game, gathered Oran Berries do not enter the player's inventory but are immediately eaten by Snorlax.

Oran Berries are integrated into Greedent's moveset in Pokémon UNITE. Greedent drops an Oran Berry into the play area when executing its boosted Basic Attack and Defense Curl, or when sustaining damage due to its Cheek Pouch Ability. Its moves Stuff Cheeks and Covet generate multiple Oran Berries. Any player character, including Greedent's opponents, can consume these dropped Oran Berries to restore HP. Oran Berries remain on the ground for 10 seconds before despawning. Finally, Greedent eats an Oran Berry during its Unite Move, Berry Belly Flop, restoring its own health.

As one of the most frequently depicted Berries in the animation, the Oran Berry commonly aids in healing weakened Pokémon. It made its debut in The Lotad Lowdown, shown growing at the Pretty Petal flower shop. Brawly's fridge contained Oran Berries in Just One of the Geysers. Pokéblock, Stock, and Berry showed Oran Berries for sale at the Lilycove Berry Market, while Berry, Berry Interesting depicted their use in Pokéblock production. Brock provided Oran Berries to a wild Nuzleaf in Leave It To Brocko!, and Abigail's Berry basket in Cooking Up a Sweet Story! contained them. Oran Berry trees appeared in Buizel Your Way Out Of This!, where Buizel was also observed eating one. Hungry for the Good Life! also featured Oran Berries. In Trials and Adulations!, Dawn's Ambipom retrieved Oran Berries for her injured Mamoswine. A wild Tangrowth, which had been using Absorb to drain energy from other Pokémon to restore a damaged tree in Promoting Healthy Tangrowth!, left Meowth an Oran Berry for his recovery. Oran Berries were seen cultivating in Lulu's garden in Three Sides to Every Story!. In PK21, Oran trees were present; Meowth's attempt to pick a Berry mistakenly led to him grabbing Marill's tail, prompting Marill to use Sing and lull Meowth, Wobbuffet, and Mime Jr. to sleep. Brock combined Durin and Oran Berries into an energy drink to treat a poisoned Pichu in The Brockster Is In!, but Pichu was too weak to drink it and was instead healed by Brock's newly evolved Chansey's Soft-Boiled. Iris gave an Oran Berry to a wild Venipede in A Venipede Stampede!. Ash and Iris's race to an Oran tree and back in Ash and Trip's Third Battle! remained unfinished after they encountered Trip. Oran Berries were visible in Eindoak Town in the movies White—Victini and Zekrom and Black—Victini and Reshiram. The Beartic Mountain Feud! also showcased Oran Berries. Iris offered a wild Bouffalant an Oran Berry in Baffling the Bouffalant!. In All for the Love of Meloetta!, Iris and Cilan discovered an Oran tree on a cliff, where Iris used her Emolga to retrieve a Berry for the sick Meloetta. A flashback in Kyurem VS. The Sword of Justice showed Keldeo and Virizion eating Oran Berries. N presented Ash's Pikachu with an Oran Berry in The Name's N! to aid its recovery from James's Amoonguss's attack. Oran Berries were incorporated into a medicine in The Island of Illusions!. Wayfarer Island cultivated Oran trees in Butterfree and Me!, where Ash and his friends gathered Berries for a wild Caterpie they were attempting to help evolve, but were subsequently driven off by an angered Ursaring, whose territory it was. Clemont distributed an Oran Berry to Ash's Pikachu, Froakie, and newly captured Fletchling in A Battle of Aerial Mobility!. Sherman's Furfrou grooming salon featured Oran Berries in Grooming Furfrou!. Penelope offered Ash's Froakie an Oran Berry in Kindergarten Chaos! after her Sylveon had bested Froakie in battle. A group of wild Malamar and Inkay were observed feeding Oran Berries to forest Pokémon near Grace Tower in Facing the Grand Design!. Ash gifted Oran Berries to his Pokémon in Under the Pledging Tree!. Dr. White's garden in A Fork in the Road! A Parting of the Ways! contained growing Oran Berries. Meowth was seen consuming Oran Berries from a pile in Good Friends, Great Training!. For the Dendemille Town Pokémon Showcase in Performing with Fiery Charm!, Oran Berries served as Poké Puff ingredients. A Pokémon Center featured Oran Berries in Adventures in Running Errands!. Oran Berries were also part of the Anistar City Pokémon Showcase in A Performance Pop Quiz!. A wild Litleo was seen eating an Oran Berry from an Oran tree in A Fiery Rite of Passage!. In A Dancing Debut!, Oran Berries were used in the Couriway Town Pokémon Showcase and later included in Ash and his friends' meal. Volcanion and the Mechanical Marvel depicted Oran Berries at the Nebel Plateau. Ash and his friends utilized Oran Berries for a feast at Professor Sycamore's laboratory in Battling With a Clean Slate!. Oran Berries appeared in First Catch in Alola, Ketchum-Style!. In That's Why the Litten is a Scamp!, Litten retrieved an Oran Berry from a pile and presented it to an elderly Stoutland it lived with. Treasure Hunt, Akala Style! showed Oran Berries during Lana's and Mallow's respective treasure hunts on Akala Island. Oran Berries constituted part of Snowy's meal in Night of a Thousand Poses!. Mallow was seen feeding Oran Berries to an injured Snorlax in A Mission of Ultra Urgency!. Bewear's den contained piles of Oran Berries in Acting True to Form!. The banned episode SM064 featured an Oran Berry at a wild Passimian group's nest. Leon gave Ash an Oran Berry to heal his Pikachu after Ash's defeat against him in The Climb to Be the Very Best!. Sword and Shield, Slumbering Weald!

Title: Persim Berry

The Persim Berry, known in Japanese as キーのみ (Kī Fruit), made its debut as a type of Berry in Generation III. Functionally, it serves as the spiritual successor to Generation II's Bitter Berry, performing the exact same role when employed by or on a Pokémon. For trainers playing the Generation V titles, selling a Persim Berry to the gourmet enthusiast located on Route 5 yields a sum of $20. Should a Pokémon holding this Berry become confused, it will automatically consume the Persim Berry it carries, thereby alleviating its confusion. If a Pokémon is forced to eat a Persim Berry through moves like Fling, Pluck, Bug Bite, or Item Urge, the consuming Pokémon is similarly cured of confusion. Administering a Persim Berry from the player's Bag to a Pokémon will likewise resolve its confused status, depleting the Berry in the process. Within Pokémon Platinum, players had the option to trade Persim Berries for various accessories at the Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop. The Pokémon Dream World allowed for Berry exchanges for Décor, with specific Décor items stipulating Persim Berries as a requirement. In Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, this Berry can be swapped for Stickers at the Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop.

The Persim Berry is also present in Pokémon Sleep, where it stands among the Berries collectible by helper Pokémon for consumption by Snorlax. These Berries augment Snorlax's Strength, with their potency doubling if a Persim Berry happens to be Snorlax's preferred kind. The fundamental Strength boost provided by a Persim Berry scales with the level of the helper Pokémon responsible for collecting it. Normal-type helper Pokémon within a player's active team are capable of gathering Persim Berries. Consistent with other Berries in the game, collected Persim Berries do not enter the player's inventory; instead, Snorlax consumes them instantly.

In the anime, Persim Berries first appeared in "A Fork in the Road! A Parting of the Ways!", observed thriving in Dr. White's garden. During "Tripped Up by Torkoal," Ruby retrieved several Berries, a Persim Berry among them, for the purpose of Berry Blending. An instance of a Persim Berry was noted in "I Dare Ya, Altaria... Knock Chic off My Shoulder!", forming part of Sapphire's Berry collection from her journeys. There exists a compilation of cards either bearing the name Persim Berry or depicting a Pokémon holding one. This particular Berry has also been showcased across various officially licensed merchandise and collectible figures. Notwithstanding its functional kinship with Generation II's Bitter Berry in curing confusion, the Persim Berry itself lacks a bitter taste. Indeed, in Generations IV and VIII, bitterness is uniquely absent from the Persim Berry's flavor profile. Its nomenclature and visual aesthetic likely draw inspiration from persimmon fruits.

Title: Lum Berry

Known in Japanese as ラムのみ (Lum Fruit), the Lum Berry made its debut as a Berry in Generation III. It serves as the direct successor to the Generation II MiracleBerry, sharing the exact same restorative properties whether a Pokémon holds or uses it. In the Generation V titles, players have the option to sell this Berry to the gourmet maniac found on Route 5 for a price of $20. When a Pokémon holding a Lum Berry becomes afflicted by any non-volatile status condition or confusion, the Berry is automatically consumed, subsequently curing the Pokémon of its ailment. If another Pokémon is forced to consume a Lum Berry through moves such as Fling, Pluck, or Bug Bite, the consumer itself is similarly relieved of its non-volatile status condition or confusion. Direct application from the Bag to a Pokémon will also remove that Pokémon's non-volatile status condition, resulting in the Berry's consumption.

Its utility in Pokémon Legends: Arceus extends to healing any status condition on a Pokémon, or it can be strategically thrown near wild Pokémon to capture their focus. During the era of the Pokémon Dream World, participants were able to trade various Berries for Décor; specific Décor items called for Lum Berries. In a similar vein, Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl allows players to trade Lum Berries for Stickers at the Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop.

Within Pokémon Masters EX, the Lum Berry's application is restricted to specific sync pairs possessing it as an item move. In Pokémon Sleep, the Lum Berry is among the types of Berries that companion Pokémon can collect and provide to Snorlax. These Berries contribute to Snorlax's Strength, with their potency doubling if the Lum Berry happens to be Snorlax's preferred food. The initial Strength boost provided by each Berry scales with the level of the helper Pokémon responsible for collecting it. Only Bug-type helper Pokémon within the player's active team are capable of gathering Lum Berries. Consistent with all Berries in Pokémon Sleep, any Lum Berries gathered bypass the player's inventory and are immediately consumed by Snorlax.

In the world of Pokémon Pokopia, Lum Berries are found growing natively on Lum Trees. Here, Ditto can consume them to replenish PP, and they can also be offered to other Pokémon to restore their vitality. The anime first acknowledged Lum Berries in *Beating the Bustle and Hustle!*, where James noted their presence on the Sinnoh Pokémon Hustle Refreshment Room menu alongside Old Gateau. Their physical debut occurred in *Three Sides to Every Story!* when Team Rocket pilfered a quantity from Lulu. Jessie, mistaking the intended recipient, consumed a Lum Berry herself, unaware that her Yanmega was the one meant to eat them during battle. Khoury was observed vending Lum Berries from his stall at the Johto Festival in *An Egg Scramble!*. In *Crisis at Ferroseed Research!*, Professor Malveaux directed Case to formulate a treatment for Pikachu, which incorporated Lum Berry extract. Jessica showcased a pair of Lum Berries in *Grooming Furfrou!*. A Lum Berry was depicted in a newspaper utilized by Jessie to evade Ash and his companions in *To Find a Fairy Flower!*. Ash presented Lum Berries to his Pokémon as a token of affection in *Under the Pledging Tree!*. Dr. White's garden featured growing Lum Berries in *A Fork in the Road! A Parting of the Ways!*. Lum Berries were spotted at a Pokémon Center in *Adventures in Running Errands!*. They were integrated into the Anistar City Pokémon Showcase in *A Performance Pop Quiz!*. During the preparation of a feast at Professor Sycamore's laboratory in *Battling With a Clean Slate!*, Ash and his friends made use of Lum Berries. An Alola Berry market displayed Lum Berries in *Alola to New Adventure!*. They made an appearance in *First Catch in Alola, Ketchum-Style!*.

Lillie included Lum Berries in a meal she offered to Pikachu in *The Ol' Raise and Switch!*. A Lum Berry was situated within a wild Passimian group's nest in SM064. Pikachu Valley contained Lum Berries in *A Plethora of Pikachu!*. In *Betrayed, Bothered, and Beleaguered!*, Pikachu managed to pluck a Lum Berry from its tree, only for a Spearow to swipe it. Mimey successfully retrieved it using Psychic, but the Berry remained uneaten due to Pikachu's need to protect Mimey from an incoming Spearow flock. In *The Power of Us*, a film separate from the main TV series continuity, Team Rocket was seen vending Lum Berries in Fula City. These Berries proved crucial in halting the spread of Effect Spore gas, as Toren ingeniously crafted an improvised cure using them. Friede utilized Lum Berries to pacify an agitated group of Pokémon in *The Treasure After the Storm!*. Numerous Lum Berries were visible in *The Future I Choose*. Eri employed a Lum Berry to resolve the confusion afflicting the Revavroom attached to the Caph Starmobile in *Take a Breath*, following its agitation by a wild Squawkabilly.
Within *Pinsir Me, I Must Be Dreaming*, both Noland's rental Mawile and Emerald's rental Pinsir were equipped with a Lum Berry, though neither was actually used. During their confrontation, Mawile's Lum Berry was swapped with Emerald's rental Linoone's Choice Band via Linoone's Trick move. Emerald's Starmie was observed healing itself from sleep with its held Lum Berry during his Battle Pike challenge in *Moving Past Milotic*. Platinum referenced Lum Berries in *The Final Dimensional Duel VII* while discussing her findings on Jupiter's Tangrowth's signature move, Natural Gift, with Palmer. In *Homecoming and the Brilliant Professional Golfer*, Moon discovered a Lum Berry in Lush Jungle while searching for a rare Mirage Berry, with additional Lum Berries also found among a collection gathered by local Pokémon assisting her quest.
The Lum Berry also exists as a Pokémon Tool card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game, initially introduced in the Japanese ADV Era through the Treecko, Torchic, and Mudkip Constructed Starter Decks. It was subsequently included in the EX Ruby & Sapphire expansion. When this Trainer card is attached to a Pokémon afflicted by a Special Condition, that Pokémon recovers from all Special Conditions during Pokémon Checkup, after which the card must be discarded. A reprint in the Sword & Shield series introduced errata, altering the effect's timing to occur at the end of each turn instead of during Pokémon Checkup. Its visual design and nomenclature are potentially inspired by the Chinese plum.

Title: Sitrus Berry
The Sitrus Berry, known in Japanese as オボンのみ (Obon Fruit), is a variety of Berry first introduced in the third generation of Pokémon games. It is considered the successor to the Generation II Gold Berry, initially possessing an identical effect when used on or by a Pokémon. In Generation IV, the Sitrus Berry's effect was altered to instead replenish 25% of the user's maximum HP. Within the Generation V titles, this Berry can be sold to the gourmet maniac located on Route 5 for $20.

If a Pokémon holds this item, and its HP drops below 50% at the end of a turn, it will be consumed, replenishing 30 HP.
