Should a Pokémon's HP fall under 50% while holding a Sitrus Berry, the item will be automatically consumed, restoring 25% of that Pokémon's maximum HP. When forcibly ingested through moves such as Fling, Pluck, or Bug Bite, it restores 25% of the consuming Pokémon's maximum HP. A known glitch in Generation V means that if a Pokémon's HP falls below half due to self-inflicted confusion damage, the Sitrus Berry will not activate.

Utilizing it from the Bag on a Pokémon will restore 30 HP to that specific Pokémon, and the item is consumed upon use. Alternatively, when employed from the Bag, it will restore 25% of the target Pokémon's maximum HP, being consumed in the process. If the Pokémon possessing the Berry has the Ripen ability, the restorative effect is doubled, restoring 50% of its maximum HP. In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, the Sitrus Berry replenishes 50% of a Pokémon's maximum HP and can additionally be tossed near wild Pokémon to divert their focus.

Within the Pokémon Dream World, players were able to trade Berries for Décor items, with Sitrus Berries being a requirement for certain Décor. In Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, these Berries can be exchanged for Stickers at the Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop.

The Sitrus Berry makes an appearance in the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon franchise as a consumable food item that restores HP. Specifically, in Red and Blue Rescue Team and Explorers, it restores 100 HP and adds 5 to the Belly stat. If consumed when HP is full, it permanently increases maximum HP by 2. In Pokémon Super Mystery Dungeon and Rescue Team DX, its effect is to restore 200 HP and fill Belly by 2. Eating it at maximum HP will permanently boost max HP by 5. For Pokémon: Magikarp Jump, Sitrus Berries can manifest as food items for Magikarp consumption. In Pokémon Masters EX, Sitrus Berries are exclusively available for use by specific sync pairs possessing them as an item move.

Pokémon Sleep includes the Sitrus Berry among the Berries that helper Pokémon can collect and provide to Snorlax. These Berries boost Snorlax's Strength, with the effect being doubled if it's Snorlax's preferred Berry. The fundamental Strength increase from the Berry is dependent on the level of the helper Pokémon responsible for gathering it. Rock-type helper Pokémon within the player's team are capable of gathering Sitrus Berries. Consistent with all other Berries in the game, collected Sitrus Berries do not enter the player's inventory but are instead immediately consumed by Snorlax.

In Pokémon UNITE, Sitrus Berries are present across all game stages. On the Standard Battle and Ranked Battle maps, specifically Remoat Stadium and Theia Sky Ruins, they are found in pairs immediately behind each team's foremost goal zones. This arrangement is mirrored on other stages within the game. However, in Mer Stadium, only one of the two Sitrus Berries is situated on the path behind the leading goal zones, with the second berry positioned just off-road, a short distance behind the first. Despite Auroma Park having shared goal zones, two Sitrus Berries still manifest close to each goal zone, albeit nearer to the openings of the map's upper-left and bottom-right corners. Shivre City stands as an exception, presenting a chain of Sitrus Berries along the routes connecting the goal zones. Consuming a Sitrus Berry restores health to the player who utilizes it. Players possess the capability to consume Sitrus Berries even when they are situated on the opposing team's side of the map. While most Pokémon cannot consume Sitrus Berries at full health, Greedent is an exception, permitted to do so because of its Berry-centric moveset. After consumption, Sitrus Berries will respawn once a specific duration has elapsed. Nevertheless, these Berries will cease to appear and will despawn if their associated goal zone is destroyed.

In "Just One of the Geysers," Sitrus Berries were observed among numerous food items within Brawly's refrigerator. "Pokéblock, Stock, and Berry" featured Sitrus Berries among the various other Berries offered for sale at the Lilycove Berry Market. "Berry, Berry Interesting" included appearances of Sitrus Berries. Abigail's Berry basket contained Sitrus Berries in "Cooking Up a Sweet Story!". Sitrus Berries were featured in "Hungry for the Good Life!". In "Promoting Healthy Tangrowth!", a wild Tangrowth, which had been using Absorb to drain energy from Pokémon to revitalize a lightning-damaged tree, left Sitrus Berries for the Pokémon it had attacked. Ash's backpack was shown to contain Sitrus Berries in "Purrloin: Sweet or Sneaky?". "The Beartic Mountain Feud!" included Sitrus Berries. A Sitrus Berry was spotted at a Pokémon Center during "The Island of Illusions!". Pikachu retrieved a Sitrus Berry from a tree using an electric attack for a hungry Dedenne in "A Shockingly Cheeky Friendship!". Sitrus Berries were present at a Furfrou grooming salon in "Grooming Furfrou!". Ash presented Sitrus Berries to his Pokémon as a gift during "Under the Pledging Tree!". Meowth was depicted consuming Sitrus Berries in "Good Friends, Great Training!". "Adventures in Running Errands!" featured Sitrus Berries at a Pokémon Center. Sitrus Berries played a role in the Anistar City Pokémon Showcase during "A Performance Pop Quiz!". Pikachu extended a Sitrus Berry to Squishy in "From A to Z!", but Squishy declined, opting to draw energy from sunlight instead. Team Rocket attempted to use a Sitrus Berry as bait for a wild Litleo they aimed to capture in "A Fiery Rite of Passage!", but their plan was thwarted when Litleo's father, a Pyroar, detected and attacked them. "A Dancing Debut!" showcased Sitrus Berries initially during the Couriway Town Pokémon Showcase and subsequently as an element of Ash and his friends' meal. Florges endeavored to entice a Carbink, which was causing disruption in the wetlands, with Sitrus Berries in "A Diamond in the Rough!". Nevertheless, Carbink successfully seized the Berries undetected. Sitrus Berries were observed at the Nebel Plateau in "Volcanion and the Mechanical Marvel." Ash and his companions incorporated Sitrus Berries into a feast they prepared at Professor Sycamore's laboratory during "Battling With a Clean Slate!". Tapu Koko dropped two Sitrus Berries in "Alola to New Adventure!". Anela's store in Hau'oli City stocked Sitrus Berries, as seen in "First Catch in Alola, Ketchum-Style!" and "One Journey Ends, Another Begins...". Litten was observed possessing a Sitrus Berry in "That's Why the Litten is a Scamp!", which an Alolan Persian attempted to pilfer. Subsequently in the episode, Litten delivered the Berry to an elderly Stoutland with whom it resided. Throughout Pokémon the Series: Sun & Moon, Sitrus Berries frequently appeared within the Berry pile in Bewear's den. "A Team-on-Team Tussle!" featured Sitrus Berries. Anela distributed Sitrus Berries to Ash's Rowlet, Rockruff, Litten, and Snowy in "The Ol' Raise and Switch!". Snowy initially showed reluctance, but Litten approached and personally offered one for her to consume. Sitrus Berries were present in "Treasure Hunt, Akala Style!" during Lana's inaugural treasure hunt on Akala Island. While searching for a Miracle Seed as a dish ingredient in "Currying Favor and Flavor!", Ash and Pikachu discovered Sitrus Berries inside a hollow stump within Lush Jungle. "A Mission of Ultra Urgency!" showed Sitrus Berries included in the Ultra Guardians' medical kit. A portion of these Berries was subsequently used to revive a Snorlax that had been depleted by a Buzzwole. Within the same installment, Team Rocket was also observed collecting Sitrus Berries in a forested area. The banned episode SM064 featured Sitrus Berries. Anela's basket of Berries, gifted to Ash and his classmates' Pokémon in "Real Life...Inquire Within!", contained Sitrus Berries. Tapu Bulu administered a Sitrus Berry to heal Lycanroc between the second and third rounds of Ash's grand trial match against Nanu in "Guiding an Awakening!". Following their training battle in "Parallel Friendships!", Dia's Zeraora presented Ash's Pikachu with a Sitrus Berry. During "Mind-Boggling Dynamax!", a Snorlax leaped to consume a Sitrus Berry that had dropped from a tree. The Snorlax then expelled a Sitrus seed, which subsequently developed into a massive tree upon the Sleeping Pokémon's belly after it Gigantamaxed. This tree yielded a gigantic Sitrus Berry, which Ash, Goh, Pikachu, and Scorbunny employed to prompt the Snorlax to relocate.

Title: Figy Berry

The Figy Berry (Japanese: フィラのみ Fira Fruit) is a variety of Berry first introduced in Generation III.

Within the Generation V games, this Berry could be sold to the gourmet maniac situated on Route 5 for a price of $20. When a Pokémon holds a Figy Berry, its HP will be restored by ⅛ of its maximum value if its health drops to ½ or below. However, this causes confusion in Pokémon that do not appreciate its spicy flavor. Specifically, Pokémon with Modest, Timid, Calm, or Bold Natures will become confused. If consumed unwillingly through moves like Fling, Pluck, or Bug Bite, the Figy Berry restores ⅛ of the consumer's maximum HP and induces confusion in any consumer that dislikes its spicy taste.

Later, the Figy Berry's restorative effect when held by a Pokémon was updated: it began restoring ½ of the holder's HP when its health fell to ¼ or below. This amount was subsequently reduced to ⅓ of the holder's HP. If the Pokémon possessing the Berry has the Ripen Ability, it will instead restore ⅔ of its maximum HP.

When utilized from the Bag on a Pokémon, the Figy Berry initially replenished ⅛ of the Pokémon's maximum HP and was consumed after use. Over time, the Bag-use effect evolved, first restoring ½ of the Pokémon's maximum HP, then later ⅓ of its maximum HP. In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, a Figy Berry used from the Bag restores ½ of a Pokémon's maximum HP.

Should a wild Pokémon that dislikes spicy flavors consume a Figy Berry, it will briefly pause its actions.

In the Pokémon Dream World, players had the option to trade Berries for Décor, and certain Décor items specifically required Figy Berries.

Figy Berries are present in Pokémon Sleep, where they can be gathered by helper Pokémon and given to Snorlax. These Berries contribute to raising Snorlax's Strength, with the effect doubling if the Figy Berry is Snorlax's favorite. The base Strength increase is determined by the level of the helper Pokémon responsible for gathering it. Only Ground-type helper Pokémon in the player's team are able to gather Figy Berries. As with all Berries in Pokémon Sleep, gathered Figy Berries do not enter the player's inventory but are immediately consumed by Snorlax.

The Figy Berry made its initial appearance in the episode Beheeyem, Duosion, and the Dream Thief!, where Cilan required them for a spicy meal. Upon realizing he lacked them, Beheeyem produced some for him. Cilan later employed some Figy Berries to awaken Meowth. They were observed at Oranguru's house in Mallow and the Forest Teacher!. In Very Vexing Volbeat, Amber commanded his Volbeat to use Trick, exchanging its Figy Berry for the Blue Orb carried by Tabitha's Torkoal. During Grumpy Gliscor, Lax employed a Figy Berry as one of the Berries thrown at a wild Gliscor attacking the S.S. Sinnoh, aiming to distract it. A Figy Berry was among the ingredients Diamond used to prepare food for himself, his Pokémon, and Riley while training at Iron Island in Lucky Lucario II. Moon utilized a Figy Berry from a Berry tree in The Delivery of Rotom and the Girl as an ingredient for painkiller medicine for Sun's injured foot. Moon discovered a Figy Berry in Lush Jungle in Homecoming and the Brilliant Professional Golfer during her quest for a special Mirage Berry. The Figy Berry is also featured on the Naughty Mimikyu card sleeves.

The naming and visual characteristics of the Figy Berry may draw inspiration from a common fig.

Title: Wiki Berry

The Wiki Berry, known in Japanese as ウイのみ Wi Fruit, first appeared in Generation III as a new type of Berry. In the Generation V games, trainers had the option to sell this Berry to the gourmet maniac situated on Route 5 for a price of $20.

When held by a Pokémon, a Wiki Berry initially provided a recovery of ⅛ of its maximum HP once its health fell to ½ or below. However, this came with a drawback: Pokémon disliking the dry flavor would become confused. Specifically, this confusion affected Pokémon possessing Adamant, Jolly, Careful, or Impish Natures. If a Wiki Berry was forcefully consumed via moves such as Fling, Pluck, or Bug Bite, it would also restore ⅛ of the consumer's maximum HP, and similarly confuse any consumer with an aversion to the dry taste.

Later iterations of the mechanic saw changes to its healing properties. The Wiki Berry began restoring ½ of the holder's HP when its health dropped to ¼ or below. Subsequently, this restored amount was adjusted to ⅓ of the holder's HP. Furthermore, if the holding Pokémon possessed the Ability Ripen, the Berry would instead restore a greater ⅔ of its maximum HP.

When utilized directly from the Bag on a Pokémon, the Wiki Berry would restore ⅛ of that Pokémon's maximum HP before being consumed. This effect was later modified, first to restore ½ of a Pokémon's maximum HP when used from the Bag, and then subsequently changed again to restore ⅓ of its maximum HP via Bag usage. In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, applying a Wiki Berry from the Bag restores ½ of a Pokémon's maximum HP. Wild Pokémon that have a dislike for dry flavors will briefly pause their actions after consuming a Wiki Berry.

During the era of the Pokémon Dream World, players could exchange various Berries for Décor items, with certain Décor requiring Wiki Berries.

In Pokémon Sleep, the Wiki Berry is obtainable as one of the Berries collected by helper Pokémon to be fed to Snorlax. These Berries contribute to Snorlax's Strength, with the effect doubling if the Wiki Berry happens to be Snorlax's favorite. The fundamental amount of Strength gained from the Berry is determined by the level of the helper Pokémon that gathered it. Specifically, Dark-type helper Pokémon in the player's team are capable of gathering Wiki Berries. Consistent with all Berries in this particular game, gathered Wiki Berries are not added to the player's inventory; instead, they are immediately consumed by Snorlax.

The Wiki Berry made its animated debut in "Beheeyem, Duosion, and the Dream Thief!", where Cilan had some among his extensive collection of cooking ingredients. He expressed disappointment, however, as he actually required Figy Berries for his spicy culinary creation. The Berry also appeared in "First Catch in Alola, Ketchum-Style!". Throughout "Pokémon the Series: Sun & Moon", Wiki Berries were frequently visible within the pile of Berries found in Bewear's den. In "Night of a Thousand Poses!", sliced Wiki Berries constituted a portion of Snowy's meal. "Real Life...Inquire Within!" featured Wiki Berries as part of a Berry basket presented to Ash and his classmates' Pokémon by Anela. In "The Young Flame Strikes Back!", Pikachu, Poipole, and Togedemaru were observed eating a few Wiki Berries. Additionally, numerous Wiki Berries were seen in "The Future I Choose".

In the manga, specifically in "Adding It Up with Plusle & Minun I", Minun offered a Wiki Berry to Ruby's Mightyena, Nana. Due to Nana's aversion to the Berry's dry taste, she became confused upon eating it.

A curious detail in Pokémon Sword and Shield is the Wiki Berry's Curry description, which refers to its "sour flavor," despite the Berry actually contributing a dry flavor to the curry recipe.

The nomenclature of the Wiki Berry might stem from the kiwifruit, though its visual design distinctly features prominent spikes, reminiscent of a horned melon.

Title: Mago Berry

The Mago Berry, known in Japanese as マゴのみ (Mago Fruit), first appeared as a Berry type in Generation III. Within the Generation V titles, players could sell this Berry to the gourmet maniac situated on Route 5 for a price of $20. When a Pokémon holds a Mago Berry, its HP is restored by ⅛ of its maximum once its health falls to ½ or less. However, this sweet-tasting Berry induces confusion in Pokémon that find its flavor unappealing (specifically, those possessing Brave, Quiet, Sassy, or Relaxed Natures). If a Pokémon is forced to eat a Mago Berry through moves like Fling, Pluck, or Bug Bite, it gains ⅛ of its total HP back, and confusion will strike any consumer with a distaste for its sweet flavor. Subsequently, this Berry's effect when held was changed to restore ½ of the holder's HP upon their health falling to ¼ or less. The amount restored by a held Mago Berry was later reduced to ⅓ of the Pokémon's total HP. Should the holding Pokémon possess the Ripen Ability, the Berry will instead recover ⅔ of its maximum HP. Administering a Mago Berry from the Bag to a Pokémon recovers ⅛ of that Pokémon's maximum HP, and the Berry is expended upon use. At a later point, using a Mago Berry from the Bag on a Pokémon began restoring ½ of its maximum HP. Following another update, a Mago Berry administered from the Bag to a Pokémon would restore ⅓ of its maximum HP. For Pokémon Legends: Arceus, this Berry's effect is to restore ½ of a Pokémon's maximum HP when consumed. In the wild, a Pokémon that dislikes sweet tastes will temporarily halt its actions if it consumes a Mago Berry.

During the era of the Pokémon Dream World, players had the option to trade various Berries for Décor, with certain Décor items specifically necessitating Mago Berries. The Mago Berry is present in Pokémon Sleep, where it serves as one of the Berries collectible by helper Pokémon to be fed to Snorlax. These Berries increase Snorlax's Strength, with their impact doubling if they happen to be Snorlax's preferred Berry. The foundational Strength gain provided by the Berry is dependent on the level of the helper Pokémon responsible for its collection. Psychic-type helper Pokémon within a player's team are capable of gathering Mago Berries. Consistent with all Berries in Pokémon Sleep, any Mago Berries gathered do not enter the player's inventory but are instead instantly consumed by Snorlax.

The inaugural appearance of Mago Berries occurred in "Pokéblock, Stock, and Berry," an episode where Ash received some to counteract the lingering sourness from a Nomel Berry he had consumed previously. During "Crisis at Ferroseed Research!," Professor Malveaux instructed Case to formulate a remedy for Pikachu, a concoction that incorporated Mago Berry extract. In "Currying Favor and Flavor!," Ash and Mallow found themselves needing to traverse a patch of slumbering Fomantis to retrieve a Mago Berry from a Mago tree. "Got Meltan?" depicted Ash's Rowlet attempting to substitute Meltan's missing hex nut with a Mago Berry, an effort that Meltan swiftly dismissed. Within "Healing the Healer!," Mago Berries were part of the assortment Goh collected for a Suicune and various forest Pokémon to consume. Several Mago Berries made an appearance in "The Future I Choose." "Adding It Up with Plusle & Minun I" featured Minun presenting a Mago Berry to Ruby's Mumu. As Mumu did not appreciate the Berry's sweet flavor, it consequently fell into confusion after consumption. For Emerald's Trainer to eat, Mago Berries were among the Berries that his Sceptile expertly sliced using Leaf Blade in "Sneaky Like Shedinja II." A Mago Berry formed part of Professor Juniper's meal in "Fussing and Fighting." Her attention diverted by a phone call from Fennel, the Berry was then roasted and consumed by Tepig. While searching for a distinctive Mirage Berry in Lush Jungle, Moon discovered a Mago Berry during "Homecoming and the Brilliant Professional Golfer." Additional Mago Berries were also visible within a collection of Berries amassed by local Pokémon assisting her quest.

Uniquely among Berries, the Mago Berry's English designation forms a component of another Berry's name. It is conjectured that the Mago Berry's appellation and visual characteristics derive from the mango fruit.

Title: Aguav Berry

The Aguav Berry, known as バンジのみ (Banji Fruit) in Japanese, originated as a Berry variant in Generation III. Within Generation V titles, players had the option to sell an Aguav Berry to the gourmet enthusiast located on Route 5 for a sum of $20. If a Pokémon holds an Aguav Berry, it will recover one-eighth of its total HP once its current HP falls to half or less; however, this effect inflicts confusion upon Pokémon with an aversion to bitter tastes. This specifically applies to Pokémon possessing Naughty, Rash, Naive, or Lax Natures. Upon being forcibly consumed through moves like Fling, Pluck, or Bug Bite, the Berry restores one-eighth of the consumer's maximum HP and induces confusion in any consumer that dislikes its bitter taste.

In an updated mechanic, a held Aguav Berry now replenishes half of the holder's HP when their health drops to one-quarter or less. Subsequently, the amount restored when held was decreased to one-third of the holder's HP. Should the Pokémon possess the Ripen Ability, it will instead regain two-thirds of its total HP.

Utilizing an Aguav Berry from the Bag on a Pokémon restores one-eighth of that Pokémon's maximum health. The item is consumed following its application. Later revisions to game mechanics altered this, so an Aguav Berry used directly from the Bag on a Pokémon now restores half of its maximum HP. Further updates adjusted the Bag-use restoration to one-third of the Pokémon's maximum HP. In *Pokémon Legends: Arceus*, using this item from the satchel on a Pokémon yields a recovery of half its maximum HP.

A wild Pokémon with an aversion to bitter tastes that consumes an Aguav Berry will momentarily halt its actions. During the era of the Pokémon Dream World, players had the opportunity to trade various Berries for Décor items. Certain Décor specifically necessitated Aguav Berries for exchange. Within *Pokémon Legends: Arceus*, when a massive mass outbreak event is active in a particular zone, players are able to offer 5 Aguav Berries to Mai's Munchlax at any regional base camp, thereby unveiling the Pokémon featured in those outbreaks.

In the narrative of *Adding It Up with Plusle & Minun I*, a Plusle presented an Aguav Berry to Ruby's Delcatty, named Kiki. Nevertheless, due to Kiki's distaste for the Berry's bitter flavor, the Delcatty became confused subsequent to its consumption. The storyline *Homecoming and the Brilliant Professional Golfer* depicts Aguav Berries among a collection of various Berries amassed by Pokémon in the Lush Jungle, as they assisted Moon in her quest for a unique Mirage Berry. Both the nomenclature and visual appearance of the Aguav Berry potentially derive inspiration from the guava fruit.

Title: Iapapa Berry

Note: For the character "Ida," who made an appearance in "Balloons, Brionne, and Belligerence!," refer elsewhere. The Iapapa Berry, known in Japanese as イアのみ (Ia Fruit), was introduced as a new Berry variant in Generation III. In the Generation V video games, players could sell an Iapapa Berry for $20 to a discerning gourmet character found on Route 5.

When held by a Pokémon, an Iapapa Berry will replenish ⅛ of its maximum HP once its health falls to ½ or below. However, it concurrently inflicts confusion upon Pokémon that are averse to sour tastes. Specifically, this effect targets Pokémon possessing Lonely, Mild, Gentle, or Hasty Natures. Should this Berry be consumed forcibly through moves like Fling, Pluck, or Bug Bite, the user regains ⅛ of its maximum HP and similarly becomes confused if it dislikes the sour flavor.

The Iapapa Berry's held effect was later updated to restore ½ of the holder's HP when its health drops to ¼ or less. Subsequently, the restored amount was decreased to ⅓ of the holder's maximum HP. A Pokémon holding the Berry that possesses the Ripen Ability will instead recover ⅔ of its total HP.

When administered from the Bag to a Pokémon, it restores ⅛ of that Pokémon's maximum HP, and the Berry is consumed. This Bag-use effect was later updated to restore ½ of a Pokémon's maximum HP. More recently, using an Iapapa Berry from the Bag now restores ⅓ of a Pokémon's maximum HP. Specifically within Pokémon Legends: Arceus, its function restores ½ of a Pokémon's maximum HP when used. Wild Pokémon with an aversion to sour tastes will momentarily halt their actions if they consume an Iapapa Berry.

Within the Pokémon Dream World, players had the option to trade Berries for Décor items, with certain Décor requiring Iapapa Berries.

Iapapa Berries made their anime debut in "Pokéblock, Stock, and Berry," where they were available for purchase at the Lilycove Berry Market. During "Oshawott's Lost Scalchop!," Ash's Oshawott attempted to substitute its missing scalchop with an Iapapa Berry. This effort proved unsuccessful, as Ash's Pikachu's Thunderbolt attack incinerated and cleaved it, exposing it as a stone fruit. Following this, Iris and Cilan sampled the Berry, remarking on its flavor-enhancing qualities. Throughout "Pokémon the Series: Sun & Moon," Iapapa Berries were frequently observed among the Berry hoard in Bewear's den. They also featured in "A Team-on-Team Tussle!"

In "Currying Favor and Flavor!," Ash and Pikachu discovered Iapapa Berries within a hollowed stump in Lush Jungle while searching for a Miracle Seed to be used as a dish ingredient. Anela presented Ash and his classmates' Pokémon with a basket of Berries in "Real Life...Inquire Within!," which contained Iapapa Berries. Several Iapapa Berries were sighted in "The Future I Choose."

In "What's on Your Mind, Psyduck?," Tyler utilized Iapapa Berries for a pot of pie fillings. Unfortunately, Haru inadvertently knocked over the pot while pursuing Psyduck. Tyler remained unconcerned, however, having additional Iapapa Berries readied by Pansear, Pansage, and Panpour. Subsequently, Haru employed an Iapapa Berry to help Psyduck focus its psychic abilities, a task it accomplished by precisely cleaving the Berry.

In "Chancing Upon Chingling," an Iapapa Berry was among those Diamond and Lax strategically used to trip a Team Galactic Grunt at the Team Galactic HQ, allowing Diamond to appropriate his uniform and infiltrate as a Grunt. While aiding Moon's search for a special Mirage Berry in "Homecoming and the Brilliant Professional Golfer," a collection of Pokémon in Lush Jungle gathered a pile of Berries that included multiple Iapapa Berries.

The appellation and physical characteristics of the Iapapa Berry likely draw inspiration from the papaya fruit.

Title: Razz Berry

The Razz Berry (Japanese: ズリのみ Zuri Fruit) debuted as a type of Berry during Generation III. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, this Berry notably features two additional variants: the Silver Razz Berry and the Golden Razz Berry. Within the Generation V titles, players have the option to sell a Razz Berry to the gourmet maniac located on Route 5 for $500.

In most core series games, excluding Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu!, Let's Go, Eevee!, and Pokémon Legends: Arceus, Razz Berries primarily serve as ingredients for items like Pokéblocks and Poffins, lacking direct usage. However, in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, deploying a Razz Berry from the Bag simplifies the capture of a wild Pokémon. This action consumes the Berry, and its effect ceases if the wild Pokémon escapes a Poké Ball. A wild Pokémon cannot consume another Berry while one is already active. Pokémon Legends: Arceus provides a dual utility: it restores 1/3 of a Pokémon's maximum HP, and wild Pokémon that eat one become easier to catch.

The Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop in Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum allows players to trade Razz Berries for various accessories. In Pokémon Diamond and Pearl, the player can exchange: Furthermore, in the Pokémon Dream World, Berries were tradable for Décor, and specific Décor pieces necessitated Razz Berries. For Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, the same flower shop facilitates the exchange of Razz Berries for Stickers.

The Razz Berry holds the distinction of being the inaugural Berry introduced in Pokémon GO. When fed to a wild Pokémon, it increases the likelihood of capture by applying a ×1.5 multiplier to its base catch rate. This effect persists until the Pokémon manages to break out of a thrown Poké Ball. Additionally, these Berries can be provided to Pokémon stationed at Gyms under the player's team's control to boost their motivation levels. Availability for Razz Berries begins at level 8, with rewards granted upon each level-up from Lv. 8 through 20, and subsequently every two levels from Lv. 22 onwards.

In the episode Adding It Up with Plusle & Minun I, Razz Berries were observed growing aboard the Abandoned Ship. Its design and name are potentially derived from raspberries.

Title: Bluk Berry

The Bluk Berry, known in Japan as ブリーのみ (Burī Fruit), made its debut as a type of Berry during Generation III. Gamers playing Generation V titles could exchange a Bluk Berry for $500 by selling it to the gourmet maniac found on Route 5. These Berries serve solely as an ingredient, lacking direct application, and are notably utilized in the creation of Pokéblocks and Poffins. Players in Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum had the option to trade Bluk Berries for various accessories at the Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop. Within the Pokémon Dream World, players were able to swap Berries for Décor items, with certain Décor specifically demanding Bluk Berries. More recently, in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, Stickers can be obtained by exchanging Bluk Berries at the Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop.

In Pokémon Quest, the Bluk Berry functions as a cooking component instrumental in luring wild Pokémon. Categorized as a blue ingredient, it possesses soft, small, and sweet characteristics. Expeditions undertaken on Tumblecube Island offer a chance to acquire Bluk Berries. Their blue hue means they frequently appear in locations such as Backforth Brook, Belly Button Cave, and Hushed Highlands. Additionally, these Berries may be acquired through the random process of recycling Power Stones. Magikarp in Pokémon: Magikarp Jump can consume Bluk Berries, which appear as a food item.

Within Pokémon Sleep, the Bluk Berry is among those collectible Berries that helper Pokémon can gather and provide to Snorlax. These Berries contribute to increasing Snorlax's Strength, with the effect being doubled if it aligns with Snorlax's preferred Berry. The initial Strength boost from the Berry is contingent on the level of the helper Pokémon responsible for collecting it. Specifically, Ghost-type helper Pokémon on a player's team are capable of gathering Bluk Berries. Consistent with all Berries in the game, any collected Bluk Berries are not placed into the player's inventory but are instead promptly consumed by Snorlax.

The Bluk Berry first appeared in the episode "Now That's Flower Power!", where Mr. Big, a renowned Contest expert, incorporated them into his fireworks presentations. Unfortunately, May's Torchic consumed all of his newly harvested Berries, compelling May to seek replacements. That same episode revealed that consuming Bluk Berries imbues both human and Pokémon hair with a greater sheen, a detail Team Rocket overheard and subsequently pursued for their own gain. In "Pokéblock, Stock, and Berry", Bluk Berries were offered for sale at the Lilycove Berry Market. May included Bluk Berries as one of the ingredients for her Pokéblocks during "Berry, Berry Interesting". Abigail's Berry basket in "Cooking Up a Sweet Story!" contained Bluk Berries. Another appearance for Bluk Berries was in "Hungry for the Good Life!". Ash presented Bluk Berries to his Pokémon as a gift in "Under the Pledging Tree!". The Anistar City Pokémon Showcase, featured in "A Performance Pop Quiz!", utilized Bluk Berries as part of its presentation. Within "Volcanion and the Mechanical Marvel", Bluk Berries were observed at the Nebel Plateau. "First Catch in Alola, Ketchum-Style!" also showcased Bluk Berries. Throughout "Pokémon the Series: Sun & Moon", Bluk Berries frequently turned up among the heap of Berries within Bewear's den. When Lana embarked on her initial treasure hunt on Akala Island in "Treasure Hunt, Akala Style!", Bluk Berries made an appearance. Ash and Pikachu discovered Bluk Berries nestled within a hollowed-out stump in Lush Jungle during "Currying Favor and Flavor!", while they were on the lookout for a Miracle Seed to use as a dish component. Anela's basket of Berries, presented to Ash and his classmates' Pokémon in "Real Life...Inquire Within!", contained Bluk Berries. In "The Tale of You and Glimwood Tangle!", a group of three Impidimp were seen pelting Chloe, her Eevee, and a Galarian Ponyta with Bluk Berries.

"Adding It Up with Plusle & Minun I" depicted numerous Bluk Berries as one of the many varieties of Berries thriving on the Abandoned Ship. A Bluk Berry was observed in "Problematic Probopass and Mad Magnezone II", mixed with several other Berries that Cyrus's Probopass's Mini-Noses excavated from Lax's fur, during an attempt to recover Cyrus's camera that Lax had concealed there. During "Lucky Lucario II", a Bluk Berry was noted among the Berries Diamond employed while preparing meals for himself, his Pokémon, and Riley during their training on Iron Island. Jupiter utilized a Bluk Berry in "Getting Past Gastrodon" to convert her Tangrowth's Natural Gift into a Fire-type move, in a battle against Candice's Abomasnow. Professor Juniper's dinner in "Fussing and Fighting" included a Bluk Berry.

The in-game bag description for the Bluk Berry in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, as well as the Generation VII titles, erroneously described it as sour. This inaccuracy was later rectified in Pokémon Sword and Shield, where its texture was properly translated as dry. Although present within the game data of Pokémon GO, the Bluk Berry remains an unused item. Both the visual design and nomenclature of the Bluk Berry potentially draw inspiration from blackberries.

Title: Nanab Berry

The Nanab Berry (Japanese: ナナのみ Nana Fruit), a particular kind of Berry, first appeared in the third generation of games. For Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee! specifically, this Berry also exists in two alternative forms: the Silver Nanab Berry and the Golden Nanab Berry. During Generation V playthroughs, players can sell a Nanab Berry to the gourmet enthusiast located on Route 5 for a sum of $500. Across most core series titles, excluding Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee! as well as Pokémon Legends: Arceus, Nanab Berries serve solely as crafting components for items like Pokéblocks and Poffins, rather than being directly consumable.

Within Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, deploying a Nanab Berry from the Bag decreases the likelihood of a wild Pokémon shifting position during an attempted capture. This action expends the Nanab Berry. Should the wild Pokémon escape from its Poké Ball, the Berry's influence ceases. A wild Pokémon is unable to consume an additional Berry if another Berry's effects are already active. In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, its application restores one-third of a Pokémon's maximum HP. Furthermore, a wild Pokémon that eats a Nanab Berry will exhibit reduced movement speed on the overworld map.

Players in Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum had the option to trade Nanab Berries for various accessories at the Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop. Similarly, in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, these Berries can be exchanged for Stickers at the same establishment. During the Pokémon Dream World era, players could trade Berries for Décor items, with specific Décor requiring Nanab Berries.

Pokémon GO saw the introduction of the Nanab Berry on February 16, 2017. Before December 2, 2020, applying a Nanab Berry to a wild Pokémon reduced its propensity to attack or dodge by 95%, thereby simplifying Poké Ball throws. Since December 2, 2020, the Nanab Berry entirely eliminates the Pokémon's capacity to deflect or evade incoming Poké Balls. This particular Berry's effect persists until the Pokémon escapes from a thrown ball. When provided to a Gym defender, a Nanab Berry replenishes its motivation by an amount 25% greater than what a Razz or Pinap Berry offers. Players can begin acquiring Nanab Berries from level 4. They are granted as level-up rewards every four levels, commencing at level 23. Nanab Berries are also repeatably obtained from unspecified sources.

Within Pokémon UNITE, Nanab Berries are among the collection of Berries that Greedent collides with during the execution of its Unite Move, Berry Belly Flop.

The Nanab Berry first appeared in the episode Slaking Kong, where it was part of a haul of Berries that Team Rocket coerced a group of wild Aipom into collecting for them. In Ka Boom with a View!, a Nanab Berry was dislodged from a tree by Spenser's Venusaur after it slid into it; Spenser later offered these same Berries to Ash, Pikachu, and Swellow. Abigail's Berry basket contained Nanab Berries in Cooking Up a Sweet Story!. They were also depicted in Hungry for the Good Life!. Briefly, in The Name's N!, Nanab Berries garnished some special Pokémon food prepared by Cilan. Ash presented Nanab Berries as gifts to his Pokémon in Under the Pledging Tree!. Bonnie and Meowth discovered a Nanab Berry tree in So You're Having a Bad Day!. During A Performance Pop Quiz!, Nanab Berries were incorporated into the Anistar City Pokémon Showcase. For a feast prepared at Professor Sycamore's laboratory in Battling With a Clean Slate!, Ash and his companions utilized Nanab Berries. In Episode 4 of Where Are You Going, Eevee?, an Eevee encountered a Nanab Berry tree shortly after reaching Melemele Island, climbing it and shaking loose several Nanab Berries to consume. Additional Nanab trees were featured in Episode 6 of Where Are You Going, Eevee?, where Eevee distributed some Nanab Berries to Pikachu and his friends. Ash and Goh employed a Nanab Berry as a lure for a group of wild Mankey in A Snow Day for Searching!. In Healing the Healer!, Nanab Berries were among the assortment of Berries Goh collected for a Suicune and other forest Pokémon. The Dusk Ball That Wouldn't Open depicted Ult and Ryan disputing ownership of Nanab Berries from a tree – Ult desired to eat them, while Ryan required them for his Grafaiai – only for both to be chased off by the owner's Mabosstiff. Finally, in Problematic Probopass and Mad Magnezone II, a Nanab Berry was observed amidst numerous Berries that Cyrus's Probopass's Mini-Noses unearthed from Lax's fur while attempting to recover Cyrus's camera.

The visual design and nomenclature of the Nanab Berry potentially derive from bananas.

Title: Wepear Berry

The Wepear Berry, referred to in Japanese as セシナのみ (Seshina Fruit), first appeared as a type of Berry in Generation III. In the Generation V video games, players had the option to sell this Berry to the gourmet maniac located on Route 5 for $500. Wepear Berries cannot be consumed directly by Pokémon or the player; their sole purpose is as an ingredient for crafting, notably for items such as Pokéblocks and Poffins. Within Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum, the Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop allowed players to trade Wepear Berries for various accessories. During the era of the Pokémon Dream World, players could exchange Berries for Décor items, and specific Décor pieces specifically required Wepear Berries for acquisition. In the more recent Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, the Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop again offers an exchange, with players trading Wepear Berries for Stickers. An instance of this Berry's appearance in media is documented in Problematic Probopass and Mad Magnezone II, where Cyrus's Probopass's Mini-Noses extracted a Wepear Berry from Lax's fur while attempting to retrieve Cyrus's camera. Although present in the game data of Pokémon GO, the Wepear Berry currently serves no function in that game. Its design and name are thought to be inspired by the common pear.

Title: Pinap Berry

Known in Japanese as パイルのみ (Pairu Fruit), the Pinap Berry debuted as a Berry type in Generation III. Within Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, two distinct variations, the Silver Pinap Berry and the Golden Pinap Berry, also exist. Its sale price in Generation V titles is $500 when offloaded to the gourmet enthusiast located on Route 5. Excluding Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu!, Let's Go, Eevee!, and Pokémon Legends: Arceus, Pinap Berries serve exclusively as components for crafting items like Pokéblocks and Poffins in core series entries, rather than being usable directly. When playing Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, deploying a Pinap Berry from the Bag on an encountered wild Pokémon enhances the likelihood of receiving additional item rewards upon its capture. This action expends the Berry. Should the wild Pokémon escape a Poké Ball, the Berry's benefit is nullified. Furthermore, a wild Pokémon cannot consume a new Berry while the effects of a previous one persist. Within Pokémon Legends: Arceus, it functions to restore 1/3 of a Pokémon's total HP. A wild Pokémon that partakes of a Pinap Berry will provide increased Exp. Points when successfully captured. Players in Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum may trade Pinap Berries for accessories at the Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop. The Pokémon Dream World previously allowed players to swap Berries for Décor items, with certain Décor specifically demanding Pinap Berries.

Its debut in Pokémon GO occurred on February 16, 2017. Applying a Pinap Berry to a wild Pokémon in Pokémon GO causes it to yield double the usual Candy upon capture. This effect persists only until the wild Pokémon successfully breaks free from a thrown Poké Ball. When given to a Gym defender, a Pinap Berry boosts its motivation equivalently to a Razz Berry. Players can begin acquiring Pinap Berries from level 18 onwards. They are also bestowed as level-up rewards every four levels, commencing at level 21. Furthermore, these Berries have been featured in finite-duration Shop boxes and are available repeatedly through the completion of certain Field Research or Special Research assignments.

The Pinap Berry made its initial appearance in Mallow and the Forest Teacher!, where Abe and a wild Oranguru utilized them for crafting Pinap Juice. Episode SM064 depicted a Pinap Berry residing within a wild Passimian group's nest. In That New Old Gang of Mine!, Mallow was observed carrying a Pinap Berry alongside Honey. During Problematic Probopass and Mad Magnezone II, a Pinap Berry was discovered amidst numerous other Berries that Cyrus's Probopass's Mini-Noses excavated from Lax's fur in an attempt to recover Cyrus's camera. It has also graced the artwork of the Brilliant Stars Chimchar card and appeared on various officially licensed merchandise and figures. Its nomenclature and visual design potentially derive from the pineapple fruit.

Title: Pomeg Berry

The Pomeg Berry, known in Japan as ザロクのみ (Zaroku Fruit), is a variety of Berry first appearing in Generation III. Within the Generation V titles, trainers had the option to sell this Berry to the gourmet enthusiast located on Route 5 for a sum of $20. Its utility in Pokémon Ruby and Sapphire was limited solely to its role as a Pokéblock ingredient, lacking any direct in-battle or out-of-battle application. Beginning with Pokémon Emerald, and continuing in subsequent games (with the exception of Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness), the Pomeg Berry gained a new function: it could be administered from the Bag to a Pokémon. This action concurrently boosts the Pokémon's friendship (providing +10 when friendship is 0-99, +5 when it is 100-199, and +1SwSh-/+2E-USUM if it's 200-254) while simultaneously diminishing its HP Effort Values (EVs) by precisely ten points, ensuring the value does not drop below 0. Exclusively in Generation IV, if a Pokémon's HP EV was greater than 110 upon consumption, this Berry would instead set the EV to 100.

Within the now-defunct Pokémon Dream World, players were able to trade various Berries for Décor items, with certain Décor specifically necessitating Pomeg Berries for acquisition. Though the Pomeg Berry itself has yet to make an appearance in animated form, its distinctive blossoms were depicted in the episode "The Grass-Type is Always Greener!." It was during this episode that Ash, his companions, and Cheryl discovered a Combee nearby, an event that provided their initial clue leading towards Amber Castle. Later, in "A Conk on Cranidos's Cranium," the Pomeg Berry played a role in Diamond and Pearl's manzai comedy act. Notably, the Chuang Yi translation erroneously identified it as a Rowap Berry. Its presence was also observed in "Problematic Probopass and Mad Magnezone II," where a Pomeg Berry was among the assorted Berries unearthed by the Mini-Noses of Cyrus's Probopass from within Lax's fur, during their attempt to recover Cyrus's camera.

This Berry's characteristic of reducing a Pokémon's HP EVs has famously given rise to the "Pomeg glitch." This exploit allows the HP of a Pokémon with already low HP to be driven so low that it experiences an underflow. This particular glitch is known to manifest in Pokémon Emerald, Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum. Its visual appearance and nomenclature are possibly derived from the fruit pomegranate.

Title: Kelpsy Berry

Hailing from Generation III, the Kelpsy Berry (Japanese: ネコブのみ Nekobu Fruit) is a specific type of Berry. In the Generation V installments, this Berry can be sold for $20 to the gourmand on Route 5. For players of Pokémon Ruby and Sapphire, the Kelpsy Berry served exclusively as a Pokéblock ingredient, lacking any direct use. However, starting with Pokémon Emerald, and notably excluding Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, the Kelpsy Berry gained direct usability from the Bag. When applied to a Pokémon, it concurrently elevates the Pokémon's friendship level (+10 for friendship values 0-99, +5 for 100-199, and +1 in Sword and Shield or +2 in Emerald through Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon for 200-254) while also diminishing its Attack EVs by ten points, never dropping below 0. Solely in Generation IV, if a Pokémon's EV for a stat surpassed 110 when consumed, this Berry would instead set that EV to 100. In Pokémon Emerald, an opportunity existed to exchange a Kelpsy Berry for an Iron if the Favor Lady in the Lilycove City Pokémon Center specifically requested 'something whamish.' Within the Pokémon Dream World, players had the facility to trade various Berries for Décor items; certain Décor pieces particularly required Kelpsy Berries. The Kelpsy Berry first appeared in the anime episode "The Brockster Is In!", where Ash found it during his search for a Pecha Berry needed to cure a group of poisoned Pokémon. Kelpsy Berries were also depicted in "A Team-on-Team Tussle!". In "Problematic Probopass and Mad Magnezone II," a Kelpsy Berry was among the many Berries that Cyrus's Probopass's Mini-Noses unearthed from Lax's fur while attempting to retrieve Cyrus's camera. The name "Kelpsy Berry" may be inspired by kelp, and its visual appearance could be derived from kelp, vanilla, or tick clover fruit.

Title: Qualot Berry

This specific Berry, known as the Qualot Berry (Japanese: タポルのみ Taporu Fruit), was first introduced in Generation III.

In the games of Generation V, players could sell a Qualot Berry to the gourmet maniac found on Route 5 for $20.

During the Pokémon Ruby and Sapphire titles, the Qualot Berry could not be directly consumed but served solely as an ingredient for crafting Pokéblocks.

Starting with Pokémon Emerald and continuing through subsequent core series games (excluding Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness), the Qualot Berry gained the function of being usable from the Bag on a Pokémon. Its application simultaneously boosts friendship (+10 for 0-99, +5 for 100-199, +1SwSh-/+2E-USUM for 200-254) and reduces the Pokémon's Defense Effort Values by ten points, never falling below zero.

Exclusively in Generation IV, if a Pokémon's Defense EV surpassed 110 upon use, this Berry would reduce it to exactly 100.

Within the Pokémon Dream World, players had the option to trade Berries for various Décor items. Certain Décor items required Qualot Berries.

The Qualot Berry made an appearance in the manga "Another Day with Gardevoir". Its likeness has also been featured in the artwork of several Pokémon cards.

The Qualot Berry's name is thought to be inspired by a loquat, and its visual design may similarly derive from a loquat or a kumquat.

Title: Hondew Berry

Known in Japan as ロメのみ (Rome Fruit), the Hondew Berry was first introduced as a particular type of Berry in the third Pokémon generation. During the Generation V games, players had the option to sell this Berry to the gourmet enthusiast located on Route 5 for $20. In Pokémon Ruby and Sapphire, its function was restricted to being an ingredient for Pokéblocks, rendering it unusable directly. From Pokémon Emerald onward, with the exception of Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, the Hondew Berry became usable directly from the Bag on a Pokémon. This application boosts the Pokémon's friendship: by +10 when friendship is 0-99, by +5 when it's 100-199, and by +1 (in Sword and Shield) or +2 (from Emerald to Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon) when it's 200-254. Concurrently, it diminishes the Pokémon's Sp. Atk EVs by ten points, though never below zero. Solely within Generation IV, if a Pokémon's Sp. Atk EV was above 110 when this Berry was administered, it would be reduced precisely to 100. The Pokémon Dream World offered an exchange system where players could trade various Berries for Décor. Among these, specific Décor pieces mandated Hondew Berries for their acquisition. The story 'Sneaky Like Shedinja II' depicted Emerald's Sceptile using Leaf Blade to cut Hondew Berries, preparing them for its Trainer's consumption. In 'The Final Dimensional Duel VII,' Jupiter employed a Hondew Berry to alter her Tangrowth's Natural Gift move into a Ground-type attack during her battle against Marley's Arcanine. It is widely believed that the Hondew Berry's appearance and nomenclature draw inspiration from the honeydew melon.

Title: Grepa Berry

The Grepa Berry, designated as ウブのみ (Ubu Fruit) in Japanese, is a variety of Berry that debuted in Generation III. Within the Generation V titles, this Berry can be vended to the gourmet enthusiast situated on Route 5 for a price of $20. In Pokémon Ruby and Sapphire, the Grepa Berry holds no direct utility, functioning solely as an element in crafting Pokéblocks. Beginning with Pokémon Emerald, and excluding Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, the Grepa Berry becomes usable from the Bag on a Pokémon. Its effects are twofold: it enhances the Pokémon's friendship (granting +10 at friendship levels 0-99, +5 for 100-199, and +1 in SwSh or +2 in E-USUM for 200-255) and simultaneously diminishes its Special Defense EVs by ten points, though never below 0. Exclusively in Generation IV, if the Special Defense EV is above 110 when used, this Berry will reset it to 100. In Pokémon Emerald, a player can trade a Grepa Berry for a Protein should the Favor Lady in the Lilycove City Pokémon Center request 'something slippery'. Alternatively, a Grepa Berry can be exchanged for a Calcium if the Favor Lady seeks 'something roundish'. In the Pokémon Dream World, players were able to swap Berries for Décor; specific Décor items mandated the use of Grepa Berries.

The Grepa Berry appears in Pokémon Sleep as one of the Berries collectible by helper Pokémon to be fed to Snorlax. These Berries boost Snorlax's Strength, with the effect doubling if the Grepa Berry is Snorlax's preferred food. The fundamental amount of Strength gained from the Berry is determined by the level of the helper Pokémon that gathered it. Only Electric-type helper Pokémon within the player's team are capable of gathering Grepa Berries. In line with all other Berries in the game, gathered Grepa Berries bypass the player's inventory and are immediately consumed by Snorlax.

In the episode "Three Sides to Every Story!", Lulu was observed cultivating Grepa Berries in her garden alongside various other Berry types. The Spiky-eared Pichu, who featured in "Arceus and the Jewel of Life," is famously fond of Grepa Berries, a fact highlighted in the "Get Fired Up, Spiky-eared Pichu!" ending song. In "One Journey Ends, Another Begins...", Grepa Berries were among the assortment Anela offered at her market in Hau'oli City. While searching for a Miracle Seed to use as a cooking ingredient in "Currying Favor and Flavor!", Ash and Pikachu discovered Grepa Berries, among other kinds, inside a hollowed-out stump in Lush Jungle. "Acting True to Form!" displayed Grepa Berries within the heap of Berries in Bewear's lair. The unaired episode SM064 featured Grepa Berries at the nest of a group of wild Passimian. In "The Young Flame Strikes Back!", a Grepa Berry was incorporated into the meal for Pikachu, Poipole, and Togedemaru. For "Sours for the Sweet!", Grepa Berries served as a crucial component in Ulu's bread. Numerous Grepa Berries were visible in "The Future I Choose." In "Chancing Upon Chingling," a Grepa Berry was among those used by Diamond and Lax to subdue a Team Galactic Grunt at the Team Galactic HQ, allowing Diamond to appropriate the Grunt's attire and impersonate him.

The Grepa Berry has also been represented in various officially licensed merchandise and collectible figures. Its nomenclature may be inspired by the fruit "grape," while its physical appearance could be modeled after a radish.

Title: Tamato Berry

The Tamato Berry, known in Japanese as マトマのみ (Matoma Fruit), is a variety of Berry first appearing in Generation III. In Generation V titles, players could sell a Tamato Berry to the gourmet enthusiast located on Route 5 for a sum of $20. Its initial role in Pokémon Ruby and Sapphire was solely as a Pokéblock ingredient, lacking direct usability. Beginning with Pokémon Emerald (and in all subsequent main series games, with the exception of Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness), trainers became able to apply the Tamato Berry directly from their Bag to a Pokémon. This action simultaneously boosts the Pokémon's friendship (providing +10 when friendship is 0-99, +5 for 100-199, and +1 in SwSh or +2 in E-USUM for 200-255) and reduces its Speed Effort Values (EVs) by ten points, never dropping below zero. Uniquely in Generation IV, if a Pokémon's Speed EV was greater than 110 upon consumption, the Berry would reset that EV to exactly 100. In Pokémon Emerald, a Tamato Berry could be traded for an HP Up at the Lilycove Pokémon Center, provided the Favor Lady requested 'something pointy'. Within the Pokémon Dream World, players had the option to trade various Berries for Décor items, with some requiring Tamato Berries for acquisition.

A recurring humorous element involving Tamato Berries emerged in episodes like "The Lotad Lowdown" and "Berry, Berry Interesting," where numerous characters, including Ash and Team Rocket, would discover and attempt to consume one. The Berries' intense spiciness consistently overwhelmed them, leading to temporarily swollen lips. "Pokéblock, Stock, and Berry" featured Tamato Berries among the diverse selection available at the Lilycove Berry Market. During "Shocks and Bonds," Brock revealed that Ace Trainer Katie, a competitor against Ash in the Ever Grande Conference, favored Tamato Berries as an addition to her spaghetti. Abigail's Berry basket in "Cooking Up a Sweet Story!" contained several Tamato Berries. Lulu cultivated Tamato Berries alongside various other Berry types in her garden, as depicted in "Three Sides to Every Story!". In "Double-Time Battle Training!", Candice described the "Snowpoint Tamato Berry Ice Cream Bar" as a confection featuring cold ice cream encasing spicy Tamato Berry jelly. Ash inadvertently consumed the entire bar, inspiring Dawn's Flame Ice strategy. During "PK23," while searching for a Rinka Berry, Piplup confidently presented Meloetta, Pikachu, and Snivy with a Tamato Berry plant, mistakenly believing it to be their target. After being corrected, Piplup defiantly sampled a Tamato Berry from the plant, only to experience its overpowering heat. Meowth and Wobbuffet, observing Meloetta, subsequently discovered and ate the Berries, similarly suffering from their intense spice. Sherman's Furfrou grooming salon featured Tamato Berries in "Grooming Furfrou!". Jessie used a newspaper depicting a Tamato Berry to conceal herself from Ash and his companions in "To Find a Fairy Flower!". Ash presented Tamato Berries as gifts to his Pokémon in "Under the Pledging Tree!". Jessie incorporated Tamato Berries into her Dendemille Town Showcase performance during "Performing with Fiery Charm!". In "Hoopa and the Clash of Ages," Hoopa swapped a stolen donut with a Tamato Berry. Chespin then unknowingly picked up and bit into the Berry, instantly reacting to its exceedingly spicy flavor. Tamato Berries were utilized in the Anistar City Pokémon Showcase in "A Performance Pop Quiz!". Anela's offering to Litten in "First Catch in Alola, Ketchum-Style!" included a Tamato Berry among other Berries. "A Team-on-Team Tussle!" featured Tamato Berries. When Lana undertook her initial treasure hunt on Akala Island in "Treasure Hunt, Akala Style!", Tamato Berries were encountered. While searching for a Miracle Seed as a dish ingredient in Lush Jungle during "Currying Favor and Flavor!", Ash and Pikachu discovered Tamato Berries among other Berries inside a hollowed-out tree stump. An Alolan Meowth forcibly fed a Tamato Berry to Team Rocket's Meowth in "Acting True to Form!" to silence him, instantly overwhelming him with its potent spiciness and briefly causing his lips to swell. "A Recipe for Success!" depicted Tamato Berries, with Meowth accidentally consuming one. During the Pokémon Grand Eating Contest in "Crowning the Chow Crusher!", Goh instructed his Skwovet to extract a Tamato Berry from its tail and squeeze its juice onto a Pecha Tart, enabling Skwovet to complete the dish and advance. "The Future I Choose" featured multiple Tamato Berries.

Courtney utilized Tamato Berry juice, applied to her gloves, to temporarily incapacitate Sapphire's vision in "Adding It Up with Plusle & Minun II." Cyrus's Probopass unearthed a Tamato Berry from Lax's fur using its Mini-Noses, among numerous other Berries, while attempting to recover Cyrus's camera in "Problematic Probopass and Mad Magnezone II." Lax hurled a Tamato Berry, among other Berries, at a wild Gliscor assaulting the S.S. Sinnoh in "Grumpy Gliscor" to divert its attention. Diamond included Tamato Berries in the food he prepared for himself, his Pokémon, and Riley during their training on Iron Island, as shown in "Lucky Lucario II." Professor Juniper's dinner in "Fussing and Fighting" included a Tamato Berry. A multitude of Tamato Berries formed part of a Berry pile collected by Pokémon in Lush Jungle during "Homecoming and the Brilliant Professional Golfer," aiding Moon in her quest for a unique Mirage Berry.

Various officially licensed merchandise and figures have depicted the Tamato Berry. Notably, the Tamato Berry stands as the sole EV-reducing Berry in Generation III that deviated from the standard growth time or Berry yield shared by its counterparts. Its nomenclature and visual design potentially draw inspiration from a tomato. Additionally, its appearance might reference a horned melon, or the distinctive texture found on certain chili pepper varieties, such as the Carolina Reaper.

Title: Cornn Berry

The Cornn Berry (Japanese: モコシのみ Mokoshi Fruit) made its debut as a Berry type in Generation III. Trainers playing the Generation V installments of the games have the option to sell a Cornn Berry to the gourmet maniac situated on Route 5 for a sum of $500. These Berries cannot be consumed directly and are exclusively utilized as ingredients, notably in the crafting of Pokéblocks and Poffins. In the original Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum versions, players had the opportunity to trade Cornn Berries for accessories at the Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop. Player exchange possibilities were also present in Pokémon Diamond and Pearl. Furthermore, within the now-discontinued Pokémon Dream World, participants could swap various Berries for Décor items, with Cornn Berries specifically being a prerequisite for certain Décor. More recently, in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, the player can exchange Cornn Berries for Stickers at the Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop. In the manga series, specifically "Stunning Staravia & Stinky Skuntank I", a Cornn Berry was observed to fall from Lax's fur after the Advanced level Grunt's Gible struck him with the move Dragon Rush. The visual aesthetic and nomenclature of the Cornn Berry are possibly derived from either corn or broomcorn.

Title: Magost Berry

The Magost Berry (Japanese: ゴスのみ Gosu Fruit) is a type of produce introduced within Generation III of the Pokémon series. In games from Generation V, players have the option to sell this Berry to the gourmet enthusiast located on Route 5 for a price of $500. Magost Berries cannot be consumed directly by Pokémon or Trainers; their sole utility lies in their role as an ingredient, notably in the creation of Pokéblocks and Poffins.

In the Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum titles, trainers could trade Magost Berries for accessories at the Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop. Later, in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, this same shop allowed for the exchange of Magost Berries for Stickers. Within the Pokémon Dream World, these Berries were among those that could be bartered for various Décor items, with certain Décor specifically requiring Magost Berries.

Several Magost Berries made their first appearance in the episode "Currying Favor and Flavor!" where Ash and Pikachu discovered them hidden inside a hollowed-out tree stump within Lush Jungle, during their search for a Miracle Seed needed for a dish. In "A Pokémon to Help Me!", Haru incorporated multiple Magost Berries into his cooking, roasting them to a precise doneness preferred by Pansear. Haru later sought assistance from Shinx and Luxray to cook these Berries, utilizing their electric powers. Emerald's Sudowoodo prepared Magost Berry juice for her Trainer to drink in "Sneaky Like Shedinja II". During "Stunning Staravia & Stinky Skuntank I", a Magost Berry was among the items dislodged from Lax's fur after an Advanced level Grunt's Gible used Dragon Rush. In "The Final Dimensional Duel VII", Jupiter employed a Magost Berry to alter her Tangrowth's Natural Gift ability into a Rock-type move during a battle against Marley's Arcanine. A Magost Berry was observed as part of Professor Juniper's meal in "Fussing and Fighting".

This particular Berry sprouts from the tree situated on the belly of Gigantamax Snorlax, sharing its growth location with Sitrus Berries and Haban Berries. The visual characteristics and nomenclature of the Magost Berry are likely inspired by the mangosteen fruit.

Title: Rabuta Berry

Hailing from Generation III, the Rabuta Berry (Japanese: ラブタのみ Rabuta Fruit) is a distinct type of Berry. Players in the Generation V titles could divest themselves of Rabuta Berries by selling them for $500 to the gourmet maniac found on Route 5. This particular Berry lacks direct in-battle or field utility, serving solely as an ingredient for creations like Pokéblocks and Poffins. Within Pokémon Emerald, should the Favor Lady in the Lilycove Pokémon Center request 'something pointy', a Rabuta Berry can be traded for an HP Up. The Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop offers accessories in exchange for Rabuta Berries in Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum. During the Pokémon Dream World's operational period, Berries, including Rabuta Berries, could be traded for various Décor items; specific Décor pieces specifically mandated Rabuta Berries for acquisition. More recently, in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, players can obtain Stickers by trading Rabuta Berries at the Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop. In the chapter 'Chancing Upon Chingling', a Rabuta Berry played a pivotal role when Diamond and Lax covertly gave one to a Team Galactic Grunt at the Team Galactic HQ, enabling Diamond to acquire the Grunt's uniform and assume a disguise. The visual and linguistic design of the Rabuta Berry potentially draws inspiration from rambutans.

Title: Nomel Berry

Introduced in Generation III, the Nomel Berry (Japanese: ノメルのみ Nomel Fruit) is a specific variety of Berry. Its market value is established in the Generation V games, where a gourmet enthusiast situated on Route 5 will purchase it for $500. Nomel Berries cannot be utilized directly but are instead designated for use as components in recipes, such as for crafting Pokéblocks and Poffins.

Specific trading opportunities exist across various titles. In Pokémon Emerald, players can exchange a Nomel Berry for an Iron if the Favor Lady at the Lilycove City Pokémon Center requests 'something wham-ish'. Across Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum, Nomel Berries can be traded for accessories at the Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop. The Pokémon Dream World once permitted players to swap Berries for Décor, with certain Décor items specifically necessitating Nomel Berries. This trade function for Stickers also continues in the Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop within Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl.

The Nomel Berry has made several appearances in other media. In the manga chapter *Pokéblock, Stock, and Berry*, free samples of Nomel Berry slices were distributed; Pikachu enjoyed them, while Ash was overwhelmed by their tartness. During *PK23*, Oshawott mistakenly presented a Nomel Berry plant to Meloetta, Pikachu, and Snivy, believing it to be the Rinka plant they were searching for. Upon being corrected, Oshawott defiantly consumed a Nomel Berry, only to discover its intense sourness. Meowth and Wobbuffet, who were tailing Meloetta, subsequently found and ate the Berries themselves, experiencing the same overwhelming tartness. Nomel Berries were also included in a meal Lillie prepared for Pikachu in *The Ol' Raise and Switch!*. In episode *SM064*, a Nomel Berry was observed residing within the nest of a wild Passimian group. In the manga chapter *Chancing Upon Chingling*, Diamond and Lax strategically employed a Nomel Berry, among others, to incapacitate a Team Galactic Grunt at the Team Galactic HQ, allowing Diamond to take the Grunt's attire and assume a disguise. Furthermore, a Nomel Berry was depicted as part of Professor Juniper's dinner in *Fussing and Fighting*.

It is theorized that the Nomel Berry's physical appearance and nomenclature are inspired by lemons.

Title: Spelon Berry

The Spelon Berry (known in Japanese as ノワキのみ Nowaki Fruit) made its debut as a Berry variety in Generation III. During Generation V titles, players could vend this Berry to the gourmet enthusiast located on Route 5, fetching a price of $500. Spelon Berries lack direct utility; their sole function is as a component in creations like Pokéblocks and Poffins. Within Pokémon Emerald, the Favor Lady at the Lilycove Pokémon Center would trade an HP Up for a Spelon Berry, provided her request was for 'something pointy'. Trainers in Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum had the option to swap Spelon Berries for various accessories at the Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop. In Pokémon Diamond and Pearl, players had exchange capabilities. Separately, within the Pokémon Dream World, Berries could be traded for Décor, and specific Décor items required Spelon Berries.

During the episode "Celebrating the Hero's Comet!", Meowth consumed a Spelon Berry meant for Alexa's Noivern, resulting in an oral sensation akin to the burning caused by a Tamato Berry. Alexa subsequently offered one to Noivern, which apparently relished the spicy taste. Furthermore, "The Dream Continues!" depicted her providing Noivern with another Spelon Berry. Ash presented his Pokémon with Spelon Berries as a token in "Under the Pledging Tree!". Jessie incorporated Spelon Berries into her Dendemille Town Showcase act, as seen in "Performing with Fiery Charm!". For the Anistar City Pokémon Showcase featured in "A Performance Pop Quiz!", Spelon Berries were utilized. Anela's market in Hau'oli City, depicted in "One Journey Ends, Another Begins...", included Spelon Berries among its available stock. A meal prepared by Lillie for Pikachu in "The Ol' Raise and Switch!" contained Spelon Berries.

Numerous Spelon Berries were observed in "Adding It Up with Plusle & Minun I", identified as one of several Berry types flourishing on the Abandoned Ship. Ruby attempted to dispatch Mumu to collect some, but during the endeavor, the Mudfish Pokémon encountered and was assaulted by a pair of wild Plusle and Minun residing on the vessel. "Problematic Probopass and Mad Magnezone II" showcased a Spelon Berry amidst a collection of Berries unearthed from Lax's fur by Cyrus's Probopass's Mini-Noses, during an effort to recover Cyrus's camera. Both the visual design and the nomenclature of the Spelon Berry potentially draw inspiration from the horned melon, alternately termed a spiked melon.

Title: Pamtre Berry

The Pamtre Berry, known in Japan as シーヤのみ (Shīya Fruit), made its debut as a Berry item in Generation III. During Generation V gameplay, this Berry could be vended to the gourmet enthusiast located on Route 5 for a sum of $500. Unlike some other items, Pamtre Berries lack direct application; their utility is solely as a component, particularly in crafting Pokéblocks and Poffins. Players in Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum had the option to trade Pamtre Berries for various accessories at the Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop. Within the Pokémon Dream World, players were able to swap Berries for Décor items, with certain Décor specifically necessitating Pamtre Berries. Pokémon Sleep features the Pamtre Berry among the collectible Berries that helper Pokémon can procure to nourish Snorlax. These Berries augment Snorlax's Strength stat; should the Pamtre Berry be Snorlax's preferred kind, this increase is amplified, becoming twice as potent. The fundamental quantity of Strength bestowed by a Pamtre Berry is contingent upon the level of the helper Pokémon responsible for its collection. Only Flying-type helper Pokémon present in a player's active team are capable of gathering Pamtre Berries. Consistent with all other Berries within the game, once collected, Pamtre Berries bypass the player's inventory and are instead promptly consumed by Snorlax. The episode "Is That Really the Best Option?" of Pokémon Concierge showcases Pamtre Berries, where they are transformed into beverages by being sliced open, exposing their internal hollowness. Furthermore, a Pamtre Berry plant is featured in the same installment, visually resembling an actual palm tree from the real world, a depiction divergent from their portrayal in the core series titles. During "Dramatic Drapion & Crafty Kricketune II," Lax hurled a Pamtre Berry, among other Berries, at a pair of Scientists pursuing the protagonists; however, the Scientists' Kricketune and Drapion effortlessly cleaved through each one. The item description for the Pamtre Berry in Pokémon Ruby, Sapphire, and Emerald indicates a belief that it originates from a region distinct from Hoenn. Nevertheless, as of now, naturally appearing Pamtre Berries remain unobtainable across all game iterations. Both the visual design and the appellation of the Pamtre Berry are potentially inspired by a palm nut and a palm tree, in that order.

Title: Watmel Berry

This specific type of fruit, known as a Watmel Berry (カイスのみ Kaisu Fruit in Japanese), first appeared in the third generation of games. Players in the fifth generation titles had the option to sell a Watmel Berry to the gourmet maniac located on Route 5 for a price of $500. These berries lack direct utility and serve exclusively as an ingredient, for instance, when crafting Pokéblocks or Poffins. Within the Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum versions, individuals could trade Watmel Berries for various accessories at the establishment known as the Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop. Accessing the Pokémon Dream World allowed players to trade Berries for various Décor items. Specifically, certain Décor required Watmel Berries for exchange.

During the events of PK23, a Snivy retrieved a Watmel Berry from a Watmel Berry plant, employing it to lure a Snorlax away from a hole where a Rinka Berry had fallen, as the Snorlax had positioned itself directly over the opening. Ash and his companions incorporated Watmel Berries into a feast they prepared at Professor Sycamore's laboratory, as depicted in "Battling With a Clean Slate!". In the episode "One Journey Ends, Another Begins...", Watmel Berries were observed among the selection of Berries available at Anela's market stall in Hau'oli City. Within "Mallow and the Forest Teacher!", Watmel Berries were spotted at the dwelling of a wild Oranguru. In "Sneaky Like Shedinja II," Emerald's Sceptile was shown using Leaf Blade to slice various Berries for its Trainer's consumption, with a Watmel Berry being visible among them. During "Stunning Staravia & Stinky Skuntank I," an Advanced level Grunt's Gible used Dragon Rush to strike Lax, dislodging a Watmel Berry, among others, from its fur. Subsequently, as the Grunt descended from a shelf, he inadvertently stepped on the Watmel Berry, causing him to slip and consequently drop Platinum's Pokédex, which he had been attempting to pilfer. In "Dramatic Drapion & Crafty Kricketune II," Lax hurled several Berries, including a Watmel Berry, at two pursuing Scientists; however, the Scientists' Kricketune and Drapion effortlessly sliced through each projectile. "Lucky Lucario II" depicted a Watmel Berry being part of the assortment of Berries Diamond utilized when preparing meals for himself, his Pokémon, and Riley during their training on Iron Island.

Although its official description asserts it can exceed 20 inches (50 cm) in size, thereby claiming the title of the largest Berry, those acquired by players measure a mere 9.8 inches (25 cm). This places them smaller than numerous other Berries, notably the Belue Berry, which holds the true record as the largest, reaching 11.8 inches (30.0 cm). The visual appearance and appellation of the Watmel Berry likely draw inspiration from a watermelon.

Title: Durin Berry

This Berry, known in Japanese as ドリのみ (Dori Fruit), made its debut in Generation III. Within Generation V titles, players have the option to sell a Durin Berry for $500 to the gourmet connoisseur found on Route 5. Durin Berries are not consumable items themselves; their utility lies solely as components in creations like Pokéblocks and Poffins. Should the Favor Lady at the Lilycove Pokémon Center in Pokémon Emerald request "something pointy," a Durin Berry can be traded for an HP Up. Similarly, an Iron is obtainable by exchanging a Durin Berry if her request is for "something wham-ish." In Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum, the Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop offers accessories in exchange for Durin Berries. The Pokémon Dream World allowed for Berries to be traded for various Décor items. Several Décor pieces specifically mandated Durin Berries for their acquisition.

In Pokémon Sleep, the Durin Berry is among the types of Berries that helper Pokémon collect and offer to Snorlax. These Berries boost Snorlax's Strength, with the effect becoming twice as potent if it aligns with Snorlax's preferred Berry type. The initial Strength increment from a Durin Berry is directly linked to the level of the helper Pokémon responsible for its gathering. Only Grass-type helper Pokémon within a player's active team are capable of gathering Durin Berries. Consistent with all Berries in this game, any Durin Berries collected bypass the player's inventory, instead being promptly consumed by Snorlax.

During the episode "The Brockster Is In!", Dawn discovered a Durin Berry during her search for a Pecha Berry. Subsequently, Brock utilized a combination of Durin Berries and Oran Berries to formulate an energizing beverage for a poisoned Pichu. Ash and his companions incorporated Durin Berries into a celebratory feast prepared at Professor Sycamore's laboratory in "Battling With a Clean Slate!". "First Catch in Alola, Ketchum-Style!" featured the appearance of Durin Berries. These Berries have been observed on numerous occasions within the Berry pile at Bewear's den, notably in "The Sun, the Scare, the Secret Lair!", "Getting to Know You!", "Mimikyu Unmasked!", and "Acting True to Form!". While searching for a Miracle Seed as a dish component in Lush Jungle, Ash and Pikachu uncovered Durin Berries, among other varieties, within a hollow stump during "Currying Favor and Flavor!". Episode SM064 depicted Durin Berries at a nest belonging to a group of wild Passimian. Anela's Berry basket, presented to Ash and his classmates' Pokémon in "Real Life...Inquire Within!", contained Durin Berries. During her assistance at Oranguru's establishment in "A Recipe for Success!", Mallow provided a meal of Durin and Belue Berries to a flock of Fearow. "Chancing Upon Chingling" showed a Durin Berry as one of the Berries utilized by Diamond and Lax to incapacitate a Team Galactic Grunt at the Team Galactic HQ, enabling Diamond to acquire his uniform and assume a disguise. During "The Final Dimensional Duel VII," Jupiter employed a Durin Berry to transform her Tangrowth's Natural Gift attack into a Water-type maneuver during a confrontation with Marley's Arcanine. The visual characteristics and nomenclature of the Durin Berry potentially draw inspiration from the durian fruit.

Title: Belue Berry

Originating in Generation III, the Belue Berry (Japanese: ベリブのみ Beribu Fruit) holds a distinct place among its kind. Within the Generation V titles, players have the option to vend this particular Berry to the gourmet enthusiast situated on Route 5 for a sum of $500. Unlike some other Berries, Belue Berries lack direct application, serving solely as components in crafting items like Pokéblocks and Poffins. Players in Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum had the opportunity to trade Belue Berries for various accessories at the Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop. Within the now-defunct Pokémon Dream World, players were able to swap Berries for Décor, with Belue Berries being a prerequisite for certain decorative items.

In Pokémon Sleep, the Belue Berry functions as a collectible item, gathered by helper Pokémon and subsequently provided to Snorlax. These Berries contribute to Snorlax's Strength, with their impact magnified twofold if the Belue Berry happens to be Snorlax's preferred food. The fundamental Strength increase from each Berry is directly tied to the level of the helper Pokémon responsible for its collection. Specifically, Steel-type helper Pokémon on the player's team are capable of gathering Belue Berries. Consistent with other Berries in the game, any Belue Berries gathered do not enter the player's personal inventory but are instead consumed by Snorlax without delay.

Its inaugural appearance occurred in the episode 'Aipom and Circumstance!', where Hank possessed one in his satchel. Its tart quality proved instrumental in assisting Ash's Aipom with the removal of a lodged crown from her head.
