Belue Berries later featured as an element of the Anistar City Pokémon Showcase in 'A Performance Pop Quiz!'. A lone Belue Berry made an appearance in 'First Catch in Alola, Ketchum-Style!', and a collection of them was observed within Bewear's den during 'Acting True to Form!'. In 'A Recipe for Success!', Mallow discerned that a flock of Fearow showed a fondness for Belue Berries while aiding at Oranguru's establishment, prompting her to subsequently prepare a meal incorporating both Belue and Durin Berries for them. Furthermore, 'A Test in Paradise!' depicted two Dragonite harvesting Belue Berries on Dragonite Island.

Within the pages of 'Lucky Lucario II', Belue Berries were observed among the various Berries utilized by Diamond to prepare sustenance for himself, his Pokémon, and Riley during their training regimen on Iron Island. Tru employed Razor Leaf to finely chop the Berries, which Lax then deposited into a cooking pot.

This particular Berry has also appeared in various official merchandise and figures. Notably, Belue Berries are depicted on Poké Lid #125, situated in Tomiya, Japan. Measuring 11.8 inches (30.0 cm), it holds the distinction of being the most sizable Berry a player can acquire. This is in contrast to Watmel Berries, which, despite being cataloged at 9.8" (25.0 cm) on their labels, have their descriptions noting that specimens growing up to 20" (50 cm) have been discovered.

The etymology of the Belue Berry's name likely derives from the blueberry. Its physical design, however, potentially draws inspiration from Solanum melongena, commonly recognized as eggplant or aubergine, depending on regional nomenclature.

Title: Occa Berry

Hailing from Generation IV, the Occa Berry (known as オッカのみ or Occa Fruit in Japanese) stands as a distinct variety of Berry. Within the Generation V titles, players had the option to sell an Occa Berry to the gourmet maniac found on Route 5 for $20. Should a Pokémon holding this item be struck by a supereffective Fire-type attack, the Berry is consumed, effectively halving the incoming damage. If the holder possesses the Ripen Ability, the damage taken is instead multiplied by 1/4. In the Pokémon Dream World, players once had the opportunity to trade various Berries for Décor, with certain Décor pieces specifically mandating Occa Berries for acquisition.

The Occa Berry also features as a Pokémon Tool card within the Pokémon Trading Card Game, making its debut during the English Scarlet & Violet Series (concurrent with the Japanese Scarlet & Violet Era). Its initial release occurred in the Japanese Stellar Miracle expansion and the English Stellar Crown expansion, showcasing artwork by Studio Bora Inc. This particular Occa Berry card confers a singular, one-time effect: it reduces damage by 60 from an opponent's Fire-type Pokémon's attack on the Pokémon it's attached to. Following the application of its effect, the Occa Berry card is then discarded. Furthermore, the Occa Berry has been depicted in various officially licensed figures and goods. It's speculated that the Occa Berry's visual aesthetic and nomenclature draw inspiration from cacao, particularly the cacao fruit pod.

Title: Passho Berry

The Passho Berry, known in Japanese as イトケのみ (Itoke Fruit), made its debut as a Berry variant in Generation IV. Players in the Generation V titles could offer a Passho Berry to the gourmet enthusiast located on Route 5 for a price of $20. When a Pokémon holding this item is struck by a Water-type move that is supereffective against it, the Passho Berry is consumed, diminishing the incoming attack's damage by half. Should the Pokémon possess the Ripen Ability, the damage reduction is further enhanced, resulting in the attack dealing only 1/4 of its original power. Within the Pokémon Dream World, individuals had the option to trade various Berries for Décor items, with certain Décor explicitly demanding Passho Berries as currency.

Their first anime appearance occurred in the episode "A Battle by Any Other Name!", where Ash, his companions, and Miette gathered these Berries for incorporation into Poké Puffs during the Poké Puff Contest. "To Find a Fairy Flower!" featured a depiction of a Passho Berry within a newspaper that Jessie utilized to conceal herself from Ash and his group. During "The Forest Champion!", an Ursaring was observed pilfering Passho and Liechi Berries from a collection of Pokémon residing in the forest. Ash presented Passho Berries to his Pokémon as tokens of affection in "Under the Pledging Tree!". The Anistar City Pokémon Showcase, depicted in "A Performance Pop Quiz!", incorporated Passho Berries into its presentation. They also made an appearance in "A Team-on-Team Tussle!". A Passimian nest contained a Passho Berry in the unaired episode SM064. Jupiter strategically employed a Passho Berry in "Tackling Tangrowth" to transform her Tangrowth's Natural Gift attack into a Water-type maneuver, leading to a quick victory over Platinum's Rapidash.

This Berry also exists as a Pokémon Tool card within the Pokémon Trading Card Game, making its introduction during the English Scarlet & Violet Series (known as the Scarlet & Violet Era in Japan). Its initial release occurred in the Japanese Paradise Dragona subset and the English Surging Sparks expansion, featuring artwork by Studio Bora Inc. The Passho Berry card provides a single-use benefit: it lessens the damage taken by the attached Pokémon from an opponent's Water-type Pokémon's attack by 60 points. Once this effect has been activated, the Passho Berry card is sent to the discard pile. Its aesthetic and nomenclature potentially derive from the passion fruit.

Title: Wacan Berry

Known in Japanese as ソクノのみ (Sokuno Fruit), the Wacan Berry was introduced as a distinct type of Berry in Generation IV. In the Generation V installments, this Berry could be sold to the gourmet maniac on Route 5 for $20. If a Pokémon holding a Wacan Berry is struck by a supereffective Electric-type attack, the Berry is consumed, and the damage taken is halved. Should the holder also possess the Ripen Ability, the damage is instead multiplied by 1/4. Within the Pokémon Dream World, players had the option to exchange Berries for Décor, with certain Décor items specifically requiring Wacan Berries. In the mobile game Pokémon: Magikarp Jump, Wacan Berries can appear as a food source for Magikarp. The manga chapter "Problematic Probopass and Mad Magnezone II" featured a Wacan Berry among numerous other Berries that Cyrus's Probopass's Mini-Noses unearthed from Lax's fur while attempting to retrieve Cyrus's camera. A subsequent collection of images displays trading cards either named Wacan Berry or showing a Pokémon holding one. The Wacan Berry's visual concept may be rooted in the candle tree, and its name potentially derives from either a candle tree or a wax candle.

Title: Rindo Berry

The term 'Rindo' also serves as a navigational pointer to Florinda Showers, a character known by the Japanese name Rindō. Known in Japanese as リンドのみ (Rindo Fruit), this particular Berry variety made its debut in Generation IV. Players of the Generation V titles had the option to liquidate a Rindo Berry, fetching a price of $20 from the gourmet connoisseur found on Route 5. Should a Pokémon holding a Rindo Berry sustain a supereffective Grass-type assault, the Berry is consumed, effectively reducing the incoming damage by half. Moreover, for a Pokémon endowed with the Ripen Ability, the damage taken is further lessened, being multiplied by 1/4 instead. The Pokémon Dream World offered a mechanism for players to trade Berries for decorative items, with specific Décor requiring the submission of Rindo Berries. Regarding Pokémon: Magikarp Jump, Rindo Berries occasionally manifest as sustenance suitable for consumption by Magikarp. The visual representation of the Rindo Berry potentially draws inspiration from a loment, a tamarind pod, a pea, or a soybean. Its nomenclature, meanwhile, is theorized to derive from the tamarind fruit.

Title: Yache Berry

Introduced as a specific type of Berry in Generation IV, the Yache Berry is known in Japanese as ヤチェのみ (Yache Fruit). In titles from Generation V, this Berry could be sold to the gourmet fanatic located on Route 5 for $20. Should a Pokémon holding a Yache Berry be struck by a supereffective Ice-type attack, the Berry is consumed, effectively halving the incoming damage. For a holder with the Ripen Ability, this damage is instead multiplied by 1/4. In the Pokémon Dream World, players had the option to trade Berries for Décor, with certain Décor items specifically requiring Yache Berries.

Regarding Pokémon Sleep, the Yache Berry is among the Berry varieties that helper Pokémon can gather to feed Snorlax. Consuming Yache Berries boosts Snorlax's Strength, with this effect doubling if the Berry happens to be Snorlax's favorite. The initial Strength value added by the Berry is contingent upon the level of the helper Pokémon responsible for its collection. Only Dragon-type helper Pokémon within the player's team are able to forage for Yache Berries. Consistent with all other Berries in Pokémon Sleep, collected Yache Berries bypass the player's inventory, being instantly consumed by Snorlax. The visual appearance and appellation of the Yache Berry possibly derive from a cherimoya.

Title: Chople Berry

Originating in Generation IV, the Chople Berry (Japanese: ヨプのみ Yop Fruit) is a specific type of Berry. In the Generation V games, this Berry could be exchanged with the gourmet maniac found on Route 5 for $20. Should a Pokémon equipped with a Chople Berry be struck by a supereffective Fighting-type move, the Berry is consumed, and the damage received is subsequently halved. If the holder benefits from the Ripen Ability, the incoming damage is instead multiplied by 1/4. Within the Pokémon Dream World, players were able to trade Berries for various Décor, and certain Décor items specifically required Chople Berries. The Chople Berry is also one of the Berries prominently featured on the Naughty Mimikyu card sleeves. Its design and name are potentially inspired by the wax apple.

Title: Kebia Berry

First appearing in Generation IV, the Kebia Berry (Japanese: ビアーのみ Biah Fruit) is a specific type of Berry. Within the Generation V games, players could exchange this Berry with the gourmet maniac situated on Route 5 for a monetary sum of $20. Its primary battle utility is triggered when a Pokémon holding it is struck by a supereffective Poison-type attack; upon impact, the Kebia Berry is consumed, effectively reducing the incoming damage by half. Should the holder also possess the Ripen ability, the damage taken is further mitigated, being multiplied by 1/4. In the Pokémon Dream World, participants had the option to trade various Berries for Décor. Certain Décor pieces specifically required Kebia Berries for acquisition. The Grumpy Gliscor story arc featured a Kebia Berry among the Berries that Lax launched at a wild Gliscor assailing the S.S. Sinnoh, aiming to divert its attention. Both the design and designation of the Kebia Berry are posited to be inspired by the akebia plant.

Title: Shuca Berry

This particular kind of Berry, identified as シュカのみ Shuca Fruit in Japanese, made its debut in Generation IV. In the Generation V games, players could exchange a Shuca Berry for $20 with the gourmet enthusiast located on Route 5. Should a Pokémon holding a Shuca Berry be struck by a supereffective Ground-type offensive move, the Berry will be consumed, reducing the incoming damage from that attack by half. If the holder possesses the Ripen ability, the damage is instead multiplied by 1/4. In the Pokémon Dream World, participants had the option to trade Berries for Décor; certain Décor selections specifically necessitated Shuca Berries. During "The Guardian's Challenge!", a Shuca Berry was observed as a component of the meal Mallow prepared for Ash and his classmates' Pokémon. The episode "Problematic Probopass and Mad Magnezone II" featured a Shuca Berry among a multitude of Berries unearthed from Lax's fur by Cyrus's Probopass's Mini-Noses while attempting to retrieve Cyrus's camera. Furthermore, a Shuca Berry was included in Professor Juniper's dinner during "Fussing and Fighting." The Shuca Berry's visual appearance and designation are potentially based on a cashew.

Title: Coba Berry

Known in Japanese as バコウのみ (Baco Fruit), the Coba Berry is a variety of Berry first appearing in Generation IV. Within Generation V titles, players have the option to sell a Coba Berry to the gourmet enthusiast situated on Route 5, fetching a price of $20. When a Pokémon holding this item takes a supereffective Flying-type attack, the Coba Berry is consumed, and the incoming damage is reduced by half. Should the Pokémon possess the Ripen Ability, the damage calculation results in a multiplication of 1/4 instead. During the era of the Pokémon Dream World, participants were able to trade various Berries for Décor items; among the Décor options available, some specifically called for Coba Berries. An episode titled The Guardian's Challenge! depicted a Coba Berry included in the meal Mallow prepared for Ash and his classmates' Pokémon companions. Its distinctive appearance and nomenclature are possibly derived from the fruit known as a babaco.

Title: Payapa Berry

This particular type of Berry, known in Japanese as ウタンのみ (Utan Fruit), made its debut in Generation IV. Within the Generation V titles, players had the option to sell a Payapa Berry to the gourmet maniac located on Route 5 for a price of $20. Should a Pokémon holding this item be struck by a supereffective Psychic-type attack, the Payapa Berry will be consumed, effectively halving the damage received from that attack. If the Pokémon additionally possesses the Ripen Ability, this damage reduction is further enhanced, causing the attack to inflict only 1/4 of its original damage.

In the Pokémon Dream World, players were able to trade various Berries for Décor items; Payapa Berries were required for certain Décor. An instance of a Payapa Berry being used was observed in the episode "The Guardian's Challenge!", where it formed part of a meal Mallow prepared for Ash and his classmates' Pokémon. Several Payapa Berries were featured in "I'm a Role Model Now?". Initially collected in a basket, these berries were subsequently levitated into the air due to Haru's Psyduck experiencing a headache, necessitating Haru to catch each one as they fell. In the manga "Rrmbll!! Serious Tower Battle", Henry provided his Kubfu with a Payapa Berry to hold while undertaking the challenge of the Tower of Waters, allowing it to endure a supereffective Zen Headbutt from a Psyduck and subsequently achieve victory.

The Payapa Berry was introduced into the Pokémon Trading Card Game as a Pokémon Tool card during the English Scarlet & Violet Series, corresponding to the Japanese Scarlet & Violet Era. Its initial release occurred in the Japanese Stellar Miracle expansion and the English Stellar Crown expansion, featuring artwork crafted by Studio Bora Inc. The Payapa Berry card carries a single-use effect that reduces by 60 the damage dealt to the attached Pokémon by an opponent's Psychic-type Pokémon's attack. After this effect is applied, the Payapa Berry card is then discarded. While the name "Payapa Berry" in most languages suggests a connection to the papaya fruit, its visual design and its names in Japanese and Chinese indicate a potential inspiration from the calabash instead.

Title: Tanga Berry

The Tanga Berry, identified in Japanese as タンガのみ (Tanga Fruit), debuted as a type of Berry in Generation IV. In the Generation V titles, trainers had the opportunity to sell a Tanga Berry to the gourmet maniac on Route 5 for $20. Should a Pokémon holding this item be hit by a supereffective Bug-type attack, the Tanga Berry is consumed, mitigating the incoming damage by half. However, if the holder possesses the Ripen Ability, the resulting damage is instead multiplied by 1/4. Through the Pokémon Dream World, players once exchanged Berries for Décor pieces, with certain Décor requiring Tanga Berries for acquisition. The Tanga Berry is also among the Berries depicted on the Naughty Mimikyu card sleeves. The Berry's design and appellation likely derive from the pitanga.

Title: Charti Berry
Emerging in Generation IV, the Charti Berry (Japanese: ヨロギのみ Yorogi Fruit) is a specific kind of Berry. Within the Generation V titles, players had the option to sell this Berry to the gourmet enthusiast situated on Route 5, fetching a price of $20. Should a Pokémon holding a Charti Berry take a supereffective hit from a Rock-type move, the Berry is consumed, thereby reducing the incoming damage by half. Furthermore, if the Pokémon possesses the Ripen Ability, this damage reduction is even greater, resulting in the damage being multiplied by 1/4 instead. The Pokémon Dream World allowed trainers to swap various Berries for Décor items; among these, particular Décor items necessitated the use of Charti Berries. The Charti Berry made an appearance in Grumpy Gliscor, where it was among the Berries utilized by Lax to distract a wild Gliscor that was assaulting the S.S. Sinnoh. Its name and visual appearance potentially draw inspiration from a Chinese artichoke.

Title: Kasib Berry

In games from Generation V, players could sell this Berry to the gourmet maniac found on Route 5 for $20. When a Pokémon holding a Kasib Berry is struck by a supereffective Ghost-type attack, the Berry is consumed, and the incoming damage is halved. A subsequent gallery displays various cards from the Pokémon Trading Card Game that either mention or depict the Kasib Berry. Title: Haban Berry

The Haban Berry (Japanese: ハバンのみ Haban Fruit) entered the world of Pokémon as a Berry variety in Generation IV. Within the Generation V games, this Berry can be sold to the gourmet enthusiast on Route 5 for a price of $20. If a Pokémon holding a Haban Berry is struck by a supereffective Dragon-type attack, the Berry is consumed, and the damage received from that attack is halved. For a holder with the Ripen Ability, the damage taken is further reduced, being multiplied by 1/4. In the Pokémon Dream World, participants could trade Berries for various Décor items. Specific Décor pieces were obtainable by exchanging Haban Berries.

Haban Berries made their prominent debut in the episode "Tasting the Bitter with the Sweet!". During their childhood, Mallow and Lana wandered lost in a forest until they were found by a Drampa known as Grandpa Forest, who provided them with Haban Berries to eat. In the present day, Harper and Sarah similarly encountered Drampa and subsequently brought Haban Berries home for Lana to see, prompting Mallow and Lana to recall their earlier experience. Ash, Mallow, and Lana later sought out Drampa to express their gratitude, eventually discovering it near a Haban tree. After dealing with Team Rocket's attempt to capture Drampa, the group consumed Haban Berries directly from the tree, despite their awareness of the fruit's bittersweet taste. In "Why Not Give Me a Z-Ring Sometime?", a Drampa on Ula'ula Island was observed collecting Haban Berries, only for them to be stolen by the item-pilfering Gengar called the Greedy Rapooh. Within "A Recipe for Success!", Mallow prepared Haban Berry juice for Grandpa Forest when it paid a visit to Oranguru's establishment. During "Grumpy Gliscor", Lax utilized a Haban Berry among other Berries, throwing it at a wild Gliscor that was attacking the S.S. Sinnoh, as a means to distract it.

Main article: Haban Berry (Prismatic Evolutions 111)
The Haban Berry was introduced as a Pokémon Tool card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game during the English Scarlet & Violet Series (which corresponds to the Japanese Scarlet & Violet Era). Its initial release occurred in the Japanese Terastal Fest ex subset and the English Prismatic Evolutions special expansion, featuring illustrations crafted by Studio Bora Inc. This Haban Berry card possesses a single-use effect that reduces attack damage dealt by an opponent's Dragon-type Pokémon to the attached Pokémon by 60. Once its effect is applied, the Haban Berry is discarded.

Haban Berries, along with Sitrus Berries and Magost Berries, are found growing on the tree situated atop the colossal belly of Gigantamax Snorlax. The visual design and name of the Haban Berry may draw inspiration from the strawberry guava fruit.

Title: Colbur Berry

The Colbur Berry, known in Japanese as ナモのみ (Namo Fruit), made its debut as a Berry type starting with Generation IV. Within the Generation V titles, players had the option to sell a Colbur Berry to the gourmet enthusiast located on Route 5, fetching a price of $20. Should a Pokémon holding this item suffer a supereffective Dark-type attack, the Colbur Berry will be consumed, effectively reducing the incoming damage by half. When a Pokémon with the Ripen Ability is the holder, the damage reduction becomes even greater, resulting in the damage being multiplied by 1/4. During the era of the Pokémon Dream World, players were able to trade Berries in exchange for various Décor items. Certain Décor items specifically demanded Colbur Berries for acquisition. The Colbur Berry also appears in the Pokémon Trading Card Game, designated as a Pokémon Tool card, first appearing in the English Scarlet & Violet Series (known as the Scarlet & Violet Era in Japan). Its initial release occurred in the Japanese Super Electric Breaker expansion, followed by its English counterpart, the Surging Sparks expansion, featuring artwork meticulously crafted by Studio Bora Inc. This particular Colbur Berry card confers a singular benefit: it decreases the damage dealt by an opponent's Darkness-type Pokémon's attack to the attached Pokémon by 60. Once its protective effect has been utilized, the Colbur Berry card must then be discarded. Both the visual representation and the nomenclature of the Colbur Berry are potentially inspired by the plant known as a cocklebur.

Title: Babiri Berry

The Babiri Berry (Japanese: リリバのみ Ririba Fruit) first appeared as a specific kind of Berry in Generation IV. In the Generation V video games, trainers could sell the Babiri Berry to the gourmet maniac situated on Route 5 for a price of $20. Its primary function is to protect a Pokémon holding it: when struck by a supereffective Steel-type attack, the Babiri Berry is used up, and the damage received from that attack is reduced by half. If the Pokémon possessing the Berry also has the Ripen Ability, this damage is instead multiplied by 1/4. In the now-defunct Pokémon Dream World, players had the opportunity to trade Berries for Décor; certain Décor items specifically necessitated Babiri Berries. A notable instance of its use occurred in the manga chapter *Dealing with Dragonite*, where Platinum's Froslass held a Babiri Berry, allowing it to endure a Steel Wing attack from Argenta's Dragonite before subsequently defeating it with Ice Shard. In the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Babiri Berry is brought forth as a Pokémon Tool card within the English Scarlet & Violet Series (known as the Japanese Scarlet & Violet Era). It initially launched in the Japanese Super Electric Breaker expansion and its English counterpart, the Surging Sparks expansion, with its visual design crafted by Studio Bora Inc. The Babiri Berry card provides a singular effect: it decreases damage from an opponent's (Metal-type) Pokémon's attack dealt to the attached Pokémon by 60. Following the application of this effect, the Babiri Berry card is then discarded. Furthermore, the Babiri Berry is among the various Berries illustrated on the Naughty Mimikyu card sleeves. The nomenclature and appearance of the Babiri Berry might derive from the biriba fruit.

Title: Chilan Berry

For the e-Reader-exclusive Berry found in Pokémon Ruby and Sapphire, consult the entry for Chilan Berry (Generation III). The Chilan Berry (Japanese: ホズのみ Hozu Fruit) is a specific kind of Berry first appearing in Generation IV. Within the Generation V titles, this Berry can be vended to the gourmet enthusiast situated on Route 5 for a sum of $20. When a Pokémon holding a Chilan Berry sustains damage from a Normal-type offensive move, the Berry is consumed, and the incoming damage from that attack is halved. Should the Pokémon possess the Ripen Ability, the damage reduction is amplified, resulting in the attack's power being quartered (multiplied by 1/4). During Generation IV gameplay, the Berry's effect would additionally trigger for the move Struggle, and also when a Pokémon damaged itself through confusion, notwithstanding that these instances dealt typeless damage. However, starting from Generation V, the Chilan Berry's activation no longer applies to Struggle. Within the Pokémon Dream World, players had the option to trade Berries for various Décor items, and certain Décor pieces specifically mandated the acquisition of Chilan Berries. An appearance of a Chilan Berry occurred in 'Problematic Probopass and Mad Magnezone II,' where it was observed among numerous Berries excavated from Lax's fur by the Mini-Noses of Cyrus's Probopass, during an attempt to recover Cyrus's camera. Distinct from other Berries that mitigate damage, the Chilan Berry reduces the power of Normal-type attacks (excluding those that inflict direct damage) irrespective of type effectiveness, rather than exclusively targeting super effective damage. This particular characteristic is likely due to Normal-type moves typically being unable to achieve super effective status. It is important to note that the Chilan Berry does not diminish the effectiveness of the move Present if that move results in healing the target. Uniquely among Berries, holding a Chilan Berry causes the move Natural Gift to become Normal-type. An entirely separate Berry featured in Pokémon Ruby and Sapphire shares this exact name. Furthermore, it stands as the sole Berry whose appellation has been repurposed for another. However, this Generation III iteration of the Berry is tied exclusively to its corresponding e-Reader card, rendering it unobtainable in Generation IV titles, and it will transform into an Enigma Berry if transferred between games. When procuring Chilan Berries via Join Avenue, both the English and German descriptive texts contain an inaccuracy, claiming they "weaken a supereffective Normal-type attack," akin to other Berries that reduce damage. This particular misstatement is absent from the original Japanese text, as well as descriptions in other languages. The visual aesthetic and nomenclature of the Chilan Berry potentially draw inspiration from a Chinese lantern.

Title: Liechi Berry

Known in Japan as チイラのみ Chyla Fruit, the Liechi Berry is a specific type of Berry first introduced in Generation III. In Generation V games, it fetches $20 when sold to the gourmet maniac on Route 5. This Berry grants the holder a one-stage Attack boost when their HP falls to ¼ or below. However, if the holder possesses Gluttony, consumption occurs earlier, at ½ HP or below. With the Ripen Ability, the Attack increase becomes two stages. If another Pokémon forces its consumption via moves such as Fling, Pluck, Bug Bite, Teatime, or Item Urge, the consumer still gains a one-stage Attack rise.

"Who, What, When, Where, Wynaut?" depicted Liechi Berries as the primary food source for Mirage Island's Wynaut; May received a basket of these rare Berries as a parting gift. "The Forest Champion!" saw an Ursaring steal Liechi and Passho Berries from forest Pokémon. Ash gifted Liechi Berries to his Pokémon in "Under the Pledging Tree!". They were part of the Anistar City Pokémon Showcase in "A Performance Pop Quiz!". The movie "Volcanion and the Mechanical Marvel" showed Liechi Berries at the Nebel Plateau. Liechi Berries also appeared in "First Catch in Alola, Ketchum-Style!" and "A Team-on-Team Tussle!". They frequently showed up in Bewear's den's Berry pile, specifically in "The Sun, the Scare, the Secret Lair!", "Getting to Know You!", "Mimikyu Unmasked!", and "Acting True to Form!". In "Currying Favor and Flavor!", Ash and Pikachu discovered Liechi Berries, among other types, within a hollowed-out Lush Jungle stump while seeking a Miracle Seed for a dish. "With a Spoink in Your Step I" briefly featured a Liechi Berry plant on Mirage Island, where Plusle and Minun hid. A Liechi Berry concluded Professor Juniper's dinner in "Fussing and Fighting". Its design and name are potentially inspired by the lychee fruit.

Title: Ganlon Berry
The Ganlon Berry, known in Japanese as リュガのみ (Ryuga Fruit), made its debut as a Berry type in Generation III. During the Generation V games, this item can be sold for $20 to the gourmet maniac situated on Route 5. When held by a Pokémon, the Ganlon Berry activates to boost its Defense stat by one stage once its HP drops to ¼ or less of its maximum. However, a Pokémon with the Gluttony Ability will eat its Ganlon Berry at an earlier point, specifically when its HP reaches ½ or below. Furthermore, a holder with the Ripen Ability experiences an enhanced effect, causing its Defense to ascend by two stages. If the Ganlon Berry is consumed against its holder's will via moves such as Fling, Pluck, Bug Bite, Teatime, or Item Urge, the Pokémon performing the consumption receives a one-stage increase to its Defense.

Title: Salac Berry

Hailing from Generation III, the Salac Berry (Japanese: カムラのみ Camula Fruit) is a specific variety of Berry. In the Generation V installments of the main series games, players can sell this item to the gourmet maniac located on Route 5 for a payout of $20. The principal effect of a Salac Berry in battle is to elevate the holder's Speed by a single stage once their HP drops to ¼ or less of its total. If the Pokémon holding it possesses the Gluttony Ability, it will consume the Berry earlier, specifically when its HP falls to ½ or below. Furthermore, a Pokémon equipped with the Ripen Ability gains a more substantial advantage, experiencing a two-stage increase in its Speed. Should the Berry be forcibly consumed by an opponent via moves such as Fling, Pluck, or Bug Bite, or abilities like Teatime or Item Urge, the consumer's Speed stat will be raised by one stage.

Within Pokémon UNITE, Salac Berries can be found on every playable stage with the sole exception of Shivre City. These Berries generally manifest in locations closer to the map's central areas rather than the individual team bases. Eating a Salac Berry in this game grants a temporary boost to the player's Pokémon's movement speed. After being used, Salac Berries will respawn once a predetermined period of time has passed.

In the Pokémon Adventures manga, the Salac Berry has played a role in key moments. In "Once More into the Unown," Blue used a Salac Berry on his Porygon2, enabling it to quickly infiltrate R's systems and liberate his grandfather and Green's parents. The Berry's effect was subsequently refreshed when Porygon2 employed the move Recycle. Later, in "Going Ashore and Neighboring Akala Island," Moon utilized a Salac Berry on her Rowlet to enhance its speed, which facilitated its escape from Team Skull. The name "Salac Berry" may be derived from the salak fruit, and its design could potentially be inspired by a cactus.

Title: Petaya Berry

Originating in Generation III, the Petaya Berry (known as ヤタピのみ Yatapi Fruit in Japanese) is a distinct variety of Berry. In the Generation V titles, players are able to sell this particular Berry to the gourmet enthusiast located on Route 5 for $20. A Pokémon holding a Petaya Berry will have its Special Attack stat boosted by one stage when its HP falls to ¼ or less. If the Pokémon possessing it has the Gluttony ability, it will consume its Petaya Berry at ½ HP or below. Furthermore, if the holder has the Ripen ability, the Special Attack increase becomes two stages. Should the Petaya Berry be forcibly eaten by another Pokémon via Fling, Pluck, Bug Bite, Teatime, or Item Urge, the consumer's Special Attack will ascend by one stage. The Petaya Berry's name and physical appearance are likely inspired by the pitaya fruit.

Title: Apicot Berry

The Apicot Berry, identified in Japanese as ズアのみ (Zua Fruit), was introduced as a new kind of Berry in Generation III. In the Generation V installments of the games, trainers have the option to exchange an Apicot Berry for $20 by selling it to the gourmet maniac located on Route 5. Under typical conditions, this Berry will augment the holder's Special Defense by one stage once their HP drops to ¼ or less. However, if the Pokémon possesses the Gluttony Ability, it will consume the Apicot Berry earlier, specifically when its HP reaches ½ or less. Should the holder possess the Ripen Ability, the Special Defense boost is enhanced, increasing by two stages instead. Furthermore, when forcibly eaten by moves such as Fling, Pluck, Bug Bite, Teatime, or Item Urge, the consuming Pokémon's Special Defense receives a one-stage increase. The Apicot Berry's nomenclature and visual design are likely derived from the apricot fruit.

Title: Lansat Berry

Originating in Generation III, the Lansat Berry (Japanese: サンのみ Sang Fruit) constitutes a distinct variety of Berry. In the Generation V games, this Berry commands a price of $30,000 when sold to the gourmet maniac on Route 5. When held by a Pokémon, a Lansat Berry elevates its critical-hit ratio by two stages once the holder's HP drops to ¼ or below. However, for a holder with the Gluttony ability, the Berry is consumed when its HP falls to ½ or below. The Ripen ability does not influence the Lansat Berry's effect. If consumed forcibly via moves like Fling, Pluck, Bug Bite, or Item Urge, it bestows a two-stage critical-hit ratio increase upon the consumer. In the episode Treasure Hunt, Akala Style!, Mallow uncovered a Lansat Berry during a Stoutland Search treasure hunt organized by Olivia for Ash and his classmates. This specific Berry was noted for being larger than typical Lansat Berries. A holographic screen also displayed two Lansat Berries during Sophocles's inaugural treasure hunt. The Lansat Berry's appellation may derive from a langsat, and its visual design might be inspired by the Sun.

Title: Starf Berry

Hailing from Generation III, the Starf Berry (スターのみ Star Fruit in Japanese) is a distinctive kind of Berry. Within the Generation V titles, players have the option to sell a Starf Berry to the gourmet maniac found on Route 5, fetching a price of $30,000. Its primary effect involves a random stat boost: when held by a Pokémon whose HP falls to ¼ or less, the Starf Berry will augment one of its Attack, Defense, Special Attack, Special Defense, or Speed stats by two stages. For a Pokémon possessing the Gluttony ability, the Berry is consumed earlier, specifically when its HP reaches ½ or lower. Furthermore, if the Pokémon holds the Ripen ability, the chosen stat receives an even greater boost, increasing by four stages. Should the Starf Berry be forcefully consumed through moves such as Fling, Pluck, Bug Bite, or the Ability Item Urge, the consuming Pokémon will likewise see one of its Attack, Defense, Special Attack, Special Defense, or Speed stats randomly elevated by two stages. Regarding its aesthetics, the Starf Berry's star points in Generation III corresponded to the five distinct flavors: red for spicy, blue for dry, pink for sweet, green for bitter, and yellow for sour. Curiously, the pink hue was altered to orange starting from Generation IV, a change for which the reasons remain unstated. Its design and nomenclature are thought to draw inspiration from the real-world starfruit.

Title: Enigma Berry

Introduced in Generation III, the Enigma Berry (Japanese: ナゾのみ Nazo Fruit) is a distinctive type of Berry. Its initial appearance in Generation III designated it as one of a select group of Berries exclusively procurable by scanning Pokémon Battle e cards using the e-Reader peripheral. By Generation IV, its classification shifted to that of a conventional Berry, though its scarce availability persisted. Further on, in Generation V, players might occasionally encounter it as an item held by an NPC partner within the Battle Subway. Within the Generation V titles, this Berry also fetches a significant price, commanding $30,000 when vended to the gourmet enthusiast located on Route 5.

During Generation III gameplay, the moniker "Enigma Berry" served as a blanket term encompassing all Berries acquirable in Pokémon Ruby and Sapphire by interacting with Pokémon Battle e Berry cards via the e-Reader. This collection comprised the Pumkin, Drash, Eggant, Strib, Chilan, and Nutpea Berries from Pokémon Battle e: Series 1. Additionally, Pokémon Battle e: Series 2 introduced the Ginema, Kuo, Yago, Touga, Niniku, and Topo Berries, which were exclusive to Japan.

Upon the initial scan of an Enigma Berry card, players are granted a single Berry by Norman at the Petalburg Gym. This acquisition process is repeatable, provided an Enigma Berry card is rescanned and the player possesses no e-Reader Berries in their Bag or PC storage when engaging in conversation with Norman. Should a different card be scanned, every existing instance of that specific Berry within the game – whether in the Bag, the PC, held by a Pokémon, or cultivated in soft soil – undergoes a complete transformation into the properties of the newly scanned Berry. Furthermore, Berry plants already in existence but not yet bearing fruit will adapt to the growth rate and anticipated yield of the new Berry upon progression to their subsequent growth stage.

A Pokémon retaining this item when transferred via Pal Park to Generation IV titles will find its held Berry converting into an Enigma Berry, irrespective of the original card utilized for its acquisition. Typically, the Berry will bear the name designated by the Enigma Berry card itself, for example, when viewed in the Bag or during Berry Blending activities. Nevertheless, specific scenarios exist where its nomenclature reverts to "Enigma Berry": primarily when a held Berry is visible within the PKMN Data interface of the Pokémon Storage System, and also when an opposing player's Pokémon holds or consumes it within the Pokémon Cable Club Colosseum.

An unintended oversight in the Japanese edition of Pokémon Box Ruby & Sapphire permitted the deposit of Pokémon carrying these Berries into the Box system and their subsequent withdrawal to a different game cartridge. Should the recipient game have no prior record of scanning an Enigma Berry card, it would default to placeholder data for the Enigma Berry—data that remains otherwise unutilized in standard gameplay. This particular issue was rectified in the Western localized versions of Pokémon Box. Within the Generation III titles, an Enigma Berry resists attempts at theft by moves like Thief or Covet, and cannot be swapped via Trick. Furthermore, a player will be barred from entry into the Pokémon Cable Club Trade Center, the Pokémon Wireless Club Union Room, or the Pokémon Wireless Club Direct Corner Trade Center if any Pokémon in their active party holds this particular Berry. Similarly, a Pokémon equipped with this Berry cannot be exchanged with Pokémon Colosseum or Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, nor can it be archived within the Western releases of Pokémon Box Ruby & Sapphire.

Functionally, the Enigma Berry replenishes one-quarter (¼) of the holder's maximum HP whenever the Pokémon takes damage from a super effective attack. An identical restoration of one-quarter (¼) of the holder's maximum HP occurs if the Berry is forcefully consumed through moves such as Fling, Pluck, Bug Bite, or Item Urge. The original source text would typically continue with a compilation of cards explicitly named or featuring an Enigma Berry. Its nomenclature may draw inspiration from the concept of an "enigma," while its visual representation might be derived from either a question mark or a sunflower seed.

Title: Micle Berry

A specific type of Berry, the Micle Berry (identified in Japanese as ミクルのみ Micle Fruit) first appeared in Generation IV. In the Generation V titles, Trainers have the option to exchange a Micle Berry for $30,000 by selling it to the gourmet maniac found on Route 5. This Berry enhances the accuracy of the holder's subsequent move by a factor of 1.2 (approximated as 4915/4096 from Generation V forward) when the Pokémon's HP falls to ¼ or less. If the Pokémon carries the Gluttony Ability, it will consume its Micle Berry earlier, specifically when its HP drops to ½ or below. The Micle Berry played a role in A Heated Battle Begins!!, where Yū equipped his Shaymin with one during a fight against his rival, Shin, enabling Shaymin to deliver a precise Leaf Storm to Shin's Empoleon, resulting in its fainting and Yū's triumph. Both the design and nomenclature of the Micle Berry are believed to be based on the Miracle fruit, with which it shares the property of making other foods taste sweeter.

Title: Custap Berry

The Custap Berry, known in Japanese as イバンのみ (Iban Fruit), debuted as a type of Berry starting in Generation IV. Within the Generation V titles, players are able to sell a Custap Berry for $30,000 to the gourmet maniac located on Route 5. When its HP falls below ¼, a Custap Berry bestows upon its holder the ability to move first within its priority bracket. Pokémon with the Gluttony ability, however, will consume their Custap Berry earlier, once their HP drops to ½ or below. Upon reaching the requisite HP, the holder will immediately consume the Custap Berry at the very onset of a turn, prior to any Pokémon being recalled. This specific timing means that a Pokémon holding a Custap Berry, if under the influence of Unnerve, cannot consume it until the turn *following* the recall of all opposing Pokémon with Unnerve; conversely, it is able to be eaten on the turn an opposing Pokémon with Unnerve enters the battle. Its name and visual characteristics are believed to draw inspiration from the custard apple fruit.

Title: Jaboca Berry

Known in Japanese as ジャポのみ (Japo Fruit), the Jaboca Berry emerged as a new kind of Berry in Generation IV. Within the Generation V series of games, trainers have the option to exchange a Jaboca Berry for a substantial sum of $30,000 with the gourmet enthusiast found on Route 5. Should a Pokémon equipped with a Jaboca Berry take damage from a physical offensive maneuver, the attacking Pokémon suffers a recoil effect, losing 1/8 of its total HP. This effect is amplified if the Berry's holder possesses the Ability Ripen, causing the assailant to forfeit 1/4 of its maximum HP. An appearance by a Jaboca Berry was noted in the episode The Guardian's Challenge!, where it featured prominently in a meal prepared by Mallow for the Pokémon belonging to Ash and his fellow students. A possible inspiration for the Jaboca Berry's visual aesthetic is thought to be a cluster of grapes. Additionally, its nomenclature potentially draws from the jabuticaba fruit.

Title: Rowap Berry

The Rowap Berry, known in Japanese as レンブのみ (Renbu Fruit), first appeared as a new Berry variant in Generation IV. Should players acquire a Rowap Berry in the Generation V titles, it commands a price of $30,000 when sold to the gourmet enthusiast located on Route 5. Should a Pokémon be holding this item when it's struck by a special attack, the offending attacker will suffer damage equivalent to 1/8 of its total HP. However, if the Pokémon clutching the Berry possesses the Ripen Ability, the damage dealt to the attacker is doubled, becoming 1/4 of their maximum HP. Visually, the Rowap Berry's appearance might draw inspiration from a berry of the Syzygium genus. Furthermore, its nomenclature possibly derives from either a rose apple or a water apple.

Title: Bright Powder

Introduced in Generation II, the Bright Powder (Japanese: ひかりのこな Light Powder) is a held item designed to reduce an opponent's accuracy. Before Generation VI, its name was styled as BrightPowder. When held, it historically caused moves aimed at its bearer to have an additional 20/256 (approximately 8%) chance to miss, a mechanic that also applied to one-hit knockout moves. Its current effect reduces the accuracy of moves targeting the holder by 10% of their normal effectiveness. Importantly, Bright Powder no longer affects one-hit knockout moves.

A noteworthy appearance occurs in Pokémon Colosseum: during the cutscene battle at the Relic Stone between Eagun and Cipher Peon Skrub, Skrub's Shadow Hitmontop is depicted holding a Bright Powder. However, when the player subsequently battles Skrub and can snag Hitmontop, it is instead equipped with a Black Belt.

In Pokémon Diamond and Pearl, the item's name was rendered with a lowercase 'p' in plural form ("Brightpowders") and when prefixed by the indefinite article ("a Brightpowder"). These textual inconsistencies were corrected in Pokémon Platinum and Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, where both instances correctly displayed with a capital 'P' ("BrightPowders" and "a BrightPowder"), aligning with the item's standard capitalization.

Generation II – III – IV – V – VI – VII – VIII – IX

Title: White Herb

Known in Japanese as しろいハーブ (White Herb), this held item first made its debut in Generation III. It completely restores any of the holder's stats that have been lowered. If the stat stages of a Pokémon carrying it become negative, the White Herb is consumed, and all such negative stat stages are reset to neutral. Should a multistrike move activate Tangling Hair, Gooey, or Weak Armor, the Pokémon's White Herb will not be used until the very final strike has concluded. Furthermore, if a Pokémon holding a White Herb triggers Tangling Hair or Gooey from several opponents, the item will not be expended until every instance of those Abilities has fully activated for that specific attack.

A Pokémon with Weak Armor, if struck by Knock Off, Thief, Covet, or a physical attack from a Pokémon with the Magician Ability, will have its held White Herb removed or stolen before it gets an opportunity to activate. However, if a Pokémon with Weak Armor is hit by Fling when a White Herb is the flung item, the White Herb's effect will apply before Weak Armor causes a reduction in its Defense. When a Pokémon uses a contact move that lowers its own stats (such as Superpower) against a Pokémon with Pickpocket, its held White Herb will be pilfered before it has a chance to function.

For a Pokémon holding a White Herb that possesses either Defiant or Competitive, these Abilities will activate first, granting the user the Attack or Special Attack boost, and *then* the White Herb will restore the lowered stat. Nevertheless, if the stat that was enhanced by the Ability is the exact same stat that was diminished, the White Herb will never get a chance to activate. If a Pokémon with Contrary has one of its stats decreased by an O-Power, its held White Herb will not activate until after a Pokémon has subsequently used a move. In Crisis at Ferroseed Research!, Professor Malveaux instructed Case to formulate a treatment for Pikachu that included White Herb extract.

Title: Macho Brace

Debuting as a held item in Generation III, the Macho Brace (Japanese: きょうせいギプス, literally "Coercive Cast") provides a specific utility. When a Pokémon carries this item during a battle, all effort values it accumulates are effectively doubled. This benefit stacks additively with Pokérus, but importantly, it does not apply to effort values gained through the use of vitamins or feathers. A significant trade-off for equipping this item is that the holder's Speed stat is reduced by half throughout the duration of any battle. It is worth noting that the Macho Brace is not available for acquisition in Generation VII or in the more recent Pokémon Scarlet and Violet titles.

Beyond the games, the Macho Brace has made appearances in other media. The Pokémon Company International features the Macho Brace alongside Machamp in its official Pokémon Held Item Pins product line. In the anime episode "Under the Pledging Tree!", a vendor in Coumarine City offered a collection of Macho Braces to Ash when he was pondering gift ideas for an upcoming festival. An earlier episode, "The Path to the Pokémon League," depicted A.J.'s Pokémon using an item named a "Strength Intensifier," which bore a distinct visual resemblance to the Macho Brace.

Title: Quick Claw

The Quick Claw, a held item initially introduced in Generation II, serves to enhance the probability of the Pokémon equipping it acting first. Its Japanese appellation is せんせいのツメ, which translates to Head-Start Claw. This item provides its bearer with an approximate 23% (specifically, 60 out of 256) chance of commencing action within its designated priority bracket. Furthermore, it offers a 20% likelihood of moving first in its priority bracket, a condition that remains unaffected by phenomena such as Trick Room or Stall. Should a Quick Claw be acquired, lost, or suppressed during a turn, its influence on turn order will not take effect until the start of the following turn. From Generation IV onwards, Quick Claw's activation triggers an animation at the beginning of the turn. Moreover, starting with Generation V, the message confirming Quick Claw's activation is displayed prior to a player's ability to flee from battle or forfeit. In the narrative 'Interesting Interactions Involving Illumise', a Quick Claw was documented as the held item of a rental Skarmory that Emerald utilized at the Battle Factory. Additionally, in 'A Cheeky Charizard Change-Up I', Emerald's Metagross, on loan from Crystal, was equipped with a Quick Claw for its confrontation with Tucker. This allowed it to achieve a faster speed than Tucker's Charizard and subsequently confine it with a supereffective Rock Tomb. However, this proved insufficient to knock out the Flame Pokémon, and Charizard ultimately defeated Metagross using Overheat. The alternative reading of Quick Claw's Japanese name, "Teacher's Claw," clarifies why it is bestowed as a gift by the instructor at the Pokémon Trainers' School in Hoenn. This item has been present across Generations II, III, IV, V, VI, VII, VIII, and IX.

Title: Soothe Bell

The Soothe Bell (Japanese: やすらぎのすず Peace Bell), a type of held item, debuted in Generation III. Its primary function is to enhance the growth of the holder's friendship. When a Pokémon holds this item, its friendship gains are magnified by 50%. Its effect specifically boosts only the *foundational* increase in a Pokémon's friendship, and does not multiply any additional gains from a Luxury Ball or the location where the Pokémon was met. Although it provides a 50% increase to this original friendship boost, this percentage is added *after* any effects from the Luxury Ball and met location have been calculated and applied.

In Pokémon: Magikarp Jump, the Soothe Bell functions as a Friendship Item, available for purchase at a cost of 500. It notably summons Eevee to serve as a helper Pokémon.

The Soothe Bell made its initial anime appearance in "Showdown At Linoone," depicted as one of the objects Tokin had collected. Later, a pair of Soothe Bells was featured in "Tag! We're It...!" as the designated prize for the winners of the Hearthome City Tag Battle Competition, with one bell awarded to each triumphant Trainer. In the episode "Smells Like Team Spirit!", the competition concluded with Ash and Paul securing victory, earning each of them a Soothe Bell. However, Paul opted not to keep his, instead giving it to Ash. Ash's collection of Soothe Bells briefly reappeared at the beginning of "In The Shadow of Zekrom!", visible on his desk alongside numerous other gifts and awards from his journey through the Sinnoh region. Virgil utilized a Soothe Bell in "Lost at the League!" to bring calm to a Garbodor and a group of Trubbish. In "Suffering Psyduck," one of Mr. Backlot's Maids presented Platinum with a Soothe Bell, expressing gratitude for her visit to the mansion. An illustration of a Soothe Bell is also present in the artwork for Pikachu (BW-P Promo 120). Beyond the games and anime, the Soothe Bell has been depicted in various officially licensed figures and merchandise.

Title: Mental Herb

The Mental Herb (Japanese: メンタルハーブ Mental Herb) is a valuable held item that first appeared in Generation III. Its primary function is to free the Pokémon holding it from infatuation. Starting from Generation V, its utility expanded to cure various other mental status conditions.

When a Pokémon is afflicted by infatuation, the Mental Herb it holds is consumed, immediately resolving the infatuation. If, however, a Pokémon possessing the Ability Oblivious is *solely* infatuated while holding a Mental Herb, Oblivious will activate and remove the infatuation, thus preserving the Mental Herb from consumption.

From Generation V onwards, if the holder becomes affected by infatuation or any of the following disruptive effects—Taunt, Encore, Torment, Heal Block, or Disable (including Disable inflicted by Cursed Body)—the Mental Herb is consumed, and all such negative conditions are simultaneously removed. A specific timing nuance exists for Pokémon with Oblivious: if such a Pokémon holding a Mental Herb is affected by Taunt or becomes infatuated (or both), the Mental Herb will be consumed to cure these conditions *before* Oblivious has a chance to activate and cure infatuation or Taunt.

Furthermore, should a Pokémon equipped with a Mental Herb trigger the Cursed Body or Cute Charm Abilities of multiple opposing Pokémon with a single attack, the Mental Herb will be expended right after the *first* Ability successfully activates. Consequently, by the time any subsequent Abilities would attempt to take effect, the Pokémon will no longer be holding the Mental Herb.

Title: Choice Band

Known in Japan as こだわりハチマキ (Fixation Headband), the Choice Band is a held item that first appeared in Generation III. Its primary function is to enhance the holder's Attack stat, but this comes with the significant drawback of restricting the Pokémon to using only a single move. It forms part of a trio of "Choice" items, with its counterparts, Choice Scarf and Choice Specs, offering similar boosts to other statistics.

Specifically, when a Pokémon holds the Choice Band, its Attack is amplified by 50%. However, this power increase forces the Pokémon to repeatedly execute the very first move chosen upon entering battle or after being switched in. This specific effect is then reset the moment the Pokémon is withdrawn. The Choice Band's Attack amplification initially also extended to the damage incurred from confusion.

A peculiar glitch was present in Pokémon Diamond and Pearl: if a Pokémon executed U-turn and then switched into another Pokémon holding a Choice Band that also knew U-turn, the newly sent-out Pokémon would become locked into using U-turn. This particular issue was rectified in all subsequent game releases. From Generation V onwards, another scenario could lead to a glitch: if a Pokémon was already locked into a move due to a held Choice Band, and the item was removed while its restrictive effect was temporarily negated, the Pokémon would then be affected by the "Choice item lock glitch." However, the Choice Band's power bonus no longer extends to confusion damage.

Should a Pokémon equipped with a Choice Band attempt to use a move other than the one it's locked into (such as through external commands like Instruct or Dancer), that action will fail, and no Power Points (PP) will be consumed. When a Pokémon holding a Choice Band undergoes Dynamax, the item's effects are temporarily nullified; both the stat increase is disabled, and the Pokémon is no longer subject to move restrictions. Upon the conclusion of Dynamax, the stat boost is restored, and any previous move lock is re-enabled.

In the Pokémon Adventures manga, specifically the chapter "Pinsir Me, I Must Be Dreaming," Emerald rented a Linoone from the Battle Factory that was holding a Choice Band. This item was crucial for strengthening its Frustration during several matches. The Choice Band later proved instrumental in defeating Noland's Mawile, as Linoone utilized Trick to swap items, thereby trapping Mawile into repeatedly using Iron Defense. Furthermore, in "Deduction Time," Hugh's Pidove used Bestow to transfer a Choice Band to a Team Plasma Grunt's Klang, which subsequently became locked into using Protect, rendering it unable to attack.

The Choice Band has also been featured in the Pokémon Trading Card Game, with various cards depicting the item. Beyond the games and TCG, it has appeared in several pieces of officially licensed merchandise. Notably, one of Pikachu's alternate costumes in Super Smash Bros. for Nintendo 3DS/Wii U features the beloved Pokémon sporting a Choice Band on its head.

Title: Amulet Coin

Known in Japanese as おまもりこばん (Amulet Koban), the Amulet Coin is a held item, first appearing in Generation II, designed to boost the prize money acquired from battling Trainers. Its effect is to double the prize money received post-battle, provided the Pokémon holding it actively participates in the encounter. Additionally, it grants a twofold increase to the coins collected through the moves Pay Day, G-Max Gold Rush, and Make It Rain. For players in Pokémon Colosseum's Story Mode and Pokémon XD's colosseums, this monetary bonus extends to the prize money awarded upon completion of a set. Exclusively in Pokémon Ruby and Sapphire, a minor glitch causes an incorrect display of the amount of money gathered from Pay Day if it was enhanced by an Amulet Coin; however, the player still receives the accurate, boosted total. While it does not combine its effects with Luck Incense or additional Amulet Coins, this item's bonus *does* stack with Happy Hour, Prize Money Power, and Roto Prize Money. Its effect is activated once the Pokémon holding it participates in combat. Notably, it remains active regardless of the Klutz Ability and persists even if the holder faints, or if the Amulet Coin itself is somehow removed or nullified.

Within Pokémon: Magikarp Jump, the Amulet Coin functions as a Friendship Item, awarded for conquering the Great League. Its specific utility there is to summon Meowth as a helper Pokémon. Across Pokémon the Series, the Amulet Coin holds a reputation as a bringer of good fortune. It featured in the episode Capacia Island UFO!, initially discovered by Ash. When Ash and Team Rocket subsequently explored a UFO belonging to a group of Beheeyem, the extraterrestrial Pokémon revealed they had misplaced a crucial item for their craft's flight, which resembled an Amulet Coin. Ash offered the Amulet Coin he possessed, but it proved to be incorrect. The Beheeyem were actually searching for a distinct yet visually similar object that James had located earlier.

The Amulet Coin also became a Pokémon Tool card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game, debuting in the Great Encounters expansion during the English Diamond & Pearl Series (or the Japanese DP Era). When players attach this Trainer card to their Active Pokémon, it grants them the ability to draw an extra card at the conclusion of their turn. Furthermore, an Amulet Coin is depicted in the artwork for the Pokémon Collector card, originating from the HeartGold & SoulSilver expansion. Beyond its in-game and anime appearances, the Amulet Coin has been showcased in various officially licensed figures and merchandise.

Title: Cleanse Tag

An item designed to be held by a Pokémon, the Cleanse Tag (Japanese: きよめのおふだ Purification Ofuda) was introduced in Generation II. Its primary function is to reduce the rate at which wild Pokémon are encountered when carried by a Pokémon belonging to the player. More precisely, if any Pokémon within the player's party holds this item, the wild Pokémon encounter rate is cut in half. However, if the Cleanse Tag is exclusively held by the lead Pokémon in the party, the wild Pokémon encounter rate is instead diminished by one third. This item's effects remain active even if the Pokémon holding it has fainted.

The Cleanse Tag's ability to lower encounter rates does not activate if the Pokémon equipped with it possesses the Abilities Illuminate, Arena Trap, No Guard, Stench, White Smoke, or Quick Feet. Likewise, its functionality is suppressed if the Pokémon has Sand Veil or Snow Cloak and the corresponding weather condition is in effect. Nevertheless, the Cleanse Tag's reduction in encounters is still cumulative with the overworld benefits provided by Abilities like Intimidate and Keen Eye.

Title: Soul Dew

Known in Japanese as こころのしずく (Soul Dew), this held item made its debut in Generation III of the Pokémon world. From its introduction through Generation VI, the Soul Dew significantly enhanced the Special Attack and Special Defense stats of both Latios and Latias, providing a 50% boost to each. However, beginning with Generation VII, its function evolved; it now amplifies the damage dealt by Psychic-type and Dragon-type moves executed by Latios or Latias. This power increase is approximately 20%, with the precise in-game multiplier being 4915/4096.

Prior to Generation VII, the item faced a ban from a multitude of official competitive formats and battle facilities, including official tournaments, Random Matchup, the Battle Subway, the Battle Institute, the Battle Maison, and almost all PWT tournaments. Furthermore, in Generations III and IV, its effects were entirely suppressed if employed within the Battle Tower or Battle Frontier. Yet, the modification to its in-game effect in Generation VII brought a change to its legality, permitting its use in current official tournaments, Battle Spot, and other battle facilities.

The Soul Dew holds a prominent role in the film Pokémon Heroes: Latios & Latias, where it serves as a crucial guardian artifact for Alto Mare, a city in the Johto region. Its backstory in the movie reveals it was bestowed upon an elderly couple in Alto Mare countless years ago, after a Latios and a Latias courageously defended the metropolis from an invading malevolent entity. Subsequently, it was secreted away within the city's Secret Garden. The movie's antagonists, Annie and Oakley, sought to acquire the Soul Dew, concurrently pursuing the city's reigning Legendary protectors, Latios and Latias.

When affixed to Alto Mare's Defense Mechanism, the Soul Dew empowers any operator of the device to inundate the city with water, seal all escape routes, and reawaken the Fossil Pokémon Kabutops and Aerodactyl housed in the city museum. Nevertheless, if wielded by an individual with malevolent intentions, the gem would darken and ultimately shatter. The original Soul Dew met its destruction during the film's climax, but a new one materialized when Latios selflessly sacrificed his life to shield Alto Mare from a colossal tidal wave, channeling his very spirit to forge a substitute for the ruined artifact.

Beyond the main games and film, the Soul Dew was introduced as a Trainer card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game during the Japanese e-Card Era. Featured in the Theater Limited VS Pack half deck, its artwork was created by Hiromichi Sugiyama. This Trainer card allows a player to search their deck for a Pokémon with "Alto Mare" in its name, placing it onto their Bench, and also optionally moving an Energy card from another of their Pokémon to this newly benched Pokémon before shuffling their deck. The Soul Dew has also been represented across various officially licensed merchandise and figures. In the climactic Japanese trailer for the fifth Pokémon movie, Bianca reveals to Ash that the Soul Dew is, in essence, the solidified tears of Latios and Latias.

Title: Deep Sea Tooth

This particular held item, known as the Deep Sea Tooth (Japanese: しんかいのキバ Deep-Sea Fang), made its debut in Generation III. Its formatting varied over time: prior to Generation VI, it was styled as DeepSeaTooth, and in the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, it is referred to as Deepseatooth. The item serves two primary functions for Clamperl: it facilitates its evolution into Huntail, and simultaneously boosts the Special Attack stat of any Clamperl holding it. Specifically, trading a Clamperl while it holds this item will trigger its evolution into Huntail, a process that consumes the Deep Sea Tooth.

The Deepseatooth is also found in the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, specifically in Red Rescue Team, Blue Rescue Team, Explorers of Time, Explorers of Darkness, and Explorers of Sky. In these games, a Clamperl can evolve into Huntail by presenting both a Link Cable and the Deepseatooth at either Luminous Cave or Luminous Spring. This method of evolution consumes both the Link Cable and the Deepseatooth. In terms of direct interaction within a dungeon, if the item is thrown, it inflicts 1 damage (or 2 damage if it is sticky). Attempting to use this item in a dungeon will result in its consumption without any subsequent effect.

In the episode "The Evolutionary War," a Deep Sea Tooth and a Deep Sea Scale played significant roles, enabling Keith's Clamperl to evolve into a Huntail and Nancy's Clamperl into a Gorebyss, respectively. That same episode revealed that Deep Sea Teeth could be discovered on Island C among the A-B-C Islands. Another appearance occurred in "Showdown At Linoone," where Tokin was shown to possess two Deep Sea Teeth among his collected items. Regarding its textual presentation in games, Pokémon Diamond and Pearl rendered the item's name with a lowercase 's' when pluralized or preceded by an indefinite article (e.g., "DeepseaTeeth" and "a DeepseaTooth"). This was later rectified in Pokémon Platinum, and Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, where both instances were corrected to use a capital 'S' ("DeepSeaTeeth" and "a DeepSeaTooth"), aligning with the item's standard proper noun capitalization.

Title: Deep Sea Scale

Known in Japanese as しんかいのウロコ (Deep-Sea Scale), the Deep Sea Scale is a held item that debuted in Generation III. Before Generation VI, its English name was rendered as DeepSeaScale, and within the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon franchise, it was referred to as Deepseascale. Its primary function is to facilitate the evolution of Clamperl into Gorebyss. Additionally, when Clamperl holds it, the item bestows a boost to that Pokémon's Special Defense. The specific condition for this evolution is trading a Clamperl while it is holding the item, a process that results in the consumption of the Deep Sea Scale.

Within the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, the Deepseascale is present in *Red Rescue Team* and *Blue Rescue Team*, as well as *Explorers of Time*, *Explorers of Darkness*, and *Explorers of Sky*. In these titles, evolving Clamperl into Gorebyss requires the player to present both a Link Cable and a Deepseascale at either the Luminous Cave or Luminous Spring. Both the Link Cable and the Deepseascale are consumed during this evolution. When thrown in a dungeon, it inflicts 1 damage, or 2 if it is sticky. Attempting to use the Deepseascale as an item within a dungeon will consume it without any discernible effect.

In the episode "The Evolutionary War," both a Deep Sea Tooth and a Deep Sea Scale were featured. These items facilitated the evolution of Keith's Clamperl into a Huntail and Nancy's Clamperl into a Gorebyss, respectively. During the events of this episode, it was disclosed that Deep Sea Scales could be discovered on Island B among the A-B-C Islands. Another Deep Sea Scale made an appearance in "Showdown At Linoone," numbered among the various objects collected by Tokin.

In *Pokémon Diamond* and *Pearl*, the item's name displayed with a lowercase 's' when pluralized as "DeepseaScales" or preceded by an indefinite article as "a DeepseaScale." However, these naming conventions were rectified in *Pokémon Platinum*, *HeartGold*, and *SoulSilver*, where both instances correctly presented with a capital 'S', appearing as "DeepSeaScales" and "a DeepSeaScale," aligning with standard capitalization.

Within *Pokémon HeartGold* and *SoulSilver*, players could acquire a DeepSeaScale before unlocking the National Pokédex. This was achievable by capturing a wild Chinchou that was carrying the item, or by pilfering it using a move like Thief or Covet. Despite this early availability, the item had limited utility at that stage (other than for trading purposes), given that its benefits solely apply to Clamperl, a Pokémon unavailable before the National Pokédex is acquired. Since the item's description explicitly refers to Clamperl, this scenario presented the sole opportunity to view the name of a Pokémon not included in the Johto Pokédex prior to obtaining the National Pokédex. Exceptions to this include the Battle Frontier (which merely requires Hall of Fame entry, not the National Pokédex) and interactions with other game cartridges.

Title: Smoke Ball

This article describes the held item originating in Generation II. To find information on the consumable item introduced in Pokémon Legends: Arceus, refer to Smoke Bomb. The Smoke Ball (Japanese: けむりだま Smoke Ball) is a held item that made its debut in Generation II.

Its core function is to ensure the Pokémon carrying it can escape from wild encounters without fail. When equipped by a player's Pokémon, a successful retreat from wild opponents is guaranteed. This item, however, does not override trapping moves that might impede escape. Yet, a Pokémon bearing a Smoke Ball can successfully flee and execute Teleport, irrespective of any trapping moves or Abilities affecting it.

In a situation where the player's Pokémon holding a Smoke Ball faints, and the player chooses to flee rather than dispatching another Pokémon, escape is still assured. Nevertheless, following the fainting of a player's Pokémon that was holding a Smoke Ball, the player is no longer guaranteed a successful flee. In Generation III exclusively, using the Smoke Ball triggers a distinct animation. Wild Pokémon, even when equipped with a Smoke Ball, are unable to successfully use Teleport if they are trapped.

A Pokémon carrying this item can always perform Teleport successfully, even if under the influence of trapping moves or Abilities. When confronting the Nihilego at Aether Paradise, if it possesses a Smoke Ball, it gains the capacity to flee on the fifth turn of the battle, even if it is trapped. In the game Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, despite the infrequent applicability of its primary effect due to the general inability to flee from most battles, the Smoke Ball's properties are still present. Should a wild Trapinch with the Arena Trap Ability be encountered at a Poké Spot, the player's Pokémon can successfully use Teleport if it holds a Smoke Ball.

Title: Everstone

Originating in Generation II, the Everstone (Japanese: かわらずのいし Unchanging Stone) is a held item designed to inhibit a Pokémon's Evolution. Within the Generation V titles, an item collector located in Icirrus City's Pokémon Center will purchase an Everstone for $1000. The specific Evolution methods thwarted by a Pokémon clutching an Everstone vary across different game iterations. Universally, in every game featuring the Everstone, it blocks Evolutions initiated by gaining a level during combat or via a Rare Candy. This encompasses Evolutions contingent on reaching a specific level, possessing high friendship, being in particular areas, knowing a distinct move, among other conditions. For Pokémon that normally evolve through trade without needing a specific held item, attaching an Everstone can halt this process when traded, unless the trade is with a Generation I game.

During Generations II and III, the Everstone effectively stopped Kadabra from transforming into Alakazam. However, from Generation IV onward, Kadabra will still evolve into Alakazam even when holding an Everstone, a consequence of a specific programmed override. Should either Karrablast or Shelmet possess an Everstone during a trade with the other, neither creature will undergo Evolution. Across Generations II, III, and then from VI through VIII, a Pokémon equipped with an Everstone cannot evolve via an Evolution stone. Specifically in Generation VIII, the message "Cannot use" displays if a player tries to employ an Evolution stone on a Pokémon holding an Everstone, even if that Pokémon would normally be compatible.

Upon the initial interaction with a scroll at either the Tower of Waters or Tower of Darkness, if Kubfu is holding an Everstone, Mustard will observe this and permit the player to forgo its Evolution. When Galarian Yamask or Galarian Farfetch'd fulfill their Evolution conditions while holding an Everstone, they will not evolve. Similarly, Applin or Sinistea, when carrying an Everstone, will resist Evolution even when exposed to their specific evolutionary catalysts.

In Pokémon Emerald, and in Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum, if a female Pokémon or a Ditto holds an Everstone within the Pokémon Day Care, the resulting offspring has a 50% likelihood of inheriting that parent's Nature. This mechanic was adjusted in Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, and in Pokémon Black and White, where either parent holding an Everstone gains a 50% chance to transmit its Nature. However, in Generation IV, this Nature inheritance fails if the breeding parents originate from games of differing languages (a side effect related to the Masuda method). From Pokémon Black 2 and White 2, and throughout Generation VI and beyond, when an Everstone is held by either parent in the Pokémon Day Care or Pokémon Nursery, the offspring is guaranteed to possess the Nature of the Everstone-bearing parent. If both parents are equipped with Everstones, each parent has an equal probability of imparting its Nature.

Within the Pokémon Nursery, when breeding creatures capable of producing regionally variant offspring, if a parent belonging to the same evolutionary family as the child holds an Everstone, the resulting Pokémon will adopt the regional form of that Everstone-holding parent, irrespective of the breeding location. (Typically, regionally variant offspring are born in the form native to the region where breeding occurs.) Should both parents be holding Everstones, the regional form of the offspring will match that of the female parent. Nevertheless, a known oversight in Pokémon Sword and Shield dictates that breeding a Unovan Stunfisk with a Galarian Stunfisk, both holding Everstones, invariably yields Unovan Stunfisk offspring, regardless of the parents' genders.

In Pokémon: Magikarp Jump, the player commences with an Everstone that prevents Magikarp's Evolution into Gyarados. This item cannot be discarded but can be shattered by persistently tapping Magikarp. For Pokémon Quest, the Everstone's effect can be activated or deactivated when customizing a Pokémon's Power Charm. When active, it halts a Pokémon's Evolution.

In the episode "Stopped in the Name of Love!", Nurse Joy of Sandalstraw Town provided Dawn with an Everstone to support Piplup's wish to delay evolving; in the English dub, he sought to retain his memories with Dawn as a Piplup, while the original Japanese version indicated fear of becoming aggressive like Dawn's Mamoswine after evolving. Dawn then presented it to Piplup to prevent his transformation into Prinplup. Curiously, Piplup was never subsequently depicted holding the Everstone. "No Stone Unturned!" featured Ash's Rowlet discovering and consuming an Everstone, thereby forfeiting its potential to evolve into Dartrix. Coincidentally, this also led to Rowlet mastering Seed Bomb, a move Ash was attempting to teach it instead of the then-unlearnable Bullet Seed. The short "The Pancham Who Wants to Be a Hero" showed an Everstone worn by a Pancham for the majority of its runtime, until it was ultimately discarded, permitting the Pancham to evolve into Pangoro. In "(Violent) Earthquake! Cyrus's Consuming Ambition!!", it came to light that Mitsumi had covertly fastened an Everstone to Hareta's Piplup prior to his journey, believing Hareta would progress more rapidly with less powerful Pokémon. Once Mitsumi took off the Everstone, Piplup promptly evolved into Prinplup, and then into Empoleon.

The Everstone has also been released as exclusive Pokémon Center merchandise, available as a plush accessory or keychain.

Generation I, Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, and Pokémon Legends: Arceus lack the Everstone because the held item mechanic is absent. Consequently, these titles offer no direct means to prevent a Pokémon's Evolution from being initiated. Nonetheless, players can still halt an Evolution by pressing B during the Evolution animation, depending on the specific method of Evolution. As a B-press cannot cancel a trade-triggered Evolution, players cannot simply trade an unevolved Pokémon in games without the held item feature. Likewise, outside of Generations II and III, it is impossible to trade a Kadabra without it evolving into Alakazam. Mindy, in Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, Platinum, Brilliant Diamond, and Shining Pearl, provides the player with an in-game trade for a Haunter in Snowpoint City. Since this particular Haunter is equipped with an Everstone, it fails to evolve into Gengar.

Pokémon Scarlet and Violet's Biology class Final Exam includes a question about the Everstone's interplay with Evolution stones: "If a Pokémon is holding an Everstone, will using an item that induces Evolution, such as a Fire Stone, cause it to evolve?". In initial versions of these titles, the designated "correct" response (judged by exam scoring) was that an Everstone would block a compatible Pokémon from evolving via its stone, despite this not being the Everstone's effect since Generation IV. A subsequent update rectified this, altering the correct answer to "Yes, it will [evolve]."

Title: Focus Band

The Focus Band (Japanese: きあいのハチマキ Fighting Spirit Headband) is a held item type that made its debut in Generation II, offering a chance to prevent fainting. Originally, if a Pokémon equipped with a Focus Band was struck by a damaging move that would otherwise cause it to faint, there was an approximately 12% (30/256) likelihood of the item activating, allowing the Pokémon to survive with 1 HP. In these initial generations, should the Focus Band trigger during any strike of a multi-hit attack, the Pokémon would endure all subsequent strikes from that same attack. It also did not activate against damage from self-inflicted confusion.

Beginning in Generation V, the Focus Band's activation chance was standardized to 10%. Furthermore, its mechanics evolved to allow the holder to survive self-inflicted confusion damage. For multi-strike moves, if the Focus Band activates, the Pokémon now only survives the specific strike that triggered it, necessitating independent activation for each following hit. Regardless of generation, the Focus Band can enable its holder to survive both Future Sight and Doom Desire.

In Pokémon UNITE, the Focus Band operates as a held item that restores the user's HP over 3 seconds when their health drops below 25%; specifically, this recovery amounts to 8/11/14% of missing HP, accompanied by a 100/90/80-second cooldown period, corresponding to Grade 1, 10, and 20 respectively. Additionally, it boosts the user's Defense and Sp. Defense stats: by 2 and 0 respectively at Grade 1, and by 30 for both at Grade 30.

Pichu appears to wear a Focus Band in one of its available palette swaps within Super Smash Bros. Ultimate. In the episode "The Journalist from Another Region!", a Pokémon Sumo Tournament, part of the Grand Harvest Festival on Harvest Island, offered a Focus Band as its grand prize.
