Ash ultimately claimed victory, subsequently presenting the item to his Pignite for use as a trophy. During "The Last Battle VI," Gold equipped each of his Pokémon team members with a Focus Band for their climactic confrontation with the Masked Man.

Within the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Focus Band debuted as a Pokémon Tool Trainer card during the English Neo Series (known as the Japanese Neo Era) within the Neo Genesis expansion. This particular card has never received a reprint. Should a Pokémon holding a Focus Band be Knocked Out, its owner has the option to flip a coin. If the result is heads, the Pokémon remains in play with 10 HP, and the Focus Band is then discarded.

The Focus Band has also made appearances in various officially licensed merchandise and figures. In "Bibarel Gnaws Best!", an adept stonecutter and his Bibarel were depicted wearing headbands that bore a resemblance to Focus Bands. At the episode's conclusion, he presented similar headbands to Ash, Brock, and Dawn. Golgo's Octillery in My Pokémon Ranch features a distinct model, visibly sporting its Focus Band. It has been consistently present across core series games from Generation II through IX.

Title: Lucky Egg

This particular item, identified in Japanese as しあわせタマゴ (Lucky Egg), was initially introduced in Generation II. Within Generation V games, the Lucky Egg holds a specific resale value, fetching $200 when sold to the gourmet fanatic located on Route 5. When a Pokémon holds this item, it experiences a 150% increase in the experience points it would ordinarily acquire. This beneficial effect is cumulative with other methods of boosting experience gain, such as the natural amplification of experience received by Pokémon from a different Original Trainer. However, it offers no advantage for experience gained through Auto Battles. When activated, a Lucky Egg doubles the experience points earned by the player for a period of 30 minutes. Lucky Eggs are continuously available for purchase from the in-game Shop using PokéCoins. Additionally, players are awarded a Lucky Egg upon reaching level 9, and then consistently every 5 levels thereafter, starting from level 10. In the episode "First Catch in Alola, Ketchum-Style!", Rotom Pokédex made reference to the Lucky Egg while delivering Blissey's Pokédex entry. In the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Lucky Egg made its debut as a Pokémon Tool Trainer card within the Arceus expansion during the English Platinum Series (known as the Japanese Platinum Era). Its first reprinting occurred in the Sword & Shield series. If the Pokémon it is attached to is Knocked Out and sent to the discard pile due to damage from an opponent's attack, the player is then entitled to draw cards until their hand contains 7 cards.

Title: Scope Lens

First appearing in Generation II, the Scope Lens (ピントレンズ, or Focus Point Lens, in Japanese) functions as a held item. In the main series games, it elevates the holder's critical hit ratio by a single stage. Within Pokémon UNITE, this held item boosts the damage dealt by critical basic attacks by a percentage of the user's Attack stat; this effect is precisely 45% at Grade 1, 60% at Grade 10, and 75% at Grade 20. Furthermore, in UNITE, it enhances both the user's critical hit rate and critical hit damage; at Grade 1, these increases are 0.4% and 0.8% respectively, while at Grade 30, they climb to 6% and 12%. Its presence is also noted across several Pokémon Mystery Dungeon titles, including Red Rescue Team and Blue Rescue Team, Explorers of Time, Explorers of Darkness, and Explorers of Sky, Keep Going! Blazing Adventure Squad, and Gates to Infinity. When equipped by a Pokémon in these Mystery Dungeon adventures, it boosts the Pokémon's critical hit probability by a fixed 30%/15%. An entry in "Interesting Interactions Involving Illumise" highlighted a Scope Lens as the held item for a rental Ludicolo employed by Emerald during a Battle Factory challenge. The Magnifier, a Trainer card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game sharing the same Japanese name as the Scope Lens, debuted in the English Neo Series (known as the Japanese Neo Era) within the Neo Destiny expansion. This card has not seen a reprint since its initial release. It functions by attaching to a Pokémon for a single turn; any Pokémon attacking while equipped with the Magnifier disregards Resistance. The Scope Lens has been present across Generation II, III, IV, V, VI, VII, VIII, and IX.

Title: Metal Coat

Known in Japanese as メタルコート, the Metal Coat is a held item first introduced in Generation II of the Pokémon franchise. Its primary role is to facilitate the evolution of specific Pokémon: Onix into Steelix, and Scyther into Scizor. Beyond evolution, it also serves to boost the damage output of Steel-type moves when held by a Pokémon. This power increase stands at 10% in certain contexts and 20% in others for Steel-type attacks launched by the holder.

In the main series games, an Onix or Scyther will undergo evolution into Steelix or Scizor, respectively, when traded while holding a Metal Coat. This evolutionary process consumes the item. However, this evolution will not occur if the trade links to a Generation I game. For Pokémon Legends: Arceus, where held items are absent, the Metal Coat simply needs to be used directly on an Onix or Scyther to trigger its evolution, with the item still being consumed.

The Metal Coat also features in the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, appearing in titles such as Red Rescue Team, Blue Rescue Team, and the Explorers of Time, Darkness, and Sky trio. Within these games, offering both a Link Cable and a Metal Coat at either Luminous Cave or Luminous Spring enables the evolution of Onix into Steelix or Scyther into Scizor. Both the Link Cable and Metal Coat are consumed in this process. In a dungeon setting, throwing the Metal Coat inflicts 1 damage (or 2 if sticky), but merely using it will consume the item without any beneficial effect.

In Pokémon Conquest, a distinct evolution method applies: Onix or Scyther can evolve into Steelix or Scizor if its Warrior, equipped with a Metal Coat, defeats an opposing Warrior's Pokémon in a way that allows for recruitment. Additionally, in Conquest, the Metal Coat grants Steel-type Pokémon a one-stage increase in their Attack stat. It can be acquired from the traveling merchant for 5000 gold and sold for a price ranging from 2500 to 3250 gold.

The Metal Coat became available in Pokémon GO on February 16, 2017. Here, it is an essential component for evolution: Onix requires 50 Onix Candy and a Metal Coat to transform into Steelix, while Scyther needs 50 Scyther Candy and a Metal Coat to become Scizor. While generally rare, Metal Coats can be obtained by players level 10 and above. Notably, from March 21, 2017, onwards, spinning a Photo Disc for seven consecutive days guarantees a player an Evolution item, which can include a Metal Coat. It is also a reward for specific Special Research tasks.

In Pokémon Sleep, the Metal Coat is also a consumable item vital for evolution. For Onix to evolve into Steelix, 80 Onix Candy, a Metal Coat, and a Linking Cord are all required.

Outside of gameplay, the Metal Coat has made appearances in other media. In the anime episode "Searching for Chivalry!", Wikstrom provided Goh's Scyther with a Metal Coat, leading to its evolution into Scizor via a trade machine while the item was held. In the manga, specifically "Vexing Vespiquen & Unmanageable Mothim II," Diamond discovered a Metal Coat within Byron's vacation home on Iron Island, subsequently giving it to his Shieldon to hold.

In Pokémon Sword and Shield, players can acquire the Metal Coat from Stow-on-Side's bargain shop or by interacting with the Digging Duo. The item has been present across Generations II, III, IV, V, VI, VII, VIII, and IX.

Title: Leftovers

The held item known as Leftovers (たべのこし in Japanese) debuted in Generation II, providing a consistent recovery of its bearer's hit points over time. Within Generation V titles, this item could be bartered for $200 with the gourmet enthusiast found on Route 5. Each turn's conclusion sees the holder regain 1/16 of its total HP, with the recovery amount always rounded down, yet never dropping below a single hit point. Notably, in Generation IV, Leftovers would restore health even under the effects of Heal Block; however, from Generation V and subsequent generations, Pokémon afflicted by Heal Block are impervious to its healing properties. Beyond combat, provided the Pokémon is not within its Poké Ball, Leftovers provides health restoration once every ten seconds.

In Pokémon: Magikarp Jump, it functions as a Friendship Item, purchasable for 500, and serves to summon Snorlax as an assisting Pokémon. For Pokémon UNITE players, Leftovers act as a held item that replenishes the user's health when not engaged in battle, simultaneously boosting their overall HP and HP regeneration rate. Five seconds post-combat, the user begins to regenerate health at a rate of 1%, 1.5%, or 2% per second, corresponding to Grades 1, 10, and 20. Specifically, at Grade 1, the item confers an increase of 16 to HP and 0 to HP recovery, escalating to 360 HP and 9 HP recovery at Grade 30.

Throughout this particular story arc, Leftovers were observed on numerous occasions, equipped by several different Pokémon. Its initial mention occurred within "Interesting Interactions Involving Illumise," noted as the item held by a rental Rhyhorn deployed by Emerald at the Battle Factory. The item's debut on-panel came in "Pinsir Me, I Must Be Dreaming," where a virtual Trainer's rental Aggron utilized it at the Battle Factory. Furthermore, Emerald's rented Sceptile, during his confrontation with Noland in "Gotcha Where I Wantcha, Glalie," was equipped with Leftovers. Lucy's Milotic, though never visually depicted with it, was disclosed to possess Leftovers as its held item in "Just My Luck...Shuckle." Greta's Umbreon also carried Leftovers in the chapter titled "Sneaky Like Shedinja."

In the Pokémon Trading Card Game, Leftovers was introduced as a Pokémon Tool Trainer card within the Great Encounters expansion, marking its appearance during the English Diamond & Pearl Series (known as the Japanese DP Era). This particular iteration of Leftovers allowed the removal of a single damage counter from the attached Active Pokémon at the conclusion of the user's turn. Later, Leftovers was brought back as a Pokémon Tool card with an altered effect in the 151 expansion, coinciding with the English Scarlet & Violet Series (the Japanese Scarlet & Violet Era). This newer version heals 20 damage from the attached Active Pokémon at the end of the user's turn. Consequently, the Great Encounters print of Leftovers underwent an erratum, causing its ability to align with that of the 151 print.

Officially licensed merchandise has included Leftovers on multiple occasions. Notably, in Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, every Pokémon employed by Cook Trainers is equipped with Leftovers. The item has been present across generations: II, III, IV, V, VI, VII, VIII, and IX.

Title: Dragon Scale

Debuting in Generation II, the Dragon Scale (Japanese: りゅうのウロコ Dragon Scale) functions as a held item. Its primary purpose is to facilitate Seadra's evolution into Kingdra. Due to an oversight in Pokémon Gold, Silver, and Crystal, a held Dragon Scale increased Dragon-type move power by 10%, despite the Dragon Fang being the item intended for this effect (which itself had no effect). Conversely, in Pokémon Stadium 2, both the Dragon Fang and Dragon Scale provide a power enhancement for Dragon-type attacks. From Generation III onward, however, the Dragon Scale offers no combat utility.

To evolve Seadra into Kingdra, it must be traded while holding the Dragon Scale, a process that consumes the item. This evolution will not occur if the trade link is to a Generation I game.

The Dragon Scale also features in the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, appearing in Red Rescue Team and Blue Rescue Team, as well as Explorers of Time, Explorers of Darkness, and Explorers of Sky. Within these games, providing both a Link Cable and a Dragon Scale at Luminous Cave or Luminous Spring allows Seadra to evolve into Kingdra, consuming both items in the process. When thrown, it inflicts 1 damage (or 2 if sticky). Using this item within a dungeon will consume it without any noticeable effect.

In Pokémon GO, the Dragon Scale is indispensable for specific evolutions; for example, Seadra requires 100 Horsea Candy in addition to a Dragon Scale to become Kingdra. This item joined Pokémon GO's inventory on February 16, 2017. While generally scarce, Dragon Scales can be rarely acquired starting at level 10. However, from March 21, 2017, players are assured an Evolution item as a bonus for spinning a Photo Disc on the seventh consecutive day. Furthermore, a Dragon Scale is granted upon finishing part 6 of the Special Research titled "A Ripple in Time".

In the anime episode Looking Out for Number Two!, Ash and Goh discovered a Dragon Scale within a submerged ancient submersible, which Goh later presented to a wild Seadra. In Ampharos Amore, Silver's Seadra evolved into Kingdra after being temporarily traded to Gold while clutching a Dragon Scale. In Yikes, Yanmega! II, Roseanne disclosed her research into Evolution items, such as the Dragon Scale, as part of her studies on Pokémon Evolution. The artwork of the Unleashed Kingdra card prominently displays a Dragon Scale.

Generation II – III – IV – V – VI – VII – VIII – IX

Title: Light Ball

This held item, known in Japanese as でんきだま (Electric Orb), made its debut in Generation II. When equipped by a Pikachu, it enhances the Pokémon's statistics. Specifically, a Pikachu holding it will experience a doubling of its Special Attack. Additionally, it doubles the damage output of its moves. Furthermore, for a Pikachu, this item simultaneously doubles both its Attack and Special Attack stats. This item functions correctly with variants such as Cosplay Pikachu, Pikachu in a cap, and Gigantamax Pikachu. Should either parent of a Pichu hold a Light Ball at the moment an Egg is generated, the resulting Pichu upon hatching will possess the move Volt Tackle. Beginning with Generation VI, if a Pichu learns Volt Tackle through this breeding method (and was indeed bred in Generation VI or later), the Move Reminder can re-teach it the move if it happens to forget it. In the case of Pokémon obtained from Generation I titles, transferring them to a Generation II game is required for the item to manifest.

Within Pokémon: Magikarp Jump, the Light Ball functions as a Friendship Item, earned as a prize upon conquering the Friend League. Its effect is to call upon Pikachu to assist as a helper Pokémon. Moreover, considering the reveal that Pibu's Super Rising Thunder is, in fact, Volt Tackle in PSs2, this implies that either Pika or Chuchu must have been equipped with a Light Ball.

The Light Ball also appears as a Pokémon Tool card within the Pokémon Trading Card Game, making its debut during the English Mega Evolution Series (known as the Japanese MEGA Series). Its initial launch occurred in the Japanese MEGA Dream ex subset, followed by its introduction in the English Ascended Heroes expansion, featuring illustrations by Studio Bora Inc. Mirroring its role in the video games, the Light Ball card provides an advantage to the Pikachu ex it is attached to, causing its assaults to inflict an additional 50 damage on the opposing Active Pokémon ex.

Due to several factors – Pikachu's absence from wild encounters in Unova, the change from Generation V onward where Thief and comparable moves no longer permanently acquire items from Trainers' Pokémon, and Poké Transfer's inability to transfer held items – the Light Ball was inaccessible in Generation V unless obtained through special events. Nevertheless, it is programmed into the game as an item that wild Pikachu can hold, with a 1%*/5% chance. It has been present across Generations II, III, IV, V, VI, VII, VIII, and IX.

Title: Soft Sand

The Soft Sand (Japanese: やわらかいすな Soft Sand), a held item first appearing in Generation II, serves to increase the power of Ground-type moves. It enhances the holder's Ground-type attacks by 10% or, in later instances, by 20%. In Pokémon: Magikarp Jump, this item takes the form of a Friendship Item, bestowed as a reward for conquering the Ultra League. It subsequently summons Mudkip, who acts as a helper Pokémon. This item has been available across Generations II, III, IV, V, VI, VII, VIII, and IX.

Title: Hard Stone

Known in Japanese as かたいいし (Hard Stone), this particular held item debuted in Generation II, designed to enhance the might of Rock-type maneuvers. In the Generation V installments of the games, a Hard Stone can be sold to an item maniac situated inside the Icirrus City Pokémon Center for a sum of $500. It grants a 10% increase to the power of the holder's Rock-type moves. Furthermore, it delivers a 20% boost to the power of the holder's Rock-type moves. Noland's rental Golem was observed holding a Hard Stone during his match against Emerald, as detailed in Pinsir Me, I Must Be Dreaming and Gotcha Where I Wantcha, Glalie.
Generation II – III – IV – V – VI – VII – VIII – IX

Title: Miracle Seed

Known in Japanese as きせきのタネ (Miracle Seed), this held item first appeared in Generation II, enhancing the potency of Grass-type techniques. Its effect is to empower the holder's Grass-type moves, increasing their potency by 10%. A stronger effect sees it boosting the holder's Grass-type moves by 20%.

Within the mobile game Pokémon: Magikarp Jump, the Miracle Seed functions as a Friendship Item. Players acquire it upon successful completion of the Luxury League, and it calls forth Bulbasaur to act as a helper Pokémon.

The episode Currying Favor and Flavor! depicted Ash and Mallow finding a Miracle Seed. This discovery occurred in a hollowed-out stump brimming with Berries, located in Lush Jungle, during a quest from Olivia to gather components for the "Akala Curry".

It has been present across Generations II, III, IV, V, VI, VII, VIII, and IX.

Title: Magnet

Known in Japanese as じしゃく (Magnet), this held item first appeared in Generation II, serving to amplify the power of Electric-type moves. It boosts the power of the Pokémon's Electric-type moves by 10% when held. Alternatively, it provides a 20% power increase to the holder's Electric-type moves.

A memorable appearance for this item occurred in The Last Battle IV. During the ultimate confrontation with the Masked Man, Lt. Surge utilized a Magnet to strengthen Raikou's attacks. Curiously, the Magnet featured in this chapter was portrayed as a lodestone, contrasting with the horseshoe magnet design later established in the games. This visual distinction is attributed to the chapter's release preceding the Magnet receiving a dedicated sprite.

Within the Pokémon Trading Card Game, a Magnet is depicted as a held item on the Stormfront Magnemite card. Its effect is to decrease Magnemite's retreat cost for each Magnemite present on the player's Bench. This item has maintained its presence across Generation II, III, IV, V, VI, VII, VIII, and IX.

Title: Mystic Water

Known in Japanese as しんぴのしずく (Mysterious Droplet), the Mystic Water is a specialized held item introduced in Generation II, designed to amplify the force of Water-type attacks. It serves to boost the power of the holder's Water-type moves by 10%. Additionally, the item has been observed to elevate the power of the holder's Water-type moves by 20%.

Within the game Pokémon: Magikarp Jump, the Mystic Water functions as a Friendship Item. It is awarded as a prize after successfully defeating the Quick League and, when obtained, summons Piplup to act as a helper Pokémon.

The Mystic Water has also made several appearances in various Pokémon narratives. In the episode "The Perfect Match!", it was presented to the champion of the Whirl Cup. During "Balloons, Brionne, and Belligerence!", Ida and Kanoa embarked on a search for a Mystic Water within a sunken ship, with Ash, Lana, and Mallow joining their expedition. Upon its discovery by Kanoa, he gifted it to Ida, who subsequently wore it as a necklace. Furthermore, in "The Last Battle IV", Misty utilized a Mystic Water to enhance the strength of Suicune's moves during her final confrontation with the Masked Man.

This item has been consistently featured across Generation II, Generation III, Generation IV, Generation V, Generation VI, Generation VII, Generation VIII, and Generation IX.

Title: Poison Barb

The Poison Barb (Japanese: どくバリ Poison Barb) is an equipped item that first appeared in the second generation of the core Pokémon games. Its primary function is to amplify the effectiveness of Poison-type techniques when held by a Pokémon. Initially, it augmented the potency of its wielder's Poison-type moves by 10%. Subsequently, this enhancement increased to 20%. In the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Poison Barb debuted as a Pokémon Tool card during the English Sun & Moon Series (concurrent with the Japanese Sun & Moon Era) within the Sun & Moon expansion. When this specific Trainer card is affixed to the player's Active Pokémon, should an opponent inflict damage upon that Pokémon, the Attacking Pokémon then becomes Poisoned. Its presence spans from Generation II to IX, encompassing all intervening installments.

Title: Spell Tag

This entry focuses on the specific item that amplifies type power. For the separate item used to deter Pokémon, refer to Cleanse Tag. Known in Japanese as のろいのおふだ (Cursed O-fuda), the Spell Tag is a held item originating from Generation II, designed to augment the might of Ghost-type attacks. It increases the damage output of the bearer's Ghost-type maneuvers by 10%. Additionally, it enhances the potency of the bearer's Ghost-type maneuvers by 20%.

Within Pokémon: Magikarp Jump, the Spell Tag functions as a Friendship Item, acquirable for a sum of 400. Its effect is to call forth Mimikyu, who then acts as a helper Pokémon. In the world of Pokémon Pokopia, the Spell Tag manifests as a diminutive Lost Relic. The Spell Tag has also made its debut as a Pokémon Tool card within the Pokémon Trading Card Game, specifically in the Lost Thunder expansion from the Sun & Moon Series (known as the Sun & Moon Era in Japan). Should the Pokémon bearing the Spell Tag be Knocked Out by damage inflicted by an opposing attack, its owner gains the ability to distribute 4 damage counters among their opponent's Pokémon as they see fit. A Secret Rare version of this card also exists within the identical expansion.

Beyond gameplay, the Spell Tag has prominently appeared alongside the Gastly evolutionary line in the "Looming Shadows" statue created by First 4 Figures. It also features on various products from the YonayonaGhost collection, an exclusive offering from the Pokémon Center, and is depicted on the Item Mania card sleeves. Present across Generations II through IX.

Title: Twisted Spoon

This held item, introduced in Generation II, is known in Japanese as まがったスプーン (Twisted Spoon) and was styled as TwistedSpoon before Generation VI. Its purpose is to augment the power of Psychic-type moves. Initially, it elevated the power of the holder's Psychic-type moves by 10%. This enhancement was later increased, providing a 20% boost to the power of the wielder's Psychic-type moves. A Twisted Spoon was also depicted in the episode "Climbing the Tower of Success!" as an object Ricky needed to locate during the Wishing Bell Festival scavenger hunt. It has been present in Generation II, Generation III, Generation IV, Generation V, Generation VI, Generation VII, Generation VIII, and Generation IX.

Title: Charcoal

The held item known as Charcoal (もくたん in Japanese) first appeared in Generation II. Its primary function is to enhance the efficacy of Fire-type maneuvers executed by the Pokémon carrying it. Initially, this enhancement amounted to a 10% power increase for the holder's Fire-type moves, a figure that later rose to 20%. Within the game Pokémon: Magikarp Jump, Charcoal functions as a Friendship Item, obtainable for a cost of 600. When utilized in this title, it calls upon Charizard to act as a helper Pokémon.

The item was also featured in the anime episode "A Farfetch'd Tale." During their journey through Ilex Forest, Ash and his companions encountered a young boy named Sylvester. Sylvester was endeavoring to master the creation of "purifying charcoal," following the traditions of his teacher (or his father, as per the English dub). Brock clarified that this purifying charcoal is, in fact, burnt wood, possessing properties useful for fuel or for cleansing water and air. As the episode concluded, Ash and his friends were presented with some charcoal as a token of gratitude. In the manga series, specifically in "The Last Battle IV," Blaine employed a piece of Charcoal to amplify the strength of Entei's Fire-type attacks during his climactic confrontation with the Masked Man.

A Flareon figure, set for release on January 16, 2025, by The Pokémon Company International, includes Charcoal. This collectible is part of the "Evolving Personalities" figure collection. A resident of Lavaridge Town remarks on Charcoal's use in preparing herbal medicine; however, within gameplay, the item lacks any actual medicinal utility. Notably, Charcoal stands alone as the only type-enhancing item available for purchase at a Poké Mart. It has appeared consistently from Generation II through III, IV, V, VI, VII, VIII, and IX.

Title: Dragon Fang

The Dragon Fang (Japanese: りゅうのキバ Dragon Fang) emerged in Generation II as a held item designed to amplify the power of Dragon-type moves. A known programming oversight in the handheld games rendered the Dragon Fang ineffective when held; its intended benefit was erroneously applied to the Dragon Scale instead. In Pokémon Stadium 2, however, it correctly boosted the power of the holder's Dragon-type moves by 10%. For players in Pokémon Gold and Silver, acquiring the Dragon Fang from Dragon's Den is a crucial step to receive the Rising Badge from Clair. Should a player possess a Dragon Fang obtained through other means, such as trading with another trainer, without having collected the specific one from Dragon's Den, Clair will accuse them of cheating and refuse to award the badge. This item typically enhances the power of the holder's Dragon-type moves by 10%. In certain cases, it provides a 20% increase to the power of the holder's Dragon-type moves. Once a player achieves friendship level 100 with Lance at the Trainer Lodge, a replica of a Dragon Fang becomes available for purchase in the Lodge Exchange, intended for display within the Trainer Lodge.

A Dragon Fang made its anime debut in "Beauty is Skin Deep," depicted as a cherished family treasure of Clair's, safeguarded within the Blackthorn Gym. This particular fang is said to belong to an ancient Dragon Pokémon that once terrorized Blackthorn City. The Dragon Fang plays a pivotal role in a ritual aimed at bringing peace to all Dragon-type Pokémon. This ritual entails the Blackthorn Gym Leader carefully placing the fang on a special ceremonial table, then pouring water over it. This action causes a radiant light dragon to burst forth and ascend into the sky. Legend states that this celestial dragon transforms into a star, watching over all Dragon-type Pokémon. In the episode "Fangs for Nothin'," Team Rocket illicitly seized the aforementioned Dragon Fang, interrupting a battle between Ash and Clair at the Blackthorn Gym. It was successfully retrieved from them in the subsequent episode. Another Dragon Fang was seen in "Showdown At Linoone" as one of the various items Tokin had collected. The item has featured across Generation II – III – IV – V – VI – VII – VIII – IX.

Title: Shell Bell

The Shell Bell (known as かいがらのすず in Japanese) is a held item initially introduced in Generation III, designed to incrementally restore a Pokémon's HP. Its composition involves Shoal Salts and Shoal Shells. When the holder successfully inflicts damage with any move, it recovers HP equivalent to 1/8 of the damage dealt. This recovery is always rounded down but will not fall below 1 HP. However, no HP is restored if the move fails to inflict damage, such as when False Swipe is used against a Pokémon already at 1 HP.

Regarding multi-strike moves, the holder's HP is replenished after each individual strike, based solely on the damage from that specific hit. Alternatively, for other contexts involving multi-strike moves, HP recovery occurs after the final strike, factoring in the total damage from all hits combined. Shell Bell effects are not triggered by Future Sight or Doom Desire.

There is conflicting information regarding substitutes: in some cases, the Shell Bell activates even when a move exclusively hits a Pokémon's substitute, while in others, it will not activate under the same circumstances. Similarly, concerning Heal Block, the Shell Bell has been described as capable of healing a Pokémon regardless of this status condition, yet it has also been stated that it cannot heal a Pokémon affected by Heal Block.

If a Pokémon manages to steal a Shell Bell using Thief or Covet, that Pokémon will regain HP from the very move that secured the item. The same recovery occurs if a Pokémon acquires a Shell Bell via the Magician ability with the move it used to steal it. When interacting with Pickpocket, Shell Bell's activation precedes the ability, meaning HP is still restored for the move even if the item is subsequently taken.

Shell Bell's HP restoration takes place after the holder recovers HP from a draining move or sustains damage from recoil, Rough Skin, Iron Barbs, or the Rocky Helmet. Notably, if a move's power is amplified by Sheer Force, the Shell Bell's recovery effect is bypassed.

Beyond the main series, the Shell Bell appears in Pokémon: Magikarp Jump as a Friendship Item purchasable for 500, summoning Greninja as an assisting Pokémon. In Pokémon UNITE, it functions as a held item that restores the user's HP upon landing a move, subject to a 10-second cooldown, simultaneously boosting their Sp. Atk and CD Reduction. Each successful hit restores HP equal to 45/60/75 + 35/40/45% of the user's Sp. Attack at Grades 1, 10, and 20, respectively. At Grade 1, this item provides a 1.6 increase to Sp. Attack and 0% CD reduction, escalating to 24 Sp. Attack and 4.5% CD reduction at Grade 30.

An instance of a Shell Bell was observed in the anime episode The Slugma-Powered Home, situated on a fireplace within Anna's grandparents' residence. This item has also been featured across various officially licensed merchandise.

Title: Sea Incense

Introduced in Generation III, the Sea Incense (Japanese: うしおのおこう, or Seawater Incense) is a type of held item. Before Generation IX, its specific function was to enable Marill and Azumarill to produce Azurill Eggs. When used in combat, its effect perfectly mirrors that of the Mystic Water. It provides a 5% boost to the power of the holder's Water-type moves. Furthermore, it also augments the power of the holder's Water-type moves by 20%. In Pokémon Day Care or the Pokémon Nursery, if either parent holds this item within a pairing that would ordinarily yield a Marill Egg, an Azurill Egg is generated instead. The Sea Incense and the Wave Incense are the sole two incenses to possess precisely the same effect during battle.

Title: Lax Incense

Introduced in Generation III, the Lax Incense (known in Japanese as のんきのおこう, or "Carefree Incense") functions as a held item. This particular incense enables Wobbuffet to generate Wynaut Eggs. During battles, its combat utility mirrors that of the Bright Powder. It diminishes the accuracy of incoming moves aimed at the Pokémon holding it by 5% of their original precision. Furthermore, it causes moves targeting the holder to have their accuracy reduced by 10% of their base value. Specifically for breeding, if this item is held by either progenitor within a Pokémon Day Care or Nursery pairing that would normally yield a Wobbuffet Egg, a Wynaut Egg will be yielded in its place. An instance of a Lax Incense was depicted in PT10, during a scene where a boy tended to his Primeape by brushing it. Notably, this incense bears a name identical to a Pokémon Nature across several languages: English, French, Japanese, Korean, and Chinese. While the corresponding Nature in French is "Mild," its counterpart in Japanese, Korean, and Chinese is "Relaxed." By happenstance, both of these Natures invariably influence the Defense stat (either positively or negatively), yet neither has any impact on the Attack stat.

Title: Metal Powder

Known in Japanese as メタルパウダー (Metal Powder), this held item first appeared in Generation II. Its primary function is to enhance the Defense statistic of a Ditto carrying it. When a Ditto possesses this item, its Defense and Special Defense are augmented by 50%, a boost that persists even after transformation. However, within Pokémon Stadium 2, Metal Powder exclusively provides this Defense and Special Defense increase while Ditto remains untransformed. For a Ditto holding it, its Defense stat is doubled. This specific effect is inactive once Ditto has transformed. The item's availability covers Generation II – III – IV – V – VI – VII – VIII – IX.

Title: Thick Club

Known in Japanese as ふといホネ (Thick Bone), the Thick Club is a held item that made its debut in Generation II. Its primary effect is to augment the Attack stat of any Cubone or Marowak carrying it. Specifically, when held by Cubone, any form of Marowak, or even a Pokémon that has transformed into one of these, its Attack stat is doubled. However, in the Gold, Silver, and Crystal games, this doubling benefit does not extend to Pokémon merely transformed into Cubone or Marowak. Also in Gold, Silver, and Crystal, any Pokémon stat that attains or surpasses 1024 (possibly due to holding a Thick Club) will be reset to its value modulo 1024. In the episode "Showdown At Linoone," two Thick Clubs were observed among Tokin's collection of retrieved items. One of these clubs mirrored the bone Marowak typically carries, while the other bore a resemblance to Cubone's bone. The Bag sprite for the Thick Club was identical to that of the Rare Bone up until the release of Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. This item has been consistently available from Generation II through Generation IX.

Title: Red Scarf

Introduced in Generation III, the Red Scarf (Japanese: あかいバンダナ Red Bandanna) is an equipable item that Pokémon can hold, primarily designed to augment the holder's Coolness attribute within a Pokémon Contest. Specifically, when a Pokémon holds this accessory, it elevates its Coolness during key phases: the initial round of a standard Pokémon Contest, the Visual Competition stage of a Super Contest, and the Introduction Round within a Contest Spectacular. During Generation III gameplay, this enhancement specifically adds 20 points to the Coolness stat, capping its total at 255 points. Notably, prior to his participation in the Master Rank Contest, Ruby acquired all five distinct scarves. He strategically employed these items to boost the corresponding conditions for five of his Pokémon, a tactic that resulted in each Pokémon's Contest victory. This achievement completed Ruby's segment of the 80-day challenge alongside Sapphire. In particular, he equipped the Red Scarf to improve Nana's Coolness. Furthermore, it is plausible that the inclusion of the Red Scarf as a held item in certain Manaphy distributions alludes to May's bandana, which Manaphy wears in the film *Pokémon Ranger and the Temple of the Sea*.

Title: Blue Scarf

First appearing in Generation III, the Blue Scarf (known as あおいバンダナ or Blue Bandanna in Japanese) functions as a held item. Its primary effect is to boost a Pokémon's Beauty during a Pokémon Contest. Specifically, its presence enhances the Beauty stat for the primary round of a Pokémon Contest, the Visual Competition within a Super Contest, and the Introduction Round of a Contest Spectacular. During Generation III, this bonus specifically added 20 points, potentially pushing the total up to a maximum of 255 points.

Prior to his participation in the Master Rank Contest, Ruby received all five scarves. He strategically employed these items to augment the specific conditions of five of his Pokémon, resulting in each of them securing a Contest victory. This accomplishment allowed Ruby to complete his segment of the 80-day challenge alongside Sapphire. For instance, he utilized the Blue Scarf to specifically boost Feefee's Beauty.

Title: Pink Scarf

The Pink Scarf (Japanese: ピンクのバンダナ Pink Bandanna) is a held item originating in Generation III. This accessory elevates the holder's Cuteness attribute within Pokémon Contests. When a Pokémon equips it, its Cuteness receives a boost during the primary round of a Pokémon Contest, the Visual Competition of a Super Contest, and the Introduction Round of a Contest Spectacular. In Generation III, this increase amounts to 20 points, capping the total at 255 points. Before his participation in the Master Rank Contest, Ruby was provided with all five condition-enhancing scarves. He then used these items to improve the respective traits of five of his Pokémon, which led each to win its Contest and fulfill Ruby's portion of the 80-day challenge with Sapphire. Specifically, he applied the Pink Scarf to boost Kiki's Cuteness.

Title: Green Scarf

Appearing first in Generation III, the Green Scarf (Japanese: みどりのバンダナ Green Bandanna) is a type of held item. Its primary function is to boost the Cleverness of the Pokémon carrying it during a Pokémon Contest. When equipped, it specifically elevates a Pokémon's Cleverness during the primary round of a Pokémon Contest, the Visual Competition of a Super Contest, and the Introduction Round of a Contest Spectacular. Within Generation III games, this enhancement provides 20 additional points, up to a total of 255 points. Before contending in the Master Rank Contest, Ruby was presented with all five scarves. He strategically employed these to augment the relevant conditions of five of his Pokémon, leading all of them to secure Contest victories and thereby fulfilling Ruby's part of the 80-day challenge with Sapphire. Among these, he utilized the Green Scarf to enhance Fofo's Smartness.

Title: Yellow Scarf

Known in Japanese as きいろのバンダナ (Yellow Bandanna), the Yellow Scarf first appeared in Generation III as a type of held item. Its primary function is to boost the Toughness of the Pokémon holding it during Pokémon Contests. Specifically, a Pokémon equipped with this item receives a Toughness increment in the initial stage of a standard Pokémon Contest, the Visual Competition of a Super Contest, and the Introduction Round of a Contest Spectacular. During Generation III, this enhancement amounted to an extra 20 points, pushing the Toughness score towards a maximum of 255 points. In a notable event, Ruby acquired all five Contest scarves before facing the Master Rank Contest. He strategically utilized these items to boost the corresponding conditions of five of his Pokémon, ultimately securing victories for each in their Contests and successfully completing his segment of the 80-day challenge alongside Sapphire. The Yellow Scarf, in particular, was applied to strengthen Mumu's Toughness stat.

Title: Wide Lens

The Wide Lens (Japanese: こうかくレンズ Wide-Angle Lens) is a held item that first appeared in Generation IV, providing a slight enhancement to the equipping Pokémon's hit rate. This item improves the holder's precision by 10%, a gain numerically represented as 4505/4096 from Generation V onwards. In the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Wide Lens made its debut as a Pokémon Tool card during the Japanese XY Era. Its initial release occurred in the Japanese Emerald Break expansion, subsequently making its English debut within the Roaring Skies expansion. When this specific Pokémon Tool card is affixed to a Pokémon, any of that Pokémon's attacks aimed at Benched Pokémon will correctly factor in Weakness and Resistance, a mechanic usually disregarded for those on the bench.

Title: Muscle Band

First appearing as a held item in Generation IV, the Muscle Band (Japanese: ちからのハチマキ Power Headband) is an accessory a Pokémon can carry. Its primary effect is to augment the might of the Pokémon's physical moves. Specifically, it elevates the damage of these physical attacks by 10%, a value precisely implemented as a 4505/4096 multiplier from Generation V through Pokémon Scarlet and Violet.

Within Pokémon UNITE, the Muscle Band functions as a held item enabling its wielder to inflict extra damage via basic attacks, scaling with the target's remaining HP (subject to a 0.35s cooldown per target), while also enhancing their Attack and Attack Speed. This supplementary damage is 1%, 2%, or 3% of the target's current HP at grades 1, 10, and 20 respectively, capped at a maximum of 120, 240, or 360 at those same grades. Concurrently, it boosts Attack by 1% and Attack Speed by 0% at Grade 1, escalating to an increase of 15% for Attack and 7.5% for Attack Speed at Grade 30.

As an Item card, the Muscle Band debuted in the Pokémon Trading Card Game within the English XY Series (known as the Japanese XY Era) as part of the XY expansion. When a Pokémon in the TCG holds the Muscle Band, its attacks inflict an additional 20 damage upon the opponent's Active Pokémon, prior to any Weakness and Resistance calculations. Furthermore, an image of the Muscle Band is visible in the artwork for Pikachu (BW-P Promo 120).

Title: Wise Glasses

Wise Glasses (Japanese: ものしりメガネ Wise Glasses) are a held item first introduced in Generation IV. This item bolsters the power of the equipped Pokémon's special moves. The boost to the holder's special moves is a 10% increase, which has been precisely approximated as a multiplier of 4505/4096 from Generation V up to Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Within the game Pokémon UNITE, the Wise Glasses function as an equipable accessory that enhances the user's Sp. Attack, leveraging both additive and multiplicative effects. At Grade 1, the additive increase is +10, growing to +39 by Grade 30. Correspondingly, the multiplicative increase offers a 3% bonus at Grade 1, 5% at Grade 10, and 7% at Grade 20.

Title: Expert Belt

Originating in Generation IV, the Expert Belt (Japanese: たつじんのおび Expert Obi) is a held item crafted to enhance the power of supereffective attacks. It boosts the damage of the holder's supereffective moves by 20%; this enhancement is implemented using a 4915/4096 multiplier in games from Generation V through Pokémon Scarlet and Violet.

The Expert Belt makes an appearance in *Toasty!! Battle Against Toxapex*, where Sonia discovered it in Turffield by following hints provided by a little girl. Subsequently, in *Stealth!! Infiltrate the Energy Plant*, she passed the item to Henry. He, in turn, equipped it to his Sirfetch'd, Lancelot, for use during his Gym battle against Opal.

In the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Expert Belt was introduced as a Pokémon Tool Trainer card during the English Diamond & Pearl Series (known as the Japanese DP Era), featuring in the Arceus expansion. The Pokémon to which this card is attached receives +20 HP, and its attacks deal 20 more damage to the opposing Active Pokémon (before applying Weakness and Resistance). A significant drawback, however, is that if the Pokémon equipped with this card is Knocked Out, the opponent claims 1 more Prize card.

Title: Light Clay

Originating in Generation IV, Light Clay (Japanese: ひかりのねんど Light Clay) serves as a held item. When a Pokémon carrying this item executes Light Screen, Reflect, or Aurora Veil, the duration of these move effects is prolonged to eight turns, rather than their usual five. Should one of these moves be appropriated via Snatch, the enhanced duration is solely contingent on the Pokémon *ultimately using the move* possessing the Light Clay, irrespective of the original user's item status. Within Generation VII titles, if Light Clay extends these effects, the user interface's turn counter will show an 8-turn duration for the player whose Pokémon activated the move; their opponent's display, however, will initially indicate only 5 turns. Following the completion of five turns of the effect, the interface then updates to consistently display an 8-turn duration for both participating players.

Title: Life Orb

This accessory, known in Japanese as いのちのたま (Life Orb), was introduced in Generation IV as a held item. It enhances the might of the Pokémon's attacks, yet in turn inflicts harm upon the user whenever a damaging maneuver is performed. Specifically, it elevates the damage output of the Pokémon's damaging moves by a precise 30%. Following the execution of a damaging move, the Pokémon holding it suffers recoil damage equivalent to 10% of its total maximum HP (this value is rounded down, though it will never be less than 1).

The power increase does not apply to moves that operate outside the standard damage calculation formula (though the holder still endures the recoil), nor does it affect self-inflicted confusion damage. When a Pokémon successfully acquires a Life Orb through moves like Thief or Covet, the Orb itself does not empower the stealing move; however, the attacker still takes damage from the use of the move that secured the item. Its effect triggers prior to the ability Pickpocket; consequently, should an attacking Pokémon steal a Life Orb via Pickpocket, the original holder's Life Orb will still cause damage to the attacker for that specific move. Similarly, if a Pokémon employing the Magician ability takes a Life Orb, it will incur damage from the very move used to obtain the item. Should the wielder unleash Fling, the Life Orb will augment that move's power without causing damage to the user.

This item's activation sequence occurs subsequent to the holder receiving damage from sources such as Iron Barbs and Rocky Helmet. Furthermore, its effect triggers after the holder restores HP with a draining move, or after suffering damage from general recoil and the Rough Skin ability. For multi-hit attacks, the holder's damage is applied only after the final strike has landed. Given that Life Orb causes indirect damage, a Pokémon possessing the Magic Guard ability will be entirely shielded from its detrimental effects. A Pokémon with Sheer Force will avoid Life Orb damage for any move that ability enhances. When held by a Dynamax Pokémon, the inflicted damage is calculated as 10% of the Pokémon's maximum HP *before* it Dynamaxes.

Historically, the Life Orb would inflict damage upon its holder without any on-screen message appearing. Previously, moves like Future Sight and Doom Desire would not cause the user to take Life Orb damage, and striking a substitute formerly prevented the holder from suffering Life Orb damage. The 30% damage amplification, since its introduction, is technically approximated as a 5324/4096 multiplier (with the specific exception of Pokémon Legends: Z-A, where the multiplier is precisely 1.3 once more). Currently, the Life Orb *does* display an on-screen message when its damage effect triggers. As of recent changes, if a Pokémon holds a Life Orb when its previously-used Future Sight or Doom Desire inflicts damage (even if the Orb was not held at the time of the move's initial use), it will now suffer the associated Life Orb damage. Furthermore, the Life Orb now inflicts damage upon its holder even when the target is a substitute. The Life Orb *does* indeed amplify the damage output of Future Sight and Doom Desire.

Title: Power Herb

Introduced in Generation IV, the Power Herb (Japanese: パワフルハーブ Powerful Herb) is a distinct held item. It enables its holder to execute an attack that typically requires a preparatory turn to be performed in a single turn. Should a Pokémon employ an attack with a charging requirement (excluding Sky Drop), the Power Herb is promptly used up following the charge, which allows the attack's final stage to commence without delay. Crucially, this accelerated execution does not cancel the charging stage itself; therefore, stat buffs like the Defense increase from Skull Bash, or the Special Attack boosts from Meteor Beam and Electro Shot, are still granted. Conversely, the item remains unconsumed if a Pokémon uses Solar Beam or Solar Blade during harsh sunlight, or Electro Shot during rain.

In the manga chapter The Tournament Continues, Cheren's Unfezant famously employed a Power Herb to bypass Sky Attack's typical charging phase, leading to a quick victory against Leo's Deino. Additionally, in Flash and Cosmog's Secret, Moon deduced the Totem Lurantis she was training against held a Power Herb, noting its ability to instantly use Solar Blade without any initial charge-up.

Title: Toxic Orb

The Toxic Orb (Japanese: どくどくだま Toxic Orb) functions as a held item that debuted in Generation IV. It causes the holder to become badly poisoned. Upon turn completion, this item applies the badly poisoned status to its bearer. Its activation occurs after a Pokémon would normally suffer poison damage, consequently, no poison damage is incurred during the initial turn it receives the affliction from the Toxic Orb. The Toxic Orb's effect also triggers after a Pokémon is relieved of its status condition through affection, the Hydration Ability, or the Shed Skin Ability. Notably, it can even inflict poison on a Poison-type or Steel-type holder, provided its Ability is Corrosion.

Title: Flame Orb

A held item first introduced in Generation IV, the Flame Orb (Japanese: かえんだま Flame Orb) inflicts a burn upon its wielder. This burn specifically applies at the conclusion of each turn. The Flame Orb's activation point is set to occur after the moment a Pokémon would typically receive burn damage. As a result, a Pokémon does not take burn damage on the very turn it becomes burned by this item. Moreover, the Orb's effect triggers after a Pokémon is healed of a status condition through mechanisms such as affection, the Hydration Ability, or the Shed Skin Ability.

Title: Focus Sash

Debuting as a held item in Generation IV, the Focus Sash (known in Japanese as きあいのタスキ, or Fighting Spirit Sash) prevents its holder from fainting from a single attack. If the Pokémon carrying this item has its full HP and is struck by a move that would ordinarily cause it to be knocked out (this includes one-hit knockout moves), the Focus Sash allows it to endure with just 1 HP. It also provides protection against damage caused by self-inflicted confusion. In its original generation, it could shield against all strikes of a multi-strike move, though it offers no defense from damage originating from Future Sight or Doom Desire. Once activated, the Focus Sash is expended and vanishes.

However, starting from Generation V, the Focus Sash's effect on multi-strike moves was modified to only protect its holder from the first strike. Furthermore, a consumed Focus Sash will now be restored after the conclusion of a battle.

In the manga chapter Walkthrough! Battle Tower!!, Yū had equipped his Infernape with a Focus Sash, which enabled it to survive a Golem's Earthquake at the Battle Tower and subsequently secure victory with a Grass Knot. Darach's Houndoom was also observed using a Focus Sash in Getting the Drop on Gallade II, which preserved it from being knocked out by a supereffective Focus Blast from Platinum's Lopunny. In the very same chapter, Platinum was revealed to have secured a rented Focus Sash for her Empoleon to hold during the Battle Castle challenge.

The Focus Sash was later introduced as a Pokémon Tool card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game, appearing during the Japanese XY Era (which corresponds to the English XY Series). It was first unveiled in the Japanese Rising Fist expansion and the English Furious Fists expansion, adorned with artwork by Toyste Beach. In Japan, it received a reprint later in The Best of XY subset. Within the TCG, if a Fighting-type Pokémon card with this particular card attached has full HP and is about to be Knocked Out by damage from an opponent's Pokémon's attack, it is not Knocked Out; instead, its remaining HP becomes 10. Subsequently, the Focus Sash card is detached from that Pokémon and moved to the discard pile. The Focus Sash has additionally been featured across various forms of officially licensed merchandise.

Title: Zoom Lens

Hailing from Generation IV, the Zoom Lens (known in Japan as フォーカスレンズ Focus Lens) is an equipable item designed to enhance the accuracy of the Pokémon holding it. Specifically, it provides a 20% boost to the accuracy stat (represented as a 4915/4096 multiplier from Generation V onwards) when targeting an opponent who has already acted during that turn. Within the pages of Magnificent Meditite & Really Riolu I, a Zoom Lens was obtained by Diamond as a prize at the Veilstone Game Corner. He acquired it using Coins that Platinum had previously won from the slot machines. Subsequently, Platinum equipped the Zoom Lens onto Chimler for training purposes. Notably, the item's in-game description in Diamond and Pearl contains an error, stating it enhances the critical hit ratio instead of its true effect of improving accuracy.

Title: Iron Ball

Introduced as a held item in Generation IV, the Iron Ball (くろいてっきゅう Black Iron Ball in Japanese) exerts specific effects on its holder. It grounds the Pokémon carrying it and causes its Speed to decrease. More precisely, the Iron Ball halves the holder's Speed and renders it grounded. This grounded status makes the Pokémon vulnerable to damaging Ground-type moves; Rototiller; Arena Trap; the entry hazards Spikes, Toxic Spikes, and Sticky Web; and terrain. This vulnerability persists even if the Pokémon is Flying-type, possesses the Ability Levitate, or is under the influence of Telekinesis or Magnet Rise. Notably, an Iron Ball does not impede the successful use of Magnet Rise or Telekinesis.

When held by a Flying-type Pokémon, it takes 1× damage from Ground-type moves if its secondary type does not inherently resist or possess a weakness to Ground-type attacks. The item's Speed-halving effect remains active even if the holder has Klutz or is affected by Embargo. More generally, a Flying-type Pokémon equipped with an Iron Ball will sustain 1× damage from Ground-type moves, irrespective of any other types it may possess. However, these effects are suspended during Inverse Battles or if the holder is already grounded by another means. All of the Iron Ball's effects are nullified by Klutz, Embargo, and Magic Room.

In the lore of Diamond and Pearl, an Iron Ball was discovered among metal items pulled from the ground in Celestic Town by the magnetic field emanating from Cyrus's Magnezone. It later appeared as an item used in conjunction with Lax's Fling during Platinum's Gym battle against Fantina in Mirages of Mismagius II. When thrown, the Iron Ball enables Fling to achieve its highest base power of 130. In Pokémon Sword and Shield, this base power status is shared by TR43 (Overheat) and TR71 (Leaf Storm), which contain moves with 130 base power. Starting from these same games, Big Nugget also attains this equivalent status. In Pokkén Tournament, if Machamp is selected as the character, it will occasionally throw two Iron Balls three times before the fight commences.

Title: Lagging Tail

This held item, first appearing in Generation IV, carries the Japanese name こうこうのしっぽ (Second-Batter Tail). It imposes a significant delay on the Pokémon equipped with it, ensuring that Pokémon acts last within its move's priority bracket, utterly disregarding the Pokémon's Speed stat. Its effect remains constant and is not influenced by the move Trick Room. Should a Lagging Tail be acquired, removed, or suppressed mid-battle, its impact on turn order will not manifest until the commencement of the subsequent turn. When several Pokémon equipped with either a Lagging Tail or Full Incense utilize moves sharing the same priority level, the Pokémon possessing the lesser Speed stat will take its turn initially, irrespective of Trick Room's presence. Notably, a Pokémon benefiting from the Ability Stall will execute its action prior to any Pokémon holding a Lagging Tail or Full Incense. However, if a Pokémon possesses the Stall Ability *and* is simultaneously holding a Lagging Tail or Full Incense, the Stall Ability will cease to influence the Pokémon's turn order. In scenarios where multiple Pokémon, either possessing the Ability Stall or holding a Lagging Tail or Full Incense, employ moves of identical priority, their actions will be ordered according to their relative Speed stats; this relative order is, however, inverted if Trick Room is active.

Title: Destiny Knot

The Destiny Knot (Japanese: あかいいと Red Thread), a held item, debuted in Generation IV. If its bearer becomes infatuated, the Pokémon it is infatuated with simultaneously becomes infatuated. As of Generation VI, when a Pokémon carrying this item is placed in a Day Care, Nursery, or participates in a picnic, any resulting Eggs will inherit 5 individual values (IVs) randomly from either parent, rather than the usual 3. Each IV is chosen independently from a random parent.

Title: Black Sludge

Making its debut in Generation IV, the Black Sludge (くろいヘドロ Black Sludge in Japanese) serves as a held item. Its impact varies: it restores HP for Poison-type Pokémon that hold it, but inflicts harm on all other types. When a Poison-type Pokémon is its current bearer, it recovers 1/16 of its maximum HP (rounded down, though never less than 1 HP) at the conclusion of each turn. Conversely, if a Pokémon without the Poison typing holds this item, it endures damage equal to 1/8 of its total HP (also rounded down, but never below 1 HP) at the end of every turn. Pokémon equipped with the Ability Magic Guard are entirely immune to this particular damage. In Generation IV, the Black Sludge's healing properties functioned regardless of Heal Block. However, from Generation V onward, it cannot restore health to Pokémon afflicted by Heal Block. Within Pokémon: Magikarp Jump, the Black Sludge is recognized as a Friendship Item, available for purchase at a price of 600. Obtaining it results in Gengar appearing as a helper Pokémon.

Title: Icy Rock

Known in Japanese as つめたいいわ (Icy Rock), this held item first appeared in Generation IV. Its primary function is to prolong the effects of either hail or snow. When a Pokémon equipped with an Icy Rock initiates a hailstorm using the moves Hail or Max Hailstorm, or via the Ability Snow Warning (a mechanic that began in Generation VI), the hailstorm will persist for eight turns rather than the standard five. In Generation IX, however, due to the absence of hail, the Icy Rock's effect shifts to extending snow, which can be created by the moves Snowscape or Chilly Reception, or the Ability Snow Warning.

Within Generation VII titles, should hail's duration be augmented by an Icy Rock, the in-game user interface will initially show an 8-turn duration for the player who initiated the effect, but only a 5-turn duration for their adversary. Once five turns of hail have elapsed, the user interface will then consistently reflect an 8-turn duration for both players.

Specifically in *Omega Ruby* and *Alpha Sapphire*, a unique condition involves the Icy Rock: if a nicknamed Regice, holding this item, is present in the player's party alongside Regirock and Registeel, it facilitates the appearance of Regigigas within the Island Cave. This encounter, however, is not possible during nighttime hours or prior to defeating or capturing either Groudon or Kyogre.

In *Pokémon Quest*, the Icy Rock functions as an ingredient for cooking, a process that entices wild Pokémon. It is categorized as a blue, hard, precious mineral ingredient. Players can acquire Icy Rocks as a potential reward from expeditions undertaken on Tumblecube Island. Given their blue designation, Icy Rocks are most frequently discovered in zones such as Backforth Brook, Belly Button Cave, and Hushed Highlands. Additionally, there's a chance to obtain them randomly by recycling Power Stones.

Title: Smooth Rock

The Smooth Rock (Japanese: さらさらいわ Smooth Rock) is a held item that debuted in Generation IV. Its primary function is to prolong the duration of sandstorms. When a Pokémon carrying this item initiates a sandstorm through the moves Sandstorm or Max Rockfall, or the Abilities Sand Stream or Sand Spit, the weather condition will persist for eight turns instead of the standard five. A notable exception exists: prior to Generation VI, this item had no influence on Sand Stream, as that Ability inherently caused sandstorms to last indefinitely in those games. Specifically in the Generation VII games, should a sandstorm's duration be extended by the Smooth Rock, the user interface's turn counter will initially indicate 8 turns for the player whose Pokémon generated the effect, while showing only 5 turns for their opponent. Nevertheless, after five turns of the sandstorm have elapsed, the user interface will then update to display 8 turns for both players.

Title: Heat Rock

As a held item first introduced in Generation IV, the Heat Rock (known in Japanese as あついいわ, or Hot Rock) primarily functions to prolong the effect of harsh sunlight. When a Pokémon holding this item summons harsh sunlight—whether via the moves Sunny Day or Max Flare, or through the Abilities Drought or Orichalcum Pulse—the weather condition will endure for eight turns, rather than the typical five. (It should be noted that before Generation VI, this item had no influence on Drought, as that Ability created harsh sunlight of indefinite length in those earlier game installments.) Within Generation VII games, if harsh sunlight's duration is extended by the Heat Rock, the user interface's turn counter for these effects will show 8 turns to the player whose Pokémon activated the move, yet simultaneously display 5 turns to their opponent. Once five turns of harsh sunlight have transpired, the user interface will then uniformly indicate an 8-turn duration for both players.

Title: Damp Rock

Debuting in Generation IV, the Damp Rock (しめったいわ Damp Rock in Japanese) functions as a held item. Its primary purpose is to prolong the effect of rain. When a Pokémon equipped with this item initiates rain via the moves Rain Dance or Max Geyser, or through the Drizzle Ability, the inclement weather persists for eight turns rather than the standard five. (It's worth noting that before Generation VI, this item had no impact on Drizzle, as that Ability generated rain of indefinite duration in those particular titles.) Within Generation VII games, when the Damp Rock prolongs rain, the user interface's turn counter for such effects initially shows an 8-turn duration for the player whose Pokémon initiated the move, but only a 5-turn duration for their adversary. However, once five turns of rain have elapsed, the display then updates to indicate an 8-turn duration for both participants. Furthermore, in the title Pokémon: Magikarp Jump, the Damp Rock is categorized as a Friendship Item, available for purchase at 350, and its function is to call forth Slowpoke as a helper Pokémon. Lastly, The Pokémon Company International chose the Damp Rock to be featured alongside Pelipper within their Pokémon Held Item Pins merchandise collection.

Title: Choice Scarf

An accessory held by Pokémon, the Choice Scarf, known in Japanese as こだわりスカーフ (Fixation Scarf), debuted in Generation IV. Its effect enhances the wielder's Speed stat, yet restricts them to executing a single move repeatedly. This item has two equivalent items, the Choice Band and Choice Specs, which provide boosts to alternative stats. A Pokémon equipping it gains a 50% increase to its Speed, but is subsequently limited to using only the first attack chosen. This particular effect ceases once the Pokémon is switched out of battle.

During Pokémon Diamond and Pearl, a glitch existed where if a Pokémon utilized U-turn and then switched into another Pokémon that was holding a Choice Scarf and also knew U-turn, the incoming Pokémon would become restricted to U-turn. This issue was rectified in all later game iterations. Beginning with Generation V, a Pokémon already constrained to a move by a Choice Scarf, if that item is taken away while its influence is temporarily absent, becomes susceptible to the Choice item lock glitch. Should a Pokémon wielding a Choice Scarf attempt an action different from its locked move (for instance, via Instruct or Dancer), that action will not succeed, and no Power Points will be expended.

When a Pokémon equipped with a Choice Scarf performs Dynamax, the item's properties are suspended for the duration; its stat boost becomes inactive, and the Pokémon is freed from its move constraints. Upon the conclusion of Dynamax, the Speed bonus is reinstated, and any prior move restriction resumes. Within Pokémon: Magikarp Jump, the Choice Scarf functions as a Friendship Item, available for acquisition at a cost of 600. It has the unique ability to call forth Gardevoir to assist as a helper Pokémon. This item has also appeared in various officially sanctioned merchandise.

Title: Sticky Barb

An item originating from Generation IV, the Sticky Barb (Japanese: くっつきバリ Sticky Barb) functions as a held item. It inflicts damage upon its current bearer at the end of each turn, but notably, it transfers to a new holder through physical contact. Specifically, at the conclusion of every turn, the Sticky Barb drains 12.5% of its holder's maximum HP. A unique transfer mechanic is triggered if a Pokémon lacking a held item strikes a Pokémon equipped with a Sticky Barb using a contact move, including Knock Off; the Sticky Barb will then migrate to the Pokémon that performed the attack. This transfer mechanism overrides even the Sticky Hold Ability of the original holder. However, the Sticky Barb's transfer is prevented if the intended recipient possesses a substitute. Furthermore, Pokémon endowed with the Magic Guard Ability are entirely immune to the Sticky Barb's damaging effects.

Title: Power Bracer

A held item that made its debut in Generation IV, the Power Bracer (Japanese: パワーリスト Power Wrist) is. This Power item grants Attack effort values to its bearer whenever experience is obtained. While engaged in battle, a Pokémon holding this item obtains 4 Attack EVs each time it earns experience (in Horde Battles, EVs are still given per Pokémon knocked out, even though experience is gained all at once); Pokérus doubles this effect. A specific game mechanic note: in Generation IV, level 100 Pokémon are unable to accrue EVs through battle; this changed from Generation V onwards, where they do accumulate EVs whenever they would normally receive experience. During battles, the Speed stat of the Pokémon holding this item is decreased by half. Currently, a Pokémon equipped with this item receives 8 Attack EVs instead of 4 for every instance of experience gain in battle. Commencing with Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, if a parent holds this item during breeding at either a Pokémon Day Care or a Pokémon Nursery, that parent's Attack IV is assured to be inherited by the offspring. However, if both parents are equipped with a Power Bracer, a random selection determines which parent's Attack IV is passed down.

Title: Power Belt

Introduced in Generation IV, the Power Belt (Japanese: パワーベルト Power Belt) functions as a specific held item. This particular Power item bestows Defense effort values upon the Pokémon carrying it each time experience is acquired. Specifically, any Pokémon equipped with this item receives 4 Defense EVs upon gaining experience from a battle encounter (note that in Horde Battles, EVs are still granted for every Pokémon defeated, even if experience is accumulated simultaneously); additionally, the effect is magnified to double by Pokérus. Historically, in Generation IV, Pokémon at level 100 were unable to acquire EVs through battle; however, beginning with Generation V, they do obtain EVs whenever they would normally earn experience. During combat, the Speed stat of the Pokémon carrying this Power Belt is reduced by half. Currently, a Pokémon equipped with this item obtains 8 Defense EVs, rather than 4, whenever it earns experience during battle. Commencing with Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, when this item is affixed to a parent Pokémon undergoing breeding at either a Pokémon Day Care or a Pokémon Nursery, the Defense IV of that particular parent is guaranteed to be inherited by the offspring. Should both parent Pokémon possess a Power Belt, a single parent will be randomly chosen to transmit its Defense IV.

Title: Power Lens

This held item, known in Japanese as パワーレンズ (Power Lens), made its debut in Generation IV. Classified as a Power item, it bestows Special Attack effort values (EVs) upon the Pokémon carrying it each time experience is acquired. While held, a Pokémon receives 4 Special Attack EVs for every instance of experience gain during a battle (note that in Horde Battles, EVs are granted per defeated Pokémon, even though experience accumulates simultaneously). The item's EV-granting effect is subject to doubling by Pokérus, allowing the Pokémon to subsequently obtain 8 Special Attack EVs instead of the standard 4 when experience is earned during battle. (A specific mechanic note: in Generation IV, Pokémon at level 100 were unable to earn EVs through battle; however, starting from Generation V, these Pokémon began to acquire EVs whenever they would otherwise earn experience.) A drawback of holding this item is that the Pokémon's Speed stat is reduced by half during combat. Beginning with Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, when breeding at a Pokémon Day Care or Pokémon Nursery, if a parent holds a Power Lens, its Special Attack IV is guaranteed to be inherited by the offspring. Should both parents be equipped with a Power Lens, the Special Attack IV from one of them will be transmitted to the child, chosen through a random process.

Title: Power Band

For its reference in the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, please consult Scarf (Mystery Dungeon) → Power Band. This held item, designated パワーバンド (Power Band) in Japanese, was initially introduced in Generation IV. Classified as a Power item, it bestows Special Defense effort values upon the holder whenever experience is gained. In battle, a Pokémon equipped with this item acquires 4 Special Defense EVs for each instance of experience gain (even if experience is received simultaneously in Horde Battles, EVs are awarded per fainted Pokémon); this effect is doubled if the Pokémon has Pokérus. (It's important to note that in Generation IV, level 100 Pokémon could not obtain EVs from battle; from Generation V onwards, however, they accrue EVs whenever they would earn experience.) The Speed stat of the Pokémon holding this item is halved during combat. Furthermore, a Pokémon holding this item now obtains 8 Special Defense EVs instead of 4 each time it gains experience during battle. Beginning with Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, if a parent Pokémon is carrying a Power Band during breeding at a Pokémon Day Care or a Pokémon Nursery, that parent's Special Defense IV will always be passed down to the offspring. If both parent Pokémon are holding a Power Band, one will be chosen at random to transmit its Special Defense IV.

Title: Power Anklet

Originating in Generation IV, the Power Anklet (Japanese: パワーアンクル Power Ankle) is a held item. This Power item's function is to bestow Speed effort values upon its wielder each time they accrue experience. A Pokémon equipped with this item initially acquired 4 Speed EVs whenever it gained experience in battle (with EVs being awarded for each Pokémon knocked out in Horde Battles, despite experience being gained all at once); this effect is doubled if the Pokémon has Pokérus. (In Generation IV, level 100 Pokémon were unable to gain EVs in battle; however, from Generation V onward, they acquire EVs whenever they would earn experience.) During active combat, the Speed stat of the Pokémon bearing this item is halved. As of a later update, a Pokémon holding this item now receives 8 Speed EVs instead of 4 whenever it earns experience during a fight. Furthermore, from Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver and beyond, when breeding at a Pokémon Day Care or Nursery, if a parent holds a Power Anklet, that parent's Speed Individual Value (IV) will unfailingly be passed down to the offspring. If both parents are holding a Power Anklet, one parent's Speed IV will be chosen at random for inheritance by the child.

Title: Power Weight

The Power Weight (Japanese: パワーウエイト Power Weight) is a particular type of held item that first appeared in Generation IV of the Pokémon games. Classified as a Power item, it functions by bestowing HP effort values (EVs) to the Pokémon holding it whenever that Pokémon acquires experience.

A Pokémon equipped with this item gains 4 HP EVs each time it earns experience in a battle. For Horde Battles, EVs are awarded for every Pokémon defeated, even if all experience is obtained at once. If the holder is afflicted with Pokérus, this EV gain is doubled. In Generation IV, Pokémon at level 100 were unable to accrue EVs from battle; however, from Generation V onward, they gain EVs whenever they would normally earn experience. A drawback of carrying this item is that the Pokémon's Speed stat is halved during battle. Currently, a Pokémon holding this item receives 8 HP EVs, an increase from the original 4, whenever it gains experience in battle.

Starting with Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, the Power Weight also plays a role in breeding. If a parent Pokémon holds this item at a Pokémon Day Care or a Pokémon Nursery, its HP Individual Value (IV) is guaranteed to be inherited by the offspring. Should both parents be holding a Power Weight, the child will inherit the HP IV from one of the parents, chosen at random.

Main article: Cynthia's Power Weight (Destined Rivals 162)

In the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Power Weight item was introduced as the Pokémon Tool card "Cynthia's Power Weight." It was released as part of the English Scarlet & Violet Series and the Japanese Scarlet & Violet Era. This card made its debut in the Japanese Hot Wind Arena subset and the English Destined Rivals expansion, featuring artwork created by Studio Bora Inc. It is designed to exclusively benefit Pokémon of the "Cynthia" Trainer's archetype in the TCG, boosting the maximum HP of the attached Cynthia's Pokémon by 70.

Title: Big Root

An accessory held by Pokémon, the Big Root (Japanese: おおきなねっこ Big Root) made its debut in Generation IV. When a Pokémon equips this item and utilizes a draining move or Strength Sap, it recovers 30% more HP than it would ordinarily. Furthermore, this item also grants an additional 30% HP recovery from the effects of Leech Seed, Ingrain, and Aqua Ring. From Generation V onward, this enhancement factor precisely translates to 5324/4096. Conversely, should the holder of a Big Root be targeted by Liquid Ooze, it will sustain amplified damage.

In Pokémon Quest, the Big Root serves as a cooking ingredient, instrumental in luring wild Pokémon. It can be acquired as a potential ingredient upon the successful completion of expeditions. Additionally, recycling Power Stones offers a chance to randomly yield this item. The item's textual description inaccurately claims it "boosts the power of HP-stealing moves"; in reality, its effect is solely to augment the HP recovered by the user, rather than to inflict more damage upon the target. This misleading description remained uncorrected until the release of Pokémon Sun and Moon.

Title: Choice Specs

This held item, known in Japanese as こだわりメガネ (Fixation Glasses), made its debut in Generation IV of the Pokémon series. Its primary effect is to augment the Special Attack stat of the Pokémon equipping it, though this comes at the cost of restricting the Pokémon to a single designated move. Two related items exist that enhance alternative stats: the Choice Band and the Choice Scarf. Upon being held by a Pokémon, it confers a substantial 50% increase to that Pokémon's Special Attack, but subsequently limits its move usage to only the initial one chosen. This restrictive effect is lifted and reset once the Pokémon holding it is switched out.

Notably, a glitch present in Pokémon Diamond and Pearl caused a specific interaction: if a Pokémon executed U-turn, then switched into another Pokémon equipped with Choice Specs which *also* knew U-turn, the newly switched-in Pokémon would become locked into using only U-turn. This particular issue was rectified in all subsequent game releases. Beginning with Generation V, a specific scenario triggers the Choice item lock glitch: should a Pokémon already be bound to a single move because of Choice Specs, and then the Choice Specs itself is removed *while* its locking effect is negated, the Pokémon will still experience the consequences of this item lock glitch. Should a Pokémon holding Choice Specs attempt to use a move different from the one it is currently locked into (e.g., via the moves Instruct or Dancer), that attempt will fail, and no Power Points (PP) will be consumed. Dynamaxed Pokémon exhibit immunity to the influence of Choice Specs; neither the move-locking restriction nor the stat enhancement applies to them. If a Pokémon initially locked into a move by Choice Specs then undergoes Dynamax, upon the conclusion of its Dynamax state, it reverts to being restricted to only the move it was previously bound to.

Within the realm of Pokémon UNITE, the Choice Specs functions as a held item that periodically, every 8 seconds, amplifies damage dealt to a single target. This damage amplification amounts to 40/50/60 plus 30/35/40% of the holder's Special Attack, corresponding to item grades 1, 10, and 20 in sequence. Furthermore, the item itself contributes to an increase in Special Attack, providing a bonus of +10 at Grade 1 and +39 when it reaches Grade 30.

During the "Yū vs. Shin, Rival Showdown!!" battle, Shin equipped his Wailord with Choice Specs, which significantly boosted its Water Spout, enabling it to overcome Yū's Heatran. Nevertheless, this very tactic was cleverly countered by Yū, who deployed his Quagsire; Quagsire's Water Absorb Ability effectively nullified Water Spout's potency, thereby securing a victory for Yū against his opponent. The Choice Specs have additionally appeared as a motif in various officially sanctioned merchandise items.

Title: Flame Plate

Known in Japanese as ひのたまプレート (Fireball Plate), the Flame Plate is a held item that made its debut in Generation IV. Its classification shifts to a Key Item specifically in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. This item belongs to the category of Plates, all of which share a connection with Arceus. When held, it enhances the power of the Pokémon's Fire-type moves by 20%. Should the holder employ Judgment, that move transforms into a Fire-type attack. An Arceus clutching this item will have its Multitype ability activate, converting it into its Fire-type manifestation. Starting from Generation V, various item-manipulating effects, including moves like Trick and Fling, are rendered ineffective at either bestowing a Plate upon Arceus or dislodging one from it. During Generation IV, Pokémon possessing the Multitype ability were impervious to item-manipulating effects.
