Specifically, for Arceus, Multitype enables a transformation into its Fire-type configuration when this Plate is equipped. Within Pokémon Legends: Arceus, one can directly utilize the Flame Plate from the satchel on Arceus itself, thereby altering its type to Fire. This particular Plate bears a readable inscription that states: "The power of defeated giants infuses this Plate." In Pokémon: Magikarp Jump, the Flame Plate functions as a Friendship Item, available for purchase at a cost of 250, and its effect is to summon Litten as a helper Pokémon.

The Flame Plate, among the 15 other Plates then in existence, featured in the film *Arceus and the Jewel of Life*. Here, Arceus employed them to manipulate its type, and nearly perished after sacrificing all Plates to obliterate a meteor endangering what would later be known as Michina Town. One of these lost Plates was discovered by Damos, who subsequently restored it to Arceus. This act reinvigorated Arceus, allowing it to retrieve the remaining 15 Plates. A flashback sequence in the movie *Hoopa and the Clash of Ages* showcased the Flame Plate, the Splash Plate, and the Earth Plate. In this scene, Ghris was depicted leveraging their collective energies to forge the Prison Bottle. During *The Arceus Chronicles (Part 1)*, it came to light that Team Galactic had obtained a Flame Plate. They then employed it, alongside a Heatran captured by Saturn, to energize a contraption designed to resurrect Cyrus. Nevertheless, by *The Arceus Chronicles (Part 2)*, the device malfunctioned and spectacularly backfired, resulting in Heatran's rampage and its subsequent consumption of the Flame Plate. Ultimately, in *The Arceus Chronicles (Part 4)*, Arceus successfully reclaimed the Flame Plate.

Petrel was in the process of gathering Plates as part of Team Rocket's larger scheme. Following Silver's theft of the limited number of Plates Petrel had managed to locate, Petrel opted to permit Silver to amass all 16 Plates, intending to repossess them simultaneously at a later stage. Through his Weavile's strategic message delivery to Sneasel and Weavile throughout the Johto region, Silver soon successfully accumulated all 16 Plates for his own possession. At the Sinjoh Ruins, Gold yielded the Plates to Archer, who then appeared to exert control over Arceus with them, commanding it to conjure anew Dialga, Palkia, and Giratina. When the Pokédex holders unleashed their Pokémon's most powerful assaults to thwart the creation, Arceus absorbed every single Plate. The Flame Plate's image can be found in the artwork of the AR3 Arceus card, which originates from the Platinum: Arceus expansion.

Title: Splash Plate

Introduced in Generation IV, the Splash Plate (known in Japanese as しずくプレート, or Water Drop Plate) functions as a held item. Within Pokémon Legends: Arceus, it takes on the role of a Key Item. This item belongs to the category of Plates, all of which share a connection with Arceus. Its primary effect is to amplify the power of Water-type moves executed by the Pokémon holding it by 20%. Should the holder unleash Judgment, that move transforms into a Water-type attack. The Ability Multitype enables an Arceus equipped with this Plate to assume its Water-type form. Beginning with Generation V, item-manipulation techniques like Trick or Fling are incapable of bestowing a Plate upon Arceus or dislodging one from it. In contrast, during Generation IV, item-manipulating effects were universally ineffective against any Pokémon possessing the Multitype Ability. Furthermore, in Pokémon Legends: Arceus, one can utilize the Splash Plate directly from the satchel on Arceus itself, resulting in its transformation to the Water typing. The Plate bears a legible inscription, stating: "The rightful bearer of a Plate draws from the Plate it holds."

Within the game Pokémon: Magikarp Jump, the Splash Plate functions as a Friendship Item, obtainable for 500, and it has the effect of summoning Popplio to act as a helper Pokémon.

The Splash Plate first appeared in the episode Journey to the Unown!, making its debut alongside the Draco Plate and Iron Plate. Here, Saturn and a pair of Team Galactic Grunts positioned these items within a chamber inside the Solaceon Ruins, aiming to acquire the Spear Key and rouse the resident Unown. These Plates collectively amplified the powers associated with the Dragon, Steel, and Water types of Palkia and Dialga. Its next appearance was in the film Arceus and the Jewel of Life, where it was featured with the 15 other Plates then in existence. Arceus employed these to alter its type, and nearly perished after misplacing all the Plates subsequent to annihilating a meteor threatening the future site of Michina Town. Damos discovered one of these missing Plates and gave it back to Arceus, thereby restoring its strength sufficiently to retrieve the remaining 15 Plates. This gesture earned Damos Arceus's trust, leading the Alpha Pokémon to separate the Earth Plate, Splash Plate, Meadow Plate, Zap Plate, and Draco Plate, forming them into the Jewel of Life for Damos to enrich the land. The Splash Plate's inclusion in the Jewel of Life symbolized water.

A flashback sequence in Hoopa and the Clash of Ages depicted the Splash Plate, the Flame Plate, and the Earth Plate together, as Ghris was observed leveraging their combined energies to forge the Prison Bottle. During The Arceus Chronicles (Part 4), Arceus specifically selected the Splash Plate from its collection of 17 Plates to adopt the Water typing, subsequently encasing a rampaging Heatran within a watery dome.

Petrel's objective was to gather the Plates as part of Team Rocket's broader scheme. When Silver purloined the handful of Plates Petrel had acquired, Petrel opted to allow Silver to amass all 16 Plates, intending to reclaim them all simultaneously later on. Through his Weavile dispatching messages to other Sneasel and Weavile throughout Johto, Silver successfully assembled all 16 Plates for his own possession. Within the Sinjoh Ruins, Gold yielded the Plates to Archer, who then employed them to ostensibly gain command over Arceus, instructing it to manifest new versions of Dialga, Palkia, and Giratina. Upon the Pokédex holders deploying their Pokémon's most powerful assaults to halt this creation, every single Plate was reabsorbed into Arceus.

The Splash Plate also notably appears in the artwork for the AR4 Arceus card, which belongs to the Platinum: Arceus expansion.

Title: Zap Plate

Known in Japanese as いかずちプレート (Lightning Plate), the Zap Plate is an equipable item first introduced in Generation IV, one of several "Plates" deeply connected to Arceus. When a Pokémon holds this item, its Electric-type moves gain a 20% power increase. Should the holder utilize the move Judgment, that attack transforms into an Electric-type move. Moreover, an Arceus equipped with this Plate will, via its Multitype ability, convert into its Electric-type manifestation. In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, this Plate functions as a Key Item and can be directly deployed from the player's satchel on Arceus to alter its type to Electric. Regarding item manipulation, beginning with Generation V, techniques such as Trick and Fling are incapable of bestowing or removing a Plate from Arceus. In Generation IV, item-altering effects could not impact any Pokémon with the Multitype ability.

The Zap Plate itself bears an inscription that reads: "The third being raged, raining down bolts of anger." This artifact, alongside the 15 other Plates then in existence, featured prominently in the film *Arceus and the Jewel of Life*. In the movie, Arceus employed these Plates to modify its type and nearly met its end after losing them all, having just obliterated a meteor threatening the area that would later be known as Michina Town. Damos later discovered one of these missing Plates and returned it to Arceus, which empowered it to retrieve the remaining 15. This act cultivated Arceus's trust, leading it to separate the Earth Plate, Splash Plate, Meadow Plate, Zap Plate, and Draco Plate, combining them into the Jewel of Life to enable Damos to ensure the land's prosperity. The Zap Plate was specifically incorporated into the Jewel of Life to unify the energies of the Earth, Splash, and Meadow Plates.

The Zap Plate was again seen, among the 17 other type-based Plates, in *The Arceus Chronicles (Part 4)*, where it remained in Arceus's possession. In the manga continuity, Petrel was engaged in gathering these Plates for Team Rocket's agenda. After Silver successfully purloined the handful of Plates Petrel had located, Petrel chose to allow Silver to assemble all 16 Plates, planning to recover them all simultaneously thereafter. Through his Weavile's strategic message delivery to Sneasel and Weavile across the Johto region, Silver eventually collected all 16 Plates for himself. At the Sinjoh Ruins, Gold yielded these Plates to Archer, who then seemingly manipulated Arceus, compelling it to conjure Dialga, Palkia, and Giratina. However, when the Pokédex holders unleashed their Pokémon's most formidable attacks to halt this creation, Arceus reabsorbed every Plate. Finally, the Zap Plate's likeness is depicted in the artwork of the AR6 Arceus card within the Platinum: Arceus expansion.

Title: Meadow Plate

Known in Japanese as みどりのプレート (Verdure Plate), the Meadow Plate is a type of held item that made its debut in Generation IV. In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, it functions as a Key Item. Belonging to a group of artifacts known as Plates, it shares a connection with the Pokémon Arceus. When held, it grants a 20% increase in power to the holder's Grass-type moves. Should the holder utilize the move Judgment, that attack transforms into a Grass-type. Arceus's ability, Multitype, will cause it to assume its Grass-type form when this item is held. Starting from Generation V, item-manipulating effects, including moves like Trick and Fling, are unable to transfer a Plate to Arceus or remove one from it. However, in Generation IV, any Pokémon possessing the Multitype ability was immune to such item-manipulating effects. Furthermore, Multitype transforms an Arceus carrying this Plate into its Grass-type manifestation. Within Pokémon Legends: Arceus, a trainer can use the Meadow Plate directly from their satchel on Arceus, thereby causing it to change to the Grass type. The Meadow Plate bears an inscription which reads: "The powers of Plates are shared among Pokémon."

As a Friendship Item in Pokémon: Magikarp Jump, the Meadow Plate is available for purchase at a cost of 450. Acquiring it will summon Rowlet as a helper Pokémon.

This Plate, along with the 15 other Plates extant during that period, featured in the film "Arceus and the Jewel of Life." In the movie, Arceus employed them to alter its type and faced near-fatal peril after relinquishing all Plates subsequent to obliterating a meteor that threatened the region destined to become Michina Town. Damos recovered one of these missing Plates, returning it to Arceus, an act that empowered Arceus to retrieve the remaining 15 Plates. This gesture earned Arceus's trust, leading it to detach the Earth Plate, Splash Plate, Meadow Plate, Zap Plate, and Draco Plate, forming them into the Jewel of Life, enabling Damos to cultivate the land. The inclusion of the Meadow Plate within the Jewel of Life symbolized grass and various forms of plant life.

In "The Arceus Chronicles (Part 4)," the Meadow Plate was depicted among Arceus's possessions, accompanied by the 17 other type-based Plates. Petrel was engaged in gathering these Plates as part of Team Rocket's scheme. Following Silver's theft of the limited number of Plates Petrel had acquired, Petrel opted to permit Silver to amass all 16 Plates, intending to reclaim them collectively thereafter. Through his Weavile's strategic messaging for Sneasel and Weavile throughout the Johto region, Silver successfully collected all 16 Plates for himself. At the Sinjoh Ruins, Gold relinquished the Plates to Archer, who then apparently exerted control over Arceus with them, commanding it to conjure Dialga, Palkia, and Giratina anew. Upon the Pokédex holders unleashing their Pokémon's ultimate attacks to halt this creation, Arceus reabsorbed all of the Plates. The Meadow Plate's image has graced the illustration of the AR2 Arceus card, originating from the Platinum: Arceus expansion.

Title: Icicle Plate

This item, known in Japanese as つららのプレート (Icicle Plate), functions as a held item originating from Generation IV. Within Pokémon Legends: Arceus, it classifies as a Key Item. It belongs to the category of Plates, each having a connection to Arceus. When held, it enhances the potency of the holder's Ice-type attacks by 20%. Should the holder employ Judgment, that move transforms into an Ice-type attack. The Ability Multitype enables an Arceus clutching this item to assume its Ice-type form. Beginning with Generation V, effects that manipulate items—like Trick and Fling—are incapable of either equipping Arceus with a Plate or dispossessing Arceus of one. However, in Generation IV, item-manipulating effects could not influence any Pokémon possessing the Multitype Ability. An Arceus holding this specific item will have its form altered to Ice type by the Multitype Ability. Specifically in Pokémon Legends: Arceus, this Plate is usable directly from the satchel on Arceus, thereby reconfiguring it to the Ice type. Upon inspection, the Icicle Plate reveals an inscription that reads: "Two beings of time and space set free from the Original One."

This Icicle Plate, alongside the then-existent 15 other Plates, featured in the film Arceus and the Jewel of Life. In this narrative, Arceus employed them to shift its type, and nearly perished upon the complete loss of these Plates. This occurred after it had obliterated a meteor menacing the area destined to become Michina Town. Damos subsequently discovered one of these missing Plates and restored it to Arceus, thereby empowering it to summon the remaining 15 Plates. Furthermore, the Icicle Plate, accompanied by the 17 other type-specific Plates, was seen in The Arceus Chronicles (Part 4), residing with Arceus. Petrel had been gathering these Plates as part of Team Rocket's scheme. Following Silver's theft of the limited number of Plates he had secured, Petrel opted to permit him to accumulate all 16 Plates, intending to reclaim them all simultaneously thereafter. Through his Weavile's strategic message dispersal to Sneasel and Weavile throughout Johto, Silver swiftly amassed all 16 Plates into his own possession. Subsequently, at the Sinjoh Ruins, Gold relinquished the Plates to Archer, who seemingly utilized them to command Arceus, instructing it to manifest Dialga, Palkia, and Giratina anew. As the Pokédex holders deployed their Pokémon's most powerful attacks to halt this creation, every Plate was subsequently reabsorbed by Arceus.

Title: Fist Plate

A held item first appearing in Generation IV, the Fist Plate (こぶしのプレート in Japanese) functions as a Key Item within Pokémon Legends: Arceus. This item is categorized among the various Plates linked to Arceus. Its effect is to enhance the power of the Pokémon holding it when using Fighting-type moves by a factor of 20%. Should a Pokémon utilize Judgment while holding this Plate, the move will transform into a Fighting type. Furthermore, for an Arceus specifically, the Multitype ability will cause it to adopt its Fighting-type form when equipped with this item.

Starting from Generation V, mechanisms that manipulate items, like Trick and Fling, are unable to either bestow a Plate upon Arceus or remove one from it. During Generation IV, Pokémon possessing the Multitype ability were impervious to item-manipulating effects. As before, Multitype transforms an Arceus equipped with this item into its Fighting-type manifestation. Within Pokémon Legends: Arceus, this Plate can be directly activated from the satchel when applied to Arceus, thereby altering its type to Fighting.

Upon inspection, the Fist Plate reveals an etched inscription which reads: "The rift is born of disorder on the other side of this world." In the film Arceus and the Jewel of Life, the Fist Plate, along with the then-total of 15 other Plates, was depicted. Arceus employed these to modify its type and nearly perished after sacrificing them all to obliterate a meteor threatening the future site of Michina Town. Damos subsequently recovered one of these displaced Plates, restoring it to Arceus. This act granted Arceus the necessary power to summon back the remaining 15 Plates.

Later, in The Arceus Chronicles (Part 4), the Fist Plate was shown in Arceus's possession, accompanied by the other 17 Plates corresponding to types. Petrel was in the process of accumulating these Plates as part of a scheme for Team Rocket. Following Silver's theft of the limited number of Plates Petrel had acquired, Petrel opted to allow Silver to consolidate all 16 Plates, intending to reclaim them simultaneously thereafter. Through his Weavile's strategic placement of messages for Sneasel and Weavile throughout Johto, Silver successfully amassed all 16 Plates for his own collection. Ultimately, at the Sinjoh Ruins, Gold relinquished the Plates to Archer, who then appeared to commandeer Arceus using them, directing it to bring forth Dialga, Palkia, and Giratina once more. However, as the Pokédex holders unleashed their Pokémon's most powerful assaults to halt the re-creation, all the Plates were reabsorbed into Arceus.

Title: Toxic Plate

This held item, known in Japanese as もうどくプレート Toxic Plate, first appeared in Generation IV. In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, it functions as a Key Item. It is counted among the Plates, which are inherently connected to Arceus. When equipped, it bestows a 20% power enhancement upon the holder's Poison-type moves. Should the holder unleash Judgment, that move transforms into the Poison type. The Multitype ability dictates that an Arceus carrying this item will adopt its Poison-type variant. Starting with Generation V, item-manipulating effects (like Trick or Fling) are incapable of assigning a Plate to Arceus or disarming Arceus of a Plate. In Generation IV, item-manipulating effects were ineffective against any Pokémon with Multitype. As such, an Arceus holding this item will be altered to its Poison-type form by Multitype. Within Pokémon Legends: Arceus, this Plate can be utilized directly from the satchel on Arceus, thereby altering its type to Poison. This Plate bears an inscription that can be read: "The rules of time and space change within the opposite world."

In the film Arceus and the Jewel of Life, the Toxic Plate, along with the then-existing 15 other Plates, was shown as Arceus employed them for type alteration. Arceus nearly perished after losing all of these Plates, following its destruction of a meteor that threatened the future site of Michina Town. Damos recovered one of these missing Plates and presented it back to Arceus, which then gained the power to summon the remaining 15 Plates. The Toxic Plate, accompanied by the 17 other type-based Plates, was featured in The Arceus Chronicles (Part 4), held by Arceus. Petrel was engaged in gathering these Plates as part of Team Rocket's scheme. Following Silver's theft of the Plates Petrel had acquired, Petrel opted to allow Silver to amass all 16 Plates, intending to reclaim them all simultaneously afterward. Employing his Weavile to relay messages to Sneasel and Weavile throughout Johto, Silver successfully collected all 16 Plates for his own purposes. At the Sinjoh Ruins, Gold handed over the Plates to Archer, who then appeared to commandeer Arceus, commanding it to reconstruct Dialga, Palkia, and Giratina. As the Pokédex holders deployed their Pokémon's most powerful attacks to halt the creation, Arceus absorbed all of the Plates. This specific Plate was one of two that Petrel initially discovered before Silver absconded with them.

Title: Earth Plate

Known in Japanese as だいちのプレート (Earth Plate), this held item first appeared in Generation IV. In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, it functions as a Key Item. It belongs to the category of 'Plates', which share a direct connection with the legendary Pokémon Arceus. Any Ground-type moves executed by the holder receive a 20% power enhancement. Should the holder employ the move Judgment, that attack will manifest as Ground-type. An Arceus clutching this item will transform into its Ground-type variant due to its Multitype ability. Beginning with Generation V, techniques that manipulate items, like Trick or Fling, are incapable of either equipping Arceus with a Plate or disarming it of one. During Generation IV, Pokémon possessing the Multitype ability were immune to the effects of item-manipulating moves. An Arceus equipped with this Plate will shift into its Ground-type appearance thanks to its Multitype ability. Within Pokémon Legends: Arceus, one can directly utilize the Earth Plate from the satchel on Arceus to effect its transformation into the Ground type. The Earth Plate bears a readable inscription, which states: "When the universe was created, its shards became this Plate."

This Plate, alongside the then-existing fifteen other Plates, made an appearance in the film *Arceus and the Jewel of Life*. Here, Arceus employed them to alter its type and nearly perished upon losing every Plate after obliterating a meteor. Damos discovered one of these missing Plates and presented it back to Arceus, which subsequently provided Arceus with the fortitude to summon the remaining fifteen Plates. This gesture gained Damos Arceus's confidence, leading Arceus to detach the Earth Plate, Splash Plate, Meadow Plate, Zap Plate, and Draco Plate, forming them into the Jewel of Life. This was done so Damos could cultivate the land destined to become Michina Town. Its inclusion in the Jewel of Life symbolized the soil of the territory.

In a flashback sequence during the movie *Hoopa and the Clash of Ages*, the Earth Plate was featured alongside the Splash Plate and Flame Plate, with Ghris depicted harnessing their collective energies to forge the Prison Bottle. In *The Arceus Chronicles (Part 4)*, the Earth Plate, accompanied by the seventeen other type-specific Plates, was observed in Arceus's keeping. Petrel actively gathered the Plates as part of Team Rocket's overarching strategy. Following Silver's theft of the limited number of Plates Petrel had acquired, Petrel opted to permit Silver to amass all sixteen Plates, intending to reclaim them simultaneously at a later stage. By utilizing his Weavile to disseminate messages to Sneasel and Weavile throughout Johto, Silver successfully collected all sixteen Plates for his own possession. Gold relinquished the Plates to Archer at the Sinjoh Ruins; Archer then appeared to exert control over Arceus with them, instructing it to conjure Dialga, Palkia, and Giratina anew. Upon the Pokédex holders deploying their Pokémon's most powerful assaults to halt the creation process, Arceus absorbed every single Plate. It was one of two Plates unearthed by Petrel before being subsequently pilfered by Silver. Having deduced the Plates' representation of Pokémon types, Silver employed the Earth Plate during *Raising the Stakes* with Rhyperior, enhancing his Rhyperior's Earth Power move. Its likeness is present in the artwork for the AR8 Arceus card, originating from the Platinum: Arceus expansion.

Title: Sky Plate

Introduced in Generation IV, the Sky Plate (Japanese: あおぞらプレート Blue Sky Plate) functions as a held item. In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, it holds the classification of a Key Item. This artifact is one of the Plates, items intrinsically linked to the legendary Pokémon, Arceus. When held, it grants a 20% increase in the power of the holder's Flying-type moves. Furthermore, if the holder employs the move Judgment, that attack transforms into a Flying-type move. The ability Multitype enables an Arceus clutching this item to assume its Flying-type form.

Regarding game mechanics, from Generation V onwards, specific item-manipulating effects like Trick and Fling are unable to transfer a Plate to Arceus or remove one from it. Prior to this, in Generation IV, no item-manipulating effects could influence any Pokémon possessing the Multitype ability. Within Pokémon Legends: Arceus, the Sky Plate can be activated directly from the satchel while interacting with Arceus, subsequently altering its type to Flying. The Plate also features an inscription, which reads: "The being poured the remains of its powers into stone and buried it deep."

The Sky Plate made an appearance alongside 15 other Plates (totaling 16) in the movie *Arceus and the Jewel of Life*. In this film, Arceus utilized these Plates to dynamically alter its type. It nearly perished after losing all of them subsequent to destroying a meteor that endangered the land destined to become Michina Town. Damos later discovered one of the lost Plates and returned it to Arceus, which then empowered Arceus to retrieve the remaining 15. The Sky Plate, along with the 17 other type-based Plates (totaling 18), was also featured in *The Arceus Chronicles (Part 4)*, under Arceus's direct control.

In a separate narrative, Petrel diligently gathered Plates as part of Team Rocket's grand scheme. After Silver purloined the few Plates Petrel had managed to acquire, Petrel strategically permitted Silver to collect all 16 Plates, intending to reclaim them all at once later. Through his Weavile dispatching messages to other Sneasel and Weavile across the Johto region, Silver successfully amassed all 16 Plates for himself. At the Sinjoh Ruins, Gold eventually relinquished the Plates to Archer, who seemingly used them to exert control over Arceus, commanding it to manifest Dialga, Palkia, and Giratina. Ultimately, the Pokédex holders deployed their Pokémon's ultimate attacks to thwart this creation, resulting in Arceus absorbing all of the Plates.

Title: Mind Plate

This held item, known in Japanese as ふしぎのプレート (Mysterious Plate), first appeared in Generation IV. Within Pokémon Legends: Arceus, it functions as a Key Item. Belonging to the category of "Plates," it shares a direct connection with the Pokémon Arceus. Psychic-type moves executed by the Pokémon holding this item receive a 20% power increase. Should the holder employ the move Judgment, it transforms into a Psychic-type attack. The ability Multitype enables an Arceus equipped with this Plate to assume its Psychic-type form. Beginning with Generation V, item-manipulation techniques, including moves like Trick and Fling, are incapable of either attaching a Plate to Arceus or detaching one from it. During Generation IV, however, any item-manipulating effects were ineffective against Pokémon possessing the Multitype ability. Consequently, Arceus holding this specific item, due to Multitype, will become its Psychic-type variant. In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, placing the Mind Plate directly from the satchel onto Arceus will similarly alter it to the Psychic type. The Plate also bears an inscription, which reads: "The Original One breathed alone before the universe came."

This item, along with the fifteen other Plates existing then, was featured in the film *Arceus and the Jewel of Life*. In the movie, Arceus utilized these Plates to adjust its type and nearly perished after expending them all to obliterate a meteor threatening the nascent Michina Town. Subsequently, Damos recovered one of the lost Plates and restored it to Arceus. This act provided Arceus with the necessary power to summon the remaining fifteen Plates. Later, in *The Arceus Chronicles (Part 4)*, the Mind Plate was shown among all seventeen other type-based Plates, under Arceus's control.

In the manga, Petrel initiated a collection of the Plates as part of Team Rocket's broader strategy. Following Silver's theft of the few Plates Petrel had acquired, Petrel opted to allow Silver to amass all sixteen Plates, intending to reclaim them simultaneously thereafter. Through his Weavile's distribution of messages to Sneasel and Weavile across the Johto region, Silver successfully accumulated all sixteen Plates for himself in short order. Subsequently, at the Sinjoh Ruins, Gold relinquished the Plates to Archer, who seemingly used them to exert control over Arceus, commanding it to manifest Dialga, Palkia, and Giratina anew. As the Pokédex holders deployed their Pokémon's most powerful attacks to halt this creation process, Arceus absorbed every single Plate.

The Mind Plate also appears in the artwork of the AR7 Arceus card, which belongs to the *Platinum: Arceus* expansion.

Title: Insect Plate

Known in Japanese as たまむしプレート (Jewel Beetle Plate), the Insect Plate debuted in Generation IV as a held item. Within Pokémon Legends: Arceus, it functions as a Key Item. This item is part of the Plates collection, each intimately connected to Arceus. It confers a 20% power increase to the holder's Bug-type moves. Should the holder unleash Judgment, that attack transforms into a Bug-type. Arceus, when equipped with the Insect Plate, undergoes a transformation to its Bug-type form due to its Multitype ability. Starting from Generation V, various item-manipulating effects, including Trick and Fling, are unable to either grant a Plate to Arceus or divest Arceus of a Plate. In Generation IV, however, any Pokémon possessing the Multitype ability was immune to item-manipulating effects. In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, players can utilize the Insect Plate directly from their satchel on Arceus, thereby changing its type to Bug. The Plate bears an inscription that reads: "Where all creation was born, that is the being's place of origin."

This Plate, alongside the other fifteen extant Plates, made an appearance in the film *Arceus and the Jewel of Life*. There, Arceus employed them to alter its type. It nearly perished after losing every Plate, having just used their power to demolish a meteor that endangered the future Michina Town. Damos discovered one of these missing Plates and restored it to Arceus, enabling the deity to re-summon the remaining fifteen.

In *The Arceus Chronicles (Part 4)*, the Insect Plate was among the seventeen type-based Plates held by Arceus. Petrel was in the process of gathering these Plates for Team Rocket's overarching scheme. Following Silver's theft of the few Plates Petrel had acquired, Petrel opted to permit Silver to amass all sixteen, planning to reclaim them all simultaneously later. Through his Weavile's strategic message delivery to Sneasel and Weavile across the Johto region, Silver successfully accumulated all sixteen Plates. At the Sinjoh Ruins, Gold relinquished the Plates to Archer, who then appeared to commandeer Arceus, commanding it to reconstruct Dialga, Palkia, and Giratina. As the Pokédex holders employed their Pokémon's most powerful attacks to halt this creation event, Arceus absorbed every Plate.

Title: Stone Plate

Known in Japanese as がんせきプレート (Rock Plate), the Stone Plate first appeared in Generation IV as a distinct type of held item. Within Pokémon Legends: Arceus, it takes on the role of a Key Item. As one of the mystical Plates, it is inherently associated with Arceus. When equipped, it bestows a 20% power boost to the holder's Rock-type moves. Should the holder utilize Judgment, that move's type transforms to Rock. The Multitype Ability ensures that an Arceus holding this particular item will assume its Rock-type form. From Generation V onward, game mechanics dictate that item-transferring maneuvers (such as Trick and Fling) cannot be used to give a Plate to Arceus or to remove one from it. In Generation IV, item-manipulating effects were universally unable to affect Pokémon with Multitype. Notably, in Pokémon Legends: Arceus, this Plate can be deployed from the satchel directly onto Arceus, initiating its transformation into the Rock type. The Stone Plate's surface can be examined, revealing the following engraving: "It gathers power from the Plates, listening to the flute's song."

The Stone Plate, along with the other fifteen Plates of that era, featured prominently in the movie *Arceus and the Jewel of Life*. In the film, Arceus leveraged these artifacts to alter its elemental typing, nearly perishing after losing them all while annihilating a meteor threatening what would later become Michina Town. A character named Damos discovered one of these lost Plates and returned it to Arceus, providing the strength needed for Arceus to retrieve the remaining fifteen. The Stone Plate was also depicted in *The Arceus Chronicles (Part 4)*, where it was among the seventeen type-based Plates in Arceus's possession.

In a different storyline, Petrel pursued the Plates as part of Team Rocket's wider scheme. After Silver managed to purloin the few Plates Petrel had collected, Petrel cunningly decided to permit Silver to gather all sixteen Plates, planning to reclaim them en masse later. Through his Weavile relaying messages to Sneasel and Weavile across the Johto region, Silver successfully amassed all sixteen Plates for himself. Ultimately, at the Sinjoh Ruins, Gold surrendered these collected Plates to Archer. Archer subsequently used them in an apparent attempt to subjugate Arceus, commanding it to conjure recreations of Dialga, Palkia, and Giratina. When the Pokédex holders unleashed their Pokémon's most powerful attacks to thwart this creation, Arceus absorbed all the Plates.

Title: Spooky Plate

Known in Japanese as もののけプレート (Specter Plate), the Spooky Plate is a held item that made its first appearance in Generation IV. Within Pokémon Legends: Arceus, it functions as a Key Item. This particular Plate is one of several associated with the mythical Pokémon, Arceus.

When a Pokémon carries the Spooky Plate, its Ghost-type moves receive an enhancement, causing them to inflict 20% more damage. Should the holder utilize the move Judgment, the attack itself converts to a Ghost type. The Multitype Ability enables an Arceus equipped with this item to shift into its Ghost-type manifestation.

Regarding game mechanics, from Generation V onward, specific item-manipulating moves, such as Trick and Fling, are unable to either bestow a Plate upon Arceus or remove one from it. In Generation IV, however, item-altering effects could not impact any Pokémon possessing the Multitype Ability. In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, the Spooky Plate can be directly used from the satchel on Arceus, thereby changing its type to Ghost.

The Spooky Plate features an inscribable surface, which reveals the engraving: "The other side of this world was given by the Original One to its raging third."

This item has also played a role in the Pokémon anime and manga. In the movie *Arceus and the Jewel of Life*, the Spooky Plate was among the 15 Plates then in existence. Arceus famously employed these items to alter its type and nearly met its end after losing all of them following its destruction of a meteor that endangered what would eventually become Michina Town. A lost Plate was later discovered by Damos and returned to Arceus, providing it with the necessary strength to recall the remaining 15. The Spooky Plate, along with the other 17 type-specific Plates, was seen under Arceus's possession in *The Arceus Chronicles (Part 4)*.

In the manga, Petrel began gathering Plates as part of Team Rocket's grand strategy. After Silver managed to pilfer the few Plates Petrel had acquired, Petrel opted to permit Silver to accumulate all 16 Plates, intending to recover them all at once later. With Weavile dispatching messages to Sneasel and Weavile across the Johto region, Silver soon successfully amassed all 16 Plates for himself. At the Sinjoh Ruins, Gold eventually surrendered these Plates to Archer. Archer then seemingly exerted control over Arceus using the Plates, commanding it to recreate Dialga, Palkia, and Giratina. When the Pokédex holders deployed their Pokémon's most powerful attacks to prevent the creation, Arceus absorbed all of the Plates.

Title: Draco Plate

The Draco Plate, known in Japanese as りゅうのプレート (Dragon Plate), is a held item first introduced in the fourth generation of Pokémon games. In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, it functions as a Key Item. It is one of several Plates connected to the Legendary Pokémon Arceus. This item provides a 20% power increase to Dragon-type moves used by the Pokémon holding it. When Judgment is used by a Pokémon equipped with this Plate, the move's type transforms into Dragon. Furthermore, an Arceus holding this specific Plate will have its form altered to its Dragon type due to the Multitype Ability. Beginning with Generation V, techniques that manipulate items, such as Trick and Fling, are unable to either grant a Plate to Arceus or remove one from it. In Generation IV only, item-manipulating effects could not influence any Pokémon possessing the Multitype Ability.

Within Pokémon Legends: Arceus, players can utilize the Draco Plate directly from their satchel on Arceus, thereby changing its type to Dragon. The Plate bears an readable inscription that reads: "Three beings were born to bind time and space."

This Plate, along with the Iron Plate and Splash Plate, made its initial animated appearance in "Journey to the Unown!". In this episode, Saturn and two Team Galactic Grunts placed these Plates within a room in the Solaceon Ruins. Their objective was to acquire the Spear Key and awaken the Unown residing there. These Plates were collectively used to enhance the combined powers of the Dragon, Steel, and Water types associated with Palkia and Dialga.

The Draco Plate, alongside the fifteen other Plates existing at that time, was featured again in the movie "Arceus and the Jewel of Life." There, Arceus employed them to shift its own type, coming close to death after losing all Plates while destroying a meteor. Damos subsequently discovered one of these lost Plates and returned it to Arceus, providing it with the necessary strength to recall the remaining fifteen. This act earned Arceus's trust, prompting it to manifest the Earth Plate, Splash Plate, Meadow Plate, Zap Plate, and Draco Plate as the Jewel of Life. This allowed Damos to cultivate the land that would eventually become Michina Town. The Draco Plate's specific inclusion in the Jewel of Life served to bolster the efficacy of the other Plates.

In "The Arceus Chronicles (Part 4)," the Draco Plate was seen among all eighteen type-based Plates, under Arceus's possession. Petrel was actively gathering these Plates for a Team Rocket scheme. After Silver managed to steal the few Plates Petrel had acquired, Petrel strategically decided to allow Silver to collect all sixteen Plates, planning to reclaim them simultaneously later. Silver, utilizing his Weavile to relay messages to other Sneasel and Weavile throughout the Johto region, successfully amassed all sixteen Plates. At the Sinjoh Ruins, Gold yielded the Plates to Archer, who then seemingly exerted control over Arceus, commanding it to recreate Dialga, Palkia, and Giratina. When the Pokédex holders unleashed their Pokémon's ultimate attacks to thwart this creation, all the Plates were reabsorbed by Arceus.

Notably, the Draco Plate stands alone as the only Plate capable of influencing which moves Arceus could learn. Prior to Generation VIII, Arceus's ability to be taught the move Draco Meteor was contingent upon it holding a Draco Plate.

Title: Dread Plate

Introduced in Generation IV, the Dread Plate (Japanese: こわもてプレート Frightening Plate) functions as a held item. In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, this item is classified as a Key Item. As one of the Plates intrinsically linked with Arceus, it bestows a 20% boost to the power of the holder's Dark-type moves. Should the holder utilize the move Judgment, the attack's typing will convert to Dark. An Arceus holding this Plate will transform into its Dark-type form via its Multitype ability.

Regarding game mechanics, from Generation V onward, effects designed to manipulate items, such as Trick and Fling, are unable to either equip a Plate onto Arceus or remove one from it. Previously, in Generation IV, these item-manipulating effects could not impact any Pokémon with the Multitype ability. Within Pokémon Legends: Arceus, the Dread Plate can be activated from the satchel directly on Arceus, thereby changing its type to Dark.

The Dread Plate carries an engravable inscription that reads: "Two makes matter, and three makes spirit, shaping the world."

This Plate, along with the 15 other Plates known at the time, made an appearance in the movie Arceus and the Jewel of Life. There, Arceus employed them to shift its type and nearly perished after all its Plates were lost following its destruction of a meteor that endangered the region that would later become Michina Town. Damos later discovered one of these lost Plates and returned it to Arceus, enabling the latter to recall its remaining 15 Plates.

In The Arceus Chronicles (Part 4) manga, the Dread Plate was among the 17 other type-based Plates in Arceus's possession. Petrel was engaged in collecting the Plates for Team Rocket's strategic objectives. After Silver absconded with the few Plates Petrel had acquired, Petrel made the tactical decision to permit Silver to gather all 16 Plates, intending to reclaim them collectively later. Thanks to his Weavile leaving messages for Sneasel and Weavile across Johto, Silver successfully amassed all 16 Plates for himself. At the Sinjoh Ruins, Gold surrendered the Plates to Archer, who then seemingly gained control of Arceus, commanding it to manifest Dialga, Palkia, and Giratina. When the Pokédex holders unleashed their Pokémon's ultimate attacks to thwart this creation, Arceus absorbed all the Plates.

The Dread Plate has also been depicted in the artwork for the AR1 Arceus card from the Platinum: Arceus expansion.

Title: Iron Plate

Introduced in Generation IV, the Iron Plate (known in Japanese as こうてつプレート, or Steel Plate) functions as a held item. In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, its classification shifts to a Key Item. It belongs to a category of items known as Plates, each having a connection to the legendary Pokémon Arceus. This item enhances the potency of any Steel-type move executed by its holder, increasing its power by 20%. Furthermore, if the holder unleashes Judgment, that attack will specifically manifest as a Steel-type move. The ability Multitype transforms an Arceus equipped with this Plate into its Steel-type variant. Beginning with Generation V, item-manipulating techniques like Trick and Fling are ineffective at either equipping a Plate onto Arceus or detaching one from it. During Generation IV, a broader rule prevented such item-altering effects from impacting any Pokémon possessing the Multitype ability. While Multitype naturally shifts an Arceus holding this item to its Steel-type form, in Pokémon Legends: Arceus, the Iron Plate can instead be activated directly from the satchel, causing Arceus to adopt the Steel type. This Plate bears an inscription that can be deciphered, revealing the words: "That which fills the other side of the world can shape the rage and mold it."

Its inaugural appearance was in the episode *Journey to the Unown!*, where it featured alongside the Draco Plate and Splash Plate. Saturn, accompanied by two Team Galactic Grunts, deposited these Plates within a chamber situated in the Solaceon Ruins. Their objective was to acquire the Spear Key and rouse the Unown residing there. These specific Plates collectively amplified the Dragon, Steel, and Water types associated with Palkia and Dialga. Subsequently, the Iron Plate, among the full collection of 16 Plates then in existence, made another appearance in the film *Arceus and the Jewel of Life*. In this narrative, Arceus employed them to alter its elemental typing. The deity nearly perished after sacrificing all its Plates to obliterate a meteor endangering the area destined to become Michina Town. A key moment occurred when Damos recovered one of these displaced Plates and restored it to Arceus, thereby re-empowering Arceus to summon the remaining 15 Plates.

Later, in *The Arceus Chronicles (Part 4)*, this Plate was featured alongside the 17 other type-based Plates, all belonging to Arceus. Concurrently, Petrel was engaged in assembling these Plates on behalf of Team Rocket's agenda. When Silver purloined the limited number of Plates Petrel had managed to locate, Petrel strategically opted to permit Silver to amass all 16 Plates, intending to reclaim them in a single sweep afterward. Utilizing his Weavile to disseminate communications to Sneasel and Weavile throughout Johto, Silver successfully consolidated all 16 Plates for his own possession. Within the Sinjoh Ruins, Gold relinquished the Plates to Archer. Archer subsequently exploited them to ostensibly command Arceus, issuing directives for it to manifest new versions of Dialga, Palkia, and Giratina. However, when the Pokédex holders unleashed their Pokémon's most powerful attacks to thwart this creation, Arceus reabsorbed every single Plate. The Iron Plate also makes an appearance in the artwork of the AR9 Arceus card, which hails from the *Platinum: Arceus* expansion.

Title: Odd Incense

Introduced in Generation IV, the Odd Incense (Japanese: あやしいおこう Mysterious Incense) is a specialized held item. This incense plays a role in breeding, enabling Mr. Mime and Mr. Rime to produce Mime Jr. Eggs. During battles, its effect mirrors that of the Twisted Spoon; it enhances the power of the holder's Psychic-type moves by 20%. Furthermore, if held by either parent within a Pokémon Day Care or Pokémon Nursery pairing that would normally generate a Mr. Mime Egg, the item causes a Mime Jr. Egg to be produced instead.

Title: Rock Incense

The Rock Incense (Japanese: がんせきおこう Rock Incense) is a held item first appearing in Generation IV. Its battle utility is identical to that of the Hard Stone, increasing the power of the holder's Rock-type moves by 20%. When held by a parent in the Pokémon Day Care or Nursery, it causes a Bonsly Egg to be produced instead of a Sudowoodo Egg. Prior to Generation IX, this incense was unique in allowing Sudowoodo to produce Bonsly Eggs.

Title: Full Incense

This held item, known in Japanese as まんぷくおこう (Full Incense), first appeared in Generation IV. It functions as an incense that facilitates Snorlax producing Munchlax Eggs. Specifically, if either parent in a Pokémon Day Care or Pokémon Nursery pairing holds it, and that pair would typically generate a Snorlax Egg, a Munchlax Egg is instead produced.

In battle, its effect mirrors the Lagging Tail. It obliges the holder to act last within its specific priority bracket, irrespective of its Speed. This item's influence remains constant even when Trick Room is active. If a Full Incense is obtained, lost, or suppressed during a battle, its impact on turn order will not take effect until the start of the subsequent turn.

When multiple Pokémon equipped with either a Lagging Tail or a Full Incense use moves of equal priority, the Pokémon with the *lower* Speed stat will act first (this remains true regardless of Trick Room's presence). Conversely, a Pokémon possessing the Stall Ability will move *before* any Pokémon holding a Lagging Tail or a Full Incense. Nevertheless, if a Pokémon with the Stall Ability *also* holds a Lagging Tail or Full Incense, the Stall Ability does not influence when that Pokémon moves. If multiple Pokémon, either due to the Stall Ability or holding a Lagging Tail or Full Incense, utilize moves of the same priority, their relative turn order is determined by Speed (reversed if Trick Room is in effect).

Title: Wave Incense
Introduced in Generation IV, the Wave Incense (Japanese: さざなみのおこう Wavelet Incense) functions as a held item. This specific incense allows Mantine to produce Mantyke Eggs. In combat situations, its effect is precisely the same as the Mystic Water, providing a 20% power increase to the holder's Water-type moves. Furthermore, when held by either parent within a Pokémon Day Care or Pokémon Nursery pairing that would normally yield a Mantine Egg, a Mantyke Egg is generated instead. Among all incenses, this item and the Sea Incense are unique in sharing an identical battle effect.

Title: Rose Incense

The Rose Incense, known as おはなのおこう (Flower Incense) in Japanese, is a held item that made its debut in Generation IV. While in battle, its effect is identical to the Miracle Seed, boosting the strength of the holder's Grass-type moves by 20%. Beyond combat, this distinctive incense is used in breeding, allowing Roselia and Roserade to produce Budew Eggs. More specifically, should one of the parents in a Pokémon Day Care or Nursery pairing hold the Rose Incense, and that pairing would typically result in a Roselia Egg, a Budew Egg will be yielded instead.

Title: Luck Incense

For the Pokémon Sleep item, refer to Incense (Sleep). Known in Japanese as こううんのおこう (Good Luck Incense), the Luck Incense is a held item that debuted in Generation IV. Before Generation IX, this incense facilitated the production of Happiny Eggs by Chansey and Blissey. During battles, its effect mirrors that of the Amulet Coin. When a Pokémon holding it participates in a battle, the Luck Incense causes the prize money obtained afterward to be doubled. Furthermore, it also multiplies the coins collected from moves such as Pay Day and G-Max Gold Rush by two. The Luck Incense's effect does not combine with an Amulet Coin or additional Luck Incenses; nevertheless, it does stack with Happy Hour and Prize Money Power. Its effect activates as soon as the Pokémon participates in battle while equipped with it. It remains active regardless of Klutz, and its influence persists even if the Pokémon holding it faints, or if the Luck Incense is removed or negated. Should this item be held by either parent within a Pokémon Day Care or Pokémon Nursery pairing that would typically yield a Chansey Egg, a Happiny Egg will be generated in its place. Funko unveiled a luck-themed Pikachu figure, which depicts both a Luck Incense and an Amulet Coin, on February 13, 2019, as part of their A Day With Pikachu figure collection.

Title: Pure Incense

First appearing in Generation IV, the Pure Incense (Japanese: きよめのおこう Purify Incense) is a specialized held item. Its primary purpose is to allow Chimecho to lay Chingling Eggs. In overworld exploration, its effects mirror those of the Cleanse Tag; equipping it to the first Pokémon in the player's party reduces the frequency of wild Pokémon encounters by one-third. When breeding at the Pokémon Day Care or Pokémon Nursery, if either parent holds this item and their combination would typically produce a Chimecho Egg, a Chingling Egg is generated instead. Interestingly, despite Chimecho and Chingling not being present in Pokémon Sword and Shield, the Pure Incense can still be acquired in those titles.

Title: Protector

Known in Japanese as プロテクター (Protector), this held item made its debut in Generation IV. Its primary purpose is to facilitate Rhydon's evolution into Rhyperior. In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, directly applying the Protector to Rhydon triggers its transformation into Rhyperior. Alternatively, a Rhydon can achieve this evolution by being traded while holding the item. Both of these evolutionary methods result in the consumption of the Protector. The item also features in the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon titles: Explorers of Time, Explorers of Darkness, and Explorers of Sky. Within these games, if the player presents both a Link Cable and a Protector at Luminous Spring, Rhydon can evolve into Rhyperior, an action that depletes both the Link Cable and the Protector. When propelled as a projectile, the Protector inflicts 1 point of damage, or 2 if it possesses the sticky status. However, utilizing this item within a dungeon environment will consume it without yielding any discernible effect. In Pokémon Conquest, Rhydon evolves into Rhyperior if its associated Warrior is equipped with a Protector, and Rhydon itself defeats an opposing Warrior's Pokémon in a manner that allows recruitment. The traveling merchant offers this item for acquisition at a price of 5000 gold, and it can be divested for 2500-3250 gold. In the manga Yikes, Yanmega! II, Diamond's Lickilicky, named Kit, used a Protector to shield itself from the soundwaves of Mars's Yanmega swarm. Later, Roseanne disclosed that the Protector was among the items she had employed in her research into Pokémon Evolution. Raising the Stakes with Rhyperior unveiled that Silver had entrusted Blue with a Protector for his Rhydon to hold, enabling its metamorphosis into Rhyperior during a trade conducted between the two Trainers. Subsequent to this event, Blue allowed Silver to keep the Rhyperior as a provisional stand-in for his lost Ursaring.

Title: Electirizer

First introduced in Generation IV, the Electirizer (Japanese: エレキブースター Elecbooster) functions as a held item. Its primary function is to facilitate Electabuzz's evolution into Electivire; this transformation occurs when Electabuzz is traded while gripping the item. The Electirizer is consumed during this evolutionary process. In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, where held items are absent, Electabuzz can be evolved into Electivire by simply using the Electirizer on it, which also consumes the item. This peculiar item is also found in Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Explorers of Time, Explorers of Darkness, and Explorers of Sky. Within these games, offering both a Link Cable and an Electirizer at the Luminous Spring allows for Electabuzz's evolution into Electivire. Both the Link Cable and the Electirizer are consumed upon this action. When thrown, the Electirizer deals 1 damage, increasing to 2 damage if it is sticky. Attempting to use this item directly within a dungeon will consume it without producing any effect. An Electirizer is also prominently depicted in the artwork for the Triumphant Electivire Pokémon Trading Card Game card.

Title: Magmarizer

The Magmarizer, known in Japanese as マグマブースター (Magmabooster), is a held item that debuted in Generation IV. Its primary function is to enable Magmar's evolution into Magmortar. This transformation occurs when Magmar is traded to another Trainer while equipped with the item, and the Magmarizer is consumed upon the evolution's completion. In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, where the held item mechanic is absent, the Magmarizer instead triggers Magmar's evolution into Magmortar simply by being used on it, which also expends the item.

This item also features in the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, appearing in Explorers of Time, Explorers of Darkness, and Explorers of Sky. Within these games, Magmar can evolve into Magmortar at the Luminous Spring by offering both a Link Cable and the Magmarizer; both items are consumed in the process. If flung within a dungeon, the Magmarizer inflicts 1 damage, increasing to 2 damage if it possesses the sticky status. Attempting to use the item while inside a dungeon will consume it, but no effect will occur. The Magmarizer is depicted in the artwork for the Triumphant Magmortar card. Furthermore, in the episode "Yikes, Yanmega! II," Roseanne disclosed her research into various Evolution items, including the Magmarizer, as part of her studies on Pokémon Evolution.

Title: Dubious Disc

The Dubious Disc (Japanese: あやしいパッチ Dubious Patch) is a category of held item that debuted in Generation IV. It serves as the catalyst for Porygon2's transformation into Porygon-Z. When a Porygon2 is traded while clutching this item, it triggers the evolution into Porygon-Z, and the Dubious Disc is subsequently consumed. In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, given the lack of held items in that title, the Dubious Disc is simply utilized directly on Porygon2 to prompt its evolution, thereby depleting the item.

This object also makes an appearance in Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Explorers of Time, Explorers of Darkness, and Explorers of Sky. Within these games, Porygon2 can evolve into Porygon-Z by presenting both a Link Cable and a Dubious Disc at Luminous Spring, a process which expends both items.

If thrown in a dungeon, this item will inflict 1 damage, increasing to 2 damage if it is sticky. Attempting to use the Dubious Disc within a dungeon setting will consume it without producing any discernible effect.

A Dubious Disc is depicted within the artwork for the Triumphant Porygon-Z card. Porygon-Z's Pokédex descriptions suggest the Dubious Disc was modified with the intention of allowing Porygon-Z to operate in different dimensions, though an unanticipated glitch in its programming reportedly corrupted Porygon-Z, preventing it from fulfilling this designed purpose.

Title: Reaper Cloth

Introduced in Generation IV, the Reaper Cloth (known in Japanese as れいかいのぬの, or Spirit World Cloth) functions as a held item. Its primary purpose is to facilitate the evolution of Dusclops into Dusknoir. When a Dusclops is traded while clutching this item, it triggers its transformation into Dusknoir, a process that results in the item's consumption. However, within Pokémon Legends: Arceus, where the held item mechanic is absent, the Reaper Cloth merely requires direct application to Dusclops to initiate its evolution.

The Reaper Cloth also features in the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon titles: Explorers of Time, Explorers of Darkness, and Explorers of Sky. There, at Luminous Spring, players can evolve Dusclops into Dusknoir by presenting both a Link Cable and a Reaper Cloth; this specific method also depletes both the Link Cable and the Reaper Cloth. Should it be thrown, it inflicts 1 point of damage, or 2 if it is sticky. Deploying this item within a dungeon environment will consume it without yielding any benefit.

In Pokémon Conquest, Dusclops undergoes evolution into Dusknoir provided it defeats a Warrior's Pokémon in a way that permits recruitment, and its own Warrior simultaneously bears a Reaper Cloth. The traveling merchant offers the Reaper Cloth for purchase at 5000 gold, and it can be resold for an amount between 2500 and 3250 gold.

Within "Problematic Probopass and Mad Magnezone III," Fantina's Duskull utilized its Reaper Cloth to rescue Diamond and Pearl from an impending fall. Roseanne divulged her research into various Evolution items, including the Reaper Cloth, during her studies of Pokémon Evolution in "Yikes, Yanmega! II."

On the Secret Wonders Duskull trading card, a Reaper Cloth is depicted as a held item; this particular card enables Duskull to evolve on the same turn it is put into play. This stands in contrast to other media where the Reaper Cloth specifically facilitates Dusclops's evolution into Dusknoir, instead of Duskull's evolution into Dusclops. The song "Ghost Dive" contains lyrical references to the Reaper Cloth. Both the Reaper Cloth's Japanese designation and entries in Dusknoir's Pokédex allude to the Ghost World.

Title: Razor Claw

The Razor Claw (Japanese: するどいツメ Razor Claw) is a specialized held item introduced in Generation IV. Its primary functions include enabling the evolution of specific Pokémon and enhancing the holder's critical hit capabilities. When held, it notably elevates the Pokémon's critical hit ratio by one stage. It also serves as an evolutionary trigger: a regular Sneasel holding this item will evolve into Weavile upon leveling up at night, while a Hisuian Sneasel will transform into Sneasler when leveled up during the day. This evolutionary process always consumes the Razor Claw.

Within the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, the Razor Claw appears in *Explorers of Time*, *Explorers of Darkness*, and *Explorers of Sky*. In these titles, a Sneasel can evolve into Weavile at Luminous Spring by offering both a Lunar Ribbon and a Razor Claw, with both items being consumed in the process. If thrown in a dungeon, this item inflicts 1 damage (or 2 if sticky). Attempting to use the Razor Claw directly within a dungeon will consume it without any noticeable effect.

In *Pokémon Conquest*, the Razor Claw plays a role in evolution and combat. A Sneasel evolves into Weavile if its link improves while its Speed stat is 175 or higher and its Warrior is equipped with a Razor Claw. Beyond evolution, it also boosts the likelihood of attacks striking enemies' weak spots. This item can be acquired from the traveling merchant for 5000 gold, and sold for a return of 2500-3250 gold.

For *Pokémon UNITE* players, the Razor Claw functions as a held item that augments basic attacks. After a move is executed, the user's subsequent basic attack within three seconds deals additional damage to a single foe. This bonus damage is calculated as 10/15/20 plus 40/45/50% of the user's Attack stat, corresponding to grades 1, 10, and 20 respectively. If the user is categorized as a melee attacker, this enhanced basic attack also applies a 30% slow to enemies for one second, subject to a 1.5-second cooldown. Furthermore, the item intrinsically improves the holder's Attack and critical hit rate; at Grade 1, it grants +1 Attack and +0.6% critical hit rate, escalating to +15 Attack and +2.1% critical hit rate by Grade 30.

In *Pokémon Sleep*, the Razor Claw is a necessary ingredient for evolving specific Pokémon, and it is expended upon use. Specifically, Sneasel requires 80 Sneasel Candy and a Razor Claw to evolve into Weavile.

Within the anime continuity, Silver's Sneasel in *Dealing With A Koffing Fit* evolved into Weavile after gaining experience while holding a Razor Claw, an item previously given to Silver by Green. The artwork for the *Undaunted Weavile* card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game also prominently features a Razor Claw.

Title: Razor Fang

Designated as the するどいキバ in Japanese, the Razor Fang is a held item that made its debut in Generation IV. Its primary functions are enabling Gligar's evolution into Gliscor and granting the Pokémon holding it the ability to make opponents flinch with its attacks. Specifically, it provides a 10% flinching opportunity for particular moves wielded by the Pokémon. Each individual strike of a multi-hit attack possesses its own independent probability of inflicting flinching on the target. If these particular moves are part of an implied specific roster, their 10% flinch rate remains impervious to the Serene Grace Ability. Exclusively within Generation IV, owing to the unique resolution of the item's effect, U-turn can induce flinching only if the Pokémon substituted into battle is the one clutching the Razor Fang. For all other offensive techniques that lack an inherent flinch probability, the Razor Fang bestows a 10% chance of causing each target to flinch. This particular flinch rate *is* influenced by the Serene Grace Ability and the rainbow effect from Fire Pledge and Water Pledge. It is important to note that the Razor Fang's effect does not activate Sheer Force; conversely, Sheer Force will nullify a move's built-in flinch chance if that move also carries another additional effect.

Regarding evolution, Gligar transforms into Gliscor upon gaining a level during nighttime hours while equipped with the Razor Fang. This evolutionary process expends the item. In the specific context of Pokémon Legends: Arceus, Gligar's evolution into Gliscor is achieved by simply *using* the Razor Fang on it at night, which also consumes the item.

The Razor Fang also features in the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series, specifically in Explorers of Time, Explorers of Darkness, and Explorers of Sky. Within those Mystery Dungeon titles, a Gligar can be evolved into Gliscor by presenting both a Lunar Ribbon and a Razor Fang at Luminous Spring; this action consumes both items. If hurled, it inflicts 1 damage (or 2 if it is sticky). Simply using the item within a dungeon will deplete it without any discernible effect.

In the episode titled Fighting Fear with Fear!, Ash acquired a Razor Fang from Gary, subsequently employing it later in that same episode to evolve his Gligar into Gliscor. The artwork for the Undaunted Gliscor Pokémon Trading Card Game card prominently displays a Razor Fang.

Title: Explorer Kit

Should you be seeking the item from Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Explorers of Time, Darkness, and Sky, consult Pokémon Exploration Team Kit. The Explorer Kit (Japanese: たんけんセット Exploration Set) is a designated Key Item granting entry to the Underground/Grand Underground, an expansive subterranean realm situated beneath the entire Sinnoh region. A player may utilize the Explorer Kit anywhere across Sinnoh, excluding the interiors of buildings and other built structures, to gain instant access to this underground domain. Additionally, digging is not possible beneath elevated constructs such as bridges. When employed, the player is prompted to save their game, and an alert confirms the activation of DS Wireless Communications. The kit then teleports the player from their surface spot to the corresponding point within the Underground's/Grand Underground's labyrinthine tunnels.

The Explorer Kit furnishes all essential implements for exploration, excavation, and collection within the Underground/Grand Underground. Upon the initial use of certain tools, descriptive notes regarding their functions are presented to the player.
**Hammer:** This tool serves to excavate through walls in pursuit of spheres and treasures. It delivers a greater impact to the wall compared to the pickaxe, dislodging multiple tiles simultaneously; however, this results in the wall collapsing at twice the rate. A wall will give way after exactly 25 hammer strikes.
**Pickaxe:** The pickaxe is similarly employed for tunneling through walls. It produces a lesser impact on the wall than the hammer, causing fewer tiles to be removed per strike. Consequently, the wall maintains its integrity for a longer duration before succumbing to collapse. In multiplayer scenarios, if another player is mining nearby, a pickaxe icon will materialize on the current player's screen at the corresponding location of the other player's hits. The wall will collapse after 49 pickaxe hits.
**Map:** Visible on the top screen, the map illustrates the entirety of the Underground/Grand Underground and highlights areas within walls suitable for excavation. The subterranean labyrinth is divided into six distinct segments, each accessible from varying points across Sinnoh. These sections are equivalent concerning available vendors and mining treasures. In multiplayer mode, the map additionally displays the positions of other players within the Underground/Grand Underground.
**Radar:** The radar's purpose is to pinpoint all treasures, spheres, and traps throughout the Underground/Grand Underground. By tapping the touch screen, the radar activates and reveals the locations of all such items in the player's immediate vicinity. Should a sparkle manifest on the floor via the radar, it signifies either a trap or a sphere; traps are denoted by white sparkles, whereas spheres exhibit gold. If both are concealed in a single spot, a trap is discovered before a sphere. When a sparkle appears within a wall, it indicates a bulging section ripe for mining.

The Explorer Kit additionally provides receptacles for carrying Spheres and Decorations (also referred to as Goods). Spheres serve as the monetary unit of the Underground/Grand Underground, tradable with vendors found within the area. These vendor explorers remain fixed in their locations, capable only of rotating in place. Each of the three distinct vendor types accepts Spheres in exchange for either goods, traps, or treasures. Goods are generally understood as items utilized within the Underground/Grand Underground to adorn a player's Secret Base, encompassing articles like dolls, chairs, and tables. Beyond the Underground, Goods may also be acquired from the fourth floor of the Veilstone Department Store, or obtained from Mr. Goods upon fulfilling specific conditions. The scarcity of Goods is directly correlated with the value of the Sphere requested in trade. For instance, an Iron Beam is valued at a Prism Sphere 9, while a Pink Dresser commands a Green Sphere 54, thereby establishing the Pink Dresser's significantly higher rarity compared to the Iron Beam. In the episode "Double Trouble with Dialga and Palkia I," it was revealed that Byron and the Underground Man employed the Explorer Kit to excavate subterranean passages beneath Mt. Coronet, enabling the Sinnoh Gym Leaders to ambush the Team Galactic Commanders at the Spear Pillar. A scaled-down replica of the shovel and helmet from the Explorer Kit was part of the Sinnoh goods set within the Pokémon World Market merchandise collection, produced by Re-Ment and sold exclusively at the Pokémon Center. Notably, a backpack replica was not included.

A known programming error prevents the Explorer Kit from being used when the player occupies the space two tiles to the right of the Bug Catcher positioned at Eterna City's western entrance. In this specific scenario, a message is displayed: "Rowan's words echoed... <player>! There's a time and place for everything! But not now." This particular glitch manifests exclusively in Pokémon Platinum. While other Key Items from Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum are present, the Explorer Kit's data is conspicuously absent from Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver. Nevertheless, consistent with all Generation IV Key Items, its data is indeed incorporated into the Generation V and VI games.

Title: Fashion Case

A Key Item, known as the Fashion Case (アクセサリーいれ, or Accessory Container, in Japanese), made its debut in Generation IV. This item provides storage for both Accessories and Backdrops for the player character. Accessories are categorized into several distinct sections, including Fluffs, Pebbles and Boulders, Scales, Feathers, Facial Hair, Natural, Flowers, Specs, Misc, and Contest prizes, among various other classifications. Players can also acquire a total of seventeen unique Backdrops. Upon initial acquisition in the Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum versions, the Fashion Case is pre-loaded with two Backdrops and a selection of Accessories. Extra Accessories can be sourced through several methods: Pokémon might find them in Amity Square, they are awarded as prizes for triumphing in Super Contests, the Massage Girl in Veilstone City may discover them, they can be exchanged for Berries at Floaroma Town's Pick a Peck of Colors Flower Shop, or numerous Non-Player Characters (NPCs) might hand them over. However, within HeartGold and SoulSilver, random Accessories are randomly collected by the Pokémon at the head of the party during walks, can be won through the Accessory drawing held in the Goldenrod Tunnel, or are gifted by various NPCs. Nevertheless, their utility in HeartGold and SoulSilver is limited exclusively to use in photo opportunities within the Goldenrod Tunnel. Further Backdrops are obtainable by winning at the Pokémon Lottery Corner, receiving them from the woman located in the Global Terminal, or by participating in the Accessory drawing within the Goldenrod Tunnel. Furthermore, a specific woman residing in the Pal Park building offers particular Accessories or Backdrops to the player, provided certain Pokémon Game Boy Advance cartridges are inserted into the Nintendo DS's GBA slot. Before making her debut in a Pokémon Super Contest, as chronicled in Perturbed by Pachirisu, Platinum meticulously organized her Fashion Case to facilitate swift retrieval of Accessories for adorning her Prinplup during the Visual Competition segment of the Normal Rank Coolness Contest she was getting ready for. Despite her preparations, moments before the event, a Pachirisu owned by another participant clandestinely disarranged her Fashion Case. Even with this sabotage attempt by her rivals, Platinum successfully managed to equip her Pokémon with accessories within the stringent 60-second time limit.

Title: Pal Pad

The Pal Pad, officially known in Japanese as ともだちてちょう (Friend Notebook), is a Key Item first introduced in Generation IV. This device empowers players to register fellow trainers as friends, subsequently enabling them to convene within the Wi-Fi Club for engaging in Pokémon battles and facilitating trades. These multiplayer functionalities operate via the Nintendo Wi-Fi Connection, where each individual game is assigned a distinct Friend Code. Participants can exchange these codes to establish connections in the Wi-Fi Club. For a player to connect with a friend online, both individuals must ensure they have each other registered within their own Pal Pad. Registering a friend requires knowledge of their chosen name and Friend Code; however, the name entered does not necessarily need to correspond with the friend's actual player character's name. Friend registration can occur manually by entering Friend Codes into the Pal Pad, or automatically when players trade Pokémon with one another in the Union Room. Beyond its primary registration purpose, the Pal Pad also allows users to view a list of their registered friends and to check their own Friend Code. A player's unique Friend Code is generated upon their initial connection to any Wi-Fi compatible area within the game, such as a Pokémon Center or the Battle Tower. Furthermore, the Pal Pad keeps track of the total number of trades a player has completed, as well as their wins and losses against other players in Wi-Fi battles. In Pokémon Platinum, and in both Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, the Pal Pad additionally records how many times players have secured victories through the Battle Frontier's facilities alongside a friend, plus the number of times they have played Wi-Fi Plaza Games together. The Pal Pad has the capacity to register up to 32 friends.

This item also made its appearance in the Pokémon Trading Card Game during the Japanese XY Era. Launched as one of the XY-P Promotional cards, the Pal Pad Item card features the debut artwork by the new illustrator, PLANETA. This particular Trainer card enables the player to retrieve two Supporter cards from their discard pile and then shuffle them back into their deck. Later, during the English Sword & Shield Series (corresponding to the Japanese SWSH Era), the card's text received a minor adjustment to include the phrase "up to," offering players the option to recover fewer than the maximum specified number of cards.

Title: Old Charm

Known in Japanese as こだいのおまもり (Ancient Omamori), the Old Charm is a Key Item first introduced during Generation IV. This particular good-luck charm is fashioned from Pokémon bones. En route to Celestic Town, Cynthia entrusts this Old Charm to the player, requesting its delivery to her grandmother, the town's revered elder. Following the player's successful defense of the town's ancient ruins against the machinations of Team Galactic, the charm is presented to Cynthia's grandmother, who then grants permission to investigate the site. Should a player attain friendship level 100 with Cynthia within the Trainer Lodge, a replica of the Old Charm can be acquired from the Lodge Exchange for display there. An Old Charm was featured in the episode Luxray Vision!, owned by Matthew. Team Rocket, using the alias "The Bandit Beauties Three," sought to pilfer the charm but was ultimately thwarted by Marble, her Luxray, Ash, and his friends. In Dialga's Secret Keys, the Old Charm made an appearance when Cynthia handed it to Hareta and Mitsumi, charging them with its conveyance to her grandmother in Celestic Town. After Hareta prevented Team Galactic from obliterating the Celestic Town ruins with an explosive device, he delivered the Old Charm to Cynthia's grandmother.

Title: Galactic Key

Introduced in Generation IV, the Galactic Key (Japanese: ギンガだんのカギ, translated as Galaxy Gang's Key) functions as a specialized Key Item. Across Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, Platinum, Brilliant Diamond, and Shining Pearl, this device enables players to unlock the secured entry points to both the Galactic Warehouse Underpass and the lobby area of the Team Galactic HQ. Furthermore, during Episode RR, the Galactic Key serves a distinct purpose: it either disengages the mirrored door within Lusamine's room at Team Rocket's Castle or, should that door already be open, it activates the warp panel situated behind it. This item is designed for single use, breaking and being consumed immediately after its application.

Title: Red Chain

The Red Chain (Japanese: あかいくさり Red Chain) functions as a Key Item within Pokémon Legends: Arceus. While it appears in the game, it cannot be acquired through legitimate gameplay. This artifact, although unobtainable, holds significant narrative weight, echoing its prominent role in the lore of the Sinnoh-based titles where it also exists in the game's data as a Key Item.

In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, Cogita first reveals the Red Chain's potential, asserting its capacity to halt the space-time rift looming over Mount Coronet. To assemble the chain, the player must journey to Hisui's three lakes, gathering specific components from each of the lake guardians: Azelf's Fang, Uxie's Claw, and Mesprit's Plume. Once these materials are brought to the Shrouded Ruins, the lake guardians themselves craft the Red Chain for the player. With the completed chain, the player ascends Mount Coronet to its summit. Depending on whether the player collaborated with Adaman or Irida to find the components, either Dialga or Palkia will manifest at the Temple of Sinnoh. However, when the player attempts to deploy the Red Chain to pacify the Legendary Pokémon, the item shatters. After the player successfully captures this initial Legendary, its counterpart materializes, forcing the player to retreat. Guided by a clue from the recently caught Legendary Pokémon, Professor Laventon ingeniously repurposes the Red Chain's fragments, combining them with a sample of Origin Ore, to fashion the Origin Ball. This unique Poké Ball is subsequently used by the player to capture the remaining Legendary Pokémon at the Temple of Sinnoh.

Conversely, in the Sinnoh-based games, specifically Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, Brilliant Diamond, and Shining Pearl, Team Galactic's commanders—Mars, Jupiter, and Saturn—successfully capture Mesprit, Uxie, and Azelf, respectively, from their native lakes. They transport these Pokémon to Team Galactic HQ, where gems are extracted from their forms to forge the Red Chain. Despite the player's efforts to liberate the lake guardians, the Red Chain is ultimately completed. At the Spear Pillar, Cyrus deploys the chain to summon and exert control over either Dialga or Palkia. Yet, as the Legendary Pokémon prepares to fulfill Cyrus's ambition of creating a new universe, the lake guardians suddenly appear, obliterating the Red Chain and thereby preventing the cosmic restructuring.

Because the player managed to free the lake guardians after only one Red Chain had been created, Cyrus is compelled to fashion a second chain through artificial means. Employing both of these chains, he summons both Dialga and Palkia to the Spear Pillar, intending to entirely recreate the universe. Although the lake guardians attempt to intervene once more, they prove incapable of maintaining equilibrium against both Legendary Pokémon simultaneously. However, Giratina, enraged by Cyrus's actions, emerges and drags Cyrus into the Distortion World, a confrontation that presumably results in the destruction of the Red Chains.

Information gleaned from Cyrus's computer illuminates his understanding of the Red Chain: Ancient myths suggest a Pokémon used its power to create Sinnoh.
