However, traditional Poké Balls restrict this Pokémon's full capabilities. The Red Chain, conversely, can shackle this Pokémon, allowing its immense power to be utilized without limitation. The computer also postulates a connection between the Pokémon of the lakes and the Pokémon of Mt. Coronet, stating that capturing the lake Pokémon would "free" the Mt. Coronet Pokémon. Furthermore, it details that crystals extracted from the lake Pokémon can be used to fashion a Red Chain, which can then be used to summon and bind the mythical Pokémon, compelling it to obey commands.

In Pokémon the Series, the Red Chain's origin diverges from the games; it is not derived from lake guardian crystals but rather from meteorites found in Veilstone City, which have been imbued with the power of Arceus. This infusion explains the chain's potent ability to control Legendary Pokémon. Its initial mention occurs in Enter Galactic!, where Saturn reveals that although Team Galactic failed to secure all Veilstone City meteorites, the quantity they acquired should suffice for crafting the Red Chain. Later, in Double Team Turnover!, Mars reports to Cyrus that the Fuego Ironworks successfully concluded their experiments on the meteorites, advancing the project to its final phase. The chain's completion and transfer to Team Galactic HQ are depicted in Unlocking the Red Chain of Events!. Notably, Charon deliberately leaves behind a fragment of the chain for the International Police to discover; their attempt to scan it results in their database being entirely wiped. In The Needs of Three!, following Hunter J's successful capture and delivery of the lake guardians to Team Galactic, Cyrus attaches a Red Chain fragment to each, enabling him to control them. Despite this, the three Legendary Pokémon manage to teleport Ash, Brock, and Dawn to their location before being fully subdued. During The Battle Finale of Legend!, Cyrus utilizes the Red Chains, coupled with the powers of the lake guardians and the Adamant and Lustrous Orbs, to summon Dialga and Palkia to the Spear Pillar, forcing them to initiate the creation of a new universe. As the chains coil around the two Legendary Pokémon, they assume a wheel-like form, reminiscent of the structure surrounding Arceus's body. Nevertheless, through the combined efforts of Ash, Brock, Dawn, and Cynthia, the Red Chains binding both the lake guardians and Dialga and Palkia are shattered, halting the genesis of the new universe.

A distinct Red Chain is featured in The Gates of Warp! and Showdown at the Gates of Warp!, fabricated by Alternate World Team Rocket. This chain was used to lock Dialga and Palkia into a continuous battle, which caused Pokémon in the alternate Sinnoh region to de-evolve and eventually revert into Eggs, enabling the villainous team to easily steal them. After Alternate World Team Rocket's defeat, Dialga's power began to spiral out of control. Ash, Dawn, Goh, Chloe, and their alternate world counterparts, unable to break the Red Chain, beseeched Arceus to intervene and stop the conflict between Dialga and Palkia. In response to their plea, a spectral form of Arceus emerged from the Red Chain itself, causing it to vanish and bringing an end to the battle.

In The New World manga, Cyrus is depicted employing the Red Chain to subjugate and control Dialga and Palkia. The chain is subsequently broken by Giratina's Shadow Ball attack, liberating Dialga and Palkia from Cyrus's grasp and allowing their escape.

The Red Chain's manga debut is in Halting Honchkrow, where its completion at the Team Galactic HQ is shown. In Creeping Away from Cradily, Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum successfully free the lake guardians from Team Galactic's confinement before a second Red Chain can be forged. This forces Cyrus to substitute it with an artificially duplicated, less potent copy. Double Trouble with Dialga and Palkia I features Cyrus using both chains to summon Dialga and Palkia to the Spear Pillar, compelling them to battle each other to eradicate the existing world and usher in a new one. However, in Double Trouble with Dialga and Palkia II, Sinnoh's Gym Leaders, participating in the confrontation against Team Galactic, attack and manage to sever the duplicate chain. Although the two Legendary Pokémon persist in their fight, Cyrus's simultaneous control over both is broken. Finally, in Double Trouble with Dialga and Palkia VI, the three Pokédex holders of Sinnoh exploit this vulnerability, destroying the remaining chain and thereby stopping the clash between Dialga and Palkia.

Another instance of the Red Chain first appeared in (Violent) Earthquake! Cyrus's Consuming Ambition, where Cyrus successfully created it by siphoning energy from the three lake guardians. In The Advent of the Legendary Pokémon!!, Cyrus employs the chain to awaken and command Dialga at the Spear Pillar, aiming to reset the world to its primordial state and forge a new universe. Yet, amidst the ensuing struggle, the lake guardians, drawn by Hareta's earnest desire to free Dialga's spirit, unexpectedly materialize. They utilize their Psychic abilities to empower Dialga, enabling it to destroy the chain and dismantle Cyrus's grand scheme.

The Red Chain's presence as a dummied-out Key Item in Generation IV suggests that during its development, it might have been envisioned to serve a similar function to other Legendary artifacts from Generations II and III. Items such as the Rainbow Wing, Silver Wing, Red Orb, and Blue Orb all play a role in summoning the respective pairs of Legendary game mascots for Johto and Hoenn. Regardless of its original intent, the Red Chain holds no practical purpose if it is illicitly introduced into the Bag. The crafting process of the Red Chain in Pokémon Legends: Arceus may draw inspiration from the Norse mythology tale of Gleipnir. Gleipnir is famously composed of six purportedly impossible ingredients, paralleling how the lake guardians, despite their descriptions, do not overtly possess plumes, fangs, or claws.

Title: Town Map
The Town Map (Japanese: タウンマップ Town Map) serves as a cartographic instrument accessible across numerous main series Pokémon installments. Its iteration found within the Pokégear device is additionally known as the Pokégear Map. This item is designated as a Key Item in all games from Generation I and their respective remakes, Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum, every Generation V title, and Pokémon X and Y.

Beyond player-held items, fixed Town Maps can be observed on walls within certain structures beginning from Generation I. These include Pokémon Centers in Generations III and IV, Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, and the gates present in Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, Platinum, Brilliant Diamond, and Shining Pearl.

Access to the Town Map varies in some games: In the Johto region, players acquire a Map Card subsequent to a guided tour of Cherrygrove City provided by the Guide Gent, enabling them to consult the Town Map via their Pokégear. The Hoenn region's PokéNav or PokéNav Plus integrates a Town Map function. Similarly, Sinnoh's Pokétch features map-related capabilities. For Generation VIII and the Galar region, the Town Map is incorporated directly into the game's menu system. The Alola region's Rotom Pokédex provides a comprehensive Town Map for each of its four islands, alongside the exterior of Aether Paradise. In the Hisui region, the Arc Phone fulfills the role of the Town Map. The Paldea region also utilizes the Rotom Phone for its Town Map functionality.

Japanese editions of Pokémon Red, Green, Blue, and Yellow initially came with a physical poster depicting the Kanto map, labeled "Pocket Monsters Town Map." In Pokémon Gold, Silver, Crystal, HeartGold, and SoulSilver, the Pokégear's map is referred to as "Town Map" by the Guide Gent in Cherrygrove City, by individuals calling the player through the Pokégear's phone, and during the quiz at the Goldenrod Radio Tower. Conversely, the character greeting the player upon entering Kanto on Route 27 calls it the "Pokégear Map."

In Pokémon Ruby, Sapphire, and Emerald, the map function from the PokéNav can also be seen displayed on walls in specific buildings. While these wall-mounted maps are called a "Town Map" in Japanese, this designation was omitted in the English localization. For example: the literal translation reads "<player>'s face came closer to the Town Map of the Hoenn region," whereas the localization states "<player> took a closer look at the Hoenn region map." Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire's PokéNav Plus BuzzNav identifies the AreaNav map as a Town Map, as demonstrated by the phrase: "<Pokémon> checked a destination with <player> using the Town Map at... <location>." Professor Kukui confirms the detailed map within the Rotom Pokédex is named "Town Map" in Pokémon Sun, Moon, Ultra Sun, and Ultra Moon.

On the Town Map, the player's current position is indicated by their overworld sprite. A blinking square cursor permits the selection of alternative locations; this cursor defaults to the player's current spot but can be manipulated by pressing up or down. The title of the currently highlighted location appears at the screen's apex.

The standard selectable locations, which are navigated sequentially, include: Pallet Town, Route 1, Viridian City, Route 2, Viridian Forest, Diglett's Cave, Pewter City, Route 3, Mt. Moon, Route 4, Cerulean City, Route 24, Route 25, Sea Cottage, Route 5, Route 6, Vermilion City, S.S. Anne, Route 9, Rock Tunnel, Route 10, Lavender Town, Pokémon Tower, Route 8, Route 7, Celadon City, Saffron City, Route 11, Route 12, Route 13, Route 14, Route 15, Route 16, Route 17, Route 18, Fuchsia City, Safari Zone, Sea Route 19, Seafoam Islands, Sea Route 20, Cinnabar Island, Sea Route 21, Route 22, Route 23, Victory Road, Indigo Plateau, and Power Plant.

Certain places are only selectable when the player is physically present in that specific location, as the map automatically shows the current spot. These include: Underground Path (Kanto Routes 5–6), Underground Path (Kanto Routes 7–8) (both labeled "Underground Path" on the Town Map), Team Rocket Hideout (labeled "Rocket HQ"), Silph Co., Pokémon Mansion (labeled "PKMN Mansion"), Pokémon League, and Cerulean Cave. However, once the player shifts the cursor away, these locations become unselectable again until the Town Map is reopened. Irrespective of the player's location, pressing up or down initially selects Route 1 or Power Plant, respectively.

Due to a programming oversight, if a player opens the Town Map while on Route 1 and presses up, it will initially appear to do nothing, as the selection moves from Route 1 (the current position) back to Route 1. The player must press up twice to highlight Viridian City, the subsequent location. Similarly, from the Power Plant, pressing down initially yields no apparent change; two presses are required to select Indigo Plateau, the preceding location.

The default displayed location name is "Silph Co." when the map is used anywhere inside the company building, including the elevator. An exception occurs on the first floor of Silph Co. (excluding the elevator), where "Saffron City" is displayed instead. When used outdoors or in the lobby, the map defaults to "Indigo Plateau." However, within the Elite Four, Champion, or Hall of Fame rooms, "Pokémon League" is displayed (even though items like the Town Map are unusable in the Hall of Fame, the name is still stored in game data).

The English version of the Town Map refers to "Cerulean Cave," while the Japanese version names it ななしのどうくつ (Nameless Cave). This divergence is also reflected by the English game manual and some official English guides, which call it the "Unknown Dungeon." In this generation, the Japanese Town Map has a 9-character display limit for place names. This limit is sufficient for most Japanese location names except Silph Co., but common words such as シティ (City), タウン (Town), しま (Island, as in Cinnabar Island), こうげん (Plateau, as in Indigo Plateau), and カンパニー (Company, as in Silph Co.) are often omitted. For example, "Cerulean City" is fully displayed in English, but its Japanese counterpart appears as ハナダ (Hanada) instead of ハナダシティ (Hanada City). Silph Co. is shown as シルフほんしゃビル (Sylph Main Office Building), potentially a shortened form of シルフ カンパニー ほんしゃ ビル (Sylph Company Main Office Building).

In Pokémon Gold, Silver, and Crystal, the Town Map becomes available as a Pokégear function upon obtaining the Map Card (Japanese: マップカード Map Card). In these games, the Town Map dynamically shows the current region, initially Johto, and then Kanto once the player ventures there. The graphical representation of towns and cities on the Town Map is gender-dependent: orange squares for a boy protagonist, blue for a girl. A view of the Town Map, sans Pokégear buttons, is visible on the Pokédex Area screen.

Initially, the map in Generation III's Kanto remakes only presents the Kanto region. After the player explores the Sevii Islands, Celio provides an enhanced map that includes One, Two, and Three Islands. Following entry into the Hall of Fame and acquiring the National Pokédex, Celio performs a final upgrade, unveiling the remaining four Sevii Islands. Players can freely switch between the four distinct map sections (mainland Kanto; One, Two, and Three Island; Four and Five Island and Navel Rock; and Six, Seven, and Birth Island) as each new map segment is obtained.

Some locations incorporate a "guide" (Japanese: ガイド guide) offering details about significant spots. This feature saw expansion in Pokémon Diamond and Pearl, covering every location on the Town Map. The Sinnoh Town Map includes descriptive flavor text for all locations visited by the player, as well as for all routes, regardless of whether they have been visited. However, special areas like Fullmoon Island only appear on the map once the player reaches them. The Sinnoh map itself visually resembles a Nintendo DS. Its acquisition occurs in Jubilife City, at the Trainers' School, when the player's rival, Barry, receives the Parcel from his mother. Platinum's Town Map location descriptions appear to be directly carried over from Pokémon Diamond and Pearl; for instance, Route 212's description mentions a house for trading shards for TMs, yet in Platinum, the TM-trading woman has been replaced by a Move Tutor. In HeartGold and SoulSilver, mirroring the Generation II titles, the Town Map...

Title: Coin Case

Making its debut in Generation I, the Coin Case (Japanese: コインケース Coin Case) functions as a Key Item. Its primary function involves holding the currency obtained from Game Corners. This item is dedicated to holding coins, the exclusive currency of the Celadon Game Corner, Goldenrod Game Corner, Mauville Game Corner, and Veilstone Game Corner. Possession of this item within the player's Bag is mandatory for acquiring coins (whether through purchase or other means), engaging in the games, or exchanging coins for available prizes. Its storage capacity differs across generations: in Generation I, II, and III titles, it holds a maximum of 9,999 coins, whereas in Generation IV games, this limit increases significantly to 50,000 coins. Any coins received that would surpass the current limit are consequently lost, and the player is subsequently prevented from purchasing further coins. It's important to note that the Coin Case is not a prerequisite for participation at either the Joyful Game Corner or the Game Corner found in Mossdeep City. When activated from the Bag in the overworld, this item displays the total number of coins the player currently possesses. A known translation error in the English localizations of Pokémon Gold and Silver is responsible for glitches involving the Coin Case within those specific titles.

Title: Old Rod

This essential Key Item, known in Japan as ボロのつりざお (Worn-out Fishing Rod), first appeared in Generation I, granting trainers the ability to catch Pokémon from aquatic environments. Its more potent counterparts are the Good Rod and Super Rod. With the Old Rod, players can specifically draw Pokémon out of any water source. The Pokémon reeled in using this item typically possess lower levels compared to those caught with the Good Rod or Super Rod. Within Pokémon: Magikarp Jump, players utilize the Old Rod to catch Magikarp, receiving progressively superior versions (So-So, Good, Great, Rare, Pro's, and Supreme) during their journey, which enable them to discover new Magikarp patterns. Cilan, during the episode Best Wishes Until We Meet Again!, referenced Mr. Briney of Dewford Town's upcoming Old Rod Fishing Rally, leading to Cilan's departure for Johto to compete in the event. In File 2: Cubone, Red acquired an Old Rod from the Fishing Guru located in Vermilion City. It also featured in the Pokémon Pocket Monsters manga.

This item was adapted into a Trainer card for the Pokémon Trading Card Game, debuting in the English Neo Series (known as the Neo Era in Japan). Its initial release was in the Japanese Awakening Legends expansion, prior to its English introduction in the Neo Revelation expansion, showcasing artwork by Keiji Kinebuchi. A subsequent reprint, featuring new artwork from Shizurow, emerged in the Japanese Offense and Defense of the Furthest Ends expansion, later reaching English players through the EX Dragon Frontiers expansion. As a TCG card, Old Rod allows a player to flip two coins; if both results are heads, the player may retrieve a Baby Pokémon (specific to Neo Revelation), Basic Pokémon, or an Evolution card from their discard pile and add it to their hand. Conversely, if both coins land on tails, the player is permitted to search their discard pile for a Trainer card instead and return it to their hand. During Generation I, the Old Rod exclusively yielded Magikarp, invariably at level 5.

Title: Good Rod

The Good Rod, identified in Japanese as いいつりざお (Good Fishing Rod), was introduced as a Key Item in Generation I. This essential tool enables players to engage in fishing activities within various aquatic environments. It occupies an intermediate position between the less potent Old Rod and the more powerful Super Rod. Utilizing the Good Rod allows for the capture of wild Pokémon from water, with these Pokémon appearing at higher levels compared to those caught with the Old Rod, but at lower levels than those encountered using the Super Rod.

The Good Rod later transitioned into the Pokémon Trading Card Game, appearing as a Trainer card (known as an Item card in Japan) during the English HeartGold & SoulSilver Series. Its first availability was in the Leafeon Deck, part of the Leafeon vs Metagross Expert Deck, before its English debut in the Unleashed expansion, featuring an illustration by Wataru Kawahara. In the TCG, playing the Good Rod requires the player to flip a coin. If the coin shows heads, the player can locate a Pokémon from their discard pile and place it at the top of their deck. Conversely, if tails is flipped, the player instead searches their discard pile for a Trainer card to place on top of their deck. In its inaugural generation, the Good Rod's catches were limited to Poliwag and Goldeen, both consistently appearing at level 10.

Title: Super Rod

This Key Item, known in Japanese as すごいつりざお (Amazing Fishing Rod), made its debut in Generation I. It provides trainers the ability to cast a line into water sources to capture wild Pokémon. Compared to its less potent counterparts, the Old Rod and Good Rod, the Super Rod enables the player to find Pokémon at elevated levels.

The Super Rod has made appearances across various Pokémon media. During the anime episode "Climbing the Tower of Success!", it was a crucial item for Iris in the Wishing Bell Festival's scavenger hunt. In the manga adaptation, Pokémon Adventures, Red received the Super Rod from the Fishing Guru located on Silence Bridge (File 3: Giovanni). He promptly utilized it to secure a Psyduck. Later, in File 4: Charizard, Red was observed employing the Super Rod again, this time to ponder the enigmatic Pokémon within Cerulean Cave. Its presence is also noted in the Pokémon Pocket Monsters manga series.

Within the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Super Rod debuted as a Trainer card during the English Neo Series (Japanese: Neo Era). Its initial release occurred in the Japanese expansion "Gold, Silver, to a New World..." and the English "Neo Genesis" set, featuring art by Keiji Kinebuchi. This card instructed players to flip a coin: a heads result allowed them to retrieve an Evolution card from their discard pile into their hand, while tails permitted them to add a Basic Pokémon to their hand instead. The Super Rod later underwent reclassification as an Item card during the English Black & White Series (Japanese: Black & White Era), acquiring an entirely new function. Its updated ability permitted players to shuffle any combination of three Pokémon and Basic Energy cards from their discard pile back into their deck. Subsequently, during the Scarlet & Violet Series (Japanese: Scarlet & Violet Era), a minor wording adjustment was made, adding "up to" to its text, thereby granting players the flexibility to return fewer than three cards if desired.

Notably, in Generation V, the Super Rod stands as the sole fishing rod accessible to players. Although the Old and Good Rods remain within the game's code, they are unobtainable through normal gameplay and are unusable. If illicitly acquired via hacking, these items possess the unique characteristic of being capable of being held by a Pokémon, a trait not shared by standard Key Items.

Title: Sprayduck

Making its debut in Generation IV, the Sprayduck is a Key Item, known in Japanese as コダックじょうろ (Koduck Watering Can). Its design visually emulates a Psyduck-shaped watering can. This item grants players the capability to irrigate their cultivated Berries. Consistent and appropriate watering over an adequate duration ensures that the ensuing Berry trees will produce a greater abundance of Berries for harvesting and storage. In the episode "Oh Do You Know The Poffin Plan," a Sprayduck had a brief appearance, with Forsythia and her Roserade employing it to tend to the botanical life surrounding her home. It resurfaced for a short moment alongside Forsythia in "Unlocking the Red Chain of Events!". An Aroma Lady utilized a Sprayduck during the introductory scenes of "Arceus and the Jewel of Life". In "Honey for Combee," Platinum was shown inspecting a Sprayduck. Furthermore, the Sprayduck has been showcased in numerous pieces of officially licensed merchandise.

Title: Poffin Case

A Key Item introduced in Generation IV, the Poffin Case (Japanese: ポフィンケース Poffin Case) functions as storage for Poffins. These confections, prepared from Berries, can be held within the case, which accommodates up to 100 units. Players may fill it with Poffins they've crafted at the Poffin House or purchased from the Veilstone Department Store. In "Perturbed by Pachirisu," Platinum received a Poffin Case from the Pokémon Fan Club Chairman while preparing for a Pokémon Super Contest. The gallery below showcases Pokémon Trading Card Game cards that reference or depict the Poffin Case. Additionally, this item has been incorporated into various officially licensed figures and merchandise.

Title: Bicycle This entry pertains to the Key Item known as Bicycle. Information regarding the bicycle variations found in the Hoenn region can be found under Mach Bike and Acro Bike. For details on the bicycle model present in the Galar region, consult Rotom Bike. Referred to as じてんしゃ (Bicycle) in Japanese, the Bicycle is a Key Item acquired within the games, designed to enable quicker overworld travel. These items are typically procured from dedicated Bicycle Shops. During the Generation I titles and their remakes, Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen, the Miracle Cycle establishment in Cerulean City lists Bicycles for sale at a price of $1,000,000. This sum exceeds the maximum possible monetary amount a player can accumulate in these particular games by $1. Consequently, players are required to exchange a Bike Voucher to secure this item. The Bicycle functions as a toggle, switching the player between riding and walking. Utilizing this item permits quicker traversal compared to simply walking, employing Running Shoes, or wearing Roller Skates. Mounting the Bicycle is prohibited if the player is currently indoors, engaged in surfing, or accompanied by another character. Should the player enter an indoor area or commence surfing, they will automatically dismount the Bicycle. In Pokémon Yellow, HeartGold, and SoulSilver, any Pokémon following the player will re-enter its Poké Ball when the player begins cycling. While actively riding the Bicycle, both the Running Shoes and Roller Skates become unavailable for use. Moreover, the Bicycle enables passage along designated Cycling Roads, specifically Routes 16 through 18 in Kanto, the Seaside Cycling Road on Route 110 in Hoenn, and Route 206 in Sinnoh. Dismounting the Bicycle is not permitted while on any Cycling Road. When mounted, the overworld background music is superseded by a distinct Cycling theme. Upon returning to walking, the standard overworld music will resume. During Generation IV, the Bicycle's movement value, which influences wild Pokémon encounters, is set at 70. This value surpasses that of other movement methods, with the exception of long grass (in Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum, the long grass encounter value matches that of bicycling; conversely, in Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, it is 60 when walking and 80 when running). Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, Platinum, Brilliant Diamond, and Shining Pearl In these games, the Bicycle is equipped with two distinct gears: third and fourth. Players may alternate between these gears by pressing the B button. Fourth gear provides greater speed, enabling ascent of muddy slopes and two-tile jumps from bike ramps. Third gear operates at a reduced speed, facilitating only one-tile jumps from ramps and preventing travel up muddy slopes. Exclusively in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, players are presented with a choice of red, blue, green, or yellow bicycles; however, selection is permanent, and all models share identical functionality. Furthermore, in these specific titles, the player character dons the Cycling Style attire when riding the bicycle or engaging in battles on a Cycling Road. It is important to note that in Pokémon Diamond and Pearl solely, bicycles cannot be operated in snowy areas or within gatehouses. The Nintendo 3DS's Circle Pad allows for multidirectional movement. Upon obtaining the Bicycle, players are offered a choice between a green and a yellow model; once selected, this choice is final, though both versions are functionally identical. A known glitch exists: if a player saves while on a Cycling Road and then initiates a new game, the Bicycle (and Surf outside of battle) will be unusable anywhere in the new save file until one of the following actions is performed for the first time: blacking out (either in combat or on the overworld), using Dig, Teleport, or Fly outside of battle, deploying an Escape Rope, or entering and subsequently exiting a Cycling Road. Within Pokémon Ruby, Sapphire, and Emerald, along with their remakes Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, two distinct Bicycle models are available: the Mach Bike, which offers greater speed than a standard Bicycle, and the Acro Bike, which matches the standard Bicycle's speed but also allows for performing stunts. Both models grant access to previously unreachable locations. In Generation III, players are unable to possess both bicycles concurrently, instead swapping models at Rydel's Cycles. In Generation VI, players also initially swap models at Rydel's Cycles. However, they can eventually hold both the Mach Bike and Acro Bike simultaneously in their Bag after successfully promoting Rydel's Cycles to three specific NPCs: a Hex Maniac found on a northern muddy slope in the Route 111 Desert, necessitating the Mach Bike for ascent; a Bird Keeper located within the northernmost bridge maze on Route 119, requiring Surf and Waterfall in addition to the Acro Bike; and a Triathlete who rides a bike southeast of the Pokémon Day Care on the Battle Resort. Pokémon Sword and Shield present players with the Rotom Bike. This particular bicycle possesses the ability to perform temporary high-speed dashes. Subsequent upgrades, specifically the Water Mode, allow it to traverse aquatic surfaces. In Pokémon Sun, Moon, Ultra Sun, and Ultra Moon, the function of rapid overworld transportation is assumed by various Ride Pokémon, exemplified by Tauros Charge. Within Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, players gain the ability to mount specific walking Pokémon; additionally, the traditional Cycling Road is reimagined as Pokémon Road, a location that encourages Trainers to journey alongside their Pokémon on foot. Pokémon Legends: Arceus permits players to utilize certain Ride Pokémon, like Wyrdeer, for swifter travel than on foot. Following the commencement of their Treasure Hunt in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, players are able to ride their partner Koraidon or Miraidon. Bicycles make infrequent appearances in Pokémon the Series, as Ash and his companions typically traverse their journeys on foot. Their usage is generally confined to situations of necessity, such as on Cycling Roads or for expediting travel. Misty's bicycle was the inaugural model depicted, first appearing in Pokémon - I Choose You!. Ash "borrowed" it to flee a group of Spearow, and it was inadvertently demolished when his Pikachu unleashed a Thunder Shock on them. Misty subsequently recovered her bike from Nurse Joy in Viridian City during the episode Gotta Catch Ya Later. Pikachu's destruction of a female traveling partner's bicycle evolved into a recurring comedic element across the subsequent two series: May's bicycle met its fate in Get the Show on the Road!, and Dawn's in Two Degrees of Separation. The ability to rent bicycles for use on Cycling Roads has been depicted, notably in The Bridge Bike Gang and One Big Happiny Family!. While certain characters possess bicycles, they seldom employ them; for instance, Professor Oak utilized a bicycle in The Power Of One and The Road Most Traveled! to reach Delia's residence. Barry was observed with a bike in Gotta Get a Gible!, stating he acquired it in Eterna City during a free bicycle distribution event; this bike was ultimately consumed by a wild Gible. In The Bridge Bike Gang, Chopper's bike shared a visual similarity with Zapdos. Mallow's bicycle was featured in Lillie's Egg-xhilarating Challenge!. In Pokémon Origins, Red frequently employed a bicycle for mobility. He was shown acquiring it at the start of File 2: Cubone, receiving it in exchange for a Bike Voucher from the Pokémon Fan Club Chairman. Within Play Misty For Me, it was disclosed that Misty's Bicycle, which mirrored the anime's storyline by being accidentally demolished by Ash's Pikachu, was a bespoke item valued at $10,000. Professor Oak utilized a bicycle to pursue Pokémon that had escaped his Laboratory in Bulbasaur Come Home!. Red engaged in a cycling competition traversing Routes 11 and 12 in Wake Up—You're Snorlax!, riding a bicycle obtained through a coupon from the Pokémon Fan Club Chairman. In Victim of Venusaur, Red used his bicycle to arrive and assist Lt. Surge and Bill in their confrontation with Bruno on Cerise Island. Gold engaged in a bicycle race against Whitney, spanning from Goldenrod City to the National Park, as depicted in Smeargle Smudge and How Do You Do, Sudowoodo?; Gold, however, modified his Bicycle into a push scooter. Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum were gifted Bicycles by Rad Rickshaw as an expression of thanks following their rescue of him from Team Galactic in Ring Around the Roserade II; the three protagonists subsequently used these new Bicycles to traverse the Cycling Road. Rad Rickshaw's ownership of a tandem bike was also revealed in Cautious Clefairy. A Bicycle was observed within the Miracle Cycle shop located in Cerulean City. Strip 14 features Delia mentioning that Ash possesses a bicycle. Within the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Bicycle made its debut as an Item card during the English Black & White Series (corresponding to the Japanese BW Era). Initially launched in the Cold Flare expansion, the Bicycle subsequently appeared in the Plasma Storm expansion, with both versions illustrated by Toyste Beach. This Trainer card replicates the effect of Mail from Bill from the Neo Destiny expansion, permitting the player to draw cards until their hand contains four cards. Pokémon Power #1 includes an advertisement on page 3 for bicycles available for purchase at

Title: Member Card

This unique Key Item, known in Japanese as メンバーズカード (Member's Card), is obtainable exclusively through special events across Pokémon Platinum, Brilliant Diamond, and Shining Pearl versions. Within Pokémon Platinum, it held the position of the second among four distinct event-related collectibles in the Sinnoh region. Its distribution came after the Secret Key, and it was subsequently followed by Oak's Letter and the Azure Flute, which was not yet released at that time.

Possessing the Member Card allows a trainer entry into Canalave City's Harbor Inn. Upon entering, an encounter with an enigmatic elderly man leads to a vivid nightmare, prompting a journey to Newmoon Island. There, the Mythical Pokémon Darkrai becomes available for capture. Should Darkrai be successfully acquired, a resounding voice addresses the player, who then awakens from the nightmare, finding Darkrai either in their active party or stored within the PC. The opportunity to re-engage Darkrai in battle is present if it was previously fled from or knocked out.

Prior to utilizing the Member Card for access to the Harbor Inn, the trainer is required to have encountered Cresselia, acquired the Lunar Wing, and employed it to alleviate the nightmares Darkrai imposes upon Eldritch's son. Regarding its distribution in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl: The in-game description for the Member Card states that its most recent marked date was half a century prior. Furthermore, its official artwork displays the specific date "1958.12.1", precisely fifty years before its initial Japanese rollout. When a player collects this item from a deliveryman at a Poké Mart, it is identified by the name "Member's Card".

Title: Azure Flute

Known as てんかいのふえ (Heaven Flute) in Japanese, the Azure Flute functions as a Key Item across the Sinnoh region titles and in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. Its primary function is to grant passage to the Hall of Origin, the dwelling place of Arceus. Throughout Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum, this item remained inaccessible. Although conceived as an event-exclusive reward, it was ultimately never released to players. Conversely, within Pokémon Legends: Arceus, the Azure Flute becomes available during the primary narrative, evolving from the Celestica Flute. Successfully capturing Arceus in Pokémon Legends: Arceus unlocks the Azure Flute as a save data incentive for Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl.

To utilize the Azure Flute in the traditional Sinnoh adventures, trainers must first meet specific prerequisites: capturing either Dialga or Palkia, achieving Hall of Fame status, and acquiring the National Pokédex. Upon arriving at the Spear Pillar, crowning Mount Coronet, and advancing a single step northward from its entry point, the player will be prompted to employ the flute. This action triggers a distinctive, unsettling melody, followed by a reload of the Spear Pillar area, revealing a precipitous stairway ascending to the Hall of Origin. Once employed and Arceus has been captured, subsequent attempts to play the Azure Flute at the Spear Pillar will cease to open the Hall of Origin. Instead, the message "The Azure Flute echoed hollowly..." will display. However, if Arceus is accidentally defeated, the flute can be re-used to resummon the Pokémon.

In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, the Celestica Flute undergoes a transformation into the Azure Flute following the player's triumph over Volo and Giratina within the Temple of Sinnoh. Its usage at the Temple of Sinnoh, contingent on completing the Hisui Pokédex of all non-Mythical Pokémon, grants access to the Hall of Origin for an encounter with Arceus. Mirroring the Celestica Flute's functionality, players can employ it to summon the various rideable Pokémon: Wyrdeer, Ursaluna, Basculegion, Sneasler, and Hisuian Braviary.

A key point is that the Azure Flute was never officially released during the fourth generation of games. Junichi Masuda clarified in a 2013 interview with Nintendo World Report that concerns regarding the item's potential for player confusion ultimately led to the decision against its distribution. Even without its distribution, the Wonder Card text accompanying the Arceus given out for Pokémon's 20th Anniversary contained a direct reference to the Azure Flute. Intriguingly, in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, this item holds the distinction of potentially being the very first object a player acquires.

Within Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum, mere possession of the Azure Flute—even through means like save file modification to place it directly in the Bag—is insufficient for accessing the Hall of Origin. Players were required to genuinely receive a Wonder Card containing the Azure Flute via Mystery Gift and then retrieve it from the Poké Mart deliveryman, an action that correctly activates the necessary event flag. Among event items in Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum, the Azure Flute stands alone as the sole item never distributed; notably, other event items saw distribution exclusively for Platinum. In Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, a cryptic radio broadcast accessible within the Ruins of Alph bears a resemblance to the Azure Flute's sound. This transmission also boosts the likelihood of encountering Unown forms that the player has not yet documented.

The Japanese phrase てんかいのふえ translates directly to "flute of heaven." The component 天界 (てんかい) denotes "heaven" or "the heavens," encompassing both the celestial expanse and a religious concept of heaven, akin to its English counterpart. 笛 (ふえ) specifically refers to a flute or a comparable wind instrument. The English term "Azure" describes the shade of a clear, blue sky and, through metonymy, serves as a poetic descriptor for such a sky itself.

Title: Contest Pass

A Contest Pass (Japanese: コンテストパス Contest Pass) is an essential Key Item for participating in Pokémon Contests. Its front bears an illustration of an award Ribbon. In the games Pokémon Ruby, Sapphire, Omega Ruby, and Alpha Sapphire, possessing a Contest Pass is a prerequisite for entry into Pokémon Contests. However, in Pokémon Emerald, a Contest Pass is not needed; instead, solely a Pokéblock Case fulfills the requirement. For Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum, as well as Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, entry into Pokémon Super Contests/Super Contest Shows merely demands appropriate formal attire, which Johanna provides upon the player's initial visit to the Contest Hall.

Within the animated series, Contest Passes fulfill an identical role to their game counterparts, being mandatory for Pokémon Contest participation. In this canon, Coordinators are permitted to acquire a Contest Pass by registering at any Contest Hall. However, these Passes are geographically restricted, valid only for competitions within their region of acquisition, and possess an expiration date, necessitating renewal. Exemptions apply to unsanctioned events, such as the Terracotta Contest, where the Pokémon Activities Committee does not oversee, and thus, Contest Passes are not a prerequisite.

Contest Passes made their debut in All Things Bright and Beautifly!, when Jessie attempted to participate in the Rustboro Contest with an expired Pass. Jessie reacted with dismay upon learning her Pass was no longer valid, though the receptionist assured her that renewal was an option. Once renewed, Jessie secured the final spot in the tournament. Subsequently, Ash, May, and Brock arrived, also aspiring to compete; despite the attendant confirming the contest had reached its full capacity, Passes were nonetheless provided for them.

In The Saffron Con, May acquired her Kanto region Contest Pass, accompanied by a Ribbon case. Brock's Kanto Contest Pass was secured in What I Did for Love! following an encounter with Yuma, a fellow Pokémon Breeder and past Grand Festival champion. Captivated by her, Brock promptly proceeded to the reception desk to register for the Gardenia Contest after she encouraged his participation. During Mounting a Coordinator Assault!, as Dawn rehearsed for her initial stage appearance, she observed Ash's Aipom showing an affinity for Pokémon Contests and a desire to join. She persuaded Ash to compete, and Brock advised him to expedite registration due to the impending Jubilife Contest. Dawn then remembered she had neglected to sign up herself. Consequently, Ash and Dawn hastened to the Contest Hall to secure their Contest Passes and register, also receiving a rule book, a Ribbon case, a Ball Capsule, and an envelope brimming with Seals. Chloe was given a Hoenn Contest Pass by Lisia in Reuniting for the First Time!, subsequent to Lisia scouting her for a potential showing at the Lilycove City Contest Spectacular. Ruby's Hoenn Contest Pass was acquired in Mind-Boggling with Medicham.

Title: Magma Stone

The Magma Stone (Japanese: かざんのおきいし Volcano Stone) is a crucial Key Item that debuted in the Generation IV games, with a strong connection to the Legendary Pokémon Heatran. In Pokémon Black 2 and White 2, players can trigger an encounter with a wild Heatran by bringing the Magma Stone to a specific chamber within Reversal Mountain. In Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum, as well as Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, this artifact is found resting in Stark Mountain. Pokémon Platinum further explains that the Magma Stone possesses the ability to control Heatran, the Legendary creature residing in that very mountain. Despite its existence in the games' data as an item, the player cannot legitimately acquire it, and it provides no in-game effect even if obtained through hacking.

In Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, Brilliant Diamond, and Shining Pearl, Buck initially removes the Magma Stone from Stark Mountain, but he reinstates it after receiving guidance from his grandfather at the Survival Area. Conversely, in Pokémon Platinum, Charon appropriates the Magma Stone from Stark Mountain, only for Looker to have his Croagunk confiscate it. Subsequent to Charon's apprehension, Buck returns the Magma Stone to its rightful resting place. Once the Magma Stone has been returned, players gain the opportunity to encounter a wild Heatran precisely where the item originally lay.

The Magma Stone also featured prominently in the manga series, specifically in "The Magma Stone" storyline. There, Charon's act of dislodging it from its stone altar inside Stark Mountain caused Heatran to awaken and embark on a destructive rampage throughout the mountain. Aided by Looker and his Croagunk, Buck ultimately succeeded in repositioning the Magma Stone onto the altar, thereby resealing Heatran and causing its disappearance. Its initial appearance in the manga was documented in "Interrupting Ivysaur," where Buck aimed to protect it from Team Galactic by taking it away. However, as revealed in "Exit Empoleon," Buck's removal of the Magma Stone inadvertently unsealed Heatran, unexpectedly enabling Charon to capture the Lava Dome Pokémon. The Magma Stone also appeared in "A Clash! Hareta vs. Charon" and "The Anger of Legendary Pokémon Heatran," where Charon's activities at Stark Mountain led to it being knocked off its pedestal, once more causing Heatran to rampage. Hareta eventually restored the stone to its proper location, which in turn pacified Heatran.

Title: Parcel

Designated as The Parcel (Japanese: おとどけもの Parcel), this Key Item, first appearing in Generation I, was originally known as Oak's Parcel until Generation IV. It has also been variably identified as a package, order, or simply a parcel.

Across Pokémon Red, Blue, Yellow, FireRed, and LeafGreen, this particular Parcel holds a unique Poké Ball, which Professor Oak retains. However, in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, the Parcel encloses Razz Berries, which Professor Oak then distributes between the player and Trace. For Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, Platinum, Brilliant Diamond, and Shining Pearl, the Parcel's contents are two Sinnoh region Town Maps; Barry subsequently hands one of these to the player.

Across Pokémon Red, Blue, Yellow, FireRed, LeafGreen, Let's Go, Pikachu!, and Let's Go, Eevee!, the player may present this Parcel to Professor Oak by engaging him in conversation within his Laboratory, provided the item is present in their Bag. Exclusively in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, upon acquiring the Parcel from the Viridian City Poké Mart clerk, players are granted the choice to promptly hand it over to Professor Oak. Conversely, in prior iterations, players were necessitated to traverse Route 1 entirely to convey this item.

Upon its successful delivery to Professor Oak, the player and rival receive several benefits:
* An entry within Professor Oak's Fame Checker becomes accessible.
* Professor Oak becomes capable of assessing the player's Pokédex completion.
* The PC system gains an "Oak's PC" option, specifically for Pokédex completion evaluations.
* Daisy Oak presents the player with a Town Map, provided interaction occurs.
* The obstruction posed by the old man or the Team Rocket trio at Viridian City's northern egress is removed.
* The rival temporarily appears for an optional skirmish on Route 22.
* The player gains the functionality to establish connections with other games for Pokémon battles or trades.
* The ability to purchase goods from the Viridian City Poké Mart is granted to the player.

A specific glitch impacts Japanese Pokémon Red, Green, Blue, and Yellow, as well as all editions of Pokémon Yellow: should the player acquire additional Pokémon (for instance, by evolving their initial partner Pokémon) prior to handing over the Parcel, it becomes permanently impossible to deliver. This occurs because the game erroneously registers the player as already possessing the Pokédex, prompting Professor Oak to offer rating services instead of accepting the item. Consequently, players face an irreversible inability to advance beyond Viridian City, with critical in-game milestones—such as receiving the Pokédex and Town Map, purchasing goods from the Poké Mart, or linking with other games—remaining inaccessible.

Within Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen, if the Parcel is acquired through unintended glitches rather than from the Viridian City Poké Mart as designed, delivery will be impossible until it is legitimately obtained. The game's verification system relies on an event flag confirming the Parcel's proper reception, rather than simply its current presence in the Bag. Conversely, should the Parcel be legitimately received from the Poké Mart and subsequently removed from the Bag via glitches, the delivery event proceeds successfully even in the item's absence.

In Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, Platinum, Brilliant Diamond, and Shining Pearl, upon presenting the Parcel to the player's rival, Barry, within Jubilife City's Trainers' School, it is unsealed, disclosing its contents: two Town Maps. The player and Barry then each claim one.

Title: Griseous Orb

The Griseous Orb, identified in Japanese as はっきんだま (Platinum Orb), is a held item first introduced in Pokémon Platinum. When equipped by Giratina, it boosts the power of the Pokémon's Dragon- and Ghost-type moves by 20%. Prior to Pokémon Legends: Arceus, this item also served to transform Giratina into its Origin Forme while held. It stands among the special orbs associated with legendary Pokémon. Its benefits persist even when item-negating effects, such as Embargo and Klutz, are active. Moreover, item-manipulating effects like Trick and Fling are incapable of transferring a Griseous Orb to or from Giratina. However, because it no longer causes Giratina's form change, the Griseous Orb is now vulnerable to these item-manipulating and negating effects. From Generation IV through Generation VIII, the Griseous Orb enabled Giratina to sustain its Origin Forme outside the confines of the Distortion World. In Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, an alternative item, the Griseous Core, must be held to initiate Giratina's Origin Forme transformation. During Pokémon Platinum and HeartGold and SoulSilver, the Griseous Orb could only be held by Giratina, and Pokémon possessing it were untradeable. Beginning with Generation V, these specific restrictions were lifted.

Title: Gracidea

This entry focuses on the Gracidea item. For the geographical feature depicted in the eleventh film, please refer to Gracidea (Sinnoh). Known in Japanese as グラシデアのはな (Gracidea Flower), the Gracidea is a Key Item first appearing in Pokémon Platinum. This floral item, visually similar to the blossoms adorning Shaymin's head, grants Land Forme Shaymin the ability to transform into its Sky Forme. In certain cultures, arrangements of these Shaymin-like flowers are exchanged as tokens of appreciation, reflecting Shaymin's designation as the Gratitude Pokémon.

Applying the Gracidea from the Bag to a Land Forme Shaymin triggers its transformation into Sky Forme. However, this action is not possible during nighttime hours or if Shaymin is afflicted with the frozen status condition. Shaymin reverts to its Land Forme automatically under several circumstances: at night, when frozen, upon being stored in the PC (in Generations prior to VII), upon being retrieved from the PC (in Generation VII), when placed into Pokémon Bank, or when left at the Pokémon Day Care or Pokémon Nursery. It's important to note that in Generations IV and V, all legitimately acquired Shaymin possess the fateful encounter flag. Uniquely in Pokémon Legends: Arceus, obtaining the Gracidea can be challenging; even with Shaymin in possession, the item remains inaccessible unless the player has the save data bonus from Pokémon Sword and Shield.

Within the Japanese iteration of My Pokémon Ranch, the Gracidea manifests as a toy following the acquisition of the Platinum-Compatible Version. Its functionality mirrors that in the core series games, enabling Shaymin to alternate between its Land and Sky Formes. This item only becomes visible if the player possesses the Gracidea on their Platinum game cartridge. Since this specific update was never released internationally, My Pokémon Ranch players outside of Japan cannot obtain the Gracidea.

In Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Explorers of Sky, the Gracidea is present. When deployed within a dungeon, the item is consumed, transforming Shaymin into its Sky Forme and boosting its Movement Speed by 1 point. Shaymin then shifts back to its Land Forme upon exiting the dungeon or arriving at a rest stop. Pokémon Ranger: Guardian Signs features a Gracidea field within the Latolato Trail. During a Special Mission centered around Shaymin, the Pokémon utilizes these particular flowers from the field to undergo its transformation. Gracideas can be observed in the Flower Zone of PokéPark Wii: Pikachu's Adventure. To induce their blooming, these Gracideas require watering. Once bloomed, Shaymin transforms and subsequently leads the player character towards the Sky Pavilion.

The cinematic debut of Gracideas occurred in Giratina and the Sky Warrior, where Ash, Dawn, and Brock encountered Layla and her spouse, Moose. The couple was en route to Layla's great-grandmother's centennial celebration, intending to present her with a Gracidea bouquet as a gesture of thanks. While journeying with Shaymin, Layla forged a connection with the Gratitude Pokémon, and upon its contact with the bouquet, Shaymin instantly adopted its Sky Forme. Subsequently, Shaymin rejoined other members of its kind within a vast field brimming with Gracideas. During the film's closing credits, Delia, Johanna, and Lola were shown accepting Gracidea gift baskets, presumably dispatched by their respective children. That identical Gracidea field resurfaced in a flashback sequence within Arceus and the Jewel of Life, when Sheena and Kevin journeyed to the location. An episode titled Keeping in Top Forme! showcased a Gracidea garden, where only a handful of its flowers remained alive. The Shaymin befriended by Marley utilized these surviving Gracideas to transform into its Sky Forme, alongside two other Shaymin, before migrating in search of an alternative garden. Strategy Begins at Home! depicted flowers resembling Gracideas growing in Johanna's personal garden. Gracidea flowers featured in Memories in the Mist!, appearing in a liminal realm between life and death conjured by Tapu Fini's mist. Their blossoms unfurled as Mallow and her mother expressed mutual gratitude during their reunion, which followed the mist's temporary revival of Mallow's mother's spirit. A lone Gracidea was also sighted along the Ancient Poni Path, coinciding with Mallow observing a Shaymin approaching her. During Thank You, Alola! The Journey Continues!, Ulu presented Mallow with Gracideas he discovered en route home. Shaymin swiftly made contact with the blossoms, transforming into its Sky Forme. Furthermore, Gracideas were observed blooming near Aina's Kitchen as Mallow witnessed Shaymin departing with other members of its species.

In the manga, a Gracidea made its debut in The Final Dimensional Duel IV, where Marley acquired it from Floaroma Meadow before proceeding to Route 224. There, she sought out Shaymin, requesting its aid in the conclusive confrontation against Team Galactic, adhering to the directives outlined in Oak's Letter. Within The Final Dimensional Duel X, Shaymin employed that very same Gracidea to transform into its Sky Forme, enabling it to combat Giratina, which was under Charon's command. Pokémon X and Y depict Gracideas within a painting exhibited at the Lumiose Museum. Additionally, in Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Explorers of Sky, a bow adorned with a Gracidea-like design can be seen embellishing the Sky Gifts discovered atop Sky Peak.

Title: Apricorn Box

Designated a Key Item, the Apricorn Box (Japanese: ぼんぐりケース Bonguri Case) was first introduced in Generation IV. This item enables players to gather and store Apricorns. A maximum of 99 units of each Apricorn type can be stored simultaneously. Upon acquiring the Apriblender from the Pokéathlon Dome, players are also able to utilize it directly from their Apricorn Box. In the episode 'An Egg Scramble!', Apricorn Boxes were among the items Khoury sold at the Johto Festival. Additionally, the Pokémon HeartGold SoulSilver: Pokédex Completion Comic depicted an Apricorn Box, used by a female Trainer to demonstrate to Soul how Apricorns yield different Poké Balls.

Title: Squirt Bottle

This essential Key Item, known in Japanese as ゼニガメじょうろ (Zenigame Watering Can), made its debut in Generation II. Prior to Generation VI, its name was formatted as SquirtBottle. Its design resembles a Squirtle-shaped watering can. Across Pokémon Gold, Silver, Crystal, HeartGold, and SoulSilver versions, players utilize it to drench a Sudowoodo masquerading as a tree, which impedes progress on Route 36, thereby initiating a confrontation with the wild creature. Upon the conclusion of this battle, the Sudowoodo ceases to obstruct the route leading to Ecruteak City. Furthermore, within Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, the Squirt Bottle serves the purpose of irrigating Berry plants cultivated within Berry Pots. Main article: Squirt Bottle (Wisdom of Sea and Sky 152). As an Item card within Pokémon Trading Card Game Pocket, the Squirt Bottle debuted as part of the Wisdom of Sea and Sky expansion. Its effect permits the player to discard a (Fire) Energy from the opponent's Active Pokémon. Before Pokémon Journeys: The Series, portrayals of Squirt Bottles in Pokémon the Series featured legs, a design element absent in the games. In the episode Type Casting, James applied a Squirt Bottle to a wild Sudowoodo, mistakenly believing it to be a Grass type. It subsequently resurfaced in Here's Lookin' at You, Elekid, shown in a fantasy sequence where James used it to water his Victreebel. A different Squirt Bottle was observed in Johto Photo Finish, with Mimey employing it to water the flora at the Ketchum residence. Miranda's personal Squirt Bottle featured in The Princess and the Togepi, utilized for watering her garden. Delia employed a Squirt Bottle to care for her flowers in The Road Most Traveled!. Professor Oak utilized a Squirt Bottle in I Choose You! to provide water to both a Bellsprout and an Oddish. Whitney's Cleffa was depicted using a Squirt Bottle on a Sudowoodo in the episode A Huge Mysterious Tree!!. A gallery showcasing Pokémon Trading Card Game cards that reference or prominently display the Squirt Bottle can be found below. Additionally, the Squirt Bottle has been included in numerous officially licensed figures and merchandise.

Title: Lure Ball

Bearing the Japanese name ルアーボール (Lure Ball), this variety of Poké Ball made its debut in Generation II. For capturing wild Pokémon, it offers an increased likelihood of success when dealing with fishing encounters. It stands as one of the distinctive Apricorn Poké Balls crafted by Kurt, specifically fashioned from Blue Apricorns. When deployed from a Trainer's Bag during a wild Pokémon encounter, its purpose is to ensnare the target Pokémon. Its catch rate modifier varies: 3×, 5×, or 4× if the encounter involves fishing, but a standard 1× multiplier for all other types of encounters. A unique exception occurs when attempting to capture an Ultra Beast, where the modifier is invariably fixed at 410/4096× (approximately 0.1×). With the discontinuation of fishing mechanics in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, the Lure Ball's functionality was updated to mirror that of the Dive Ball. Consequently, it now applies a 4× catch rate boost when deployed against a Pokémon situated on or within water. However, in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet versions up to and including v1.1.0, a programming error caused this item to consistently apply only a 1× catch rate modifier, irrespective of the environmental conditions. This issue was subsequently rectified in a patch released between v1.1.0 and v3.0.0. In Pokémon Legends: Z-A, the enhanced capture rate operates similarly to its Scarlet and Violet implementation, albeit with the bonus reduced to 3.5×. Standard limitations apply, preventing its use in scenarios where Poké Balls are generally unusable, such as wild encounters featuring multiple adversaries or when facing a trial Pokémon. Should a Trainer attempt to use it during a Trainer battle, the opposing Trainer will typically swat it aside, resulting in a lost turn (and the permanent loss of the ball itself before Generation IV). Exclusively within Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, Lure Balls are prevented from being held by Pokémon. This measure was implemented to thwart their transfer via trade to Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum, titles in which the item does not naturally occur.

In the episode "Going Apricorn!", a Lure Ball was visualized during Maizie's exposition on Apricorn Poké Balls. "Gettin' The Bugs Out" saw both Ash and Misty presented with Lure Balls personally crafted for them by Kurt. During "The Totodile Duel," Ash and Misty simultaneously deployed their Lure Balls in an attempt to capture a wild Totodile, with both trainers desiring the Pokémon. While one of the Balls successfully ensnared the creature, its ownership remained ambiguous. Consequently, Brock organized a battle to determine who would claim the Big Jaw Pokémon. Ash emerged victorious, gaining Totodile, while Misty retained the unused Lure Ball. "Trouble's Brewing" showed Sumomo housing her Vaporeon in a Lure Ball. Misty captured a Corsola with her Lure Ball in "A Corsola Caper!". A Lure Ball appeared on a promotional poster for the Whirl Cup in "Octillery The Outcast". Christopher's Kingdra was revealed to reside within a Lure Ball affixed to his fishing line in "Dueling Heroes". More recently, in "Found You, Fuecoco!", Nidothing's video content included a mention of the Lure Ball.

Within the manga, "Teddiursa's Picnic" depicted a Lure Ball in a fantasy sequence illustrating Maizie's explanation of Apricorn Poké Balls. In "Slugging It Out with Slugma," Crystal's collection of Apricorn Poké Balls included a Lure Ball. "Off Course with Corsola" showed several Pokémon captured by Crystal for Professor Oak housed in Lure Balls, and later in that same installment, Crystal herself used one to catch a Corsola. "Absolutely Azumarill" featured multiple Lure Balls in a basket of Apricorn Poké Balls brought to Crystal by Mirei. In "The Great Search! Let's Rescue The Slowpoke!", Gold employed Lure Balls, gifts from Kurt, to aid in rescuing Slowpoke from the inundated Slowpoke Well. Black's Gyarados was revealed to be contained within a Lure Ball in "A Spectacular Battle To Save Ampharos!!". Lastly, in "Let's Survive This Sea Battle," a sea captain lent Gold his Remoraid, which resided in a Lure Ball.

Making its initial appearance in the Pokémon Trading Card Game's Skyridge expansion, the Lure Ball distinguishes itself from standard Poké Balls by retrieving cards from the discard pile instead of the deck. For every coin flip that results in heads, up to a maximum of three, players can reclaim an Evolution card from their discard pile and add it to their hand. This card later saw a reprint in the Celestial Storm expansion.

Regarding its visual design, the Lure Ball is depicted with a green exterior in Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver and its initial Trading Card Game prints. Conversely, official artwork consistently portrays it with a blue outer shell. From Generation V onwards, the in-game coloration also transitioned to blue. Nonetheless, the Celestial Storm reprint of the Lure Ball card, issued in 2018, notably retained the original TCG artwork, once again showing it as green. The animated series has seen the Lure Ball's color scheme alter on two occasions. Initially, in its early appearances, it sported a green hue, which later shifted to blue during Pokémon the Series: Gold and Silver. Yet, following the animated series's move to full digital animation with "Here's Lookin' at You, Elekid," Lure Balls reverted to their green coloring, a shade they have retained continuously since.

Title: Level Ball

The レベルボール (Level Ball) in Japanese is a variety of Poké Ball that made its debut in Generation II. Designed for capturing wild Pokémon, its effectiveness increases when the player's Pokémon possesses a significantly higher level compared to the target. This item is among Kurt's distinctive Apricorn Poké Balls, crafted specifically from Red Apricorns. During a wild encounter, deploying it from the Bag initiates an attempt to capture the Pokémon, applying the subsequent catch rate adjustments: A 1× multiplier applies if the player's Pokémon is at or below the wild Pokémon's level. The multiplier becomes 2× if the player's Pokémon's level surpasses the wild Pokémon's, yet remains less than double its level. A 4× boost is granted if the player's Pokémon's level is at least twice, but not yet four times, that of the wild Pokémon. Finally, an 8× multiplier is active when the player's Pokémon's level is four times or more greater than the wild Pokémon's.

In Pokémon Gold, Silver, and Crystal, a programming oversight causes the wild Pokémon's current HP and any status ailments to be disregarded during catch rate computations; solely the wild Pokémon's level factors into the calculation when a Level Ball is employed. For Pokémon Legends: Z-A, should the player not have an active Pokémon, the Level Ball's catch rate modifier consistently defaults to 1×. When targeting an Ultra Beast, this modifier is invariably fixed at 410/4096× (approximately 0.1×). Usage of the Level Ball is prohibited in contexts where any Poké Ball is unusable, including wild encounters featuring multiple adversaries or battles against a trial Pokémon. Should it be deployed during a Trainer battle, the opponent Trainer will deflect the ball, resulting in a lost turn for the player (and the permanent loss of the ball itself before Generation IV).

Exclusively in Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, Level Balls are non-holdable. This restriction was implemented to prevent Pokémon from carrying them when traded into Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum, as the item is absent from those titles.

During Maizie's explanation of Apricorn Poké Balls in Going Apricorn!, a Level Ball was depicted within a fantastical sequence. Similarly, in Teddiursa's Picnic, a Level Ball featured in a fantasy moment as Maizie discussed Apricorn Poké Balls. Crystal captured several Slugma using Level Balls in Slugging It Out with Slugma; these captures were later revisited in a flashback during The Final Battle IV. Off Course with Corsola showed some of the Pokémon Crystal had obtained for Professor Oak housed within Level Balls. Absolutely Azumarill depicted multiple Level Balls among a collection of Apricorn Poké Balls that Mirei presented to Crystal. In It Takes Xatu to Tango, Crystal launched a Level Ball at Arceus to gauge its power. Since the item proved ineffectual against the Alpha Pokémon, she opted for a Heavy Ball instead. Gold was given a Level Ball by Kurt in For Pikachu's Sake!! The Search For The Missing Farfetch'd, a token of gratitude for assisting in rescuing the Slowpoke from drowning within Slowpoke Well. Subsequently, in Let's Fight For The Future!!, he deployed it to seize and halt the Black Tyranitar's destructive rampage. The Level Ball holding the Black Tyranitar was thereafter immured beneath a statue in Cianwood City.

The Level Ball made its debut as an Item card within the Pokémon Trading Card Game during the English Black & White Series (known as the Japanese BW Era). Its initial release occurred in the Japanese Psycho Drive expansion and the English Next Destinies expansion, featuring illustrations by 5ban Graphics. Later, during the English XY Series (the Japanese XY Era), it saw a reprint in the Japanese Bandit Ring expansion and the English Ancient Origins expansion, showcasing fresh artwork by Toyste Beach. Yet another reprint occurred during the English Sword & Shield Series (the Japanese Sword & Shield Era), appearing in the Japanese Rapid Strike Master expansion and the English Battle Styles expansion, available as both a Regular card and a hyper rare variant, both with new artwork by Ryo Ueda. This particular card enables players to scour their deck for a Pokémon possessing 90 HP or less and transfer it directly to their hand.

Title: Moon Ball

The Moon Ball (Japanese: ムーンボール Moon Ball), a distinctive type of Poké Ball, first appeared in Generation II. Its primary function is to capture wild Pokémon, and it is designed to be particularly effective against species that evolve through the use of a Moon Stone. This item is among Kurt's signature Apricorn Poké Balls, meticulously crafted from Yellow Apricorns. When deployed from the player's Bag during a wild encounter, it initiates an attempt to catch the encountered Pokémon.

However, the Moon Ball shares the same usage restrictions as other Poké Balls, rendering it unusable in certain scenarios, such as wild encounters involving multiple opponents or when facing a trial Pokémon. Should a Trainer attempt to use it in a battle against another Trainer, the opponent will deflect the Ball, resulting in a wasted turn. Prior to Generation IV, this action also caused the Ball itself to be consumed.

In Pokémon Gold, Silver, and Crystal, a notable oversight occurred concerning the Moon Ball's catch rate. While it was intended to confer a higher catch rate for Pokémon evolving with a Moon Stone, its base catch rate in this generation was always 1×. Due to a programming error, the Moon Ball instead applied a 4× catch rate modifier only if the wild Pokémon possessed at least two branched evolutions. The specific conditions for this bonus were: the first evolution branch had to involve an Evolution stone or another item used directly from the Bag, and the second evolution branch had to be tied to the 10th method of Evolution (index number 0x0A). This 10th method, however, does not legitimately exist in these games, as only five index numbers for evolution methods were implemented. This anomalous behavior arose because the game's code erroneously checked a memory address that would store the method of a Pokémon's second branched evolution, rather than verifying the specific item used for evolution. Furthermore, instead of looking for the Moon Stone's index number (0x08 in Generation II), it incorrectly sought the Burn Heal's index number (0x0A, which incidentally was the Moon Stone's index in Generation I).

In Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, the Moon Ball's mechanics were re-calibrated. If the wild Pokémon belonged to an evolutionary family that included a Pokémon capable of evolving with a Moon Stone, it received a 4× catch rate modifier; otherwise, the modifier remained 1×. Subsequently, the condition was refined: if the wild Pokémon itself was a species that evolves via Moon Stone (a change from including its entire evolutionary line), it benefited from a 4× catch rate modifier; otherwise, the modifier reverted to 1×. When targeting an Ultra Beast, the catch rate modifier is uniquely set to 410/4096×, which approximates 0.1×. Exclusively within HeartGold and SoulSilver, Moon Balls could not be held by Pokémon. This restriction was implemented to prevent issues if such a Pokémon were traded to Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, or Platinum, where the Moon Ball item does not exist.

The Moon Ball has made several appearances in other media. In "Going Apricorn!" and "Teddiursa's Picnic," a Moon Ball was depicted in a fantasy sequence during Maizie's explanation of Apricorn Poké Balls. Satsuki was shown to house her Jolteon within a Moon Ball in "Trouble's Brewing." Additionally, in "Slugging It Out with Slugma," a Moon Ball was visible among Crystal's collection of Apricorn Poké Balls.

Contrary to a frequent misunderstanding in Pokémon Gold, Silver, and Crystal, the Moon Ball offered no enhanced catch rate against Pokémon that evolve using a Burn Heal. As previously detailed, this was due to the aforementioned oversight, where the game incorrectly prioritized branched evolution checks over verifying specific evolution items. Historically, observing the Moon Ball's catch rate bonus on Munna was impossible through legitimate gameplay before Pokémon Sword and Shield, as Munna could not be encountered in the wild in any games where Moon Balls were obtainable. This same limitation applied to Skitty prior to Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl.

Title: Heavy Ball

Known in Japanese as ヘビーボール (Heavy Ball), this variety of Poké Ball was introduced in Generation II. This entry pertains to the Poké Ball crafted from a Black Apricorn; for the distinct version featured in Legends: Arceus, please consult Heavy Ball (Hisui). Its primary purpose is to capture wild Pokémon, with its success rate directly correlating to the target Pokémon's weight. Handcrafted by Kurt from Black Apricorns, it is recognized as one of his distinctive Apricorn Poké Balls.

When deployed from the Bag during a wild encounter, it attempts to secure the wild Pokémon. The Heavy Ball exhibits greater efficacy against heavier Pokémon and diminished effectiveness against lighter ones, with its precise influence determined by several discrete weight categories (the specific modifiers are detailed below). Should the calculated catch rate exceed 255, it is consequently capped at 255. A unique catch rate modifier of 410/4096× (~0.1×) is invariably applied when used on an Ultra Beast; however, the Heavy Ball's standard weight-based modification still factors into the underlying catch rate calculation.

Exclusively in Pokémon Crystal, a glitch causes the retrieved weights for Pokémon whose index numbers are divisible by 64 (namely Kadabra, Tauros, and Sunflora) to become corrupted junk data. This data is then misinterpreted as extraordinarily high weights, resulting in these particular Pokémon incorrectly receiving a +40 capture modifier. In contrast, in Gold and Silver, these Pokémon's weights are correctly ascertained, yielding a -20 catch modifier. In Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, although the ±0 and -20 modifiers were intended to mirror Generation II's functionality, the -20 modifier erroneously checks if the catch rate is less than 1024, rather than the Pokémon's weight (which is internally represented in tenths of kilograms). As the catch rate is perpetually less than 1024, if the ±0 modifier's "less than 204.8 kg" weight condition is met, the -20 modifier's condition will always be met simultaneously.

Across all titles apart from Pokémon Sun and Moon, if utilizing the Heavy Ball would result in a negative catch rate for the wild Pokémon, that rate is instead adjusted to 1. In Pokémon Sun and Moon, however, this scenario causes the catch rate to be set to 0, thereby precluding a successful capture of such a Pokémon with a Heavy Ball in those games. If the player employs the Heavy Ball on a transformed wild Pokémon (whether by Transform or Imposter), the post-transformation weight is considered for calculation, rather than its original weight. The Heavy Ball cannot be utilized in circumstances where Poké Balls generally are not permitted, such as in wild battles featuring two or more active opponents or against a trial Pokémon. When used in a Trainer battle, the opposing Trainer will deflect the ball, thereby wasting the turn (and the ball itself prior to Generation IV). Solely in Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, Heavy Balls cannot be held by Pokémon. This measure prevents them from being held by Pokémon traded to Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum, where the item does not exist.

Despite its unavailability as an item in Generation VI, a Heavy Ball is visually represented within the Poké Ball Boutique in Lumiose City. Its appearance has been noted in various media. In "Going Apricorn!", a Heavy Ball was featured in a fantasy sequence during Maizie's explanation of Apricorn Poké Balls. Brock received a Heavy Ball, crafted for him by Kurt, in "Gettin' The Bugs Out," though it has not been mentioned or depicted since. In "Gulpin it Down," Nurse Joy provided Professor Jacuzzi with a Heavy Ball for the purpose of catching a giant Gulpin. Silver was given a Heavy Ball by Kurt in "Teddiursa's Picnic," which Kurt had fashioned from a Black Apricorn Silver had earlier supplied; Silver then used this Ball in the subsequent chapter to capture a wild Ursaring that was assaulting Gold and Maizie. A Heavy Ball was observed among Crystal's collection of Apricorn Poké Balls in "Slugging It Out with Slugma." In "Off Course with Corsola," some of the Pokémon Crystal had caught for Professor Oak were seen within Heavy Balls. Crystal attempted to capture Suicune using a Heavy Ball in "Suddenly Suicune (Part 1)," but to her considerable shock, it quickly broke free. "Absolutely Azumarill" depicted several Heavy Balls within a basket of Apricorn Poké Balls delivered to Crystal by Mirei. Crystal later endeavored to catch Arceus with a Heavy Ball in "It Takes Xatu to Tango"; although Arceus was momentarily drawn into the Ball, it rapidly escaped. Gold utilized a Heavy Ball, a gift from Kurt, in "The Great Search! Let's Rescue The Slowpoke!" to rescue an unusually large Slowpoke from drowning in the flooding Slowpoke Well. In "A Spectacular Battle To Save Ampharos!!," it was revealed that Jasmine keeps her Steelix in a Heavy Ball. In the Pokémon HeartGold SoulSilver: Pokédex Completion Comic, Soul successfully captured a Steelix using a Heavy Ball he acquired from a female Trainer. Furthermore, a Heavy Ball appeared in "Get the Legendary Pokémon!"; Red's intention was to use it on one of the Legendary beasts, but he inadvertently used it on a Ditto instead.

In the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Heavy Ball functions as an Item card, originating during the English Black & White Series (which corresponds to the Japanese BW Era). It first launched in the Next Destinies expansion, was subsequently reprinted in the Japanese Master Deck Build Box EX, and later saw a worldwide reprint for the BREAKthrough expansion. This particular iteration was also incorporated into the Japanese The Best of XY compilation. When a player activates the Heavy Ball, they are permitted to search their deck for a Pokémon possessing a Retreat Cost of 3 or more, reveal it, and then place it into their hand. The player must then shuffle their deck.

Exclusive to Pokémon Sun and Moon, a Heavy Ball will invariably fail to capture any Pokémon that weighs less than 220.46 lbs (100.0 kg) and concurrently has a base catch rate of 20 or less. This phenomenon occurs because the application of the -20 modifier results in a negative catch rate, which the game then resets to 0. The only capturable Pokémon affected by this specific interaction are Beldum and the guardian deities.

Beginning with Generation VI, the Heavy Ball's aesthetic design was updated to include two additional blue protrusions on its back, visually mirroring the pair already present on its front.

Title: Fast Ball

This item, known in Japan as スピードボール (Speed Ball), is a specific variant of Poké Ball first appearing in Generation II. Also referred to as the Speed Ball, players can employ it to capture wild Pokémon, observing an enhanced success rate when used on swift or easily escapable creatures. Crafted from White Apricorns, it stands as one of Kurt's distinctive Apricorn Poké Ball creations. Deploying it from the Bag during an encounter with a wild Pokémon initiates a capture attempt. For specific Pokémon species, this Ball applies an augmented catch rate modifier.

Usage of the Fast Ball is prohibited under circumstances where any Poké Ball is disallowed, for instance, in wild battles involving multiple foes or when challenging a trial Pokémon. In a Trainer confrontation, the opponent deflects the ball, resulting in a lost turn for the player (and the permanent loss of the item before Generation IV). Should the wild Pokémon be Magnemite, Grimer, or Tangela, the Fast Ball benefits from a 4× catch rate multiplier; for other species, it defaults to a 1× modifier. Despite its design intent to be more potent against all Pokémon capable of escaping, a programming error limits its heightened efficacy solely to Magnemite, Grimer, and Tangela – the initial three Pokémon listed with a 10% fleeing probability. The underlying code was meant to query all three categories of Pokémon possessing fleeing capabilities (those with a 10% chance to flee, those with a 50% chance to flee, and those that consistently flee).

If a wild Pokémon possesses a base Speed stat of 100 or greater, the Fast Ball applies a 4× catch rate modifier; otherwise, the modifier remains 1×. When employed against an Ultra Beast, the catch rate modifier is invariably fixed at 410/4096× (approximately 0.1×). Exclusively within Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, Fast Balls are rendered unholdable.
