This restriction prevents them from being attached to Pokémon transferred to Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum, titles in which the item is absent. Generation II's Japanese item text describes 「にげあしの はやい ポケモン」 (Pokémon quick to run away), a phrase localized as "fast Pokémon."

In the episode Going Apricorn!, Ash, Brock, and Misty were each gifted a Fast Ball by Kurt. Brock subsequently utilized his Fast Ball to capture a Pineco in the same episode, but Ash and Misty's Fast Balls have not reappeared or been referenced, save for Ash's making a brief visual appearance in the fifth Japanese opening theme, Ready Go!, and the Pokémon: Master Quest dub opening, Believe in Me. Trouble's Brewing disclosed that Tamao housed her Umbreon within a Fast Ball. A Fast Ball featured in a dream sequence during Maizie's exposition on Apricorn Poké Balls in Teddiursa's Picnic. Slugging It Out with Slugma showed a Fast Ball among Crystal's collection of Apricorn Poké Balls. Crystal employed Fast Balls to capture a cluster of Staryu in Surrounded by Staryu. Off Course with Corsola depicted some of Professor Oak's Pokémon, caught by Crystal, residing in Fast Balls. Absolutely Azumarill displayed multiple Fast Balls within a basket of Apricorn Poké Balls delivered to Crystal by Mirei. In The Great Search! Let's Rescue The Slowpoke!, Gold employed Fast Balls, a gift from Kurt, to aid in rescuing Slowpoke from the deluged Slowpoke Well.

In the card game, the Fast Ball enables a player to examine their deck one card at a time, drawing the initial Evolution card discovered into their hand, then reshuffling the deck. This card first appeared in the Skyridge expansion. From Generation V onwards, official illustrations and in-battle visuals portray the Fast Ball as red. Conversely, its Bag icon and Pokémon Global Link artwork show it as orange, a color it also exhibits during battles in Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver. Its aesthetic incorporates a stylized letter 'S'. This design element references its Japanese moniker, Speed Ball.

In Generation II, although the Fast Ball was designed for enhanced efficacy against Pokémon prone to fleeing, its English item description for that generation incorrectly suggested it was more effective against inherently fast Pokémon. From Generation V onward, the description misleadingly indicates superior performance against Pokémon capable of fleeing, despite its actual increased effectiveness being against fast Pokémon in those titles. Consequently, only in Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver does the Fast Ball's English description accurately reflect its in-game function. Page 21 of the Pokémon Gold and Silver: Complete Pokédex erroneously describes this item: "Use a Fast Ball when you're trying to catch a Pokémon that runs away quickly, such as Raikou or Skarmory." As previously detailed, Generation II games do not apply the 4× catch rate modifier to Raikou or Skarmory, and wild Skarmory, unlike Raikou, do not flee from encounters. Nevertheless, Raikou does benefit from this enhanced catch rate modifier starting from Generation IV.

Title: Friend Ball

Originating in Generation II, the Friend Ball (フレンドボール in Japanese, sometimes referred to as the Friendship Ball) is a specialized type of Poké Ball designed for capturing wild Pokémon. Its unique property causes any Pokémon caught with it to become notably more friendly. This item is among Kurt's distinctive Apricorn Poké Balls, manufactured from Green Apricorns. When deployed from the Bag during a wild encounter, it attempts to ensnare the wild Pokémon with a standard catch rate multiplier of 1×. Upon successful capture, the Pokémon's friendship value is immediately set to 200 in games from Generations II through VII, or 150 from Generation VIII onward. However, it grants no friendship bonus to Pokémon that hatch from eggs kept in a Friend Ball.

If used against an Ultra Beast, the item's catch rate modifier is instead fixed at 410/4096× (approximately 0.1×). Consistent with other Poké Balls, it cannot be employed in circumstances where Poké Balls are generally restricted, such as wild battles featuring two or more active opponents or against a trial Pokémon. Should an attempt be made to use it in a Trainer battle, the opposing Trainer will bat the ball away, rendering the turn's action futile; prior to Generation IV, this also resulted in the consumption of the ball itself.

Exclusively within Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, Friend Balls are prevented from being held by Pokémon. This restriction was implemented to avoid their transfer to Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum, titles where the item does not exist. Many Pokémon Breeders prefer to keep their Pokémon in Friend Balls. Despite being an unobtainable item in Generation VI, a Friend Ball is visibly displayed within the Poké Ball Boutique in Lumiose City.

In the episode *Going Apricorn!*, a Friend Ball appeared in a fantasy sequence during Maizie's explanation about Apricorn Poké Balls. Within *Teddiursa's Picnic*, Gold received a Friend Ball from Kurt, subsequently using it in the next chapter to capture a Teddiursa for Maizie. *Slugging It Out with Slugma* featured a Friend Ball amongst Crystal's array of Apricorn Poké Balls. Gym Leader Chuck keeps his Pokémon in Friend Balls, evidenced by his Poliwrath in *The Secret Of The Fighting Type Pokémon* and his Primeape in *Let's Fight For The Future!!*. Debuting in the Pokémon Trading Card Game's *Skyridge* set, the Friend Ball enables a player to search their deck for a Pokémon that shares a type with one of their opponent's Pokémon. It was later reprinted in *Celestial Storm*. A Kids' WB promotional spot aired during the fourth season, showcasing various Apricorn Poké Balls, incorrectly identified the Friend Ball as the "Stun Ball."

Title: Love Ball

Debuting in Generation II, the Love Ball (ラブラブボール, Lovey-Dovey Ball in Japanese) represents a specialized category of Poké Ball. Its primary function involves the capture of wild Pokémon, exhibiting an enhanced success rate when employed against a creature of the opposite gender compared to the player's active Pokémon. This item stands among Kurt's distinctive Apricorn Poké Balls, meticulously fashioned from Pink Apricorns. Activation from the Bag during a wild encounter prompts an attempt to secure the encountered Pokémon.

Within Pokémon Gold, Silver, and Crystal, despite its intended increased effectiveness against opposite-gender Pokémon, a programming error instead causes it to apply an 8× catch rate modifier exclusively to wild Pokémon that match both the species and gender of the player's active Pokémon; all other scenarios receive a 1× multiplier. From Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver onward, the mechanics were corrected: an 8× catch rate modifier is applied if the wild Pokémon shares the same species but is of the opposite gender to the player's active Pokémon, whereas other situations yield a 1× modifier. Specifically in Pokémon Legends: Z-A, its catch rate modifier consistently remains 1× if no Pokémon is currently deployed by the player. Conversely, when targeting an Ultra Beast, the catch rate multiplier is invariably fixed at 410/4096× (approximately 0.1×).

This item is impermissible for use in scenarios where standard Poké Balls are generally restricted, for instance, during wild encounters featuring multiple opponents or when facing a trial Pokémon. Should it be deployed during a Trainer battle, the opposing Trainer will deflect the ball, thereby wasting the current turn (and the item itself in Generations prior to IV). Exclusively in Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, Love Balls cannot be held by Pokémon. This measure prevents them from being carried into Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum via trade, as the item is nonexistent in those titles.

Notwithstanding its unavailability as a transferable item in Generation VI, a Love Ball is visually represented in Lumiose City's Poké Ball Boutique. Within the episode Going Apricorn!, a Love Ball was depicted in a fantasy sequence as Maizie elucidated on Apricorn Poké Balls. The Professors' New Adventure! featured a chandelier stand designed after a Love Ball, observed during the nuptials of Professor Kukui and Professor Burnet. Furthermore, in Teddiursa's Picnic, a Love Ball made a fantasy appearance during Maizie's discussion of Apricorn Poké Balls. During Slugging It Out with Slugma, one of Crystal's Apricorn Poké Balls was identifiable as a Love Ball. The manga chapter For Pikachu's Sake!! The Search For The Missing Farfetch'd depicted Whitney's acquisition of a Love Ball from Kurt.

The Love Ball entered the Pokémon Trading Card Game as an Item card during the English Scarlet & Violet Series (corresponding to the Japanese Scarlet & Violet Era). Its initial release came in the Japanese Crimson Haze subset and the English Twilight Masquerade expansion, featuring artwork by Studio Bora Inc. The card's effect permits the player to search their deck for a Pokémon that shares its name with one of the opponent's Pokémon in play, adding it to their hand, then instructing the player to shuffle their deck. Trouble's Brewing revealed that each of the Kimono Sisters housed their Pokémon within an Apricorn Poké Ball. Although the specific Poké Ball for Koume's Flareon was not depicted, the design on her kimono strongly implies it would have been a Love Ball. The game itself never explicitly states that the enhanced catch rate modifier requires the target Pokémon to be the same species as the player's Pokémon.

Title: Park Ball

For trainers inquiring about the Poké Ball referred to as the Park Ball in Generation II, please note that the correct item is the Sport Ball. Identified in Japanese as パークボール (Park Ball), this particular type of Poké Ball was first introduced in Generation IV. Its function is to facilitate the re-capture of Pokémon that have been migrated to Pal Park. When deployed during a Catching Show encounter within Pal Park, it guarantees the capture of the targeted Pokémon without fail. A player does not assume ownership of a Pokémon caught in this manner until they have successfully concluded the Catching Show and opted to integrate the captured Pokémon into their Storage System. Pokémon that have migrated retain the Poké Balls they were contained in during Generation III; consequently, no Pokémon can ever have a Park Ball designated as its default Poké Ball. In the games Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, despite being an unused item, when activated from the Poké Balls pocket during a battle, the Park Ball displays the standard Poké Ball's visual graphics and animation instead of its own. Upon participating in Pal Park's Catching Show, the player is provided with six Park Balls, exclusively for use during the event. These balls are not deposited into the player's Bag, and any remaining, unused Park Balls must be returned if the player chooses to exit the Catching Show. The Park Ball was featured as an image in the manga chapter Partner Promises!. In Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum, the Park Ball is not programmed as an inventory item. This status changed in Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, where it was added as an inventory item and has maintained that programming since, though it is never actually visible within the Bag. Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl altered the Park Ball's descriptive text to reference Ramanas Park instead of Pal Park, despite the fact that Park Balls are neither obtainable nor utilized within Ramanas Park. This description was subsequently reverted in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, once again referencing Pal Park as it originally did. The Park Ball and the Strange Ball are the sole Poké Balls that cannot be a Pokémon's assigned Poké Ball, a condition stemming from Pokémon caught in Pal Park retaining their original Poké Balls. The Park Ball, Cherish Ball, and Strange Ball are the only Poké Balls across all core series games that have never been obtainable as items in any core series title.

Title: Sport Ball

Originating in Generation II, the Sport Ball (Japanese: コンペボール Competition Ball) was initially known as the Park Ball (Japanese: パークボール Park Ball) within that generation, appearing as "ParkBall" in its battle menu listing. Its primary function is to facilitate the capture of wild Pokémon during the Bug-Catching Contest held at the National Park. The item's name was subsequently changed due to the introduction of a distinct Poké Ball, also named Park Ball, in Generation IV. When utilized in a wild encounter, this Poké Ball provides a 1.5× modifier to the catch rate. In Pokémon Gold, Silver, Crystal, HeartGold, and SoulSilver, players are permitted to possess these Poké Balls solely during a Bug-Catching Contest. Notably, in the Generation II titles, as an unused asset, the Park Ball exhibited a minor graphical anomaly when deployed from the Ball Pocket during battle. Beginning with Pokémon Sword and Shield's The Isle of Armor DLC, the Sport Ball became accessible outside of the Bug-Catching Contest and can now be employed in standard wild encounters. Participants in the Bug-Catching Contest are provided with 20 Sport Balls, intended for exclusive use within the competition. These are not placed in the player's Bag, and any remaining Sport Balls are retrieved upon the conclusion of the event. In the episode "The Bug Stops Here," Ash and Casey each received a Park Ball to use during their Bug-Catching Contest. Casey successfully caught a Weedle with her Park Ball, while Ash managed to capture a Beedrill. Ultimately, Ash's catch secured his victory in the contest, earning him a Sun Stone and his new Beedrill as prizes; the Beedrill was subsequently transferred to a regular Poké Ball before he decided to gift it to Casey. A Park Ball also appeared in volume 13, where it was featured in an explanation detailing the various Poké Balls known at that time. The Sport Ball's Pokémon Global Link artwork depicts an 'S' emblem on its upper half, contrasting with its in-game representation, which instead features a standard Poké Ball symbol.

Title: Red Apricorn

The Red Apricorn (Japanese: あかぼんぐり Red Bonguri) is a specific variety of Apricorn first introduced in Generation II. Its primary function is the creation of a Level Ball, a transformation that can only be performed by Kurt. When processed within the Apriblender, it yields Aprijuice that provides a boost to the Power Performance stat.

This particular Apricorn made an appearance in the episode "Going Apricorn!", where Kurt dispatched Ash, Misty, and Brock to gather some. However, the trio encountered one of Team Rocket's pitfall traps, preventing them from even reaching the Apricorn tree. In "Yikes, It's Yanma!", an Apricorn lottery was implemented at Indigo Plateau to randomly determine the pairings of Kanto and Johto Gym Leaders for an interregional exhibition tournament. Both Blaine and Clair were assigned a Red Apricorn during this draw.

Notably, in Generations II and IV, item names were restricted to 12 characters, including spaces. Due to this constraint, the Red Apricorn stands as the sole Apricorn with its color completely spelled out in its English name. Furthermore, trees that bore Burnt Berries in Generation II were replaced by Red Apricorn trees in Generation IV.

Title: Blue Apricorn

Known in Japanese as あおぼんぐり (Blue Bonguri), this particular variant of Apricorn, which was designated "Blu Apricorn" until Generation VI, made its debut in Generation II. Its primary utility lies in the crafting of a Lure Ball. Specifically, the item can be transformed into a Lure Ball by Kurt. When utilized within the Apriblender, it yields Aprijuice that is capable of boosting the Skill Performance stat. Blue Apricorns were featured in the episode "Going Apricorn!", where Kurt dispatched Ash, Misty, and Brock to gather Apricorns. Despite numerous failed attempts at collecting the items, the group ultimately managed to procure two Blue Apricorns and a single Black Apricorn, which Kurt subsequently fashioned into a pair of Lure Balls and one Heavy Ball for them. In "Yikes, It's Yanma!", an Apricorn-based lottery system was employed to randomly determine the matchups between Kanto and Johto Gym Leaders for an interregional exhibition tournament held at the Indigo Plateau. Both Brock and Jasmine received a Blue Apricorn apiece in this lottery. It's noteworthy that Ice Berry trees from Generation II were replaced by Blue Apricorn trees starting in Generation IV. A unique instance of item data rearrangement occurred between Generation IV and Generation V, where the Ylw Apricorn and Blu Apricorn swapped their respective index numbers. These two items are the sole examples of such an index number exchange between those particular generations.

Title: Yellow Apricorn

A distinct variety of Apricorn, identified as the Yellow Apricorn (Japanese: きぼんぐり Yellow Bonguri), was introduced in Generation II. Before Generation VI, its nomenclature was rendered as Ylw Apricorn. This item is utilized in the creation of a Moon Ball, a process specifically performed by Kurt. When inserted into an Apriblender, it yields Aprijuice that contributes to boosting the Stamina Performance stat. Yellow Apricorns made their initial appearance in the episode "Going Apricorn!", where Kurt dispatched Ash, Misty, and Brock to collect these Apricorns. However, the particular tree they located was infested by a group of Pineco, thereby preventing the harvesting of any Apricorns from it. In "An Egg Scramble!", Khoury was observed selling Yellow Apricorns among his merchandise at the Johto Festival. "Yikes, It's Yanma!" depicted an Apricorn lottery employed to randomly determine the pairings of Kanto and Johto Gym Leaders for an interregional exhibition tournament hosted at Indigo Plateau; in this event, Lt. Surge and Morty each acquired a Yellow Apricorn. Significantly, in Generation IV, the areas occupied by PRZCureBerry trees in Generation II were replaced by Yellow Apricorn trees. Moreover, the Ylw Apricorn and Blu Apricorn exchanged their index numbers between Generation IV and Generation V, an alteration exclusively observed for these two items across those specific generations.

Title: Green Apricorn

Known in Japanese as みどぼんぐり (Green Bonguri), the Green Apricorn made its debut in Generation II as a specific variety of Apricorn. Before Generation VI, its name appeared as Grn Apricorn. This item serves as a key ingredient for crafting a Friend Ball. Specifically, Kurt is the individual responsible for transforming Green Apricorns into Friend Balls. When processed through an Apriblender, it yields Aprijuice capable of enhancing a Pokémon's Jump Performance stat. Its presence in the anime was notable in the episode 'Going Apricorn!', where Kurt dispatched Ash, Misty, and Brock on a mission to gather these items. Their harvesting attempt was thwarted, however, as a swarm of Beedrill assaulted them before any Apricorns could be collected from the tree. Within Generation IV titles, Green Apricorns were distributed across eight distinct locations, making them considerably the most prevalent among their kind. Generally, the trees bearing Green Apricorns occupy spots that were home to Berry and MysteryBerry trees back in Generation II. Uniquely among Apricorns, its Japanese appellation abbreviates the color within its name, utilizing みど (mido) instead of the full みどり (midori).

Title: Pink Apricorn

A distinctive variety of Apricorn, the Pink Apricorn (Japanese: ももぼんぐり Pink Bonguri) made its debut in Generation II, having been labeled as Pnk Apricorn before Generation VI. Its primary purpose lies in its conversion into a Love Ball, a task exclusively performed by Kurt. Furthermore, when processed within the Apriblender, it yields a specific Aprijuice formulation known to elevate the Speed Performance stat. These Apricorns made an appearance in the episode "Going Apricorn!", where Kurt dispatched Ash, Misty, and Brock to gather them. Nevertheless, Maizie informed the protagonists that the particular Pink Apricorns they had discovered were not yet mature enough for collection. A notable change occurred in Generation IV, as the PSNCureBerry trees from Generation II's landscapes were supplanted by Pink Apricorn trees.

Title: White Apricorn

An Apricorn variant first appearing in Generation II, the White Apricorn (known as しろぼんぐり, or White Bonguri, in Japanese) was previously designated as Wht Apricorn before Generation VI. This item serves as the raw material for crafting a Fast Ball. Specifically, Kurt possesses the expertise to transform White Apricorns into Fast Balls. When processed within the Apriblender, it yields an Aprijuice devoid of taste or any functional impact. In the episode Going Apricorn!, White Apricorns were observed growing on a tree situated adjacent to Kurt's residence. Subsequently, he presented Ash, Misty, and Brock with individual Fast Balls, each created from White Apricorns harvested from that very tree. During Yikes, It's Yanma!, an Apricorn lottery mechanism was employed to arbitrarily determine pairings among Gym Leaders from the Kanto and Johto regions for an interregional exhibition tournament held at the Indigo Plateau. Both Blue and Chuck each acquired a White Apricorn in this lottery, setting up a confrontation between Blue and his past mentor.

Title: Black Apricorn

Known as くろぼんぐり (Black Bonguri) in Japanese, the Black Apricorn is a specific variety of Apricorn that made its debut in Generation II. Before Generation VI, its designation was "Blk Apricorn". This item is primarily utilized in the crafting of a Heavy Ball, a task expertly performed by Kurt. Furthermore, when processed within the Apriblender, the Black Apricorn yields Aprijuice capable of enhancing a Pokémon's Power and Stamina Performance stats.

The Black Apricorn's presence extends to the anime, notably in the episode "Going Apricorn!", where Kurt dispatched Ash, Misty, and Brock to gather Apricorns. Despite numerous unsuccessful attempts at collection, the trio ultimately secured two Blue Apricorns and one Black Apricorn. Kurt subsequently transformed these into a pair of Lure Balls and a Heavy Ball for the group. Another instance appears in "Teddiursa's Picnic", where Silver obtained a Heavy Ball from Kurt, fashioned from a Black Apricorn Silver had previously entrusted to him. Regarding game world changes, the Bitter Berry trees found in Generation II were substituted with Black Apricorn trees starting from Generation IV.

Title: Dowsing Machine
The Dowsing Machine (Japanese: ダウジングマシン Dowsing Machine), a Key Item designed to locate concealed objects in the user's immediate surroundings, has undergone various naming conventions and functional evolutions. Prior to Generation IV, it was known as the Itemfinder, and in Pokémon HeartGold, SoulSilver, and all Generation V titles, it adopted the moniker Dowsing MCHN. The method by which it detects items and signals their presence changes significantly across different games.

In certain modern Pokémon adventures, the Dowsing Machine's specific role is superseded by other mechanics. For instance, in Pokémon Sun, Moon, Ultra Sun, and Ultra Moon, the task of discovering hidden items falls to the Poké Ride Stoutland Search. Similarly, in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, the player's partner Pokémon and any walking Pokémon assume this duty. In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, the rideable Ursaluna performs this function. Pokémon Sword, Shield, Scarlet, and Violet simplify this altogether, with hidden items manifesting as visible sparkles in the overworld, thus negating the need for any specialized detection tool.

The Itemfinder or Dowsing Machine serves its primary purpose of unearthing hidden items. In the initial installments, Pokémon Red, Blue, and Yellow, attempting to use the Itemfinder when no hidden object is nearby elicits the message: "Nope! ITEMFINDER isn't responding." Conversely, if a hidden item is present within the current screen's view, four distinct musical chimes will sound, accompanied by the text: "Yes! ITEMFINDER indicates there's an item nearby." Notably, this generation's Itemfinder is unable to pinpoint the hidden coins located within the Rocket Game Corner. Pokémon Gold, Silver, and Crystal largely mirror Generation I's Itemfinder mechanics. However, in these titles, the Itemfinder gains Key Item status and can be assigned to the Select Button for quicker accessibility.

Generation III introduced significant enhancements, marking the first time the Itemfinder conveyed information about an item's direction and proximity. In Pokémon Ruby, Sapphire, and Emerald, the device still required the hidden item to be "on-screen," much like its predecessors. Upon detecting an item and sounding chimes, the player character would orient themselves towards the item's location. The accompanying message for a nearby item states: "Huh? The ITEMFINDER's responding! There's an item buried around here!" When no item is detected under normal usage, the text reads: "... ... ... ...Nope! There's no response."

Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen further refined the Itemfinder with directional arrows. The number of arrows indicated the player's closeness to the hidden item: two arrows if five to eight tiles away, and four arrows if within four tiles. Each appearing arrow was accompanied by a musical chime. The textual feedback remained consistent with Pokémon Ruby, Sapphire, and Emerald. These versions also introduced certain items that *required* the Itemfinder for acquisition, not just easier discovery. These specific items could only be found if the player was standing directly on their tile, at which point they were automatically obtained. Using the Itemfinder on such tiles resulted in three stars appearing above the player. Six items fall into this category: the Soothe Bell (in Pokémon Tower after rescuing Mr. Fuji), Leftovers (on Routes 12 and 16 after battling their respective Snorlax), the Macho Brace (in Viridian Gym after Giovanni's disappearance), the PP Max (on Cape Brink, on a lone patch of ground surrounded by the lake), and the Sacred Ash (on Navel Rock after facing Ho-Oh). The dialogue prompted by finding these particular items is: "Oh! The ITEMFINDER's shaking wildly! There's an item buried underfoot!" Uniquely in FireRed and LeafGreen, the Itemfinder could also detect hidden coins in the Rocket Game Corner.

Generation IV's Dowsing Machines differed considerably, leveraging the Nintendo DS's touch screen capabilities. In Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum, the Itemfinder's functions were integrated into the Pokétch's Dowsing Machine app. This app presented a Cartesian plane with the player at its center, operating like a radar. Tapping the touch screen emitted a radiating circle and a "ping." If an item was nearby, a pulsing radar circle would appear repeatedly. Should the hidden item be within this circle's radius, a dot would continuously blink while the radar circle emanated from the touched spot. Any action by the player would reset the app's display. Plates, however, had a unique detection signature: while a repeated circle would appear as with other items, the gray dot signifying a hidden item was much smaller and only became visible when the Plate's precise location was touched.

In Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, the Dowsing MCHN functioned as a Key Item, utilizing the touch screen for item detection, where each grid tile corresponded to an overworld tile. Unlike its Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum counterpart, this Dowsing Machine featured a circular grid and could be operated while the player was in motion. Four lines surrounding the central grid would shift in response to the touch area's proximity to a hidden item, illuminating when an item was near. Normal grid touches displayed dark red dots and lines, accompanied by a slow series of low-frequency beeps. As the player moved closer to a hidden item while touching the grid, the dots and lines would gradually change from red to white. A blinking dot would appear on the grid tile representing the item's exact location, while the beeping intensified in speed and frequency, indicating greater proximity. Furthermore, the Pokéwalker offered a Dowsing Machine mini-game for item discovery, costing 3 Watts to play. In this mini-game, players were granted two guesses, and a failed first attempt provided feedback on whether the item was adjacent to the chosen spot.

In Pokémon Black, White, Black 2, and White 2, the device was once again dubbed the Dowsing MCHN. Although it still appeared on the touch screen during use, it ceased accepting stylus input, serving purely as a visual display. When the player was close to a hidden item, bars and an arrow would indicate its direction. Standing directly on an item's tile caused the bars to cross, revealing a square adorned with four small arrows.

Pokémon X and Y and Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire introduced a completely reimagined Dowsing Machine. Common limitations across both pairs of games included its inability to function underwater or within most buildings, and players could not simultaneously ride a Bicycle or Surf while operating it. In Pokémon X and Y, the player character wielded two rods that projected an electric current forward. As the character moved, these rods emitted two streams of current that changed color based on the player's facing direction and distance from a hidden item. The colors progressed from blue, to green, to orange, with a musical chime accompanying each color shift to signal increasing proximity. When positioned directly in front of a hidden item, the electric currents would cross, turn red, and a distinct musical chime would play. In Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, the Dowsing Machine manifested as a head-mounted device with two electrodes protruding from the top. These electrodes would alter their color, similar to the X and Y version, as the player neared a hidden item. When directly confronting a hidden item, the electrodes would turn red and vibrate. While wearing this Dowsing Machine, players were prevented from sneaking.

Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl feature the Dowsing Machine Pokétch app, which operates identically to its implementation in Pokémon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum.

The Item Finder card is featured within the Pokémon Trading Card Game and Pokémon Trading Card Game 2: The Invasion of Team GR!.

In the game Withered Wasteland, the Dowsing Machine is an item that can either be discovered or crafted. When utilized in conjunction with a Pokémon possessing the Search specialty, it enables Ditto to seek out hidden treasures within an area. These treasures encompass Ancient Artifacts, CDs, Pokémon dolls, or Mysterious Slates. To activate the Dowsing Machine, Ditto must solicit assistance from a Pokémon with

Title: Rage Candy Bar

The Rage Candy Bar (Japanese: いかりまんじゅう Rage Manjū) debuted as a medicine item in the Generation II Pokémon games. Before Generation VI, its item display format was RageCandyBar. It is known as a local delicacy specific to Mahogany Town, and in Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, it holds the classification of a Key Item.

When utilized from the Bag on a Pokémon, this item restores 20 HP, functionally identical to a Potion. Furthermore, a Rage Candy Bar can cure a Pokémon of all non-volatile status ailments—poison, paralysis, sleep, burn, and freeze—as well as confusion.

Specific in-game interactions vary: In Generation V titles, the RageCandyBar can be vended to a gourmet enthusiast situated on Route 5 for $6000. Within Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, it can be exchanged with an NPC located on the Underground Path connecting Routes 5 and 6 for TM64 (Explosion). A distinct event in Pokémon Black and White allows players to use a RageCandyBar on one of the Darmanitan statues in the Desert Resort; this action consumes the item and triggers a battle with the awakened Level 35 Darmanitan, each possessing its Hidden Ability, Zen Mode.

The item's name rendering has evolved through different generations. In Generation II, like other items, its name appeared as Ragecandybar, entirely in capital letters and without spaces. For dialogue mentions in Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen, it was styled "Rage Candybar," shifting to "Rage Candy Bar" for dialogue in Pokémon Platinum. When it reappeared as an item in Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver and all Generation V games, the name was formatted "RageCandyBar" in CamelCase, a design choice necessitated by the 12-character limit and standard capitalization for item names at the time. Beginning with Generation VI, due to an increased character limit, its name is now consistently displayed as "Rage Candy Bar" with spaces.

Culturally, the Rage Candy Bar is modeled after the Japanese manjū, a sweet-filled bun often sold as confectionery and frequently unique to specific locales, mirroring the candy bar's association with Mahogany Town. The translation to "candy bar" was adopted to resonate more with English-speaking players. The item's in-Bag sprite, depicting it in a small box, effectively disguised any translation discrepancy by resembling a candy bar wrapper. Nob Ogasawara, known by his alias Douglas Dinsdale (or Doug Dinsdale), asserted in an archived Something Awful forums thread available on the Let's Play Archive that "Rage Candy Bar" was a mistranslation decided by Nintendo of America. He posited that "Rage Dumpling" or "Rage Bun" would have been more accurate renderings of いかりまんじゅう, especially considering the twelve-character limit then in place.

Although the item is not present in the internal game data of Generation III or the Sinnoh-based Generation IV games, it receives mentions through dialogue. In Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen, an NPC on Two Island reveals it as Elite Four member Bruno's preferred delicacy, noting his disappointment when the local shop had sold out. Pokémon Platinum's dialogue indicates it was supposedly available in Veilstone Department Store's basement, though the vendor perpetually reports being out of stock. Professor Rowan, another known admirer of the item, can occasionally be found in the basement, expressing his dismay over the shortage, with an NPC there claiming responsibility for purchasing all available Rage Candy Bars.

Beyond the games, Rage Candy Bars have appeared in various forms of media. In the anime episode "An Egg Scramble!", they were among the goods sold by Khoury at the Johto Festival and also seen among items stolen by Team Rocket. "Raising Raichu" featured an advertising banner for Rage Candy Bars on the roof of Mahogany Town's souvenir shop. In the manga, specifically "Out-Odding Oddish," Gold was depicted consuming recently acquired Rage Candy Bars, even tossing one into Whitney's mouth, almost causing her to choke. Gold later gifted one to Bruno, who instantly cherished its delicious flavor. In "All About Arceus IX," Whitney, shown to have romantic feelings for Bruno, sent him a boxful of Rage Candy Bars, much to his delight. Ryoku of the Seven Sages was seen clutching a box of Rage Candy Bars in "To Make a Musical" before entering Relic Castle to capture Volcarona for Ghetsis. As merchandise, a miniature Rage Candy Bar and its packaging were included in the Johto goods set from the Pokémon World Market line, produced by Re-Ment and exclusive to the Pokémon Center. It also appears beside Togepi in The Pokémon Company International's Pokémon Delicious Adventure figure series.

Title: Red Orb
This legendary artifact, known in Japan as べにいろのたま (Crimson Orb), debuted as a Key Item in Generation III and holds a strong connection to Groudon. Commencing with Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire in Generation VI, it functions as a held item that enables Groudon to achieve Primal Reversion, transforming into Primal Groudon immediately when a battle begins. Within Pokémon Sapphire, Team Aqua procures the Red Orb from Mt. Pyre's peak, employing it to rouse Kyogre. Nevertheless, their possession of the incorrect orb prevents them from exerting control over the super-ancient Pokémon as they had planned. Once the player has either captured or vanquished Kyogre, they can revisit Mt. Pyre's summit to witness Team Aqua Leader Archie restoring the orb to its rightful position. Subsequently, the player participates in its return, Archie leaves, and Team Aqua makes no further appearances. Pokémon Emerald recounts a parallel sequence of events to Ruby and Sapphire, distinguishing itself by the presence of both Team Magma and Team Aqua, leading to the theft of both the Red Orb and the Blue Orb. The player, however, does not acquire the Red Orb in this title. For Pokémon SoulSilver players, Mr. Pokémon bestows the Red Orb upon them after they have triumphed over Red and secured both the National Pokédex and a Kanto first partner Pokémon from Professor Oak. Receipt of the orb then permits the player to encounter Groudon within the Embedded Tower. In Pokémon Omega Ruby, Team Magma seizes the Red Orb from Mt. Pyre's peak and employs it to awaken Groudon. Unlike the Generation III iterations, Team Magma possesses the correct orb, which they utilize to unleash Groudon's true Primal Reversion power. Upon exiting the Cave of Origin, Team Magma Leader Maxie hands the player the stolen Red Orb. Pokémon Alpha Sapphire sees the player receive the Red Orb and a request to reunite it with its counterpart, soon after Team Aqua purloins the Blue Orb from Mt. Pyre's summit. Subsequently, in Sootopolis City, Wallace and Maxie elaborate on the Red Orb's capabilities: shielding the player inside the Cave of Origin, counteracting Primal Reversion, and pacifying the roused Kyogre. Reaching the cave's deepest sections, the player dons the Aqua Suit and inserts the Red Orb, thereby generating a protective red barrier around their person. Within the Mega Dimension DLC of Pokémon Legends: Z-A, a Groudon encountered in the Hyperspace Desolate Land possesses a Red Orb, which becomes active during the second phase of the battle against Team MZ. In Pokémon SoulSilver, possession of the Red Orb is a prerequisite for encountering Groudon in the Embedded Tower. When a Groudon holds this item, it facilitates Primal Reversion, transforming it into Primal Groudon at the onset of a battle. It remains immune to item-negating effects (e.g., Embargo and Klutz). Furthermore, item-manipulating effects (such as Trick and Fling) are incapable of bestowing a Red Orb upon Groudon or detaching one from it. Pokémon equipped with a Red Orb in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire are barred from being traded to Pokémon X and Y. Its initial mention occurred in A Three Team Scheme!, an episode where Shelly disclosed her ownership of the Red Orb. Notably, the English dub mistakenly identified it as the Rose Orb during this broadcast. The Red Orb's debut appearance was in Gaining Groudon, depicted as being in Archie's possession. He wielded it to command Kyogre subsequent to Shelly's liberation of the Pokémon from Team Magma's custody, leading to the destruction of their mobile sea base. During The Scuffle of Legends, following Team Rocket's accidental release of Kyogre into the ocean after an unsuccessful capture, Archie once more employed the Orb for control. Over time, the Orb gradually began to assert its influence over Archie, compelling him to unleash the Legendary Pokémon on a destructive rampage, even endangering his own team members with the threat of drowning. This influence intensified until the Orb assimilated into Archie's physique, driving him to complete madness. Subsequent to Groudon's victory over Kyogre, the Red Orb extricated itself from Archie's form and was promptly destroyed, alongside the Blue Orb. The Red Orb, termed "Red Jewel" in the Chuang Yi translation, initially surfaced in I'm Always Grumpig First Thing in the Morning II, where Tate and Liza protected it on Mt. Pyre, alongside the Blue Orb. Despite their diligent efforts to safeguard both Orbs, Magma Admin Blaise ultimately succeeded in stealing them. In The Beginning of the End with Kyogre & Groudon II, within the Seafloor Cavern, Groudon was roused by Team Magma and Kyogre by Team Aqua. Maxie subsequently seized command of both via the Orbs, aiming to grant Groudon a decisive edge. Yet, the simultaneous control of two Legendary Pokémon proved excessively demanding on his mental faculties, prompting him to entrust the Blue Orb to Tabitha, enabling the Magma Admin to manage Kyogre for him. During the execution of his directive, Tabitha was ambushed by Aqua Admin Amber, who utilized her Volbeat's Trick to pilfer the Blue Orb and deliver it to Archie. Subsequently, Ruby and Sapphire confronted Maxie and Archie in battle but struggled to reach them. Once Ruby's Castform, Fofo, successfully vanquished the two villainous leaders using Weather Ball, Ruby attempted to retrieve the Orbs from Maxie and Archie's grasp, only to discover they had begun merging with their wielders, rendering them irremovable. Apprehending that complete absorption would make the Orbs permanently irretrievable, Ruby instructed his Delcatty, Kiki, to employ Covet, but this effort proved futile. The Orbs subsequently fused entirely with the two individuals, manifesting Groudon's and Kyogre's patterns on their arms, a clear sign that the villainous team leaders were now under the Legendary Pokémon's control, rather than controlling them. Maxie and Archie then seized Ruby and Sapphire and ascended, propelled by the Orbs' powers being invoked by Groudon and Kyogre. Once the two Legendary Pokémon commenced their clash in Sootopolis City, Ruby and Sapphire recognized that the Meteorite fragments provided by Winona possessed the capacity to halt the Orbs' sway over Maxie and Archie, given their inherent power to nullify natural forces. By employing a wild Absol's Magic Coat to deflect the meteorite's energy towards the two malevolent team leaders, Ruby and Sapphire successfully restored them to their normal states, though this action simultaneously unleashed a significant energy burst, rendering both Trainers unconscious. They subsequently regained consciousness on Mirage Island, where they then engaged in training alongside Juan, Tate, and Liza, in anticipation of the culminating confrontation. Throughout this training period, Ruby ascertained that the Orbs necessitated human control for their powers to reach full potential and for them to integrate into their controllers. This revelation prompted him to understand that the Orbs now resided within both him and Sapphire, having selected them as their new command agents. Juan advised Ruby and Sapphire that mastering control over the Orbs was crucial, as insufficient mental fortitude would lead to them being controlled by the Orbs, mirroring the fates of Maxie and Archie. Amidst the training, Courtney appeared, extending her assistance to Ruby to halt the Kyogre-Groudon conflict, acknowledging that the situation had escalated far beyond Team Magma's or Team Aqua's original intentions, and preventing the destruction of the entire Hoenn region was paramount. Initially, Ruby hesitated to accept the proposition but ultimately consented to safeguard Sapphire from potential possession by the Blue Orb. Concluding their training, Ruby and Sapphire departed Mirage Island and approached Sootopolis City. After Ruby had already extricated the Red Orb from his person and Sapphire the Blue Orb from hers, he secured Sapphire within Wallace's aircar for her protection, then departed alongside Courtney. Their attempts to quell the clash between the two Legendary Pokémon proved unsuccessful. Nevertheless, with Norman's arrival accompanied by Rayquaza, Hoenn's third super-ancient Pokémon, Ruby managed to meld its power with that of the Orbs, ultimately pacifying Kyogre and Groudon and returning them to their dormant states. Archie and Maxie subsequently emerged from the Cave of Origin, reclaiming the Orbs with the intent to reignite the conflict, but were thwarted by Plusle and Minun. With both men incapacitated, Ruby deployed his sixth Pokémon—Celebi—to seize and obliterate the Orbs, thereby bringing the confrontation to a decisive close. Following this, Giovanni collected the gemstones that had coalesced from the shattered Orbs, divulging that these were, in fact, the Orbs' genuine manifestations: the Ruby and the Sapphire. In Beedrill Prepares, Blaise disclosed his secret observation of Giovanni appropriating the Orbs' fragmented remnants. He was too daunted by Giovanni's immediate presence to attempt their recovery.

Title: Blue Orb
Known in Japanese as あいいろのたま (Indigo Orb), the Blue Orb first appeared as a Key Item in Generation III. This fabled artifact shares a deep connection with the Legendary Pokémon, Kyogre. Beginning with Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire in Generation VI, it functions as a held item, enabling Kyogre to undergo Primal Reversion and transform into Primal Kyogre the moment it enters a battle.

Within Pokémon Ruby, Team Magma forcibly takes the Blue Orb from Mt. Pyre's summit, intending to use it to awaken Groudon. However, since Team Magma possesses the incorrect orb, their plan to control the ancient Pokémon fails. After the player either captures or defeats Groudon, a return to Mt. Pyre's peak allows observation of Team Magma Leader Maxie placing the orb back in its rightful spot. Subsequently, the player also returns the orb, and Maxie departs, marking Team Magma's final appearance in the game. In Pokémon Emerald, the narrative largely mirrors the events of Ruby and Sapphire, but with the added involvement of both Team Magma and Team Aqua, resulting in the theft of both the Blue Orb and the Red Orb. Notably, the player does not come into possession of the Blue Orb in this game.

In Pokémon HeartGold, the Blue Orb is presented to the player by Mr. Pokémon after the formidable Trainer Red has been vanquished, and both the National Pokédex and a Kanto first partner Pokémon have been acquired from Professor Oak. Once the player obtains this orb, Kyogre can be encountered at the Embedded Tower.

Pokémon Alpha Sapphire sees Team Aqua seize the Blue Orb from Mt. Pyre's summit, deploying it to rouse Kyogre. Unlike the Generation III games, Team Aqua wields the appropriate orb, which they utilize to unleash Kyogre's true might through Primal Reversion. Following the events at the Cave of Origin, Team Aqua Leader Archie hands over the Blue Orb, which he had previously stolen, to the player. Conversely, in Pokémon Omega Ruby, shortly after Team Magma purloins the Red Orb from Mt. Pyre's summit, the player receives the Blue Orb with the directive to reunite the two. Later, in Sootopolis City, Wallace and Archie reveal the Blue Orb's unique capabilities: it can safeguard the player within the Cave of Origin, reverse the Primal Reversion process, and pacify the enraged Groudon. Upon reaching the deepest parts of the cavern, the player equips the Magma Suit and inserts the Blue Orb into it, materializing a protective blue barrier around themselves.

In the Mega Dimension DLC of Pokémon Legends: Z-A, the Kyogre encountered in the Hyperspace Primordial Sea carries a Blue Orb, which activates during the second phase of Team MZ's confrontation with it. To encounter Kyogre at the Embedded Tower in Pokémon HeartGold, possession of the Blue Orb is a prerequisite. When held by a Kyogre, it triggers Primal Reversion, transforming it into Primal Kyogre as soon as it enters battle. This item remains unaffected by effects that negate items, such as Embargo and Klutz. Furthermore, item-manipulating effects like Trick and Fling are incapable of bestowing a Blue Orb upon Kyogre or removing one from it. Pokémon holding a Blue Orb in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire cannot be exchanged via trade to Pokémon X and Y.

The Blue Orb's initial mention in the anime occurred in "A Three Team Scheme!", where Tabitha disclosed his ownership of it, though the English dub erroneously called it the Indigo Orb in that installment. Its on-screen debut was in "Gaining Groudon," depicted as being in Maxie's possession, intended for Groudon's control. Amidst the pandemonium when Shelly liberated Kyogre from Team Magma's custody, Pikachu managed to seize the Blue Orb. Unexpectedly, the orb fused with Pikachu, possessing him and granting a colossal power boost, yet simultaneously instigating sudden rampages. In "The Scuffle of Legends," it was unveiled that Groudon had intentionally prompted the Blue Orb's fusion with Pikachu to enlist his aid in breaking free from Team Aqua's submarine. After Groudon's release, it engaged Kyogre in battle. Once Kyogre was defeated, the Blue Orb detached itself from Pikachu's form and was subsequently annihilated, alongside the Red Orb.

The Blue Orb made another appearance in "The Cavern," where it was shown to be in Archie's grasp at the Seafloor Cavern. Despite Shelly's earnest attempts to dissuade him from his scheme, Archie hurled the Orb at the slumbering Kyogre, causing it to awaken and transform into Primal Kyogre.

In the manga, the Blue Orb (referred to as the "Blue Jewel" in the Chuang Yi translation) first appeared in "I'm Always Grumpig First Thing in the Morning II," where Tate and Liza protected it, alongside the Red Orb, on Mt. Pyre. Despite their valiant efforts, Magma Admin Blaise ultimately stole both Orbs. In "The Beginning of the End with Kyogre & Groudon II," at the Seafloor Cavern, Groudon and Kyogre were awakened by Team Aqua and Team Magma, respectively. Maxie initially exerted control over both Legendary Pokémon using the Orbs, aiming to favor Groudon. However, the mental strain of simultaneously controlling two such powerful beings proved too much. Consequently, he entrusted the Blue Orb to Tabitha, tasking the Magma Admin with controlling Kyogre on his behalf. While carrying out his orders, Tabitha was ambushed by Aqua Admin Amber, who employed her Volbeat's Trick to snatch the Blue Orb and deliver it to Archie.

Later, Ruby and Sapphire confronted Maxie and Archie in a battle but struggled to reach them. Once Ruby's Castform, Fofo, finally defeated the two nefarious team leaders with Weather Ball, Ruby attempted to reclaim the Orbs from their hands, only to discover they had begun merging with their wielders, making removal impossible. Realizing that complete absorption would render the Orbs irretrievable, Ruby instructed his Delcatty, Kiki, to use Covet, but to no avail. The Orbs then fully integrated with the two men, causing Groudon and Kyogre's distinct patterns to manifest on their arms, signifying that the Legendary Pokémon were now manipulating the villainous leaders, rather than the inverse. Maxie and Archie then seized Ruby and Sapphire, taking flight as the Orbs' energies responded to Groudon and Kyogre's call.

After the two Legendary Pokémon commenced their fierce battle at Sootopolis City, Ruby and Sapphire comprehended that the Meteorite fragments, gifted by Winona, possessed the power to neutralize the Orbs' influence over Maxie and Archie, given their ability to halt the forces of nature. Utilizing a wild Absol's Magic Coat to redirect the meteorite's energy toward the two team leaders, Ruby and Sapphire successfully restored them to their normal states, though the resulting powerful energy burst rendered both Trainers unconscious. They later reawakened on Mirage Island, where they dedicated time to training with Juan, Tate, and Liza, in preparation for the decisive confrontation. During their training, Ruby deduced that the Orbs required a human controller to maximize their power and to allow for their complete absorption. This insight led him to realize that the Orbs now resided within him and Sapphire, having chosen them as their new controllers. Juan cautioned Ruby and Sapphire that they had to master control over the Orbs, as insufficient mental fortitude would inevitably lead to them being controlled, just as Maxie and Archie had been.

During this period of intense training, Courtney appeared, offering her assistance to Ruby in quelling the escalating battle between Kyogre and Groudon. She expressed that the conflict had spiraled far beyond the intentions of either Team Magma or Team Aqua, and there was no sense in allowing the entire Hoenn region to face destruction. Initially, Ruby hesitated to accept her proposition, but ultimately agreed to safeguard Sapphire from the potential possession by the Blue Orb. Concluding their training, Ruby and Sapphire departed Mirage Island and arrived near Sootopolis City. Having already extracted the Red Orb from his own body, and with Sapphire having extracted the Blue Orb from hers, Ruby secured Sapphire within Wallace's aircar for her protection before leaving with Courtney. Their initial attempts to halt the clash between the two Legendary Pokémon proved fruitless. However, with Norman's arrival alongside Rayquaza, Hoenn's third super-ancient Pokémon, Ruby was able to combine its immense power with that of the Orbs to finally cease the conflict and pacify both Kyogre and Groudon, sending them back to their slumber. Archie and Maxie then emerged from the Cave of Origin, reclaiming the Orbs in an attempt to reignite the battle, but were thwarted by Plusle and Minun. With the two men immobilized, Ruby dispatched his sixth Pokémon—Celebi—to snatch and destroy the Orbs, effectively bringing the conflict to an end. Subsequently, the gemstones that materialized from the shattered Orbs were collected by Giovanni, who disclosed that these gemstones represented the Orbs' authentic forms: the Ruby and

Title: Jade Orb

Known in Japanese as もえぎいろのたま, or the Verdant Orb, this Key Item made its debut in Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver. This legendary artifact shares a deep connection with the majestic Rayquaza. Acquiring the Jade Orb is a prerequisite for encountering Rayquaza within the Embedded Tower.

While the Jade Orb itself has not appeared in the Pokémon Adventures manga, a pivotal moment in "It All Ends Now V" saw Norman disclose the Pokémon Association's past endeavor: they had captured and studied Rayquaza with the aim of fabricating a "Green Orb" (翠色の宝珠 Green Orb) to bring it under their dominion. This project, however, remained incomplete. "The Final Battle IV" revealed that Professor Yanase eventually discarded the core of this unfinished Green Orb, which was subsequently discovered by Emerald. He then incorporated it into his personal accessories.

In "The Final Battle IX," Ruby put forth the theory that this core's inherent power to command Pokémon assisted Emerald in pacifying creatures that were in a frenzy due to Guile Hideout's influence. PS611 unveiled that Emerald later passed the Green Orb core to Norman, who transported it to the research facility housing Rayquaza in captivity. There, Norman employed specialized machinery to enhance its purity. This purification process not only imbued the core with a jade hue but also significantly amplified its inherent strength. Despite this boost, Norman found himself unable to harness the core to control Rayquaza.

When Ruby interacted with the core, he managed to exert a minor degree of control over Rayquaza, sufficient to guide its movements. Sapphire's interaction, conversely, granted her the ability to delve into Rayquaza's psyche, enabling her to comprehend its thoughts. Later, as Ruby and Sapphire made ready to journey into space aboard Rayquaza to neutralize the Grand Meteor Delta, the Green Orb core was affixed into the Aqua Suit Sapphire wore. A specific game restriction dictates that once the player obtains the Jade Orb, they are prevented from taking a photo with Chuck, irrespective of whether he is outside the Fighting Dojo.

Title: Enigma Stone

Known in Japan as なぞのすいしょう (Enigmatic Crystal), the Enigma Stone serves as a Key event item exclusively distributed through Wi-Fi for players of Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver. This object holds the distinction of being both the inaugural and sole officially distributed event item specifically for these titles. A strong parallel can be drawn between the Enigma Stone and the Eon Ticket; much like its Generation III counterpart, possession of the Stone instigates the encounter with the Eon Pokémon absent from the wild encounters in the player's specific game version. Specifically, HeartGold players will find Latios, while SoulSilver players will encounter Latias. While any player possessing the Pokédex is eligible to download the Enigma Stone, the in-game event itself remains inaccessible until they have both achieved Hall of Fame status and acquired the National Pokédex. Should the Stone be presented at the Pewter Museum of Science, the wandering Pokémon Champion Steven Stone will arrange for a researcher to inspect it, leading to the revelation that the item is, in fact, the Soul Dew. Following this brief interaction, one of the twin Eon Pokémon will assault the player, its identity determined by the game version in use, thereby providing a chance for its capture. This legendary creature will persistently reappear at the designated location until successfully caught. Wi-Fi distribution periods for this item spanned November 27, 2009, to January 11, 2010, for Japanese copies of the games, and July 31, 2010, to August 27, 2010, for North American and PAL editions. Its debut in Japan coincided with the announcement of the 10th Anniversary Mew event for HeartGold and SoulSilver.

Title: Unown Report

A Key Item in Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, the Unown Report (Japanese: アンノーンノート, or Unknown Notebook) supersedes the Unown Mode of the Pokédex from Generation II. It documents the player's captured Unown forms. With each additional Unown, researchers gain further insight into the species and the ruins' past, and the player records these new findings in the Report.

Full research completion requires capturing all 26 alphabetical Unown forms, opening the hidden rooms within each of the four stone panel chambers, and finishing Page 7. After these steps, the ! and ? Unown forms will appear in the ruins' main chamber for capture.

Unlocking specific pages occurs under various conditions: Page 5 becomes available after catching an Unown. Page 6 unlocks when enough Unown are caught to fill Page 2, simultaneously sorting all captured Unown alphabetically. Page 7 is accessible once all 26 alphabetical Unown forms are obtained. Page 8 requires opening all four hidden rooms and observing the entrance room's inscription. Finally, Page 9 is revealed upon catching both the ! and ? Unown forms.

The Report includes the following transcribed passages:
"Found a strange puzzle in the ruins. Solved it, and suddenly a hole opened up in the ground and I dropped into the room below! Should have known that ancient ruins would have surprising tricks like that. There was a stone statue where I fell. The place might have been a reception hall. Then, suddenly, the Unown appeared. That puzzle was like a key somehow. It woke the Unown up from their sleep. These ruins have become difficult."
"If you turn on the radio in the ruins, you can receive mysterious radio waves! It's proof that the Unown communicate with each other using radio waves! There are as many Unown as there are letters in the alphabet. Right now, we're confirming the existence of 26 kinds of Unown. Do you think you can find all 26 of them?"
"It seems that individually they don't make much sense, but when the various Unown are arranged in order, they begin to make sense. In nature, they seem to be scattered around randomly, but the truth is they're arranged in a very specific order. In this report, the scattered Unown were sorted and put into order by the research-center staff. That makes the Unown much easier to understand."
"Scratched on the floor of the ruins are Unown letters. Finding the room feels like a huge discovery! The Researchers were very excited and said they were going to go check it out. There aren't just 26 different kinds of Unown, Two more have been discovered! But that begs the question of where those other two kinds are hiding..."
"The characters found scratched on the floor in the small room are transcribed here in this report. The Researchers were saying they'll need to examine the ruins again, but...?"
"In the past, it seems that Unown lived with relatives of humans in these ruins. In the lower levels of these ruins is a Pokémon statue. We've come to understand that it was those relatives who made that statue. The Unown are very timid creatures, so they don't go outside the ruins. Even if a bunch of people gathered near the ruins, it is said that the ones who wished for Unown and humans to again coexist have all left."

Unown A is unique for its associated word change; initially the adjective "Angry" in Unown Mode and Japanese versions, it became the verb "Anger" in non-Japanese releases to align with its Pokémon Power on its debut TCG card.

Title: Blue Card

The Blue Card (Japanese: ブルーカード Blue Card) is a Key Item that first appeared in Generation II. This item allows the player character to participate in Buena's daily broadcast, Buena's Password, where they can answer questions to accumulate points on their Blue Card. A correct answer earns the player 1 point. These "Blue Points" can then be spent like currency to claim prizes at the Goldenrod Radio Tower. Players are restricted to holding a maximum of 30 points on the card at any single time. Upon reaching exactly 30 points, the player gains the ability to save Buena's contact information in their Pokégear. Prizes become available for collection at the Goldenrod Radio Tower once specific point thresholds are met. Although the 30-point limit remains constant, a player who achieves this maximum can swap their current Blue Card for a blank one. After reaching the 30-point mark, registering Buena's number into the Pokégear also becomes possible. In the Korean editions of Gold and Silver, Teru-sama ID 0x74 is identified as "Blue Card," a designation that mirrors the actual Blue Card item ID present in Pokémon Crystal and Pokémon Stadium 2.

Title: Slowpoke Tail

This entry focuses on the prized commodity. Regarding the culinary component linked to it, refer to Smoke-Poke Tail. Known in Japanese as おいしいシッポ (Delicious Tail), the Slowpoke Tail was spelled SlowpokeTail before Generation VI. This valuable collectible is found exclusively within Generation II titles. It consists of a Slowpoke's detached caudal appendage. While its data persists in the majority of core series games from Generation IV onwards, and it receives mentions across numerous other titles, acquisition is limited solely to the Generation II entries. Its sole functional utility lies in its sale. In Pokémon Gold, Silver, Crystal, HeartGold, and SoulSilver, a vendor situated by the Pokémon Center on Route 32 proposes selling a Slowpoke Tail for $1,000,000. This transaction is impossible, as the sum exceeds the maximum currency the player can possess in these specific games. Subsequently, the player uncovers Team Rocket's activities: excising Slowpoke Tails from Slowpoke within the Slowpoke Well, located in Azalea Town. Following Team Rocket's defeat, the individual on Route 32 vanishes. In Pokémon X and Y, Restaurant Le Yeah in Lumiose City features Slowpoke Tail among its culinary offerings. Within the Pokémon Sun and Pokémon Moon Special Demo Version, a Police Officer stationed at the Hau'oli City Marina investigates what he suspects to be a Slowpoke Tail trade, only to discover it's merely two individuals admiring each other's Slowpoke. A display shelf in the Thrifty Megamart in Pokémon Sun and Moon, Ultra Sun, and Ultra Moon showcases items like Peppered Slowpoke Tail on Salad, Slowpoke Tail Quiche, and Slowpoke Tail Sandwiches, none of which are available for player acquisition. Furthermore, Slowpoke's Pokédex entry in Pokémon Sun mentions its tail frequently detaches without injury to the creature, subsequently regrowing. Conversely, its Pokémon Moon Pokédex entry notes that dried Slowpoke tails frequently serve as ingredients in Alolan home-cooked stews. Sierra in Pokémon GO is depicted with a Slowpoke Tail dangling from the rear corner of her satchel. This item functions as a cooking ingredient, possessing a base Strength of 342. It can be incorporated into various recipes. Slowpoke Tail is collectible by the player's helper Pokémon. Upon discovering Professor Gordon's Slowpoke ensnared in ice, Tim Goodman and Rachel Myers extracted it, but its tail detached due to being frozen fast. Pikachu retrieved the tail, inquiring after Slowpoke's well-being, only to observe its appendage had already regenerated. Slowpoke permitted Pikachu to retain the severed tail. Subsequently, Tim and Pikachu came across a famished and agitated Galarian Darmanitan. Pikachu employed the Slowpoke's tail as an enticement, drawing Darmanitan closer; the Pokémon then inadvertently scooped up Pikachu along with the treat. Following Tim's rescue of Pikachu, Darmanitan consumed the Slowpoke tail and became placated. In Ursaring Major, Team Rocket severed the tails of a Slowpoke herd situated by the Slowpoke Well near Azalea Town, intending their sale for financial gain. Nevertheless, Silver and his Ursaring intervened, preventing their scheme. Two Team Skull Grunts vended grilled Slowpoke Tails at the Iki Town festival, with one such tail making an appearance in The Party Crasher and Guzma the Destroyer. The inaugural explicit reference within the games to humans consuming Pokémon occurs when a Team Rocket Grunt highlights the Slowpoke Tail's status as a culinary delicacy.

Title: Clear Bell

This Key Item, known in Japanese as とうめいなスズ (Transparent Suzu), made its debut in Generation II of the Pokémon series. It stands as one of the fabled relics, alongside the Rainbow Feather, intricately linked with the legendary Pokémon Ho-Oh and Suicune. Within Pokémon Crystal, this item grants the player access to an encounter with Suicune on the Tin Tower's ground floor; notably, this particular Suicune remains stationary, unlike its counterparts, requiring the player to either defeat or secure it.

In Pokémon HeartGold, it represents the specific kind of bell incorporated into Ecruteak City's Bell Tower. Its power, when combined with the Rainbow Wing's influence and the Kimono Girls' ritualistic dance, facilitates Ho-Oh's summoning to the aforementioned Bell Tower. Although Suicune again displays a notable interest in the player, it holds no connection to the Clear Bell's function in this scenario. For Pokémon SoulSilver, the Tidal Bell serves as its functional equivalent. Analogous to the Tidal Bell not being essential for encountering Lugia in HeartGold – where the Pokémon manifests with merely the Silver Wing acquired in Pewter City – the Clear Bell is similarly not mandatory for a Ho-Oh encounter in SoulSilver.

Within Pokémon Omega Ruby, Captain Stern bestows the Clear Bell upon the player in trade for the Scanner, a prerequisite for encountering Ho-Oh aboard Sea Mauville. In the game Pokémon Pokopia, Clear Bells are craftable items, requiring five Rainbow Feathers and five ingots of Rare Pokémetal for their creation. The player acquires the crafting schematic for this item subsequent to befriending Raikou, Entei, and Suicune. Should Ho-Oh be soaring above, sounding the Clear Bell will compel it to descend, thereby enabling the player to befriend the legendary bird.

The Clear Bells, known as "Crystal Bells" within Pokémon the Series: Gold and Silver, made an appearance in the episode titled "For Ho-Oh the Bells Toll!". Team Rocket pilfered several of these bells from the Tin Tower, planning to use them for Ho-Oh's summoning, unaware that their functionality differed from this belief. Nevertheless, Jessie inadvertently shattering one of the bells provoked the local Bug-type Pokémon, which subsequently trapped the villains in webbing, thus detaining them well before Morty, Ash, Misty, Brock, and Eusine arrived. As demonstrated by Jessie, human hands alone cannot successfully ring the Crystal Bells, though legend dictates they chime spontaneously upon Ho-Oh's proximity.

In the Chuang Yi translation, this item, termed the "Invisible Bell," was furnished to the Team Rocket Elite Trio by the Masked Man for their mission to capture Suicune; its inherent power permitted the wielder to penetrate the crystal barriers Suicune could erect for self-defense. Following Crystal and Eusine's thwarting of their Suicune capture attempt, Eusine confiscated the Clear Bell from the trio in the chapter "Suddenly Suicune (Part 2)." During the subsequent chapter, he employed it to circumvent a crystal wall Suicune had manifested at the Tin Tower, thereby rescuing Crystal who was trapped within. "The Last Battle V" depicted Misty's unsuccessful endeavor to co-trap the Masked Man using Suicune's crystal wall; instead, she alone became imprisoned as the Masked Man's Gastly utilized Curse, debilitating Suicune and preventing it from dispelling the barrier. Providentially, Eusine shortly arrived, deploying his Clear Bell to rescue Misty. Nevertheless, despite his ownership of the Clear Bell, Suicune subsequently selected Crystal as its new partner, a choice that greatly disappointed Eusine.

While the item's Japanese and English appellations suggest a degree of transparency, its sprite in HeartGold and SoulSilver completely lacks clarity or even the blue hue characteristic of the Crystal game cartridge; this changes only during Ho-Oh's summoning, at which point it radiates a brilliant bluish-white luminescence, appearing to become transparent. For Korean Gold and Silver, Teru-sama ID 0x46 is designated as 크리스탈방울 Clear Bell, aligning with the actual Clear Bell item ID found in Pokémon Crystal and Pokémon Stadium 2, notwithstanding the fact that these latter titles were never released in the Korean language.

Title: Lost Item

Known in Japanese as おとしもの (Lost Item), the Lost Item made its debut as a Key Item in Generation II. This particular item is a Poké Doll that belongs to the character Copycat. Within the game's dialogue, it is also referred to as a Clefairy Poké Doll, or ピッピにんぎょう (Pippi Doll) in Japanese, and is often simply called a Clefairy doll. Although unused in subsequent generations, its depiction evolved into a Mime Jr. doll, a change reflected in both English and Japanese versions. Copycat consistently refers to it as a "Clefairy Poké Doll" in English dialogue across both Generation II and Generation IV. The Official Nintendo Player's Guides for Pokémon Gold and Silver and Pokémon Crystal list this item as Clefairy Doll in their respective item sections (page 109 in the Gold and Silver guide; page 151 in the Crystal guide). However, within the walkthrough portions of these same guides, it is identified as both Clefairy Doll and Poké Doll (page 65 of the Gold and Silver guide; page 64 of the Crystal guide). Correspondingly, in the Japanese dialogue of Generation II and IV, Copycat identifies it as ピッピにんぎょう (Pippi Doll), which shares its name with the standard Poké Doll item in Japanese. Its Japanese description further clarifies its identity: in Generation II, it's called ピッピにんぎょう (Pippi Doll; Clefairy Doll); in Generation IV, ピッピの にんぎょう (Doll of Pippi; Doll of Clefairy); and from Generation V onward, it's referred to as マネネの 人形(にんぎょう) (Doll of Manene; Doll of Mime Jr.). Upon its successful return to Copycat, she rewards the player with a Pass for the Magnet Train. Curiously, despite its last usage being in Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, the Lost Item's sprite and description from Generation V onwards consistently depict a Mime Jr., for reasons that remain unclear.

Title: Pass

This entry focuses on the specific Pass needed to board the Magnet Train; for other applications, consult Pass (disambiguation). Designated as a Key Item, this Pass – known in Japanese as ていきけん (Season Pass) during Generation II and リニアパス (Linear Pass) from Generation IV onward – is indispensable for travelers wishing to utilize the Magnet Train service connecting Goldenrod City in Johto with Saffron City in Kanto. Its debut occurred in Generation II. Exclusively within Generation II, the Japanese designation for this Pass denoted a style of rail ticket permitting unrestricted transit between two designated stops for a finite duration, typically a week or a month. Subsequently, beginning with Generation IV, the Japanese terminology shifted to directly reference the Magnet Train itself, which is internally referred to as リニア (Linear). Within in-game dialogue, alternate appellations for this item include Magnet Train Pass, Pass for the Magnet Train, and (with lowercase spelling) rail Pass. Early guidebooks sometimes identified it as a Railway Pass. Once the Machine Part has been successfully delivered to the Power Plant and the Pass is present in the player's Bag, unrestricted travel aboard the Magnet Train becomes available between Saffron City and Goldenrod City. This particular item cannot be activated directly from the Bag interface; instead, players are required to engage with the attendant stationed at either train terminal.

Title: Machine Part

For those seeking the equivalent item in Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, please refer to Machine Part (Orre). This particular Machine Part, designated きかいのぶひん in Japanese, is a Key Item that made its debut in Generation II. Its recovery in Pokémon Gold, Silver, Crystal, HeartGold, and SoulSilver allows the Power Plant's staff to resume operations, consequently restoring full functionality to both the Magnet Train and the Lavender Radio Tower. In appreciation for its return, the Power Plant manager awards the player a TM07 (Zap Cannon) or TM57 (Charge Beam). A distinct quirk of Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver is that, despite being a hidden item, the Machine Part remains undetectable by the Dowsing MCHN.

Title: Mystery Egg

A Key Item originating in Generation II, the Mystery Egg (known as ふしぎなタマゴ, or Mystery Egg, in Japanese) serves a crucial role. This peculiar egg was discovered by the Day-Care Couple, who subsequently entrusted it to Mr. Pokémon. Mr. Pokémon then requests the player to deliver the Mystery Egg to Professor Elm for analysis, recognizing Elm's specialized knowledge concerning Pokémon Evolution. Upon the player's victory over Falkner and acquisition of the Zephyr Badge, Elm's aide will return the item, confirming its true nature as a Pokémon Egg. Should the player elect to keep this Egg in their active party, it will eventually hatch into a Togepi. Presenting the newly hatched Togepi to Professor Elm will prompt him to reward the player with an Everstone. The moment this item is acquired from Mr. Pokémon, the game designates Cherrygrove City's Pokémon Center as the player's most recently visited recovery point. Consequently, in scenarios where the player blacks out or employs the Teleport move outside of combat (despite its legitimate unavailability at this stage), they will be transported to this specific Pokémon Center. This prevents them from returning directly to their home and thus circumventing the initial confrontation with their rival.

Title: Tidal Bell

The Tidal Bell (Japanese: うみなりのスズ Tide Suzu) is a distinguished Key Item that made its debut in Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver. It is recognized as one of the mythical artifacts connected to Lugia, often mentioned alongside the Silver Feather. Within Pokémon SoulSilver, this specific type of bell is integrated into the architecture of Ecruteak City's Bell Tower. When deployed, this item's influence, combined with the power of the Silver Wing and the ceremonial performance of the Kimono Girls, facilitates Lugia's summoning to the Whirl Islands. In Pokémon Alpha Sapphire, players receive the Tidal Bell from Captain Stern in exchange for the Scanner, an acquisition essential for encountering Lugia at Sea Mauville. For those playing Pokémon Pokopia, Tidal Bells can be constructed by combining five Silver Feathers and five ingots of Rare Pokémetal. The necessary crafting schematic for the Tidal Bell is unlocked after the player establishes friendship with Articuno, Zapdos, and Moltres. Sounding the Tidal Bell when Lugia is observed flying above will prompt the legendary Pokémon to descend to the player's location, thereby enabling a bond to be formed with Lugia.

Title: Lock Capsule

Known in Japanese as ロックカプセル (Lock Capsule), this Key Item is designed to be unobtainable, making its initial appearance within the game data of Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver. It has never been legitimately accessible in any Pokémon title. The Lock Capsule exhibits distinct menu sprites when viewed in Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver compared to Pokémon Black and White. While it was intended to be the sole method of acquiring TM95 (Snarl) in Pokémon Black and White, it ultimately went unreleased, rendering that TM unobtainable through normal means in those games. However, in Pokémon Black 2 and White 2, TM95 can be acquired through standard gameplay. The Lock Capsule serves no functional purpose within Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver.

Should the Lock Capsule be obtained via Mystery Gift, an option labeled "VIEW LOCK CAPSULE CARD" will appear on the Mystery Gift menu. This card will display the text: "The Lock Capsule is held in the strictest safekeeping." Subsequently, transferring it to Pokémon Black and White using the Relocator will cause the Wonder Card's message to update to: "The Lock Capsule was delivered safely!". Within Pokémon Black and White, the item can be opened by Mr. Lock in Castelia City. This action grants the player access to the exclusive TM95 (Snarl) stored inside. Furthermore, opening the Lock Capsule reveals a letter, which Mr. Lock reads aloud, stating: "I entrust the Technical Machine in the Lock Capsule to a trustworthy Trainer in the Unova region. Don't allow it to fall into the hands of villains like Team Rocket."

Title: Orange Mail

Known in Japanese as オレンジメール (Orange Mail), this particular form of Mail is exclusively featured within the Generation III Pokémon titles. It serves to carry a message, comprising up to nine distinct words and phrases chosen from the easy chat system's predefined vocabulary. The message's layout consists of four lines, each accommodating two slots, plus a final fifth line with a single slot. Players compose these messages when assigning the Orange Mail to a Pokémon as a held item. Further phrases can be unlocked by consulting the Hipster character found in Mauville City. Sending a message to other games within the same generation is accomplished by trading the Pokémon equipped with this item. The message inscribed on the Mail persists for as long as a Pokémon continues to hold it or it resides in the Mailbox. Should this item be placed in the Bag or the Item Storage System, it loses the capacity to store a message; however, an existing message can be intentionally erased during this storage process. Following the deletion of its message, the item becomes available for a fresh inscription. A Pokémon that is currently holding an Orange Mail faces several restrictions: it cannot be deposited into the PC, left at the Pokémon Day Care, traded to Pokémon Colosseum or XD: Gale of Darkness, or transferred to Pokémon Box: Ruby & Sapphire or subsequently to Generation IV games via the Pal Park. The held item status can be nullified by the move Knock Off. Conversely, the Orange Mail is impervious to theft attempts by the moves Covet or Thief.

Title: Harbor Mail

Known in Japan as ハーバーメール (Harbor Mail), this particular form of Mail is exclusively available within the Generation III titles.
