It allows for the inscription of a message comprising a maximum of nine words or phrases, which must be selected from the Easy Chat System's predefined vocabulary. This message is organized across four lines, each containing two slots, plus a final fifth line with a single slot. A Trainer composes the message at the moment they assign the item to a Pokémon for holding. Further phrases for use with this system can be unlocked by consulting the Hipster character residing in Mauville City.

To transmit the message to other games from the same generation, players must trade the Pokémon equipped with this item. The inscribed message persists and remains accessible as long as the Harbor Mail is being held by a Pokémon or if it is placed in the Mailbox. However, when stored in the Bag or within the Item Storage System, this item cannot contain any message; a player does, however, have the choice to erase any previously existing message upon placing it in these storage locations. After an existing message has been cleared from the item, it becomes available for the player to inscribe a new one.

A Pokémon carrying this item faces several restrictions: it cannot be deposited into the PC, left at the Pokémon Day Care, exchanged with Pokémon Colosseum or XD: Gale of Darkness, or moved to Pokémon Box: Ruby & Sapphire or to Generation IV games via the Pal Park. While the move Knock Off is capable of dislodging this item, it is immune to being pilfered by the moves Covet or Thief.

Players in Pokémon Ruby, Sapphire, and Emerald possess the option to trade a single Harbor Mail for the Coin Case, a transaction that occurs in Mauville City.

Title: Glitter Mail

Glitter Mail (Japanese: キラキラメール Glitter Mail) functions as a Mail item, exclusively available in the Generation III games. This item can store a message, comprising a maximum of nine words and phrases selected from the Easy Chat System's predetermined list. Its structure features four rows each with two slots, alongside a fifth row containing one slot. Players compose the message at the point of assigning this item to a Pokémon as a held item. Further phrases are unlockable by the player via the Hipster residing in Mauville City. Its embedded message can be conveyed across other titles within the same generation by means of trading the Pokémon that holds this particular item. Its content remains accessible while this item is either held by a Pokémon or deposited in the Mailbox. No message can be present within this item upon its storage in the Bag or the Item Storage System. Nevertheless, an option exists to remove any prior message when relocating it to these locations. Following the deletion of the message from this item, the player gains the capability to repurpose it with fresh content. A Pokémon carrying this item cannot be deposited into the PC, left at the Pokémon Day Care, exchanged with Pokémon Colosseum or XD: Gale of Darkness, nor can it be transferred to Pokémon Box: Ruby & Sapphire or to Generation IV titles via the Pal Park. The move Knock Off is capable of dislodging this item. However, it is impervious to theft by the moves Covet or Thief. Glitter Mail constitutes one of six Mail variants included in a letter set released exclusively by the Pokémon Center, which debuted in November 2021.

Title: Mech Mail

Known in Japanese as メカニカルメール (Mechanical Mail), the Mech Mail is a unique Mail item found solely within the Generation III titles. This particular Mail item holds a message comprising a maximum of nine words or phrases, which must be selected from the easy chat system's predefined options. Its structure consists of four rows, each accommodating two slots, complemented by a fifth row containing a single slot. Players compose the message content at the moment they assign the Mech Mail for a Pokémon to hold. Further phrases for use with this system can be unlocked by consulting the Hipster character in Mauville City. Transmission of the message to other games within the same generation is achievable by trading the Pokémon equipped with this item. The message's content persists for as long as the Mech Mail is either affixed to a Pokémon or stored within the Mailbox. Should the item be placed in the Bag or the Item Storage System, it cannot retain a message; however, during the process of placing it into these locations, an existing message may optionally be cleared. Following the deletion of its message, the item becomes available for players to apply a fresh one. A Pokémon carrying Mech Mail faces several restrictions: it cannot be deposited into the PC or the Pokémon Day Care. Furthermore, it is ineligible for trades to Pokémon Colosseum or XD: Gale of Darkness, nor can it be transferred to Pokémon Box: Ruby & Sapphire or onward to Generation IV titles via the Pal Park. While the Mech Mail is susceptible to removal by the move Knock Off, it is impervious to theft attempts by Covet or Thief.

Title: Wood Mail

Known in Japan as ウッディメール (Woody Mail), the Wood Mail functions as a Mail item found solely within Generation III titles. This item permits the inscription of a message comprising up to nine words and phrases, drawn from the easy chat system's pre-established vocabulary, arranged into a grid of four rows each containing two slots, plus a final fifth row with a single slot. Players compose its content upon instructing a Pokémon to hold it. Further phrases become accessible via the Hipster located in Mauville City. Its embedded message is transferable to other games of the same generation through the trade of the Pokémon carrying it. The message persists while the item is either affixed to a Pokémon or retained within the Mailbox. When placed in the Bag or the Item Storage System, the Wood Mail cannot carry a message. Nevertheless, an existing message can be manually erased during storage in these locations. Following message deletion, players gain the ability to repurpose the item with fresh content. A Pokémon equipped with this item faces several restrictions: it cannot be deposited into the PC or the Pokémon Day Care, nor can it be traded to Pokémon Colosseum or XD: Gale of Darkness. Furthermore, transfer to Pokémon Box Ruby & Sapphire or to Generation IV titles via the Pal Park is also prevented. The move Knock Off is capable of dislodging this item. Conversely, it remains impervious to theft by Covet or Thief.

Title: Wave Mail

Known in Japanese as クロスメール (Cross Mail), the Wave Mail is a specific variety of Mail item found only within the Generation III Pokémon titles. This item allows for the composition of a message containing a maximum of nine words or phrases, drawn from the easy chat system's predefined vocabulary. The message layout consists of four rows, each with two slots, followed by a fifth row containing a single slot. Players compose their messages at the moment this item is assigned to a Pokémon to hold. Further phrases become available for use after consulting the Hipster located in Mauville City. Transmission of the message to other games from the same generation is achievable by trading the Pokémon that holds the Wave Mail. Its content persists while the Wave Mail is either equipped by a Pokémon or deposited within the Mailbox. However, the item cannot retain a message when placed in the Bag or the Item Storage System; consequently, when storing it in these locations, an existing message can be optionally erased. Should the message be removed, the player gains the ability to inscribe a new one onto the item. A Pokémon equipped with this item faces several restrictions: it cannot be deposited into the PC or the Pokémon Day Care, nor can it be traded to Pokémon Colosseum or Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness. Furthermore, it is prevented from being transferred to Pokémon Box: Ruby & Sapphire or migrated to Generation IV games via the Pal Park. The Wave Mail is susceptible to removal by the move Knock Off. Nevertheless, it is immune to being stolen through the use of Covet or Thief.

Title: Bead Mail

Bead Mail, known in Japanese as トレジャーメール Treasure Mail, functions as a specific category of Mail, found solely within the Generation III titles. Players can embed a message within it, comprising a maximum of nine words or phrases drawn from the easy chat system's predetermined lexicon. This message adopts a layout of four rows, each containing two slots, plus a final fifth row with a single slot. A message is composed by the player at the point they instruct a Pokémon to hold this particular item. Additional phrases for use with Bead Mail can be acquired by the player after speaking with the Hipster character residing in Mauville City.

This message can be transmitted between games of the identical generation by engaging in a trade involving the Pokémon that is currently holding the Bead Mail. The embedded message persists and is accessible as long as the Bead Mail remains either attached to a Pokémon or placed within the Mailbox system. However, if the Bead Mail is situated in the Bag or the Item Storage System, it cannot retain any message. Nonetheless, an existing message can be intentionally erased when the item is deposited into either of these locations. Following the deletion of a message from the Bead Mail, the player gains the capacity to employ the item once more, imbuing it with a fresh message.

A Pokémon that carries Bead Mail is prevented from being deposited into the PC, left at the Pokémon Day Care, exchanged with Pokémon Colosseum or Pokémon XD: Gale of Darkness, or migrated to Pokémon Box: Ruby & Sapphire or subsequently to Generation IV titles via the Pal Park. The move Knock Off is capable of dislodging this item. Conversely, it is immune to being stolen through the effects of Covet or Thief.

Title: Shadow Mail

Known in Japanese as シャドーメール, the Shadow Mail is a specific variant of Mail found solely within the Generation III Pokémon titles. Players can compose a message of up to nine words or phrases, drawn from the easy chat system's predefined lexicon. This message is organized across four rows, each containing two slots, plus a final fifth row with a single slot. Message creation occurs when a trainer assigns the item to a Pokémon for holding. Further phrases become available by speaking with the Hipster located in Mauville City. This message can be transmitted to other Generation III games through the act of trading the Pokémon that possesses the item. Its message persists as long as the Shadow Mail is either clutched by a Pokémon or housed within the Mailbox. However, if the item is kept in the Bag or the Item Storage System, it cannot retain a message; yet, when moving it to these locations, the option arises to erase any previously inscribed text. Following the deletion of its message, the player is free to repurpose the item with fresh content. A Pokémon equipped with this item faces several restrictions: it cannot be deposited into the PC, left at the Pokémon Day Care, traded to Pokémon Colosseum or XD: Gale of Darkness, or transferred to Pokémon Box: Ruby & Sapphire or across to Generation IV titles via the Pal Park. The Shadow Mail can be dislodged by the move Knock Off. Nevertheless, moves like Covet or Thief are incapable of stealing it.

Title: Tropic Mail

This particular kind of Mail, known in Japanese as トロピカルメール (Tropical Mail), appears only within the games of Generation III. A message can be composed on it, utilizing a maximum of nine words or phrases drawn from the easy chat system's predetermined lexicon. This message is arranged into a layout of four rows each containing two slots, plus a final fifth row with a single slot. Players customize the message at the moment they have a Pokémon hold this item. More expressions for use in messages become available after conversing with the Hipster located in Mauville City. Should the Pokémon carrying this item be traded, its inscribed message will transfer along to other game cartridges from the same generation. The content of the message persists while the item is either affixed to a Pokémon or kept within the Mailbox. Conversely, no message can be present on this item if it is placed in the Bag or the Item Storage System; however, when moving it to these locations, an existing message can optionally be erased. Subsequent to a message's deletion from the item, the player gains the ability to inscribe a fresh one. A Pokémon equipped with this item faces several restrictions: it cannot be deposited into the PC or the Pokémon Day Care, nor can it be traded to Pokémon Colosseum or XD: Gale of Darkness. Furthermore, transfer to Pokémon Box: Ruby & Sapphire or onward to Generation IV games via the Pal Park is also prohibited. While the move Knock Off is capable of dislodging it, Tropic Mail is impervious to theft by moves such as Covet or Thief. Within the narrative of The Beginning of the End with Kyogre & Groudon VI, Ruby utilized a Tropic Mail to convey to Wallace, his instructor, his choice to confront Team Magma. This Tropic Mail was also included among the 6 distinct varieties of Mail showcased in a letter set launched exclusively by the Pokémon Center in November of 2021.

Title: Dream Mail

Exclusively featured in the Generation III games, the item known as Dream Mail (Japanese: ドリームメール Dream Mail) functions as a distinct variety of Mail. Its contents comprise a message of up to nine words and phrases, drawn from the easy chat system's pre-established selection. This message is arranged across four rows, each with a pair of slots, and concludes with a solitary slot in a fifth row. The player inscribes the message at the point of instructing a Pokémon to grasp this item. Access to further phrases can be gained from the Hipster located within Mauville City. Transmission of the message to other titles of the identical generation is accomplished by trading the Pokémon equipped with this item. Its content persists while the item is either carried by a Pokémon or lodged in the Mailbox. Nevertheless, should the item be placed in the Bag or the Item Storage System, it will not carry a message; however, an existing message can be voluntarily removed upon its deposit into these areas. Subsequent to a message's deletion from the item, the player acquires the capability to repurpose it with fresh text. Numerous constraints apply to a Pokémon holding this item: it is barred from being placed in the PC or the Pokémon Day Care. Moreover, trading it to Pokémon Colosseum or XD: Gale of Darkness is prohibited, as is its transfer to Pokémon Box: Ruby & Sapphire or into Generation IV titles via Pal Park. The attack Knock Off possesses the power to detach this item. Conversely, it proves resistant to being pilfered by either Covet or Thief.

Title: Fab Mail

The Fab Mail, known in Japanese as ミラクルメール (Miracle Mail), is a particular variety of Mail found exclusively within the Generation III games. It is designed to hold a message composed of up to nine distinct words or phrases, which must be selected from the easy chat system's predefined list. This message is structurally arranged into four rows, each containing two slots, followed by a final fifth row with one slot. Players compose this message at the moment they assign the item for a Pokémon to hold. The phrase selection can be expanded by players who seek out the Hipster located in Mauville City.

Transmission of the message to other games from the same generation is achieved by trading the Pokémon currently holding this item. The embedded message remains intact as long as the Fab Mail is being held by a Pokémon or is stored within the Mailbox. However, when this item is placed into the Bag or the Item Storage System, it cannot contain any message. In these storage locations, there is an option provided to erase any existing message. Once a message has been cleared from the item, the player gains the ability to repurpose it with new content.

A Pokémon carrying this item faces several limitations: it cannot be deposited into the PC, placed in the Pokémon Day Care, or traded to either Pokémon Colosseum or XD: Gale of Darkness. Additionally, it cannot be transferred to Pokémon Box: Ruby & Sapphire or moved into Generation IV games using the Pal Park. In battle, Knock Off is capable of removing this item. Nevertheless, it is impervious to theft by moves such as Covet or Thief.

Title: Retro Mail

Exclusively appearing in Generation III games, the Retro Mail (Japanese: レトロメール Retro Mail) functions as a specific category of Mail. Its purpose is to store a message, comprising up to nine distinct words or phrases, all drawn from the easy chat system's predetermined lexicon. This message is organized into four rows, each containing two slots, capped by a fifth row with a single slot. Players compose this message at the point of assigning the item to a Pokémon for holding. Further phrases can be made accessible by interacting with the Hipster character found in Mauville City. Transmission of the embedded message to other games within the identical generation is achievable through the act of trading the Pokémon that possesses this item. The message's content persists and remains accessible while the item is either clutched by a Pokémon or deposited within the Mailbox. However, any message stored on this item becomes inaccessible if it's placed in the Bag or the Item Storage System. Nonetheless, players are presented with the choice to erase any currently inscribed message when transferring it to these storage locations. Upon the removal of its message, the item can subsequently be repurposed by the player to accommodate a fresh inscription. A Pokémon currently holding this particular item faces several restrictions: it cannot be deposited into the PC system, left at the Pokémon Day Care, traded to titles such as Pokémon Colosseum or XD: Gale of Darkness, nor can it be transferred to Pokémon Box: Ruby & Sapphire or subsequently moved into Generation IV games via the Pal Park function. The item's attachment can be severed through the use of the move Knock Off. Conversely, it resists attempts at theft by the moves Covet or Thief.

Title: Mach Bike

Designated as a Key Item and first appearing in Generation III, the Mach Bike (known in Japanese as マッハじてんしゃ Mach Bike) is one of two specialized bicycles available for players to ride in Pokémon Ruby, Sapphire, Emerald, Omega Ruby, and Alpha Sapphire, serving as the counterpart to the Acro Bike. This item enables players to switch between riding the Mach Bike and traveling on foot. Commencing a ride on the Mach Bike is not possible while indoors or actively surfing. Should a player transition indoors or begin surfing, they will automatically dismount the Mach Bike. Furthermore, the Running Shoes become unusable as long as the player is mounted on the Mach Bike. Disembarking from the bike is also prohibited on any Cycling Road. While riding, the overworld music is replaced by the distinct Cycling theme, which reverts to the standard overworld music once the player resumes walking.

Across Pokémon Ruby, Sapphire, Emerald, Omega Ruby, and Alpha Sapphire, the Mach Bike allows for considerably faster movement than both standard Bicycles and the Acro Bike, achieving speeds of 15 steps per second in Generation III and 16 steps per second in Generation VI. If the player is already riding, utilizing this item will instead cause them to dismount and walk. Its high speed facilitates traversal across cracked tiles without breaking through—such tiles are found in Generation III within Granite Cave, Sky Pillar, and Mirage Tower—and enables riding directly up muddy slopes, provided sufficient momentum is maintained. Any collision will result in a reset of the bike's speed. Navigating the Seaside Cycling Road necessitates the use of either the Mach Bike or the Acro Bike. On this specific road, the player's elapsed time and the count of their collisions while journeying from the northern entrance to the southern exit are precisely measured and recorded, forming part of the data exchanged with other players through record mixing.

In Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen, the Mach Bike (alongside the Acro Bike) exists within the game's data, though it cannot be obtained through legitimate gameplay. When used in these titles, the item causes the player to ride the regular Bicycle. When attempting to access the Route 17 Cycling Road, the game specifically verifies an event flag indicating the player has received the Bicycle from the Miracle Cycle shop in Cerulean City. Consequently, even if a player possesses the Mach Bike but has not obtained the standard Bicycle from Miracle Cycle, entry to the Cycling Road will still be denied.

A notable instance of its use occurred in the "Dreadful Dealing with Dusclops" event, where Wally utilized a Mach Bike. This bike, left for him by Norman at the base of the Sky Pillar, allowed Wally to swiftly cross cracked floors to ascend the tower. Upon reaching the 49th floor, Wally encountered a wild Dusclops, which manifested a black hole that absorbed the Mach Bike before the Dusclops was ultimately vanquished by Wally's Grovyle.

Further appearances include advertisements for both the Mach and Acro Bikes on the shelves of Rad Rickshaw's Cycle Shop in Generation IV. In Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, the various bikes are distinguished by notably different bell sounds. Additionally, in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, the Bike Maniac in Cerulean City proudly displays several Mach Bikes within his residence.

Title: Acro Bike

Known in Japanese as ダートじてんしゃ (Dirt Bike), the Acro Bike is a Key Item that made its debut in Generation III. This particular bicycle is one of the available mounts for players in Pokémon Ruby, Sapphire, Emerald, Omega Ruby, and Alpha Sapphire, functioning as the direct counterpart to the Mach Bike. Its primary function involves allowing players to alternate between riding it and proceeding on foot. Mounting the Acro Bike is not possible while indoors or surfing; similarly, players are automatically dismounted if they enter a building or begin surfing. While riding the Acro Bike, the Running Shoes become unusable. Furthermore, dismounting is prohibited on any Cycling Road. Traveling on the Acro Bike replaces the ambient overworld music with the distinctive Cycling theme, which reverts to the standard overworld music upon the player dismounting.

In Pokémon Ruby, Sapphire, Emerald, Omega Ruby, and Alpha Sapphire, the Acro Bike maintains the same travel pace as other bicycles—specifically 9.95 steps per second in Generation III and 10.67 steps per second in Generation VI. However, it distinguishes itself by enabling players to execute various tricks, such as performing wheelies and bunny hops, which are crucial for accessing specific locations. If a player is already riding the Acro Bike, using the item again will cause them to dismount and walk. This bike also facilitates traversal over the white rails present on Route 119 and within the Hoenn Safari Zone. Repeatedly bunny hopping on an Acro Bike in tall grass can trigger encounters with wild Pokémon, though these hops do not contribute to the step count for the Safari Game or Repel effects. Both the Acro Bike and Mach Bike are mandatory for riding on the Seaside Cycling Road. Unique to the Acro Bike, its use on the Seaside Cycling Road will not result in a recorded time, due to its comparative "easy to turn" nature compared to the Mach Bike. The specific inputs for its tricks are: holding the B Button for under two seconds for a standing wheelie; holding B then pressing the +Control Pad for a moving wheelie or bunny hop; simultaneously pressing B and the +Control Pad in a direction perpendicular to current travel for a sideways hop; and pressing B and the +Control Pad in the opposite direction of travel simultaneously for a hop that reverses direction.

In Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen, while the Acro Bike (alongside the Mach Bike) is present in the game's data, it cannot be legitimately acquired. If utilized, this item causes the player to ride the standard Bicycle. The game performs a specific check when players attempt to enter the Route 17 Cycling Road, requiring an event flag that signifies receipt of the Bicycle from Cerulean City's Miracle Cycle shop. Consequently, even if a player possesses the Acro Bike but has not obtained the regular Bicycle from Miracle Cycle, entry to the Cycling Road will remain blocked. The Bicycle also appeared as an Item card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game, released during the English XY Series (known as the Japanese XY Era). It debuted in the Primal Clash expansion and was later featured in the Japanese The Best of XY collection. When played, the Arco Bike allows the player to inspect the top two cards of their deck, select one to add to their hand, and discard the other.

Miscellaneous details include advertisements for both Mach and Acro Bikes found on shelves in Rad Rickshaw's Cycle Shop during Generation IV. In Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, the bells on the various bicycles produce noticeably distinct sounds. Furthermore, in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, the Bike Maniac residing in Cerulean City prominently displays several Acro Bikes within his home.

Title: Wailmer Pail

The Wailmer Pail, known in Japanese as ホエルコじょうろ (Hoeruko Watering Can), debuted as a Key Item in Generation III. This watering can is distinctly shaped like a Wailmer. Its primary function is to enable players to hydrate planted Berries. When watering is performed accurately, the resulting Berry trees will yield a greater quantity of Berries for harvesting and storage.

Exclusively in Pokémon Emerald, the Wailmer Pail serves an additional purpose: watering a deceptive tree in the Battle Frontier. Doing so unveils a wild Sudowoodo, which then engages the player in battle. Within the Trainer Lodge, once a player achieves a friendship level of 100 with May, a replica Wailmer Pail becomes available for acquisition from the Lodge Exchange, intended for display.

Beyond its in-game utility, the Wailmer Pail has made appearances in various media. Rita used it to water her Berries at the Pretty Petal flower shop, as depicted in The Lotad Lowdown. Courtney was also observed utilizing a Wailmer Pail in It All Ends Now VIII. This item has been featured across numerous officially licensed figures and merchandise. Uniquely, it is the sole watering can whose aesthetic is derived from a Pokémon that was introduced in the same generation as the watering can itself. Additionally, a Wailmer Pail can be observed inside the player's residence in Pokémon Sun and Moon.

Title: Soot Sack

Originating in Generation III, the Soot Sack (はいぶくろ, or Ash Sack, in Japanese) serves as a Key Item. Its primary function is to enable players to gather volcanic ash from locations such as Route 113 and Jagged Pass. Specifically in Generation III, this item's storage capacity maxes out at 9,999 steps' worth of ash. A notable instance of its use occurred in "Tanks, but No Tanks, Anorith and Lileep," where Ruby, seeking to be recognized as Wallace's disciple, took multiple Soot Sacks from Wallace's automobile. He subsequently went about collecting volcanic ash. Following this, Wallace utilized the collected material to exchange for a flute.

Title: Letter

This entry focuses on the particular missive intended for Steven. For information regarding other correspondences within the games, consult Letter (disambiguation). Known in Japanese as ダイゴへのてがみ (Letter to Daigo), the Letter is a distinct Key Item that debuted in Generation III. This document originates from Mr. Stone and is addressed to his son, Steven. It is presented to Steven within the confines of Granite Cave, a necessary step to advance the core narrative. Upon the successful delivery of this Letter, Steven rewards the player character with either TM47 or TM51, which teaches the move Steel Wing, as recompense for their assistance. Furthermore, in the Pokémon Ruby, Sapphire, and Emerald versions, reporting back to Mr. Stone after the delivery nets the player an Exp. Share. In the manga chapter "Mowing Down Ludicolo," Sapphire received the Letter directly from Mr. Stone, who had entrusted her with its delivery to Steven. Later, in "A Royal Rumble with Regirock, Regice and Registeel I," Sapphire encountered Steven and handed him the Letter, thereby concluding her assigned task. The Letter itself contained a vital piece of paper featuring Braille script, which completed the previously incomplete slab detailing the legendary giants. By deciphering this text, Steven was able to deduce that Wailord and Relicanth were the crucial elements required to awaken Regirock, Regice, and Registeel.

Title: Eon Ticket

For information on the musical piece, refer to Eon Ticket (song). Known in Japanese as むげんのチケット (Infinite Ticket), the Eon Ticket functions as an event-specific Key Item across Pokémon Ruby, Sapphire, Emerald, Omega Ruby, and Alpha Sapphire. This item is unobtainable through regular gameplay; rather, its acquisition necessitates a download at a special promotional event or via an Eon Ticket e-Card.

Within Pokémon Ruby, Sapphire, and Emerald, once a player has obtained the Eon Ticket from Norman and subsequently been inducted into the Hall of Fame, their initial attempt to embark on the S.S. Tidal from Lilycove City Harbor will see a Sailor, rather than the regular attendant, offering passage to Southern Island. This island is where the Eon Pokémon not found roaming Hoenn resides: Latias is present in Pokémon Ruby, Latios in Pokémon Sapphire, and in Pokémon Emerald, the specific Pokémon depends on the player's decision. The Legendary Pokémon engages the player in a fixed, non-fleeing battle, distinct from a roaming encounter. Post-battle, returning to Southern Island is impossible in Pokémon Ruby and Sapphire, irrespective of the battle's result. However, in Pokémon Emerald, access to the island is unrestricted following the battle, regardless of its conclusion.

In Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, upon receiving the Eon Ticket from a deliveryperson at a Pokémon Center, players are directed to speak with Norman. Should a player converse with Norman in the Petalburg Gym after acquiring both the Mega Bracelet and the Eon Ticket, he will accompany them to Slateport Harbor. There, a Sailor substitutes the standard attendant, transporting the player to Southern Island. Deep within the island's forest awaits Latias/Latios, initiating a battle upon interaction. Unlike the initial encounter with an Eon Pokémon, this specific one does not possess its Mega Stone. Once captured, the player will promptly discover a Soul Dew on the ground, which is then placed into their Bag. If the battle concludes without the Pokémon being caught, it will simply continue to gaze at the player.

Global distribution methods for the Eon Ticket varied considerably. For instance, in North America, players could transfer the Eon Ticket into their games via Mystery Events, utilizing an e-Reader in conjunction with an Eon Ticket e-Card. European players had the option to acquire the Eon Ticket at various promotional gatherings, or alternatively, download it through Mystery Events facilitated by special Nintendo cartridges via a Game Link Cable. The Eon Ticket grants passage to Southern Island, enabling the capture of either Latios or Latias – specifically, the one not encountered roaming across Hoenn. This wild Pokémon is guaranteed to be holding a Soul Dew upon capture, representing the sole legitimate method of obtaining this item in Generation III. After departing Southern Island, re-entry becomes impossible in Ruby and Sapphire, even if the ticket is retained; however, in Emerald, the island remains continuously accessible.

Players who obtained their Eon Ticket through an e-Card or a promotional event possess the capability to disseminate up to 30 Eon Tickets to other players using record mixing. Crucially, tickets acquired through record mixing cannot be re-shared. For international versions of Emerald, record mixing served as the exclusive acquisition method for the Eon Ticket, owing to the absence of the Mystery Event function in those editions. The count of Eon Tickets a player can distribute diminishes by one for each game connected during the distribution process, regardless of whether the recipient already possesses the ticket. A tangible Eon Ticket was provided with Nintendo Power vol. 173 (November 2003). The Eon Ticket e-Card, a promotional Pokémon e Card, was accessible at E3 2003, within Nintendo Power vol. 173 (November 2003), and for a limited period at Toys "R" Us outlets. This card facilitates the transfer of the Eon Ticket via Mystery Events into the Key Items pocket of a US Version of Pokémon Ruby or Pokémon Sapphire. In Mexico, the Eon Ticket was distributed during the Electronic Game Show 2003, a video game convention hosted at the World Trade Center of Mexico City from October 23 to 26. This distribution effort was a collaborative venture between Club Nintendo Mexico and Nintendo of America. To obtain the item, attendees simply visited the Nintendo booth, where staff provided them with the Eon Ticket e-Card. Spain saw the Eon Ticket distributed across various locations in December 2003. To receive the item, players needed to attend a designated location between 10:00 AM and 3:00 PM, bringing their Game Boy Advance (any model) and their Pokémon Ruby/Sapphire cartridge. A Nintendo employee would then transfer the item using an e-Reader and an Eon Ticket e-Card.

The reintroduction of the Eon Ticket was announced for the remakes, Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire. A serial code for downloading the ticket was provided in the December 2014 issue of CoroCoro magazine. In the United States, Play! Pokémon League Leaders were supplied with codes to disseminate among participants at nearly one thousand Pokémon League venues. New York City's Nintendo World Store also offered the Eon Ticket until January 14, 2015. Furthermore, in the United Kingdom, 15 members of the public were chosen to receive the Eon Ticket, tasked with relaying it to specific GAME stores nationwide via StreetPass, which would then distribute it to visiting customers. This initiative was officially promoted as "The Great Eon Ticket Relay" event. Players could acquire the Eon Ticket through StreetPass from another player who had originally obtained it via StreetPass or a serial code. Until March 28, 2018, when the Nintendo Zone service largely ceased, it was also feasible to receive the item from other players online via StreetPass Relay when visiting Nintendo Zones. An Eon Ticket acquired through StreetPass will not be recorded in the Mystery Gift Card Album, consequently leading to its delivery as the final gift by the delivery girl. A scaled-down version of the Eon Ticket was part of the Hoenn goods set from the Pokémon World Market merchandise series, produced by Re-Ment and exclusively available at the Pokémon Center. The Eon Ticket was additionally released as a decorative "ticket" bundled with a ticket case, forming part of the Psycho Cyber goods collection, also exclusive to the Pokémon Center. In Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, upon presentation of the Eon Ticket, Norman comments on not having seen such a ticket for 11 years. This dialogue serves as an Easter egg, marking the duration between the item's initial distribution in January 2003 and the launch of Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire in November 2014.

Title: Scanner

This Key Item, known as たんちき (Detector) in Japanese, was initially introduced in the third Pokémon generation. Trainers can present the Scanner to Captain Stern within Slateport City. In return, he offers a choice of evolution items for Clamperl: either a DeepSeaScale or a DeepSeaTooth. Alternatively, a trainer might receive a Clear Bell or a Tidal Bell, both crucial for encountering legendary Pokémon Ho-Oh or Lugia at a location on Sea Mauville.

Its debut in the manga was in *Adding It Up with Plusle & Minun II*. Here, Courtney and Tabitha successfully absconded with the device from the Abandoned Ship, even with attempts by Ruby and Sapphire to prevent them. Subsequently, Blaise undertook modifications to the Scanner, repairing its damaged components. This enhancement allowed it to pinpoint the Red and Blue Orbs on Mt. Pyre. The Scanner was seen again in *Beedrill Prepares*, remaining in Blaise's possession. He deployed it to follow the Orbs as they made their way back to Hoenn, working alongside Archie and Maxie.

Title: Meteorite

The Meteorite, known in Japanese as いんせき (Meteorite), debuted as a Key Item in the third generation of games. Across the core series, this item serves various purposes. In Pokémon Ruby, Sapphire, Emerald, FireRed, and LeafGreen, it lacks direct utility for the player, instead functioning as a delivery item for an NPC, yielding a reward upon exchange. For instance, in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, during the Delta Episode, the Meteorite is consumed by Rayquaza, granting it the capacity for Mega Evolution. Furthermore, Pokémon Scarlet and Violet utilize it to facilitate Deoxys's transformation among its quartet of forms.

Specifically, within Pokémon Ruby, Sapphire, and Emerald, players may hand the Meteorite back to Professor Cozmo at his residence in Fallarbor Town, receiving TM27 (Return) as compensation. Similarly, in Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen, after rescuing Lostelle from Berry Forest, players can present the Meteorite to her father at the Joyful Game Corner on Two Island, thereby acquiring a Moon Stone. In Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, the Meteorite's physical appearance and descriptive text evolve alongside the player's progression in the main narrative. Its initial transformation, from default to second form, occurs after players secure the Devon Scope on Route 120. The shift from its second to its third form happens subsequent to defeating Brendan or May in Lilycove City. Its ultimate metamorphosis, from third to final form, is triggered upon the player's victory over Zinnia in Granite Cave. During the Delta Episode's culmination, the player offers it to Rayquaza, providing the vital energy required for Mega Evolution. Within Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, a crucial condition dictates that no Rayquaza can undergo Mega Evolution until the specific story-relevant Rayquaza has ingested the Meteorite (a constraint absent in Generation VII).

Regarding Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, activating the Meteorite from the Bag on Deoxys permits players to cycle through Deoxys's forms in a predetermined sequence: from Normal Forme to Attack Forme, then to Defense Forme, next to Speed Forme, and finally back to Normal Forme. Beginning with Generation IV, specifically in Pokémon Diamond and Pearl, Meteorites have also manifested as interactive environmental objects in select locales. Engaging with these objects, provided Deoxys is part of the player's party, facilitates its form alteration. Some such meteorites exclusively enable Deoxys to adopt a singular specific form, while others allow a full four-form rotation, mirroring the functionality of the Key Item itself.

In Pokémon Shuffle, the Meteorite grants the player the ability to Mega Evolve both Rayquaza and Shiny Rayquaza. Acquisition of this item in Shuffle is possible by defeating Mega Rayquaza in Stage 300, which serves as the concluding stage of the Roseus Center. It was also offered as a reward during the Shiny Mega Rayquaza competitive stages throughout the game's active service period, enabling the Mega Evolutions for these Pokémon. Within Pokémon GO, the Meteorite functions as an item to instruct Rayquaza in the Charged Attack Dragon Ascent, a prerequisite for its Mega Evolution.

The Meteorite made an appearance in the episode "Fight for the Meteorite!". Here, Professor Cozmo, utilizing a scanner, successfully located it on Mt. Chimney. Immediately after its discovery, Team Magma appeared, intent on its theft, but the subsequent arrival of Team Aqua inadvertently provided Professor Cozmo and Ash with an opportunity to escape with the Meteorite. While concealed from Team Magma, Professor Cozmo elucidated to Ash that the Meteorite originated as a fragment of a vast asteroid that had descended from outer space. Subsequently, a Team Magma Golbat purloined the Meteorite, depositing it into a device designed to provoke Mt. Chimney's eruption. With insufficient time to extract the Meteorite and avert the catastrophe, Professor Cozmo made the decision to hurl the entire apparatus into the volcano, thereby annihilating both the machine and the Meteorite.

In episode HZ122, Friede entrusted Liko and Roy with a briefcase holding an object intended for the Black Rayquaza, which he had recovered from space. By HZ129, the contents of the briefcase were revealed to be a Meteorite; this was then given by the younger Rising Volt Tacklers to Rayquaza at the Dragon Shrine, where it had been recuperating since the Laqua conflict, thus rejuvenating it to full strength.

May was depicted as the owner of the Meteorite in "The Wish". After Zinnia's unsuccessful attempt to Mega Evolve Rayquaza, May permitted the legendary creature to consume the Meteorite, thereby reinstating its capacity for Mega Evolution. In "Two Groups Attacking! Magma VS. Aqua," the Meteorite appeared, fulfilling a function identical to its portrayal in Pokémon the Series: Ruby and Sapphire. Within this manga adaptation, the Meteorite is designated as the Grand Meteor (Japanese: グランメテオ), described as an extraterrestrial stone capable of neutralizing natural energy. Professor Cozmo initially unearthed it at Meteor Falls. During "Bubble Bubble Toil and Azumarill II," Team Aqua deceived Professor Cozmo, gaining access to the Grand Meteor to halt Mt. Chimney's volcanic eruptions, a move calculated to elevate Hoenn's sea levels and advance their objectives. Despite Sapphire's valiant efforts to intervene, she proved powerless to prevent the Grand Meteor-powered machinery from rendering Mt. Chimney dormant. Following Team Aqua's escape, Sapphire pursued them, yet only managed to recover a fractured piece of the Grand Meteor. Later, in "The Beginning of the End with Kyogre & Groudon XIV," Ruby and Sapphire directed attacks from Mumu and Chic at the Grand Meteor fragment, generating an energy surge that successfully dislodged the Red and Blue Orbs from Maxie's and Archie's bodies. "It's Starting to Make Sense Now" unveiled, via Sird, that the Grand Meteor served as the genesis point for the two Deoxys under Team Rocket's command. These Pokémon came into existence when a laser beam from a Mossdeep Space Center shuttle impacted the Grand Meteor, thereby mutating a space virus into two distinct Pokémon. In PS616, Professor Cozmo made the startling discovery that the meteoroid endangering the world was, in fact, the Grand Meteor, which had mysteriously re-entered space and significantly augmented its mass. To avoid any ambiguity from using the same designation, Nonkichi proposed christening the meteoroid "Grand Meteor Delta."

Title: Devon Scope

Originating as a Key Item in Generation III, the Devon Scope (Japanese: デボンスコープ Devon Scope) is a product of Devon Corporation. Its primary function allows players to detect otherwise invisible Pokémon. This capability enables encounters with hidden Kecleon on Routes 119 and 120, and additionally provides a path beyond Route 120's northern bridge, granting access to the Fortree Gym. While its core functionalities remain consistent with the original titles, the item reveals two additional Kecleon subsequent to the completion of two significant narrative arcs. Once Groudon or Kyogre have been addressed, a player may uncover one such Kecleon within the ladies' hot springs of Lavaridge Town. Following the conclusion of the Delta Episode, another Kecleon can be located concealed behind the astronaut face board within the Mossdeep Space Center. Notably, this latter Kecleon is characterized by its Hidden Ability, Protean. In Pokémon Pinball: Ruby & Sapphire, the Devon Scope makes an appearance within the Kecleon bonus stage. Players can dislodge it from a tree, temporarily rendering the normally invisible Kecleon visible for a short time. The Devon Scope first surfaced in the installment 'A Royal Rumble with Regirock, Regice and Registeel I,' where Phoebe utilized it to examine a damaged stone tablet that bore information regarding Regirock, Regice, and Registeel. Subsequently, in 'Salamence Evolves,' Mr. Stone was depicted using a Devon Scope to conduct remote surveillance of Sapphire and Emerald's Mega Evolution training sessions in Dewford Town, all while situated on the S.S. Tidal. A physical toy recreation of the Devon Scope was made available as part of Bandai's Pokémon Trainer's Kit Advance 3 toy line, launching in February 2004.

Title: Bike Voucher

The Bike Voucher (Japanese: ひきかえけん Voucher) is a Key Item first introduced in Generation I. This crucial item enables players to acquire a Bicycle for free from the bike shop situated in Cerulean City. Typically, a Bicycle commands a hefty price of $1,000,000, an amount that surpasses the maximum currency a player can hold in these specific games.

It made a brief appearance in File 2: Cubone, where Red was seen handing it over for a Bicycle, and he mentioned receiving it from the Pokémon Fan Club Chairman. Furthermore, in Wake Up—You're Snorlax, it was disclosed that Red had obtained one from the Pokémon Fan Club Chairman and subsequently exchanged it for a bike off-panel. In Pokémon Power (issue 2, page 3), information indicates that numerous Bike Vouchers were distributed, each exclusively redeemable for a '98 model bike. This was attributed to the Bike Shop making space for new inventory, a detail correlating with the magazine issue's September 1998 cover date.

Title: Gold Teeth

Designated as a Key Item, the Gold Teeth (Japanese: きんのいれば Gold Dentures) made their debut in Generation I. Players have the option to return this artifact to its rightful owner, the Safari Zone Warden, in exchange for HM04 (Strength). Alternatively, upon their return, the Warden will impart the Secret Technique Strong Push to the player's partner Pokémon.

Beyond the core series, the Gold Teeth are featured in Pokémon Pokopia as a Lost Relic available for retrieval. In the episode Start the Countdown, Starmie, Baoba was momentarily seen spitting them out in a fit of surprise when a Team Rocket airship appeared in the sky above him.

The Gold Teeth also play a role in PZ07. Satoshi first learned of them from the Safari Zone Warden's assistants. Later, during his exploration of the Safari Zone, Satoshi managed to fish up a Dratini. A Fisherman then offered to trade Satoshi for the Dratini, proposing his collection of Water Pokémon. Though initially hesitant, Satoshi accepted the deal once he noticed the Gold Teeth tucked away inside one of the Fisherman's Poké Balls. On his journey back to the Warden, Satoshi encountered Koga, who confessed to having purloined the dentures as a form of protest against the Warden's decision to establish the Safari Zone where a ninja village once stood. Eventually, Satoshi successfully returned the dentures to the Warden, thereby restoring his ability to speak clearly.

The Hoopa V card from the Crown Zenith expansion showcases the Gold Teeth within its artwork. A related Key Item, the Steel Teeth, exists in Pokémon Colosseum and can be given back to an old man. However, unlike the Gold Teeth, returning the Steel Teeth is entirely optional.

Title: Lift Key

The Lift Key (Japanese: エレベータのカギ Elevator Key), first appearing in Generation I, serves as a vital Key Item. It enables trainers to operate the elevator within Team Rocket's Hideout, unlocking access to the bottom floor segment containing Giovanni's office. A minor glitch in Generation I involved the Lift Key: if a player positioned themselves to the left of the Team Rocket Grunt possessing it and initiated dialogue, the key would appear directly beneath the player's character upon the Grunt dropping it. Despite this unusual placement, the player retains mobility and can still acquire the key by moving downwards. This oversight was corrected in Pokémon Yellow, where the Grunt automatically yields the Lift Key immediately upon defeat.

Title: Silph Scope

The Silph Scope (Japanese: シルフスコープ Sylph Scope), a Key Item introduced in Generation I, was developed by Silph Co. Its purpose is to enable the identification of ghosts. Possessing the Silph Scope in their Bag permits the player to engage in standard Pokémon battles against the wild Pokémon encountered within Lavender Town's Pokémon Tower. Without it, these Pokémon manifest as unidentified apparitions. The player must have the Silph Scope to confront the ghost Marowak, which obstructs the final staircase, allowing access to Pokémon Tower 7F and, ultimately, the acquisition of the Poké Flute from Mr. Fuji. In Pokémon Red, Blue, Yellow, FireRed, and LeafGreen, any Poké Balls (including Master Balls) thrown at unidentified ghosts are wasted; thus, the Silph Scope is necessary to capture any Pokémon in the Pokémon Tower. The ghost Marowak, however, is uncatchable, and Poké Balls thrown at her are still lost even with the Silph Scope.

In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, the Silph Scope also prevents the player from being warped back to the Pokémon Tower's first floor, a common outcome of being touched by an unidentified ghost. While still required to circumvent the ghost Marowak, in these titles, its presence triggers a cutscene rather than a battle with her.

The Silph Scope made an appearance in File 2: Cubone, held by a small contingent of Team Rocket Grunts who had Mr. Fuji captive at the Pokémon Tower. Similar to its function in the games, the Silph Scope possessed the capability to reveal the true identity of both Ghost-type Pokémon and genuine spirits. Blue later stole the Silph Scope from the Grunts and subsequently gave it to Red, allowing him to identify the ghost he was fighting as Marowak, Cubone's deceased mother. Following this, Red returned the Silph Scope to the Rocket Grunts, who presumably retained it as they departed after being defeated in battle by Red.

Green utilizes a modified Silph Scope designed to detect all invisible Pokémon. In this particular canon, Green is also credited as the inventor of the Silph Scope. Green's Silph Scope first appeared in The Jynx Jinx, where she employed it, along with a data disc she had pilfered from Team Rocket, to pinpoint and identify Mew. In Kalling Kadabra, Green used the Silph Scope to inspect the barrier over Saffron City. During Zap! Zap! Zapdos, Green deployed the Silph Scope to see through her Horsea's Smokescreen during her battle against Sabrina at Silph Co. In Now You See Me..., when an invisible Pokémon attacked Green aboard the Seagallop ferry, she used the Scope to unveil it, though she couldn't recognize it. Red and Blue later recovered the Silph Scope and utilized its playback function to observe the entity that had attacked Green.

In Surprised by Sneasel, Silver was also revealed to possess a Silph Scope, which he used to examine the Team Rocket airship as it appeared above him and Yellow. In Right on Time, Rhydon, Green used her Silph Scope to inspect a burning piece of wreckage that had detached from the Team Rocket airship, where she spotted Silver trapped within the flames.

The Silph Scope initially appeared in Get Rid Of The Ghost Pokémon!!. Red's rival, Blue, was shown with one, using it to discern Gengar's movements during its battle with Clefairy. However, Clefairy seized the Silph Scope and used it to its advantage, leading to Gengar's defeat. Unlike its game counterpart, this Silph Scope is shaped like glasses and was not depicted as originating from Silph Co.

The Silph Scope was observed in Celadon City. After Satoshi had been frightened away from the Pokémon Tower by a Gastly, he saw several Team Rocket Grunts exiting the Tower, each employing a Silph Scope to remain unaffected by the ghosts. Satoshi then discreetly followed the Grunts to the Rocket Hideout, where he snatched one of the Scopes found there. Using the device, he was able to bypass the Tower's ghosts and reach the top floor, where he encountered Mr. Fuji, who was being held captive by Team Rocket.

The Silph Scope has been featured on merchandise from The Pokémon Company International's Lavender Town Collection. A sign positioned next to the Pokémon Tower in Lavender Town advertises the Silph Scope, declaring, "Make the Invisible Plain to See!"

In Generation I, if the player uses a Poké Doll to escape from the Marowak ghost, it will vanish as if it had been defeated. This allows the player to progress through the game's story without acquiring the Silph Scope, making the battle at the Rocket Hideout unnecessary. Also in Generation I, if the player views a Pokémon's status screen, the ghost's sprite will change to that of the actual Pokémon, though it still cannot be battled or caught.

Title: Fame Checker

The Fame Checker (Japanese: ボイスチェッカー Voice Checker) is a Key Item present in Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen. Its function is to compile diverse information on notable individuals, like Gym Leaders. This dark red device features a simple design, with a screen approximately the size of the Pokédex display. On its left are four black speaker holes, while the right boasts a Poké Ball-shaped button or wheel, divided into three distinct segments.

Within Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen, players can gather Fame Checker data on various characters. Every character listed possesses six unlockable entries, each tied to a specific in-game quote about them. Encountering such dialogue automatically registers it in the player's Fame Checker. Each entry includes a title (e.g., "Favorite kind of Pokémon", "Friends and Family"), a location (not necessarily the save point), and the quoted character or text. An icon showing the overworld sprite of the quote's source also accompanies it. Before unlocking, entries display as a question mark, lacking a title or unlock hint. Quotes found pre-acquisition instantly show as unlocked entries once the Fame Checker is obtained. Sources for these quotes are diverse: the character, major characters, unnamed NPCs, NPC Trainers, signs, or the Pokémon Journal, among others. Upon collecting all six data pieces for a person, the player can access a direct message from that character.

Generation III characters with Fame Checker information are listed; clicking a sprite leads to their transcript. In Pokémon Emerald, the Fame Checker exists in game data but is unobtainable legitimately and non-functional.

The Fame Checker debuted in 'Return to Pallet Town'. Upon Red and Blue's return to Professor Oak's Laboratory there, they found three Fame Checkers Professor Oak had left for them and Green. Via these devices, the two Pokédex holders heard Professor Oak's voice, requesting they deposit their Pokédexes into the PC Item Storage System, causing surprise and confusion. Later, 'Going Green' revealed Orm had coerced Professor Oak into making these messages, part of Team Rocket's plan for Oak to reverse-engineer their own Pokédex for catching Deoxys. Unknown to Orm, Professor Oak secretly activated Green's Fame Checker during the attack, recording their conversation.

Bandai released a Fame Checker-inspired toy in January 2005, part of the Pokémon Trainer's Kit Advance 5 line. Koga's Fame Checker entries contain an error, misidentifying his daughter Janine as 'Charine'.

Title: Berry Pouch

A Key Item, the Berry Pouch (Japanese: きのみぶくろ Berry Sack), made its debut in Generation III. It serves to store Berries gathered by the player, effectively replacing the Berries pocket that first appeared in Pokémon Ruby and Sapphire. By Generation V, the game's bag pockets were rebranded as "cases" and appeared in-game as a collection of distinct containers. Among these, a separate pouch specifically for Berries is present, possibly referencing the original Berry Pouch from Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen.

Title: Rainbow Pass

First introduced as a Key Item in Generation III, the Rainbow Pass bears the Japanese name レインボーパス (Rainbow Pass). It is recognized as one of the several passes necessary for navigating the Sevii Islands. This item empowers players to journey between Vermilion City's port and the various Sevii Islands aboard the Seagallop ferries. A prerequisite for obtaining the Rainbow Pass is the prior acquisition of the Tri-Pass, which grants initial access to One Island, Two Island, and Three Island. Once the Rainbow Pass is also secured, a player's travel capabilities extend to Four Island, Five Island, Six Island, and Seven Island. Furthermore, possession of the Rainbow Pass is essential for utilizing the event-exclusive AuroraTicket or MysticTicket to reach Birth Island and Navel Rock, respectively (though these special tickets can be acquired before the Rainbow Pass itself). In the manga installment My, My, My Mimic, Bill provided Red and Blue with a Rainbow Pass each, which they subsequently used to board a Seagallop Ferry bound for Four Island. The Going Green story arc disclosed that Green had already obtained both her Tri-Pass and Rainbow Pass before the arc began, enabling her to visit the Sevii Islands and capture Moltres on Mt. Ember.

Title: Tea

Debuting in Generation III, Tea (おちゃ Green Tea in Japanese) functions as a Key Item. Presenting Tea to a Saffron City guard grants passage into the metropolis via any of its entrances. During Generation I, parched guards obstructing Saffron City's access points would accept Fresh Water, Soda Pop, or Lemonade. However, the Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen remakes revised this requirement, mandating Tea as the necessary offering. This revision was likely implemented to prevent an exploit stemming from Pokémon's item-holding ability—a mechanic absent in Generation I. Such an oversight would have allowed a FireRed or LeafGreen player to trade one of the three drinks from another game, gaining Saffron access as early as reaching Cerulean. Curiously, this specific exploit *is* achievable in the Generation I titles by utilizing Pokémon Stadium 2's Color Case to transfer the item from a different game's Bag or PC storage. Despite the absence of the held item mechanic in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, the Tea item was retained for two distinct rationales: the consistent availability of Fresh Water within the Underground Paths, and to avert a softlock scenario where players might deplete their funds, rendering them unable to afford a beverage.

Title: Ruby

First, the Generation III video games are identified as Pokémon Ruby and Sapphire Versions. Subsequently, the Generation VI installments are Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, accompanied by their animated trailer: Pokémon Omega Ruby and Pokémon Alpha Sapphire Animated Trailer. For players utilizing the GameCube, the dedicated Pokémon storage system is Pokémon Box Ruby & Sapphire. Additionally, the Pinball series features Pokémon Pinball: Ruby & Sapphire.

Regarding manga adaptations, the Pokémon Adventures series encompasses the Ruby & Sapphire arc, which draws from the Generation III games, and the Omega Ruby & Alpha Sapphire arc, based on the Generation VI titles. The successor to Pokémon Pocket Monsters in manga form is Pokémon Ruby-Sapphire. Furthermore, the saga concerning twin brothers Alpha and Omega is entitled Pokémon Omega Ruby Crimson Passion and Pokémon Alpha Sapphire Indigo Wisdom.

Turning to characters, Brendan (game) is recognized as the male player character in Ruby, Sapphire, and Emerald. The Pokémon Adventures manga introduces Ruby (Adventures), while the Pokémon Ruby-Sapphire manga features Ruby (Ruby-Sapphire). In the Pokémon Battle Frontier manga, Rald serves as Brendan's distinct counterpart. Moreover, Brendan's twin counterparts within the CPIW manga are identified as Alpha (CPIW) and Omega (CPIW). Sapphire (Ruby-Sapphire) denotes the female Pokémon Ruby-Sapphire manga character, who is known as Ruby in the Japanese version. From the anime, Ruby (EP094) is a resident of the Seven Grapefruit Islands located in the Orange Islands.

Several locations and items bear the 'Ruby' designation: Ruby Volcano, an area discovered within Pokémon Rumble World, and Ruby Lake, a dungeon present in Pokémon Mystery Dungeon (WiiWare). Lastly, the Key Item known as Ruby (item) can be found in Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen.

Title: Sapphire

Regarding the Generation III main series game, refer to Pokémon Ruby and Sapphire Versions.
The Generation VI game is covered under Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire.
The official trailer for these specific games is available at Pokémon Omega Ruby and Pokémon Alpha Sapphire Animated Trailer.
For the Pokémon storage system compatible with the GameCube console, consult Pokémon Box Ruby & Sapphire.
The Pinball-themed game is Pokémon Pinball: Ruby & Sapphire.
The Pokémon Adventures manga installment based on the Generation III games is the Ruby & Sapphire arc.
Conversely, the Pokémon Adventures manga chapter inspired by the Generation VI games is the Omega Ruby & Alpha Sapphire arc.
The direct continuation of Pokémon Pocket Monsters is Pokémon Ruby-Sapphire.
Information on the manga depicting twin brothers Alpha and Omega can be found under Pokémon Omega Ruby Crimson Passion and Pokémon Alpha Sapphire Indigo Wisdom.
The female player character featured in Ruby, Sapphire, and Emerald is known as May (game).
May (anime) represents her counterpart in the animated series.
The Pokémon Adventures manga character is Sapphire (Adventures).
For the Pokémon Ruby-Sapphire manga character, see Sapphire (Ruby-Sapphire).
Ruby (Ruby-Sapphire) refers to the male Pokémon Ruby-Sapphire manga character, identified as Sapphire in the Japanese version.
An area located within Pokémon Rumble World is the Sapphire Sea.
The Key Item present in Pokémon FireRed and LeafGreen is Sapphire (item).
Lastly, the theme deck included in this particular set is Sapphire (TCG).

Title: Old Sea Map

Known in Japanese as ふるびたかいず (Old Sea Chart), the Old Sea Map functions as a special Key Item obtainable only through distribution events in Pokémon Emerald. Its sole purpose is to grant passage to Faraway Island, a secluded locale where trainers have the opportunity to discover and capture a wild Mew. With this artifact in their possession, players can journey to Faraway Island aboard the S.S. Tidal to find the elusive Mythical Pokémon.

Possessing the Old Sea Map enables a unique sequence of events: at Lilycove City Harbor, conversing with the woman near the S.S. Tidal prompts her to summon the ship's captain. Although the captain initially dismisses the possibility of sailing to the map's indicated destination, he is swiftly admonished by Mr. Briney. The kind old man intervenes, criticizing the captain's reluctance and offering his own services to transport the player to Faraway Island, despite its considerable distance from the Hoenn region. Upon completing the voyage, the player disembarks onto Faraway Island, a small landmass characterized by a dense forest. There, deep within the woods, Mew makes an appearance, playfully darting away. Following a brief chase and a game of evasion, the player successfully corners Mew, leading to a Pokémon battle.

Main article: List of other event distributions in Generation III → Old Sea Map Distribution of the Old Sea Map was confined solely to Japan and Taiwan, exclusively for the Japanese release of Pokémon Emerald. In Japan, the item could be obtained during Pokémon Festa, held from July 17 to August 21, 2005, and later at PokéPark, from September 1 to 25, 2005. Taiwan's distribution took place at PokéPark between June 23 and September 24, 2006, requiring individuals to possess a 'Pokémon Download Qualification Certificate' (神奇寶貝下載資格證). This certificate was issued upon the purchase of a ticket for the film Lucario and the Mystery of Mew.

No distributions of the Old Sea Map ever occurred for non-Japanese language versions of the game. For these international releases, Mew itself was directly provided through various alternative events across the Generation III titles. In the manga chapter Bested by Banette, Ultima acquired the Old Sea Map by pilfering it from Team Rocket's hidden collection of valuable artifacts at Trainer Tower. Her intention was to aid her long-time acquaintance, Mr. Briney, in locating Faraway Island. However, upon informing Briney of her successful retrieval, Ultima was astonished to discover that the veteran seafarer had already navigated to the island independently, without the map's assistance.

Both the Old Sea Map and its associated in-game event were conceived by Shigeki Morimoto, the individual credited with Mew's initial design.

Title: Douse Drive

The Douse Drive (Japanese: アクアカセット Aqua Cassette) is a held item that debuted in Generation V. It stands as one of the specialized Drives associated with Genesect, specifically correlating with the Water type. When a Pokémon equips this item and utilizes the move Techno Blast, that attack's type is converted to Water. Furthermore, holding this particular item causes a Genesect to transform into its Douse Drive form. A unique game mechanic dictates that item-manipulating effects, such as Trick and Fling, cannot be used to bestow a Drive upon Genesect or remove one from it. This Drive, along with its counterparts, was featured in the film *Genesect and the Legend Awakened*, where it was observed attached to a member of the Genesect Army. While its purpose in the movie aligned with altering Techno Blast's type, it also functioned as a key identifier, distinguishing its owner from the other Genesect. The Genesect carrying the Douse Drive exhibited a unique personality, generally being timid and benevolent unless compelled to act by its leader. Within the *Innocent Scientist* storyline, Colress was in possession of the Douse Drive and the other Drives.

Title: Shock Drive

Debuting in Generation V, the Shock Drive (known in Japan as イナズマカセット, or Lightning Cassette) functions as a held item. It belongs to a category of items called Drives, all intrinsically linked to the Pokémon Genesect. This particular item is aligned with the Electric type. When a Pokémon holding it employs the move Techno Blast, that attack's type is converted to Electric. Furthermore, a Genesect equipped with this item will transform into its Shock Drive form. Notably, game mechanics that manipulate items, such as Trick or Fling, are unable to either equip Genesect with a Drive or divest it of one.

This item, alongside its counterparts, featured in the film *Genesect and the Legend Awakened*, where it was observed affixed to a member of the Genesect Army. In the movie, its function was not only to modify Techno Blast's type but also served as a crucial identifier for its specific owner among the other Genesect. The character Colress, known as both "Mr. Perfect" and the "Innocent Scientist," engaged in battle against Blake utilizing a Genesect. Provoked by one of Blake's taunts, Colress furiously swapped the Drive affixed to Genesect's back for a Shock Drive, aiming to exploit a type advantage against Blake's Dewott with the ancient Pokémon. Following Blake's successful defeat and capture of the Genesect, the Shock Drive was consequently acquired by him. Subsequently, the Shock Drive was entrusted to the Magician for the purpose of reverse-engineering additional Burn, Chill, and Douse Drives.

Title: Burn Drive

Debuting as a held item in Generation V, the Burn Drive (known in Japanese as ブレイズカセット Blaze Cassette) is one of the Drives, a category of held items intrinsically associated with the Pokémon Genesect. Its elemental affiliation is Fire. When held, it causes Techno Blast to become a Fire-type move and transforms Genesect into its Burn Drive form. Furthermore, item-manipulating effects like Trick and Fling are unable to transfer or remove a Drive from Genesect.

Outside of game mechanics, the Burn Drive, alongside the other Drives, made an appearance in the movie *Genesect and the Legend Awakened*, where it was affixed to a member of the Genesect Army. While still changing Techno Blast's type in the film, it also acted as a vital tool for distinguishing its owner from other Genesect.

Colress, known both as 'Mr. Perfect' and the 'Innocent Scientist', engaged Blake in battle using a Genesect. Upon Blake's defeat and capture of the Genesect, he obtained the Shock Drive. This item was then given to the Magician, whose task was to reverse-engineer new sets of Burn, Chill, and Douse Drives. Blake later deployed the Burn Drive in a skirmish against Team Plasma on the Plasma Frigate. However, as the Drive was still incomplete, Genesect sustained injuries from its own attack and was left exhausted after using Techno Blast on some Team Plasma Grunts.

Afterward, Blake harnessed the Fire-type Techno Blast to free the Swords of Justice from being frozen in ice. Following this, Cobalion forcefully removed the damaged Burn Drive from Genesect, causing it to explode.

Title: Chill Drive

The Chill Drive, known in Japanese as フリーズカセット (Freeze Cassette), is a held item first introduced in Generation V. It is one of the specialized Drives associated exclusively with Genesect, and this particular variant corresponds to the Ice type. When held by a Pokémon using Techno Blast, the move's type transforms to Ice. Furthermore, a Genesect equipped with this item will assume its unique Chill Drive form. Notably, item-manipulating effects such as Trick and Fling are incapable of either attaching a Drive to Genesect or removing one from it. This item's presence consistently changes a Genesect to its Chill Drive form. The Chill Drive, along with its counterparts, featured in the movie *Genesect and the Legend Awakened*, attached to a member of the Genesect Army. In the film, while it served the familiar purpose of altering Techno Blast's type, it also functioned as the primary means to distinguish its specific owner from the other Genesect. In the *Innocent Scientist* manga, Colress was depicted as possessing the Chill Drive and the other Drives.

Title: Sweet Heart

Making its debut in Generation V, the Sweet Heart (Japanese: ハートスイーツ Heart Sweets) is classified as a medicinal item. This confection, shaped like a heart-shaped chocolate, functions to restore a Pokémon's HP. Within the Generation V games, players can sell this item to the gourmet maniac on Route 5 for $100. When employed from the Bag, it provides a healing effect of 20 HP to the chosen Pokémon. In Mistralton City, a woman offers a Heart Scale in return for ten Sweet Hearts. Uniquely, in Pokémon Black 2 and White 2, during Valentine's Day (February 14) and White Day (March 14), this same woman will accept only five Sweet Hearts for the trade. James's Morpeko was observed eating a Sweet Heart in the episode Pride of a Champion!. These items are conceptualized after heart-shaped chocolates, a common gift among lovers, and their name is a pun on the term of endearment "sweetheart".

Title: Greet Mail

The Greet Mail, known in Japanese as はじめてメール (Greeting Mail), is a category of Mail item found exclusively within the Generation V games. It accommodates a message using up to three sentence slots, which players fill with words and phrases from the easy chat system's predefined list. This design ensures readability across all language versions of the games. Players compose this message when assigning the item to a Pokémon to hold. The message transmits to other games of the same generation when the Pokémon holding it is traded. Its message persists as long as the item remains held by a Pokémon or is deposited in the Mailbox. While messages cannot be attached to this item when it's kept in the Bag or Item Storage System, an option exists to delete any current message upon its storage in these locations. After a message is deleted, the player can reuse the item with a new composition. Pokémon carrying the Greet Mail cannot be deposited into the PC or the Pokémon Day Care. Like other held items, it prevents the Pokémon holding it from being transferred to Generation VI games via the Poké Transporter and Pokémon Bank. The item is susceptible to removal by Knock Off. However, it cannot be stolen by moves like Covet, Thief, or by the Pickpocket Ability. Additionally, it causes Bestow and Fling to fail if the holder attempts to use them, and similarly, Switcheroo or Trick will fail whether used by or on the holder.

Title: Favored Mail

Exclusively available in Generation V titles, the Favored Mail (referred to in Japanese as だいすきメール Loveable Mail) serves as a distinct form of Mail. Users can compose a message within it, utilizing up to three sentence slots that are populated with predefined words and phrases from the easy chat system. This design choice ensures readability across different language versions of the games. The specific message options for Favored Mail include: "It's great because it's <word 1>. Don't you agree?" A player inputs the message when assigning the Favored Mail to a Pokémon as a held item. The inscribed message can be conveyed to other games within the same generation by initiating a trade of the Pokémon that is currently holding this item. Its message persists while the item is either held by a Pokémon or kept within the Mailbox. However, the item loses its message when placed in the Bag or the Item Storage System; nevertheless, players have the option to erase any current message when moving it to these locations. After a message is removed from the Favored Mail, the player gains the ability to repurpose it with fresh content. A Pokémon equipped with this item is prevented from being deposited into the PC or entrusted to the Pokémon Day Care. Consistent with restrictions on other held items, a Pokémon bearing this item cannot be moved to Generation VI games via the Poké Transporter or Pokémon Bank. Knock Off is capable of dislodging this item. Conversely, it resists theft from moves like Covet or Thief, and also the Pickpocket Ability. Furthermore, if the holder attempts to use Bestow or Fling, these moves will fail, and Switcheroo or Trick will similarly be ineffective whether used by or on the holder.

Title: Thanks Mail

Known in Japanese as かんしゃメール (Gratitude Mail), the Thanks Mail is a specific form of Mail item found exclusively in the Generation V titles. Players compose its message, which occupies up to three distinct sentence slots, by selecting from a predetermined pool of words and phrases provided by the easy chat system. This design choice ensures the message remains comprehensible across all language versions of the games. The message itself is crafted by the player at the moment this item is assigned to a Pokémon to be held. Transmission of this message to other games within the same generation is accomplished through trading the Pokémon that holds it. Its content persists while the item is either carried by a Pokémon or housed within the Mailbox. When placed into the Bag or the Item Storage System, this item cannot carry a message; however, users are given the option to erase any existing message upon its storage in these locations. After a message has been cleared from the item, players gain the ability to compose and assign a fresh one. A Pokémon equipped with Thanks Mail is prevented from being deposited into the PC or the Pokémon Day Care. Consistent with other held items, a Pokémon carrying Thanks Mail cannot be migrated to Generation VI titles via the Poké Transporter and the Pokémon Bank. The item can be dislodged by the move Knock Off. Nevertheless, it resists theft from moves like Covet and Thief, as well as the Pickpocket Ability. Furthermore, it causes the moves Bestow and Fling to fail when attempted by the Pokémon holding it, and likewise prevents Switcheroo or Trick from succeeding, whether used by or targeting the holder. Visually, the Thanks Mail features Shaymin, and its Japanese designation directly echoes Shaymin's Pokédex category as the "Gratitude Pokémon."

Title: Inquiry Mail

Exclusive to the Generation V games, the Inquiry Mail (Japanese: しつもんメール Enquiry Mail) functions as a particular form of Mail. Its content comprises a message featuring a maximum of three customizable sentence slots, which players populate using predefined words and phrases from the easy chat system's established lexicon. A key design principle was to ensure its readability irrespective of the game's language. The specific phrases available for Inquiry Mail messages include: "It bothers us, doesn't it? I'm talking about <word 1>." Players compose the message at the moment they instruct a Pokémon to hold this item. Transmitting the message to other Generation V games is accomplished by trading the Pokémon equipped with this item. The message persists and can be accessed so long as the item remains attached to a Pokémon or is housed within the Mailbox. While this item cannot contain a message when kept in the Bag or the Item Storage System, users are provided with the choice to remove any present message upon its placement in these locations. Following the deletion of a message from the item, the player gains the capability to repurpose it with fresh content. A Pokémon carrying this particular item is barred from being deposited into either the PC or the Pokémon Day Care. In alignment with other held items, a Pokémon equipped with Inquiry Mail is prevented from being transported to Generation VI titles via the Poké Transporter and the Pokémon Bank services. The move Knock Off is capable of dislodging this item. Nevertheless, it resists theft attempts from moves like Covet and Thief, as well as the Pickpocket Ability. Furthermore, its presence causes Bestow and Fling to fail if the holder attempts to use them, and similarly results in the failure of Switcheroo or Trick whether employed by or against the holder.

Title: Like Mail

The Like Mail, known in Japanese as おすすめメール (Recommendation Mail), functions as a specific category of Mail, found solely within the Generation V titles. This item facilitates messages composed of up to three distinct sentence sections, populated by selecting from a predetermined inventory of words and phrases provided by the easy chat system. This design ensures cross-linguistic readability across all versions of the game. A player crafts the message for this item at the moment it is assigned to a Pokémon to hold. The message inscribed can be conveyed to other games within the same generation via a trade involving the Pokémon carrying it. Its message persists as long as the item is either held by a Pokémon or housed within the Mailbox. Conversely, no message can be associated with this item when it resides in the Bag or the Item Storage System; however, users retain the ability to erase any extant message upon placing the item into these locations. Following the deletion of its message, the item becomes available for reuse by the player, ready to be imprinted with new content. A Pokémon equipped with this item is barred from being deposited into the PC or the Pokémon Day Care. Consistent with other held possessions, a Pokémon bearing this item cannot be moved into Generation VI games via the Poké Transporter and the Pokémon Bank. The item is susceptible to removal by the move Knock Off. Nevertheless, it resists theft attempts from moves like Covet or Thief, as well as the Pickpocket Ability.
