Furthermore, it causes the moves Bestow and Fling to fail if initiated by the holder, and likewise causes Switcheroo or Trick to fail, whether used by or against the Pokémon holding it.

Title: Reply Mail

Known in Japanese as おかえしメール (Return Mail), the Reply Mail is a distinct type of Mail available solely in the Generation V games. It allows for a message, structured into a maximum of three sentence fields, which players can populate using a predefined collection of words and phrases from the easy chat system. This design choice ensured that messages would be comprehensible across games in any language. Players compose the message when assigning this item for a Pokémon to hold. The embedded message can be conveyed to other games of the same generation by trading the Pokémon currently holding the Mail. The message itself endures as long as the item is held by a Pokémon or kept within the Mailbox. However, this item cannot retain a message when stored in the Bag or the Item Storage System; though, an option is provided to delete any existing message upon storing it in these locations. Once a message is removed, the player gains the ability to repurpose the item with a new message. A Pokémon equipped with Reply Mail is prohibited from being placed in the PC or in the Pokémon Day Care. Aligning with restrictions for other held items, a Pokémon carrying this item cannot be transferred into Generation VI games via the Poké Transporter or the Pokémon Bank. The item can be dislodged by the move Knock Off. Conversely, it is immune to theft attempts from Covet, Thief, or the Pickpocket Ability. Furthermore, it causes the moves Bestow and Fling to fail when initiated by its holder, and similarly causes Switcheroo or Trick to fail whether used by or directed at the Pokémon holding it. The Reply Mail was one of 6 Mail varieties featured in a Pokémon Center-exclusive letter set released in November 2021.

Title: Prism Scale

Introduced in Generation V, the Prism Scale (Japanese: きれいなウロコ Beautiful Scale) functions as a held item. It facilitates the evolution of Feebas into Milotic; specifically, Feebas transforms into Milotic when traded while equipped with this item. Upon evolution, the Prism Scale is consumed. Its inclusion in Generation V addressed the absence of mechanisms for raising a Pokémon's Beauty stat within those particular titles. Nevertheless, even in games where the Prism Scale is present, Feebas retains its capacity to evolve by leveling up, provided its Beauty stat is sufficiently elevated. In Chapter 4 of Detective Pikachu, a Prism Scale is featured, fastened to Milo Green's headband. Milo recounts acquiring the scale after a Pokémon rescued him from drowning, a creature subsequently identified as a Milotic. This specific Prism Scale's presence additionally serves as the catalyst for the evolution of a Feebas that Milo has befriended. "The Cuteness Quotient!" also showcased a Prism Scale, affixed to Jinny's Feebas. During an assault by Team Rocket, the Prism Scale became dislodged; however, Jinny promptly reattached it to Feebas, leading to its evolution into Milotic. The Pokémon Company International's Held Item Pins line highlights the Prism Scale alongside Feebas. Within Pokémon Black and White, the Prism Scale experienced an erroneous categorization in the Bag, being classified as a general item rather than its proper designation as a held item. This error was rectified in Black 2 and White 2, only to be reinstated in X and Y, resulting in its placement among valuable items instead of Evolution items throughout Generations VI and VII.

Title: Eviolite

The Eviolite, known in Japanese as しんかのきせき (Evolution Stone), made its debut as a held item in Generation V. This item grants a 50% increase to both the Defense and Special Defense stats of any Pokémon holding it, provided that Pokémon has not yet reached its final evolutionary stage. The Eviolite's effect is contingent on a Pokémon's species and form possessing a non-zero value in its "personal data" – internal information outlining a Pokémon's fundamental characteristics – specifically pertaining to an Evolution method. This applies even if that evolution method is not genuinely attainable. While this generally operates as intended across most species and forms, certain peculiar scenarios arise, including cases where an Evolution method is coded but unaccessible, alongside specific hardcoded exceptions.

Should only a regional variant of a species be capable of evolving, the Eviolite's benefit will be exclusive to that regional form. Meltan does not receive the Eviolite's boost because its evolution is restricted to Pokémon GO, rather than the core series games. Similarly, specific forms like Cosplay Pikachu, Pikachu in a cap, and Eternal Flower Floette are unaffected due to lacking a coded Evolution method, despite their base species being able to evolve. Furthermore, Pikachu, Meowth, and Eevee carrying the Gigantamax Factor are not impacted. This is because they are hardcoded to neither evolve (unless the Gigantamax Factor is removed with Max Soup) nor receive the Eviolite's statistical increase when possessing this factor.

Conversely, male Combee and Salandit *do* benefit from the Eviolite, even though only their female counterparts can evolve. This is because they share the identical species and form with the females, thus possessing the requisite "non-zero Evolution value" in their personal data. The Eviolite also confers its boost to specific Pokémon that *can* evolve, even when their evolution requirements cannot be met. For instance, in Pokémon Sword and Shield, Galarian Slowpoke was affected prior to the availability of the Galarica Cuff or Galarica Wreath in the Expansion Pass. In Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Ursaring and Stantler receive the effect despite their evolution methods being unobtainable within those titles. Similarly, Hisuian Qwilfish was affected for the same reason before The Indigo Disk update introduced its evolution into Overqwil. Before Hydrapple's debut in The Indigo Disk, Dipplin also benefited from the item.

When held by a Pokémon that has undergone transformation, the statistical enhancement is determined by its original species, not the species it has transformed into. Monta's Boldore was observed holding an Eviolite in Challenge the Battle Subway!.

Within the Pokémon Trading Card Game, Eviolite debuted as a Pokémon Tool card during the English Black & White Series (corresponding to the Japanese BW Era) as part of the Noble Victories expansion. When this Trainer card is affixed to a Basic Pokémon, it decreases any damage inflicted upon that Pokémon by attacks by 20, a reduction applied after accounting for Weakness and Resistance.

The name Eviolite likely stems from "Evolution" combined with the "-ite" suffix common in mineral nomenclature. Its Japanese name, しんかのきせき (Shinka no Kiseki), cleverly functions as a pun, signifying both "Evolution Stone" and "Evolution Miracle."

Title: Float Stone

Known in Japanese as かるいし (Pumice Stone), the Float Stone is a held item that made its debut in Generation V. Within the Generation V games, trainers have the option to sell the Float Stone to an item maniac located inside the Icirrus City Pokémon Center for $1000. Concerning weight modifications, Autotomize is always processed before other weight-altering effects, the Float Stone included. It's important to note that a Pokémon's weight cannot be reduced below 0.1 kg. This item has no impact on the Heavy Ball, as that particular Poké Ball calculates its success based on the Pokémon's species' inherent weight, not its temporarily adjusted current weight.

In Pokémon UNITE, the Float Stone functions as a held item that grants its wielder increased movement speed when out of combat, in addition to boosting their Attack and Speed attributes. Five seconds after exiting combat, the user's movement speed sees an enhancement of 10% at Grade 1, 15% at Grade 10, and 20% at Grade 20. The Attack and Speed stats are augmented by 1.6 and 0 respectively at Grade 1, progressing to an increase of 24 for Attack and 150 for Speed at Grade 30.

The Float Stone was introduced as a Pokémon Tool card within the Pokémon Trading Card Game. Its initial appearance was in the Plasma Freeze expansion during the English Black & White Series (corresponding to the Japanese BW Era), illustrated by 5ban Graphics. A subsequent re-release featured artwork by Toyste Beach during the XY Series, specifically in the BREAKthrough expansion. This Trainer card provides the Pokémon it is attached to with a Retreat Cost of zero.

Title: Rocky Helmet

The Rocky Helmet (known in Japanese as ゴツゴツメット, or "Rugged Helmet") made its debut as a held item in Generation V. It serves to inflict retaliatory damage upon Pokémon that strike its wielder with contact moves. Specifically, if a Pokémon employs a move that makes physical contact with a Pokémon equipped with a Rocky Helmet, the attacking Pokémon suffers retribution amounting to 1/6 of its maximum HP. Each distinct strike from a multi-strike move that makes contact will independently trigger the Rocky Helmet's effect.

Should a Pokémon using Thief, Covet, or Knock Off, or one possessing the Ability Magician, be knocked out by the target's held Rocky Helmet, it is prevented from acquiring or discarding the target's Rocky Helmet. In link battles and battle facilities, if both Trainers' Pokémon are simultaneously incapacitated due to a held Rocky Helmet, victory is awarded to the Trainer whose Pokémon bore the item. However, a specific anomaly in version 1.0 of Pokémon Sun and Moon meant that even then, in link battles and battle facilities, if two Trainers' Pokémon fainted concurrently from a held Rocky Helmet, the Trainer whose Pokémon held the Rocky Helmet would emerge victorious. This behavior was corrected from version 1.1 of Pokémon Sun and Moon onward, where the Trainer whose Pokémon did not hold the Rocky Helmet now wins the match.

In Pokémon UNITE, this held item functions differently, causing nearby adversaries to suffer recoil damage. This effect is triggered when the wielder sustains at least 10% of their maximum HP in a single blow, with a 2-second cooldown. The inflicted damage corresponds to 3%, 4%, or 5% of the user's max HP at item Grades 1, 10, and 20 respectively. Additionally, it augments the user's HP and Defense stats, providing 18 HP and 0 Defense at Grade 1, escalating to 270 HP and 51 Defense at Grade 30.

The item made an appearance in the anime episode Under the Pledging Tree!, where a market vendor in Coumarine City presented it to Ash among other goods, after Ash had pondered aloud about gift ideas for an approaching festival.

Within the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Rocky Helmet debuted as a Pokémon Tool card during the English Black & White Series (known as the BW Era in Japan), specifically within the Noble Victories expansion (the Japanese Red Collection), featuring illustrations by Ryo Ueda. It later saw a reprint as both a standard and an Ultra Rare Secret card in the English Boundaries Crossed expansion, once again featuring art from the same illustrator. During the English Sword & Shield Series (the Japanese SWSH Era), the card was reissued in the Vivid Voltage expansion (part of the Japanese Legendary Heartbeat subset), this time with artwork by Studio Bora Inc. Subsequently, for the Scarlet & Violet Series (known as the Scarlet & Violet Era in Japan), Rocky Helmet received another reprint, illustrated by Ayaka Yoshida; this particular version showcases the updated Trainer card layout and the revised Pokémon Tool card classification that commenced with this Series. When this card is affixed to a player's Active Pokémon, should an opposing Pokémon inflict damage upon that Pokémon (even resulting in a Knock Out), the player is permitted to place two damage counters onto the attacking Pokémon.

On January 19, 2013, a collectible strap depicting Scraggy alongside a Rocky Helmet became available, launched as part of the Pokémon Goods For Adventure Campaign and exclusively sold at the Pokémon Center. More recently, on March 27, 2025, the Rocky Helmet also appeared on a mini towel, part of the Pokémon Sento goods collection, which is also an exclusive offering from the Pokémon Center.

Title: Air Balloon

The term "Balloon" typically redirects to this entry. For information on Team Rocket's balloon, consult Team Rocket's mechas#Meowth Balloon. An Air Balloon (Japanese: ふうせん Balloon) is a held item that debuted in Generation V. It renders the Pokémon holding it ungrounded. This ungrounded state confers immunity to damaging Ground-type moves and numerous associated effects. Upon being struck by any damaging attack, whether it affects the holder or its substitute (even if the holder possesses the Disguise Ability), the Air Balloon shatters. A notification appears when a Pokémon holding an Air Balloon enters battle, provided its effect isn't nullified.

The Air Balloon's ungrounding effect is suppressed under specific circumstances: when Gravity is active, or if the holder is affected by Ingrain, Smack Down, or Thousand Arrows. However, these conditions do not prevent the Air Balloon from being destroyed. Conversely, an Air Balloon will not burst if Magic Room is in effect, if the holder has the Klutz Ability, or if it is under the influence of Embargo. Once an Air Balloon bursts, it cannot be retrieved using Recycle or Pickup. The Air Balloon is destroyed prior to being stolen by moves like Thief or Covet, or by the Magician Ability. Terrain effects on a Pokémon are assessed before an Air Balloon bursts; consequently, terrain cannot safeguard a Pokémon holding an Air Balloon from a status condition inflicted by the move that causes the Air Balloon to burst. The destruction of an Air Balloon will activate the Abilities Unburden and Symbiosis.

In the anime episode Challenge the Battle Subway!, Monta's Snivy was seen holding an Air Balloon. Within the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Air Balloon debuted as a Pokémon Tool card during the English Sword & Shield Series (known as the Japanese Sword & Shield Era). Its initial release occurred in the Japanese Sword expansion, subsequently appearing in English within the Sword & Shield expansion, featuring artwork by Yoshinobu Saito. Additionally, a Secret Full Art version was released in both the aforementioned Japanese and English expansions, also illustrated by the same artist. This card decreases the holder's Retreat Cost by (2 Energy).

Title: Red Card

This held item, known in Japanese as レッドカード (Red Card), made its debut in Generation V and is consumed upon use. When a Pokémon holding this item takes damage from an opposing Pokémon's attack, the Red Card forces the attacking Pokémon to switch out. Provided the holder survives a damaging hit, the Red Card is expended, compelling the Pokémon responsible for the move (even if an ally) to be swapped out for a randomly chosen team member. Should a wild Pokémon possess a Red Card in a Single Battle, its activation will cause the player's attacking Pokémon to flee the encounter. However, in wild encounters such as Horde Encounters, Double Battles, or SOS Battles featuring two wild Pokémon, the Red Card prompts the player's attacking Pokémon to switch out instead of fleeing. When several Pokémon each holding a Red Card are struck by one attack, only the Red Card of the Pokémon with the highest Speed stat will trigger. This item fails to activate if the assailant is a wild Pokémon, or if its Trainer lacks available Pokémon for a switch-in, regardless of whether the Red Card's effect would typically cause a switch-out or a flee. Furthermore, if a Pokémon's substitute absorbs the incoming attack, the Red Card remains inactive. Moves enhanced by the Sheer Force Ability will not cause the Red Card to activate. For multistrike moves, the Red Card's effect takes place following the final hit.

Should the attacker possess Suction Cups, be a Dondozo benefiting from Commander with a Tatsugiri, or be rooted by Ingrain, the Red Card will still be consumed and activate; however, it will be unsuccessful in forcing that Pokémon to switch out or flee. This holds true even if the attacker is Ghost-type, has the Run Away Ability, or is equipped with a Shed Shell or Smoke Ball. Neither trapping moves (excluding Ingrain) nor trapping Abilities can stop a Pokémon from being compelled to switch out or flee by the Red Card. Items such as Knock Off, Thief, Covet, or the Magician Ability will remove or steal the Red Card before it can trigger. A Pokémon holding a Red Card with the Pickpocket Ability can still pilfer an attacker's item via Pickpocket even after its own Red Card has switched out the assailant. Conversely, if Pickpocket steals a Red Card, that particular Red Card is unable to activate for the current attack. The Red Card initiates the attacker's switch-out prior to the activation opportunity of its own held Life Orb or Shell Bell. When a Pokémon equipped with a Red Card is struck by a combination move (such as Grass Pledge, Fire Pledge, or Water Pledge) and survives, and the attacker is subsequently switched out, the intended combination effect will fail to materialize.

If a Pokémon holding a Red Card is hit by Dragon Tail or Circle Throw and does not faint, that Pokémon itself is forced to switch out or flee, preventing its Red Card from having a chance to activate. Should a Pokémon holding a Red Card be struck by U-turn, Volt Switch, or Flip Turn without fainting, the Red Card will activate and compel the attacker to switch to a randomly selected Pokémon, rather than one chosen by their Trainer. Emergency Exit and Wimp Out will not trigger if a Red Card is consumed during the same move. Similarly, U-turn, Volt Switch, and Flip Turn will not cause their user to switch out if they are the ones holding a Red Card. While Dynamaxed Pokémon are immune to being switched out by the Red Card's effect, the item will nonetheless be consumed. In Pokémon Trading Card Game Pocket, the Red Card manifests as an Item card that compels a player's adversary to shuffle their current hand back into their deck and then draw three new cards. For the Pokémon Trading Card Game, Red Card was introduced as an Item card within the XY expansion, part of the English XY Series (Japanese XY Era). This particular Trainer card instructs the opponent to shuffle their hand into their deck and then draw 4 cards.

Title: Binding Band

The Binding Band (Japanese: しめつけバンド Binding Band) is a held item that debuted in Generation V. When held, it amplifies the damage inflicted by binding moves. Prior to Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, binding applied by the holder would deal 1/8 of the target's maximum HP at the end of each turn, instead of the base 1/16. From Pokémon Scarlet and Violet onwards, this effect was enhanced; binding now deals 1/6 of the target's maximum HP per turn, improved from 1/8. Visually, the item's depiction changed: before Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, it was presented as a Möbius strip, while its Scarlet and Violet artwork features a regular two-sided band.

Title: Absorb Bulb

An item designated to be held by Pokémon, the Absorb Bulb (Japanese: きゅうこん Bulb), debuted in Generation V. It has the property of increasing the holder's Special Attack stat upon receiving a damaging Water-type attack. Specifically, should the holder be struck by an offensive Water-type technique, the Absorb Bulb is spent, granting a one-stage boost to its Special Attack. The effect will not trigger if the Pokémon's Special Attack stat stages are already at their maximum of +6. An exception applies if its Ability is Contrary, in which case activation is prevented when the holder's Special Attack stat stages are at -6. Furthermore, activation fails to occur if the holder is shielded from or rendered immune to the Water-type move, such as by Abilities like Water Absorb or Wonder Guard. When several Pokémon, each equipped with an Absorb Bulb or Luminous Moss, are simultaneously struck by a Water-type attack, each impacted Pokémon will utilize their item before Symbiosis has the chance to transfer any items, including the Absorb Bulb or Luminous Moss itself.

Title: Cell Battery
First appearing in Generation V, the Cell Battery (Japanese: じゅうでんち Charge Battery) is a specific type of held item. When a Pokémon holding this item is struck by a damaging Electric-type move, its Attack stat will be raised. More precisely, if the holder takes damage from an Electric-type attack, the Cell Battery is consumed, and the Pokémon's Attack increases by one stage. This effect does not trigger if the holder has already reached +6 Attack stat stages. An exception exists for Pokémon with the Contrary Ability, in which case the item will not activate if the holder is at -6 Attack stat stages. Furthermore, the Cell Battery fails to activate if the holder is protected from or otherwise unaffected by the Electric-type move, for example, due to the Motor Drive Ability or being a Ground-type Pokémon. Should an Electric-type move simultaneously hit multiple Pokémon, each carrying a Cell Battery, every Pokémon that is struck will consume its Cell Battery before the Ability Symbiosis can transfer any item, even other Cell Batteries.

Title: Eject Button

Known in Japanese as だっしゅつボタン (Break-Out Button), the Eject Button is a held item that is consumed upon use, first appearing in Generation V. Its primary function is to compel the Pokémon holding it to withdraw from battle should it suffer damage from an attack. Provided the holder takes damage from an offensive move and is not knocked out, the Eject Button is expended, and the Pokémon is instantly substituted with another selected by its Trainer. Activation is prevented if the Pokémon is wild or if its Trainer possesses no available Pokémon for substitution. Furthermore, the Eject Button remains inactive if the damage is absorbed by a Pokémon's Substitute. Moves empowered by the Sheer Force Ability will not trigger the Eject Button's effect. In the case of multi-hit attacks, the Eject Button's effect manifests following the final strike. When a single attack strikes multiple Pokémon, each equipped with an Eject Button, only the Eject Button held by the Pokémon with the highest Speed stat among them will activate. Neither Suction Cups, nor various trapping moves, Abilities, or the Dynamax phenomenon can impede a Pokémon's forced withdrawal caused by the Eject Button. Moves such as Knock Off, Thief, Covet, or the Magician Ability will divest the holder of its Eject Button before it can have a chance to activate. Should a Pokémon acquire an Eject Button through Pickpocket, that particular Eject Button is unable to activate in response to the very same incoming attack. When a Pokémon holding this item is struck by Dragon Tail or Circle Throw, it is compelled to switch out or flee the battle prior to its Eject Button having any chance to activate. Should U-turn or Volt Switch cause a Pokémon's Eject Button to activate, the Pokémon that initiated the U-turn or Volt Switch attack will not be switched out itself. For Pokémon possessing Emergency Exit or Wimp Out, the Eject Button will take precedence, activating before either of these Abilities can trigger. The item played a role in the "Symbiosis Eject Button glitch." During this glitch, if an ally's Eject Button was consumed, the Symbiosis Ability would activate, passing an item to the departing ally after it switched out but prior to its substitute entering the field. Any item transferred via Symbiosis in this scenario was incapable of activating immediately. Currently, an Eject Button's activation no longer triggers Symbiosis. Within Pokémon UNITE, the Eject Button functions as a battle item, becoming available once a Trainer reaches Level 11. It grants the ability to instantly warp a fixed distance, subject to a 70-second cooldown.

Title: Fire Gem

Originating in Generation V, the Fire Gem (known in Japan as ほのおのジュエル, or Flame Jewel) functions as a held item. It belongs to the category of Gems and becomes unobtainable as a held item from Generation VI forward. A Pokémon holding this item will see the power of its initial Fire-type attack boosted by 50%, whereupon the item is expended. Attempts to use the move Fling will be unsuccessful if the Pokémon is clutching a Fire Gem. Notably, the move Fire Pledge will not cause a Fire Gem to be used up. Similarly, when a Pokémon holds this item, the initial Fire-type move executed gains a 30% power boost, being consumed thereafter. During the Wishing Bell Festival's scavenger hunt, a Fire Gem served as one of the required search objects in the episode 'Climbing the Tower of Success!'.

Title: Water Gem
Debuting in Generation V, the Water Gem (Japanese: みずのジュエル, or Water Jewel) is categorized as a held item. It belongs to the broader group of Gems and became an unobtainable held item from Generation VI onward. Should a Pokémon be holding this item, the power of its inaugural Water-type move is augmented by 50%, leading to the item's consumption. However, the move Fling will invariably fail if the user possesses a Water Gem. The Water Gem is also never consumed when Water Pledge is utilized. In a separate effect, when a Pokémon holds it, the power of the first Water-type move used is increased by 30%, and it is consumed after this use.

Title: Electric Gem

The Electric Gem (Japanese: でんきのジュエル Electric Jewel) is a particular held item first introduced in Generation V. It is classified as one of the Gems and became an unobtainable held item from Generation VI onwards. When a Pokémon is holding this item, it increases the power of the first Electric-type move used by 50%, and is consumed after use. If the user is holding an Electric Gem, Fling will fail. Additionally, when held by a Pokémon, it increases the power of the first Electric-type move used by 30%, and is consumed following its activation.

Title: Grass Gem

Introduced in Generation V, the Grass Gem (Japanese: くさのジュエル, Grass Jewel) is a specific held item. Categorized as one of the Gems, it became an unobtainable held item from Generation VI onward. When held by a Pokémon, its first Grass-type move's power is increased by 50%, and is consumed after use. The move Fling fails if the user holds a Grass Gem. However, Grass Pledge never consumes a Grass Gem. Additionally, when held by a Pokémon, the first Grass-type move's power is increased by 30%, and is consumed after use.

Title: Ice Gem

This held item, known in Japanese as こおりのジュエル (Ice Jewel), made its debut in Generation V under the name Ice Gem. Categorized among the various Gems, it became inaccessible as a held item from Generation VI onward. A Pokémon gripping this item will see the strength of its initial Ice-type attack boosted by 50%, after which the item is used up. Should a Pokémon attempt to use Fling while clutching an Ice Gem, the move will not succeed. Moreover, when a Pokémon is holding this item, its first Ice-type move gains a 30% power increase before the item is expended.

Title: Fighting Gem

Known in Japanese as かくとうジュエル (Fighting Jewel), the Fighting Gem first made its debut in Generation V as a held item. Categorized as one of the "Gems," it became an unobtainable held item from Generation VI onwards. When a Pokémon is equipped with this item, its initial Fighting-type attack benefits from a 50% power boost, after which the item is consumed. Notably, the move Fling will fail if the user is holding a Fighting Gem. Furthermore, if a Pokémon holds it, the power of its first Fighting-type move is increased by 30%, and the item is expended after being used.

Title: Poison Gem

Originating in Generation V, the Poison Gem (Japanese: どくのジュエル Poison Jewel) is classified as a held item. It is a member of the Gem series, becoming an unobtainable held item from Generation VI onward. When a Pokémon is equipped with it, the power of the first Poison-type move that Pokémon uses is boosted by 50%, and the item is subsequently consumed. If a Pokémon carries a Poison Gem, the move Fling will not succeed. Furthermore, should a Pokémon hold this item, its initial Poison-type attack will gain a 30% power enhancement before the Gem is consumed.

Title: Ground Gem

The Ground Gem (Japanese: じめんのジュエル Ground Jewel), a held item, was introduced in Generation V. It belongs to the category of Gems and has been an unobtainable held item since Generation VI. When equipped by a Pokémon, it augments the power of the first Ground-type move employed by 50%, vanishing thereafter. Should a Pokémon attempt to use Fling while holding this Gem, the move will fail. Additionally, when a Pokémon is holding this item, the initial Ground-type attack used benefits from a 30% power enhancement, and the item is consumed upon use.

Title: Flying Gem

Known in Japan as ひこうのジュエル (Flying Jewel), the Flying Gem is a held item originating from Generation V. As one of the 'Gems,' it became unobtainable as a held item from Generation VI onward. A Pokémon holding it sees its first Flying-type move's power increased by 50%, consuming the item in the process. Fling cannot be successfully used by a Pokémon holding a Flying Gem. If a Flying Gem is consumed immediately before executing Acrobatics, the move gains double its base power (treated as if no item were held), alongside the Gem's own power enhancement. Separately, when held, the item boosts a Pokémon's first Flying-type move by 30%, then being consumed.

Title: Psychic Gem

Known in Japanese as エスパージュエル (Esper Jewel), the Psychic Gem is a specific kind of held item that debuted in Generation V. Belonging to the 'Gems' category, this item became unobtainable as a held item starting from Generation VI. A Pokémon clutching this gem will see the power of its initial Psychic-type attack boosted by 50%, after which the item is consumed. Attempts to use Fling will prove unsuccessful if the user is currently holding a Psychic Gem. Furthermore, if a Pokémon holds this item, its first Psychic-type move gains a 30% power increase before the gem is expended.

Title: Bug Gem

The Bug Gem (Japanese: むしのジュエル Bug Jewel) is a held item that debuted in Generation V. Classified among the Gems, it became an unobtainable held item from Generation VI forward. When a Pokémon holds this item, it amplifies the power of the first Bug-type move employed. In Generation V, this power increase was 50%, whereas from Generation VI onward, it would be 30%. Regardless of the generation, the item is consumed upon use.

Title: Rock Gem

Known in Japanese as いわのジュエル Rock Jewel, the Rock Gem debuted in Generation V as a held item. It is categorized among the various Gems and has been unobtainable as a held item from Generation VI onward. When equipped by a Pokémon, it increases the power of the first Rock-type move used by 50%, and is consumed after its effect. Separately, the text also states that when a Pokémon holds it, the item elevates the power of the initial Rock-type move performed by 30%, being consumed following its activation. Should a Pokémon attempt to use Fling while holding a Rock Gem, the move will invariably fail.

Title: Ghost Gem

Introduced in Generation V, the Ghost Gem (Japanese: ゴーストジュエル Ghost Jewel) is a specific type of held item. It belongs to the category of Gems, and its acquisition becomes impossible as a held item from Generation VI onward. When a Pokémon holds this item, it amplifies the power of the first Ghost-type move used by 50%, being consumed afterward. The move Fling will invariably fail if the user is currently holding a Ghost Gem. Furthermore, when a Pokémon holds this item, it boosts the power of the first Ghost-type move used by 30%, and is consumed after use.

Title: Dark Gem

Making its debut in Generation V, the Dark Gem (known in Japanese as あくのジュエル, or Evil Jewel) is categorized as a held item. This item belongs to the family of Gems and became unobtainable as a held item from Generation VI forward. When a Pokémon carries it, the power of the first Dark-type move unleashed is amplified by 50%, with the item being consumed post-use. The move Fling cannot be successfully executed by a Pokémon equipped with a Dark Gem. Additionally, while held, it also boosts the power of the initial Dark-type move performed by 30%, after which it is consumed.

Title: Steel Gem

A held item introduced in Generation V, the Steel Gem (Japanese: はがねのジュエル Steel Jewel) is categorized among the Gems. It became an unobtainable held item from Generation VI onwards. Should a Pokémon be holding this item, the power of its first Steel-type move is increased by 50%, with the item being consumed after this effect. Fling will fail if the user is currently holding a Steel Gem. Separately, when held by a Pokémon, it delivers a 30% boost to the power of the first Steel-type move utilized, and is consumed after use.

Title: Cover Fossil

Referred to in Japanese as ふたのカセキ (Cover Fossil), this ancient relic first appeared in Generation V. It possesses the capability to be regenerated into the Pokémon Tirtouga when brought to one of several designated locations, and it stands as the counterpart to the Plume Fossil.

The Cover Fossil was prominently featured in the animated episode "A Restoration Confrontation! Part 1." Discovered initially by Ferris, it was subsequently appropriated by Team Rocket. Drawing upon stolen data from Professor Juniper and Fennel's fossil resurrection apparatus, together with stones seized from Chargestone Cave, the villainous group managed to resurrect it as a Tirtouga. This Tirtouga then underwent evolution into a Carracosta in the subsequent episode before being eventually repatriated to its own time stream through a time gate situated within Twist Mountain.

In the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Cover Fossil first surfaced as an Item card within the Noble Victories expansion, initially released as part of the Japanese Red Collection. This card grants players the ability to inspect the bottom seven cards of their deck, locate a Tirtouga among them, and then position it onto their Bench. The other six cards are subsequently reintegrated into the deck through shuffling. (Main article: Antique Cover Fossil (Stellar Crown 129))

With the advent of the Scarlet & Violet Series, the Antique Cover Fossil emerged within the Stellar Crown expansion, debuting concurrently with Japan's Stellar Miracle. Unlike its predecessor, this particular card is structured similarly to other Item cards that represent Fossils. It offers players the option to place it onto their Bench, functioning as if it were a Colorless-type Basic Pokémon. Its inherent Ability serves to prevent all effects of attacks from opposing Pokémon directed at it. Additionally, Unidentified Fossil cards, classified as Fossil Trainer cards, transform into Basic Pokémon upon play. Though these do not specifically feature the Cover Fossil, they nonetheless enable the evolution into specific Tirtouga.

Beyond its in-game and TCG appearances, the Cover Fossil has been immortalized in a range of officially licensed merchandise.

Title: Plume Fossil

The Plume Fossil (Japanese: はねのカセキ Feather Fossil) is a fossil item that made its debut in Generation V, capable of being revived into an Archen. It serves as the direct counterpart to the Cover Fossil. When brought to specific designated locations, this fossil can be regenerated into an Archen.

One Plume Fossil made an appearance in the episode *Archeops In The Modern World!*, having been entrusted to Professor Juniper and Fennel by Lenora. They successfully brought forth a female Archen from this ancient relic by channeling Musharna's dream energy. With the timely aid of Ash and his Tranquill, this newly revived Archen learned to fly, subsequently evolving into Archeops. Another instance saw Kiawe discovering a Plume Fossil in *Treasure Hunt, Akala Style!*, during the 'Stoutland Search' treasure hunt organized for Ash and his classmates by Olivia. Additionally, a Plume Fossil was displayed on a holographic screen during Sophocles's initial treasure-hunting endeavor.

In the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Plume Fossil was introduced as an Item card within the *Noble Victories* expansion, first launched in the Japanese *Red Collection*. This card allows players to examine the bottom seven cards of their deck, reveal any Archen found among them, and immediately place it onto their Bench. The remaining six cards are then shuffled back into the deck.

More recently, the *Scarlet & Violet Series* unveiled the Antique Plume Fossil (White Flare 79) in the *White Flare* expansion, which also released under the same name in Japan. Unlike its earlier namesake, this card aligns with other Fossil Item cards by permitting the player to play it directly onto the Bench as if it were a Colorless-type Basic Pokémon. It features an Ability that prevents all damage to itself from attacks by opposing Pokémon while it resides on the Bench. While not depicting a Plume Fossil directly, Unidentified Fossil cards, which are Fossil Trainer cards that become Basic Pokémon, can still facilitate the evolution of certain Archen.

The Plume Fossil has also been showcased across various officially licensed merchandise. A curious discrepancy in its lore exists: while the item's in-game description indicates it came from a "Pokémon that (once) flew/lived in the sky," the Backpacker character in Pokémon Black and White, who dispenses fossils, states that the Plume Fossil belonged to a Pokémon from an ancient forest that could not fly. This potential contradiction might be a deliberate reference, considering current scientific understanding of the Archaeopteryx genus.

Title: Liberty Pass

Known in Japanese as リバティチケット (Liberty Ticket), the Liberty Pass functions as a unique Key Item, obtainable solely through special events. Its primary purpose in Pokémon Black and White is to grant players access to Liberty Garden, a location central to encountering the Mythical Pokémon Victini. Upon acquiring this pass, trainers must proceed to Liberty Pier, situated at the furthest west point within Castelia City's bustling harbor. There, the vessel's captain will identify the Liberty Pass and, in response, willingly transport the player to Liberty Garden. Should the player lack possession of the pass, the ship will, however, remain moored, unable to depart. Its distribution coincided with the launch of Pokémon Black and White, occurring wirelessly across all regions, and additionally through select retail partners in countries outside of Japan. The duration of its availability varied from one nation to another. In the narrative "At Liberty on Liberty Garden," White was depicted owning two Liberty Passes. She, alongside Black, utilized these passes to embark on a journey from Castelia City to Liberty Garden, undertaking a quest for Victini at the behest of Professor Juniper.

Title: Dream Ball

Known in Japanese as ドリームボール (Dream Ball), this Poké Ball type made its debut in Generation V. Its primary function involves capturing wild Pokémon encountered exclusively within the Entree Forest. From Generation VIII onwards, its efficacy increases significantly when deployed against a Pokémon that is asleep. Any Pokémon brought over from the Pokémon Dream Radar will be contained within a Dream Ball. When a player engages with a Pokémon in the Entree Forest, a distinctive wild encounter commences, enabling capture without requiring a battle. During this specific type of encounter, a single Dream Ball is provided in the player's Bag alongside their standard Poké Balls; should this Dream Ball remain unused, it disappears once the encounter concludes. Every Poké Ball thrown in the Entree Forest results in an infallible capture of the wild Pokémon. In a standard wild encounter, when selected from the Bag, it attempts to secure the wild Pokémon. Its catch rate modifier is 4× when targeting a sleeping Pokémon; otherwise, it is 1×. Within Pokémon Legends: Z-A, a 4× catch rate modifier is similarly applied if it's thrown at a drowsy Pokémon. Even if a Pokémon is observed sleeping in the overworld due to its inherent behavior, this specific state will not activate the Dream Ball's special catch rate bonus. Although inaccessible as an item in Generation VI, an instance of the Dream Ball is nevertheless displayed within the Poké Ball Boutique located in Lumiose City. The Dream Ball also made its debut in the Pokémon Trading Card Game as an Item card, appearing during the English Sword & Shield Series, known as the Japanese Sword & Shield Era. Its initial release occurred in the Japanese Eevee Heroes subset, subsequently reaching English audiences in the Evolving Skies expansion, featuring artwork crafted by Ryo Ueda. This card permits a player to search their deck for any Pokémon and place it directly onto their Bench, a privilege contingent solely upon the Dream Ball having been claimed as a face-down Prize card. Significantly, this capability extends to Evolution Pokémon, which typically require the Evolution mechanic to enter play. Visually, the Dream Ball shares a resemblance with the Pokémon Musharna.

Title: Prop Case

Recognized in Japanese as グッズケース (Goods Case), the Prop Case is a specialized Key Item initially featured in Generation V. This item serves as a receptacle for the various Props players utilize to adorn their Pokémon, specifically for participation in Pokémon Musicals. For those playing Pokémon Black 2 and White 2, if a Memory Link was established with either a Pokémon Black or White game, the Prop Case will already be equipped with the Props previously acquired in that linked save file. The conception of the Prop Case itself, alongside the complete design and other components of the Pokémon Musical, is attributed to White, first appearing in the narrative "To Make a Musical." In the episode "Special Delivery," a critical shipment of 200 Prop Cases, intended for distribution at the Pokémon Musical's grand debut, faced a major delay due to its delivery truck becoming ensnared in traffic behind the elevated Driftveil Drawbridge. To circumvent this issue, White requested assistance from Skyla, whose aircraft was instrumental in ensuring the Prop Cases reached Nimbasa City promptly.

Title: Dragon Skull

Designated as a Key Item, the object known as the Dragon Skull (Japanese: ドラゴンのホネ Dragon Bone) made its debut in Generation V. This artifact, a skull from a Dragon-type Pokémon, specifically appears to be that of a Dragonite, and is an exhibit at the Nacrene Museum. Following the player's acquisition of the Basic Badge, Team Plasma orchestrates the theft of this Dragon Skull from the Nacrene Museum. The player subsequently tracks down and confronts the responsible Team Plasma Grunt at the remote section of Pinwheel Forest. Upon successfully reclaiming the skull, the player promptly delivers it back to Lenora. Due to the item being acquired and relinquished within the identical cutscene, players are prevented from inspecting the Dragon Skull within their Bag.

A different account unfolds in the Pokémon Adventures manga, specifically in 'The Mystery of the Missing Fossil.' Here, Team Plasma's heist from the Nacrene Museum involved not just the skull, but the complete Dragon Fossil, alongside numerous other priceless artifacts. Black committed to assisting in the recovery of the stolen goods. While tracking the pilfered items, he met Burgh, who was also recruited by Lenora to aid in resolving the predicament. The duo quickly located the Dragon Fossil near the fringes of Pinwheel Forest, only for it to astonishingly begin an assault against them. Black promptly deduced that the culprits behind the theft were a contingent of Team Plasma Grunts along with Gorm, one of the Seven Sages. They were manipulating the skeleton using Gorm's Cofagrigus. Assisted by Burgh, Black managed to overcome Cofagrigus. Once Team Plasma recognized that the skeleton held no connection to the Legendary Pokémon they sought, they withdrew. This allowed for the secure return of the Dragon Fossil and all other purloined artifacts to the Nacrene Museum. Within Pokémon X and Y, a Dragon Skull makes an appearance, displayed on a shelf inside the Ambrette Town Fossil Lab.

Title: Balm Mushroom

Referred to as Balm Mushroom (Japanese: かおるキノコ Fragrant Mushroom), and designated BalmMushroom prior to Generation VI, this valuable commodity made its debut in Generation V. In the Generation V video games, players can sell the BalmMushroom to the gourmet maniac found on Route 5 for $25000. In a similar vein, Pokémon Sword and Shield allows for the Balm Mushroom to be sold to the valuable item buyer within Stow-on-Side for $25,000. The Balm Mushroom's only practical utility lies in its sale. Nonetheless, in Pokémon Quest, a Balm Mushroom serves as a cooking ingredient, utilized to attract various wild Pokémon. It is formally classified as a Mushroom. Successfully completing expeditions is one method by which a Balm Mushroom can be obtained as an ingredient. Balm Mushrooms are most commonly discovered in the locales of Parched Peak and Farside Fjord. They may also be randomly acquired through the process of recycling Power Stones. In the episode 'Food Fit for a Kingambit!', Balm Mushrooms were included among the House Specials offered at the Anything Goes Café.

Title: Big Nugget

The Big Nugget (Japanese: でかいきんのたま, meaning "Giant Gold Orb") is a valuable article initially introduced in the fifth generation of games. It functions as an amplified and more profitable iteration of the standard Nugget. In the Generation V titles, it can be vended to the ore enthusiast inside the Icirrus City Pokémon Center, yielding $30000. Conversely, during Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, players are able to procure a Big Nugget daily from Office Worker Royce in Po Town for $5,000. This item holds no practical application apart from its sell value. Following the Nugget's Japanese designation, きんのたま (kin no tama, "golden ball"), which serves as a Japanese colloquialism for testicles, the Big Nugget's Japanese name appends でかい (dekai), literally rendering it a "gigantic testicle." From Pokémon Sword and Shield onwards, the Big Nugget shares the distinction with the Iron Ball of granting Fling its highest potential base power.

Title: Pearl String

Known in Japanese as おだんごしんじゅ (Pearl Dango), the Pearl String is a valuable commodity that first appeared in Generation V. Throughout the Generation V titles, players can exchange this item with the ore maniac located within the Icirrus City Pokémon Center, receiving $25000 for it. Conversely, in the Pokémon Sword and Shield games, Stow-on-Side's valuable item buyer will acquire the Pearl String for $27,500. Beyond its exchange for currency, the Pearl String possesses no other practical utility. It was also depicted in artwork promoting the Pokémon Diving merchandise collection, a line of products exclusively available through the Pokémon Center.

Title: Comet Shard

An item of considerable worth, the Comet Shard (Japanese: すいせいのかけら Comet Shard), made its debut in Generation V. During Generation V gameplay, this artifact could be exchanged for $60000 by presenting it to the ore maniac situated within the Icirrus City Pokémon Center. Later, in the Pokémon Sword and Shield titles, its market value decreased to $30,000 when offered to the valuable item buyer in Stow-on-Side. Beyond its inherent monetary value, the Comet Shard serves no practical function. A notable occurrence is that the Star Piece unearthed by Sophocles during a Stoutland Search treasure hunt was erroneously identified as a Comet Shard in the dub of "Treasure Hunt, Akala Style!".

Title: Relic Crown
The term Ancient Crown refers to this article. For the accessory encountered in the Mystery Dungeon series, consult Treasure Collection → Crown. Known in Japanese as こだいのおうかん Ancient Crown, the Relic Crown represents a valuable artifact that debuted in Generation V. Across the Generation V titles, players may liquidate the Relic Crown by offering it to the eccentric billionaire residing in Undella Town, fetching a price of $300000. Its utility extends no further than its capacity to be vended. A notable similarity exists between the Relic Crown and the headpiece Ghetsis bestows upon N in the opening sequence of Pokémon Black and White.

Title: Casteliacone

Known in Japanese as ヒウンアイス Hiun Ice Cream, the Casteliacone functions as a medicinal item first seen in Generation V. This item, a local delicacy originating from Castelia City, effectively removes all non-volatile status conditions and confusion from a Pokémon. Within Generation V titles, the Casteliacone fetches a price of $2000 when sold to the gourmet maniac situated on Route 5. Players in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire have a singular opportunity to acquire a Casteliacone from a Fairy Tale Girl in Mauville City for $200, provided Regice is present in their party. When administered to a Pokémon directly from the Bag, it alleviates all non-volatile status conditions—specifically poison, paralysis, sleep, burn, and freeze—as well as confusion. Furthermore, in Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, a Casteliacone, when held by a nicknamed Regice in the party alongside Regirock and Registeel, is instrumental in causing Regigigas to appear in Island Cave during daylight hours, subsequent to the defeat or capture of Groudon or Kyogre.

In the game Pokémon: Magikarp Jump, Casteliacones are among the consumable food items available for Magikarp. These become accessible once a Trainer reaches Rank 60, with an unlock cost of 92,874. The Casteliacone received its initial mention in the episode A Venipede Stampede!. During their visit to Castelia City, Iris inadvertently guided her companions to the treat's vendor stand, only to discover it was shut. This very stand was referenced once more in Climbing the Tower of Success!, when Ash, his friends, and Stephan were engaged in solving a quiz during the Wishing Bell Festival. Fortunately, Cilan's recollection of having previously consumed a Casteliacone provided the crucial insight needed to deduce the correct solution.

Casteliacones also featured as merchandise available for purchase at the Vertress Conference, depicted in both Lost at the League! and A Unova League Evolution!. During the latter episode, Bianca acquired multiple cones from an ice cream vendor and extended some to Ash and his companions. Team Rocket, in disguise, peddled Casteliacones during Ash and Goh's trip to Castelia City in A Close Call... Practically!. For Errand Endurance!, Team Rocket dispatched Pelipper to procure a Casteliacone to complement their apple pie. Concerned the forgetful Water Bird Pokémon might fail the task unassisted, the trio assumed the guise of ice cream vendors, handing Pelipper the ice cream directly before heading back to their lair. Nonetheless, upon the ice cream's arrival, both it and the pie were promptly consumed by Morpeko. James's Morpeko was observed enjoying Casteliacones in The Fiery Road to Mastership!. Within Big City Battles, White presented Black with a box of Casteliacones for use during a battle. A Casteliacone's image can be found on the Next Destinies Vanillite card. The Casteliacone has appeared in numerous officially licensed figures and various merchandise. Both in its Pokémon Global Link artwork and within Pokémon Journeys: The Series, the Casteliacone's design bears a likeness to Vanillite.

Title: Light Stone

A Key Item making its debut in Generation V, the Light Stone (Japanese: ライトストーン Light Stone) embodies Reshiram in its dormant state, serving as one of the two legendary Dragon Stones. In Pokémon Black, this item is crucial during the main storyline, summoning Reshiram to aid the player against N and Zekrom at N's Castle. For Pokémon White 2 players, it calls forth Reshiram atop Dragonspiral Tower during the post-game. The Light Stone also features in Learn with Pokémon: Typing Adventure and first appeared in the films Black—Victini and Reshiram and White—Victini and Zekrom. True to its nature as Reshiram's physical manifestation, the Light Stone is capable of glowing red due to Reshiram's inherent fire.

Its initial appearance saw it under the dominion of the Hero of Truth, representing the fatigued dragon Pokémon's hibernating form. The hero subsequently concealed the stone beneath the Sword of the Vale. In the cinematic releases, Black—Victini and Reshiram depicted Ash awakening Reshiram from the Light Stone to engage Damon's Zekrom, while White—Victini and Zekrom showed Damon discovering and utilizing the Light Stone to enlist Reshiram for his own objectives.

The Light Stone's animated series debut occurred in Ash and N: A Clash of Ideals!, where Cedric Juniper initially discovered it within the White Ruins. N swiftly confiscated it, asserting his desire to question Reshiram regarding its prior assault on Team Plasma's castle. Ash pursued N but became trapped in a sinkhole. N attempted to assist, only to fall into the same predicament alongside Ash. Leveraging Pikachu, Pignite, Krookodile, and Charizard, Ash managed to excavate a path back to the surface. There, Team Plasma had commenced an assault on the White Ruins, aiming to seize the Light Stone. When Colress activated his Pokémon Control Device on Pikachu, compelling it to attack Ash and his companions, N yielded the Light Stone to Team Plasma, ensuring Ash's safety. Following this, Ghetsis executed a ritual to awaken Reshiram, successfully restoring the Vast White Pokémon to its genuine state.

A brief appearance of the Light Stone was noted in The King Returns, as Kyurem forced N's Reshiram to regress into its stone form. Immediately thereafter, Kyurem absorbed the Light Stone, transforming into White Kyurem. In The Plan, the Light Stone was depicted as being under Hilda's ownership.

Within Pocket Monsters BW: The Heroes of Fire and Thunder, specifically in Get the Legendary Pokémon!, Shin and Alder discovered the Light Stone at the Relic Castle. Alder's subsequent apparent betrayal, which was soon revealed to be a mere test of Shin's worthiness to be accepted by the Light Stone as the Hero of Fire, led to the stone's revival into Reshiram. Shin then departed with Reshiram to confront N and Zekrom at N's Castle.

Although residing at the Nacrene Gym, the Light Stone's presence there remained undisclosed until Finding Truth. After Team Plasma's theft of the Dark Stone, Brycen observed the Light Stone rolling from its safe towards Black, an event he interpreted as Black being the Hero of Truth. Consequently, Brycen concurred with Drayden to entrust the stone to Black, fulfilling this agreement in A Cold Reception after his defeat by Black in a Gym battle. During the Pokémon League tournament in True Friends, Black's intense anger stemming from Team Plasma's successful persuasion of Cheren to adopt their ideology triggered the Light Stone's awakening into Reshiram. Black then commanded Reshiram in a battle against N's Zekrom, ultimately securing victory. Following the conclusion of the battle and Team Plasma's defeat, Reshiram began its reversion into its stone form. Ghetsis, seeking retribution against Black for thwarting his schemes, employed Colress's Beheeyem to propel Black towards the condensing stone, resulting in Black being absorbed into it as well. The Light Stone subsequently drifted away before White could seize it.

In Flying Ship, the Light Stone was observed journeying towards the Unova region alongside N and Zekrom, a movement instigated by Kyurem's awakening. Fennel later dispatched White to locate the stone at the Entralink, where she successfully retrieved it. N, who had tracked the stone's path, informed White that it had intentionally settled in the Entralink for her discovery. Dream World unveiled that the interior of the Light Stone maintained a connection to the Pokémon Dream World. After White was briefly drawn into the Stone and reunited with Black, Reshiram emerged, simultaneously liberating both Black and White from its confines.

Within Giant Chasm, Kyurem wielded the DNA Splicers to compel Reshiram to once again revert into the Light Stone before assimilating it, thereby becoming White Kyurem. In The Final Battle, Kyurem detached itself from the Light Stone, which promptly transformed back into Reshiram.

An artifact also termed "Light Stone" played a significant role in the Pokémon the Series: Ruby and Sapphire episode A Different Kind of Misty!; however, this item bears no association with Reshiram's stone.

Title: Dark Stone

Making its debut in Generation V, the Dark Stone (Japanese: ダークストーン Dark Stone) functions as a Key Item. This artifact is recognized as one of the two Dragon Stones, serving as Zekrom's quiescent manifestation. Within the narrative of Pokémon White, the Dark Stone acts as a catalyst, calling forth Zekrom to aid the player in their confrontation with N and Reshiram at N's Castle. In the post-game content of Pokémon Black 2, it is responsible for invoking Zekrom atop Dragonspiral Tower.

Its presence extends to Learn with Pokémon: Typing Adventure. The Dark Stone was also featured in the films Black—Victini and Reshiram and White—Victini and Zekrom. Reflecting its nature as Zekrom's physical form, the Dark Stone possesses the ability to emit a blue luminescence, attributed to Zekrom's inherent electricity. Its initial discovery occurred with the Hero of Ideals, who possessed it as the dormant state of the exhausted dragon Pokémon. The hero subsequently concealed this stone beneath the Sword of the Vale. Regarding the animated features, in White—Victini and Zekrom, Ash successfully roused Zekrom from the Dark Stone to combat Damon's Reshiram; conversely, in Black—Victini and Reshiram, it was Damon who located and stirred the Dark Stone, intending to enlist Zekrom for his own agenda.

A distinct artifact, known as the "Golden Dark Stone," surfaced in Explorers of the Hero's Ruin!, having been secreted away within the Hero's Ruin itself. Cedric Juniper actively pursued its discovery. This particular item diverged from the standard Dark Stone by being gold in color rather than black, and it merely summoned Zekrom as opposed to representing its inherent dormant state.

A flashback sequence in The Plan featured the Dark Stone, illustrating N's act of awakening Zekrom from it at Dragonspiral Tower. Initially, the Dark Stone rested within the Desert Resort, where Clay and his mining crew unearthed it during the events of Lights, Camera...Action!. Following his initial examination, Clay grew concerned and summoned Lenora, leveraging her specialized knowledge of ancient artifacts for further investigation. Consequently, Lenora transported the stone to her museum. Subsequently, she enlisted Drayden's assistance in scrutinizing the stone, a collaboration that led them to the realization of its true identity as Zekrom.

Team Plasma, under the direction of the Shadow Triad, targeted the Dark Stone, orchestrating an assault on the Nacrene Gym in The Battle Within with the objective of acquiring this relic. While their initial raid did not yield the stone, Ghetsis successfully manipulated Black into disclosing the entryway to the clandestine chamber housing the Dark Stone. During Finding Truth, the stone reached N at Dragonspiral Tower, enabling him to awaken Zekrom from it and secure the Pokémon's allegiance, thereby establishing himself as the Hero of Ideals. At the Abyssal Ruins, subsequent to Ghetsis obtaining the DNA Splicers, he commanded Kyurem to assimilate Zekrom, transforming both Zekrom and N (who was mounted on its back) back into the Dark Stone. Despite Kyurem's absorption of the stone, N was shortly thereafter ejected from it. Within the Giant Chasm, Kyurem expelled the Dark Stone from its form, intending to absorb Reshiram as an alternative. Zekrom subsequently re-materialized from the Dark Stone a short time later.

Title: Xtransceiver

This entry discusses the Key Item found in the Generation V Pokémon games. For information regarding the segment at the conclusion of Japanese broadcasts of Pokémon the Series: Black & White, please refer to Professor Oak's Pokémon Live Caster. The Xtransceiver, known in Japanese as ライブキャスター (Live Caster), functions as a Key Item within Generation V. Initially featured in Pokémon Black and White, it has subsequently appeared in various spin-off media. According to the player's mother, the initial "X" in Xtransceiver is pronounced as "cross," leading to the full appellation "cross-transceiver." Players receive their Xtransceiver from their mother early in the game.

Within the gameplay context, this device serves as a potent communication instrument, enabling its wielder to establish contact with other Trainers; however, players are restricted to communicating solely with other players. At different junctures throughout the story, the player receives directives from other characters, propelling the narrative forward. The player's mother, Bianca, Cheren, and Professor Juniper all place calls to the player at various points during their adventure.

Leveraging the C-Gear's wireless functionality, the Xtransceiver facilitates communication with other real-life players in close proximity. Up to four players can engage in conversations through a radio-style system, where participants record an audio message that then transmits to the others after a brief delay. Before recording, the audio's pitch can be adjusted to be higher or lower using a dedicated slider. Should players possess a Nintendo DSi, Nintendo DSi XL, Nintendo 3DS, or Nintendo 3DS XL, they also gain the capability to visually interact via their console's integrated camera. Furthermore, players can overlay drawings onto their video using a pen tool on the touchscreen, or apply stickers, with the pen and sticker colors being modifiable using a dropper tool. The option to pause video exists, allowing a still frame to be maintained.

In Pokémon Black 2 and White 2, the Xtransceiver gains a calling feature akin to those of the Pokégear and PokéNav, empowering the player to contact in-game characters through the device. Nevertheless, only plot-significant characters, along with either Curtis or Yancy, are eligible for registration, though the latter two cannot be called with the same freedom as the main characters. The player's mom is pre-registered by default. Cheren, Bianca, and Professor Juniper become registered after the player triumphs over the Aspertia Gym. Hugh is added to the contacts subsequent to sailing from Virbank City to Castelia City. Curtis or Yancy can be registered in Nimbasa City once certain prerequisites are met.

Players can still interact with other real-life players locally, as in Black and White, but in Black 2 and White 2, the Xtransceiver also offered internet connectivity when accessed via the Global Terminal within a Pokémon Center. It is important to note that internet usage is not possible if the Xtransceiver is selected directly from the Bag.

Beyond its audio and video capabilities, the Xtransceiver offers two minigames: Balloon Catch and Balloon Ka-Boom! Both games feature balloons adorned with faces. If the player utilizes a Nintendo DSi, Nintendo DSi XL, Nintendo 3DS, or Nintendo 3DS XL, their actual face can be substituted for the generic images. These minigames are exclusively playable through local wireless communication.

In Balloon Catch, participants are tasked with collecting their designated balloons as they ascend on the touchscreen. Gameplay commences with players capturing three facial expressions: a neutral look, a smile, and a "troubled" expression. Players then have 60 seconds to tap as many balloons displaying their own face as possible. Tapping one's own balloon grants two points, while tapping an opponent's balloon results in a loss of one point. During the final 20 seconds, balloons appear with increased speed and frequency on the touchscreen, and all earned points are doubled. Initially, all balloons show neutral faces. Upon a player tapping their own balloon, a smiling face is displayed. Conversely, tapping an opponent's balloon causes the troubled expression to appear. The player amassing the highest point total emerges victorious. In the event of a tie, multiple players may be declared winners.

For Balloon Ka-Boom!, players inflate balloons by alternating taps on the screen's bottom corners until they burst. The objective is to pop as many balloons as possible to maximize one's score. Players begin by capturing photos of their face showing a neutral expression, a smile, and a "surprised" look. Each balloon will first display the player's own photos, then those of their opponents once the first balloon is popped. As balloons expand, their expressions cycle through the sequence in which the photos were taken. Players can observe their opponents' balloons positioned behind their own. During the initial 40 seconds of the game, 30 taps are required to pop a balloon. In the "Final Sprint," which comprises the remaining 20 seconds, only 18 taps are needed per balloon. Players receive five points for each balloon they pop. The player with the highest score total is declared the winner. Should a tie occur, multiple players can be recognized as victors.

In Pokémon the Series, Trip was the first individual depicted using an Xtransceiver. Its inaugural appearance was in "A Rival Battle for Club Champ!", where Ash employed a built-in Xtransceiver on Luxuria Town's Battle Club computer to contact Trip and request a battle. In "Minccino—Neat and Tidy!", Bianca was also shown to possess an Xtransceiver, which she used to reach Professor Juniper. Dawn was also revealed to own one in "Piplup, Pansage, and a Meeting of the Times!", temporarily using it instead of her Pokétch, though she reverted to her Pokétch before "Nightfall? Nightmares!". Additionally, the Japanese version of Pokémon the Series: Black & White features a segment titled Professor Oak's Pokémon Live Caster, which directly references the Xtransceiver's Japanese name.

The Xtransceiver debuted in the Pokémon Adventures manga in "Lights, Camera...Action!", where White was in the process of filming a commercial for it. The commercial shoot was disrupted by a wild Galvantula attack on the camera crew, but Black successfully defeated the Pokémon. In "Their First Gym Battle," Black presented an Xtransceiver to both Cheren and Bianca after their initial encounter with White. However, after Black and White departed, Bianca realized she had neglected to ask for Black's contact number.

Xtransceiver was introduced as an Item card within the Pokémon Trading Card Game during the English Black & White Series (corresponding to the Japanese BW Era). It first launched in the Japanese Red Collection, subsequently making its English debut in the Noble Victories expansion, with both iterations featuring artwork by Ryo Ueda. The Xtransceiver card allows the player to flip a coin, and if the result is heads, they may add a Supporter card from their deck to their hand, after which the deck is shuffled.

The Xtransceiver has been included in various officially licensed figures and merchandise. In Pokémon Black and White, the Xtransceivers are colored blue for male characters and pink for female characters. For Pokémon Black 2 and White 2, the colors shift to red for males and yellow for females. Hugh's Xtransceiver is notably light purple. The watchband component of the Xtransceiver is black for most users, with the exceptions of Bianca and Rosa, whose devices feature white watchbands.

In Pokémon Black and White, when preceded by an indefinite article, the item was referred to as "an Xtransceiver." In Pokémon Black 2 and White 2, these instances were corrected to "a Xtransceiver," aligning with the in-game pronunciation. In Pokémon Black 2 and White 2, players cannot utilize the calling function while aboard the Plasma Frigate, nor can characters located on the ship be called from external locations. The Holo Caster from Pokémon X and Y operates similarly to this item and shares a comparable Japanese designation.

Title: Dragon Gem

Originating in Generation V, the Dragon Gem (known as ドラゴンジュエル or Dragon Jewel in Japanese) functions as a held item. It is categorized among the Gems, though it becomes an unacquirable held item from Generation VI onward. Should a Pokémon carry this item, the power of its inaugural Dragon-type move is increased by 50%, after which the item is consumed. Furthermore, the move Fling will not succeed if the user is currently clutching a Dragon Gem. When possessed by a Pokémon, it also enhances the potency of the first Dragon-type move executed by 30%, becoming spent post-use. A Dragon Gem made an appearance in the episode "A Connoisseur's Revenge!", where a Poké Mart clerk presented it to Iris and her Axew.

Title: Normal Gem

The Normal Gem (Japanese: ノーマルジュエル Normal Jewel) is a held item first introduced in Generation V. It is one of the various Gems, and notably, it is the sole obtainable Gem from Generation VI onwards. A Pokémon holding this item will have the power of its first Normal-type move increased by 50%, being consumed after that use. However, its actual in-game effect from Generation V through Pokémon Scarlet and Violet grants a 30% power boost (approximated as a 5325/4096 multiplier) to the first Normal-type move used, which is also consumed immediately afterward. Fling will fail if the user is equipped with a Normal Gem. Conversely, the move Struggle will never deplete a Normal Gem. The item's damage increase and consumption are prevented if the Normal move's type is altered by an external effect, such as the ability Pixilate or the move Ion Deluge.

Title: Oval Charm

The Oval Charm (Japanese: まるいおまもり Round Charm), a Key Item, was initially introduced in Pokémon Black 2 and White 2. Its function is to augment the probability of locating an Egg at either a Pokémon Day Care or a Pokémon Nursery. This item amplifies the odds of an Egg being found, with the degree of increase contingent upon the base chance that would naturally exist. Crucially, it will not facilitate the appearance of an Egg if no Egg would ordinarily be possible. Although this item is unobtainable within Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, its accompanying description has been revised to reference picnics, which serve as the current method for Pokémon breeding.

Title: Shiny Charm

Known in Japanese as ひかるおまもり (Shining Charm), the Shiny Charm debuted as a Key Item in Pokémon Black 2 and White 2. Its primary function is to boost the likelihood of discovering Shiny Pokémon. When held within a trainer's Bag, the Shiny Charm enhances the odds of a Shiny Pokémon appearance by producing extra personality values in an attempt to roll a Shiny outcome. This bonus is cumulative with the majority of other modifiers that raise the Shiny encounter rate, including strategies like the Masuda method. However, this item offers no benefit for Pokémon acquired through NPC in-game trades, gift Pokémon, or Event Pokémon received through Mystery Gift.

Its influence extends to both wild encounters (encompassing static encounters like Legendary Pokémon) and Pokémon obtained through breeding. By generating two additional personality values, it effectively triples the probability of finding a Shiny Pokémon relative to the base rate; shifting it from 1/8192 to roughly 3/8192 (approximately 1/2731). With Generation VI, the standard Shiny Pokémon encounter rate improved from 1/8192 to 1/4096. Consequently, the Shiny Charm's pair of extra rerolls now elevate the Shiny Pokémon chance from 1/4096 to about 3/4096 (around 1/1365).

In Pokémon Sword and Shield, certain encounter types remain unaffected by the Shiny Charm, including scripted encounters like the Glimwood Tangle Impidimp. A bug in the breeding mechanic causes the initial personality value roll to be skipped whenever any rerolls are introduced (e.g., from the Masuda method or Shiny Charm). This means that for breeding with the Shiny Charm in isolation, it grants only 2 total personality value rolls, leading to an approximate 2/4096 (or 1/2048) chance of hatching a Shiny Pokémon. This represents a decrease from earlier generations where the initial roll alongside both rerolls were considered, yielding a Shiny rate of 3/4096 (approximately 1/1365). Nevertheless, in Generation VIII, because the Masuda method now offers an additional reroll compared to previous generations, the probability of hatching a Shiny Pokémon when employing both the Shiny Charm and Masuda method remains consistent, delivering 8 total rolls (rather than the intended 9) for a standard Shiny rate of 8/4096 (equivalent to 1/512) instead of the calculated 9/4096 (equivalent to 1/455).

Within Dynamax Adventures, Pokémon typically possess a 1/300 Shiny chance (this determination occurs upon activity completion, when choosing one Pokémon from the four encountered, contrasting with in-activity encounters where all Pokémon display as non-Shiny). Applying the Shiny Charm alters this chance to precisely 3/300 (which simplifies to 1/100). Exclusively in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, the Shiny Charm's influence is restricted solely to breeding. For Pokémon Legends: Arceus, the Shiny Charm grants three supplementary rerolls, bringing the Shiny Pokémon encounter probability to approximately 1/1024. In Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, certain encounter categories are not impacted by the Shiny Charm, specifically other non-Tera fixed symbol encounters (also known as Wanderers). Furthermore, Pokémon Legends: Z-A sees the Shiny Charm offering three additional rerolls.

Despite its sole effect on breeding Shiny Pokémon odds in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, the item's description inaccurately claims it will "increase the chance of finding a Shiny Pokémon in the wild". This textual inconsistency matches previous game entries (excluding Generation V), and the game's internal code unequivocally deactivates the Shiny Charm for alternative encounter methods. This strongly suggests the item operates as designed, with the verbatim reuse of its description being an oversight by developers.

Title: Colress Machine

Colress's invention, known as the Colress Machine (Japanese: アクロママシーン Achroma Machine), debuted as a Key Item in Generation V, where it was abbreviated as Colress MCHN in the games. This apparatus possesses the ability to rouse slumbering Pokémon against their will. Players wield this tool in Seaside Cave to awaken a wild Crustle, found blocking passage to the Plasma Frigate, which Team Plasma had anchored after freezing Opelucid City. Upon awakening, the Crustle aggressively attacks the player, initiating a battle. Each use of the Colress Machine causes it to shatter, thereby consuming the item. Following the player's acquisition of the Insect Badge, Colress himself deploys the device in a cutscene on Route 4, clearing the path by awakening a cluster of wild Crustle that were obstructing the road.

Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon disclose that "Colress Machine" serves as a collective designation Colress assigns to several of his contraptions. The initial pair of these machines are designated Colress Machine No. 1198 (Japanese: アクロママシーン １１９８号 Achroma Machine No. 1198) and Colress Machine No. 1199 (Japanese: アクロママシーン １１９９号 Achroma Machine No. 1199), also recognized as the N-Solarizer and N-Lunarizer. Colress collaborated with the Ultra Recon Squad to develop these, aiming to harness Necrozma's immense power. During Episode RR in Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, Colress deploys an additional device, Colress Machine No. 1102 (Japanese: アクロママシーン １１０２号 Achroma Machine No. 1102), to send Ghetsis back to his original dimension, thereby rescuing Lillie. Subsequent to the player's victory over Giovanni, Colress successfully utilizes this machine to reverse the alterations Team Rainbow Rocket made to Lusamine's mansion.

The genesis of the Colress Machine's development commenced two years prior to this storyline, but certain Team Plasma members disagreed with his research, viewing it as antithetical to their principles. Consequently, they covertly devised a countermeasure to negate the machine's effects on Pokémon, entrusting a memory card containing this vital data to a ten-year-old girl.

Its initial appearance occurred in Flying Type and Innocent Scientist, where Colress employed it to compel Genesect to assault Blake and Looker. During Unforgettable Memories, Colress documented positive outcomes from his invention's preliminary trial. Subsequently, in the chapter titled Colress Machine, Whitley's mother divulged the machine's capacity for Pokémon manipulation. Within Therian Forme III, Colress commandeered Zinzolin's Kyurem using the machine, directing it to board the Plasma Frigate. For the events of N Returns, Colress handed the device to Ghetsis, enabling him to bring Kyurem to confront N and Zekrom. At Giant Chasm, Colress was found to have reacquired the machine, which he then used to direct Kyurem's assault on the Unova Pokédex holders. Unexpectedly, Colress's command over Kyurem vanished, as Rood emerged, disclosing the activation of a counter-machine designed to nullify the Colress Machine's influence, thus precluding any further control over Kyurem. Following multiple unsuccessful efforts to regain Kyurem's obedience, Colress conceded defeat and discarded the device. The Final Battle unveiled that Colress had engineered yet another iteration, a longer-range Colress Machine, which his Beheeyem had been covertly utilizing to manipulate the Red Genesect during his conflict with Blake via relayed voice commands. Although Blake's Pokémon successfully incapacitated the machine, the Red Genesect remained under Colress's dominion, attributed to his personal bond with and upbringing of the creature.

In the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Colress Machine debuted as an Item card within the English Black & White Series (corresponding to the Japanese BW Era). Initially launched within the Plasma Storm expansion, it later saw reprints in the Japanese Team Plasma's Powered Half Deck and the Team Plasma Battle Gift Set Deck Kit. Upon its activation by a player, they are permitted to scour their deck for a Plasma Energy card and affix it to any of their Team Plasma Pokémon. Subsequently, the player must then shuffle their deck.

Title: Dropped Item

A Key Item first appearing in Generation V, the Dropped Item (Japanese: わすれもの, meaning Forgotten Item) comes in two distinct versions. These include a red variant that is the property of Curtis, and a yellow variant belonging to Yancy. Fundamentally, the Dropped Item is an Xtransceiver, which is specifically owned by Curtis if the player character is female, or by Yancy if the player is male. Upon its retrieval by the player, the rightful owner will use their own Xtransceiver to initiate a call, clarifying the circumstances to the player. After accumulating ten calls from either Curtis or Yancy, they finally manage to secure adequate time off from their commitments as an idol. This enables them to recover their Xtransceiver from the player in front of the Rondez-View Ferris Wheel in Nimbasa City, at which point the player can hand over the device and register their contact information.

Title: Reveal Glass

Known in Japanese as うつしかがみ (Reflecting Mirror), the Reveal Glass is a pivotal Key Item introduced in the games Pokémon Black 2 and White 2. Its fundamental purpose is to enable the Legendary Pokémon recognized as the forces of nature to transition between their Incarnate and Therian Formes.
