When activated from the player's Bag, targeting Tornadus, Thundurus, Landorus, or Enamorus, it facilitates the immediate alteration of that Pokémon's current form.

The Reveal Glass made its animated debut in "Meloetta and the Undersea Temple!," where members of Team Rocket employed Meloetta and its recorded melody to breach the seal protecting the item within the Abyssal Ruins. Subsequently, Giovanni utilized the Glass to summon and then transform the forces of nature into their Therian Formes, intending to subjugate the Unova region. In the ensuing episode, "Unova's Survival Crisis!," after Ash's Pikachu liberated Meloetta and propelled the Team Rocket members away with a supercharged Electro Ball—a result of excess energy absorbed from Thundurus—Giovanni was enveloped by a beam of light emanating from the Reveal Glass. Overpowered by its immense energy, he coerced the Legendary Pokémon into a destructive rampage across the region. Giovanni was eventually freed from the item's influence when Jessie, James, and Meowth physically removed him from the mystical spell circle he occupied. Following Team Rocket's retreat, Ridley then took possession of the Reveal Glass, using it alongside Meloetta's song to pacify the rampaging Legendary creatures. Afterward, the Reveal Glass was re-sealed.

In the manga, the item's existence was initially foreshadowed in "The Power of Dreams," where Hood Man deduced that a special mirror would be necessary to unlock the alternate forms of the forces of nature. Team Plasma later acquired the Reveal Glass at some point prior to "Legendary Tornadus," with a contingent of their Grunts deploying it remotely on Tornadus during an attempt to capture Giallo. Its first substantive appearance in the manga was in "Colress Machine," where it was shown to be under Colress's command. He used it to shift Thundurus and Landorus into their Therian Formes to battle Blake. Ultimately, in "The Final Battle," Keldeo shattered the Reveal Glass while aiming a strike at Colress, causing the forces of nature to revert to their Incarnate Formes and enabling the Swords of Justice to triumph over them.

Visually, the Reveal Glass incorporates the distinct colors of the three original forces of nature: its left portion is blue, representing Thundurus; the right side is green, signifying Tornadus; and its upper segment is orange, akin to Landorus. With the introduction of Enamorus in Pokémon Legends: Arceus, it was disclosed that the Reveal Glass previously featured a handle, rendered in pink to match Enamorus.

Regarding its mechanics in Generation V games, the Reveal Glass becomes functional only after Landorus has been transferred from Pokémon Dream Radar. However, since this transfer is a prerequisite for obtaining the item itself, this specific behavioral constraint can only be observed through illicit means like cheating. The concept behind the Reveal Glass potentially draws inspiration from the Demon Detecting Mirror, a mythical object in Chinese folklore capable of unveiling the true identities of yaoguai. Its aesthetic design is rooted in the color palettes of Tornadus, Thundurus, and Landorus, with the design updated in Legends: Arceus to incorporate Enamorus's colors as well.

Title: Weakness Policy

A consumable held item, the Weakness Policy (じゃくてんほけん Weakness Insurance in Japanese) made its debut in Generation VI. Upon being struck by a supereffective attack, this item is consumed, significantly boosting the holder's Attack and Special Attack stats by two stages apiece. However, a Pokémon possessing the Contrary Ability will experience a reduction of two stages to both its Attack and Special Attack, unless the supereffective blow originates from an adversary with Mold Breaker. The Policy will not activate if its holder has already reached the maximum of +6 stages for both Attack and Special Attack. Conversely, if the holder's Ability is Contrary, activation is prevented when it is already at the minimum of -6 stages for both Attack and Special Attack. Furthermore, activation is bypassed if the Pokémon is guarded against or immune to the supereffective attack (for example, via an Ability like Telepathy). Moves dealing fixed damage, including Seismic Toss or Mirror Coat, are incapable of triggering the Weakness Policy's effect, even when their type would otherwise be supereffective against the Pokémon. Should a single supereffective attack strike several Pokémon simultaneously, each carrying a Weakness Policy, every affected Pokémon will consume its respective Policy prior to any item transfers occurring via Symbiosis (even other Weakness Policies).

Within Pokémon UNITE, the Weakness Policy operates as a held item, bestowing an Attack boost when the holder sustains damage. This buff endures for 3 seconds and can accumulate up to 4 times. Its magnitude is 2% at Grade 1, 2.5% at Grade 10, and 3% at Grade 20. Additionally, this item contributes to increased HP and Attack stats: 70 HP and 0 Attack at Grade 1, escalating to 210 HP and 15 Attack at Grade 30.

In the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Weakness Policy appeared as a Pokémon Tool card during the Japanese XY Era. Its initial release occurred in the Japanese Gaia Volcano expansion, followed by its English premiere in the Primal Clash expansion, where it was available as both a Regular and a Secret card. When this Pokémon Tool card is affixed to a Pokémon, that Pokémon gains immunity to Weakness.

A dual memo set, styled after both the Weakness Policy and the Blunder Policy, became available on December 12, 2024, exclusively at the Pokémon Center. The Weakness Policy also graces the artwork of the Item Mania card sleeves.

Title: Assault Vest

Introduced in Generation VI, the Assault Vest (Japanese: とつげきチョッキ Assault Vest) is a held item that substantially boosts a Pokémon's Special Defense by 50%. However, this enhancement comes with a significant drawback: it prohibits the wearer from selecting any status moves, with the sole exception of Me First.

This item's prohibition specifically targets the *selection* of status moves, not their *execution*. Consequently, a Pokémon equipped with an Assault Vest can still be compelled to perform status moves through effects like Instruct or Dancer. Furthermore, should a Pokémon obtain an Assault Vest after already having chosen a status move but before performing it – for example, via Trick from a Pokémon with the Klutz Ability – the chosen move will still be carried out successfully during that turn.

Should its holder become restricted to only status moves (e.g., due to Encore, Torment, or simply lacking non-status moves with PP), it will be compelled to resort to Struggle. Dynamax transformations do not override the item's properties, meaning it still inhibits the use of Max Guard. With the removal of Struggle in Pokémon Legends: Z-A, stemming from moves no longer consuming PP, a Pokémon holding an Assault Vest that exclusively knows status moves will be rendered incapable of executing any actions, limited instead to following its Trainer or being withdrawn from battle.

Within Pokémon UNITE, the Assault Vest functions as a held item that provides a shield against incoming Special Attack damage once the user disengages from combat, alongside boosting their HP and Special Defense stats. After remaining out of combat for 5 seconds, the user gains a temporary shield amounting to 9%, 12%, or 15% of their maximum HP, corresponding to Grades 1, 10, and 20 respectively; this shielding effect does not activate if a pre-existing Assault Vest shield is active. At Grade 1, it augments HP by 18 and Special Defense by 0, while at Grade 30, these increases become 270 for HP and 51 for Special Defense.

In the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Assault Vest made its debut as a Pokémon Tool card during the Japanese XY BREAK Era. Its initial release occurred within the Japanese Red Flash expansion, preceding its English introduction in the BREAKthrough expansion. When this Pokémon Tool card is affixed to a Pokémon, any damage dealt to that Pokémon by attacks originating from an opponent's Pokémon possessing any Special Energy is mitigated by 40 points, following the application of Weakness and Resistance.

Title: Pixie Plate
An item designed to be held by a Pokémon, the Pixie Plate (known in Japanese as せいれいプレート, or Spirit Plate) made its debut in Generation VI. Within Pokémon Legends: Arceus, it functions as a Key Item. This artifact is counted among the Plates, all intrinsically linked to the legendary Pokémon Arceus. When held, it confers a 20% boost to the damage output of the holder's Fairy-type attacks. Should the holder unleash Judgment, that move's type transforms into Fairy. Arceus, when equipped with the Pixie Plate, will have its Multitype ability activate, altering its form to its Fairy type. Items cannot be transferred to or removed from an Arceus via item-manipulating effects like Trick or Fling when it is holding a Plate. Furthermore, its Multitype ability causes Arceus to adopt its Fairy-type form while holding this specific item. In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, trainers can utilize the Pixie Plate directly from their satchel on Arceus, thereby changing Arceus to the Fairy type. An inscription can be found on the Pixie Plate, which reads: "The Original One is in all things. The Original One is nowhere at all." This item, along with the 17 other Plates corresponding to different types, was depicted in The Arceus Chronicles (Part 4) as being possessed by Arceus. Notably, the Pixie Plate stands as the sole Plate available for acquisition in Pokémon Sword and Shield, a distinction likely attributed to it being the only obtainable held item in those games at the time capable of enhancing Fairy-type move power.

Title: Ability Capsule

This medicinal item, known in Japanese as とくせいカプセル Ability Capsule, was first introduced in Generation VI. Its primary function is to alter a Pokémon's Ability. When activated from the Bag, it facilitates a direct swap between a Pokémon's two standard Abilities. Usage is strictly limited to outside of combat situations, and the capsule is consumed upon a single application. Only Pokémon species possessing a pair of standard Abilities are eligible for its effect. For instance, one could toggle a Pidgey's Ability between Keen Eye and Tangled Feet; conversely, it has no impact on a Pikachu, whose sole standard Ability is Static. Furthermore, this item cannot grant or remove a species' Hidden Ability. Notably, the Ability Capsule is powerless to alter Zygarde's Ability between Aura Break and Power Construct, nor can it modify Greninja's between Torrent and Battle Bond. Similarly, Rockruff's Own Tempo Ability is unaffected by the capsule, although its Keen Eye and Vital Spirit Abilities remain swappable. The altered Ability persists through evolution, with the specific Ability slot chosen via the Ability Capsule being maintained even if the evolved form possesses distinct standard Abilities. Consider this example: a Lillipup with Pickup would typically evolve into a Herdier (and then Stoutland) with Sand Rush. However, if that Lillipup's Ability is first modified to Vital Spirit using an Ability Capsule, its subsequent evolution will instead manifest Intimidate. Finally, the move Fling will not succeed if the executing Pokémon is clutching an Ability Capsule.

Title: Luminous Moss

Known as Luminous Moss (Japanese: ひかりごけ Luminous Moss), this held item first appeared in Generation VI. Its purpose is to augment the holder's Special Defense stat when struck by a damaging Water-type attack. Upon being hit by such a move, the Luminous Moss is expended, causing the Pokémon's Special Defense to rise by one stage. The item's effect will not activate if the holder's Special Defense has already reached its maximum of +6 stat stages. However, should the Pokémon possess the Contrary Ability, the Luminous Moss will instead not activate if its Special Defense is at the minimum of -6 stat stages. Furthermore, activation is prevented if the holder is shielded from or otherwise immune to the Water-type move, such as through Abilities like Water Absorb or Wonder Guard. If multiple Pokémon, each equipped with an Absorb Bulb or Luminous Moss, are simultaneously affected by a Water-type move, every hit Pokémon will consume its respective item before the Symbiosis Ability can transfer any item, including these specific ones.

Title: Snowball

An item known as Snowball (Japanese: ゆきだま Snowball) made its debut in Generation VI. Should the Pokémon holding it endure an Ice-type damaging move, the Snowball is used up, and its Attack stat rises by one stage. This effect will not occur if the holder has already reached +6 Attack stat stages; however, if the Pokémon's Ability is Contrary, the item similarly won't activate if the holder is at -6 Attack stat stages. Furthermore, activation is prevented if the Pokémon is protected from or otherwise unaffected by the incoming Ice-type move, such as through Abilities like Telepathy or Wonder Guard. When an Ice-type move strikes several Pokémon simultaneously, each carrying a Snowball, every affected Pokémon will consume its own Snowball before Symbiosis has an opportunity to transfer any items, even other Snowballs. Within Pokémon Legends: Arceus, players carrying Snowballs in their satchel have the option to hurl them at wild Pokémon, causing a temporary stun effect. A unique mechanic in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire involves the Snowball: if a nicknamed Regice holds one while in the party alongside Regirock and Registeel, it facilitates the appearance of Regigigas in Island Cave. This specific event, however, is precluded during nighttime hours or prior to the defeat or capture of Groudon/Kyogre.

Title: Safety Goggles

Debuting in Generation VI, the Safety Goggles (Japanese: ぼうじんゴーグル Dustproof Goggles) functions as a distinct held item. It confers upon its holder immunity to all powder and spore moves. Furthermore, it protects the Pokémon from damage originating from specific weather conditions, namely hail and sandstorm. This item also prevents the activation of the Ability Effect Spore. In the episode "Mounting an Electrifying Charge!", Ash and Charjabug wore Safety Goggles provided by Sophocles during the desert portion of the Charjabug race, specifically to avoid being blinded by the sandstorm present in that segment.

Title: Amaze Mulch

Known in Japan as とんでもこやし (Outrageous Fertilizer), the Amaze Mulch made its debut as an item in the sixth generation. This particular variety of Mulch is designed to enhance Berry cultivation, specifically by boosting harvest quantities, encouraging mutations, and accelerating soil desiccation for Berry plants. When utilized on a Berry patch, its impact includes an expedited drying of the ground, an increment of 2 Berries to the final yield, and a heightened probability of Berry mutations. These properties encapsulate the benefits offered by Rich Mulch, Surprise Mulch, and Boost Mulch all in one. Beyond its potential sale value, Amaze Mulch offers no other functional use.

Title: Gengarite

Introduced in Generation VI, the Gengarite (Japanese: ゲンガナイト Ganganite) functions as a specific held item. This particular Mega Stone enables Gengar's transformation into its Mega Evolved form, Mega Gengar. A Gengar holding this item, or when it resides in the player's Bag, can achieve Mega Evolution into Mega Gengar, provided its Trainer possesses a Key Stone. It is crucial to note that a Trainer is limited to Mega Evolving only one Pokémon per battle, meaning only a single Mega-Evolved Pokémon can exist for them at any moment. Being a Mega Stone, the Gengarite remains immune to various item-negating effects, including Embargo and the Ability Klutz. Similarly, item-manipulating moves like Trick and Fling are incapable of transferring or taking the Gengarite from Gengar. When Gengar holds this item during a Pokémon Contest Spectacular, it can Mega Evolve into Mega Gengar for the duration of the Talent Round upon performing a Spectacular Talent, again requiring its Trainer to possess a Key Stone.

Within the game Pokémon Shuffle, the Gengarite facilitates the Mega Evolution of Gengar, Spooky Gengar, and Shiny Gengar for the player. Players acquire the Gengarite in Pokémon Shuffle by vanquishing Mega Gengar in Stage 135, located within Castle Noapte. A specific Gengarite, associated with Gurkinn's Gengar, made its initial appearance in Changing Gengar. Subsequently, both Gengar and its corresponding Mega Stone were transferred to X. Additionally, the Gengarite has been depicted in numerous pieces of officially licensed merchandise.

Title: Gardevoirite

This held item, known in Japanese as サーナイトナイト (Sirnightnite), made its debut in Generation VI of the Pokémon series. It functions as the specific Mega Stone enabling Gardevoir to undergo Mega Evolution and transform into Mega Gardevoir. When a Gardevoir holds this item, it can attain its Mega-Evolved form, Mega Gardevoir, provided its Trainer possesses a Key Stone. Trainers are restricted to Mega Evolving only one Pokémon per battle, meaning only one Mega-Evolved Pokémon can be active at any single moment. Being a Mega Stone, the Gardevoirite remains immune to effects that nullify held items, such as Embargo or the Klutz Ability. Furthermore, item-swapping or item-throwing moves like Trick and Fling are incapable of transferring or dislodging a Gardevoirite from a Gardevoir. In the context of a Pokémon Contest Spectacular, a Gardevoir holding the Gardevoirite can also Mega Evolve into Mega Gardevoir, performing a Spectacular Talent, again requiring its Trainer to have a Key Stone. This Mega Evolution state persists for Gardevoir throughout the duration of the Talent Round.

Within the game Pokémon Shuffle, this item grants players the ability to Mega Evolve both Gardevoir and Shiny Gardevoir. Players in Pokémon Shuffle can acquire the Gardevoirite either by successfully completing the competitive stage featured in Week 21 of the event cycle or by achieving Trainer rank 20. It was also awarded as a prize during the Mega Gardevoir competitive stage, which ran from December 5 to 12, 2017, on both 3DS and Mobile platforms, during the game's active service period (featuring a Shiny version boss).

The Gardevoirite facilitates specific Mega Evolutions. Notably, Diantha's Gardevoir's Gardevoirite first made its appearance in the episode The Bonds of Evolution!. Her Gardevoir, known as the Embrace Pokémon, was depicted wearing its Mega Stone within a necklace around its neck. Similarly, in Volcanion and the Mechanical Marvel, Kimia's Shiny Gardevoir sported a Gardevoirite in an identical necklace around its neck, mirroring Diantha's Gardevoir's accessory. Another instance of a Gardevoirite was observed in Another Day with Gardevoir, where Yugo's Gardevoir secured it using a hair clip. Diantha herself is an owner of a Gardevoirite, first seen in the episode Chespin Sows the Seeds of Change. Her personal Gardevoir dons it as a head ornament. Within PS616, the elder of Meteor Village presented Sapphire with both a Gardevoirite and a Galladite, noting the compatibility between her Gallade and Ruby's Gardevoir. Subsequently, Ruby and Sapphire each utilized these Mega Stones to Mega Evolve their respective Pokémon, Rara and Kirly, during a short engagement. This item has also been prominently featured across various officially licensed merchandise. Notably, the Gardevoirite holds the distinction of being the inaugural Mega Stone to be shown in the main animated series, separate from the Mega Evolution Specials.

Title: Ampharosite

This held item, known in Japanese as デンリュウナイト (Denryunite), made its debut in Generation VI. It functions as the specific Mega Stone enabling Ampharos's transformation into its Mega Evolved form, Mega Ampharos. When an Ampharos carries this item, it can attain its Mega Ampharos state, provided its Trainer possesses a Key Stone. During any given battle, a Trainer is restricted to Mega Evolving a single Pokémon at a time. Being a Mega Stone, it remains immune to item-disabling effects like Embargo and Klutz. Furthermore, item-swapping moves such as Trick and Fling are incapable of transferring the Ampharosite to or from Ampharos. In a Pokémon Contest Spectacular, if Ampharos holds this item and its Trainer has a Key Stone, it will Mega Evolve into Mega Ampharos upon executing a Spectacular Talent. This Mega Evolution persists until the conclusion of that specific Talent Round. Within the game Pokémon Shuffle, players can utilize the Ampharosite to Mega Evolve Ampharos. Acquisition of the Ampharosite in Pokémon Shuffle occurs by overcoming Mega Ampharos on Stage 105, located within the Silbern Museum.

In the Mega Evolution Special II flashback, an unrefined Ampharosite was depicted, discovered by Lysandre prior to Alain's attempt to claim it. Alain then battled Lysandre for ownership, though he was defeated. Later, in Mega Evolution - Ult, Emma employed an Ampharosite to facilitate the Mega Evolution of her Ampharos. Taichi, from Pocket Monsters XY: The Legend of the Pokémon Dragon King, possesses an Ampharosite, which grants his Ampharos the ability to become Mega Ampharos. This item has also appeared across various pieces of officially sanctioned merchandise.

Title: Venusaurite

Introduced in Generation VI, the Venusaurite (Japanese: フシギバナイト Fushigibanite) functions as a specific type of held item. It serves as the Mega Stone that enables Venusaur to undergo Mega Evolution into Mega Venusaur. Should a Venusaur possess this item (or if it is present within the player's Bag), the Venusaurite permits that Venusaur to Mega Evolve into Mega Venusaur, contingent upon its Trainer also possessing a Key Stone. However, a Trainer is limited to Mega Evolving only one Pokémon per battle, meaning only one Mega-Evolved Pokémon can be active at any given moment. As a Mega Stone, it remains unaffected by item-negating effects, such as Embargo and Klutz. Furthermore, item-manipulating effects, including Trick and Fling, are incapable of either giving a Venusaurite to Venusaur or removing one from it.

In a Pokémon Contest Spectacular, a Venusaur holding a Venusaurite can Mega Evolve into Mega Venusaur when performing a Spectacular Talent, provided its Trainer also has a Key Stone. Venusaur will maintain its Mega Venusaur form until the conclusion of the Talent Round.

Within the game Pokémon Shuffle, the Venusaurite grants the player the ability to Mega Evolve Venusaur. Players can obtain it by successfully completing Mission Card 15. Additionally, the Venusaurite was offered as a prize during the Mega Venusaur competitive stage when the game's live service was active. The Venusaurite enables the following Mega Evolutions: A Trainer's Venusaurite was featured in Mega Evolution Special IV, during the ten-battle gauntlet arranged by Lysandre for Alain's training. The Venusaurite itself is physically located on a band positioned around the flower on Venusaur's back. A Venusaurite made an appearance during Gurkinn's explanation of Mega Evolution in An Adventure of Mega Proportions!. Red acquired a Venusaurite sometime prior to Blastoise Transforms, subsequently utilizing it to Mega Evolve Saur. This Mega Stone has also been showcased in various officially licensed merchandise. Notably, in Pokémon Legends: Z-A, the Venusaurite is unique among Mega Stones related to a first partner Pokémon for not being obtainable at the Stone Emporium.

Title: Charizardite X

Known in Japanese as リザードナイトX (Lizardonite X), the Charizardite X is a specialized held item first introduced in Generation VI. This Mega Stone facilitates Charizard's transformation into Mega Charizard X. When Charizard holds it (or if it resides in the player's Bag), this item enables Charizard to undergo Mega Evolution into Mega Charizard X, provided its Trainer possesses a Key Stone.

Trainers are limited to Mega Evolving only one Pokémon per battle, meaning only one Mega-Evolved Pokémon can exist at any given moment. Being a Mega Stone, it remains unaffected by effects that negate items, such as Embargo and Klutz. Similarly, item-manipulating effects like Trick and Fling cannot transfer or remove a Charizardite X from a Charizard. For Pokémon Contest Spectaculars, when held by Charizard, the Charizardite X allows it to Mega Evolve into Mega Charizard X while performing a Spectacular Talent, again contingent on the Trainer having a Key Stone. Charizard maintains its Mega Charizard X form until the conclusion of that Talent Round.

Within Pokémon Shuffle, the Charizardite X grants players the ability to Mega Evolve both Charizard and Shiny Charizard into Mega Charizard X. Players can acquire this item upon achieving Trainer rank 26. It is also made available during Week 23 of the recurring event rotation. Furthermore, the Charizardite X was a reward during the Mega Charizard X competitive stage, which ran from January 2 to 9, 2018, across both 3DS and Mobile platforms, notably featuring a Shiny version of the boss.

In the anime, Alain once possessed a Charizardite X, which he received from Lysandre alongside his Mega Ring. He fastened it around his Charizard's neck. Upon discerning Lysandre's true agenda and acknowledging his past errors, Alain discarded the Charizardite X prior to the episode Till We Compete Again!, vowing a fresh start. However, in Curtain Up! Fight the Fights!, it was revealed that Alain had obtained another Charizardite X.

In File 2: Cubone, Mr. Fuji presented Red with a Mega Stone and a Key Stone, without disclosing their true nature, merely suggesting Red would soon discover their purpose. Later, in File 4: Charizard, during Red's confrontation with Mewtwo, one of these stones was identified as a Charizardite X originating from Kalos. This stone, combined with the Key Stone, empowered Red's Charizard to Mega Evolve into Mega Charizard X, enabling it to sufficiently weaken Mewtwo for capture.

The manga "Pocket Monsters XY: The Legend of the Pokémon Dragon King" features Akira as the owner of both a Charizardite X and a Charizardite Y. In "Chesnaught Protects," X's Charmeleon, Salamè, was discovered to conceal a Charizardite X within his tail flame, explaining his peculiar habit of grasping his tail. After Salamè's evolution into Charizard, he gained the capacity to utilize the stone for Mega Evolution into Mega Charizard X.

The Charizardite X has also been represented across various pieces of officially licensed merchandise.

Title: Blastoisinite

Introduced in Generation VI, the Blastoisinite (Japanese: カメックスナイト Kamexnite) functions as a specific type of held item. It serves as the particular Mega Stone that enables Blastoise to transform into Mega Blastoise. When a Blastoise holds a Blastoisinite, or if the item resides within the player's Bag, it can Mega Evolve into Mega Blastoise, provided its Trainer possesses a Key Stone. Trainers are restricted to Mega Evolving only one Pokémon per battle, meaning only one Mega-Evolved Pokémon can be active at any given moment.

As a Mega Stone, the Blastoisinite remains unaffected by item-negating effects, such as Embargo and Klutz. Similarly, item-manipulating effects like Trick and Fling are unable to transfer a Blastoisinite to or from a Blastoise. In Pokémon Contest Spectaculars, a Blastoise holding this item can also Mega Evolve into Mega Blastoise when performing a Spectacular Talent, again requiring its Trainer to have a Key Stone. This Mega Blastoise form persists until the conclusion of the Talent Round.

Within the game Pokémon Shuffle, the Blastoisinite grants players the capability to Mega Evolve Blastoise. Players can acquire the Blastoisinite by completing Mission Card 12. Furthermore, during Pokémon Shuffle's live service period, the Blastoisinite was offered as a reward in the Mega Blastoise competitive stage.

Fundamentally, the Blastoisinite facilitates the following Mega Evolutions: A Blastoisinite owned by Siebold made an appearance in Mega Evolution Special I, first revealed during his confrontation with Alain. Blastoise wears its Blastoisinite positioned around its head. Green was depicted possessing a Blastoisinite in The Discovery, which she utilized to Mega Evolve her Blastoise during her match against Elaine. Green had acquired a Blastoisinite prior to the events of Blastoise Transforms, employing it to Mega Evolve Blasty. This item has also been showcased across various lines of officially licensed merchandise.

Title: Mewtwonite X

Known in Japanese as ミュウツナイトX Mewtwonite X, this held item debuted in Generation VI. Its primary function is as a Mega Stone, enabling Mewtwo's transformation into Mega Mewtwo X. For Mewtwo to undergo this transformation into Mega Mewtwo X, it must be holding the Mewtwonite X, or the item must be present in the player's Bag, provided its Trainer also possesses a Key Stone. It's important to note the general rule: a Trainer is restricted to Mega Evolving only one Pokémon per battle, meaning only one Mega-Evolved Pokémon can exist at any given moment. Being a Mega Stone, the Mewtwonite X possesses immunity to effects that negate items, like the moves Embargo or the ability Klutz. Furthermore, item-altering effects, such as the moves Trick and Fling, are incapable of either bestowing or confiscating a Mewtwonite X from Mewtwo.

In the context of a Pokémon Contest Spectacular, if Mewtwo holds this item and its Trainer has a Key Stone, it can Mega Evolve into Mega Mewtwo X upon performing a Spectacular Talent. This Mega Evolution persists until the conclusion of the Talent Round.

Within the game Pokémon Shuffle, the Mewtwonite X grants players the ability to Mega Evolve both Mewtwo and Shiny Mewtwo. Acquisition in Pokémon Shuffle involves triumphing over Mega Mewtwo on Stage 350, situated in the Desert Umbra. During Pokémon Shuffle's live service period, from August 29 to September 5, 2017, the Mewtwonite X was also offered as a reward. This occurred during the Mega Mewtwo X competitive stage (featuring a Shiny boss version) on both 3DS and Mobile platforms.

The Mewtwonite X enables the following Mega Evolutions: A pair of Mega Stones, specifically a Mewtwonite X and a Mewtwonite Y, were observed in Green's possession within the episode The Discovery. Following her realization that Elaine had captured Mewtwo prior to her own attempt, and subsequently suffering a defeat in battle by Elaine, Green then bestowed these Mega Stones upon her. Later, in Mewtwo Angered, Blaine was shown to own both a Mewtwonite X and a Mewtwonite Y, which he utilizes to facilitate Mewtwo's Mega Evolution. When Mewtwo's Mega Evolution into Mega Mewtwo Y was disrupted during a confrontation with Lysandre and Zygarde, Blaine promptly exchanged its held Mega Stone. This action permitted Mewtwo to transition into Mega Mewtwo X, thereby continuing the engagement. Blaine subsequently clarified that the profound connection he shares with Mewtwo empowers it to Mega Evolve multiple times within a single battle.

Finally, the Mewtwonite X has been represented across various officially licensed merchandise.

Title: Mewtwonite Y

Debuting in Generation VI, the Mewtwonite Y (Japanese: ミュウツナイトY Mewtwonite Y) functions as a held item. This particular Mega Stone's sole purpose is to facilitate Mewtwo's Mega Evolution into Mega Mewtwo Y. When Mewtwo bears the Mewtwonite Y as its held item (or if the item is simply present in the player's Bag), it gains the capacity to Mega Evolve into Mega Mewtwo Y, provided its Trainer possesses a Key Stone. Trainers are, however, restricted to a single Mega Evolution per battle, meaning only one Mega-Evolved Pokémon can exist at any given moment. Consistent with other Mega Stones, the Mewtwonite Y is immune to item-negating effects like Embargo and Klutz. Furthermore, item-manipulating moves such as Trick and Fling are powerless to transfer or remove a Mewtwonite Y from a Mewtwo.

In the context of a Pokémon Contest Spectacular, if a Mewtwo holds the Mewtwonite Y and its Trainer possesses a Key Stone, the item permits the Pokémon to Mega Evolve into Mega Mewtwo Y while performing a Spectacular Talent. This transformation to Mega Mewtwo Y persists throughout the duration of the Talent Round.

Its function extends to Pokémon Shuffle, where it enables players to Mega Evolve Mewtwo. Acquisition of the Mewtwonite Y in Pokémon Shuffle occurs upon defeating Mega Mewtwo within Stage 150, located in Jungle Verde.

The Mewtwonite Y enables the following Mega Evolutions: Featured in The Discovery were a Mewtwonite X and a Mewtwonite Y, both possessed by Green. Following her realization that Elaine had already captured Mewtwo and her subsequent defeat in battle by Elaine, Green presented both Mega Stones to her. The storyline of Mewtwo Angered unveiled Blaine as the owner of both a Mewtwonite X and a Mewtwonite Y, utilizing them to Mega Evolve Mewtwo. Once Mewtwo's Mega Evolution into Mega Mewtwo Y reverted during a confrontation with Lysandre and Zygarde, Blaine promptly exchanged its held Mega Stone, thereby enabling Mewtwo to Mega Evolve into Mega Mewtwo X and persist in the battle. Blaine subsequently clarified that owing to his unique bond with Mewtwo, the Pokémon possessed the capability to Mega Evolve multiple times within a single battle.

Title: Blazikenite

Known in Japan as バシャーモナイト (Bursyamonite), the Blazikenite is a held item that made its debut in Generation VI. This specific Mega Stone empowers Blaziken to undergo Mega Evolution, thereby transforming into Mega Blaziken. If a Blaziken holds this item, it can attain its Mega Blaziken form, provided its Trainer possesses a Key Stone. Trainers, however, are restricted to Mega Evolving only one Pokémon per battle, meaning a single Mega-Evolved Pokémon can be active at any given moment.

As a Mega Stone, the Blazikenite remains impervious to item-negating effects, such as the move Embargo or the Klutz Ability. Furthermore, item-manipulating effects like Trick and Fling are unable to transfer or remove a Blazikenite from a Blaziken. In the context of a Pokémon Contest Spectacular, if a Blaziken holds the Blazikenite and its Trainer has a Key Stone, the item permits it to Mega Evolve into Mega Blaziken specifically when showcasing a Spectacular Talent. This Mega Evolution persists until the Talent Round concludes.

In the realm of Pokémon Shuffle, the Blazikenite grants players the ability to Mega Evolve Blaziken. Players can acquire this item by completing Mission Card 17. Additionally, during the game's live service period, the Blazikenite was available as a prize in the Mega Blaziken competitive stage. The Blazikenite is instrumental for the following Mega Evolution transformations: Within the pages of 'Claydol Unleashes,' it was revealed that Steven had located a Blazikenite for Sapphire's Blaziken, Chic. Finally, the Blazikenite has made appearances in various officially licensed merchandise.

Title: Medichamite

This held item, known in Japanese as チャーレムナイト (Charemnite), first appeared in Generation VI. It functions as the specific Mega Stone enabling Medicham's Mega Evolution into Mega Medicham. When a Medicham is holding this item, it can undertake this transformation into Mega Medicham, provided its Trainer possesses a Key Stone. Trainers are restricted to Mega Evolving only one Pokémon per battle, meaning only one Mega-Evolved Pokémon can be active at any time. Being a Mega Stone, it remains impervious to effects that negate items, such as the move Embargo or the Ability Klutz. Furthermore, item-manipulation tactics like Trick or Fling are incapable of transferring the Medichamite to or from a Medicham. In the context of a Pokémon Contest Spectacular, a Medicham holding the Medichamite can still Mega Evolve into Mega Medicham for a Spectacular Talent if its Trainer possesses a Key Stone. This transformation persists until the conclusion of the Talent Round. Its function extends to Pokémon Shuffle, where it enables the player to Mega Evolve their Medicham. Players can acquire the Medichamite by besting Mega Medicham in Stage 240, located within Albens Town. The Medichamite facilitates this specific Mega Evolution. Additionally, the Medichamite has appeared across various officially licensed merchandise products.

Title: Houndoominite

Originating in Generation VI, the Houndoominite (known in Japanese as ヘルガナイト Hellganite) functions as a specific category of held item. This particular Mega Stone grants Houndoom the capability to undergo Mega Evolution, transforming it into Mega Houndoom. When a Houndoom holds this item, it can achieve its Mega Evolved state, Mega Houndoom, provided its Trainer possesses a Key Stone. It's important to note that a Trainer's actions are restricted to initiating Mega Evolution for a single Pokémon per battle, or maintaining only one Mega-Evolved Pokémon simultaneously. Due to its classification as a Mega Stone, it remains impervious to effects that would otherwise negate held items, such as the move Embargo or abilities like Klutz. Furthermore, item-altering maneuvers like Trick or Fling are incapable of transferring the Houndoominite to or from a Houndoom.

In the context of a Pokémon Contest Spectacular, a Houndoom clutching its Houndoominite can attain its Mega Houndoom form upon executing a Spectacular Talent, again requiring its Trainer to hold a Key Stone. This Mega Evolution persists for the duration, with Houndoom reverting from its Mega form only when the Talent Round concludes.

Within the game Pokémon Shuffle, players utilize the Houndoominite to enable Houndoom's Mega Evolution. Acquisition of this item was possible by triumphing in the competitive stage featured during Week 19 of the event schedule. Additionally, it served as a reward during the Mega Houndoom competitive stage throughout the game's active service period, facilitating Houndoom's Mega Evolution.

In the anime series, specifically Mega Evolution Special IV, Malva was depicted possessing a Houndoominite, integrated into a collar worn by her Houndoom around its neck. Another instance of a Houndoominite occurred in Mega Evolution - Roy, where it was held by Master Mayonnaise. Furthermore, the Houndoominite has been a recurring element in various officially licensed merchandise.

Title: Aggronite

Identified in Japanese as ボスゴドラナイト (Bossgodoranite), the Aggronite is a particular held item that made its debut in Generation VI. This Mega Stone is crucial for Aggron to achieve its Mega-Evolved form, Mega Aggron. When an Aggron carries the Aggronite, it can transform into Mega Aggron, provided its Trainer is in possession of a Key Stone. A Trainer's strategic options are limited to performing only one Mega Evolution per battle, meaning only a single Mega-Evolved Pokémon can be active at any given moment.

As a Mega Stone, the Aggronite possesses distinct characteristics; it is not influenced by item-nullifying effects, such as the move Embargo or the ability Klutz. Additionally, effects designed to manipulate items, like Trick or Fling, are incapable of either transferring an Aggronite to an Aggron or removing one already held by it.

The Aggronite's utility extends beyond battles into Pokémon Contest Spectaculars. Should an Aggron hold this item and its Trainer wield a Key Stone, the Pokémon can Mega Evolve into Mega Aggron while showcasing a Spectacular Talent. This Mega-Evolved state will endure until the conclusion of that specific Talent Round.

Within the gameplay of Pokémon Shuffle, the Aggronite grants players the power to Mega Evolve their Aggron. Players can acquire this particular Aggronite by vanquishing Mega Aggron in Stage 550, which is situated in the Nacht Carnival area.

Regarding its function, the Aggronite enables the following Mega Evolutions: This specialized item made an appearance in the episode "The Prism Between Light and Darkness!", under the command of Team Rocket Elite Officer Gozu. Curiously, the Aggronite was depicted as not being physically equipped to Aggron but rather manifesting on its head immediately preceding its Mega Evolution. The Aggronite has also been showcased in various forms of officially licensed merchandise.

Title: Scizorite

When a Scizor is holding this item, it can attain its Mega Scizor form, provided its Trainer possesses a Key Stone. During a battle, a Trainer is restricted to Mega Evolving only one Pokémon at a time, meaning only one Mega-Evolved Pokémon can be active. Title: Pinsirite

This particular Mega Stone grants Pinsir the ability to undergo Mega Evolution, transforming it into Mega Pinsir. For a Pinsir to Mega Evolve into Mega Pinsir, provided its Trainer possesses a Key Stone, the Pinsirite must either be held by the Pokémon or simply be present within the player's Bag. Trainers are restricted to Mega Evolving a single Pokémon during a battle, meaning only one Mega-Evolved Pokémon can exist on their team at any given time. Consistent with other Mega Stones, the Pinsirite remains impervious to item-negating effects like Embargo and Klutz. Furthermore, item-manipulating abilities such as Trick and Fling are unable to transfer or seize the Pinsirite from a Pinsir. When a Pinsir holds this item and its Trainer has a Key Stone, it can Mega Evolve into Mega Pinsir while executing a Spectacular Talent within a Pokémon Contest Spectacular. Acquisition of the Pinsirite in Shuffle is achieved by successfully clearing the competitive stage offered during Week 13 of the event rotation. Title: Lucarionite

An item intended to be held, known as the Lucarionite (ルカリオナイト in Japanese), first appeared in the sixth generation of games. This particular Mega Stone enables Lucario to achieve its Mega Evolved form, Mega Lucario. When a Lucario holds a Lucarionite, it can undergo Mega Evolution into Mega Lucario, provided its Trainer possesses a Key Stone. During a battle, a Trainer is restricted to Mega Evolving only a single Pokémon (meaning only one Mega-Evolved Pokémon can be active concurrently). Due to its nature as a Mega Stone, it remains impervious to effects that nullify items, like Embargo or the Klutz Ability. Furthermore, item-altering moves such as Trick and Fling are incapable of bestowing or taking away a Lucarionite from a Lucario. In the context of a Pokémon Contest Spectacular, a Lucario holding a Lucarionite can transform into Mega Lucario for its Spectacular Talent performance, given its Trainer holds a Key Stone. This Mega Evolution state persists for Lucario until the conclusion of the Talent Round.

Within the game Pokémon Shuffle, the Lucarionite grants the player the ability to Mega Evolve Lucario. Players could acquire this item either by achieving Trainer rank 4 or by finishing Mission Card 2. Additionally, during Pokémon Shuffle's live service, the Lucarionite was obtainable as a reward from the Mega Lucario competitive stage. It was also distributed via a passcode on Pokémon Shuffle Mobile between August 25 and September 30, 2015.

The Lucarionite enables the following Mega Evolutions: The item first appeared visually in the anime within a painting, which Professor Sycamore revealed to Alain during a flashback in Mega Evolution Special I, depicting the very first recorded Mega Evolution. Its physical debut occurred in the episode "The Cave of Trials!", discovered by Korrina in a cavern close to Geosenge Town. Following a confrontation with a Blaziken protecting the Lucarionite—a Pokémon later identified as belonging to Korrina's grandfather, Gurkinn—Korrina successfully acquired the Mega Stone and utilized it to Mega Evolve her Lucario. McGinty subsequently fashioned the raw Lucarionite into an armband, enabling Lucario to wear the Mega Stone. "The Aura Storm!" episode disclosed that Gurkinn possessed an additional Lucarionite. Similar to Korrina's Lucario, Gurkinn's Lucario also displays its Lucarionite on its left wrist, embedded in an armband. Within "Origins of Mega Evolution!", Gurkinn recounted the tale of the inaugural documented Mega Evolution, a narrative that prominently featured a Lucarionite. Ash secured a Lucarionite for his Lucario on Mega Island, as depicted in "An Adventure of Mega Proportions!". It was revealed in "Eyes to the Skies Once Again (Part One)" that Roy had acquired a Lucarionite, which he employs to Mega Evolve his Shiny Lucario. "Mega Evolution - Bonds" illustrated Roy obtaining his Lucarionite concurrently with his Lucario in Lumiose City. Korrina is an owner of a Lucarionite, first seen in "The Aegislash Agenda." A later revelation showed that she and her Riolu had jointly discovered the Mega Stone in the past, fulfilling a quest assigned by Gurkinn to qualify her as a Mega Evolution Successor. The Lucarionite has also been showcased in various officially licensed merchandise.

Title: Kangaskhanite

Known in Japan as ガルーラナイト (Garuranite), the Kangaskhanite is a held item that debuted in the sixth generation of Pokémon games. Title: Gyaradosite

The Gyaradosite (Japanese: ギャラドスナイト Gyaradosnite) is a specialized held item that debuted in the sixth generation of the Pokémon games. Its primary function is as the designated Mega Stone, enabling Gyarados to undergo Mega Evolution into Mega Gyarados. For a Gyarados to transform, it must either be holding the Gyaradosite or have it present in its Trainer's Bag, provided the Trainer also possesses a Key Stone. Trainers are restricted to performing only one Mega Evolution per battle, meaning only a single Mega-Evolved Pokémon can be active at any given time.

As a Mega Stone, the Gyaradosite is impervious to effects that nullify items, such as the Ability Klutz or the move Embargo. Similarly, item-manipulating effects like Trick and Fling are incapable of transferring or removing a Gyaradosite from a Gyarados that holds it. In Pokémon Contest Spectaculars, a Gyarados holding this item can Mega Evolve into Mega Gyarados when executing a Spectacular Talent, again requiring its Trainer to have a Key Stone. This Mega Evolution persists until the conclusion of the Talent Round.

In the spin-off title Pokémon Shuffle, the Gyaradosite bestows the ability to Mega Evolve both standard Gyarados and Shiny Gyarados. Players initially acquired it by successfully completing the competitive stage featured in Week 9 of the event rotation. It was also offered as a reward during the Mega Gyarados competitive stage, which ran from June 27 to July 4, 2017, across both 3DS and Mobile platforms, with the boss featuring a Shiny version of the Pokémon, during the game's active service period.

In the anime, Alain discovered an uncut Gyaradosite during "Mega Evolution Special I." Its physical appearance was later seen in "Coming Apart at the Dreams!", where Lysandre's Shiny Gyarados wore it around its neck within a holder, mirroring the one used by Alain's Charizard for its Charizardite X. It remains unconfirmed whether this particular Gyaradosite was the same one Alain had previously found or a distinct gem. Another instance of a Gyaradosite occurred in "When Regions Collide!", utilized by Misty's Gyarados. Curiously, it didn't appear as an equipped item but rather manifested on the Pokémon's head crest just before its Mega Evolution. Gurkinn also showcased a Gyaradosite during his explanation of Mega Evolution in "An Adventure of Mega Proportions!". Lysandre owns a Gyaradosite, first revealed in "Chespin Sows the Seeds of Change." After Celosia appropriated Korrina's Key Stone, it was refashioned into a Mega Ring for Lysandre, enabling him to use both it and the Gyaradosite to achieve Mega Evolution for his Gyarados. X subsequently reclaimed the Key Stone from him. The Gyaradosite has also been featured across various officially licensed merchandise.

Title: Absolite

The Absolite (Japanese: アブソルナイト Absolnite) is a held item originating from Generation VI. This Mega Stone specifically enables Absol to undergo Mega Evolution, transforming it into Mega Absol. When an Absol holds this item, it can Mega Evolve into Mega Absol provided its Trainer possesses a Key Stone. A Trainer is restricted to Mega Evolving only one Pokémon per battle, meaning only one Mega-Evolved Pokémon can be active concurrently. As a Mega Stone, it boasts immunity to effects that negate items, such as Embargo and Klutz. Furthermore, item-manipulating effects like Trick and Fling are powerless to transfer or remove an Absolite from an Absol.

In Pokémon Contest Spectaculars, an Absolite held by an Absol permits it to Mega Evolve into Mega Absol for the duration of a Spectacular Talent performance, again requiring the Trainer to have a Key Stone. Absol retains its Mega Absol form until the conclusion of that Talent Round.

Within the game Pokémon Shuffle, the Absolite facilitates Absol's Mega Evolution for the player. It can be acquired by successfully completing Mission Card 10. During its live service period, the Absolite was also offered as a prize in the competitive Mega Absol stage.

The Absolite enables the Mega Evolution of Absol, as seen in various media. Astrid's Absolite was featured in Mega Evolution Special I, attached to a pendant worn by her Absol. She utilized it to Mega Evolve her Absol against Alain's Mega Charizard X. Astrid and her Absol, with its Absolite, later made a cameo appearance in Diancie and the Cocoon of Destruction, where they battled Ash and his Hawlucha. In the manga series Pinsir Changes, Tierno and Shauna discovered an Absolite for Y’s Absol, Solsol, allowing this recently caught team member to Mega Evolve. The Absolite has also been represented in various officially licensed merchandise.

Title: Charizardite Y

Introduced in Generation VI, the Charizardite Y (known in Japanese as リザードナイトY, or Lizardonite Y) functions as a held item. This Mega Stone facilitates Charizard's transformation into its Mega Charizard Y form. A Charizard equipped with the Charizardite Y (or with the item present in the player's Bag) can Mega Evolve into Mega Charizard Y, provided its Trainer holds a Key Stone. However, Trainers are limited to Mega Evolving a single Pokémon per battle, meaning only one Mega-Evolved Pokémon can exist at any given moment. Being a Mega Stone, its functionality remains untouched by effects that negate items, such as Embargo or Klutz. Furthermore, item-manipulating moves like Trick and Fling are unable to transfer the Charizardite Y to or from a Charizard. During a Pokémon Contest Spectacular, if a Charizard is holding this Mega Stone and its Trainer possesses a Key Stone, it can transform into Mega Charizard Y when executing a Spectacular Talent. The transformation persists until the conclusion of that Talent Round.

Within the game Pokémon Shuffle, the Charizardite Y grants players the ability to Mega Evolve both Charizard and Shiny Charizard into Mega Charizard Y. Acquisition of the Charizardite Y in Pokémon Shuffle is possible upon achieving Trainer rank 7 or by completing Mission Card 8. It was also distributed as a reward during the Mega Charizard competitive stage throughout the game's active service period. The Charizardite Y enables the following Mega Evolutions:

In the episode A League of His Own!, it was revealed that Trevor had acquired a Charizardite Y prior to the episode's events, and his Charizard prominently displays this Mega Stone on a ring adorning its tail. Episode HZ122 showcased Friede possessing a Charizardite Y, which he utilized to Mega Evolve his own Charizard. Within Pocket Monsters XY: The Legend of the Pokémon Dragon King, specifically in Pocket Monsters XY: The Legend of the Pokémon Dragon King, Akira is depicted as owning both a Charizardite Y and a Charizardite X. The manga chapter Charizard Transforms disclosed Blue's acquisition of a Charizardite Y, which he employs to Mega Evolve his Charizard. Numerous pieces of officially licensed merchandise have incorporated the Charizardite Y.

Title: Alakazite

A held item first introduced in Generation VI, the Alakazite (Japanese: フーディナイト Foodinite) serves as the Mega Stone that permits Alakazam's transformation into Mega Alakazam. To initiate this Mega Evolution in battle, an Alakazam must be holding the Alakazite (or it must be present in the player's Bag), and its Trainer must possess a Key Stone. However, a Trainer is limited to Mega Evolving only one Pokémon per battle, meaning only one Mega-Evolved Pokémon can exist at any given moment. As is characteristic of Mega Stones, the Alakazite remains unaffected by item-negating effects, such as Embargo and Klutz. Furthermore, item-manipulating effects like Trick and Fling are incapable of bestowing or removing an Alakazite from an Alakazam. During a Pokémon Contest Spectacular, if an Alakazam holds the Alakazite and its Trainer has a Key Stone, it can Mega Evolve into Mega Alakazam when performing a Spectacular Talent. This Mega Alakazam form will persist until the Talent Round concludes.

Within the context of Pokémon Shuffle, the Alakazite enables the player to Mega Evolve Alakazam once its mega meter gauge is full and Alakazam occupies the first slot. Players initially obtained the Alakazite by successfully completing the competitive stage available in Week 11 of the event rotation. It was also offered as a prize during the Mega Alakazam competitive stage throughout the game's live service period. The Alakazite distinctly facilitates Mega Evolution into Mega Alakazam.

In the anime, an Alakazite was depicted in Mega Evolution Special IV, utilized by a Trainer during the ten-battle gauntlet arranged by Lysandre for Alain's training. This particular Alakazite was specifically affixed to the Alakazam's right spoon. Beyond the games and anime, the Alakazite has also been showcased in various officially licensed merchandise.

Title: Heracronite

This held item, known in Japan as ヘラクロスナイト (Heracrosnite), made its debut in Generation VI. It functions as the specific Mega Stone enabling Heracross to undertake Mega Evolution, transforming into Mega Heracross. When Heracross holds this item, it can attain its Mega-Evolved state, Mega Heracross, provided its Trainer possesses a Key Stone. A Trainer is restricted to Mega Evolving just one Pokémon per battle, meaning only a single Mega-Evolved Pokémon can be active concurrently. Consistent with other Mega Stones, it remains impervious to effects that negate items, like Embargo or Klutz. Furthermore, item-swapping or removal techniques such as Trick and Fling are ineffective at bestowing or confiscating a Heracronite from Heracross. In a Pokémon Contest Spectacular, if a Heracross is holding the Heracronite and its Trainer holds a Key Stone, it can Mega Evolve into Mega Heracross while performing a Spectacular Talent. This transformation persists throughout the duration of the Talent Round. Within the game Pokémon Shuffle, the Heracronite empowers the player to achieve Heracross's Mega Evolution. Acquisition of this item occurs upon besting Mega Heracross in Stage 210, situated within Pedra Valley. A Heracronite first appeared in the narrative "Mega Evolution - Roy," where it was under the ownership of Master Mayonnaise. Later, in "Heracross Transforms," Gurkinn was shown to have one, with his Heracross notably wearing it encircling its horn. This item has also been prominently featured across various lines of officially licensed merchandise.

Title: Mawilite

Known in Japanese as クチートナイト (Kucheatnite), the Mawilite is a held item that debuted in the sixth generation of Pokémon games. This particular Mega Stone enables Mawile to undergo Mega Evolution, transforming it into its powerful Mega Mawile form. For a Mawile to Mega Evolve into Mega Mawile while holding this item, its Trainer must possess a Key Stone. Trainers are restricted to Mega Evolving only a single Pokémon per battle, maintaining one Mega-Evolved Pokémon at a time. Due to its nature as a Mega Stone, the Mawilite remains impervious to effects that nullify items, including moves like Embargo or the Klutz Ability. Furthermore, item-altering effects like the moves Trick and Fling are incapable of transferring the Mawilite to or from a Mawile. During a Pokémon Contest Spectacular, a Mawile holding the Mawilite can achieve Mega Evolution into Mega Mawile upon executing a Spectacular Talent, provided its Trainer also holds a Key Stone. This Mega Evolved state persists for Mawile until the conclusion of the Talent Round. In the game Pokémon Shuffle, the Mawilite serves as the catalyst for players to Mega Evolve Mawile. Acquiring the Mawilite in Shuffle requires players to best Mega Mawile in battle on Stage 90, located within Sweet Strasse. This item specifically enables Mawile's Mega Evolution. In an appearance within The Bonds of Mega Evolution!, a Mawilite was seen adorning Mabel's Mawile, integrated into a piece of jewelry at the base of its head jaws. Additionally, the Mawilite has made appearances across various officially licensed merchandise.

Title: Manectite

Introduced in Generation VI, the Manectite (known in Japanese as ライボルトナイト Livoltnite) functions as a held item. This Mega Stone specifically enables Manectric's transformation into Mega Manectric. When a Manectric holds this item, it can undergo Mega Evolution into Mega Manectric, provided its Trainer possesses a Key Stone. It's important to note that a Trainer is restricted to Mega Evolving only one Pokémon per battle, meaning only one Mega-Evolved Pokémon can exist at any given moment. Like all Mega Stones, it is impervious to effects that negate items, such as Embargo and Klutz. Furthermore, item-manipulating moves like Trick and Fling are unable to transfer the Manectite to or from a Manectric. During a Pokémon Contest Spectacular, if a Manectric is holding the Manectite and its Trainer has a Key Stone, it can Mega Evolve into Mega Manectric upon performing a Spectacular Talent. This Mega Evolution state persists until the conclusion of the Talent Round.

In the context of Pokémon Shuffle, the Manectite grants the player the ability to Mega Evolve Manectric. Players could acquire it by successfully clearing the competitive stage that appeared during Week 7 of the game's event rotation. It was also offered as a prize reward during the Mega Manectric competitive stage while the game's live service was active. The item's function to enable Mega Evolution was notably showcased when a Manectite first appeared in *Fast-Thinking Froakie*. In this story, Clemont assisted X and his companions in locating it on Route 5. Regrettably, a Team Flare Grunt, whose mission involved gathering Mega Stones, had also discovered the item and absconded with it. X, Trevor, and a Manectric—the latter having initially spurred the group's search—pursued the Grunt to Parfum Palace, ultimately succeeding in retrieving the stone. This Manectric, nicknamed "Élec" after joining X's party during the pursuit, then utilized the stone to Mega Evolve, causing the Grunt to flee. Following this, Élec began to wear the Manectite within his mane, granting him the ability to Mega Evolve whenever circumstances required it. The Manectite has also been depicted across various officially licensed merchandise.

Title: Garchompite

Serving as a Mega Stone, the Garchompite (ガブリアスナイト, Gaburiasnite in Japanese) is a held item first appearing in the sixth generation of Pokémon games, primarily enabling Garchomp's Mega Evolution into Mega Garchomp. When held by a Garchomp, this item facilitates its transformation into Mega Garchomp, provided its Trainer possesses a Key Stone. Trainers are limited to Mega Evolving only one Pokémon per battle, meaning only a single Mega-Evolved Pokémon can be active on their side at any given time.

Due to its classification as a Mega Stone, the Garchompite is impervious to item-negating effects, such as those from Embargo or the Klutz Ability. Furthermore, item-manipulating moves like Trick and Fling are incapable of either transferring or removing a Garchompite from a Garchomp. In the context of a Pokémon Contest Spectacular, if a Garchomp performs a Spectacular Talent while holding this item and its Trainer has a Key Stone, it will Mega Evolve into Mega Garchomp, maintaining this form until the conclusion of the Talent Round.

In the mobile game Pokémon Shuffle, the Garchompite similarly grants players the ability to Mega Evolve Garchomp. This item could be acquired by successfully clearing a competitive stage during Week 3 of the event rotation. It was also made available as a prize during the Mega Garchomp competitive stage throughout the game's live service period. The Garchompite enables the following Mega Evolution:

The Garchompite has been depicted across various media. Its initial appearance was in Mega Evolution Special I, where it belonged to Remo, and his Garchomp, Garchoo, wore the Mega Stone around its neck. This specific Garchompite briefly reappeared in Analysis Versus Passion!. A distinct Garchompite, owned by Professor Sycamore and his Garchomp, debuted in Garchomp's Mega Bond!, with Garchomp wearing the Mega Stone as a choker. In The Fiery Road to Mastership!, Cynthia was shown to possess a Garchompite, which her Garchomp utilizes for Mega Evolution. Koji, from Pocket Monsters XY: The Legend of the Pokémon Dragon King, also owns a Garchompite, allowing his Garchomp to Mega Evolve into Mega Garchomp. This Mega Stone has additionally been featured in numerous officially licensed merchandise items.

Title: Maranga Berry

First appearing in Generation VI, the Maranga Berry (Japanese: タラプのみ Tarapu Fruit) is a particular kind of Berry. Its in-battle effect activates when the Pokémon holding it takes damage from a special move, causing its Special Defense stat to increase by one stage. This process consumes the Maranga Berry. If the holder possesses the Ripen Ability, its Special Defense instead rises by two stages. The Maranga Berry has been illustrated on the Tropical Party card sleeves. Among Berries whose names do not contain adjectives, its seven-letter English name is the longest. Both the Maranga Berry's aesthetic and its nomenclature are possibly influenced by the marang fruit.

Title: Lumiose Galette

This medicinal item, known in Japan as ミアレガレット (Miare Galette), made its debut in Generation VI. As a regional delicacy from Lumiose City, it effectively eliminates all non-volatile status conditions and confusion from a Pokémon. Players in Pokémon X and Y can purchase a Lumiose Galette for $80, provided their style stat is 50 points or higher. Directly applied from the Bag to a Pokémon, it restores the Pokémon by removing all non-volatile ailments—specifically poison, paralysis, sleep, burn, and freeze—as well as confusion. Within Pokémon: Magikarp Jump, Lumiose Galettes can manifest as a consumable food item for Magikarp. Access to them becomes available at Trainer Rank 54, with an unlock cost of 30,428.

Their initial appearance in the anime was in "Kalos League Passion with a Certain Flare!", an episode where Meyer and Clembot presented Ash, his companions, Sawyer, and their Pokémon with two boxes of these treats, commemorating Ash's progression to the Lumiose Conference finals. Further instances of the item were shown in "The First Day of the Rest of Your Life!", with Ash and Serena purchasing some for their own consumption from a Lumiose City bakery. "Turning Heads and Training Hard!" featured Ilima serving Lumiose Galettes he had transported from Kalos to Professor Kukui and Samson Oak. During "Battle Three With Bea!", Korrina remarked on Bea's fondness for Lumiose Galettes, though Korrina had been unable to obtain any for her. Bonnie supplied Ash with Lumiose Galettes in "The Future is Now, Thanks to Strategy!", occurring during his and Goh's trip to the Lumiose Gym. James's Morpeko was observed consuming a Lumiose Galette in "It's... Champion Time!".

The Pokémon Trading Card Game incorporated the Lumiose Galette as an Item card within its English Mega Evolution Series (known as the MEGA Series in Japan). Its initial release took place in the Japanese Nihil Zero expansion and the English Perfect Order expansion, featuring artwork by 5ban Graphics. This Lumiose Galette card provides a 20 damage restoration and eliminates one Special Condition (which mirrors core series status conditions) from the player's Active Pokémon. Its functionality perfectly matches that of the earlier Big Malasada card from the Sun & Moon expansion, itself depicting a different regional delicacy also known for healing status conditions. Additionally, Lumiose Galettes appear in the artwork for Hoopa from the Forbidden Light expansion and in the Full Art illustration rare version of Morpeko from the Paradox Rift expansion. Various officially sanctioned figures and merchandise have showcased the Lumiose Galette.

Title: Jaw Fossil

Appearing first in Generation VI, the Jaw Fossil (known as アゴのカセキ in Japanese) is a type of fossil. It serves as the counterpart to the Sail Fossil and, when presented at specific locations, is capable of being revived into the Pokémon Tyrunt.

Within the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Jaw Fossil debuted as an Item card in the Furious Fists expansion, initially launched in Japan as part of the Rising Fist expansion. This card permits players to examine the bottom seven cards of their deck, reveal any Tyrunt discovered among them, and then place that Tyrunt directly onto their Bench. The remaining six cards are subsequently shuffled back into the deck.

Main article: Antique Jaw Fossil (Perfect Order 68) The Mega Evolution Series later introduced the Antique Jaw Fossil card, featured in the Perfect Order expansion, which first appeared in the Japanese Nihil Zero expansion. Differing from its predecessor, this particular card aligns more closely with other Fossil Item cards, enabling its direct play onto the Bench as though it were a Colorless-type Basic Pokémon. It possesses an Ability designed to reduce damage from attacks delivered by the opponent's Active Pokémon by a fixed amount of 30.

Fossil Trainer cards known as Unidentified Fossil cards transform into Basic Pokémon upon play. Although these cards do not specifically depict a Jaw Fossil, they can nevertheless be utilized to evolve into specific Tyrunt. The Jaw Fossil has also been showcased in various officially licensed merchandise items.

Title: Sail Fossil

This fossil, known in Japanese as ヒレのカセキ (Fin Fossil), debuted in Generation VI and holds the potential to be revived into the Pokémon Amaura. It serves as the counterpart to the Jaw Fossil. When presented at specific designated locations, the Sail Fossil can indeed be successfully regenerated into an Amaura. A Sail Fossil had a fleeting presence within a flashback sequence in the episode "Coming Back into the Cold!". Its discovery transpired within a glacier, carried out by a research team affiliated with the Ambrette Town Fossil Lab; ultimately, two Amaura were brought back to life from it, with one subsequently evolving into an Aurorus.

In the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Sail Fossil first appeared as an Item card within the Furious Fists expansion, which saw its initial release in Japan as the Rising Fist expansion. This card grants players the ability to inspect the bottom seven cards of their deck, locate an Amaura among them, reveal it, and then place it directly onto their Bench. The remaining six cards are subsequently shuffled back into the deck.

A separate card, the Antique Sail Fossil (Perfect Order 69), also exists. The Mega Evolution Series included the debut of Antique Sail Fossil as part of the Perfect Order expansion, originally launched in Japan as the Nihil Zero expansion. This particular version differentiates itself from the prior Sail Fossil card, aligning instead with other Fossil-depicting Item cards by permitting the player to deploy it onto the Bench as if it were a Basic Pokémon of the Colorless type. It possesses an Ability that offers protection, negating all effects from Supporter cards played by the opponent's hand that would otherwise target it. Furthermore, the Sail Fossil makes an appearance on certain Unidentified Fossil cards. These specific cards are similarly played as if they were Basic Pokémon and have the capacity to evolve into Amaura. Beyond its in-game and TCG presence, the Sail Fossil has been showcased across various officially licensed merchandise items.

Title: Fairy Gem

Known in Japan as フェアリージュエル (Fairy Jewel), the Fairy Gem is a held item first appearing within game data during Generation VI. This item is classified among the Gems, yet it remains completely unobtainable in any game. When a Pokémon equips the Fairy Gem, it bestows a 30% power increase to the very first Fairy-type move executed. Immediately after this boost, the item is consumed. Additionally, the move Fling will invariably fail if the user is holding a Fairy Gem.

Title: Holo Caster

This entry details the Generation VI item. For information regarding the segment concluding Japanese episodes of Pokémon the Series: XY, refer to Professor Oak's Pokémon Holo Caster. Designated as a Key Item in Generation VI, the Holo Caster (Japanese: ホロキャスター Holo Caster) first appeared in Pokémon X and Y. This device, a product of Lysandre Labs, facilitates interpersonal communication via holographic projections. Players automatically acquire a Holo Caster at the outset of their adventure. Its coloration is gender-dependent: male characters receive a green one, while female characters obtain a red one. Throughout the narrative, players receive important messages from friends and allies, crucial for advancing the plot. The storyline later discloses that Team Flare covertly monitored Holo Caster transmissions. However, Sina and Dexio promptly dismantled the recording apparatus following Team Flare's defeat in Geosenge Town.

When accessed through the PSS, the Holo Caster served as a conduit for special news bulletins concerning events and tournaments, and for obtaining unique distributions from participating retailers and event sites via SpotPass. Utilizing StreetPass, users could gather reports on nearby players. These reports adopted a news-style format, with Malva acting as the reporter. The Holo Caster service ceased operation in October 2016, preceding the launch of Pokémon Sun and Moon. Within the Looker Bureau missions, Emma, Malva, and Looker are frequent users of the Holo Caster. Regardless of the active mode, the Nintendo 3DS's integrated gyroscope enables manipulation of the on-screen background behind the character. Access to the Holo Caster's SpotPass and StreetPass functionalities is facilitated through the PSS section of the PlayNav, notwithstanding the device's unavailability as an in-game item.

While not acquirable as a Key Item in Legends: Z-A, the Holo Caster is mentioned by various non-player characters. This iteration can now project full life-sized human holograms, a capability regularly employed by Tarragon and Jacinthe. Tarragon employs the Holo Caster to impersonate Canari during specific in-person events, including the Canari Quiz Whiz Contest, manipulating the hologram's movements via a distinct application. Jacinthe's holographic projection stands out with its unique purple hue (contrasting with other blue holograms) and possesses the ability to engage in battles and Mega Evolve her Pokémon.

Within Pokémon the Series, Holo Casters have predominantly been shown in the possession of individuals linked to Team Flare. The initial device made its debut in Mega Evolution Special I, owned by Alain, with a second Holo Caster subsequently featuring in Mega Evolution Special II, belonging to Lysandre. Each of the Team Flare Scientists also possesses a Holo Caster, initially observed in From A to Z!. Its appearance in The Beauty Eternal showcased Lysandre's announcement of the device during a Lumiose City press conference. It was presented as a product designed to foster stronger connections among Kalosian citizens. The Holo Caster achieved considerable success throughout Kalos, prompting Lysandre Labs to escalate its production due to overwhelming demand. Leveraging the proceeds from the Holo Caster, Lysandre was able to contribute medical equipment to a Pokémon Center. Trevor possesses a Holo Caster, first depicted in An X-cuse to Come Out and Play. He regularly employs it for communication with Professor Sycamore. The device sustained damage during the confrontation between Xerneas and Yveltal but was subsequently mended by Lysandre.

Title: Intriguing Stone

The Intriguing Stone (Japanese: すごそうないし Precious-looking Stone) is a Key Item that first appeared in Generation VI. It initially presents as an unknown Mega Stone, with its true identity brought to light in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire. Once shown to Gurkinn in the Tower of Mastery, the Intriguing Stone can be traded with a Hiker located in Shalour City for a Sun Stone. An alternative path involves taking this item to Mr. Stone at the Devon Corporation in Rustboro City, where he will identify it as a Pidgeotite.

Title: Lens Case

Introduced as a Key Item in Generation VI, the Lens Case (Japanese: レンズケース Lens Case) empowers the player to modify their character's eye color. Housing various colored contact lenses, this item allows players to alter their ocular tint within designated fitting rooms. When making these adjustments, players can select from a variety of available contacts. Game titles present distinct differences: in Pokémon X and Y, players do not acquire lenses corresponding to their default eye color, while in Pokémon Sun, Moon, Ultra Sun, and Ultra Moon, the player's character commences play already adorned with colored contacts.

Title: Looker Ticket
The Looker Tickets (Japanese: ハンサムチケット Handsome Tickets), classified as Key Items, first appeared in Generation VI. These are a collection of five items that Looker assigns the player to locate as a test of their skills during the Looker Bureau's post-game quest. Locating the five Looker Tickets, which Looker concealed across Lumiose City, is necessary for the player to advance the main storyline. In his Pokémon Masters EX sync pair story, it is revealed that Looker brought his concept of searching for Looker Tickets to Pasio, aiming to improve individuals' detective prowess. He rewards those who manage to assemble all five tickets with a restaurant coupon.

Title: Mega Ring

Appearing in Generation VI, the Mega Ring (known as メガリング in Japanese) functions as a specific Key Item. This accessory holds a Key Stone, granting the ability to initiate Mega Evolution. For Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, the Mega Bracelet serves as its equivalent. Within the Tower of Mastery, Gurkinn presents one Mega Ring to the player character and their companions; however, possessing only a single item, he asks them to collectively determine its recipient. From this assembly, merely the player and Serena/Calem express interest, prompting a battle between them to settle ownership. Following victory over Serena/Calem, the player must acquire the Rumble Badge from the Shalour Gym before ascending to the Tower of Mastery's summit to accept the ring from Korrina. Subsequent to defeating their rival in Kiloude City a single time, Professor Sycamore enhances the player's Mega Ring at the Anistar Sundial, achieved by the player making contact with the sundial. With this enhancement, the player becomes capable of locating concealed Mega Stones throughout Kalos during the hours of 8:00 PM and 9:00 PM. Rumors suggest AZ journeyed to Hoenn, investigating Mega Evolution's origins out of personal curiosity, and subsequently shared his findings with Quasartico Inc., instructing them on Mega Ring production. Upon Ange's awakening inside Prism Tower, a surge of Mega Power was unleashed, resulting in wild Pokémon experiencing Rogue Mega Evolution. In an effort to halt the tower's effects, Quasartico devised a plan to identify the most potent Mega Evolution user by distributing Mega Rings to specific Trainers of the Z-A Royale, those ranked G and higher, enabling their use of Mega Evolution.

Within Pokémon X and Y, once the player has explored Kiloude City and engaged in combat at the Battle Maison, Calem/Serena, having acquired their own Mega Ring, will challenge the player daily in the city's northern sector. In Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, Courtney/Matt are shown utilizing Mega Rings during the Delta Episode, despite these items not being depicted in their character artwork. For Pokémon Sun, Moon, Ultra Sun, and Ultra Moon, Sina and Dexio employ white Mega Rings, each fastened to their left wrist; however, only Dexio gets to employ his outside the Battle Tree. Urbain/Taunie, Naveen, and Lida are each equipped with a Mega Ring in Pokémon Legends: Z-A. This item permits the player to Mega Evolve their Pokémon, provided the Pokémon holds its corresponding Mega Stone. A Trainer is restricted to Mega Evolving a single Pokémon per battle.

In Mega Evolution Special I, Alain was observed donning a Mega Ring, which he deploys to transform his Charizard into Mega Charizard X. Mega Evolution Special II disclosed that Lysandre was the source of his Mega Ring. Post-Team Flare crisis, Alain surrendered his Mega Ring to authorities for evidentiary purposes and independently sought a replacement Key Stone. Curtain Up! Fight the Fights! later revealed Alain had acquired another Mega Ring. The Visionary depicted both Calem and Serena sporting individual Mega Rings. Nonetheless, neither ring was activated during that particular episode. The Mega Rings featured in Pokémon Horizons: The Series exhibit a design identical to those found in Pokémon Legends: Z-A. Where the Adventure Leads unveiled Roy's ownership of a Mega Ring. Eyes to the Skies Once Again (Part One) revealed that Roy and his rival Ult both obtained their Mega Rings during their joint training in the Kalos region. In Mega Evolution - Roy, Master Mayonnaise was shown as the provider of their Mega Rings. Roy employs his Mega Ring for Mega Evolving his Lucario, whereas Ult utilizes his to Mega Evolve both his Sableye and Dragonite. During Mega Evolution - Ult, Emma activated a Mega Ring to Mega Evolve her Ampharos. Episode HZ122 disclosed that Friede acquired a Mega Ring during his year-long separation from the Rising Volt Tacklers. He possesses the capability to use it to Mega Evolve his Starmie and Charizard. HZ130 unveiled Chalce as an owner of a Mega Ring. This enables her to Mega Evolve her Medicham. During his confrontation with Roy in HZ131, Master Mayonnaise leveraged a Mega Ring to Mega Evolve his Hawlucha.

As a child, Gurkinn presented X with a Mega Ring containing a Key Stone. This allows him to Mega Evolve his Li'l Kanga into Mega Kangaskhan, Salamè into Mega Charizard X, Élec into Mega Manectric, Garma into Mega Gengar, and Rute into Mega Pinsir. In Pinsir Glares, Gurkinn appointed Y as a Mega Evolution successor, equipping her with a Mega Ring that contained the Key Stone originally from his Mega Glove. Through this, she can transform her Solsol into Mega Absol. Taichi, from Pocket Monsters XY: The Legend of the Pokémon Dragon King, possesses a Mega Ring, enabling him to Mega Evolve his Ampharos into Mega Ampharos. Lysandre's Key Stone accessory is additionally referred to as a Mega Ring, though it manifests as a finger ring rather than a wrist-worn device. Grisham and Griselle are likewise seen with comparable Key Stone-embedded rings, appearing to emulate Lysandre's style.

Title: Roller Skates

The term "Roller Skate" acts as a redirect to this page; for details on the Trainer profession, refer to Roller Skater (Trainer class).
