Known in Japanese as ローラースケート (Roller Skates), the Roller Skates are a specific Key Item featured in Pokémon X and Y. Their purpose is to grant the player increased movement speed compared to running, though they are not as fast as a Bicycle. Navigation while wearing them is accomplished using the Nintendo 3DS's Circle Pad. Their design draws inspiration from inline skates. Beyond being an accessible item for the player in Pokémon X and Y, the Gym Leader Korrina and members of the Roller Skater Trainer class are also observed utilizing Roller Skates. When feasible, manipulating the Nintendo 3DS's Circle Pad automatically equips the player with the Roller Skates; conversely, pressing the D-pad while wearing them will cause the player to remove them.

Certain locales, including Route 5, Shalour Gym, Route 13, and Dendemille Town, feature grinding rails that players can traverse using their Roller Skates. These rails often contain downward slopes from which the player will dismount unless sufficient speed is maintained. Should the player skate through tall grass, their pace will be reduced to walking speed, and the Poké Radar will be unavailable for use.

A variety of tricks can be executed while sporting the Roller Skates. Tricks that involve ledges are contingent upon being taught by an NPC. However, players can immediately perform grounded 360s, parallel swizzles, and drift-and-dashes upon obtaining the item. These maneuvers are purely cosmetic and do not impart any notable gameplay advantages. A successful trick execution is confirmed by a distinct, high-pitched whooshing sound.

Korrina also appears wearing Roller Skates in Pokémon Masters EX. In the anime, Korrina was first seen wearing Roller Skates during her debut episode, "Mega Revelations!", and continued to use them in subsequent appearances. In the episode "Origins of Mega Evolution!", she was depicted traversing a roller skate obstacle course within the Shalour Gym as she arrived to challenge Ash. The Roller Skates first made an appearance in "Lucky Lucario Was Here," where Korrina employed them to execute several tricks, enhancing her Mega Lucario's agility during her confrontation with Celosia. Later, she presented Tierno with a pair of Roller Skates as a gift. Tierno's Roller Skates met their demise in "Xerneas Gives," during the ultimate confrontation with Team Flare, when Mable's Houndoom's Inferno melted them.

In the Pokémon Trading Card Game, Roller Skates were introduced as an Item card within the English XY Series (known as the Japanese XY Era). Initially released in Japan's Collection X expansion, the card subsequently debuted in English in the XY expansion. Both iterations showcase artwork by 5ban Graphics. This Trainer card allows the player to flip a coin; if heads, they may draw three cards. Furthermore, a miniature version of Roller Skates was part of the Kalos goods set within the Pokémon World Market merchandise collection, produced by Re-Ment and sold exclusively at the Pokémon Center. Finally, Korrina references her Roller Skates in the Mega Dimension DLC for Pokémon Legends: Z-A, describing them as a "shtick" she had to abandon by that point.

Title: Sprinklotad

Known in Japanese as ハスボーじょうろ (Hassboh Watering Can), the Sprinklotad made its debut as a Key Item in Generation VI. This watering implement is specifically designed in the likeness of the Pokémon Lotad. Its primary in-game function is to enable players to nourish the trees they cultivate in the Berry fields. The anime episode "Under the Pledging Tree!" featured a brief appearance of a Sprinklotad; it was among the goods presented by a vendor in Coumarine City to Ash, who was searching for festival presents. It also had a short role in PT12, where a Trainer employed it to spray water onto her Leafeon. What sets the Sprinklotad apart is that it's the sole watering can whose aesthetic is not derived from a Pokémon of only the Water type; Lotad itself is a dual Water/Grass-type creature.

Title: Latiasite

Initially brought forth in Generation VI, the Latiasite (Japanese: ラティアスナイト Latiasnite) is an equipable item. This item functions as the designated Mega Stone, enabling Latias to undergo Mega Evolution into its Mega Latias form. When Latias holds it, this Mega Stone permits the Pokémon to achieve its Mega Latias form, contingent on its Trainer's possession of a Key Stone. However, Trainers are restricted to Mega Evolving just one Pokémon per battle, meaning only one Mega-Evolved Pokémon can exist on their side simultaneously. Like all Mega Stones, it remains immune to effects that nullify items, including moves such as Embargo and abilities like Klutz. Furthermore, item-altering maneuvers, specifically the moves Trick and Fling, are unable to transfer or seize a Latiasite from Latias. In the context of a Pokémon Contest Spectacular, if a Latias holds the Latiasite and its Trainer carries a Key Stone, the item facilitates its Mega Evolution into Mega Latias when the Pokémon executes a Spectacular Talent. This Mega Latias form persists solely until the conclusion of the Talent Round.

Within the game Pokémon Shuffle, the Latiasite provides players the means to Mega Evolve Latias. Acquisition of this item in Pokémon Shuffle is achieved by clearing Level 50 of a Mega Latias escalation battle during Weeks 8 and 9 of the game's rotating events. Throughout Pokémon Shuffle's live service period, the Latiasite was additionally awarded as a prize in Latias escalation battles. The Latiasite permits the subsequent Mega Evolution: The narrative titled 'Latias Flies' disclosed the acquisition of their specific Mega Stones by both Latios and Latias. Numerous officially licensed merchandise items have showcased the Latiasite. Although the Latiasite and Latiosite Mega Stones were not formally unveiled until Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, their corresponding data is present within the game code of Pokémon X and Y, thereby allowing their transfer and subsequent use in those earlier titles.

Title: Latiosite

The Latiosite (Japanese: ラティオスナイト Latiosnite) is a held item originating from Generation VI. This Mega Stone exclusively enables Latios to transform into Mega Latios through Mega Evolution. When a Latios holds this item, it can achieve its Mega Evolution into Mega Latios if its Trainer also possesses a Key Stone. A Trainer is restricted to Mega Evolving just one Pokémon per battle, meaning only one Mega-Evolved Pokémon can be present at any given moment. As a Mega Stone, the Latiosite remains unaffected by item-disabling effects, such as the move Embargo or the ability Klutz. Furthermore, item-altering effects like Trick and Fling cannot give or take a Latiosite from Latios. In Pokémon Contest Spectaculars, a Latios clutching the Latiosite can Mega Evolve into Mega Latios during a Spectacular Talent performance, provided its Trainer has a Key Stone. Latios will maintain its Mega form until the Talent Round concludes.

In Pokémon Shuffle, the Latiosite permits players to Mega Evolve Latios. Players could acquire this item by successfully passing Level 50 in a Mega Latios escalation battle during Weeks 4 and 5 of the event rotation. The Latiosite was also awarded as a prize from Latios escalation battles during the game's live service period.

This item facilitates the following Mega Evolution:
Within the "Latias Flies" manga, both Latios and Latias were depicted as having obtained their respective Mega Stones. Ruby, using the Latiosite in conjunction with his Mega Bracelet, was able to Mega Evolve Latios and ascend into the sky. He later Mega Evolved Latios again to engage Zinnia and her Mega Salamence in combat at the Sky Pillar. The Latiosite has appeared in various pieces of officially licensed merchandise. Despite its official introduction in Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, data for both Latiosite and Latiasite existed within the code of Pokémon X and Y, allowing them to be transferred and utilized in those games.

Title: Shalour Sable

This medicinal item, known in Japan as シャラサブレ (Shala Sable), was first introduced in Generation VI. A regional delicacy from Shalour City, when applied from the Bag to a Pokémon, it eliminates all non-volatile status conditions—specifically poison, paralysis, sleep, burn, and freeze—as well as confusion. In the spin-off game Pokémon: Magikarp Jump, Shalour Sables appear as food consumable by Magikarp. Players can unlock them at Trainer Rank 48 for a cost of 15,171. An episode titled The Fiery Road to Mastership! depicted James's Morpeko eating Shalour Sables. The artwork of the Shalour Sable from Scarlet and Violet inspired its inclusion in a Dedenne figure from The Pokémon Company International's Pokémon Delicious Adventure figure line, where it can be seen from the figure's backside. Despite not being directly obtainable in Pokémon X and Y without trading, a female Tourist near the Galette Stand in Lumiose City mentions them. Uniquely among local specialties, the Shalour Sable is the only one not acquirable within its own home region across the games.

Title: Devon Parts

Known in Japanese as デボンのにもつ (Devon Bag), the Devon Parts are a specific Key Item initially featured in Generation III. In Pokémon Ruby, Sapphire, and Emerald, this item was referred to as the Devon Goods. After the player has obtained the Stone Badge, the Devon Parts are appropriated by either a Team Magma Grunt or a Team Aqua Grunt in Rustboro City. Once the player has successfully retrieved the components, Mr. Stone instructs them to personally deliver these goods to Captain Stern. The attaché case itself contains crucial mechanical components developed by the Devon Corporation. Handing these parts over to Stern at the Oceanic Museum is essential for advancing the primary storyline.

The Devon Parts first appeared in the narrative Mowing Down Ludicolo, where Team Aqua successfully stole them from Mr. Stone. Following Team Magma's later success in pilfering the Submarine Explorer 1, the two rival organizations formed a temporary pact with the goal of safely reaching Seafloor Cavern. The integration of the parts purloined by Team Aqua with the submarine seized by Team Magma enabled the submersible to delve to significantly greater depths than its standard capacity, thus allowing it to reach Seafloor Cavern. In Very Vexing Volbeat, after Amber managed to steal the Blue Orb from Tabitha and hand it to Archie, the malevolent team boss trapped both Amber and Tabitha inside the submarine, then sent it on an automated dive into the ocean's profound depths. He subsequently revealed that he had deliberately extracted the Devon Parts from the submersible, thereby sentencing the two entrapped men to death as the immense deep-sea pressure would inevitably shatter the submarine's hull. Fortunately, both Admins were rescued from their certain demise by Blaise's Armaldo.

Title: Magma Suit

Making its debut in Generation VI, the Magma Suit (Japanese: マグマスーツ Magma Suit) functions as a crucial Key Item. In the game Pokémon Omega Ruby, this specialized garment provides the player with the means to enter Groudon's secluded chamber inside the Cave of Origin. Its utility later became apparent in the Delta Episode's climax, where it was donned to ride Mega Rayquaza into outer space for a direct confrontation with Deoxys. The Magma Suit featured prominently in 'The Scoop,' being observed on a Trainer who deployed Mega Rayquaza to destroy a meteor, followed by a subsequent clash with a Deoxys. Its initial appearance in manga was in 'Kyogre Returns,' where it resided in the possession of the second Tabitha. When Ruby and Sapphire prepared to ascend into space on Mega Rayquaza to eradicate Grand Meteor Delta in 'Omega Alpha Adventure 21,' Ruby received the Magma Suit to wear for the formidable voyage. For this specific endeavor, the suit had undergone multiple upgrades, including the incorporation of a set of Key Stones supplied by the Devon Corporation, which were essential for facilitating Rayquaza's Mega Evolution. In Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, a reproduction of the Magma Suit's helmet can be purchased from Atticus at the Porto Marinada Market.

Title: Aqua Suit

This crucial Key Item, known in Japanese as アクアスーツ Aqua Suit, made its debut in Generation VI. Within Pokémon Alpha Sapphire, it grants the player access to Kyogre's specific chamber inside the Cave of Origin. The suit is later employed during the Delta Episode's climax, facilitating a journey into space atop Mega Rayquaza to confront Deoxys. Its initial appearance was documented in the manga series' Kyogre Returns chapter, where it was possessed by the second iteration of Shelly. For their subsequent mission in Omega Alpha Adventure 21, when Ruby and Sapphire geared up to pilot Mega Rayquaza into orbit to obliterate Grand Meteor Delta, the Aqua Suit was specifically provided to Sapphire for her use. To achieve this objective, the garment underwent significant enhancements, notably incorporating Key Stones supplied by the Devon Corporation to enable Rayquaza's Mega Evolution, alongside a Green Orb serving as its core for direct control. A faithful replica of the Aqua Suit's helmet is obtainable for purchase from Atticus, found at the Porto Marinada Market within the games Pokémon Scarlet and Violet.

Title: Mega Bracelet

Debuting as a Key Item in Generation VI, the Mega Bracelet (Japanese: メガバングル Mega Bangle) serves as a vital accessory for a Key Stone, enabling the extraordinary process of Mega Evolution. Its counterpart found in Pokémon X and Y is the Mega Ring. On Southern Island, Steven bestows the Mega Bracelet upon the player after either Latios or Latias, each equipped with its specific Mega Stone, joins the player's party. During Pokémon Contest Spectaculars, the player character also adorns a distinctive Mega Bracelet. Brendan's version, known as a Mega Cuff (Japanese: メガブレス Mega Bracelet), is a black band featuring a ring of prominent, interconnected red diamonds, with its Key Stone integrated directly into the band. May's variant, called a Mega Charm (Japanese: メガチャーム Mega Charm), consists of a series of cream-colored bows, where the Key Stone is set into a pink heart charm. Following the player's victory over the Champion, their neighbor (Brendan or May) is revealed to have acquired their own Mega Bracelet, which they employ in a post-credits battle on Route 103. Secret Base Trainer avatars of player characters possessing a Mega Bracelet will utilize it in combat whenever possible. This item grants the player the capability to Mega Evolve their Pokémon, provided the Pokémon holds the correct Mega Stone. Nonetheless, each Trainer is restricted to Mega Evolving only a single Pokémon per battle.

Coinciding with the introduction of Mega Evolution in Pokémon GO on August 27, 2020, the Mega Bracelet became available for purchase in the Style Shop. In contrast to the main series games, however, these are not mandatory for Mega Evolving a Pokémon; their purpose in Pokémon GO is purely aesthetic.

In "The Vision," Brendan was depicted wearing his Mega Bracelet, though he wasn't shown activating it. The "Pokémon Omega Ruby and Pokémon Alpha Sapphire Animated Trailer" featured both Brendan and May sporting their Mega Bracelets, which they employed to Mega Evolve their respective Hoenn first partner Pokémon. May also briefly showcased her Mega Charm during a Pokémon Contest Spectacular while performing alongside a Mega Altaria and Mega Audino. Within "The Wish," May appeared with her Mega Bracelet, utilizing it initially to Mega Evolve her Swampert and subsequently her recently captured Rayquaza. Ruby, Sapphire, and Emerald obtained Mega Bracelets from Ultima and Steven, equipping themselves for an impending catastrophe threatening Hoenn. During "Claydol Unleashes," Ruby effectively employed his Mega Bracelet and Latios's Latiosite to initiate the Eon Pokémon's Mega Evolution, subsequently flying with it to the Sky Pillar. Furthermore, these three aforementioned Pokédex holders possess the ability to utilize their Mega Bracelets for Mega Evolving their individual Hoenn first partner Pokémon. In "Omega Alpha Adventure 21," Ruby was observed wearing a Mega Cuff and Sapphire a Mega Charm during a Contest Spectacular. Ruby activated his Mega Cuff to Mega Evolve Diancie as part of his Appeal. Diantha's Key Stone accessory, while also designated a Mega Charm, takes the form of a golden necklace rather than a bracelet.

Title: Swampertite

Known in Japanese as ラグラージナイト (Laglargenite), the Swampertite is a specific held item first appearing in the games Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire. This Mega Stone facilitates Swampert's transformation into its Mega Evolved form, Mega Swampert. For a Swampert to undergo Mega Evolution into Mega Swampert in battle, it must be holding the Swampertite, and its Trainer must possess a Key Stone. Trainers are restricted to Mega Evolving just one Pokémon per battle, or maintaining a single Mega-Evolved Pokémon at any point during combat. Being a Mega Stone, it possesses immunity to effects that negate items, such as the move Embargo or the Klutz Ability. Similarly, item-manipulating moves like Trick and Fling are unable to transfer the Swampertite to or from a Swampert. Beyond battles, when a Swampert holds a Swampertite and its Trainer has a Key Stone, it can also Mega Evolve into Mega Swampert during a Pokémon Contest Spectacular, specifically when performing a Spectacular Talent. This Mega Evolution persists until the conclusion of the Talent Round. A Pokémon holding a Swampertite within Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire cannot be exchanged with copies of Pokémon X and Y. Within the spin-off title Pokémon Shuffle, the Swampertite enables the player to trigger Swampert's Mega Evolution. Its acquisition there is achieved upon the completion of Mission Card 18. During Pokémon Shuffle's active service period, it was additionally offered as a reward in the Mega Swampert competitive stage. The manga arc Claydol Unleashes depicts Steven discovering a Swampertite intended for Ruby's Swampert, affectionately known as Mumu. Furthermore, the Swampertite has appeared across various officially licensed merchandise.

Title: Sceptilite

Known in Japanese as ジュカインナイト (Jukainnite), the Sceptilite is a specific held item first appearing in the games Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire. This item functions as the Mega Stone necessary for Sceptile to undergo Mega Evolution, transforming it into Mega Sceptile. When a Sceptile is holding the Sceptilite, it can Mega Evolve into Mega Sceptile, provided its Trainer possesses a Key Stone. However, a Trainer is restricted to Mega Evolving just one Pokémon per battle, meaning only one Mega-Evolved Pokémon can exist at any specific time. Its nature as a Mega Stone renders it immune to item-negating effects, including those like Embargo and the ability Klutz. Furthermore, item-manipulating effects, such as Trick and Fling, are unable to transfer the Sceptilite to or from a Sceptile. In Pokémon Contest Spectaculars, a Sceptile holding a Sceptilite can achieve Mega Evolution into Mega Sceptile during a Spectacular Talent performance, again contingent on its Trainer having a Key Stone. This Mega Evolution persists for the duration until the conclusion of the Talent Round. Pokémon holding a Sceptilite in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire are prevented from being traded to Pokémon X and Y. In the game Pokémon Shuffle, players utilize the Sceptilite to enable Sceptile's Mega Evolution. Acquisition of the Sceptilite in this game is achieved upon finishing Mission Card 19. Furthermore, during its live service period, the Sceptilite was offered as a prize within the Mega Sceptile competitive stage. Ultimately, the Sceptilite's role is to facilitate the designated Mega Evolution. The Sceptilite made its initial appearance in the episode titled Valuable Experience for All!. There, it was seen affixed to a bandanna around the neck of Sawyer's Sceptile. Later, within Claydol Unleashes, it was disclosed that Steven had discovered a Sceptilite specifically for Emerald's Sceptile. Beyond the games and anime, the Sceptilite has been showcased across various officially licensed merchandise.

Title: Sablenite

First appearing in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, the Sablenite (Japanese: ヤミラミナイト Yamiraminite) serves as a specific held item. This Mega Stone facilitates Sableye's transformation into its Mega Evolved form, Mega Sableye. For this evolution to occur, a Sableye must be holding the Sablenite, and its Trainer must possess a Key Stone. Trainers are restricted to activating Mega Evolution for only one Pokémon per battle, meaning just a single Mega-Evolved Pokémon can be present at any time. As a Mega Stone, it is impervious to item-negating effects, such as the move Embargo and the Ability Klutz. Furthermore, item-manipulating effects, including the moves Trick and Fling, cannot attach or detach a Sablenite from Sableye. In Pokémon Contest Spectaculars, if a Sableye performs a Spectacular Talent while holding the Sablenite and its Trainer has a Key Stone, it will also Mega Evolve into Mega Sableye. This Mega Evolution persists until the conclusion of that specific Talent Round. In Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, a Pokémon equipped with a Sablenite cannot be traded to Pokémon X and Y.

Within Pokémon Shuffle, the Sablenite grants the player the capacity to Mega Evolve both Sableye and Spooky Sableye. Players can acquire this item by vanquishing Mega Sableye in Stage 30, located in the Night Festival area. This Sablenite is instrumental in enabling the aforementioned Mega Evolutions.

The Sablenite made its initial appearance in *Eyes to the Skies Once Again (Part One)*, under Ult's ownership, where he employed it to Mega Evolve his Sableye. It was subsequently revealed in *Mega Evolution - Roy* that Ult had discovered it in Lumiose City while collecting refuse.

Title: Altarianite

Known in Japanese as チルタリスナイト (Tyltalisnite), the Altarianite is a held item that made its debut in the games Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire. This unique item serves as the Mega Stone specifically designed to enable Altaria's transformation into Mega Altaria. When an Altaria holds this item, it can undergo Mega Evolution into its Mega Altaria form, provided its Trainer possesses a Key Stone. Trainers are restricted to Mega Evolving only a single Pokémon per battle, meaning only one Mega-Evolved Pokémon can be active at any time. Its nature as a Mega Stone grants it immunity to effects that negate items, including moves like Embargo and Klutz. Furthermore, item-manipulating techniques, such as Trick and Fling, are incapable of either bestowing or confiscating an Altarianite from an Altaria.

In the context of a Pokémon Contest Spectacular, if an Altaria carries an Altarianite and its Trainer has a Key Stone, the item facilitates Mega Evolution into Mega Altaria when the Pokémon executes a Spectacular Talent. This Mega Evolution state persists solely until the conclusion of the Talent Round. It is important to note that a Pokémon holding an Altarianite in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire cannot be transferred via trade to Pokémon X and Y.

Within the game Pokémon Shuffle, the Altarianite empowers the player to Mega Evolve Altaria. To acquire this item, players must overcome Mega Altaria on Stage 75, which is located on Galerie Rouge.

This item's role in enabling Mega Evolution has been featured in the animated series. An Altarianite owned by Drasna was seen in "Taking Two For The Team!," where she employed it to Mega Evolve her Altaria during a World Coronation Series match against Ash; however, even with this enhancement, Altaria ultimately faced defeat. Subsequently, in "Reuniting for the First Time!," Lisia was shown possessing an Altarianite, which she utilized to Mega Evolve her Altaria, Ali, during the Lilycove City Pokémon Contest Spectacular. Beyond the games and anime, the Altarianite has also been prominently featured across various lines of officially licensed merchandise.

Title: Galladite

The Galladite (Japanese: エルレイドナイト Erureidonite) is a unique held item, making its debut in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire. It functions as the Mega Stone necessary for Gallade to undergo Mega Evolution, becoming Mega Gallade. For Gallade to achieve this form, it must be holding the Galladite, and its Trainer must possess a Key Stone. A Trainer is limited to Mega Evolving a single Pokémon per battle, thus maintaining only one Mega-Evolved Pokémon at any given time. As with all Mega Stones, the Galladite is immune to effects that negate items, such as Embargo or the Klutz Ability. Likewise, item-manipulating moves like Trick and Fling cannot transfer it to or remove it from Gallade.

In Pokémon Contest Spectaculars, a Gallade holding the Galladite can also Mega Evolve into Mega Gallade by performing a Spectacular Talent, provided its Trainer holds a Key Stone. This Mega Evolution lasts until the Talent Round concludes. Galladite-holding Pokémon originating from Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire cannot be traded to Pokémon X and Y.

In Pokémon Shuffle, the Galladite facilitates Gallade's Mega Evolution for the player. Its acquisition involves defeating Mega Gallade in Stage 580, located in Prasino Woods. Story-wise, in PS616, the Meteor Village elder presented Sapphire with a Gardevoirite and a Galladite, noting the compatibility between her Gallade and Ruby's Gardevoir. Ruby and Sapphire later employed these Mega Stones to evolve Rara and Kirly during a short skirmish.

Title: Audinite

An item held by Pokémon, the Audinite (known in Japanese as タブンネナイト Tabunnenite), was first introduced in the games Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire. This particular Mega Stone enables Audino to undergo Mega Evolution, transforming it into Mega Audino. When an Audino holds the Audinite, it can Mega Evolve into Mega Audino, provided its Trainer possesses a Key Stone. Trainers are restricted to Mega Evolving a single Pokémon per battle, meaning only one Mega-Evolved Pokémon can be active at any time. Due to its nature as a Mega Stone, it remains immune to effects that prevent item usage, such as the move Embargo or the Ability Klutz. Furthermore, item-swapping or removal techniques like Trick and Fling are unable to transfer or confiscate an Audinite from Audino. In the context of a Pokémon Contest Spectacular, an Audino holding the Audinite can Mega Evolve into Mega Audino when executing a Spectacular Talent, again requiring its Trainer to have a Key Stone. This Mega-Evolved state for Audino persists until the conclusion of the Talent Round. Pokémon holding an Audinite in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire are barred from being traded to Pokémon X and Y. Shifting to Pokémon Shuffle, the Audinite grants players the capability to Mega Evolve both Audino and Winking Audino into Mega Audino. Players acquire the Audinite by vanquishing Mega Audino in Stage 10 of Puerto Blanco, which marks its final stage. The Trainer associated with Mega Audino presents this Mega Stone to the player as a prize for victory. The Audinite also facilitates specific Mega Evolutions: Possessing it permits the player to observe Audino and Winking Audino within the Pokémon List, even without having caught them, although their icons appear faded and details such as their type, attack power, and Skill remain concealed. However, since Stage 9 invariably results in Audino's capture, this observation feature is, in practice, relevant only for Winking Audino. In the episode "A Giga Battle with Mega Results!", an Audinite made an appearance when a Nurse Joy, who owned an Audino, discovered it while trying to improve their recently strained relationship. Subsequently, she utilized the Audinite along with Professor Sycamore's Key Stone to Mega Evolve her Audino for a confrontation against Team Rocket. The Audinite has also been prominently featured across various lines of officially licensed merchandise.

Title: Metagrossite

First appearing as a held item type in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, the Metagrossite (Japanese: メタグロスナイト Metagrossnite) serves as the designated Mega Stone. This item empowers Metagross to undergo its Mega Evolution into Mega Metagross. When a Metagross holds the Metagrossite, it can initiate this transformation into Mega Metagross, provided its Trainer also possesses a Key Stone. Trainers are restricted to Mega Evolving only one Pokémon per battle, meaning only a single Mega-Evolved Pokémon can be active at any given moment. Being a Mega Stone, it is immune to item-nullifying effects, such as the move Embargo or the ability Klutz. Likewise, item-manipulating effects like Trick and Fling are unable to transfer or remove a Metagrossite from a Metagross.

In the context of a Pokémon Contest Spectacular, if a Metagross is holding a Metagrossite and its Trainer has a Key Stone, it can Mega Evolve into Mega Metagross while performing a Spectacular Talent. This Mega Evolution state for Metagross will persist until the conclusion of the Talent Round.

It's notable that any Pokémon equipped with a Metagrossite in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire cannot be traded to Pokémon X and Y.

Within the game Pokémon Shuffle, the Metagrossite enables the player to Mega Evolve both Metagross and Shiny Metagross. Players can acquire the Metagrossite in Shuffle by successfully defeating Mega Metagross in Stage 500, located within Graucus Hall.

In the animated series, Steven's Shiny Metagross's Metagrossite made its debut in Mega Evolution Special II, depicted as a large bracelet on its right foreleg. Later, in Hoopa Distorts, Steven was confirmed to be in possession of a Metagrossite. The Metagrossite has also been featured across a variety of officially licensed merchandise.

Title: Sharpedonite

Bearing the Japanese name サメハダナイト (Samehadenite), the Sharpedonite first appeared as a held item in the games Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire. Categorized as a Mega Stone, its function is to enable Sharpedo to Mega Evolve into Mega Sharpedo. For a Sharpedo holding this item to undergo Mega Evolution into Mega Sharpedo, its Trainer must possess a Key Stone. It's important to note that a Trainer is limited to Mega Evolving a single Pokémon per battle, meaning only one Mega-Evolved Pokémon can be active at any given moment. Being a Mega Stone, it remains immune to item-nullifying effects, including Embargo and the Klutz Ability. Furthermore, item-changing moves like Trick and Fling are unable to transfer or seize a Sharpedonite from a Sharpedo. In the context of a Pokémon Contest Spectacular, if a Sharpedo is holding this item and performs a Spectacular Talent, it will Mega Evolve into Mega Sharpedo, provided its Trainer also possesses a Key Stone. This Mega Evolution state persists for Sharpedo throughout the duration of the Talent Round. Trading any Pokémon equipped with a Sharpedonite from Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire into Pokémon X and Y is not possible. Within the game Pokémon Shuffle, the Sharpedonite serves to enable the player to Mega Evolve Sharpedo. Players wishing to acquire the Sharpedonite in Shuffle can do so by overcoming Mega Sharpedo in Stage 600, located within Prasino Woods. During Pokémon Shuffle's live service phase, the Sharpedonite was also offered as a reward in the competitive stage featuring Mega Sharpedo. This particular Mega Stone facilitates the following Mega Evolution: within the *Blastoise Transforms* storyline, it was disclosed that Archie had acquired a Sharpedonite. Following his presumed demise, the Mega Stone subsequently came into Amber's possession. Numerous officially licensed merchandise items have showcased the Sharpedonite.

Title: Slowbronite

This held item, introduced in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, is also identified by its Japanese name, ヤドランナイト (Yadorannite). Its purpose is to serve as the specific Mega Stone enabling a Kantonian Slowbro to undergo Mega Evolution, transforming into Mega Slowbro. Should a Kantonian Slowbro possess the Slowbronite as its held item—or if the item is present within the player's Bag—it can undergo Mega Evolution into Mega Slowbro, provided its Trainer also possesses a Key Stone. It is important to note that a Trainer is restricted to Mega Evolving only a single Pokémon per battle, or maintaining only one Mega-Evolved Pokémon at any point. Being a Mega Stone, the Slowbronite is immune to effects that negate items, for example, abilities like Klutz or the move Embargo. Furthermore, item-manipulating actions like Trick or Fling are incapable of either bestowing or taking away a Slowbronite from a Slowbro. When a Slowbro holds the Slowbronite during a Pokémon Contest Spectacular, it gains the ability to Mega Evolve into Mega Slowbro specifically when executing a Spectacular Talent, assuming its Trainer possesses a Key Stone. This Mega Evolution state will persist for Slowbro until the conclusion of the Talent Round. Pokémon holding a Slowbronite in Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire are barred from being traded to Pokémon X and Y. Within the game Pokémon Shuffle, the Slowbronite grants the player the capability to Mega Evolve Slowbro. Acquisition of the Slowbronite in this game occurs by defeating Mega Slowbro on Stage 45, located on Isla Asul. The Slowbronite provides for the following Mega Evolution capability: In the narrative 'An Adventure of Mega Proportions!', a Slowbronite was sighted during Gurkinn's discourse on Mega Evolution. Additionally, in 'Slowbro Has an Idea', Jinga was shown to own a Slowbronite, which he subsequently utilized to Mega Evolve his Slowbro. The Slowbronite has also been represented across various pieces of officially licensed merchandise.

Title: Steelixite

Known in Japanese as ハガネールナイト (Haganeilnite), the Steelixite is a specific held item that first appeared in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire. This Mega Stone facilitates Steelix's transformation into its Mega Evolved form, Mega Steelix. When a Steelix holds this item, it can undergo Mega Evolution into Mega Steelix, provided its Trainer possesses a Key Stone. Trainers are restricted to Mega Evolving only one Pokémon per battle, meaning only one Mega-Evolved Pokémon can be active at any single moment. Being a Mega Stone, it remains impervious to effects that negate items, such as the move Embargo or the Klutz Ability. Furthermore, item-altering effects like Trick and Fling are unable to transfer or seize a Steelixite from a Steelix. In the context of a Pokémon Contest Spectacular, a Steelix holding the Steelixite can achieve Mega Evolution into Mega Steelix for its Spectacular Talent, assuming its Trainer possesses a Key Stone. Mega Steelix will maintain this form until the conclusion of the Talent Round. Any Pokémon carrying a Steelixite in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire is blocked from being traded to Pokémon X and Y. Within the game Pokémon Shuffle, the Steelixite grants players the ability to Mega Evolve Steelix. Players could acquire the Steelixite by successfully clearing the competitive stage featured during Week 5 of the event rotation. It was also offered as a reward during the Mega Steelix competitive stage while the game's live service was active. The Steelixite made an appearance in the episode 'When Regions Collide!', equipped by Brock's Steelix. Brock utilized it there to Mega Evolve his Steelix during a confrontation with Kiawe. Notably, the Steelixite was not visibly equipped on Steelix; instead, it materialized on its head just prior to its Mega Evolution.

Title: Pidgeotite

The Pidgeotite (Japanese: ピジョットナイト Pigeotnite) is a specific type of held item first introduced in the Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire games. This Mega Stone grants Pidgeot the ability to Mega Evolve into Mega Pidgeot. For Pidgeot to undergo this transformation in battle, it must be holding the Pidgeotite (or the item must be located in the player's Bag), and its Trainer must be equipped with a Key Stone. A Trainer is limited to performing only one Mega Evolution per battle, meaning a single Mega-Evolved Pokémon can exist at any given time.

Consistent with other Mega Stones, the Pidgeotite is impervious to effects that nullify held items, such as the moves Embargo and Klutz. Furthermore, item-manipulating effects like Trick and Fling are unable to transfer or remove a Pidgeotite from a Pidgeot.

During a Pokémon Contest Spectacular, a Pidgeot holding its Pidgeotite can also Mega Evolve into Mega Pidgeot when executing a Spectacular Talent, provided its Trainer also possesses a Key Stone. This Mega-Evolved state persists until the current Talent Round concludes.

A particular game restriction prevents any Pokémon holding a Pidgeotite in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire from being traded to Pokémon X and Y.

In Pokémon Shuffle, the Pidgeotite facilitates Pidgeot's Mega Evolution for the player. This item could be acquired by reaching Trainer rank 12, and it was additionally offered as a reward during the Mega Pidgeot competitive stage throughout the game's live service period. The Pidgeotite has also been featured within various pieces of officially licensed merchandise.

Title: Glalitite

Referred to in Japanese as オニゴーリナイト (Onigohrinite), the Glalitite is a held item originating from Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire. It serves as the Mega Stone that enables Glalie to Mega Evolve into Mega Glalie. For a Glalie to achieve this transformation, it must be holding the Glalitite, and its Trainer must possess a Key Stone. Trainers are restricted to a single Mega Evolution per battle, allowing only one Mega-Evolved Pokémon to be present simultaneously.

As a Mega Stone, the Glalitite remains unaffected by item-negating effects, such as the move Embargo or the Ability Klutz. Furthermore, item-manipulating effects like Trick and Fling are unable to transfer or dislodge a Glalitite from Glalie.

During a Pokémon Contest Spectacular, a Glalie carrying the Glalitite will Mega Evolve into Mega Glalie upon executing a Spectacular Talent, assuming its Trainer possesses a Key Stone. This Mega Glalie state persists until the Talent Round concludes.

Any Pokémon in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire holding a Glalitite is restricted from trade to Pokémon X and Y. For Pokémon Shuffle players, the Glalitite provides the means to Mega Evolve both Glalie and Winking Glalie. Acquisition of the Glalitite in Shuffle requires players to best Mega Glalie in Stage 120, located on Mt. Vinter. Moreover, the Glalitite has featured in several lines of officially licensed merchandise.

Title: Diancite

The Diancite (Japanese: ディアンシナイト Diancinite) is a specialized held item that debuted in the Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire games. It functions as the crucial Mega Stone allowing Diancie to transform into its Mega Diancie form.

When held by Diancie, this item enables it to Mega Evolve into Mega Diancie during battle, provided its Trainer possesses a Key Stone. Trainers are limited to Mega Evolving only one Pokémon per battle, meaning only a single Mega-Evolved Pokémon can be active at any given time.

Consistent with other Mega Stones, the Diancite is not affected by item-negating effects such as the move Embargo or the Ability Klutz. Additionally, item-manipulating effects, including the moves Trick and Fling, cannot transfer or remove a Diancite from Diancie.

For Pokémon Contest Spectaculars, if Diancie holds a Diancite and performs a Spectacular Talent, it can Mega Evolve into Mega Diancie when its Trainer also has a Key Stone. Diancie will remain in its Mega Diancie state until the conclusion of that specific Talent Round.

Any Pokémon holding a Diancite in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire cannot be traded to Pokémon X and Y.

In the mobile title Pokémon Shuffle, the Diancite empowers players to Mega Evolve both Diancie and Shiny Diancie. Players could obtain the Diancite by successfully completing Level 50 of a Mega Diancie escalation battle during Weeks 10 and 11 of the event rotation. Furthermore, the Diancite was awarded as a prize in Diancie escalation battles during the game's live service period: from August 22 to September 5, 2017 (across 3DS and Mobile, featuring a Shiny boss) and again from December 12 to 26, 2017 (for both 3DS and Mobile, also with a Shiny boss).

This item facilitates the following Mega Evolutions: In Omega Alpha Adventure 21, Ruby utilized Hoopa's ring to locate a Diancite for Diancie to use in a Pokémon Contest Spectacular. This allowed Diancie to Mega Evolve for his and Sapphire's joint performance.

Title: Prison Bottle

Known in Japanese as いましめのツボ (Jar of Binding), the Prison Bottle is a significant Key Item that first debuted in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire. Its primary function serves to facilitate the transformation of Hoopa Confined into its Hoopa Unbound form.

In Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, players can acquire the Prison Bottle by having a Hoopa in their active party and then entering any Poké Mart. This action causes a unique clerk to materialize near the far-left side of the counter. Upon interacting with this clerk, the game transitions to a different view as he proceeds to recount a detailed personal anecdote. Following the conclusion of his story, he bestows the Prison Bottle upon the player.

The clerk's narrative begins with him asking for a moment to share a tale from when he was minding the store on a particularly dark, moonless night, which he found exceptionally unsettling. Suddenly, the automatic sliding door spontaneously opened, revealing an elderly man described as "super-duper-scary" and moving with an unsteady, shuffling gait. The clerk felt a surge of fear as the old man approached, clutching a package, and muttering that he needed to present it. He then directly questioned the clerk, "Do you know what this is worth?" while maintaining an unblinking stare. Intimidated, the clerk nervously replied that it was indeed "really something." The old man then grinned, declaring, "Then I'll just give you this instead of money. Now give me everything you've got in this store!" Despite his bewilderment, the frightened clerk complied, hastily handing over Potions and whatever other items he could reach in exchange for the package. As the old man departed with a sinister grin, he uttered, "Three days... For a mere three days will Hoopa be unbound..." The clerk, deeply disturbed and sweating profusely, later found himself with no choice but to open the package. Inside, he discovered only a "dirty old bottle." He pondered its contents, then carefully removed the stopper, which popped open with a "Blapt!" sound, revealing nothing within. He concluded he had been thoroughly tricked. Expressing gratitude for the player's listening, the clerk then presented the bottle to the player, deeming it useless to himself.

Subsequently, the clerk voiced his lingering uncertainty, questioning whether it was truly just an ordinary old bottle. He admitted that the old man's parting words—"For a mere three days will Hoopa be unbound..."—were persistently stuck in his mind. In a later interaction, recognizing the player, he inquired about the bottle's fate, remarking that its true significance might no longer matter. Nevertheless, he confessed that the phrase, "For a mere three days will Hoopa be unbound...", continued to trouble him, unforgettable.

When utilized directly from the Bag on a Hoopa Confined, the item triggers its transformation into Hoopa Unbound, a state that lasts for a duration of three days. Hoopa Unbound will revert to its Hoopa Confined form under several conditions: upon being deposited into the PC (applicable prior to Generation VII), upon being withdrawn from the PC (beginning with Generation VII), when stored in Pokémon Bank, or if placed in either the Pokémon Day Care or the Pokémon Nursery.

The Prison Bottle played a pivotal part in the narrative of the movie Hoopa and the Clash of Ages. A century before the events depicted in the film, Hoopa Unbound unleashed widespread destruction upon Dahara City through its intense battles with various Legendary Pokémon. To counter this, a man named Ghris fashioned the Prison Bottle, harnessing the combined might of Arceus's Splash, Flame, and Earth Plates. He then employed this newly created artifact to suppress and seal away Hoopa's Unbound capabilities, subsequently concealing it.

In the contemporary storyline, Ghris's great-grandson, Baraz, uncovered the hidden bottle and released its seal. Upon physical contact with the item, Baraz became possessed by the ominous power it contained. Returning to Dahara City, he unsealed the bottle, initiating Hoopa's reversion into Hoopa Unbound, which consequently incited another rampage. Meray intervened, succeeding in resealing Hoopa's Unbound power back into the bottle.

The bottle was later purloined by Team Rocket, who sought to harness its inner energy. This act inadvertently enabled Hoopa Unbound to possess Meowth, compelling the Pokémon to release it once more. With the aid of Baraz and Meray, Hoopa managed to resist the dark power's attempt to fully control its body. Unexpectedly, the bottle then fractured into numerous fragments. The disembodied dark power coalesced into a distinct entity—a "shadow" manifestation of Hoopa. Baraz discerned that this shadow was born from Hoopa's century-long resentment of its imprisonment and had deliberately destroyed the bottle to prevent another sealing.

Recognizing the possibility of recreating the Prison Bottle, Baraz guided Meray, Clemont, Serena, and Bonnie to Dahara Tower. There, they utilized Frogadier's Water Pulse, Braixen's Flamethrower, and a wild Hippopotas's Sand Attack to replicate the foundational elements—Water, Fire, and Earth—originally used in the bottle's creation one hundred years prior. The Prison Bottle was successfully reconstructed, and Hoopa's shadow was subsequently contained within it. Although the shadow momentarily took control of Ash, Hoopa ultimately expelled its dark influence. This allowed Ash to responsibly use the bottle to empower Hoopa with its Hoopa Unbound form, thereby enabling it to safeguard the inhabitants of Dahara City from a space-time anomaly without succumbing to a loss of control. Afterwards, Hoopa calmly reverted to Hoopa Confined. During the movie's closing credits, Meray was observed transforming Hoopa into Hoopa Unbound to assist in the reconstruction of the ravaged Dahara Tower. The Prison Bottle was featured within Hoopa and the Clash of Ages, maintaining a consistent role as depicted throughout the film.

Title: Cameruptite

Known in Japanese as バクーダナイト (Bakuudanite), the Cameruptite is a held item making its debut in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire. This Mega Stone empowers Camerupt to undergo Mega Evolution, transforming it into Mega Camerupt. When a Camerupt holds this item, it can achieve its Mega-Evolved form, Mega Camerupt, provided its Trainer possesses a Key Stone. Trainers are limited to Mega Evolving one Pokémon per battle, meaning only one Mega-Evolved Pokémon can be active at any given moment.

As a Mega Stone, the Cameruptite remains unaffected by item-negating effects, such as the move Embargo or the Ability Klutz. Furthermore, item-manipulating effects like Trick and Fling cannot be used to give or remove a Cameruptite from Camerupt. Its utility extends to Pokémon Contest Spectaculars: when held by a Camerupt, the Cameruptite enables it to Mega Evolve into Mega Camerupt if its Trainer has a Key Stone and the Pokémon performs a Spectacular Talent. This Mega Evolution state for Camerupt lasts until the Talent Round concludes.

Regarding game compatibility, a Pokémon holding a Cameruptite in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire cannot be traded to Pokémon X and Y. In Pokémon Shuffle, the Cameruptite allows the player to Mega Evolve Camerupt. Players could originally obtain this item by completing the competitive stage featured in Week 15 of the event rotation. It was also offered as a prize during the Mega Camerupt competitive stage throughout the game's live service period.

In the anime, a Cameruptite was displayed during Gurkinn's explanation of Mega Evolution in the episode "An Adventure of Mega Proportions!". The episode "Blastoise Transforms" revealed that Maxie had acquired a Cameruptite, and following his apparent death, Blaise then took possession of this Mega Stone. The Cameruptite has also been depicted in various officially licensed merchandise.

Title: Lopunnite

A held item first appearing in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, the Lopunnite (Japanese: ミミロップナイト Mimilopnite) serves as the dedicated Mega Stone for Lopunny. It enables Lopunny to undertake its Mega Evolution into Mega Lopunny. When a Lopunny holds this specific item, it can Mega Evolve into Mega Lopunny, contingent upon its Trainer possessing a Key Stone. A Trainer is limited to a single Mega Evolution per battle, meaning only one Mega-Evolved Pokémon may be active at any given moment. Owing to its classification as a Mega Stone, the Lopunnite resists item-negating effects, notably Embargo and Klutz. Furthermore, item-manipulating effects like Trick and Fling are incapable of bestowing or removing a Lopunnite from Lopunny. In the context of a Pokémon Contest Spectacular, if a Lopunny bearing a Lopunnite executes a Spectacular Talent, it will Mega Evolve into Mega Lopunny, provided its Trainer also has a Key Stone. This transformation into Mega Lopunny endures until the conclusion of the Talent Round. Trainers are prevented from trading any Pokémon holding a Lopunnite from Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire to Pokémon X and Y. Turning to Pokémon Shuffle, the Lopunnite empowers the player to Mega Evolve Lopunny. It is obtained by defeating Mega Lopunny in Stage 60, which is situated on Rainbow Park. Ultimately, the Lopunnite facilitates the Mega Evolution of Lopunny, and it has also been showcased across various officially licensed merchandise.

Title: Salamencite

First appearing as a held item in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, the Salamencite is identified in Japanese as ボーマンダナイト (Bohmandanite). It functions as the specific Mega Stone enabling Salamence's transformation into its Mega Evolved form, Mega Salamence. When a Salamence carries this item, and its Trainer possesses a Key Stone, the Pokémon gains the capability to Mega Evolve into Mega Salamence. Trainers are restricted to Mega Evolving a single Pokémon per battle, meaning only one Mega-Evolved Pokémon can be active concurrently. Being a Mega Stone, it remains impervious to effects that negate items, like Embargo and Klutz. Furthermore, item-altering moves such as Trick and Fling are incapable of transferring or dislodging a Salamencite from Salamence. In a Pokémon Contest Spectacular, if a Salamence holds this item and its Trainer has a Key Stone, it can undergo Mega Evolution into Mega Salamence upon performing a Spectacular Talent. This Mega-Evolved state will persist for Salamence throughout the duration of the Talent Round. A Pokémon holding a Salamencite in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire cannot be exchanged via trade with Pokémon X and Y. Within Pokémon Shuffle, the Salamencite empowers players to Mega Evolve Salamence. Players could acquire it by triumphing over Mega Salamence in Stage 530, located in Nacht Carnival. Additionally, the Mega Salamence competitive stage offered the Salamencite as a prize during the game's live service period. An instance of the Salamencite enabling Mega Evolution is detailed in "Salamence Evolves," where Zinnia was revealed to own one. She subsequently utilized it and her Mega Anklet to facilitate her Salamence's Mega Evolution for a confrontation with Ruby and Latios. Various pieces of officially licensed merchandise have showcased the Salamencite.

Title: Beedrillite

This held item, known in Japanese as スピアナイト (Speanite), made its debut in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire. It serves as the designated Mega Stone, enabling Beedrill to undergo Mega Evolution into its Mega Beedrill form. For Beedrill to Mega Evolve into Mega Beedrill, it must be holding the Beedrillite (or the item must be in the player's Bag), provided its Trainer possesses a Key Stone. A Trainer is restricted to Mega Evolving a single Pokémon per battle, or maintaining just one Mega-Evolved Pokémon simultaneously. Due to its nature as a Mega Stone, the Beedrillite remains immune to effects that negate items, such as Embargo or the Klutz Ability. Similarly, item-altering moves like Trick and Fling are incapable of transferring the Beedrillite to or from a Beedrill. In the context of Pokémon Contest Spectaculars, if a Beedrill holds this item and its Trainer possesses a Key Stone, the Beedrillite enables it to Mega Evolve into Mega Beedrill when executing a Spectacular Talent. This Mega Evolution persists until the conclusion of the Talent Round. Beedrillites held by any Pokémon within Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire cannot be transferred via trade to Pokémon X and Y. Its function extends to Pokémon Shuffle, where it empowers the player to achieve Beedrill's Mega Evolution. Acquisition for players involves successfully clearing the Competitive stage that appears during Week 17 of the game's event rotation. Furthermore, it was distributed as a reward during the Mega Beedrill competitive stage while the game's live service was active, facilitating the aforementioned Mega Evolution. In the manga, specifically PS616, Giovanni was shown to possess a Beedrillite. He utilized it to Mega Evolve his Beedrill, illustrating his theory that the meteoroid imperiling the world might contain a Pokémon within.

Title: Key Stone For the object to which Spiritomb is bound, consult the entry for Odd Keystone.

Designated as a Key Item since its debut in Generation VI, the Key Stone (Japanese: キーストーン Key Stone) serves as the catalyst for a Pokémon's Mega Evolution, provided that Pokémon is equipped with its specific Mega Stone. This item cannot be acquired within Generation VIII games. Upon activation of a Key Stone, the Mega Stone held by a Pokémon responds, initiating the transformative process known as Mega Evolution. Trainers frequently embed their Key Stones within personal accoutrements like a Mega Ring, Mega Bracelet, Z-Ring, or Z-Power Ring; however, alternative methods of carrying are known.

Despite its crucial role in the Mega Evolution phenomenon, the precise origins of Key Stones remain largely unconfirmed. Within the narratives of Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, both Key Stones and Mega Stones are strongly implicated in events tied to ancient meteoroid impacts across the Hoenn region. Matt discovered a Key Stone, while Professor Cozmo unearthed a Galladite, each found separately in Meteor Falls, a locale historically recognized for its meteor strikes. Zinnia, speaking from Sky Pillar, relayed Draconid legends describing a massive, luminous meteoroid impact 1,000 years prior – the event that formed the crater now home to Sootopolis City. This celestial event enabled Rayquaza to heed humanity's pleas for deliverance during a dire crisis and achieve the planet's inaugural Mega Evolution; consequently, this meteoroid itself functioned as a Key Stone. Maxie or Archie, depending on the game, also entertained the notion of imbuing a Meteorite with the properties of either a Key Stone or a Mega Stone. Key Stones, distinct from Mega Stones, bear no known connection to the activation of the ultimate weapon within the Kalos region. A longstanding legend tied to the Tower of Mastery recounts a Trainer and their Lucario discovering the initial documented Key Stone and Mega Stone within its vicinity. Loading screen information from Pokémon Legends: Z-A indicates that Key Stones operate by intensifying the emotional connection between a Trainer and their Pokémon, thereby allowing the channeling of Mega Evolution's power.

The Key Stone grants the player the capability to trigger a Pokémon's Mega Evolution, provided that Pokémon holds its specific Mega Stone. Trainers are restricted to Mega Evolving a single Pokémon per battle, meaning only one Mega-Evolved Pokémon can exist on their side at any one time. In Pokémon X and Y, the Key Stone belonging to Calem or Serena is incorporated into their black Mega Ring. Brendan or May, in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, possesses a Key Stone set within their Mega Bracelet. For Pokémon Contest Spectaculars, Brandon opts for a Mega Cuff, while May utilizes a Mega Charm. Elio's or Selene's Key Stone, across Pokémon Sun, Moon, Ultra Sun, and Ultra Moon, is housed within their Z-Ring or Z-Power Ring. Nevertheless, this Key Stone is not visibly rendered on the player's in-game model. In Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, Chase or Elaine carries their Key Stone without an accessory, and it likewise does not manifest on their player model. Paxton or Harmony in Pokémon Legends: Z-A has their Key Stone integrated into a golden Mega Ring.

Numerous non-player characters (NPCs) store their Key Stones in distinctive artifacts, frequently unlike those available to the player. Korrina employs a Mega Glove (Japanese: メガグローブ Mega Glove), a white fingerless glove adorned with a red cuff, worn on her left hand. Her Key Stone is affixed to the glove's back. Lysandre sports a Mega Ring (Japanese: メガリング Mega Ring), a silver ring he wears on his left middle finger, with the Key Stone securely bezel-set into it. Diantha utilizes a Mega Charm (Japanese: メガチャーム Mega Charm), a necklace featuring a golden spiked pendant. Her Key Stone is embedded within the charm's curved section. Steven Stone's Key Stone is bezel-set into his Mega Stickpin (Japanese: メガラペルピン Mega Lapel Pin), which he affixes to the left lapel of his jacket. Wally carries a Mega Pendant (Japanese: メガペンダント Mega Pendant), a silver chain ornament with his Key Stone centrally mounted. Archie displays a Mega Anchor (Japanese: メガイカリ Mega Anchor), a golden anchor chain worn around his neck, with the Key Stone nested within the anchor's ring. Maxie employs Mega Glasses (Japanese: メガメガネ Mega Glasses), a pair of half-rim glasses featuring thick black frames, with his Key Stone integrated into the right-side connection between the frame and the temples. Lisia wears a Mega Tiara (Japanese: メガティアラ Mega Tiara), a blue hair-clip comprising three overlapping diamonds, the central one holding her Key Stone. Zinnia's Mega Anklet (Japanese: メガアンクレット Mega Anklet), a teal metallic band encircling her right calf, incorporates her Key Stone into one of its upper indentations.

During the Delta Episode, Courtney and Matt utilize Mega Rings, though these do not appear in their official artwork. Post-Delta Episode, Sidney, Phoebe, Glacia, and Drake employ generic Key Stones, absent from their artwork. Sina and Dexio each wear a white Mega Ring on their left wrist. Certain adversaries encountered at the Battle Tree and Battle Royal Dome possess generic Key Stones, which are not depicted on their character models. Any opponent capable of Mega Evolving a Pokémon uses generic Key Stones, none of which appear on their models. Urbain or Taunie, Naveen, and Lida each sport golden Mega Rings on their left wrist. Emma's Key Stone is integrated into the chest piece of her Expansion Suit. Vinnie's Key Stone is set into the palm of his left glove. Tarragon's Key Stone is fixed within his construction helmet. Canari's Key Stone is fastened to the front zipper of her crop-top. Ivor's Key Stone is implanted on the back of his right glove. Corbeau and Philippe's Key Stones are mounted on their silver Rust Syndicate lapel pins, which adorn their suit jackets. Lebanne's Key Stone is secured to the choker she incorporates into her ensemble. Grisham, Griselle, and L employ Mega Rings akin to Lysandre's from Pokémon X and Y. Grisham and Griselle don their Mega Rings on the middle finger of their left hands, whereas L carries his in his pocket. During the Z-A Royale, all adversaries capable of Mega Evolving their Pokémon utilize generic Key Stones, which remain unseen on their character models. Upon successful completion of the Raid of the Mighty General challenge, the player acquires a Mega Key.

In Mega Evolution Special I, Alain was observed wearing a Mega Ring resembling those of the Pokémon X and Y protagonists. He employed this ring to Mega Evolve his Charizard into Mega Charizard X. Mega Evolution Special II disclosed that Lysandre was the donor of his Mega Ring. Subsequent to the Team Flare crisis, Alain surrendered his Mega Ring to authorities for evidentiary purposes and committed to independently locating a replacement Key Stone. Within that same episode, Astrid's Key Stone was showcased as an earring adorning her right ear; concurrently, Remo's Key Stone was integrated into the tip of his personal umbrella. Siebold, an esteemed member of the Kalos Elite Four, had his Key Stone displayed within his necktie. Astrid's Key Stone briefly resurfaced during the introductory sequence of Diancie and the Cocoon of Destruction. In The Bonds of Evolution!, Diantha harnessed her Mega Charm's power to Mega Evolve her Gardevoir, empowering it to escape Team Rocket's grasp. This Mega Charm has also been observed in Diantha's later appearances. Mega Revelations! depicted Korrina wearing her Key Stone within her Mega Glove, an inheritance from her grandfather. Yet, her Lucario lacked a Lucarionite, preventing immediate use. Following the acquisition of a Lucarionite in The Cave of Trials!, she promptly utilized her Key Stone to Mega Evolve her Lucario. In The Aura Storm!, Gurkinn activated a Key Stone to Mega Evolve his personal Lucario. Like Korrina, he wears it in a glove on his left hand. Mabel was shown possessing a Key Stone in The Bonds of Mega Evolution!. Integrated into her walking stick, she employs it to transform her Mawile into Mega Mawile. During Diancie and the Cocoon of Destruction, a diamond previously crafted by Diancie and bestowed upon Bonnie triggered Diancie's Mega Evolution, functioning as a Key Stone. By the film's conclusion, Bonnie returned the diamond to Diancie. Steven Stone and his Mega Stickpin were central to Mega Evolution Special II. Alain, identifying the Key Stone on the Champion's person, was prompted to challenge Steven to a contest. Within the same installment, Steven and Alain unearthed an enormous Key Stone. As Lysandre's scientific team examined it, Rayquaza manifested and channeled the Key Stone's energy to Mega Evolve into Mega Rayquaza. Immediately thereafter, it vanished amidst the turmoil unleashed by Rayquaza's Draco Meteor.

Title: Meteorite Shard

Originating in Generation VI, the Meteorite Shard is categorized as a Key Item, bearing the Japanese name いんせきのかけら Meteorite Shard. Its crucial role unfolds during the Delta Episode, where it becomes integral to Professor Cozmo's scheme for deflecting an asteroid threatening to collide with the planet. Once obtained by the player from Zinnia, the item must be transported to Professor Cozmo and Steven, who await its arrival at the Mossdeep Space Center. Subsequently, Professor Cozmo seeks a more refined shard; however, this particular specimen is located by Steven at Meteor Falls, and it is Steven himself, rather than the player, who conveys it to Professor Cozmo.

Title: Eon Flute

First introduced as a Key Item in Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, the Eon Flute (Japanese: むげんのふえ Infinity Flute) is a legendary artifact linked to Latias and Latios. In Pokémon Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire, Steven Stone initially holds this item, later granting it to the player after the global disaster caused by Groudon or Kyogre is resolved. Playing the Eon Flute in these games calls forth Latios or Latias, enabling the user to soar over Hoenn's skies and travel to any city, route, or island. In the "Latias Flies" storyline, Ruby utilized the Eon Flute on Southern Island to summon Latias and Latios. It was also implied Emerald had previously possessed the artifact.

Title: Bottle Cap

First appearing in Generation VII, the Bottle Cap (known in Japanese as ぎんのおうかん, or Silver Bottle Cap) functions as an exchangeable item. A superior variant, the Gold Bottle Cap, holds greater value. Its primary function is facilitating Hyper Training, allowing a Pokémon's individual values (IVs) to be maximized in one specific stat. This service is provided by characters such as Mr. Hyper, a League Staff attendant, a Veteran, a man accompanied by an Abomasnow, or a Fist of Justice attendant.

The Bottle Cap, specifically referred to as a Silver Bottle Cap within Pokémon GO, debuted on November 10, 2025, obtainable through GO Pass Deluxe: Into the Wild. Applying a Silver Bottle Cap to a Pokémon initiates Hyper Training, boosting one of that Pokémon's IVs upon completion of certain tasks. Notably, these Silver Bottle Caps, much like Lucky Trinkets, come with an expiration date, after which they are automatically purged from the player's Bag. Unlike its core series counterpart, the GO iteration permits players to determine the extent of each IV increase using the Silver Bottle Cap. Once Hyper Training has commenced, players are allotted 365 days to finalize the process. Restrictions apply to its use: only Pokémon designated as Good Buddies or higher are eligible, and it cannot be applied to Shadow Pokémon or those with all three IVs already at their peak.

In Pokémon Masters EX, James, during certain Pokémon Center interactions, expresses dissatisfaction with Trainers who utilize their Bottle Caps for Hyper Training rather than accumulating them for collection. This sentiment alludes to James's well-known penchant for collecting bottle caps, as depicted in Pokémon the Series. The Japanese term おうかん (ōkan) possesses a dual meaning, signifying both "crown" and "bottle cap." This linguistic double entendre is visually echoed in the Bottle Cap's design, which prominently features a crown. Interestingly, despite the existence of distinct words for "bottle cap" in Korean and Chinese, their translations for the item directly transcribe the Japanese term, thereby preserving the "crown" implication.

Title: Gold Bottle Cap

An exchangeable item making its debut in Generation VII, the Gold Bottle Cap is known in Japanese as きんのおうかん (Gold Bottle Cap). It stands as a premium variant of the standard Bottle Cap. When presented to Mr. Hyper, a League Staff attendant, a Veteran, a man accompanied by an Abomasnow, or a Fist of Justice attendant, it enables a Pokémon's full set of IVs to be maximized through Hyper Training.

Within Pokémon GO, Gold Bottle Caps became available on June 23, 2025, obtainable through the GO Pass Deluxe: Ancients Recovered. Applying a Gold Bottle Cap to a Pokémon in this game starts Hyper Training, which allows for the enhancement of up to all three of that Pokémon's IVs by fulfilling specific objectives. Similar to Lucky Trinkets, these items carry an expiration date; failure to use them before this deadline results in their automatic removal from the player's Bag. Distinct from the core series games, players in Pokémon GO possess the flexibility to determine the degree to which each individual IV is boosted by the Gold Bottle Cap. Following the initiation of Hyper Training, users are granted a 365-day period to finalize the process. Usage of Gold Bottle Caps is restricted to Pokémon designated as Good Buddies or higher, and they are incompatible with Shadow Pokémon or those that already possess maximum values across all three of their IVs.

The visual representation of Gold Bottle Caps appears on the artwork for the Special Full Art version of the Galarian Perrserker V card, featured in the Lost Origin expansion. The term おうかん (ōkan) in its Japanese name carries dual meanings: "crown" or "bottle cap." This linguistic duality is reflected in the Gold Bottle Cap's aesthetic, which prominently features a crown. Even though Korean and Chinese languages utilize distinct terms for "bottle cap," their respective translations of the item's name uphold the "crown" interpretation, a consequence of their direct transcription from the Japanese original.

Title: Fishing Rod

This entry details the Key Item known as Fishing Rod. For information on distinct fishing implements, consult the pages for Old Rod, Good Rod, and Super Rod. Designated as a Key Item, the Fishing Rod (Japanese: つりざお) made its debut in Generation VII, enabling trainers to engage in fishing activities. This tool permits fishing in specific underwater rock formations. Outcomes from using this item can include reeling in a Pokémon, an item, or nothing at all. Trainers may fish either from solid ground or while utilizing Lapras Surf; however, fishing is not possible when using Sharpedo Jet.

Within any location featuring fishing spots, at least one rock pile will conspicuously emit bubbles to the surface; casting a line into such a bubbling spot significantly heightens the probability of encountering Pokémon that are typically uncommon in that area's fishing locations. Should a player successfully reel in a Pokémon or item from a bubbling spot, the bubbles will dissipate immediately and will only reappear once the player exits and subsequently re-enters the area. Furthermore, these bubbles vanish if the player moves excessively fast near the location, travels directly over it, or attempts to reel in their rod prematurely. Without the presence of bubbles, the fishing spot operates identically to all other standard fishing points within that locale.

Throughout the Alola region, locations suitable for fishing include Seaward Cave, Kala'e Bay, Melemele Sea, Paniola Town, Brooklet Hill, Akala Outskirts, Malie Garden, Secluded Shore, Seafolk Village, Poni Wilds, Poni Breaker Coast, Vast Poni Canyon, Poni Meadow, Poni Gauntlet, and Routes 7, 8, 9, 13, 14, and 15.

Additionally, the item permits fishing at visible shadows cast upon the water's surface. Within the Galar region, fishing opportunities are available at Motostoke, Hulbury, the Slumbering Weald, Galar Mine No. 2, and Routes 2, 4, 5, 6, and 9. The Wild Area further contains numerous fishing locales, specifically at East Lake Axewell, West Lake Axewell, Axew's Eye, South Lake Miloch, the Giant's Seat, North Lake Miloch, the Motostoke Riverbank, Bridge Field, the Dusty Bowl, the Giant's Mirror, the Giant's Cap, and the Lake of Outrage. On the Isle of Armor, one can find fishing spots at the Fields of Honor, the Soothing Wetlands, the Forest of Focus, Challenge Beach, the Courageous Cavern, Loop Lagoon, the Training Lowlands, the Workout Sea, the Stepping-Stone Sea, the Insular Sea, the Honeycalm Sea, and Honeycalm Island. The Crown Tundra presents fishing opportunities in the Giant's Bed, Snowslide Slope, the Giant's Foot, the Frigid Sea, and Ballimere Lake.

What follows is an enumeration of cards from the Pokémon Trading Card Game that prominently feature the Fishing Rod.

Title: Sparkling Stone

Introduced in Generation VII, the Sparkling Stone (かがやくいし in Japanese) functions as a Key Item. It serves as the base ingredient for crafting a Z-Ring or Z-Power Ring. Tapu deities bestow these stones upon island kahunas, who then transform them into Z-Rings or Z-Power Rings before presenting them to deserving Pokémon Trainers. On occasion, a tapu might directly grant a Sparkling Stone to a Trainer, bypassing the kahuna, a scenario exemplified by the player character. Professor Kukui and Lillie suggest that Sparkling Stones originate from fragments of Necrozma's form, dislodged by the ancient actions of the Ultra Recon Squad's forebears. These fragments subsequently reached Alola, having traversed an Ultra Wormhole. This characteristic is also observed in Z-Crystals.

Tapu Koko presents the player with a Sparkling Stone as an acknowledgment of their courage shown in safeguarding Nebby from a flock of aggressive wild Spearow. Following the player's initial victory over Hau, or simply upon their return to Iki Town, Hala identifies the stone. Perceiving that Melemele Island's guardian deity has acknowledged the player, he proposes to refine it. The completed item, either a Z-Ring or Z-Power Ring, is then given to the player after they conclude the ceremonial battle against Hau. Tapu Fini also bestows an additional, player-unobtainable Sparkling Stone upon Hapu when she assumes the role of Poni Island's kahuna. Hapu subsequently integrates this into her personal Z-Ring or Z-Power Ring. This particular Sparkling Stone exhibits a brown hue, differing from the white or black one the player acquired previously. To obtain a Z-Ring or Z-Power Ring, the player must present the Sparkling Stone to Hala in Iki Town for his craftsmanship.

Within the anime episode "Treasure Hunt, Akala Style!", Lana discovered a Sparkling Stone, referred to as a Z-Ring ore (Ｚリングのゲンセキ in Japanese), during a Stoutland Search treasure hunt orchestrated by Olivia for Ash and his classmates. Olivia then acquired the stone, converting it into a Z-Ring, which she presented to Lana in the subsequent episode. In "The Delivery of Rotom and the Girl," Sun was also given a Sparkling Stone by Tapu Koko. During "The Party Crasher and Guzma the Destroyer," Sun employed his Sparkling Stone as a makeshift splint for a fractured arm. In "The Final Match and a Surprising Finale!," when provoked by Gladion, Sun's Sparkling Stone inadvertently absorbed Z-Power emanating from a proximate Ultra Wormhole. This enabled Sun's Litten, named Dollar, to execute the Z-Move Inferno Overdrive, which led to the defeat of Gladion's Type: Null. The immense exertion of this attack caused Sun to immediately faint from fatigue. Subsequently, Hala retrieved the Sparkling Stone to fashion it into a Z-Ring for Sun's utilization. Upon completion, Hala arranged for Kahili to transport it to Sun at the Ruins of Life.

"The Wild Full-Power Pose of Fire" disclosed that Kiawe possessed a Z-Ring, which he had initially acquired as a Sparkling Stone from Tapu Lele half a decade prior. To hinder Sun from deploying Inferno Overdrive against him once more, Gladion commanded his Type: Null to break Kiawe's Z-Ring as Kiawe tried to offer it to Sun, though the Sparkling Stone itself remained undamaged. In "The Truth and Island Kahuna Hapu," Hapu was given a Sparkling Stone by Tapu Fini once it recognized her as the forthcoming kahuna of Poni Island. "Shock!! Father in the Pendant!" unveiled Hau's disclosure that, during his training, he successfully obtained a Sparkling Stone, which Olivia subsequently crafted into a Z-Ring for his use. Notably, Lillie, Hala, Hapu, and the publication "The Tapu and the Island Kahunas" consistently rendered the item's name in lowercase within Pokémon Sun and Moon; this capitalization error was corrected in Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon.

Title: Adrenaline Orb

Making its debut in Generation VII, the Adrenaline Orb (known in Japanese as ビビリだま, or Scare Orb) is a consumable held item. When activated from the Bag during a struggle, it heightens the likelihood of wild Pokémon summoning allies in an SOS Battle. Within Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon specifically, wild Pokémon are limited to a single ally call per combat unless a player deploys an Adrenaline Orb. It is expended post-use and permits only one activation per engagement. Should it be attempted repeatedly, the Adrenaline Orb will fail to function and will not be consumed. If the Pokémon holding it is targeted by Intimidate, the Adrenaline Orb is used up, and its Speed stat is elevated by one stage. This item is not consumed if the holder's Speed stat stages have already reached their maximum of +6; however, if the holder possesses the Contrary Ability, it will not activate if its Speed stat stages are already at -6. It triggers even if the holder remains unaffected by Intimidate due to its Ability (for instance, Hyper Cutter) or the presence of Mist, but it will not activate if the holder is unaffected because its Attack stat stages are already at -6 (or at +6 in the situation of Contrary). Should Intimidate affect multiple Pokémon, each equipped with an Adrenaline Orb, every one of these Pokémon will be impacted by Intimidate and consume their respective Adrenaline Orb *before* Symbiosis initiates the transfer of any item, including additional Adrenaline Orbs.

Title: Zygarde Cube

Introduced in Generation VII, the Zygarde Cube (Japanese: ジガルデキューブ Zygarde Cube) functions as a Key Item. This device collects Zygarde Cells and Cores. It provides the means for Zygarde to shift between its 10% and 50% Formes. Additionally, it enables Zygarde to learn its unique moves and to swap its abilities between Aura Break and Power Construct. When paired with the Reassembly Unit, it also facilitates the creation of a new Zygarde.

Professor Sycamore is credited with discovering the Zygarde Cube. Its purpose is to house Zygarde Cells and Cores, which players can then utilize with the Reassembly Unit. The Cube's capacity allows for the storage of up to 999 Cells and Cores, achievable through the Reassembly Unit's separation function. Furthermore, it enables Zygarde possessing the Power Construct Ability to alternate between its 10% and 50% Formes.

Within Pokémon Sun and Moon, players can locate Cells and Cores throughout most areas of Alola; however, these components only materialize after the Zygarde Cube has been acquired. The Alola region contains a total of 95 Cells and 5 Cores. They manifest as subtle glowing sparks on the ground: green for Cells and pink for Cores. Even if the Zygarde Cube reaches its maximum capacity, players are still able to gather additional Cells and Cores from the environment. Selecting the "Check" option on the Zygarde Cube displays a count of collected Cells and Cores from game locations, alongside the current total held. Upon reaching specific collection benchmarks, Dexio and Sina will get in touch with the player to elucidate the Zygarde Cube, its Cells, and its Cores.

Conversely, Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon feature no wild Zygarde Cells or Cores for collection. Here, the player receives the Zygarde Cube after capturing the 50% Forme Zygarde in Resolution Cave and subsequently acquiring a 10% Forme Zygarde from Dexio. The Cube, when initially obtained, already holds 40 Cells and Cores, furnishing players with a total of 100 Cells and Cores for interaction with the Reassembly Unit (assuming at least one Zygarde is separated).

Historically, the Zygarde Cube had the capability to impart five moves to Zygarde. In Pokémon Sun and Moon, teaching each move necessitated locating specific Zygarde Cores. For Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, however, all five moves became immediately available for teaching upon receipt of the Zygarde Cube. The Zygarde Cube's function of teaching Zygarde moves has since been removed. Presently, Zygarde's signature moves can be learned as level 1 moves through Jack's Move Reminding service, and Dragon Dance is acquired via TR51. In Generation VIII, Extreme Speed is no longer a learnable move for Zygarde, unless it's migrated from a Generation VII title via Pokémon HOME. Nonetheless, its capacity to learn Extreme Speed returns in Pokémon Legends: Z-A at level 81.

The Zygarde Cube currently serves to transform Zygarde into its 10% or 50% Forme and to toggle its Ability between Aura Break and Power Construct (notably, Ability Capsules and Ability Patches are ineffective on Zygarde). This item also enables players to store a maximum of 300* Zygarde Cells, which are essential for altering Zygarde's physical manifestation.
