In a separate context, the Zygarde Cube's acquisition is tied to completing 3/8 of the 'From A to Zygarde' Special Research set. Its initial appearance was documented in Following Traces of Laquium, where Hamber exhibited it to Zirc and Onia, observing his Shiny Zygarde Cell instantly entering the device. Subsequently, he entrusted the artifact to them for their quest to locate the White Zygarde. Later, during Charcadet's Wish, Zirc presented the Zygarde Cube to Amethio, aiming to uncover the White Zygarde's Cells to thwart Spinel's nefarious scheme.

In the Pokémon Adventures manga, the Zygarde Cube made its debut in Confusion and Monsters from Another World. Sun was contracted by Sina and Dexio to help in the search for Zygarde's scattered Cells and Cores throughout Alola. To facilitate this task, Dexio provided Sun with the Zygarde Cube for collecting any found Cells and Cores.

Although designated as a 'cube', its physical form is much more akin to a truncated octahedron. Both shapes, however, share octahedral symmetry, evidenced by the Zygarde Cube's square facets which can be perfectly aligned with the faces of a standard cube. According to its concept art, an unpopulated Zygarde Cube typically appears black, gradually intensifying its green luminescence as it accumulates Zygarde Cells and Cores.

Title: Ice Stone

A specific Evolution item known as the Ice Stone (こおりのいし in Japanese) made its debut in Generation VII. It facilitates the evolution of various Pokémon species, and its application results in its consumption. It triggers the metamorphosis of Alolan Sandshrew into Alolan Sandslash, and similarly, prompts Alolan Vulpix to become Alolan Ninetales. From Generation VIII onwards, Eevee can utilize it to evolve into Glaceon; however, an Eevee possessing the Gigantamax Factor is ineligible for evolution into Glaceon via an Ice Stone. Additionally, Galarian Darumaka develops into Galarian Darmanitan thanks to the Ice Stone. Beginning with Generation IX, Crabrawler's evolution into Crabominable also requires this item. In Pokémon Sleep, certain Pokémon necessitate an Ice Stone for evolution, and its use depletes the item. Evolving Alolan Vulpix into Alolan Ninetales requires 80 Vulpix Candy and an Ice Stone. For Eevee to become Glaceon, 80 Eevee Candy and an Ice Stone are essential.

The anime episode 'Not Caving Under Pressure!' marked the first appearance of an Ice Stone, discovered by Sophocles's Charjabug. This occurred as Charjabug assisted in repairing a cave populated by Alolan Sandshrew, uncovering that the location was, in reality, an Ice Stone mine where these Pokémon congregated for evolution. Following this discovery, the pack's enormous Sandshrew leader made contact with the stone and subsequently transformed into an Alolan Sandslash. Subsequently, upon leaving the cave, Charjabug was found to have acquired a second Ice Stone, which Sophocles then presented to Lillie. Lillie inquired of Snowy if it wished to employ the stone for evolution; however, Snowy indicated it wasn't yet prepared, prompting Lillie to retain possession of the item for future consideration.

This item has been included in a merchandise collection named after the Evolution stones, appearing alongside its counterparts. Furthermore, an Ice Stone was offered as part of a keychain featuring Alolan Vulpix. Its initial public revelation occurred on October 4, 2016, via an image on the official Spanish Pokémon website. Nonetheless, this image was promptly withdrawn because the stone had not been officially introduced prior. While unobtainable within the games, data for the Ice Stone is present in Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, and Eevee is coded to be capable of evolving into Glaceon through its use there.

Title: Beast Ball

Known in Japanese as ウルトラボール (Ultra Ball), the Beast Ball is a specialized Poké Ball type that made its debut in Generation VII. Its primary function is to capture wild Pokémon, demonstrating enhanced effectiveness when used on Ultra Beasts, but significantly reduced efficacy against all other Pokémon. Within the narrative of Pokémon Sun and Moon, Faba of the Aether Foundation is credited with the development of Beast Balls, a creation based upon the prior research of an unnamed precursor. However, in Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, their genesis is attributed to a collaborative effort between the Aether Foundation and the Ultra Recon Squad. When deployed from the player's Bag during a wild Pokémon encounter, the Beast Ball endeavors to secure the wild Pokémon. Should the encountered Pokémon be an Ultra Beast, its catch rate receives a 5× multiplier; conversely, for any other Pokémon, the multiplier is a mere 410/4096× (approximately 0.1×). Notably, in Pokémon Legends: Z-A, the modifier for non-Ultra Beasts is applied directly as 0.1, instead of being derived from an estimation based on a 1/4096 multiple. Certain circumstances prohibit the use of Beast Balls, mirroring restrictions on standard Poké Balls; these include wild battles featuring multiple opposing Pokémon or encounters with a trial Pokémon. Attempting to use one during a Trainer battle results in the opposing Trainer deflecting it, thereby consuming the player's turn to no effect.

A cinematic sequence depicts Lusamine deploying a Nihilego from a Beast Ball just prior to their fusion. Members of the Ultra Recon Squad consistently house their Pokémon within Beast Balls. The Poipole acquired by the player within the Max Lair is likewise contained within a Beast Ball.

Within Pokémon GO, the Beast Ball functions as a distinctive, event-specific Poké Ball, exclusively employable during Ultra Beast encounters. These possess a substantial ×20 catch rate multiplier. This catch multiplier also extends to the capture of Necrozma. At present, the Beast Ball's availability in Pokémon GO is limited to particular in-game events. During the periods these events are underway, Beast Balls become the sole type of Poké Ball permitted for catching an Ultra Beast. In Raid Battle bonus challenges, players are supplied with a complement of Beast Balls, substituting for the usual Premier Balls. Encounters earned through Research and GO Battle League rewards provide players with an unlimited reservoir of Beast Balls. It is worth noting that a potential software bug prevents the use of Berries during Ultra Beast encounters. Much like the Premier Ball, it is never deposited into the player's Bag and is not retained once the encounter concludes.

The Beast Ball made its inaugural appearance in the episode A Mission of Ultra Urgency!, an episode where the Aether Foundation furnished Ash and his classmates with numerous units for their Ultra Guardians assignments. Within that very same episode, Ash utilized a Beast Ball to effect a temporary capture of a Buzzwole. Later, in Love at First Twirl!, Ash expended nearly all Beast Balls supplied to the group in an attempt to capture a Poipole, intending to care for it until its Ultra Wormhole's location could be ascertained. His endeavor ultimately bore fruit with the final Beast Ball from the allocated container. During Rise and Shine, Starship!, both Ash and Kiawe attempted to capture a Celesteela using Beast Balls, but their efforts were unsuccessful as the Pokémon was immovably anchored to the ground. Twirling with a Bang! saw Lana and Lillie each employ a Beast Ball for the temporary capture of a Xurkitree and a Blacephalon, in that order. The Long Vault Home! chronicled Ash, Lana, Kiawe, and Mallow's individual attempts to secure a Stakataka using multiple Beast Balls, all of which the Pokémon evaded with ease. Ultimately, Ash managed to successfully capture the Stakataka after removing a statue affixed to its form and assuring it of their intention to return it to its native habitat. Beauty is Only Crystal Deep! featured Ash's use of a Beast Ball for the temporary containment of a Pheromosa, facilitating its return journey. In Living on the Cutting Edge!, Lana's attempt to capture a Kartana with a Beast Ball was thwarted when the Pokémon effortlessly severed the device. The Homecoming Crown! depicted Lillie's family using a Beast Ball to catch a Shiny Nihilego, affectionately named "Lillie," which had been caring for Mohn; Gladion subsequently assumed its guardianship.

In the Pokémon Adventures manga, Beast Balls were developed for the Aether Foundation by Blake, aided by the Ultra Recon Squad, specifically as a method for capturing Ultra Beasts. Their initial appearance occurred in Arrival at the Scientific Megalopolis, an installment where Blake, impersonating Colress, presented one hundred of these Balls to Lusamine to support her scheme of capturing the formidable Ultra Beast, Necrozma. Darkness!! The Mysterious Claws of Necrozma! showcased Lusamine employing a Beast Ball to secure the Naganadel that Phyco had utilized for traversing Ultra Space. During Madness!! Mother Lusamine!, having arrived at Ultra Megalopolis atop Naganadel, she commenced deploying her Beast Balls against Necrozma. While Lusamine's focus remained fixed on Necrozma, Sun seized the opportunity to use a discarded Beast Ball of hers to capture a Stakataka for himself. Moon, too, attempted to employ a Beast Ball to disjoin Lusamine from her merged Nihilego, though her aim proved unsuccessful. Transcend!! Ultra Necrozma! unveiled Anabel as the custodian of the final known Beast Ball, en route to Mount Lanakila where Sun and Zygarde were locked in combat with Necrozma. By Finale!! The Battle Against the Other Dimension!, Necrozma was vanquished, but its desperate final assault struck Looker and Anabel's balloon, resulting in the Beast Ball's destruction. Sun subsequently disclosed that the Beast Ball Moon had previously directed at Nihilego had serendipitously arrived at Mount Lanakila via an Ultra Wormhole. He extended the offer to Anabel, but she, believing humanity unfit to wield Necrozma's power, instead entrusted the Beast Ball to the Ultra Recon Squad, with Zossie ultimately using it to capture Necrozma.

In the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Beast Ball, introduced in the Celestial Storm expansion, grants its player the ability to inspect their Prize cards. Should an Ultra Beast be discovered among them, the player is permitted to reveal it and transfer it to their hand, replacing the original Prize card with the Beast Ball. Distinctively, in Pokémon the Series: Sun & Moon, the light emitted upon an Ultra Beast's capture by a Beast Ball displays a blend of red and blue, diverging from the solely red illumination of most other Poké Balls. Exclusively within Pokémon Sun and Moon, both Pheromosa and Kartana possess a maximum catch rate of 255, ensuring that a Beast Ball will invariably succeed in capturing them in these specific titles. Across all European language renditions of a promotional trailer for Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, Naganadel is depicted emerging from a Beast Ball. Nevertheless, no legitimate method exists to acquire a Naganadel housed within a Beast Ball in these games; it is only legitimately obtained as a gift Pokémon within a standard Poké Ball, and being gender unknown, it cannot breed to pass down a Beast Ball even if such a mechanism existed. Finally, the Beast Ball's Japanese appellation (ウルトラボール) is a direct transliteration of the English term "Ultra Ball," which, paradoxically, refers to an entirely distinct type of Poké Ball in English.

Title: Big Malasada

Introduced in Generation VII, the Big Malasada (Japanese: おおきいマラサダ Big Malasada) is a medicinal item that originates as a local specialty of the Alola region. Its primary function is to relieve a Pokémon of all non-volatile status conditions and confusion. When utilized from the Bag directly on a Pokémon, it effectively cures the afflicted Pokémon of all non-volatile status conditions, specifically poison, paralysis, sleep, burn, and freeze, along with confusion. Within Pokémon: Magikarp Jump, Big Malasadas can appear as food items for Magikarp consumption. They become available for unlock once a player reaches Trainer Rank 81, requiring a cost of 931,392. In Pokémon Sleep, the Big Malasada exists as a potential cooking recipe, requiring Pure Oil, Moomoo Milk, and Honey as its components. Big Malasada made its debut as an Item card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game during the English Sun & Moon Series (corresponding to the Japanese Sun & Moon Era). It was initially released in Japan through the Collection Moon and Sun & Moon Starter Set, which were later combined to form the English Sun & Moon expansion. The card's artwork is attributed to 5ban Graphics. This Big Malasada card allows players to heal 20 damage and remove one Special Condition (which is equivalent to status conditions found in the core series games) from their Active Pokémon. Beyond the games, Malasadas have also been prominently featured in several officially licensed figures and various goods.

Title: Yellow Nectar

This consumable, known in Japan as やまぶきのミツ (Golden Yellow Nectar), first appeared in the seventh generation of games under the name Yellow Nectar. It belongs to a group of nectars specifically linked to the Pokémon Oricorio. Applying it to an Oricorio from the player's Bag will cause the Pokémon to adopt its Pom-Pom Style. This item's effect can only be triggered when not engaged in combat. Each application expends the item. Its significance extended to the anime episode "A Seasoned Search!", where Mallow required Yellow Nectar as an ingredient for a dish at her family's restaurant, prompting her and Ash to embark on a search for it. Their quest led them to track a Baile Style Oricorio into a secluded meadow; there, they observed it consuming Yellow Nectar from a blossom, subsequently transforming into its Pom-Pom Style. Mallow then collected some Nectar directly from a flower, successfully acquiring the necessary component. Furthermore, within "Flash and Cosmog's Secret", Professor Burnet referenced her numerous trips to Melemele Meadow specifically for the purpose of gathering Yellow Nectar.

Title: Sun Flute

Known in Japanese as たいようのふえ (Taiyō no Fue), the Sun Flute is a pivotal Key Item first introduced in Generation VII. In the games Pokémon Moon and Ultra Moon, Lusamine initially possesses this artifact. After the player triumphs over Lusamine, Gladion discovers it amongst her possessions and subsequently presents it to Lillie. Conversely, in Pokémon Sun and Ultra Sun, this item is situated on Exeggutor Island, where players acquire it directly into their Bag upon discovery.

The synchronous performance of both the Sun Flute and Moon Flute at the Altar of the Sunne, a significant event in the main narrative of Pokémon Sun and Ultra Sun, triggers Nebby's evolution into Solgaleo. Similarly, when the Sun Flute and Moon Flute are harmoniously played at the Altar of the Moone during the storyline of Pokémon Moon and Ultra Moon, it facilitates Nebby's transformation into Lunala.

Regarding its animated appearances, the Sun Flute was depicted in The Eclipse, where Lillie was shown to be its holder. In that instance, when it was played alongside Selene's Moon Flute at the Altar of the Moone, it caused Nebby to evolve into Lunala. The item's debut in the anime occurred in The Truth and Island Kahuna Hapu; here, Lillie disclosed that she had brought it with her when she made her escape from Aether Paradise alongside Nebby. Upon seeing the flute, Gladion suggested proceeding to the Poni Altar. It was later bestowed upon Sun, who utilized it in conjunction with Moon's Moon Flute at the Poni Altar, enabling a Cosmoem to evolve into Solgaleo. Beyond its in-game and anime roles, the Sun Flute has also been showcased in various officially licensed merchandise and collectible figures.

Title: Moon Flute

Originating in Generation VII, the Moon Flute (つきのふえ in Japanese) functions as a Key Item. In Pokémon Sun and Ultra Sun, Lusamine holds this artifact. Following the player's triumph over Lusamine, Gladion locates it among her effects and presents it to Lillie. Conversely, in Pokémon Moon and Ultra Moon, one can find this object on Exeggutor Island. Discovering it results in the player acquiring it directly into their Bag. The synchronized playing of both the Sun Flute and the Moon Flute at the Altar of the Sunne, as part of the Pokémon Sun and Ultra Sun narrative, prompts Nebby's evolution into Solgaleo. Similarly, in the storyline of Pokémon Moon and Ultra Moon, a harmonious rendition of the Sun Flute and Moon Flute at the Altar of the Moone triggers Nebby's evolution into Lunala. Although its depiction was omitted, The Eclipse revealed the Moon Flute to be owned by Selene. Its combined performance with Lillie's Sun Flute at the Altar of the Moone led to Nebby's evolution into Lunala. Its initial appearance occurred in Battle in Vast Poni Canyon, at which point Hapu held it. Subsequently, Tapu Bulu seized it and bestowed it upon Moon, who then played it in conjunction with Sun's Sun Flute at the Poni Altar, thereby enabling Nebby's evolution into Lunala. This artifact has also been showcased across a range of officially licensed merchandise and figurines.

Title: Terrain Extender
Originating in Generation VII, the Terrain Extender (Japanese: グランドコート Ground Coat) is an item designed to be held by a Pokémon. Its primary function is to extend the active period of moves and Abilities that modify the battlefield's terrain. Should the Pokémon holding this item initiate terrain via its moves or Abilities, that terrain will persist for eight turns, rather than its usual five. When the Terrain Extender successfully prolongs a terrain's effect, the turn counter on the user interface will indicate 8 turns to the player whose Pokémon established it, while simultaneously displaying 5 turns to their opponent. After these initial 5 turns have concluded, the user interface will then uniformly show 8 turns for both players.

Title: Electric Seed

Originating in Generation VII, the Electric Seed (Japanese: エレキシード Electric Seed) is a specific kind of consumable held item. Its primary effect is to bolster the bearer's Defense when Electric Terrain is active on the field. Should a Pokémon be clutching an Electric Seed while the battle environment transitions into Electric Terrain, the item will be used up, consequently raising the Pokémon's Defense by one stage. This consumption takes place even if the holder is not directly influenced by the terrain's general effects (for example, if it is not grounded or is in the semi-invulnerable phase of a move).

The Electric Seed will not be consumed if its holder's Defense stat stages have already reached the maximum of +6. However, if the Pokémon's Ability is Contrary, the Seed will instead fail to activate if the holder's Defense stat stages are at -6.

Regarding activation priority, if Electric Terrain is established by Electric Surge and then immediately overridden by another terrain-altering Ability, the Electric Seed is consumed *before* the second Ability comes into play. Conversely, when a Pokémon carrying an Electric Seed and possessing a terrain-changing Ability switches into pre-existing Electric Terrain, its Ability will activate *prior* to the held Seed.

If the field becomes Electric Terrain and multiple Pokémon are each holding an Electric Seed, every Pokémon will consume its individual Electric Seed *before* the Symbiosis Ability can transfer any item, including other Electric Seeds.

The item's function also interacts with other status effects: If Magic Room or Embargo are set to dissipate on the same turn as Electric Terrain, the Electric Seed will activate *before* the terrain vanishes. Nonetheless, *while Magic Room is actively in effect*, the Electric Seed will not activate, despite Magic Room fading before terrain generally does.

Should an Electric Seed initially fail to activate because its holder's Defense was already at +6 (or -6 for a Contrary Ability user), it will not reactivate for that specific holder until the terrain changes, the holder is switched out, or its holder (or its Substitute) takes damage.

As of Generation IX, any Electric Seed expended during a battle will be returned to the holder once the encounter concludes.

Title: Psychic Seed

Known in Japanese as サイコシード (Psycho Seed), the Psychic Seed made its debut in Generation VII as a consumable held item. Its effect is to increase the Special Defense of the Pokémon holding it when Psychic Terrain is active. Specifically, if a Pokémon possesses a Psychic Seed when Psychic Terrain is established on the field, the item is consumed, and that Pokémon's Special Defense stat rises by one stage. This consumption occurs regardless of whether the holder would typically be affected by terrain effects, such as not being grounded or being in a semi-invulnerable state during a move. The item will not be consumed if the Pokémon's Special Defense has already reached its maximum of +6 stages. However, if the Pokémon has the Ability Contrary, it will instead not activate if its Special Defense has reached the minimum of −6 stages. Should Psychic Terrain be established by Psychic Surge and then immediately superseded by another terrain-altering Ability, the Psychic Seed's effect will trigger prior to the second Ability's activation. Conversely, when a Pokémon possessing both a Psychic Seed and a terrain-changing Ability enters the battle while Psychic Terrain is already present, its Ability takes effect before the held Seed does. Given that Magic Room and Embargo conclude before terrain effects do, if Psychic Terrain is set to vanish on the same turn as either of those moves, the Seed will still activate before the terrain disappears. In scenarios where multiple Pokémon are each holding a Psychic Seed when Psychic Terrain becomes active, every Pokémon will consume its respective Psychic Seed before Symbiosis can transfer any item, including other Psychic Seeds. Should a Psychic Seed fail to activate because its holder's Special Defense is already at +6 stages (or −6 stages if the Ability is Contrary), it will remain inactive for that Pokémon until one of the following occurs: the terrain changes, the holder switches out, or the holder (or its Substitute) takes damage. Beginning with Generation IX, if a Psychic Seed is consumed during battle, it will be restored to the holder's inventory afterward. Despite Magic Room's effect expiring before the terrain's does, the Psychic Seed will not activate.

Title: Misty Seed

Originating in Generation VII, the Misty Seed (Japanese: ミストシード Mist Seed) functions as a single-use held item. Its primary effect is to augment the Special Defense of the Pokémon holding it specifically within Misty Terrain. When a Pokémon equipped with a Misty Seed finds itself on a battlefield covered in Misty Terrain, the item is expended, and the Pokémon's Special Defense stat rises by one stage. This consumption occurs irrespective of whether the holder would typically be influenced by the terrain, such as if it is ungrounded or currently within the semi-invulnerable phase of a move. However, it will not be used if the holder's Special Defense stat has already reached its maximum of +6 stages. An exception exists for Pokémon with the Contrary Ability; for them, the Seed will not activate if their Special Defense has instead reached -6 stages.

In scenarios where Misty Terrain is established by Misty Surge but then immediately altered by a different terrain-modifying Ability, the Misty Seed is utilized prior to the second Ability's activation. Conversely, if a Pokémon enters the field already holding a Misty Seed and possessing a terrain-changing Ability while Misty Terrain is active, that Pokémon's Ability takes effect before the held item does. Given that Magic Room and Embargo conclude their effects earlier than terrain does, should Misty Terrain be scheduled to vanish on the identical turn as Magic Room or Embargo, the Misty Seed will still trigger before the terrain ceases to exist. When multiple Pokémon are each equipped with a Misty Seed upon the field transitioning to Misty Terrain, every Pokémon will consume its respective Misty Seed before Symbiosis has an opportunity to transfer any item, even other Misty Seeds. Should a held Misty Seed fail to activate because its holder's Special Defense is already at +6 stages (or -6 for a Pokémon with Contrary), it will remain inactive for that particular holder until one of these conditions is met: the terrain changes, the holder is switched out, or the holder itself (or its Substitute) takes damage.

As of Generation IX, a Misty Seed that has been consumed during a battle will be restored to its holder once the encounter concludes. Even though Magic Room typically concludes before terrain effects, the Misty Seed remains inactive throughout the duration that Magic Room is in effect.

Title: Grassy Seed

An item that can be held, known as the Grassy Seed (グラスシード Grass Seed in Japanese), made its debut in Generation VII as a single-use consumable. Its effect is to enhance the holder's Defense when Grassy Terrain is active. Should a Pokémon be clutching a Grassy Seed upon the battlefield becoming Grassy Terrain, the item is consumed, and that Pokémon's Defense stat rises by one stage. Notably, the Grassy Seed's activation and consumption occur irrespective of whether the Pokémon benefits from the terrain in other ways, such as being ungrounded or in the midst of a semi-invulnerable move turn. However, if the Pokémon's Defense stat has already reached its maximum of +6 stages, the Seed will not be used. A specific exception applies to Pokémon with the Ability Contrary, for whom the Seed will not activate if their Defense stat is already at its minimum of -6 stages.

In situations where Grassy Terrain is established via Grassy Surge and is then instantly overwritten by another terrain-modifying Ability, the Grassy Seed will be consumed prior to that subsequent Ability taking effect. Conversely, when a Pokémon carrying a Grassy Seed and possessing a terrain-altering Ability enters the field already under Grassy Terrain, its own Ability triggers before the held Seed can activate. Since Magic Room and Embargo effects conclude before any active terrain does, if Grassy Terrain is set to vanish on the same turn as either Magic Room or Embargo, the Grassy Seed will still perform its function before the terrain disappears. When multiple Pokémon are simultaneously equipped with a Grassy Seed upon the generation of Grassy Terrain, each will consume its respective Seed sequentially, preceding any item transfers, even of other Grassy Seeds, that might occur via Symbiosis. Should a Grassy Seed fail to activate because its holder's Defense is already at +6 stages (or -6 for a Pokémon with Contrary), it will remain inactive for that specific Pokémon until one of the following events occurs: the terrain environment changes, the Pokémon is withdrawn from battle, or either the Pokémon itself or its active Substitute takes damage. Starting from Generation IX, a Grassy Seed that was consumed during a battle will be restored to its holder once the encounter concludes. While Magic Room's influence concludes before any active terrain dissipates, the Grassy Seed will not be triggered.

Title: Fighting Memory

Originating in Generation VII, the Fighting Memory (ファイトメモリ Fight Memory in Japanese) functions as a specific held item. It belongs to a category of "memories" directly linked to Silvally, specifically aligning it with the Fighting type. When held, it imbues the move Multi-Attack with the Fighting type. Furthermore, Silvally's exclusive Ability, RKS System, causes the Pokémon to transform into its Fighting-type form while equipped with this particular item. Special item-altering abilities and moves, including Trick and Fling, are ineffective at either attaching or detaching any memory item, including the Fighting Memory, from a Silvally.

In the episode 'Final Rivals!', the Fighting Memory played a pivotal role when Gladion's Silvally wielded it against Ash's Melmetal during the Manalo Conference finals. This strategic choice granted Silvally a crucial type advantage, enabling it to overpower the Hex Nut Pokémon using a supereffective Multi-Attack for the victory.

Within the Pokémon Trading Card Game, specifically the Crimson Invasion expansion, the Fighting Memory is represented as a Pokémon Tool card. Its effect is contingent on being attached exclusively to a Pokémon designated 'Silvally-.' When affixed to such a Pokémon, it alters the Silvally-GX's type to Fighting.

Title: Flying Memory

Debuting in Generation VII, the Flying Memory (Japanese: フライングメモリ Flying Memory) is classified as a held item. It constitutes one of the specialized memories specifically associated with the Pokémon Silvally. This particular item directly corresponds to the Flying type. Should the Pokémon holding it use Multi-Attack, that move will automatically become Flying-type. Moreover, a Silvally holding this item will undergo a form change to its Flying-type appearance, driven by its RKS System Ability. It's noteworthy that item-manipulating effects, such as the moves Trick and Fling, are incapable of either providing or detaching a memory from Silvally.

Title: Poison Memory

Introduced in Generation VII, the Poison Memory (Japanese: ポイズンメモリ Poison Memory) functions as a specific held item. It belongs to the set of memories intrinsically linked with Silvally and aligns directly with the Poison type. When a Pokémon holding this item executes Multi-Attack, the move itself converts into a Poison-type attack. Silvally's RKS System ability causes it to transform into its Poison-type form while equipped with this particular memory. Furthermore, item-manipulating effects, including moves like Trick and Fling, are rendered unable to either bestow a memory upon Silvally or remove one from it. This held item, when carried by Silvally, prompts its RKS System ability to shift it into its Poison-type configuration.

Title: Ground Memory

Originating in Generation VII, the Ground Memory (Japanese: グラウンドメモリ Ground Memory) functions as a specific held item. It is classified among the memories uniquely associated with the Pokémon Silvally. Its elemental correspondence is to the Ground type. When its holder unleashes the move Multi-Attack, the attack's type becomes Ground. Silvally's RKS System ability triggers a transformation into its Ground-type form while holding this item. Item-manipulating effects, such as Trick and Fling, are incapable of either conferring a memory upon Silvally or divesting it of one. Furthermore, a Silvally bearing this particular item will utilize its RKS System to adopt its Ground-type manifestation.

Title: Rock Memory

Introduced in Generation VII, the Rock Memory (Japanese: ロックメモリ Rock Memory) is a specific type of held item. It belongs to the series of "memories" linked to Silvally, establishing its affiliation with the Rock type. Should its holder unleash Multi-Attack, the move's typing will convert to Rock. Furthermore, the RKS System ability allows a Silvally equipped with this item to shift into its Rock-type form. Unlike many held items, Memories cannot be transferred to or from Silvally via item-manipulating effects, such as Trick or Fling. This accessory made an appearance in Battle Royal 151!, where Gladion utilized it to confer a type advantage upon his Silvally over a Talonflame during the preliminary Battle Royal of the Manalo Conference. In the manga chapter Destroy!! Results of the Training!, Moon decided to fit a Rock Memory into Gladion's Silvally's RKS System, aiming to combat Ryuki's Turtonator more effectively.

Title: Bug Memory

The Bug Memory (Japanese: バグメモリ Bug Memory) is a held item that made its debut in Generation VII. It is categorized as one of the specialized memories designed for Silvally, and its elemental alignment is with the Bug type. Should a Pokémon holding this item unleash Multi-Attack, the move's type will convert to Bug. Silvally's distinctive RKS System Ability ensures that when this particular memory is held, the Pokémon transforms into its Bug-type manifestation. Furthermore, item-altering effects, including the moves Trick and Fling, are unable to either grant a memory to Silvally or take one away from it.

Title: Ghost Memory

This held item, identified as the Ghost Memory (Japanese: ゴーストメモリ Ghost Memory), was introduced in Generation VII. It forms part of the distinct set of memories intrinsically tied to the Pokémon Silvally, specifically correlating with the Ghost type. Should any Pokémon holding this item employ the attack Multi-Attack, the move's type is converted to Ghost. Silvally, when equipped with this item, will have its RKS System Ability activate, thereby transforming it into its Ghost-type form. Moreover, various item-manipulating effects within the game, including moves like Trick or Fling, are incapable of either bestowing a memory upon Silvally or taking one away from it.

Title: Steel Memory

The Steel Memory (Japanese: スチールメモリ Steel Memory) is a specialized held item that debuted in Generation VII. It belongs to a set of memories intrinsically linked with Silvally, specifically corresponding to the Steel type. When a Pokémon holds this item and uses the move Multi-Attack, the move's type changes to Steel. Silvally's unique RKS System ability prompts it to assume its Steel-type form when this particular item is equipped. Importantly, common item-manipulating effects, such as Trick and Fling, are incapable of either attaching a memory to Silvally or detaching one from it. Moreover, the RKS System mechanism ensures that a Silvally clutching this memory will transition into its Steel-type manifestation. In the anime episode "10,000,000 Reasons to Fight!", Gladion employed a Steel Memory on his Silvally, transforming it into a Steel type. This enabled Silvally to traverse a hazardous pool of poison that a Nihilego had generated to obstruct Gladion and Lusamine from their pursuers.

Title: Fire Memory

First appearing in Generation VII, the Fire Memory (Japanese: ファイヤーメモリ Fire Memory) functions as a held item. This item belongs to a collection of memories linked to Silvally. Its elemental affinity is the Fire type. When a Pokémon holds this item and uses the move Multi-Attack, that move transforms into a Fire-type attack. The RKS System Ability enables a Silvally equipped with this memory to shift into its Fire-type manifestation. Notably, game mechanics that typically manipulate items, like Trick or Fling, are unable to either bestow a memory upon Silvally or retrieve one from it. The RKS System also causes a Silvally carrying this particular memory to assume its Fire-type appearance. During the episode "10,000,000 Reasons to Fight!", Gladion strategically employed a Fire Memory with his Silvally, converting it to a Fire type. This transformation allowed Silvally to thaw the ice encasing it, which had been generated by Lusamine's Absol's Ice Beam. Later, in "Getting Down to The Ire!", Gladion once more utilized the Fire Memory. This time, it helped his Silvally mitigate incoming damage from Kiawe's Turtonator's Inferno Overdrive, which proved crucial for Silvally to launch a successful counterattack and secure victory in their Manalo Conference semifinals bout. The Fire Memory is also featured in the Ultra Prism expansion of the Pokémon Trading Card Game. As a Pokémon Tool card, its effect is exclusively active when it is attached to a Pokémon specifically named Silvally-. Upon attachment to such a Pokémon, it alters the Silvally-GX's type to Fire.

Title: Water Memory

This held item, known as the Water Memory (Japanese: ウオーターメモリ Water Memory), was first introduced in Generation VII. It is one of several specialized "memory" items associated exclusively with Silvally, and its properties align with the Water type. Should the Pokémon holding it utilize Multi-Attack, that move will automatically become a Water-type attack. Silvally's inherent RKS System ability will cause it to shift into its Water-type form when this particular item is in its possession. Crucially, item-manipulating effects, such as Trick and Fling, are unable to either grant Silvally a memory or remove a memory from it. Furthermore, the RKS System ensures Silvally adopts its Water-type form whenever it is clutching this item. In the Pokémon Trading Card Game, Water Memory is featured in the Team Up expansion. It functions as a Pokémon Tool card, and its effect is activated only when it is attached to a Pokémon named Silvally-. While affixed to such a Pokémon, it causes the attached Silvally-GX's type to convert to Water.

Title: Grass Memory

Introduced in Generation VII, this held item, known in Japanese as グラスメモリ Grass Memory, is one of the "memory" items exclusively associated with the Pokémon Silvally. Its elemental designation corresponds to the Grass type. If a Pokémon holding it utilizes Multi-Attack, the move transforms into a Grass-type attack. Furthermore, Silvally's RKS System Ability allows it to shift into its Grass-type form when equipped with this memory. Item-manipulating effects, such as Trick and Fling, are incapable of either granting a memory to Silvally or confiscating one from it. The Grass Memory also appears in the Pokémon Trading Card Game, specifically within the Team Up expansion. Here, it functions as a Pokémon Tool card, with its effect only active when attached to a Pokémon named Silvally-. When affixed to such a Pokémon, it converts the attached Silvally-GX's type to Grass.

Title: Electric Memory

An item intended to be held, the Electric Memory (known as エレクトロメモリ Electro Memory in Japanese) made its debut in Generation VII. This item is counted among the various Memories linked to Silvally. Its essence aligns with the Electric type. Should the Pokémon holding it utilize Multi-Attack, that move will transform into an Electric-type attack. Silvally's unique Ability, RKS System, causes it to adopt its Electric-type form while equipped with this particular item. Effects that manipulate items, for instance Trick or Fling, are unable to either bestow a Memory upon Silvally or take one away from it. The Electric Memory is featured in the Ultra Prism expansion set. Within the Pokémon Trading Card Game, it functions as a Pokémon Tool card, becoming active solely when affixed to a Pokémon specifically named Silvally-. When attached to such a Pokémon, it causes the Silvally-GX's elemental type to become Lightning.

Title: Psychic Memory

Originating in Generation VII, the Psychic Memory (Japanese: サイキックメモリ Psychic Memory) functions as a held item. It belongs to the set of 'memories' specifically linked to the Pokémon Silvally, with its elemental affiliation being the Psychic type. When held, it causes the move Multi-Attack to become Psychic-type. Silvally's unique Ability, RKS System, transforms the Pokémon into its Psychic-type form while this item is equipped. Notably, item-manipulating effects such as Trick and Fling are incapable of either bestowing a memory upon Silvally or removing one from it. The Psychic Memory also appears in the Pokémon Trading Card Game as part of the Crimson Invasion expansion. There, it is a Pokémon Tool card that only takes effect when attached to a Pokémon named Silvally-. When affixed to a Silvally-GX, this card changes its type to Psychic.

Title: Ice Memory

Debuting in Generation VII, the Ice Memory (Japanese: アイスメモリ, Ice Memory) is a distinct type of held item. It stands as one of the specific 'memory' items uniquely associated with the Pokémon Silvally, directly signifying the Ice type. Any Pokémon holding it will cause its Multi-Attack move to become an Ice-type attack. Via its RKS System Ability, a Silvally holding this item will shift into its Ice-type incarnation. Notably, item-manipulating techniques, exemplified by moves such as Trick and Fling, cannot be used to equip a memory onto Silvally or detach one from it. Furthermore, the RKS System reliably transforms a Silvally carrying this particular item into its Ice-type form.

Title: Dragon Memory

Appearing first in Generation VII, the Dragon Memory (known in Japanese as ドラゴンメモリ) functions as a held item. It belongs to the series of Memories specifically linked with the Pokémon Silvally, its essence aligning with the Dragon type. Should the Pokémon holding it utilize Multi-Attack, that move will manifest as Dragon-type. Silvally's RKS System Ability will cause it to assume its Dragon-type form when equipped with this item. Moves that swap or remove held items, such as Trick and Fling, are ineffective at attaching or detaching any Memory from Silvally. Furthermore, the RKS System ability consistently causes a Silvally clutching this particular Memory to adopt its Dragon-type appearance. Exclusively in Generation VII, for Silvally to be instructed in the move Draco Meteor, possession of a Dragon Memory was a prerequisite.

Title: Dark Memory

Debuting in Generation VII, the Dark Memory (Japanese: ダークメモリ Dark Memory) is a held item. It belongs to a series of memories associated with Silvally, specifically corresponding to the Dark type. If a Pokémon holding this item performs Multi-Attack, the move transforms into a Dark-type attack. A Silvally holding this Memory will have its RKS System Ability activate, changing it into its Dark-type form. Effects that manipulate held items, such as the moves Trick and Fling, cannot be used to equip a memory onto Silvally or remove one from it. Moreover, the RKS System causes a Silvally equipped with this item to shift into its Dark-type configuration. In "Mission: Total Recall!", after Gladion's Silvally cast off its mask, Gladion provided it with a Dark Memory, which allowed Silvally to become a Dark type and effortlessly defeat Faba's Alakazam and Hypno.

Title: Fairy Memory

Originating in Generation VII, the Fairy Memory (Japanese: フェアリーメモリ Fairy Memory) serves as a specialized held item. It is one of several 'memories' uniquely linked to the Pokémon Silvally, with this particular item aligning with the Fairy type. When a Pokémon holds it, Multi-Attack transforms into a Fairy-type move. Silvally's RKS System ability causes it to adopt its Fairy-type manifestation while equipped with this item. Mechanisms that typically alter items, including moves like Trick and Fling, are unable to either bestow a memory upon Silvally or dispossess Silvally of one. The RKS System also ensures that a Silvally carrying this item will assume its Fairy-type variant. In the episode Family Determination!, Gladion strategically utilized a Fairy Memory with his Silvally, converting it into a Fairy type. This alteration granted Silvally resistance against the onslaughts of a Totem Kommo-o, enabling it to achieve victory using a Fairy-type Multi-Attack.

Title: Rainbow Flower

Hailing from Generation VII, the Rainbow Flower (known in Japan as にじいろのはな or "Rainbow Flower") serves as a specific Key Item. Players acquire this during Mina's trial within the games Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon. Its collection marks the commencement of the final stage of Mina's trial, which culminates in a confrontation with Totem Ribombee. In the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Rainbow Flower also manifested as a Pokémon Tool card. This version saw an exclusive release in Japan. It represented one of seven distinct SM-P Promotional cards. These were distributed in special promotional packs, awarded to Pokémon Card Gym tournament participants throughout September, October, and November of 2019. A distinguishing characteristic of this particular print is the presence of the Pokémon Card Gym logo. When attached to a Pokémon, the card bestows +20 HP upon it. Furthermore, the Pokémon becomes immune to damage from an opponent's attacks while resting on the Bench.

Title: Autograph

The item known as the Autograph, called マチスのサイン (Matisse's Signature) in Japanese, first appeared in Generation VII. The description for this item in the Japanese version features Lt. Surge's inscription of "Good luck!" in English. This particular detail corresponds to his well-known habit of incorporating English words and phrases into his Japanese dialogue. The "Good luck!" text itself, as it appears in the Japanese release, is rendered with standard English letters rather than katakana. Additionally, an identical rendition of this autograph can be located in the player's room within Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon.

Title: Golden Razz Berry

Originating in Generation VII, the Golden Razz Berry (known in Japanese as きんのズリのみ, or Gold Zuri Fruit) is categorized as a specific kind of Berry. This item stands as a variation of the standard Razz Berry, serving as a superior alternative to the Silver Razz Berry when attempting to capture Pokémon. Applying it from the Bag to a wild Pokémon results in a significant boost to that Pokémon's catch rate. Its influence dissipates the moment a wild Pokémon escapes from a launched Poké Ball.

Its integration into Pokémon GO occurred on June 22, 2017. In this game, it dramatically enhances the probability of securing wild Pokémon, specifically by applying a ×2.5 multiplier to the catch rate. This beneficial impact persists until the targeted Pokémon successfully evades a Poké Ball. Furthermore, when provided to a Pokémon defending a Gym, a Golden Razz Berry entirely restores its motivation level, irrespective of prior Berry consumption. Acquisition of Golden Razz Berries is possible repeatedly by spinning a PokéStop designated as interesting by an Ultra Buddy. They have also been available as rewards through specific Special Research tasks.

Title: Golden Nanab Berry

The Golden Nanab Berry (Japanese: きんのナナのみ Gold Nana Fruit) first appeared in Generation VII. It is an enhanced version of the Nanab Berry, offering superior effectiveness over the Silver Nanab Berry when catching Pokémon. When activated from the Bag during a wild encounter, it markedly reduces the wild Pokémon's inclination to move and causes the target ring to contract more slowly. Its benefits will cease if and when the wild Pokémon breaks free from a thrown Poké Ball.

Title: Silver Pinap Berry

The Silver Pinap Berry (Japanese: ぎんのパイルのみ Silver Pairu Fruit), a distinct type of fruit item for Pokémon, made its debut in Generation VII. It functions as an enhanced variant of the standard Pinap Berry, specifically designed to improve Pokémon capture attempts. When a Trainer applies it from their Bag to a wild Pokémon, its primary effect is to elevate the probability of securing additional item rewards upon successfully catching that Pokémon. This beneficial effect remains active until the moment the wild Pokémon manages to escape from a thrown Poké Ball. Quantitatively, the Silver Pinap Berry applies a significant ×2.334 multiplier to the resulting Candy reward, which is a greater bonus than that provided by a regular Pinap Berry, and also confers a ×1.8 multiplier to the wild Pokémon's base catch rate. Furthermore, when provided to a Pokémon defending a Gym, it restores motivation at twice the rate of both Razz and Pinap Berries. Trainers can consistently acquire Silver Pinap Berries by spinning PokéStops highlighted as interesting by an Ultra Buddy; they have additionally been made available via specific Special Research objectives.

Title: Golden Pinap Berry

A specific variety of Berry, the Golden Pinap Berry (Japanese: きんのパイルのみ Gold Pairu Fruit), made its debut in Generation VII. Functionally, it stands as an improved form of the Pinap Berry, proving more potent than the Silver Pinap Berry when utilized in Pokémon capture attempts. Should this item be deployed from the Bag against a wild Pokémon, it provides a considerable boost to the chances of receiving additional Candies and Berries once that Pokémon is caught. Its beneficial effect, however, ceases the moment a wild Pokémon succeeds in escaping from a launched Poké Ball.

Title: Marble

Should your search be for the character who appeared as the character of the day in Luxray Vision! (DP059), please refer to Marble. If you were instead seeking the daily character from Crisis at Ferroseed Research!, identified in Japanese as Professor Marble, the correct individual is Professor Malveaux. The Marble (Japanese: ビーだま Marble) stands as a valuable collectible first introduced within Generation VII. Its sole practical application is its sale; it offers no other in-game utility. As a specific type of Buddy Souvenir, it carries no direct functional benefit. A Marble represents one of the various Souvenirs that a player's Buddy Pokémon might occasionally gather once it has achieved an "Ultra Buddy" level.

Title: Lure

Introduced as a utility item in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, the Lure (Japanese: むしよせコロン Bug Attractant Cologne) influences wild encounters. More potent versions, the Super Lure and Max Lure, also exist. Activating this item from the Bag while navigating the overworld will elevate the appearance frequency of uncommon wild Pokémon, notably including Shiny Pokémon, for a period spanning 100 steps. Additionally, every wild Pokémon that manifests will possess a level one greater than the highest level usually present in that particular region; for instance, if wild Pokémon in an area generally range from level 3 to level 5, all subsequent wild Pokémon encounters will occur at level 6. A Lure made a fleeting appearance within Green's Bag during the events of The Discovery.

Title: Super Lure

First appearing in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, the Super Lure (Japanese: シルバーコロン Silver Cologne) is a specific type of item. The Lure and Max Lure serve as alternative forms of this same item. When utilized from the Bag by a Trainer in the overworld, it boosts the encounter probability for rarer wild Pokémon, including Shiny Pokémon, for a duration of 200 steps. Additionally, it causes all wild Pokémon that emerge to be at a level 1 higher than the maximum level typically found in the area (for instance, if an area's wild Pokémon normally range from level 3 to level 5, all wild Pokémon encountered will instead be level 6).

Title: Max Lure

The Max Lure, called ゴールドコロン (Gold Cologne) in Japan, debuted in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!, serving as a more potent version than the Lure and Super Lure items. Activating it from the Bag within the overworld yields 250 steps of effect. During this time, it heightens the appearance frequency of rarer wild Pokémon, including Shiny Pokémon. Furthermore, any wild Pokémon encountered will be level 1 greater than the highest level typically found in the surrounding area (for instance, if an area's wild Pokémon normally range from level 3 to level 5, all wild Pokémon will instead be level 6).

Title: Pewter Crunchies

Known in Japan as ニビあられ (Nibi Arare), Pewter Crunchies made their debut as a medicinal item in Generation VII. These treats hail from Pewter City as a regional delicacy, capable of alleviating a Pokémon's non-volatile status ailments and confusion. Administering them from the Bag to a Pokémon specifically neutralizes non-volatile conditions like poison, paralysis, sleep, burn, freeze, and also clears confusion. These savory items have appeared alongside Ditto and Eevee in collectible figures from The Pokémon Company International's Delicious Adventure collection. Uniquely, Pewter Crunchies stand as the sole local specialty item introduced in a generation distinct from its region of origin. A documented consequence of consuming Pewter Crunchies is an increased sense of thirst, a detail shared by Brock and a non-player character stationed in the passageway connecting Route 7 and Saffron City. Within the game world of Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, a text discovered at either Naranja Academy or Uva Academy discloses that Pewter Crunchies haven't achieved widespread appeal, attributing this to their "forbiddingly tough appearance" and "surprisingly thin flavor."

Title: Endorsement

The Endorsement, known in Japan as すいせんじょう (Recommendation), is a Key Item debuting in Generation VIII, providing players with the authorization to join the Gym Challenge. During Crackle!! Practice Battle, Henry and Casey aimed to impress Leon and secure his endorsement for the Gym Challenge by skillfully driving away a wild Obstagoon. Confirming their aspirations, Leon promptly contacted Rose concerning the endorsements he planned to issue to the two Trainers he had recently encountered. By Chomp!! Defeat Drednaw, Henry and Casey's simultaneous endorsements had become common knowledge among the Gym Leaders, who expressed surprise at Leon's choice to back two participants concurrently. In Blazing!! Bede's Feelings, Bede revealed his own endorsement had come from Chairman Rose. Within Swaying!! Murky Memories, Hop divulged that Leon had also offered him an endorsement. He chose to refuse the offer, however, to avoid potentially diminishing Leon's standing if his own performance were to fall short after receiving such a prominent backing.

Title: Wishing Star

The Wishing Star (Japanese: ねがいぼし Wishing Star) is a Key Item that first appeared in Generation VIII, not to be mistaken for the Wishing Piece or Wishing Stone. It functions as a source of Dynamax energy, which a Trainer can integrate into a Dynamax Band to enable their Pokémon to Dynamax. Professor Magnolia explains that Wishing Stars are fragments of Eternatus, a Pokémon said to hold a particular connection to the Dynamax phenomenon.

Within the narrative of Pokémon Sword and Shield, following a confrontation on Route 2, two Wishing Stars descend from the heavens close to the player and Hop. Professor Magnolia subsequently acquires these stars, crafting them into Dynamax Bands for both Trainers. Later, it comes to light that Bede is accumulating Wishing Stars on behalf of Rose, sourcing them from Galar Mine and Galar Mine No. 2. He even makes an attempt to demolish the renowned mural in Stow-on-Side in pursuit of additional stars. At the game's climax, Rose discloses that Macro Cosmos has been feeding the collected Wishing Stars to Eternatus, intending to avert Galar's impending energy crisis projected for a millennium hence.

In the post-game content, it is discovered that Sonia possesses the Wishing Stars seized from Chairman Rose following his apprehension, keeping them at her laboratory. These stars are later pilfered by Sordward and Shielbert, aided by Sonia's disloyal assistant, as part of their scheme. Ultimately, a Wishing Star intended for a Trainer is typically brought to Professor Magnolia, who crafts it into a Dynamax Band before returning it to its owner.

In Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, although the Wishing Star itself is not obtainable, it receives a mention from a Gentleman in Floaroma Town. This occurs if the player engages him in conversation while their console holds Pokémon Sword or Shield save data. He notes that the player has encountered a Wishing Star previously and, as a result, presents them with a Jirachi.

In the anime special, Sword and Shield: The Darkest Day!, a Wishing Star made an impact near Goh and Sonia in Wedgehurst. Subsequently, Sonia fashioned it into a Dynamax Band for Goh. The episode Assistant featured a flashback where a Wishing Star underwent examination by Oleana and several Scientists at a Macro Cosmos laboratory. Two Wishing Stars were showcased in the manga arc Ooh Shiny!! Legendary Equipment. A year prior to these events, Casey and Henry observed two Wishing Stars descending into the Slumbering Weald, prompting them to enter the forest in search of them. With assistance from Henry's Farfetch'd, they successfully located the stars, and Casey, in gratitude, gifted one to Henry. Both stars were subsequently incorporated into Dynamax Bands for the Trainers. Within the manga's Watch Out!! Strong Rivals, Bede discovered three Wishing Stars after his Hatenna broke a boulder obstructing the entry to the Galar Mine. Later, in Stealth!! Infiltrate the Energy Plant, Rose and Oleana were observed bringing numerous Wishing Stars to Eternatus at the Energy Plant.

Title: Dynamax Band

The Dynamax Band, known in Japanese as ダイマックスＢ (Daimax Band), is a specialized Key Item originating from Generation VIII. Designed by Oleana, it empowers a Trainer's Pokémon to undergo Dynamax or Gigantamax transformations when within specific Power Spots, enabling them to unleash Max Moves and G-Max Moves, respectively. A Trainer is restricted to activating Dynamax or Gigantamax for one Pokémon per battle.

Its debut in Pokémon GO occurred on September 4, 2024, coinciding with the introduction of Dynamax into that game. Players acquire the Dynamax Band as a reward for completing the "To the Max!" Special Research campaign. However, its function in Pokémon GO differs from the main series, as it serves purely cosmetic purposes rather than being a prerequisite for Dynamaxing a Pokémon.

The earliest appearances of Dynamax Bands in the anime were in the episode "Flash of the Titans!", where both Lance and Leon possessed them. During the World Coronation Series finals, Lance utilized his band to Dynamax his Gyarados, while Leon employed his to Gigantamax his Charizard. Leon later demonstrated the ability to Gigantamax his Rillaboom and Cinderace with his Dynamax Band in "Curtain Up! Fight the Fights!" and "Partners in Time!", respectively. Similarly, Lance was observed Dynamaxing his Dragonite in "Pride of a Champion!". In "The Climb to Be the Very Best!", Leon presented Ash with his own Dynamax Band, allowing Ash to Gigantamax his Pikachu during their confrontation. Ash was permitted to retain the Dynamax Band subsequent to the battle. From "The Winding Path to Greatness!" onwards, Ash also gained the capability to Gigantamax his Gengar using this item.

Raihan's Dynamax Band was first showcased in "Toughing It Out!", which he used to Gigantamax his Duraludon. In "Sword and Shield: The Darkest Day!", Sonia crafted a Dynamax Band from a Wishing Star she and Goh discovered, subsequently gifting it to Goh. He later deployed it to Dynamax his Raboot in a skirmish against one of Oleana's subordinates, who had employed his own Dynamax Band to Gigantamax his Garbodor. Bea was revealed to possess a Dynamax Band in "Ultra Exciting from the Shocking Start!"; her initial use of it was seen in "A Battle of Mega Versus Max!", where she Gigantamaxed her Machamp during her third World Coronation Series match against Ash. Opal's Dynamax Band surfaced in "The Sweet Taste of Battle!", which she used to Gigantamax her Alcremie during her battle with Ash. Marnie's ownership of a Dynamax Band was depicted in "Battling Turned Up to Eleven!", where she Gigantamaxed her Grimmsnarl during her World Coronation Series encounter with Ash. Cynthia was shown to wield a Dynamax Band in "Valor: A Strategic Part of Battling!", using it to Dynamax her Togekiss during her Masters Eight Tournament battle against Ash. Kabu's Dynamax Band made an appearance in "The Bittersweet Truth".

Victor, Leon, and Hop's Dynamax Bands were featured in "The Champion"; Victor notably used his to capture Eternatus. In "Zap!! A Rising Beam of Light", Henry and Casey were each introduced with a Dynamax Band. Henry's first recorded use involved catching a Gurdurr during a Max Raid Battle. "Ooh Shiny!! Legendary Equipment" disclosed that these two Dynamax Bands had been forged from Wishing Stars that the two Pokédex holders had discovered in the Slumbering Weald a year prior.

Consistent with the main series games, every Galar Gym Leader, with the exception of Piers, possesses a Dynamax Band. This also extends to characters such as Hop, Bede, Marnie, Leon, Oleana, and Rose.

In the manga, Dynamax Bands first appeared in MTM03, wielded by Suguru Tsukino and Kira Hoshio. Their presence was noted again in the subsequent chapter. Kira began utilizing his Dynamax Band on a regular basis from MTM07 onwards. By MTM11, Kira had upgraded to a Dynamax Band + (Japanese: ダイマックスバンド＋(プラス) Daimax Band +).

The Dynamax Band has also been immortalized through its inclusion in various officially licensed figures and merchandise.

Title: Rotom Bike

This Key Item, known in Japan as ロトムじてんしゃ (Rotom Bike), made its debut in Generation VIII. Players can utilize the Rotom Bike to navigate land at an increased pace. It also features a temporary speed burst; however, this speed enhancement necessitates a recharge interval before successive uses. Upgrades are obtainable from a Watt Trader located within the Wild Area. For a cumulative cost of 1,000, 3,000, and then 5,000 Watts, players can enhance the Rotom Bike three distinct times, effectively shortening its recharge duration. Once upgraded with Water Mode, the bike gains the additional functionality of traversing aquatic surfaces.

Within The Isle of Armor expansion, two alternative aesthetic modifications become available: "Sparkling White" and "Glistening Black" designs. Both of these cosmetic alterations include visual particle effects reminiscent of Shiny Pokémon and come pre-equipped with fully upgraded speed capabilities. Users are able to freely interchange between the standard Water Mode and either of these two custom appearances whenever desired.

The chapter "Gulp!! Gulp Pokémon" disclosed that Henry acquired a Rotom Bike from an individual following his encounter with troublesome Team Yell Grunts. Furthermore, in "Swaying!! Murky Memories," it was unveiled that Casey enhanced Henry's Rotom Bike to grant it water-traversal capabilities, thereby enabling him to navigate Circhester Bay.

The Rotom Bike also manifested as an Item card within the Pokémon Trading Card Game, launching during the English Sword & Shield Series (and the corresponding Japanese Sword & Shield Era). Its initial release occurred in the Japanese V Starter Set Lightning. Subsequently, it featured in the Japanese Sword expansion prior to its English debut in the Sword & Shield expansion, featuring artwork by 5ban Graphics. The card's effect permits the player to draw cards until their hand contains six, at which point their current turn concludes. A poster displayed in Motostoke suggests that Macro Cosmos Tech is the implied manufacturer of the Rotom Bike.

Title: Large Leek

Identified in Japanese as ふといながねぎ (Large Scallion), Large Leek was introduced in Generation VIII as a cooking ingredient. When incorporated into culinary creations, it provides a base Strength value of 185. This item contributes to various recipes and is obtainable through the efforts of a player's helper Pokémon. Additionally, Large Leek has been depicted in the artwork for a keychain, which formed part of the Pokémon Yurutto goods collection, an exclusive offering from the Pokémon Center.

Title: Camping Gear

Known in Japan as キャンプセット (Camp Set), the Camping Gear functions as a Key Item that first appeared in Generation VIII. Its primary purpose is to enable trainers to establish a Pokémon Camp. However, certain environmental and competitive restrictions prevent its deployment: it is unusable on aquatic surfaces, inside structures, or during a Gym challenge, the Champion Cup tournament, or the Galarian Star Tournament. The Camping Gear also made its debut as an Item card within the Pokémon Trading Card Game, emerging during the English Sword & Shield Series, which corresponds to the Japanese Sword & Shield Era. Its initial release occurred in the Japanese Single Strike Master expansion and concurrently in the English Battle Styles expansion, featuring illustrations by sadaji. As an Item card, it provides the benefit of searching one's deck for a card and adding it to their hand; however, this action necessitates the immediate conclusion of the current turn.

Title: Rusted Sword

This legendary artifact, known in Japan as くちたけん (Rusted Sword), made its debut in Generation VIII as a held item, intrinsically linked with the mythical Pokémon Zacian. At the critical juncture of Pokémon Sword, the player's Rusted Sword, paired with Hop's Rusted Shield, acts as a beacon, summoning Zacian and Zamazenta. These legendary Pokémon then aid the two Trainers in their fight against Eternamax Eternatus atop the tower. For this particular confrontation, the Rusted Sword briefly serves as Zacian's equipped item. Post-game, the player restores the Rusted Sword to its rightful place in Slumbering Weald. Shortly thereafter, it is appropriated by Sordward; however, the player recovers the pilfered artifact by prevailing over him in combat. Prior to facing Zacian at the tower summit for its capture, the player bestows the Rusted Sword upon it as its held item.

Conversely, in Pokémon Shield, Hop acquires the Rusted Sword. In the post-game, upon his attempt to return it to the Slumbering Weald altar, Sordward seizes it. Despite Hop's efforts, he is vanquished by Sordward, who escapes with the item. Subsequently, Sordward employs the Rusted Sword to entice Zacian to the tower summit. Following the player's battle with Zacian, the legendary Pokémon departs, and Hop reclaims the Rusted Sword from Sordward before pursuing Zacian. Once Hop successfully captures Zacian in Slumbering Weald, the Rusted Sword consistently becomes its held item for all future battles in which Hop engages.

When Zacian wields this item during a battle, it undergoes a transformation into its formidable Crowned Sword form. Within combat, this also causes Zacian's move Iron Head to be replaced by Behemoth Blade.

Within Pokémon UNITE, the Rusted Sword functions as a held item, albeit one without any actual in-game effect. It is exclusively equipped by Zacian, with no other Pokémon able to possess it. This largely serves as a balancing constraint, effectively limiting Zacian to only two beneficial held items rather than the standard three. Furthermore, players begin their UNITE journey with the Rusted Sword already present in their Item Box, even prior to unlocking Zacian itself.

In the anime episode "From Here to Eternatus!" from the Sword and Shield series, Goh and Sonia located the Rusted Sword at the Slumbering Weald altar. This discovery occurred subsequent to their plea for assistance from Zacian and Zamazenta concerning the Darkest Day crisis. Goh proceeded to gather both it and the Rusted Shield, taking both artifacts with him. During "The Legends Awaken!" (Sword and Shield series), the Rusted Sword summoned Zacian to the tower's peak. Zacian then seized the Sword in its mouth, enabling its transformation into the Crowned Sword form. Following Goh's successful capture of Eternatus, Zacian abandoned its Sword. Ash and Goh were subsequently depicted restoring both the Rusted Sword and Shield to their original resting places. A flashback in "The Champion" showed the Rusted Sword in Victor's possession. When he raised it during his and Hop's engagement with Eternatus at the tower summit, it brought forth Zacian to assist them, facilitating its transition into the Crowned Sword form.

The Rusted Sword's initial appearance, alongside the Rusted Shield, was in a flashback within "Ooh Shiny!! Legendary Equipment". A year prior, Henry, joined by Casey, ventured into Slumbering Weald with the intention of observing these two legendary artifacts. After facing illusions of Zacian and Zamazenta at the forest's concluding altar and assuring them of their non-malicious intent, the two Trainers approached the items on the altar, only to be incapacitated by a brilliant flash. Upon regaining consciousness, they discovered the artifacts had vanished. "Cracking!! Brilliant Tap-Dancing" subsequently disclosed that Sordward and Shielbert were responsible for the theft of the artifacts. Within "Cracked!! The Rusted Sword and Shield", the brothers utilized these artifacts as ostensible proof of their lineage to Galar's ancient heroes. When the duo confronted Eternatus atop the tower, they brandished the artifacts to no avail; nothing occurred, and the items were propelled into the moat encircling Hammerlocke Stadium. From there, Casey's Falinks and Stunfisk retrieved them and brought them to her, after which she entrusted them to Henry for cleaning and restoration. To his dismay, however, Henry soon discovered his inability to forge or mend the relics. In "Oh My!! The Disappearance of Henry," the artifacts, along with Henry, vanished during Zacian and Zamazenta's assault on Eternatus, having been teleported by an unseen Pokémon. Casey subsequently ascertained that the artifacts had materialized in two distinct locations: the Isle of Armor and the Crown Tundra. In "Clip-Clop!! The King's Horse," Bede reported sensing traces of the Rusted Sword on Spectrier. Later, in "Rumble!! The Darkest Day Returns," he detected signals of the artifact at Spectrier's stable within the Crown Shrine, though the artifact itself was absent. After Calyrex successfully tamed its mount, it instructed Spectrier to guide it to the artifact. Upon locating it, Calyrex conveyed the item to Henry at the Crown Shrine, enabling him to cleanse it of Eternatus's lingering poison. In "Shing!! Final Form," Zacian employed the sword to transition into its Crowned Sword form.

Within the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Rusted Sword was unveiled as a Pokémon Tool card during the English Sword & Shield Series (known as the Sword & Shield Era in Japan). Its initial release occurred in the Japanese Shiny Star V expansion, subsequently making its English debut in the Shining Fates expansion, featuring illustrations by 5ban Graphics. This card enables Zacian V to inflict an additional 30 damage upon the opponent's Active Pokémon.

The Rusted Sword's design incorporates fictional runic characters, which seem to be a blend of Japanese and Old Norse scripts. Although the player receives an on-screen notification of obtaining this item in Slumbering Weald during Pokémon Sword's climax, this is merely a narrative device, as the item is not actually added to the player's inventory at that point.

Title: Rusted Shield

The Rusted Shield (Japanese: くちたたて Rusted Shield) is a legendary artifact that functions as a held item, making its debut in Generation VIII. It is intrinsically linked with Zamazenta. During the pivotal confrontation in Pokémon Shield, the player's Rusted Shield, combined with Hop's Rusted Sword, calls forth both Zamazenta and Zacian. These legendary Pokémon then assist the two Trainers in their struggle against Eternamax Eternatus at the tower's summit. For the duration of this specific battle, the Rusted Shield briefly becomes Zamazenta's equipped item. In the post-game narrative, the player restores the Rusted Shield to its place in Slumbering Weald. Shortly thereafter, it is temporarily pilfered by Shielbert; however, the player defeats him in combat to reclaim the stolen artifact. Prior to the player's challenge against Zamazenta at the tower summit for its capture, they bestow the Rusted Shield upon it as its held item.

Conversely, within Pokémon Sword, the Rusted Shield is initially acquired by Hop. During the post-game, when Hop attempts to return it to the altar within Slumbering Weald, Shielbert steals it. Hop engages Shielbert in a fight but is ultimately vanquished, allowing Shielbert to escape with the item. Later, Shielbert employs the Rusted Shield to entice Zamazenta to the tower summit. Following the player's battle with Zamazenta, the legendary Pokémon retreats, and Hop recovers the Rusted Shield from Shielbert before pursuing Zamazenta. After Hop successfully captures Zamazenta at Slumbering Weald, the Rusted Shield becomes its held item for all subsequent battles in which Hop participates.

When Zamazenta possesses this item during a battle, it undergoes a transformation into its Crowned Shield form. Furthermore, in combat, the item modifies Zamazenta's move Iron Head, changing it into Behemoth Bash.

In the Pokémon Journeys anime episode "Sword and Shield: From Here to Eternatus!", Goh and Sonia located the Rusted Shield at the altar within the Slumbering Weald after invoking the assistance of Zacian and Zamazenta to manage the Darkest Day crisis. Goh collected both it and the Rusted Sword, taking both artifacts with him. During "Sword and Shield... The Legends Awaken!", the Rusted Shield summoned Zamazenta to the tower summit. Zamazenta then seized the Shield in its mouth, enabling its transformation into its Crowned Shield form. After Goh successfully captured Eternatus, Zamazenta abandoned its Shield. Later, Ash and Goh were observed returning both the Rusted Sword and Shield to their original resting places. The Rusted Shield was featured in a retrospective sequence in "The Champion," held by Hop. When he raised it during his and Victor's confrontation with Eternatus at the tower summit, it brought Zamazenta to their aid and facilitated its shift into its Crowned Shield form.

The Rusted Shield first appeared in a flashback in the Pokémon Adventures manga chapter "Ooh Shiny!! Legendary Equipment," alongside the Rusted Sword. One year earlier, Henry, accompanied by Casey, ventured into Slumbering Weald with the intent of viewing the two legendary artifacts. After encountering illusory forms of Zacian and Zamazenta at the altar at the forest's conclusion and assuring them they had no intention of theft, the two Trainers approached the artifacts on the altar. They were then rendered unconscious by an intense flash of light. Upon regaining consciousness, the artifacts were gone. In "Cracking!! Brilliant Tap-Dancing," it was disclosed that Sordward and Shielbert had stolen the artifacts. In "Cracked!! The Rusted Sword and Shield," the brothers utilized the artifacts to "prove" their heritage as descendants of Galar's ancient heroes. When Sordward and Shielbert confronted Eternatus at the tower summit, they produced the artifacts, but nothing occurred, and the items were propelled into the moat surrounding Hammerlocke Stadium. Casey's Falinks and Stunfisk subsequently retrieved them and brought them to Casey, who then entrusted them to Henry for cleaning and mending. However, to Henry's dismay, he soon realized he was incapable of forging and repairing the artifacts. He refused to relinquish the artifacts, asserting that he had only participated in the Gym Challenge to acquire them. In "Oh My!! The Disappearance of Henry," the artifacts, along with Henry, vanished as Zacian and Zamazenta attacked Eternatus, having been teleported away by an unseen Pokémon. Casey later uncovered that the artifacts had ended up in two distinct locations: the Isle of Armor and the Crown Tundra. In "Crackle!! Retrieve the Sword & Shield," it was revealed that Galarian Moltres had discovered the Rusted Shield and concealed it at Loop Lagoon. Henry managed to reclaim the artifact from Moltres shortly before it seized him and transported him to the Crown Tundra, where he later successfully purged it of Eternatus's poison. In "Shing!! Final Form," Zamazenta employed the shield to transition into its Crowned Shield form.

The Rusted Shield was introduced as a Pokémon Tool card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game during the English Sword & Shield Series (known as the Japanese Sword & Shield Era). It was initially launched in the Japanese Shiny Star V expansion before making its English debut in the Shining Fates expansion, with illustrations by 5ban Graphics. This card allows Zamazenta V to gain 70 HP. Although the player seemingly obtains this item in Slumbering Weald during the climax of Pokémon Shield, this is not the case, as the game merely indicates its acquisition without the player actually receiving any tangible item.

Title: Fossilized Bird

This ancient artifact, known as the Fossilized Bird (カセキのトリ in Japanese), made its debut in Generation VIII. It serves as the counterpart to the Fossilized Fish. When presented at specific restoration sites in conjunction with either a Fossilized Drake or Fossilized Dino, the Fossilized Bird can be reanimated into Dracozolt or Arctozolt, corresponding to the other fossil used. An instance of the Fossilized Bird featured in the episode "A Pinch of This, a Pinch of That!", depicting Ash, Goh, and Chloe assisting Cara Liss and Bray Zenn in its excavation within the Wild Area. Subsequently, it was united with a Fossilized Dino and brought back to life as an Arctozolt. Another Fossilized Bird made an appearance in "Clip-Clop!! The King's Horse," where Marnie was depicted unearthing one for Cara Liss. By combining it with the Fossilized Drake Marnie had also procured, Cara Liss successfully restored it into a Dracozolt. Within the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Fossilized Bird is depicted on the Rare Fossil Item card, which formed part of the Darkness Ablaze expansion, originally launched as the Infinity Zone expansion in Japan. As a Fossil Trainer card, once activated, it is placed on the Bench, functioning as a Colorless-type Basic Pokémon. This card facilitates evolution into any of the Generation VIII Fossil Pokémon: Dracozolt, Arctozolt, Dracovish, or Arctovish. Pokémon restored from the Fossilized Bird possess an additional 10 points in their Attack and Special Attack stats, yet concurrently have 10 fewer points in their Defense and Special Defense stats, when contrasted with their counterparts reconstructed from the Fossilized Fish.

Title: Fossilized Fish

The Fossilized Fish (Japanese: カセキのサカナ Fossilized Fish) made its debut as a fossil in Generation VIII, serving as the counterpart to the Fossilized Bird. When presented at designated restoration sites alongside either a Fossilized Drake or a Fossilized Dino, this fossil can be regenerated, yielding a Dracovish with the former or an Arctovish with the latter. Notably, their visual representations are inverted in Pokémon the Series, aligning them with the specific Pokémon they are intended to reconstitute. In the episode "A Pinch of This, a Pinch of That!", Ash, Goh, and Chloe assisted Cara Liss and Bray Zenn in unearthing a Fossilized Fish from the Wild Area. Subsequently, it was combined with a Fossilized Drake, leading to the restoration of a Dracovish. Another instance of the Fossilized Fish occurred in "Clip-Clop!! The King's Horse", where Marnie presented one she had discovered to Cara Liss. Paired with a Fossilized Dino, also sourced by Marnie, Cara Liss successfully reconstituted it into an Arctovish.

Within the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Fossilized Fish is depicted on the Rare Fossil Item card, found in the Darkness Ablaze expansion (originally from the Japanese Infinity Zone expansion). As a Fossil Trainer card, once played, it occupies the Bench as a Colorless-type Basic Pokémon. This allows for evolution into any of the Generation VIII Fossil Pokémon: Dracozolt, Arctozolt, Dracovish, or Arctovish. In the core series games Pokémon Sword and Shield, an apparent sprite error depicts the Fossilized Fish and Fossilized Drake with exchanged visuals. Though the Fossilized Fish contributes the "-vish" head component to Dracovish and Arctovish, its in-game sprite incongruously resembles the body segment attributed to the Fossilized Drake. Conversely, the animated series presents their appearances as precisely the inverse of their in-game sprites. Pokémon resurrected utilizing the Fossilized Fish exhibit a gain of 10 points in both their Defense and Special Defense stats, while simultaneously experiencing a reduction of 10 points in their Attack and Special Attack stats, when contrasted with their counterparts reconstructed from the Fossilized Bird.

Title: Fossilized Drake
