Originating in Generation VIII, the Fossilized Drake (Japanese: カセキのリュウ, meaning Fossilized Dragon) is a type of ancient fossil. It serves as the direct counterpoint to the Fossilized Dino. When taken to designated locations and combined with either a Fossilized Bird or a Fossilized Fish, the Fossilized Drake enables the restoration of a Dracozolt or a Dracovish, respectively. Interestingly, within Pokémon the Series, the visual designs of the Fossilized Fish and Fossilized Drake are inverted, aligning them with the specific Pokémon they are intended to revive.

An instance of a Fossilized Drake appeared in the episode "A Pinch of This, a Pinch of That!", where Ash, Goh, and Chloe assisted Cara Liss and Bray Zenn in unearthing it within the Wild Area. This particular fossil was subsequently joined with a Fossilized Fish and successfully brought back to life as a Dracovish. Another Fossilized Drake was featured in "Clip-Clop!! The King's Horse," discovered by Marnie for Cara Liss. When united with a Fossilized Bird, also found by Marnie, Cara Liss was able to restore it into a Dracozolt.

In the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Fossilized Drake is depicted on the Rare Fossil Item card, part of the Darkness Ablaze expansion, which first launched in the Japanese Infinity Zone expansion. This card functions as a Fossil Trainer card; upon being played, it is placed onto the Bench as a Colorless-type Basic Pokémon. From this state, it possesses the ability to evolve into any of the Generation VIII Fossil Pokémon: Dracozolt, Arctozolt, Dracovish, or Arctovish.

Within the Pokémon Sword and Shield games, the sprites for the Fossilized Fish and Fossilized Drake seem to have been erroneously swapped. While the Fossilized Drake provides the "Draco-" body section for Dracozolt and Dracovish, its visual in-game model resembles the head component typically supplied by the Fossilized Fish. Conversely, in the animated series, their appearances are presented as the inverse of their game sprites. Pokémon resurrected using the Fossilized Drake exhibit a reduction of 10 points in both their Special Attack and Special Defense stats, alongside an increase of 20 points in Speed, when compared to their counterparts revived from the Fossilized Dino.

Title: Fossilized Dino

Known in Japanese as カセキのクビナガ (Fossilized Plesiosaur), the Fossilized Dino made its debut in Generation VIII as an ancient relic. It stands as the opposite piece to the Fossilized Drake. When taken to specific research facilities and paired with either a Fossilized Bird or a Fossilized Fish, this fossil allows for the revival of an Arctozolt or an Arctovish, in that order.

This particular fossil was showcased in the episode "A Pinch of This, a Pinch of That!", where Ash, Goh, and Chloe assisted Cara Liss and Bray Zenn in unearthing it within the expansive Wild Area. Subsequently, it was fused with a Fossilized Bird to bring an Arctozolt back to life. Another instance of the Fossilized Dino surfaced in "Clip-Clop!! The King's Horse," revealing Marnie's discovery of one for Cara Liss. With the addition of a Fossilized Fish, also located by Marnie, Cara Liss successfully reconstructed an Arctovish.

Within the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Fossilized Dino is depicted on the Rare Fossil Item card. This card, a component of the Darkness Ablaze expansion (originally from the Japanese Infinity Zone expansion), functions as a Fossil Trainer card. Once activated, it occupies the Bench as a Colorless-type Basic Pokémon, capable of evolving into any of the Generation VIII Fossil Pokémon: Dracozolt, Arctozolt, Dracovish, or Arctovish.

It's worth noting that, despite its English appellation "Dino," plesiosaurs are not true dinosaurs but rather a distinct group of marine reptiles. Pokémon resurrected from the Fossilized Dino possess an advantage of 10 additional points in both their Special Attack and Special Defense statistics, alongside a reduction of 20 points in their Speed stat, when contrasted with species revived from the Fossilized Drake.

Title: Strawberry Sweet

Originating in Generation VIII, the Strawberry Sweet (Japanese: いちごアメざいく Strawberry Amezaiku) functions as a held item. Its primary purpose is facilitating the evolution of Milcery, being one of several sweet items capable of doing so. For evolution into Alcremie, a trainer must cause their Milcery to hold this item, then perform a spinning motion in-game. This specific interaction triggers the transformation, and the item is subsequently consumed during this process.

The item made its anime debut in The Sweet Taste of Battle!, initially serving as decoration on a cupcake Goh was enjoying. Observing his recently captured Milcery's curiosity towards the sweet, Goh placed it atop her head. He then celebrated their new friendship by spinning with her, unintentionally initiating her evolution into Alcremie. Later, in a flashback featured in The Bittersweet Truth, a quartet of Strawberry Sweets was visible. During this flashback, Murdock's Milcery acquired one of these sweets and performed a spin, leading to her own evolution into Alcremie.

Title: Sweet Apple

The Sweet Apple, known in Japanese as あまーいりんご (Sweet Apple), is an evolutionary item that made its debut in Generation VIII. When used, it facilitates the evolution of Applin into Appletun, and the item is consumed in the process.

This item was integrated into Pokémon GO on April 24, 2025. In Pokémon GO, Sweet Apples are indispensable for evolving particular Pokémon, and they are used up after each application. Specifically, transforming Applin into Appletun requires 200 Applin Candy along with 20 Sweet Apples. Trainers can obtain Sweet Apples, Tart Apples, or even encounter a wild Applin by interacting with apple icons that occasionally materialize near PokéStops. Furthermore, the likelihood of these apples appearing at PokéStops is increased when a Mossy Lure Module is active. Beyond gameplay, a Sweet Apple is also featured in the visual design for the Pokémon School merchandise collection, available exclusively at the Pokémon Center.

Title: Tart Apple
Introduced in Generation VIII, the Tart Apple (known in Japanese as すっぱいりんご, or Sour Apple) is a particular type of game item. Its application prompts Applin's evolution into Flapple, which consumes the item upon use. This item was incorporated into Pokémon GO on April 24, 2025. In Pokémon GO, Tart Apples are a prerequisite for evolving certain Pokémon, and they are always consumed during the process. For Applin to evolve into Flapple in Pokémon GO, players need 200 Applin Candy and 20 Tart Apples. Tapping on apples that manifest near PokéStops can yield either Tart Apples, Sweet Apples, or even a wild Applin encounter. The occurrence rate of these apples at PokéStops is further boosted when a Mossy Lure Module is active. The Tart Apple has also been featured across various pieces of officially licensed merchandise.

Title: Throat Spray

Originating in Generation VIII, Throat Spray (Japanese: のどスプレー Throat Spray) is a specific kind of held item. When a Pokémon equips this item and successfully executes a sound-based move, the Throat Spray is consumed, and the Pokémon's Special Attack stat increases by one stage. This item's effect does not activate, and it is therefore not consumed, if the used move fails or if the ongoing battle concludes. Curiously, although Pokémon are generally said to be incapable of using manmade healing items when held, Throat Spray bears a noticeable resemblance to spray-type Potions in its visual design.

Title: Eject Pack

The Eject Pack (Japanese: だっしゅつパック Break-Out Pack) is a held item, introduced in Generation VIII, that is consumed upon use. Its primary effect causes the holder to switch out of battle whenever any of its stats are lowered. Upon a stat reduction (even one self-inflicted by moves like Overheat), the Eject Pack is used up, and the Pokémon automatically swaps places with another in the party. Furthermore, if a Pokémon carrying an Eject Pack is sent into battle while Sticky Web is active on its side of the field, it will immediately perform another switch-out. However, due to the game mechanic limiting switch-outs to one per move, an Eject Pack will not activate if its holder is hit by Parting Shot. Should multiple Pokémon attempt to activate their held Eject Buttons or Eject Packs simultaneously, only the item belonging to the fastest among them will trigger.

Title: Blunder Policy

Making its debut in Generation VIII, the Blunder Policy (referred to in Japanese as からぶりほけん, meaning Miss Insurance) is a specialized held item. If a Pokémon holding this item uses a move that misses one of its intended targets because of an accuracy or evasion check, the Blunder Policy is consumed, resulting in the holder's Speed increasing by two stages. Nevertheless, the Blunder Policy does not activate if a one-hit knockout move misses, if Triple Kick, Triple Axel, or Population Bomb conclude early due to a missed accuracy check on one of their subsequent hits, or if a move misses because its target is experiencing the semi-invulnerable turn of another move. Activation of the Blunder Policy is contingent only on a move missing; it does not trigger for other reasons a move might fail to affect a target, such as immunity based on type or Ability, or being protected by a defensive move. In a Double Battle, should Dragon Darts have missed one target due to accuracy or evasion reasons, but successfully hit the other target, that hit target will be struck twice, and the Blunder Policy will still activate even in the absence of a visible message indicating a missed target.

In the Pokémon Trading Card Game, Blunder Policy was introduced as a Pokémon Tool card during the English Sword & Shield Series (which aligns with the Japanese Sword & Shield Era). It first appeared in the Japanese S-P Promotional cards and the English Brilliant Stars expansion, with its artwork created by Ayaka Yoshida. This card allows the player to draw three cards if they get tails when flipping a coin for an attack from the Pokémon it is attached to. A pair of memo pads, designed to resemble the Weakness Policy and Blunder Policy, was launched on December 12, 2024, as an exclusive offering from the Pokémon Center.

Title: Room Service

Originating in Generation VIII, Room Service (Japanese: ルームサービス Room Service) is a consumable held item. It serves to reduce the holder's Speed stat when the move Trick Room is in effect. When Trick Room is initiated while a Pokémon holds Room Service, the item is consumed, causing a one-stage drop in the holder's Speed. Furthermore, Room Service will likewise be consumed, resulting in the same Speed decrease, if the Pokémon equipped with it enters the battlefield while Trick Room is already active. This mechanism mirrors that of Electric Seed and other terrain-based seeds.

Title: Utility Umbrella

Making its debut in Generation VIII, the Utility Umbrella (Japanese: ばんのうがさ Utility Umbrella) is an item that a Pokémon can hold. A Pokémon carrying the Utility Umbrella remains unaffected by the various impacts of rain and harsh sunlight. Conversely, all effects of sandstorm, hail, and snow proceed as usual. The Abilities and self-targeting moves of the Pokémon holding it function as if rain or sunlight were absent, with the specific exclusions of Protosynthesis and Orichalcum Pulse. For instance, a Pokémon possessing Chlorophyll, or using Growth or Synthesis, will experience the following in harsh sunlight: its Speed will not be doubled, its stats will only be boosted by 1 stage, and it will only recover half its HP, respectively. Water-type and Fire-type moves aimed at the holder will not have their power modified (neither increased nor decreased) by rain or sun. Moves whose accuracy is typically altered by rain or sun (such as Thunder) will exhibit their standard accuracy when targeting the holder. Conversely, these weather effects apply without modification to the moves used by the holder. Solar Beam, when employed by a Pokémon holding the Utility Umbrella during rain or harsh sunlight, will have its charge turns and power remain uninfluenced by the weather conditions. Should the holder use Weather Ball in rain or harsh sunlight, it will be Normal-type and its power will not be doubled, as if no weather were present. In harsh sunlight, Hydro Steam's power will not receive a 50% boost if used by a Pokémon carrying the Utility Umbrella; rather, its power will be *reduced* by 50%, akin to other Water-type moves. Notably, these particular effects are only negated if the *user* of the move is holding a Utility Umbrella; a *target's* held Utility Umbrella does not affect them. A Pokémon holding the Utility Umbrella remains susceptible to being frozen even amidst harsh sunlight. An Overcast Form Cherrim equipped with the Utility Umbrella is unable to transform into its Sunshine Form. Conversely, if a Sunshine Form Cherrim comes to hold the Utility Umbrella (for example, through a move like Trick), it will instantaneously revert to its Overcast Form. In a similar vein, a Cherrim that relinquishes the Utility Umbrella while in the sun will instantly change to Sunshine Form. Nevertheless, a Pokémon holding the Utility Umbrella remains responsive to its allies' Flower Gift. The Utility Umbrella holds no sway outside of battle, meaning Sliggoo can still achieve evolution even while equipped with the item.

Title: Dynamax Candy

Starting in Generation VIII, the Dynamax Candy (ダイマックスアメ Daimax Candy in Japanese) is a specific type of consumable item. Its purpose is to boost a Pokémon's Dynamax Level. When administered from the Bag to a Pokémon, a Dynamax Candy raises its Dynamax Level by a single point. This item can be applied to a Pokémon repeatedly until it reaches its maximum Dynamax Level of 10. Each additional Dynamax Level bestows a 5% increase to a Dynamax Pokémon's HP, calculated from its maximum base HP. Zacian, Zamazenta, and Eternatus are ineligible for Dynamax Candy due to their inherent inability to Dynamax. Nevertheless, Shedinja can receive the item; however, it has no actual impact since its HP value does not change even when it Dynamaxes.

Title: Moomoo Cheese

Moomoo Cheese (Japanese: モーモーチーズ Moomoo Cheese) made its initial appearance as an ingredient within Generation VIII. This item subsequently featured as an Item card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game during the English Sword & Shield Series, corresponding to the Japanese Sword & Shield Era. Its first release occurred in the Japanese Legendary Heartbeat subset and the English Vivid Voltage expansion, featuring visuals by 5ban Graphics. The card enables players to heal 30 damage from up to two Pokémon, provided they have Energy attached. Its nomenclature suggests a potential derivation from Moomoo Milk.

Title: Rotom Catalog

The Rotom Catalog (Japanese: ロトムのカタログ Rotom Catalog), a Key Item, made its debut in Generation VIII. This valuable tool empowers the player to modify the distinct forms of a Rotom. However, in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, a specific condition applies: a Rotom must have previously assumed a particular form within Rotom's Room at least once for that form to become accessible in the Rotom Catalog. By contrast, in Pokémon Sword and Shield, Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, and Pokémon Legends: Z-A, every form is already unlocked upon the item's receipt.

Title: Max Honey

Max Honey, an item introduced during Generation VIII, is identified in Japanese as ダイミツ (Dai Honey). This unique form of honey is specifically produced by Dynamax Vespiquen. Its primary utility involves being retrieved from the Bag and applied to a fainted Pokémon, which results in both the Pokémon's revival and a full restoration of its HP. Moreover, Max Honey functions as a crucial extra component within Max Soup for the specific purpose of either granting or removing Urshifu's Gigantamax Factor. In the game Rumble!! The Darkest Day Returns, Casey, Marvin, and Hop successfully located Max Honey on Honeycalm Island, thereby facilitating Henry's Urshifu in unlocking its Gigantamax capabilities.

Title: Max Mushrooms

The item known as Max Mushrooms (Japanese: ダイキノコ, or Dai Mushroom) first appeared in Generation VIII. A bowl of Max Soup, obtainable by trading three clusters of these fungi, offers the ability to grant a Gigantamax Factor to an eligible Pokémon or reverse this state. However, Urshifu's specific Max Soup preparation uniquely demands the addition of one comb of Max Honey during the exchange process. In battle, an active Pokémon's Attack, Defense, Special Attack, Special Defense, and Speed will all ascend by one stage when a Max Mushroom is deployed from the Trainer's Bag. Its integration into Pokémon GO occurred on December 3, 2024. When used, one Max Mushroom temporarily boosts the damage of Fast Attacks, Charged Attacks, and Max Moves/G-Max Moves performed by the player's Dynamax/Gigantamax Pokémon in Max Battles for a duration of 30 minutes. The episode "The Winding Path to Greatness!" depicted Allister assisting Ash and Goh in gathering three Max Mushroom clusters; these were then prepared into Max Soup, allowing Ash's Gengar to achieve Gigantamax form. An appearance of Max Mushrooms can also be found in the artwork for the Venipede card, part of the Chilling Reign expansion.

Title: Galarica Twig

This particular material, designated as the Galarica Twig (its Japanese name being ガラナツのえだ Galanut Branch), premiered as an item in Generation VIII. On the Isle of Armor, a woman located on a small island within the Workout Sea accepts eight Galarica Twigs in exchange for a Galarica Cuff. Similarly, within the Crown Tundra, a different woman inside the Roaring-Sea Caves offers a Galarica Wreath when presented with fifteen Galarica Twigs. Furthermore, in the Coastal Biome of Blueberry Academy's Terarium, a student in the Coastal Plaza, situated by the vending machines near the healing station, will receive Galarica Twigs. The quantity of Twigs needed for both a Galarica Cuff and a Galarica Wreath remains unchanged, at eight and fifteen, respectively.

Title: Armor Pass

Originating in Generation VIII, the Armor Pass (known in Japan as ヨロイパス, literally "Armor Pass") is classified as a Key Item. Through Wedgehurst Station, players utilize this pass to gain admittance to the Isle of Armor. A pivotal moment in Oh My!! The Disappearance of Henry saw Oleana furnish Casey, Marvin, Hop, Marnie, and Bede with both an Armor Pass and a Crown Pass each. This distribution occurred after Casey deduced that the Rusted Sword and Shield had ended up on the Isle of Armor and the Crown Tundra following their struggle against Eternatus, thus empowering the group to embark on a search for the valuable artifacts.

Title: Armorite Ore

This particular item, known as Armorite Ore (Japanese: ヨロイこうせき Armor Ore), was initially introduced in Generation VIII. Within the Master Dojo, a Master Dojo Student is prepared to teach the player any of the new moves that became available in Version 1.2.0, requiring five pieces of Armorite Ore for each instance of instruction. Furthermore, in the Training Lowlands, the Digging Pa provides a service to unearth Watts for the player, asking for seven Armorite Ore per digging session. Moreover, a female NPC located at the Workout Sea is able to fully reset the EVs of one of the player's Pokémon, accepting ten Armorite Ore for each complete reset.

Title: Mark Charm

The Mark Charm, identified in Japanese as あかしのおまもり Mark Charm, functions as a Key Item first introduced in Generation VIII. During Generation VIII gameplay, having the Mark Charm in the player's Bag substantially increases the probability, by a factor of three, for wild Pokémon to appear with a mark. In Generation IX, its effect is to similarly triple the odds of symbol encounters generating with a mark while the item resides in the player's Bag, with wanderers being an exception. It holds no influence over the bonuses applied to Pokémon encountered within Poké Portal News event mass outbreaks.

Title: Legendary Clue 1

The Legendary Clue 1 (Japanese: でんせつのメモ1 Legendary Memo 1) is a specific Key Item originating from Generation VIII. This item provides the player with deeper insights into Calyrex. Accessing its contents is a prerequisite for locating Calyrex and successfully concluding the expedition "The Sacred Bonds of Sovereign and Steed!" which Peony initiated. The information contained within Legendary Clue 1 evolves as the player advances through the narrative.

In Freezington, local lore recounts a tale of a Legendary Pokémon known as the King of Bountiful Harvests. Legends depict this monarch adorned with an immense crown upon its head; however, a wooden effigy situated near the village's central fields lacks any indication of such headwear. Calyrex manifested its presence once the crown was positioned atop this statue of the King of Bountiful Harvests. Calyrex possesses the apparent ability to communicate with humans by temporarily inhabiting their bodies—in this particular instance, Peony's. The immediate task involves gathering further intelligence on the King of Bountiful Harvests from the residents of the village.

Upon Calyrex's revelation that its steed's preferred sustenance is carrots, it became clear that obtaining this beloved food is essential to locating the king's cherished mount. The denizens of Freezington might offer assistance in this endeavor. After learning of my success in acquiring Carrot Seeds, Calyrex directed me to two distinct locations suitable for cultivating the carrots. I must choose between the snow-covered field and the plot adjacent to the graves to plant these seeds.

From the seeds sown in the snow-blanketed field, Calyrex successfully cultivated an Iceroot Carrot. Its enticing aroma drew Glastrier out, though it promptly galloped off towards Freezington. Similarly, when seeds were planted in the field by the graves, Calyrex produced a Shaderoot Carrot. This scent lured Spectrier forth, which also subsequently bolted towards Freezington.

By utilizing mane hair acquired from defeating the Pokémon that once served as the king's devoted steed, combined with the Radiant Petal grown by Calyrex itself, I successfully fabricated the Reins of Unity. My next step is to proceed to the Crown Shrine, the current dwelling place of the steed, to rendezvous with Calyrex.

Calyrex, the esteemed King of Bountiful Harvests, managed to re-establish its connection with its faithful steed thanks to the newly created Reins of Unity. Furthermore, Calyrex appears to have rediscovered its trust in humanity. In gratitude for my aid in restoring its power, Calyrex has granted permission for me to join my journey.

Title: Legendary Clue 3

Designated as a Key Item in Generation VIII, the Legendary Clue 3 (known in Japanese as でんせつのメモ3 or Legendary Memo 3) provides crucial insights into the legendary birds and their Galarian counterparts. Accessing its contents is a prerequisite for locating these magnificent Pokémon and successfully concluding Peony's expedition, titled "A Legendary Tree of a Legendary Three!". This item's textual details dynamically update throughout the player's journey. Its opening lines contain a vital directive: "At the sun's highest point, its rays illuminate a grand tree whose foliage appears to blaze. This is the gathering place for the fabled avian creatures." This significant clue is echoed once more within the text: "Upon the sun's culmination, its light directly bathes a vast, fiery-leafed tree. It is here that the mythic winged Pokémon congregate." Following this, the initial notes detail the legendary Pokémon's whereabouts: The bird of orange dashed off to the Wild Area. The bird of black flew off to the Isle of Armor. The bird of purple set off across the Crown Tundra. Upon the successful capture of each legendary bird, the pertinent entry is revised to reflect: Zapdos was caught in the Wild Area. Moltres was caught on the Isle of Armor. Articuno was caught in the Crown Tundra.

Title: Crown Pass

The Crown Pass, a Key Item, was introduced during Generation VIII and is identified in Japanese as カンムリパス (Crown Pass). This item enables the player to access the Crown Tundra, with passage facilitated via Wedgehurst Station. Within the events of Oh My!! The Disappearance of Henry, after Casey determined that the Rusted Sword and Shield had been transported to the Isle of Armor and the Crown Tundra following their clash with Eternatus, Oleana furnished Casey, Marvin, Hop, Marnie, and Bede with a Crown Pass and an Armor Pass each, allowing them to undertake the search for these significant artifacts.

Title: Radiant Petal

A Key Item making its debut in Generation VIII, the Radiant Petal (known in Japanese as かがやくはなびら, or Shining Petal) serves a crucial purpose. This unique item is combined with either a lock of White Mane Hair or Black Mane Hair to forge the Reins of Unity. Its appearance in Clip-Clop!! The King's Horse depicted its creation by Calyrex, an act that temporarily rendered the Pokémon unconscious. Subsequently, the petal, along with Black Mane Hair acquired by Bede, was synthesized by Henry into the Reins of Unity.

Title: Black Mane Hair

Designated as a Key Item, the Black Mane Hair (くろいたてがみ Black Mane in Japanese) made its debut in Generation VIII. It serves the purpose of being combined with a Radiant Petal to forge the Reins of Unity. The item featured in Clip-Clop!! The King's Horse, where Bede managed to secure it after a brief skirmish with Spectrier, whom he was pursuing across the Path to the Peak. Subsequently, Henry united this item with the Radiant Petal that Calyrex had produced, thereby fashioning the Reins of Unity.

Title: Shaderoot Carrot
Originating in Generation VIII as a Key Item, the Shaderoot Carrot is recognized in Japanese as くろいにんじん (Black Carrot). This valuable item grants the player the ability to summon Spectrier at the Crown Shrine. Its initial appearance was documented in Munch Munch!! The Mysterious Carrot, a narrative in which Calyrex cultivated it from Carrot Seeds sown by Henry, Bede, and Peonia at the Old Cemetery. The intense aroma emanating from the carrot drew Spectrier to the vicinity, yet the Pokémon, unable to discern the source of the scent, simply dashed past the group. Later, during the events of Rumble!! The Darkest Day Returns, Bede strategically placed the Shaderoot Carrot in a bowl at the Crown Shrine, aiming to lure Spectrier to that specific spot. Immediately upon Spectrier's arrival and subsequent discovery of the carrot, it consumed the item, thereby providing Calyrex with a crucial opening to employ the Reins of Unity.

Title: Dynite Ore

Dynite Ore (Japanese: マックスこうせき Max Ore) is an item type originating in Generation VIII. Within the Max Lair, a vendor offers various goods to trainers who provide them with Dynite Ore. Peonia sometimes offers to initiate a Dynamax Adventure, leading to a special Pokémon the player has not yet encountered, in exchange for five Dynite Ore pieces. If a player backs out of three or more Dynamax Adventures consecutively, the Scientist managing the Max Lair will demand that the player provide her with a quantity of Dynite Ore equal to the number of failed attempts (up to 10) before allowing subsequent participation. Near the crop plots in Freezington, an elderly individual trades Carrot Seeds for eight units of Dynite Ore.

Title: Carrot Seeds

This essential Key Item, known in Japanese as にんじんのタネ Carrot Seeds, made its debut in Generation VIII. With these seeds, trainers can obtain either the Iceroot Carrot from the crop plot located in Snowslide Slope or the Shaderoot Carrot from the Old Cemetery's crop plot. These specific carrots are used to call forth Glastrier or Spectrier, correspondingly. In the story Munch Munch!! The Mysterious Carrot, Carrot Seeds were featured as one of several keepsakes Henry was compelled to purchase while in Freezington. Following Henry, Peonia, and Bede's planting of the seeds at the Old Cemetery's crop plot, Calyrex cultivated one into a Shaderoot Carrot; its distinct aroma subsequently drew Spectrier to their location.

Title: Ability Patch

This particular item, known as the Ability Patch (Japanese name: とくせいパッチ Ability Patch), made its debut in Generation VIII of the Pokémon games. Its primary function is to alter a Pokémon's designated Ability. When activated from the player's Bag, this item specifically transforms a Pokémon's Ability slot, switching it from one of its typical standard Abilities to its unique Hidden Ability. Consequently, should the Pokémon evolve into a form possessing a distinct Hidden Ability from its pre-evolution, the evolved Pokémon will acquire that specific Hidden Ability. Furthermore, a Pokémon whose Hidden Ability was acquired through the use of an Ability Patch is capable of transmitting this Hidden Ability to its offspring through the breeding mechanic. It is important to note that the Ability Patch's application is restricted to Pokémon species that inherently possess a Hidden Ability. To illustrate, one can employ it to switch a Rookidee's Ability from either Keen Eye or Unnerve to Big Pecks; however, it is ineffective on a Metapod, as its Ability (Shed Skin) is fixed and lacks a Hidden Ability counterpart. Initially, the Ability Patch was ineffective at reverting a Pokémon's Ability from its Hidden Ability back to one of its regular standard Abilities. Currently, however, the Ability Patch possesses the capability to remove a Hidden Ability, thereby assigning the Pokémon its first available Ability slot. If a Pokémon is holding an Ability Patch, the move Fling will fail when attempted by that Pokémon.

Title: Adamant Crystal

First appearing as an item in Pokémon Legends: Arceus, the Adamant Crystal (Japanese: だいこんごうだま Great Diamond Orb) serves to enable Dialga's transformation between its standard form and its unique Origin Forme. In Generation VIII, this item was designated as a Key Item. It also stands as one of the legendary orbs connected to mythical Pokémon. Within Pokémon Legends: Arceus, using the Adamant Crystal from the satchel on Dialga initiates its form change between normal and Origin Forme. However, in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Dialga assumes its Origin Forme simply by holding the Adamant Crystal. Furthermore, when held, the item grants a 20% power increase to Dialga's Dragon-type and Steel-type moves.

Title: Lustrous Globe

The Lustrous Globe, identified in Japanese as だいしらたま (Great Pearl Orb), first appeared in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. This artifact enables Palkia to transition between its normal form and Origin Forme. It was categorized as a Key Item throughout Generation VIII and is one of the distinct orbs linked with mythical Pokémon. In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, players utilizing the Lustrous Globe from their satchel on Palkia will prompt its form transformation. In Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, however, if Palkia is equipped with a Lustrous Globe, it will switch to its Origin Forme. Moreover, it simultaneously enhances the power of Palkia's Dragon-type and Water-type moves by 20%.

Title: Griseous Core

This artifact, known in Japanese as だいはっきんだま (Great Platinum Orb), first appeared in the game Pokémon Legends: Arceus. Its primary function is enabling Giratina to transition between its Altered Forme and its Origin Forme. During Generation VIII, it was categorized as a Key Item. It is counted among the special orbs connected to legendary Pokémon. Specifically within Pokémon Legends: Arceus, activating the Griseous Core from the satchel while targeting Giratina causes the Pokémon to shift its appearance between its Altered and Origin Formes. Conversely, in the titles Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Giratina will assume its Origin Forme simply by holding the Griseous Core. Moreover, while held, it also enhances the power of Giratina's Dragon-type and Ghost-type attacks by 20%. Prior to the Griseous Core's introduction, from Generation IV to Generation VIII, these same effects were provided when Giratina held the Griseous Orb. However, since Generation IX, the Griseous Orb, when held, exclusively provides a 20% power increase to Giratina's Dragon-type and Ghost-type moves, no longer facilitating its transformation into its Origin Forme.

Title: Blank Plate

Known in Japanese as まっさらプレート (Blank Plate), this artifact functions as a Key Item within the game Pokémon Legends: Arceus. It is categorized as one of the Plates, which are deeply connected with the Pokémon Arceus. Employing it from the satchel on Arceus causes the deity's elemental affiliation to shift to Normal. An inscription can be found upon the Blank Plate, which reads: "Three beings whose power can hold both time and space fixed." In The Arceus Chronicles (Part 4), this Plate, alongside the 17 other type-specific Plates, was observed to be under Arceus's ownership.

Title: Strange Ball

The Strange Ball, known in Japanese as ストレンジボール, functions as a placeholder Poké Ball. It first appeared with the release of Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl. Its purpose is to stand in for the capture devices of Pokémon originally caught using a Poké Ball not present in the player's current game title. Beginning with Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, any Pokémon captured in a Poké Ball not accessible within that specific game will show the Strange Ball as its enclosing item. Within Pokémon Legends: Arceus, all Pokémon caught using any Poké Ball apart from the game's native Hisuian Poké Balls are presented as being held by a Strange Ball. Conversely, in Pokémon Brilliant Diamond, Shining Pearl, Scarlet, Violet, and the upcoming Legends: Z-A, Pokémon captured with any Hisuian Poké Balls are depicted as being in a Strange Ball. Notably, Pokémon Sword and Shield lack the Strange Ball; instead, Pokémon originating from Hisuian Poké Balls appear within standard Poké Balls, though Pokémon HOME retains information about their true capture device on its servers. Upon transfer back to HOME, these Pokémon subsequently revert to displaying their actual original Poké Balls.

Within Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, the Strange Ball is not a tangible item but rather a visual representation shown for Pokémon whose internal ball ID value lacks a corresponding match among the game's available Poké Balls. It transitioned into a physical item in Pokémon Legends: Arceus, a status it maintains in all succeeding titles. This item cannot be acquired through legitimate gameplay, preventing its normal deployment. Should it be acquired illicitly via hacking and then deployed from the satchel during a wild encounter, it would endeavor to capture the encountered Pokémon with a catch rate identical to that of a typical Hisuian Poké Ball, precisely 0.75×. Distinct from other Poké Balls in Legends: Arceus, the Strange Ball is not throwable by the player in the overworld environment; it is solely present within the satchel menu, absent from the throwable item selection in the screen's lower-right corner. Its utility is restricted to being chosen from the satchel menu during a combat situation. As with its Hisuian counterpart, the Strange Ball is not an item players can legitimately acquire, thus precluding its regular application. However, if obtained through hacking and utilized during a wild encounter, it would attempt to ensnare the wild Pokémon with a catch rate equivalent to a typical Poké Ball, at 1×.

From the sixth generation of games forward, Pokémon produced via breeding will inherit their containing Poké Ball from their progenitors. Nevertheless, any progeny otherwise slated to inherit a Strange Ball, Master Ball, or Cherish Ball will instead find themselves housed within a standard Poké Ball. The Strange Ball and the Park Ball are unique in being the only Poké Balls incapable of genuinely belonging to a Pokémon, a fact underscored by the way Pokémon depicted within Strange Balls nonetheless preserve their actual original Poké Ball's internal index number. In the entire core series of games, the Park Ball, Cherish Ball, and Strange Ball stand alone as the sole Poké Balls that have never been legitimately obtainable within any core series title.

Title: Legend Plate

The Legend Plate (Japanese: レジェンドプレート Legend Plate) is a specialized Key Item found in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. It stands among the collection of Plates, all intricately linked with the mythical Pokémon Arceus. When presented to Arceus, the Legend Plate causes the Pokémon to transform into the Normal type, an effect mirrored by the Blank Plate.

During combat scenarios, when Arceus employs its signature move Judgment, both the move's elemental type and Arceus's own type dynamically adjust. This adaptation is determined by the selected opponent's type and occurs prior to the attack's completion, changing Arceus's type to match Judgment's. While a trainer is in the process of choosing a move, Judgment's anticipated type against the currently selected opponent for that turn is clearly displayed. This adaptive typing functionality bears a resemblance to the move Hidden Power.

Judgment's type selection primarily targets a type that is super effective against the chosen opponent, with a specific preference for exploiting any double weaknesses present in dual-type targets. Should multiple types offer equally effective damage, Judgment will then default to the type that provides the strongest resistance to the target's *primary* elemental type, giving precedence to immunities, irrespective of the target's secondary type. For example, against Palkia, whose primary typing is Water, Judgment will invariably become Dragon-type. Similarly, when facing Giratina, whose primary type is Ghost, Judgment will consistently manifest as Dark-type. This occurs even though Fairy is immune to their shared secondary Dragon typing, as it does not resist Water or Ghost.

If a set of equally valid types still remains, Judgment will subsequently select the type that best resists the target's *secondary* type, provided one exists. If multiple types are still equally applicable after all these considerations, one will be chosen at random. This comprehensive calculation is performed anew during each turn, meaning Judgment's specific type may fluctuate among several valid options from one turn to the next.

The Legend Plate itself can be examined, bearing an inscription that reads: "From all creations, over all creations, does the Original One watch over all." It is worth noting that, due to the precise mechanics governing the Legend Plate's operation, using Judgment will never result in Arceus adopting the Normal type. This is because no Pokémon is weak to Normal-type attacks, and the concept of a typeless Pokémon does not exist within Pokémon Legends: Arceus.

Title: Rotom Phone

The Rotom Phone, known in Japanese as スマホロトム (Smartphone Rotom), is a mobile device imbued with enhanced capabilities following its possession by a Rotom. This item appears across several Pokémon game titles; it is encountered in Galar within Pokémon Sword and Shield, functions as a Key Item in Paldea in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, and will be present in Lumiose City, Kalos, in Pokémon Legends: Z-A. Its popularity extends across multiple Pokémon canons, serving as a versatile platform for various functions and applications, including a Pokédex. The Pokédex application for Paldea, in particular, was developed by Jacq.

In Pokémon Sword and Shield, the Rotom Phone acts as the primary menu interface. Through it, players gain access to their party, Bag, Town Map, Pokédex, League Card, and options, as well as the ability to save their game. It also facilitates setting up Camp in most areas, provided players have acquired Camping Gear from their mum. Connecting to the internet via the device enables access to Mystery Gift or Victory Station. Furthermore, the inherent Rotom can energize the motor of the Rotom Bike, boosting the player's travel speed and, after an upgrade, allowing for water traversal. In contrast to the Rotom Pokédex, the Rotom Phone remains silent throughout the main storyline, though it does offer navigation prompts and notifies players of occurrences in the Wild Area, such as changes in weather patterns.

For Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, the Rotom Phone menu grants access to the Map, Pokédex, and Profile. Once acquired, the device automatically appears when the main menu is opened. A unique feature allows players to grasp their Rotom Phone to cushion their fall from significant heights, slowing their descent before ground impact. Photo-taking is another capability in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, utilizing the camera app with options for front or back cameras. These captured images can be used to update the player's profile picture or Trainer icon, with filters and touch-ups available for added flair. Players can express themselves with a variety of faces and poses when taking selfies. When others join via the Union Circle, they can also feature in photos, employing their own preset gestures that are usable anywhere in the overworld. Many such gestures are unlocked through specific tasks and side quests, with some serving as homages to past characters or games, like Leon's Charizard pose. Similar to Pokémon Legends: Arceus, photos are saved directly to the system's memory or an SD Card.

Within The Teal Mask expansion, players can obtain the Roto-Stick, which broadens the camera's field of vision during selfie mode. This DLC also unlocks the ability to play three distinct music tracks through the Rotom Phone's radio, influencing NPCs' expressions when photos are taken with them, depending on the selected track. In The Indigo Disk, donating BP to the Photography Club located in the League Club Room provides access to unlockable frames and special effects for photos. The camera function is activated by pressing the Down Button. As players progress through their journey, several photographs are automatically captured to commemorate key story events. These images are displayed once and can be preserved using the console's screenshot function. Special photographs are specifically generated after successfully defeating a Team Star boss for the first time and after triumphing over a BB League Elite Four member for the first time.

Pokémon Legends: Z-A introduces a new iteration of the Rotom Phone, showcasing a familiar yet distinct design that includes three camera lenses. In this game, players can leverage the Rotom Phone's "Roto-Glide" ability to navigate across building rooftops as they traverse Lumiose City's urban environment. Consistent with the Galar and Paldea-centered titles, the Pokédex remains an application accessible on the player's Rotom Phone.

While players in Sword and Shield are limited to the default Rotom Phone, Scarlet and Violet offer customization through various cases. In The Teal Mask, changing the Rotom Phone case also alters the background patterns visible in the Rotom Phone menus. In Pokémon Masters EX, most characters do not utilize Rotom Phones because all sync pairs on Pasio are provided with a Poryphone. An exception is Raihan, whose character model depicts him holding his Rotom Phone, which he occasionally uses for selfies. Upon achieving 6★ EX status, his Rotom Phone case transforms to a gold color. He also carries his Rotom Phone in his anniversary attire, though its color does not change when he reaches 6★ EX in that specific outfit. Once players attain friendship level 100 with Raihan in the Trainer Lodge, they have the option to acquire a replica of a magenta Rotom Phone from the Lodge Exchange for display in the Trainer Lodge.

In random Pokémon Center dialogues, Jacq notes his development of a Paldean Pokédex app for Rotom Phones, while Rei references Rotom Phones as an example of a Pokémon-themed mobile device originating from Galar. In the anime, Ash received a smartphone from Professor Cerise in "Ivysaur's Mysterious Tower!". This device, along with Goh's smartphone, became a Rotom Phone after being possessed by two of Cerise's Rotom. Distinct from earlier Pokédexes in Pokémon the Series, these Rotom Phones furnish information on locations and landmarks, alongside vital statistics such as height. They have also been shown to feature a numbered Pokémon list and are the first animated Pokédexes known to register and list captured Pokémon. Akin to their game counterparts, Ash and Goh's Rotom Phones seldom initiate speech independently, though in "That New Old Gang of Mine!", Ash's Rotom Phone exhibited a belief in its own superiority over the Rotom Pokédex due to its diverse functionalities. Trainers registered for the World Coronation Series can use their Rotom Phones to identify and challenge other participants. In "Flash of the Titans!", Team Rocket was revealed to have obtained their own Rotom Phone from Giovanni. This particular device, unlike Ash and Goh's, is black and possesses a uniquely sassy and oblivious personality, which can sometimes influence the Pokédex entries it provides.

Further anime appearances include: a blue Rotom Phone with a Pokédex under Shane Seeker's ownership in "Raid Battle in The Ruins!"; a yellow Rotom Phone owned by Visquez in "Destination: Coronation!", which demonstrated its utility in helping World Coronation Series participants connect and arrange official battles; Oliver's Rotom Phone in "Dreams Are Made of These!"; Kricketina Kylie's in "Trade, Borrow, and Steal!"; a pink Rotom Phone belonging to Bea in "Solitary and Menacing!"; another pink Rotom Phone carried by Sonia in "Sword and Shield: "From Here to Eternatus!""; Professor Magnolia's white Rotom Phone in "Sword and Shield... The Legends Awaken!"; Chloe's pink Rotom Phone with a Pokédex, received from Professor Cerise in "To Train, or Not to Train!"; Rinto's green Rotom Phone in "Beyond Chivalry… Aiming to be a Leek Master!"; Gary's Rotom Phone, revealed in "Advice to Goh!"; Quillon's dark red Rotom Phone in "Leaping Toward the Dream!"; Danika's blue Rotom Phone in "Trial on a Golden Scale!"; Raihan's dark red Rotom Phone in "The Sweet Taste of Battle!"; Alternate World Ash's Rotom Phone in "Showdown at the Gates of Warp!"; Allister's dark green Rotom Phone in "Battling Turned Up to Eleven!"; Horace's Rotom Phone in "Battling in the Freezing Raid!"; Lyla and Sterling each owning a Rotom Phone in "Chasing to the Finish!"; Iris's Rotom Phone, obtained in "Curtain Up! Fight the Fights!"; and Volkner's Rotom Phone in "The Fiery Road to Mastership!".

In "The Pendant That Starts It All (Part One)", Liko, Ann, numerous other Indigo Academy students, the Rising Volt Tacklers, and the Explorers were depicted with Rotom Phones featuring the same designs as those seen in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet. Unlike the versions featured throughout Pokémon Journeys: The Series, these Rotom Phones can extend into their Pad Forme (Japanese: パッドフォルム), allowing them to function as tablets. Subsequent appearances include Roy's Rotom Phone in "The Treasure After the Storm!", Alex's in "The Future I Choose", Iono's Rotom Phone with a Magnemite Magnet Case in "Someone We Can't See! Whosawhatsit?", Tepen's usage in "The Stolen Treasure", Penny's Rotom Phone with an Eevee Case in "Welcome to Naranja Academy!", and Katy's in "Put Your Heart Into It!".

Title: Sandwich
For the Key Item called "Sandwich", please refer to Sandwich (item). In the Paldea region, "Sandwiches" (Japanese: サンドウィッチ) are a well-liked culinary item crafted from sliced bread and various fillings. Following the player's initial encounter with Nemona at Cabo Poco, their mother provides them with a plain sandwich. This item is subsequently used to nourish the injured Koraidon or Miraidon found by the player on the beach adjacent to Poco Path.

Within Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, individuals can create sandwiches during a picnic by engaging with the picnic table and choosing the "make a sandwich" option. Up to four participants can cooperate in a mini-game to prepare these sandwiches, drawing parallels to Pokéblocks, Poffins, and curry mechanics from prior Pokémon titles. Once a picnic host initiates sandwich preparation, other Union Circle participants may join by interacting with the table before the host begins the activity. Participants are allotted a 10-minute period to construct a sandwich using a variety of bread and filling choices. A completed sandwich bestows "Meal Powers," functioning similarly to Pass Powers from Generation V, O-Powers from Generation VI, and Roto Powers from Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon.

Condiments are initially spread onto the bread at the start of the assembly. Each ingredient chosen contributes a specific quantity of toppings, presented on a tray. Precision is crucial when layering ingredients and positioning the top slice of bread; any items that fall off the edge are lost. A sandwich necessitates both at least one condiment and a minimum of one ingredient; it cannot be composed solely of one or the other. Players have two preparation methods: selecting from predefined sandwich recipes in Recipe Mode — with extra recipes unlockable by conversing with individuals at a sandwich Shop while possessing a specific Herba Mystica — or by individually choosing up to ten ingredients and seasonings in Creative Mode.

Excluding Jambon-Beurres and Legendary sandwiches, every recipe type features four distinct tiers: Normal, Great, Ultra, and Master. Advancing to higher tiers demands more ingredients than the preceding ones. Any custom-made sandwiches, or recipes where too many components have fallen off, are designated as "A Tasty (Player) Original." When crafting a custom sandwich, the top slice of bread is not mandatory and its absence does not impact the resulting Meal Powers. A practical tactic involves deliberately discarding the top bread to prevent ingredient disruption. This strategy is even recognized in The Hidden Treasure of Area Zero DLC, where Crispin observes this action during his Elite Trial and questions if it's a Paldean custom.

Crafting a sandwich initiates three distinct Meal Powers, whose specific effects are determined by the chosen recipe or ingredients. These Meal Powers persist for 30 minutes from their creation. As only three Meal Powers can be active simultaneously, making an additional sandwich while existing powers are still in effect will override and replace them with the new set. Players can monitor their currently active Meal Powers and their remaining duration by pressing the right button on the D-Pad. Meal Powers are categorized into three levels, which are contingent on the point value contributed by their ingredients. With the sole exception of Egg Power, all other Meal Powers offer bonuses exclusively for one particular Pokémon type at a given time.

Beyond personal sandwich preparation for Meal Powers, a variety of premade dishes are available for purchase at numerous restaurants and food stalls across Paldea. Each of these items grants a fixed assortment of Meal Powers and is consumed upon acquisition. Every ingredient possesses three distinct value categories: flavor values, power values, and type values. Power values directly correlate with specific Meal Powers, detailed further below. Upon assembling a sandwich, the values from all included ingredients are totaled. The two greatest flavor values, the three greatest power values, and the three greatest type values are subsequently utilized to ascertain the sandwich's overall effects.

The pair of highest flavor values will each provide a +100 bonus to a particular power value, as outlined in an associated table. In instances where any of the top two flavor values are tied, a specific priority order is followed to resolve the tie, from the highest to the lowest preference: sweet > salty > sour > bitter > hot.

The trio of highest power values (after applying any flavor-based bonuses) dictates which Meal Powers are activated by the prepared food. The sole deviation is Sparkling Power, which requires the incorporation of at least two Herba Mystica to be triggered. Furthermore, the three highest type values determine the Pokémon types to which the Meal Powers will apply. Should two or more Herba Mystica be included, however, only the single highest type value is taken into account. For any ties among the three highest type values, the following hierarchy resolves the tie, from paramount to least priority: Normal > Fighting > Flying > Poison > Ground > Rock > Bug > Ghost > Steel > Fire > Water > Grass > Electric > Psychic > Ice > Dragon > Dark > Fairy.

Each sandwich produces three distinct effects, with every effect comprising a specific Meal Power, a corresponding intensity level, and a Pokémon type to which that power is directed. If we consider the three highest Meal Powers as A, B, and C in descending order, and similarly for the three highest types as A, B, and C, then the use of at least two Herba Mystica causes Powers A, B, and C to exclusively utilize type A. The intensity level of an effect is contingent on its associated power value and type value. Notably, Level 3 powers are exclusively achievable when a minimum of one Herba Mystica is incorporated.

Egg Power influences both Egg generation and item generation within the Picnic basket in several ways: Without any active Egg Power, checks for generating an Egg or an item happen every 60 seconds. Item checks occur each time, leading to a 33.66% chance for an item and a 3% chance (or 1.99% when adjusted for item checks) for an Egg to be generated every 60 seconds. For all active Egg Power levels, the interval between checks is reduced from 60 seconds to 10 seconds, and item generation checks only occur every sixth overall check, resulting in a 5.61% chance to generate an item. Egg generation rates vary based on the specific Egg Power level: Level 1 provides an approximate ×5 Egg generation rate and reduces egg cycles by ×1.25. Level 2 offers an approximate ×7 Egg generation rate and reduces egg cycles by ×1.50. Level 3 yields an approximate ×10 Egg generation rate and reduces egg cycles by ×2.

Other Meal Power effects include, but are not limited to: compelling a particular Pokémon type to appear in the wild (if feasible); boosting the Experience Points gained from defeating specific Pokémon types; enhancing the likelihood of a certain Pokémon type dropping an additional item (though this does not impact Gimmighoul Coins from Chest Forme Gimmighouls); augmenting item rewards obtained from Tera Raid Battles; increasing the spawn rate of abnormally large wild Pokémon; increasing the spawn rate of abnormally small wild Pokémon; improving the odds of encountering Shiny Pokémon in the wild; and raising the probability of wild Pokémon spawning with a special mark.

While sandwiches are a recurring sight throughout Pokémon the Series, the Paldean variety specifically debuted in the episode "An Unexpected Picnic!" Here, the Rising Volt Tacklers crafted them during a picnic outside Levincia. Later, in "The Knight in the Ruins," Liko, Roy, Dot, and Ult prepared these culinary items during their picnic in South Province (Area Three). In HZ116, Drayton assigned Roy to make sandwiches as a purported training exercise, though it was soon disclosed that Drayton was merely exploiting Roy to complete his own neglected Blueberry Quests. Episode HZ118 featured Liko making sandwiches with her Hattrem; witnessing Hattrem's thoughtful ingredient choices for Liko led Liko to recognize Hattrem's consistent selflessness and helpfulness, prompting her gratitude and triggering Hattrem's evolution into Hatterene. In "Discover! The Quaking Earth Titan!!", Violet informed Arven that Miraidon's attachment to him stemmed from its fondness for the sandwiches prepared by his family's head chef. During "Battle! Team Star, the Delinquents!!", Arven crafted a sandwich for Violet, but Miraidon consumed it before she could, requiring Arven to prepare another. Following their success in defeating Titan Klawf and locating the Sweet Herba Mystica in "Acquisition! New Ability: Dash!!", Violet, Arven, and Nemona were treated to sandwiches made by Arven featuring the herb. Upon consuming one of these herb-infused sandwiches, Miraidon acquired the ability to dash. In "Challenge! The Avant-Garde Gym Leader!!", Nemona offered Scarlet a sandwich to eat after catching up with her in Artazon. Episode PASV11 depicts Arven creating sandwiches with the Bitter Herba Mystica, feeding one to his Mabosstiff to aid its recovery, while Miraidon

Title: Tera Orb

A Tera Orb (Japanese: テラスタルオーブ Terastal Orb) is a Key Item, introduced in Generation IX, shaped like a Poké Ball. Its purpose is to enable a Trainer's Pokémon to Terastallize. A decade prior to the events of Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Professors Sada/Turo commenced investigations into the Terastal phenomenon within Area Zero. They discovered that energy from the shining crystals in Area Zero triggers this phenomenon. With Director Clavell's support, the professor then created Tera Orbs from these Terastal crystals, making Terastallization possible outside Area Zero. These orbs were subsequently shared with the Paldea League and Naranja Academy/Uva Academy.

The Tera Orb allows the player's Pokémon to Terastallize in battle. After use, it requires recharging at a Pokémon Center or an unvisited Tera Raid crystal. Exceptions to this are after the first portion of a Titan Pokémon battle, between Pokémon League battles, and following each battle inside Area Zero. In Tera Raid Battles, it starts uncharged regardless of its previous state and needs three damaging moves to charge. It can still only be used once per battle, and its current state isn't altered after the battle. During the battle with Stellar Form Terapagos at the end of The Indigo Disk DLC, it functions similarly but requires only two damaging moves and can be used up to four times. After this battle, the orb becomes overcharged with Terapagos's Terastal energy, eliminating the need for recharging between future battles.

The Tera Orb first appeared in the anime in *Nemona and Brassius and…*, where Brassius Terastallized his Sudowoodo into a Grass type during his battle against Roy. In *Fly! Wattrel!!*, Friede was revealed to own a Tera Orb, which he used to Terastallize his Charizard into a Dark type for an advantage against Spinel's Beheeyem and Magneton. *Respective Departures* revealed Hamber's ownership of a Tera Orb, used to Terastallize his Dusknoir to power up its Ghost moves during a training battle with Amethio. Hamber later presented Amethio with his own Tera Orb. Amethio first deployed his orb in *From So Far Away*, Terastallizing his Ceruledge into a pure Ghost type while facing the Black Rayquaza. *Welcome to Naranja Academy!* depicted Liko, Roy, and Dot each receiving a Tera Orb from Geeta upon their enrollment. All academy students participating in the Tera Training course receive a Tera Orb, which they are permitted to retain should they successfully complete the two assigned academy tests. Liko first used her Tera Orb in *Put Your Heart Into It!*, Terastallizing her Floragato to power up her Grass moves against Katy, who likewise used her orb to Terastallize her Teddiursa into a Bug type. Roy first used his Tera Orb in *Sparkle! The Glow of Flame and Art*, Terastallizing his Fuecoco to amplify its Fire moves during his rematch with Brassius. Dot's initial activation came in *Trending Terastallization! Dance, Dance, Quaxly!*, Terastallizing her Quaxly to empower its Water moves in a battle against Iono, who likewise Terastallized her Bellibolt to augment its Electric moves. Rika was observed using her Tera Orb in *Liko vs. Rika! Towards the Battle's End* to Terastallize her Clodsire into a pure Ground type, leading to its victory over Liko's Floragato. Larry's Tera Orb usage was seen in *Dance, Quaxly! The Blue Medali Step!*, Terastallizing his Staraptor into a pure Normal type. Ryme's orb transformed her Toxtricity into a Ghost type in *A New Song for Fuecoco*. Grusha, in *Ice Battle! Cold-Eyed Grusha*, Terastallized his Altaria into an Ice type. *Infiltrating the System! Naranja Academy in Danger!* marked Sidian's first use of his Tera Orb, Terastallizing his Garganacl to increase the potency of its Rock-type moves. The same episode also showed Dash owning an orb, but her failure in the second Tera Training test resulted in its mandatory return to Naranja Academy. In *Shine on, Terastallization! Liko vs. Roy!*, it was revealed that Coral had kept the Tera Orb obtained from her Tera Training. Coral's first usage was during *The Rising Volt Tacklers vs. The Explorers!*, Terastallizing her Scream Tail, Screamypuff, into a pure Fairy type during her confrontation with Roy. Geeta employed a Tera Orb in *Over the Top!* to Terastallize her Glimmora into a pure Rock type in her Triple Battle against Liko, Roy, and Dot. *Terastallization vs. Mega Evolution!* saw Nemona using a Tera Orb to Terastallize her Pawmot into a pure Fighting type during her battle with Roy. Penny, in *The Knight in the Ruins*, utilized a Tera Orb to Terastallize her Sylveon, boosting its Fairy moves during her battle against Dot. In *HZ117*, Lacey deployed a Tera Orb to Terastallize her Excadrill into a Fairy type during her battle against Ult. *HZ119* featured Drayton using a Tera Orb to Terastallize his Archaludon into a pure Dragon type during his rematch with Roy. Finally, *HZ127* showed Ann using a Tera Orb to Terastallize her Samurott into a Ground type during her battle against Liko.

In the manga, a Tera Orb belonging to Nemona debuted in *Breathe Out*. The following episode revealed Aliquis also owned one, with both Trainers employing Terastallization in their rematch. *Speeding! Paldea's Prince of Speed!!* marked another debut, showing Nemona using her orb to Terastallize her Pawmi into an Electric type, and Scarlet using hers to transform her Pikachu into a Flying type. In *Challenge! DJ Giacomo!!*, Violet was presented with his Tera Orb by Clive during the assault on the Segin Squad's Base, which he promptly used to Terastallize his Nymble, allowing its powered-up Bug Bite to overpower the Segin Starmobile. Consistent with the games, every Paldea League Gym Leader in the manga also possesses a Tera Orb.

As an Item card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Tera Orb is part of the English Scarlet & Violet Series (and Japanese Scarlet & Violet Era). It first appeared in the Japanese Stellar Tera Type Starter Sets and the English Surging Sparks expansion, with artwork by Ayaka Yoshida. Its effect lets players search their deck for a Tera Pokémon, reveal it, and add it to their hand. The crystals found within Tera Orbs strikingly resemble Terapagos in its dormant form.

Title: Scarlet Book

Introduced in Generation IX, the Scarlet Book (known in Japanese as スカーレットブック Scarlet Book) is a specific Key Item found exclusively within Pokémon Scarlet. Its counterpart in Pokémon Violet is the Violet Book. This artifact serves as a detailed chronicle of the Area Zero Expedition, documenting its journey into Paldea's Great Crater and the discoveries made there. Professor Raifort noted that while initially met with enthusiasm, public perception of the book gradually turned negative. People dismissed the spectacular treasures and peculiar creatures described within Area Zero as too fantastical to be real, causing the book to be widely regarded as mere fiction. A copy of this text is available for perusal in the Naranja Academy's reception area, situated on a shelf immediately to the right upon entering. The first iteration of the Scarlet Book plays a crucial role in the Zero Lab, being instrumental in deactivating Professor Sada's time machine. A secondary copy of the Scarlet Book exists solely to initiate a brief discussion with Arven at Naranja Academy, serving no other practical purpose.

Within the book, passages offer insights into the expedition:
"A photographic record from the survey team's entry point into the crater, visible in the backdrop. Good fortune blessed us with clear skies, ensuring a smooth beginning. Yet, clouds veiled the crater's distant depths, rumored to house a legendary treasure. Now, we press onward and downward."

"Enclosed within the Great Crater of Paldea lies the enigmatic Area Zero—a domain showcasing unprecedented landscapes, botanical life, and Pokémon species. Its conditions are hostile to human habitation, leading me to suspect that something entirely foreign to humankind slumbers within its abyssal reaches."

"A photograph marking a resting place for the survey team on the expedition's second day. Observe the peculiar symbols etched into the ground here—were these left by explorers from the ancient Paldean Empire, or could their origins be even older? Regardless, they seemed to carry significant meaning."

"The internal walls of Area Zero are structured with ledges that spiral downwards, forming a path for descent. Here, plant life and water sources are also present. Ancient tales speak of precious gemstones or miraculous fruits that have long reposed in Area Zero's deep confines, safeguarded by some form of guardian."

"Remarkably, the Pokémon varieties we encountered in Area Zero's upper strata appeared to differ very little from those observed outside the crater."

"Occasionally, we perceived unknown vocalizations emanating from the lower strata."

"As our descent into Area Zero progressed, we began to glimpse mysterious Pokémon—though we questioned if these massive, aggressive creatures truly qualified as Pokémon. One team member sustained a severe blow from such a beast, suffering mortal wounds and compelling us to temporarily withdraw."

"A member of the survey team inadvertently captured this image. I discern a faint resemblance to the Pokémon known as Donphan, yet distinctions such as its immense stature and the configuration of its back distinguish it—as do its formidable tusks."

"Area Zero harbored extraordinary herbs that instantaneously bestowed vitality upon consumption. We christened them Herba Mystica and endeavored to cultivate them in regions surrounding Paldea. However, before we could gather the harvest, Pokémon consumed the herbs—consequently growing in size and strength. We refer to these as Titan Pokémon."

"During our journey into Area Zero, we unearthed a buried metallic slab inscribed with an unusual emblem. Our attempts to chip off a fragment of the plate proved futile, unable to even leave a scratch. Even discerning the composition of the metal seems beyond contemporary scientific understanding. The reason for the inscription, let alone its creation, remains a mystery."

"After countless days of descent, we finally reached the very bottom of the Great Crater. There, we discovered a cavern adorned with gemstone formations, radiating a dazzling brilliance as far as the eye could see. Perhaps that peculiar light itself is the guardian said to oversee the treasure deep within the crater."

"A sketch by our artist, depicting a fantastical Pokémon as imagined. The sheer scale and ferocity of the strange Pokémon inhabiting Area Zero's lower regions ignited the artist's imagination, inspiring this drawing of what other species in these depths might resemble."

"During our exploration of Area Zero's depths, I—Heath—separated from the group and was later discovered unconscious. Upon awakening, I could only recall conversing with someone in an unfamiliar setting, as if in a dream. I was found clutching the page displayed here. The handwriting is my own, yet I possess no recollection of producing it."

"Heath, the author, photographed alongside the director of Naranja Academy. The academy's generous financial support facilitated the Area Zero Expedition, and the collected data has been shared with the institution in the hope of advancing its research and nurturing future generations of academics and scientists."

"Heath, the author of the Scarlet Book, depicted here with his partner Cyclizar. A natural historian as well as a writer, he was selected for the Area Zero Expedition and authored this account upon his return from the Great Crater of Paldea. Cyclizar also assisted the expedition by transporting the team's baggage and equipment."

(The subsequent portion, deliberately obscured and rendered unreadable, originates from the book's published edition.) "While sep▊ated from the resea▊h team in the crater's dep▊s, I found a s▊ange...en▊ty. Whether it was a ▊kémo▊, or even ▊liv▊ at all, I kn▊w no▊. It bore a s▊ll with layers of o▊rlapping he▊gons, and glea▊ed brig▊er still than ▊ mstone. Vie▊ed as a whole, it res▊led a mys▊ious, ▊illiant d▊sk."

(The following text is from Heath's original manuscript, which is currently held by his descendant, Briar.) "While separated from the research team in the crater's depths, I found a strange...entity. Whether it was a Pokémon, or even alive at all, I know not. It bore a shell with layers of overlapping hexagons, and gleamed brighter still than gemstone. Viewed as a whole, it resembled a mysterious, brilliant disk."

"I have provisionally designated this creature Terapagos. I hypothesize that its very physiology consists of the fundamental substance responsible for Pokémon's bright gleam and crystallization."

The Scarlet Book made its debut in the anime in "Liko vs. Rika! Towards the Battle's End," where Chalce was seen reading it in Naranja Academy's lobby. After she replaced it on the shelf, Dot retrieved it and began reading, attracting Terapagos's particular interest in one image. Dot subsequently borrowed the book and presented it to Liko and Roy. In the following episode, Dot also showed the book to Friede, Geeta, and Clavell, with Geeta identifying the image that captured Terapagos's attention as what is believed to be Terapagos's third form. Dot later returned the book to the library. In "Encounters at the Crystal Pool," Briar was revealed to possess the original manuscript of the Scarlet Book, which she displayed to Liko, Roy, Dot, and Friede while recounting her ancestor Heath's expedition into Area Zero. "The Wonders of the World!" unveiled that Lucius had owned a copy of the Scarlet Book a century prior, from which he gained knowledge about Terapagos. During "Entei's Fierce Battle Cry!," Briar presented the Rising Volt Tacklers with a sketch from the Scarlet Book, depicting a Pokémon sharing characteristics with Entei, which she used to corroborate Entei sightings within Area Zero. In "The Pillar of Fire," amidst the battle between the Rising Volt Tacklers and Lucius's "Entei," the creature's earth-gouging fire pillars enabled Briar to identify its true name, Gouging Fire, as listed in the Scarlet Book. "Taking the Next Steps" disclosed that Diana had acquired her own copy of the Scarlet Book. Through her study of the book, Diana theorized that for Pagogo to unleash its maximum power and eradicate all of Laquium, it would require the presence of one Pokémon from each of the 18 existing types.

The Scarlet Book is featured as a visual element in both the Regular and Full Art ultra rare versions of Explorer's Guidance, a card from the Temporal Forces expansion of the Pokémon Trading Card Game. The Explorer character illustrated on the card is depicted carrying the Scarlet Book while journeying through Area Zero. Furthermore, the Scarlet Book has been incorporated into various officially licensed merchandise items. An unresolved plot point concerns how Arven came into possession of Professor Sada's Scarlet Book, given that the player character later receives it from Sada at the Crystal Pool, a Professor who seemingly originated from the past.

Title: Violet Book

Known in Japan as バイオレットブック (Violet Book), this Key Item made its debut in Generation IX. It is an item found solely within Pokémon Violet, contrasting with the Scarlet Book, its equivalent in Pokémon Scarlet. In the Zero Lab, the initial copy of the Violet Book plays a critical role in deactivating Professor Turo's time machine. A subsequent copy serves no practical purpose beyond initiating a brief dialogue with Arven at Uva Academy. Moreover, an additional copy of the Violet Book is available for perusal in the lobby of Uva Academy.

"Accompanying a photograph depicting the survey team's entry point into the crater, visible behind them, the text notes: 'We were fortunate to have clear skies, ensuring a favorable start. However, distant clouds concealed the crater's profound depths, rumored to house a legendary treasure. Our journey now leads us onward and deeper.'"

"'Encompassed by the Great Crater of Paldea is the enigmatic Area Zero—an environment featuring unparalleled terrains, flora, and Pokémon species. This region proves hostile to human existence, leading me to believe that something entirely unfamiliar to humanity slumbers within its abyssal reaches.'"

"The record continues with a photo of a resting place for the survey team on the expedition's second day: 'Observe the peculiar symbols etched into the earth here—could they have been left by explorers during the era of the Paldean Empire, or do they predate even that period? Irrespective of their origin, they undeniably appear to carry significant meaning.'"

"'Spiral-shaped ledges line the inner walls of Area Zero, providing a descending route. This area also supports plant life and contains water sources. Ancient tales recount that invaluable gemstones or remarkable fruits have long reposed within Area Zero's core, safeguarded by an unknown entity.'"

"'Intriguingly, the Pokémon encountered in Area Zero's upper echelons displayed only minor distinctions from those observed beyond the crater's confines.'"

"'Occasionally, strange vocalizations emanated from the deeper levels.'"

"'With continued descent into Area Zero, we started to witness elusive, enigmatic Pokémon—prompting us to question if these small, vicious creatures were truly Pokémon. A member of our expedition sustained a severe injury from one such entity, proving fatal and necessitating a temporary withdrawal.'"

"'An expedition member serendipitously took this photograph. I note a slight likeness to the Pokémon called Donphan, however, distinguishing features like its texture and locomotion patterns indicate a distinct biological classification—not to mention its metallic treads.'"

"'Area Zero harbored extraordinary herbs that, when consumed, immediately bestowed vitality. We christened them Herba Mystica and endeavored to cultivate them across Paldea. Yet, prior to any harvest, Pokémon consumed the herbs—consequently developing immense size and strength. We refer to these as Titan Pokémon.'"

"'While navigating deeper into Area Zero, we discovered a subterranean metal plate bearing an unusual inscription. Efforts to extract a sample of the plate proved futile, as we couldn't even mar its surface. Ascertaining the composition of this metal appears to defy contemporary scientific understanding. The purpose of this inscription, let alone its creation method, remains a mystery to us.'"

"'After many days of descending, we finally arrived at the Great Crater's true depths. There, we encountered a cave filled with gemstone structures, radiating an intensely brilliant glow throughout the visible expanse. It is conceivable that this peculiar luminescence itself embodies the guardian believed to protect the profound treasure nestled within the crater.'"

"'This page showcases a depiction of a mythical Pokémon, conceptualized by our resident sketch artist. The diminutive size and malevolent nature of the peculiar Pokémon inhabiting Area Zero's lower levels stimulated the artist's creativity, inspiring this drawing of potential other species residing in these deep regions.'"

"'During our deep exploration of Area Zero, I—Heath—became separated from my colleagues and was subsequently discovered unconscious. Upon regaining consciousness, my only recollection was a conversation with an individual in an unknown location, much like a dream. I was discovered clutching the page presented here. Despite the handwriting being unmistakably mine, I possess no recollection of having penned it.'"

"'A photograph of the author, Heath, alongside the director of Uva Academy. The academy's substantial financial support facilitated the Area Zero Expedition. The collected information has been disseminated to the institution with the aim of advancing its research endeavors and nurturing upcoming scholars and scientists.'"

"'This image features Heath, the Violet Book's author, alongside his companion Cyclizar. As both a natural historian and an author, he was selected for the Area Zero Expedition and composed this record subsequent to his return from the Great Crater of Paldea. Cyclizar also contributed to the expedition by transporting the team's baggage and gear.'"

(The ensuing passage, redacted and rendered unreadable, originates from the book's published edition.) "While sep▊ated from the resea▊h team in the crater's dep▊s, I found a s▊ange...en▊ty. Whether it was a ▊kémo▊, or even ▊liv▊ at all, I kn▊w no▊. It bore a s▊ll with layers of o▊rlapping he▊gons, and glea▊d brig▊er still than ▊ mstone. Vie▊d as a whole, it res▊led a mys▊ious, ▊illiant d▊sk."

(This is the unaltered manuscript by Heath, now held by his descendant, Briar.) "While separated from the research team in the crater's depths, I found a strange...entity. Whether it was a Pokémon, or even alive at all, I know not. It bore a shell with layers of overlapping hexagons, and gleamed brighter still than gemstone. Viewed as a whole, it resembled a mysterious, brilliant disk."

"'I've provisionally designated this being Terapagos. I postulate that its fundamental composition consists of the pivotal substance responsible for Pokémon's luminous glow and crystallization.'"

Its inaugural appearance occurred in the episode 'Speeding! Paldea's Prince of Speed!!,' where Arven possessed it, engrossed in reading until Violet and Old Roto located him. Subsequently, the group utilized the volume as an aid in their quest for the Herba Mystica, referring to it for details as needed. A brief visual reference to the Violet Book is included in both the standard and Full Art ultra rare versions of the Ciphermaniac's Codebreaking card, part of the Temporal Forces expansion. The Ciphermaniac character portrayed on this card is depicted with the Violet Book in hand, navigating a high-tech laboratory environment. This book has also been a subject for various officially sanctioned merchandise. A puzzling inconsistency exists regarding how Arven acquired Professor Turo's Violet Book, given that the player later obtains it from Turo at the Crystal Pool, with Turo appearing to have traveled from the past.

Title: Fire Tera Shard

An item class introduced in Generation IX, the Fire Tera Shard (Japanese: テラピースほのお Tera Piece Flame) functions as a specific kind of Tera Shard. Its purpose is to alter a Pokémon's Tera Type, aligning it with the Fire elemental type. Fifty Fire Tera Shards allow for a Pokémon's Tera Type to be converted to Fire at Medali's Treasure Eatery. Exceptions to this alteration are Ogerpon and Terapagos; Ogerpon's Tera Type is already fixed as Fire when in its Hearthflame Mask form.

Title: Water Tera Shard

Introduced in Generation IX, the Water Tera Shard (Japanese: テラピースみず Tera Piece Water) is an item belonging to the Tera Shard category, utilized for modifying a Pokémon's Tera Type. This specific shard is associated with the Water type. To transform a Pokémon's Tera Type into Water, 50 Water Tera Shards must be employed at Medali's Treasure Eatery.
