Exceptions to this process are Ogerpon and Terapagos; Ogerpon, notably, maintains a fixed Water Tera Type when assuming its Wellspring Mask form.

Title: Grass Tera Shard
The Grass Tera Shard, identified in Japanese as テラピースくさ (Tera Piece Grass), was introduced in Generation IX. This specific item is a variant within the broader Tera Shard classification, primarily utilized for modifying a Pokémon's Tera Type. It specifically corresponds to the Grass elemental type. A sum of 50 Grass Tera Shards enables a Pokémon's Tera Type to be switched to Grass at the Treasure Eatery located in Medali. This alteration service, however, does not extend to Ogerpon or Terapagos, as Ogerpon already possesses a Tera Type permanently fixed to Grass while in its Teal Mask form.

Title: Rock Tera Shard
The Rock Tera Shard, an item first appearing in Generation IX, is known in Japanese as テラピースいわ (Tera Piece Rock). This particular variety of Tera Shard functions to alter a Pokémon's Tera Type, corresponding specifically to the Rock element. A total of 50 Rock Tera Shards are required at Medali's Treasure Eatery to switch a Pokémon's Tera Type to Rock. However, Ogerpon and Terapagos are exempt from this process; Ogerpon, for example, already possesses a fixed Rock Tera Type when in its Cornerstone Mask form.

Title: Dark Tera Shard

First appearing in Generation IX, the item known as a Dark Tera Shard (Japanese: テラピースあく Tera Piece Evil) is a specific type of consumable. This particular variety of Tera Shard facilitates altering a Pokémon's Tera Type, specifically correlating with the Dark type. To transform a Pokémon's Tera Type to Dark, a quantity of 50 Dark Tera Shards must be utilized at Medali's Treasure Eatery; however, Ogerpon and Terapagos are exceptions and cannot undergo this specific change. It is noteworthy that Dark Tera Shards were among three Tera Shard types—the others being Normal and Stellar Tera Shards—that were not distributed during the Shiny Treasures of Ruin Tera Shards event, despite Dark being the common type shared by all four treasures of ruin.

Title: Booster Energy

An item categorized as Booster Energy (ブーストエナジー in Japanese, or Boost Energy) made its debut in Generation IX. This item, when held by a Pokémon, triggers its Ability Protosynthesis if harsh sunlight is absent, or Quark Drive if Electric Terrain is not in effect. Its activation results in its consumption. Moves and Abilities designed to manipulate items, specifically Fling, Trick, or Symbiosis, are unable to transfer Booster Energy to or from a Paradox Pokémon, with the sole exceptions of Koraidon and Miraidon. Given that Booster Energy is restored after battle upon consumption (a mechanic applied to all non-Berry items starting from Pokémon Scarlet and Violet), it remains obtainable from wild Paradox Pokémon even if it was used up in the encounter. Within the game Pokémon Masters EX, only specific sync pairs possessing it as an item move are capable of utilizing Booster Energy. In the Pokémon Trading Card Game, Booster Energy made its debut as two distinct Pokémon Tool cards: Ancient Booster Energy Capsule and Future Booster Energy Capsule. These were part of the English Scarlet & Violet Series (known as the Japanese Scarlet & Violet Era). Their initial Japanese release occurred in the Ancient Roar and Future Flash expansions, respectively, preceding their English debut in the Paradox Rift expansion. Each of these cards offers specific benefits to the Paradox Pokémon they are attached to: An Ancient Pokémon with an Ancient Booster Energy Capsule attached gains an extra 60 HP and becomes immune to all Special Conditions, whereas a Future Pokémon holding a Future Booster Energy Capsule incurs no Retreat Cost and deals an additional 20 damage with its attacks to the opponent's Active Pokémon. Furthermore, a Booster Energy accessory accompanies the Chogokin Iron Bundle and Iron Moth figures. This accessory functions as a magnet key, illuminating the figure's lights when positioned upon it.

Title: Ability Shield

Introduced in Generation IX, the Ability Shield (Japanese: とくせいガード, or Ability Guard) is a specific type of held item. Its core purpose is to safeguard the bearer's Ability, rendering it immune to alteration or suppression from any effect. This protection extends even to influences directly originating from the Pokémon holding the item, such as those from the move Role Play or the Ability Trace. Furthermore, it defends the user from effects that normally disregard Abilities. For instance, if a Pokémon with Clear Body or Big Pecks is clutching an Ability Shield and faces an opponent with Mold Breaker using Leer, its Defense stat will not be lowered. Should a Pokémon holding an Ability Shield undergo a transformation into another Pokémon—whether through the move Transform or the Imposter Ability—it will still replicate the opponent's Ability. This newly acquired Ability will then also receive the Shield's protection.

Title: Mirror Herb

Originating in Generation IX, the Mirror Herb (Japanese: ものまねハーブ, or Imitation Herb) is a distinctive item. In combat, upon an opponent's stat augmentation, the equipped Pokémon consumes the Mirror Herb, acquiring an identical boost. Should a single move from an opponent elevate multiple stats (e.g., using Clangorous Soul while holding a Throat Spray), all such enhancements are mirrored. The Mirror Herb additionally facilitates Egg Move inheritance: if a Pokémon holding it possesses an empty move slot and shares a picnic with another Pokémon that knows an Egg Move the former can learn, the first Pokémon will acquire that move once the picnic concludes. This occurs regardless of Egg Group compatibility or shared gender, and crucially, this process does not expend the Mirror Herb.

Title: Covert Cloak

An item first appearing in Generation IX, the Covert Cloak (Japanese: おんみつマント Covert Mantle) serves a specific function. When a Pokémon carries a Covert Cloak, it gains immunity to the secondary effects of offensive moves used by opposing Pokémon. This includes effects stemming from abilities like Poison Touch, Toxic Chain, and Stench, as well as those from items such as King's Rock and Razor Fang. As an illustration, a Pokémon equipped with this item will not suffer a burn from Flamethrower, nor will it flinch from Fake Out. However, the Covert Cloak offers no protection against status moves; Thunder Wave and Leech Seed are prime examples of moves it does not influence. Furthermore, secondary effects that benefit the move's user are not negated. For instance, a Pokémon employing Flame Charge will still experience its Speed stat rising, even when striking a foe that possesses a Covert Cloak. It is noteworthy that since every move capable of causing freezing or flinching is categorized as an attacking move, the Covert Cloak effectively provides full immunity to these specific status conditions.

The item exhibits a peculiar interaction with Sparkling Aria: it prevents a Pokémon's burn from being healed if that Pokémon is the sole target of the move (this applies even in formats like Double Battles, provided no other Pokémon are affected by Sparkling Aria). Conversely, should Sparkling Aria hit multiple targets, all targeted Pokémon, including those holding a Covert Cloak, will have their burns cured. This specific behavior concerning burn curing is exclusive to Sparkling Aria. For example, the Covert Cloak will still prevent a Pokémon from suffering paralysis from Discharge, irrespective of how many targets the move hits. The Covert Cloak offers no defense against Clear Smog's ability to reset stat changes. Finally, the item itself cannot evade removal when targeted by Knock Off.

Title: Mustard

For the condiment, refer to Mustard (item). Known in Japan as マスタード (Mustard), this individual is a prominent figure within the Galar region's Isle of Armor expansion for Pokémon Sword and Shield. He once held the prestigious title of Galar Champion, and holds the distinction of being the sole character in the games with the Trainer class Dojo Master (Japanese: どうじょうぬし Dojo Master). Before his championship, Mustard served as a Fighting-type Gym Leader.

Approximately five decades before the events of Sword and Shield, Mustard secured the Galar League Champion title following an intense confrontation with Opal. He maintained this championship reign for an unparalleled 18 years, surpassing all predecessors and successors. His tenure as Champion concluded only when his battling capabilities diminished in the wake of his partner Pokémon's demise. Subsequently, he declined a proposal from the Galar Pokémon League chairman at the time to manipulate a match in his favor, choosing instead to withdraw completely from the Galar League. He embarked on extensive global travels before acquiring an unchristened island near Galar's coastline, which he then named the Isle of Armor and where he established his Master Dojo for Pokémon battle instruction.

Among his pupils was Leon, who would eventually achieve Champion status himself, though his training under Mustard remained incomplete due to his notoriously bad navigation skills, which hindered his ability to locate the Towers of Two Fists. Upon the player's arrival at the Master Dojo, Mustard enrolls them as a student, engaging in an initial, straightforward battle. He subsequently subjects all his trainees to a series of three trials, culminating in the player's victory and the receipt of a Kubfu from Mustard as a reward. Following the bonding with Kubfu, the player must facilitate its evolution at either the Tower of Darkness or the Tower of Waters, based on their preference, with Mustard serving as the ultimate challenger before Kubfu can transform into Urshifu. Once the player uncovers the means to unleash Urshifu's Gigantamax capabilities, Mustard issues a final, all-out challenge. Post-battle, a daily rematch opportunity becomes available by conversing with him while he is occupied with Pokémon Quest within the Master Dojo.

The following is an excerpt from the reverse side of Mustard's League Cards in Pokémon Sword and Shield. "Mustard, a former Fighting-type Gym Leader, is celebrated as one of Galar's most formidable Champions, maintaining an unbeaten streak for 18 years. His fervent pursuit of power led him to demand rigorous discipline, from himself and others. However, his disposition underwent a significant transformation subsequent to his post-retirement world travels. His unexpected acquisition of a desolate island astonished those around him. When questioned about his motives, he responded with a grin, "Strength manifests in many forms throughout this world. My desire is to witness every one of them!" The designation 'Isle of Armor' was also his brainchild."

"An image from approximately 50 years ago depicts Mustard's triumph over the Fairy-type Gym Leader, marking his ascent to Champion. He remained undefeated until the loss of his partner Pokémon, after which his defeat tally began to rise. Despite relinquishing the Champion's mantle, his popularity endured. His decision to retire was spurred by the then-league chairman's request for him to partake in a fixed match. Post-retirement, Mustard cultivated a profound interest in video games, a hobby he evidently suppressed during his active career to uphold his public persona." In combat, Mustard will utilize his Urshifu's Gigantamax form at the earliest chance. Following the conclusion of the Galarian Star Tournament, his Pokémon's combat levels will escalate. He employs these three Pokémon as both allies and adversaries during the Galarian Star Tournament.

The player receives a Kubfu from Mustard once they successfully navigate his trio of trials. Furthermore, Mustard possesses three swift Galarian Slowpoke, deployed in the initial Master Dojo challenge. Each of these Slowpoke boasts 31 Individual Values (IVs) in both HP and Speed, with 252 Effort Values (EVs) allocated to HP and Speed, and 6 EVs to Special Defense. They commence battle with a remarkable +6 stage boost in Speed. Though these Slowpoke encounters simulate wild Pokémon, none of the three are capturable.

In 'The Gathering of Stars,' Mustard made an appearance at his Master Dojo on the Isle of Armor. He received a visit from his former student, Leon, who, following a brief battle, presented him with an invitation to the Galarian Star Tournament. Subsequently, he was observed at Wyndon Stadium, participating in the tournament's inaugural ceremony. The subsequent details pertain to Mustard's documented Pokémon in Pokémon: Twilight Wings.

Mustard's first appearance was in 'Flutter!! Tickets to a New Land.' During their training, Klara and Avery discovered Henry Sword unconscious, afflicted by Eternatus's poison. Henry was brought to the Master Dojo for recuperation, where he was granted indefinite lodging. Klara departed to welcome new arrivals at the Armor Station, returning with Casey and Marvin, who promptly clarified their sole purpose was to find Henry. During the trio's stay on the island, Mustard entrusted Henry with his Kubfu, envisioning that exploring would broaden Kubfu's horizons and alleviate its timidness.

Within 'Bom Bom!! Kubfu and Intense Training,' as Henry, Casey, and Marvin sought the Rusted Sword and Shield, they reached the Tower of Darkness. A Master Dojo Student positioned at the entrance denied them access to the structure unless Henry and Kubfu could first overcome her in a single Pokémon battle. Despite his apprehension that they were not adequately prepared, Mustard sanctioned Henry and Kubfu's challenge against the student. However, despite its valiant attempts, Kubfu failed to secure victory. That evening, Kubfu ventured into the tower autonomously, only to be defeated by an unidentified Pokémon.

By 'Rrmbll!! Serious Tower Battle,' following several days of rigorous training, Kubfu opted to pursue its development at the Tower of Waters instead. Henry and Kubfu ascended through numerous levels, eventually reaching the summit where Mustard and his Single Strike Style Urshifu awaited them. Urshifu presented a formidable challenge, but Kubfu strategically attacked its feet, causing it to falter and creating an opening for a decisive Close Combat. Mustard then permitted Kubfu to inspect the Scroll of Waters, prompting its evolution into a Rapid Strike Style Urshifu. With their training fulfilled, Mustard proposed that Henry permanently retain Urshifu on his roster, a suggestion Henry embraced.

A compilation of Pokémon Trading Card Game entries that reference or depict Mustard or his Pokémon follows. Mustard holds the distinction of being the first non-player character Champion in the core series games to employ a Legendary Pokémon. He is also one of only two characters to possess a single-digit uniform number, the other being Leon. Notably, Mustard is the sole Champion who, despite previously specializing in a particular Pokémon type, did not maintain that specialization during his championship tenure. Nevertheless, all the Pokémon he deploys in the Galarian Star Tournament are of the Fighting type, likely a nod to his former area of expertise.

Title: Sweet Herba Mystica

Sweet Herba Mystica, referred to in Japanese as ひでん：あまスパイス (Secret: Sweet Spice), is an ingredient introduced in Generation IX. It stands as one of the five distinct Herba Mystica and can be incorporated into sandwiches during their preparation. Murdock was seen procuring various Herba Mystica types in Levincia during the episode Fly! Wattrel!!. Subsequently, in Acquisition! New Ability: Dash!!, Sweet Herba Mystica marked the first Herba Mystica discovered by Violet and Arven, after Titan Klawf led them to its whereabouts. Following the expulsion of Klawf, Arven used this specific Herba Mystica to prepare sandwiches for himself, Violet, and Nemona. When Miraidon ate one of these sandwiches, it developed the capacity to dash at an increased speed.

Title: Salty Herba Mystica

Known in Japanese as ひでん：しおスパイス (Secret: Salt Spice), Salty Herba Mystica made its debut as an ingredient in Generation IX. This item ranks among the five distinct Herba Mystica. Its primary application involves sandwich preparation, serving as a key component. The episode *Fly! Wattrel!!* depicted Murdock acquiring various Herba Mystica types within Levincia. *PASV18* records Salty Herba Mystica as the third such item uncovered by Violet and Arven, guided to its location by Titan Orthworm. Once Orthworm had been repelled, Arven prepared sandwiches utilizing the Herba Mystica for himself, Violet, Scarlet, and Nemona. Miraidon, upon consuming one of these sandwiches, subsequently acquired an enhanced jumping capability. Uniquely among the Herba Mystica, the Salty variant deviates from the standard Contest flavor nomenclature, which would have classified it as "Dry"; instead, its flavor aligns with the Chinese Five Phases system.

Title: Bitter Herba Mystica

The Bitter Herba Mystica (Japanese: ひでん：にがスパイス Secret: Bitter Spice) is a unique ingredient that debuted in Generation IX. It is distinguished as one of the five specific varieties of Herba Mystica, and its utility lies in its application as a component for crafting sandwiches. The events of Fly! Wattrel!! illustrate Murdock acquiring different kinds of Herba Mystica while in Levincia. During PASV11, Violet and Arven's efforts led them to uncover the Bitter Herba Mystica, a discovery made after their triumph over Titan Bombirdier. Arven subsequently prepared a series of sandwiches using this newfound ingredient, sharing them with himself, his Pokémon Mabosstiff, Violet, and Mireia, a Musician who had previously assisted them in confronting Bombirdier. Notably, the restorative properties of the Bitter Herba Mystica facilitated Mabosstiff's recovery to a greater extent, bringing considerable reassurance to Arven. Furthermore, when Miraidon consumed one of these sandwiches, it experienced a significant power-up, prompting Professor Turo to contact Violet and elaborate that Miraidon had regained its inherent capacity to swim.

Title: Spicy Herba Mystica

Making its debut in Generation IX, the ingredient known as Spicy Herba Mystica (Japanese: ひでん：からスパイス Secret: Spicy Spice) is classified as one of the five distinct Herba Mystica. This item finds its culinary application in the preparation of sandwiches. During the events of Fly! Wattrel!!, Liko encountered a purported advertisement for Spicy Herba Mystica being offered for sale in Levincia. She subsequently departed with the aim of obtaining some for Murdock. Nevertheless, this turned out to be a deceptive scheme orchestrated by Spinel, crafted with the intention of luring her into a trap.

Title: Apple

When discussing apples utilized as sandwich components in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, see Apple (item). For the specific apples integral to evolving Applin, refer instead to Tart Apple, Sweet Apple, and Syrupy Apple. The single apple serving as a curry ingredient within Pokémon Sword and Shield is detailed under Fancy Apple. Apples appearing throughout the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series are documented as Food (Mystery Dungeon). Regarding the apple-shaped produce thrown at wild Pokémon in Pokémon Snap, its details are at Pokémon food → Apple-shaped Pokémon food. The similar apple-like fruit found in New Pokémon Snap is known as Fluffruit.

Title: Gimmighoul Coin
This item, known in Japanese as コレクレーのコイン (Collecurei Coin), made its debut in Generation IX of the Pokémon series. Within the games Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, it functions as a TM Material. Accumulating 999 Gimmighoul Coins triggers Gimmighoul's evolution into Gholdengo upon leveling up; this process consumes the collected coins. Notably, in Pokémon Legends: Z-A, this evolution can be initiated manually. Despite their classification as a TM Material in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, these coins serve no actual purpose in crafting Technical Machines. For Pokémon GO players, Gimmighoul Coins are the requisite item for evolving Gimmighoul into Gholdengo. Diverging from most Evolution items, Gimmighoul Coins occupy no inventory slots in the player's Item Bag and are impossible to discard. Before February 27, 2023, this item was initially referred to as the ?????? Coin (Japanese: ??????コイン ?????? Coin). Its initial availability commenced on November 5, 2022, directly after the conclusion of Community Day Classic: Dratini, and a full day prior to the official unveiling of Gimmighoul's name. A trainer can facilitate Gimmighoul's evolution into Gholdengo provided they possess a minimum of 999 Gimmighoul Coins within their Item Bag. This particular evolution does not require Candy, establishing Gimmighoul as the sole Pokémon in the game to evolve without its use. As the ?????? Coin before February 27, 2023, it was classified as a Key Item and held no functional utility within the game. Acquiring one from a golden PokéStop would cause a Roaming Form Gimmighoul to manifest in the overworld; however, this Pokémon remained uncatchable until February 27, 2023. In the context of repeatable acquisition methods, both the quantity of coins obtained and the occurrence of receiving any coins are subject to randomness, barring explicit exceptions. Within Pokémon Masters EX, these coins constitute an event-exclusive item, attainable through the special event "Mysterious Old Coins" and by successfully finishing the "Get Gimmighoul Coins Mission Bingo." Players could trade them for various items at the Gimmighoul Coin Special Exchange. Turning to Pokémon Café ReMix, Gimmighoul Coins are an event-specific item procurable via the Cook and Collect feature. The "Collect with Gholdengo" event, active since March 1, 2024, spans durations of 4 to 5 days, requiring participants to gather Gimmighoul coins to recruit Gholdengo. These coins are acquired by clearing Gimmighoul icons in specific puzzles, which initially encompassed One-Minute Cooking and Slow Cooking events, but became restricted to Main Orders, Extra Orders, and Master Café Mode from September 18, 2025, onwards. Successfully clearing Roaming Form icons rewards the player with 1 coin, while Chest Form icons yield 5 coins. A daily cap of 50 coins limits collection. A grand total of 4,995 coins must be amassed to unlock all event rewards, comprising Gholdengo itself, its outfit enhancements, and all necessary Gholdengo cookies to achieve its maximum levels. Gimmighoul Coins made their initial animated appearance in "The Terapagos I Don't Know." Ludlow was the first to discover one, only for it to be promptly seized from him. Subsequently, Roy gathered a coin discarded by a Roaming Form Gimmighoul and continued his collection, having been manipulated by a Chest Form Gimmighoul that ultimately absconded with the coins. The coins became a recurring element in the series following the inclusion of this same Chest Form Gimmighoul among the wild Pokémon on the Brave Olivine in "The Knight in the Ruins," and its later recruitment to Dot's team in "Riding the Waves." Dot's Gimmighoul successfully evolved into Gholdengo in HZ120, having finally accumulated 1,000 coins. One Gimmighoul Coin was featured in "Breathe Out," where a Corviknight carried it off. Scarlet's profession as a treasure hunter was highlighted by her collection of Gimmighoul Coins, first seen in "Raid! The Dark Crew Base!!" During "Skillful! The Mysterious Treasure Hunter!!", Scarlet bested a Chest Form Gimmighoul situated at a watchtower in South Province (Area Two), obtaining 60 coins and increasing her total to 88. A flashback in "Acquisition! New Ability: Dash!!" depicted Scarlet acquiring additional coins from a different Chest Form Gimmighoul in South Province (Area Three).

Title: Auspicious Armor

An item type first introduced in Generation IX, the Auspicious Armor (イワイノヨロイ, known also as Auspicious Armor in Japanese) has a singular function. When used, it prompts a Charcadet to evolve into Armarouge, consuming the item in the process. This item first appeared in HZ123, given to Liko by her grandmother specifically for the evolution of her Charcadet. In the episode that followed, Charcadet wore the armor during a battle where it fought alongside Amethio's Ceruledge against Indi and Rubina's two Salamence. During this battle's critical moment, the armor facilitated Charcadet's Evolution into Armarouge. In PT16, a set of Auspicious Armor was discovered in Yūto's home. This specific armor had been an heirloom in Yūto's family for many generations, with a tradition linking it to Charcadet. After learning of this, Yūto's Charcadet felt compelled to make contact with the armor, which resulted in its evolution into Armarouge. A flashback in "Challenge! The Avant-Garde Gym Leader!!" depicted a set of Auspicious Armor being bestowed upon Scarlet by Mela, along with a Charcadet. Scarlet later deployed it to evolve that Charcadet into Armarouge. Uniquely among Pokémon that have evolved using Evolution items in the anime, Liko's Charcadet did not glow during its Evolution; instead, the Auspicious Armor itself was the source of the glow.

Title: Teal Mask

This entry describes the Key Item. For information concerning the expansion for Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, please consult The Teal Mask. The Teal Mask, known in Japanese as みどりのめん (Green Mask), is a Key Item first appearing in Generation IX and serves as the titular artifact of The Teal Mask expansion. Its role in the narrative dictates its return to Ogerpon. Ogerpon assumes its Teal Mask form, featuring this specific mask, as its inherent state unless it is holding either the Wellspring Mask, Hearthflame Mask, or Cornerstone Mask. The Teal Mask finds representation within the Pokémon Trading Card Game as part of the Ogre's Mask Item card (Twilight Masquerade 159), where it is illustrated alongside Ogerpon's complete set of masks. This card premiered during the English Scarlet & Violet Series (known as the Japanese Scarlet & Violet Era). It was launched in the Japanese Transformation Mask expansion and the English Twilight Masquerade expansion, with its artwork crafted by 5ban Graphics. The card's effect permits a player to search their discard pile for a Pokémon ex card bearing "Ogerpon" in its name, and then swap it with another "Ogerpon"-named Pokémon ex card currently in play, effectively simulating a form change.

Title: Fairy Feather

Known in Japanese as ようせいのハネ, the Fairy Feather is an equipable item that first appeared in Generation IX. This item enhances the offensive capabilities of the holder's Fairy-type moves, providing a 20% power boost. The Fairy Feather holds the distinction of being the final non-consumable type-specific enhancement item introduced that is not associated with a particular Pokémon, unlike relics such as Plates. Before the Fairy Feather's debut in The Teal Mask expansion, the Fairy Gem and Pixie Plate were also conceived as held items for strengthening Fairy-type assaults; however, of these two, only the Pixie Plate has ever been obtainable within the games.

Title: Wellspring Mask

Originating in Generation IX, the Wellspring Mask (Japanese: いどのめん Well Mask) is a held item. When Ogerpon equips this item, it assumes its Wellspring Mask form. It also provides a 20% increase to the power of its moves. For additional information, see the main article: Ogre's Mask (Twilight Masquerade 159). In the Pokémon Trading Card Game, the Wellspring Mask, along with Ogerpon's other masks, is featured on the Ogre's Mask Item card. This card was introduced during the English Scarlet & Violet Series (or Japanese Scarlet & Violet Era). It premiered in the Japanese Transformation Mask expansion and the English Twilight Masquerade expansion, with artwork crafted by 5ban Graphics. This card permits a player to search their discard pile for a Pokémon ex card that includes "Ogerpon" in its name and then swap it with another "Ogerpon"-named Pokémon ex card already active in play, thereby simulating a "form change."

Title: Hearthflame Mask

First introduced in Generation IX, the Hearthflame Mask (Japanese: かまどのめん, or Kamado Mask) functions as a held item. When Ogerpon equips this item, it undergoes a transformation into its Hearthflame Mask form. Furthermore, this item boosts the damage output of Ogerpon's moves by 20%. For more details, consult the main article: Ogre's Mask (Twilight Masquerade 159). Its likeness appears with Ogerpon's other masks on the Ogre's Mask card, an Item card that debuted in the Pokémon Trading Card Game during the English Scarlet & Violet Series (corresponding to the Japanese Scarlet & Violet Era). This card was made available through the Japanese Transformation Mask expansion and the English Twilight Masquerade expansion, featuring artwork by 5ban Graphics. The card grants players the ability to retrieve a Pokémon ex card featuring "Ogerpon" in its name from their discard pile. They can then swap it with another "Ogerpon"-named Pokémon ex card currently in play, thereby simulating a "form change".

Title: Cornerstone Mask

An item designed to be held, known as the Cornerstone Mask (いしずえのめん in Japanese, translating to Cornerstone Mask), made its debut in Generation IX. When Ogerpon grips this item, it assumes its Cornerstone Mask form. Should Ogerpon possess a Cornerstone Mask, its appearance transforms into its Cornerstone Mask form. This item also grants a 20% boost to the strength of Ogerpon's attacks. For additional details, consult the main article: Ogre's Mask (Twilight Masquerade 159).

In the Pokémon Trading Card Game, within the Item card "Ogre's Mask," the Cornerstone Mask is illustrated with Ogerpon's other masks. This card first appeared during the English Scarlet & Violet Series (known as the Japanese Scarlet & Violet Era). Its release took place in the Japanese Transformation Mask expansion and the English Twilight Masquerade expansion, featuring visuals created by 5ban Graphics. This card grants players the ability to retrieve an "Ogerpon" Pokémon ex card from their discard pile. They can then swap it with a different "Ogerpon"-named Pokémon ex card already active, effectively simulating a "form change."

Title: Syrupy Apple

Known in Japanese as みついりりんご (Honeycore Apple), the Syrupy Apple is a distinct item type that first appeared in Generation IX. When utilized, it initiates Applin's evolution into Dipplin, with the item being consumed in the process. This item made its debut in Pokémon GO on October 10, 2025. Within Pokémon GO, Syrupy Apples are an essential component for the evolution of particular Pokémon, and they are expended each time they are applied. Specifically, Applin's transition to Dipplin demands 200 and 20 Syrupy Apples. By interacting with apple icons that manifest near PokéStops, players have a chance to obtain Sweet Apples, Tart Apples, Syrupy Apples, or even encounter a wild Applin. Furthermore, the likelihood of these apples appearing at PokéStops is enhanced when a Mossy Lure Module is activated.

Title: Linking Cord

First appearing in Pokémon Legends: Arceus, the Linking Cord (known in Japanese as つながりのヒモ) serves as an Evolution-triggering item. Its primary function is facilitating the Evolution of specific Pokémon: Kadabra transforms into Alakazam, Machoke into Machamp, Graveler into Golem, and Haunter into Gengar. Utilizing this item initiates the Evolution process for these creatures, all of whom possess the alternative method of evolving via trading. Each application depletes the Linking Cord. In the context of Pokémon Sleep, the Linking Cord is also an essential component for evolving particular Pokémon, being consumed upon its application. Specific Evolution requirements within Pokémon Sleep are as follows: Graveler progresses to Golem with 80 Geodude Candy and a Linking Cord. Slowpoke ascends to Slowking when provided with 80 Slowpoke Candy, a Linking Cord, and a King's Rock. Haunter evolves into Gengar using 80 Gastly Candy and a Linking Cord. Lastly, Onix becomes Steelix through the combination of 80 Onix Candy, a Linking Cord, and a Metal Coat. Beginning with game version 1.0.13, using a Linking Cord directly from the Bag grants the player 5 Candies pertaining to a random Pokémon capable of evolving with this item. Despite its role in replacing standard trade Evolutions, the Linking Cord is unsuitable for Pokémon that traditionally evolve via trade while holding a specific item. In Pokémon Legends: Arceus, such Pokémon instead evolve by merely applying their designated held item directly to them. The design of this item pays homage to the Game Link Cable, the peripheral employed for Pokémon trades (and thus, trade-based Evolutions) across the initial three generations of the main series titles (excluding trades made with the Game Boy Advance Wireless Adapter). Visually, the Linking Cord's sprite in Pokémon Legends: Arceus draws inspiration from Mizuhiki, a Japanese art of knot tying. Specifically, it features an Awaji knot, symbolizing togetherness—a potential allusion to the original purpose of the Link Cable. It's worth noting that the first three installments of the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series included an Evolution item named Link Cable, which functioned comparably to the Linking Cord despite being introduced significantly earlier.
